title: The Expedition to Tabuk in Rajab of 9 AH
summary: The Messenger of God (SAAS) led an expedition to Tabuk in Rajab of 9 AH, a major military campaign that tested the resolve and sincerity of the Muslim community.
story: AN ACCOUNT OF THE EXPEDITION TO TABUK IN RAJAB OF 9 AH. Those who wept and also those who made excuses to remain behind.
lessons: Difficult trials reveal true character and commitment to one's principles.
Some people make excuses during times of hardship, but sincere believers push through even with heavy hearts.
Weeping out of genuine inability to participate in a noble cause is itself a form of devotion.
practical_advice: When facing an overwhelming obligation, assess honestly whether your barrier is genuine or an excuse born of comfort.
If you are unable to contribute in the primary way, find other meaningful ways to support a righteous cause.
Allow yourself to grieve honestly when you cannot do what you feel called to do — this grief itself is a sign of a living heart.

title: Passing by the Dwellings of Thamud at Al-Hijr
summary: On the way to Tabuk, the Messenger of God (SAAS) passed by the ancient dwellings and fortifications of the destroyed people of Thamud at Al-Hijr, a sobering reminder of divine punishment for those who reject God's messengers.
story: AN ACCOUNT OF HOW, ON HIS WAY TO TABUK, THE MESSENGER OF GOD (SAAS) PASSED BY THE DWELLINGS OF THAMUD AND THEIR FORTIFICATIONS AT AL-HIJR.
lessons: The ruins of past civilizations serve as powerful reminders of the consequences of arrogance and rejection of truth.
Reflecting on the fate of those who came before us can reorient our priorities and deepen our humility.
History is not merely academic; it carries moral and spiritual weight for those who pause to reflect.
practical_advice: When you encounter reminders of impermanence — whether ruins, loss, or endings — use them as invitations to reflect rather than reasons to despair.
Cultivate the habit of pondering the lives of those who came before you as a source of wisdom and grounding.
Let awareness of consequence guide your choices before consequences arrive, rather than after.

title: The Address of the Messenger of God at Tabuk
summary: While encamped at Tabuk, the Messenger of God (SAAS) delivered an important address to his companions at a palm tree, offering guidance and counsel during a challenging expedition.
story: AN ACCOUNT OF THE ADDRESS THE MESSENGER OF GOD (SAAS) GAVE AT TABUK AT A PALM TREE THERE.
lessons: Leadership in difficulty requires clear, grounding communication to sustain the morale and faith of those who follow.
Words of wisdom delivered at the right moment can provide direction to those who are lost or uncertain.
Even in hardship, the Prophet (SAAS) continued to teach and guide, showing that spiritual nourishment must accompany physical effort.
practical_advice: In times of collective stress, seek out or offer words that reorient people toward their higher purpose.
Do not wait for ideal conditions to share knowledge or encouragement — difficult moments are often when it is most needed.
When feeling overwhelmed by a long or difficult journey, pause and reconnect with the reason you began.

title: The Prayer Spoken for Mu'awiya b. Abu Mu'awiya
summary: At Tabuk, the Messenger of God (SAAS) offered a special prayer for Mu'awiya b. Abu Mu'awiya, a narration preserved though its authenticity is noted as uncertain.
story: AN ACCOUNT OF THE PRAYER SPOKEN FOR MU'AWIYA B. ABU MU'AWIYA IF INDEED AUTHENTIC.
lessons: Supplication and prayer for others is a profound act of love and connection, transcending physical distance.
The Prophet's (SAAS) concern extended to individuals even amid the pressures of a large military campaign.
Scholarly honesty about the authenticity of narrations reflects the integrity at the heart of Islamic tradition.
practical_advice: Make it a habit to pray for others by name — this strengthens bonds and cultivates compassion within you.
Do not overlook individuals in times of collective effort; personal attention to others' wellbeing is itself a form of leadership.
Approach inherited stories and traditions with both openness and critical reflection, valuing accuracy as an act of integrity.

title: The Arrival of the Messenger from Caesar at Tabuk
summary: While the Messenger of God (SAAS) was encamped at Tabuk, an emissary arrived from the Byzantine Emperor Caesar, marking a moment of significant diplomatic contact.
story: The Arrival of the Messenger from Caesar to the Messenger of God (SAAS) at Tabuk.
lessons: The mission of the Prophet (SAAS) was recognized beyond the boundaries of the Arabian Peninsula, demonstrating the universal scope of his message.
Diplomatic engagement requires courage and wisdom on both sides.
God's guidance opens doors that human calculation alone could never anticipate.
practical_advice: When pursuing a righteous goal, remain open to unexpected allies or acknowledgments from unexpected quarters.
Approach encounters with those very different from you with dignity and respect, as the Prophet (SAAS) consistently modeled.
Do not underestimate the reach of sincere, principled conduct — it has a way of being noticed far beyond your immediate circle.

title: The Peace Pact with the Governor of Ayla and the People of Jarba and Adhruh
summary: While encamped at Tabuk, the Messenger of God (SAAS) concluded peace agreements with the Governor of Ayla and the people of Jarba and Adhruh, demonstrating his preference for peaceful resolution over conflict.
story: The Peace Pact the Messenger of God (SAAS) made with the Governor of Ayla and the people of Jarba' and Adhruh while he was encamped at Tabuk, before his return.
lessons: Peace is always preferable to conflict when it can be achieved with justice and dignity.
The Prophet (SAAS) used his position of strength not for domination but for the establishment of secure, equitable agreements.
Even during military campaigns, the door to peaceful resolution must remain open.
practical_advice: In conflicts or disputes, actively look for openings toward peaceful resolution rather than waiting for the other side to make every move.
Strength and firmness need not exclude mercy and diplomacy — the combination is often more effective than either alone.
When you find yourself in a position of advantage, use it to secure justice and safety, not to humiliate or destroy.

title: The Dispatch of Khalid b. al-Walid to Ukaydir Duma
summary: From Tabuk, the Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched Khalid b. al-Walid on a mission to Ukaydir of Duma, extending the reach of his diplomatic and military strategy.
story: The Dispatch by the Messenger of God (SAAS) of Khalid b. al-Walid to Ukaydir Duma.
lessons: Effective leadership involves delegating to those whose strengths match the task at hand.
Strategic thinking requires extending one's vision beyond the immediate situation to address wider concerns.
Trust placed in capable hands, guided by sincere intention, yields results that surpass what any single individual could achieve.
practical_advice: Identify the strengths of those around you and delegate responsibilities that match their capabilities rather than trying to manage everything yourself.
When overwhelmed by multiple demands, prioritize by asking which tasks require your direct attention and which can be entrusted to others.
Develop the habit of thinking several steps ahead, addressing potential challenges before they become crises.

title: The Story of the Al-Dirar Mosque
summary: An account of the mosque built with hypocritical intentions, known as the Mosque of Harm (Al-Dirar), which the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered destroyed upon recognizing its true purpose.
story: AN ACCOUNT OF THE AL-DIRAR MOSQUE.
lessons: Outward religious form without sincere intention can become a vehicle for harm and division.
Discernment — the ability to see beyond appearances to true intentions — is essential for wise leadership.
A community must guard itself against those who use the language of religion to sow discord and undermine unity.
practical_advice: Regularly examine your own motivations for the religious or community acts you perform — are they genuinely for God and good, or for other purposes?
When something feels wrong despite appearing righteous on the surface, do not dismiss that feeling — investigate with wisdom.
Be cautious of initiatives framed in religious or community language that seem designed to divide rather than unite.

title: The Hadith of Ka'b b. Malik — Remaining Behind from Tabuk
summary: Ka'b b. Malik's profound account of his failure to join the Tabuk expedition, his honest admission, the social isolation he endured, and his eventual forgiveness by God, as recorded in al-Bukhari.
story: The Hadith of Ka'b b. Malik, may God be pleased with him, as quoted by al-Bukhari, may God have mercy upon him. Reference to disobedient persons other than those who had remained behind.
lessons: Honest admission of fault, even when it is terrifying, is the beginning of genuine repentance and healing.
Social isolation and the withdrawal of community support can be among the most painful human experiences, yet they can also be purifying.
God's forgiveness, when it arrives, is complete and transformative — it does not merely pardon but restores dignity.
practical_advice: When you have made a mistake, resist the urge to construct elaborate justifications. Honest acknowledgment, though painful, opens the door to genuine resolution.
If you are going through a period where relationships feel cold or distant, use the solitude to examine yourself sincerely rather than distracting yourself from the discomfort.
Remember that feelings of guilt, when responded to with honesty and turning toward God, are not signs of weakness but of a conscience that is alive and working.

title: Events After the Return from Tabuk — The Arrival of the Delegation from Thaqif
summary: After the Prophet's (SAAS) return from Tabuk to Medina, a delegation from the tribe of Thaqif arrived in Ramadan of 9 AH and accepted Islam.
story: AN ACCOUNT OF THE EVENTS AFTER THE RETURN OF THE MESSENGER OF GOD (SAAS) FROM TABUK TO MEDINA. The Arrival of a Delegation from Thaqif to the Messenger of God (SAAS) in Ramadan of 9 AH.
lessons: People come to the truth at different times and through different paths — patience with others' journeys is essential.
The return from one trial often brings new openings and opportunities for good.
The growth of a community of faith happens gradually, through countless individual decisions to seek and accept the truth.
practical_advice: After completing a difficult undertaking, resist the urge to collapse into passive rest alone — remain open to the new opportunities and responsibilities that follow.
Be patient with those who are slower to change or accept what you believe to be true; your steadiness and good example may be what eventually reaches them.
Ramadan and other sacred seasons can be powerful moments for reflection and transformation — use them intentionally.

title: The Death of Abdullah b. Ubayy, the Chief Hypocrite
summary: An account of the death of Abdullah b. Ubayy, the leader of the hypocrites in Medina, and the Prophet's (SAAS) response to his passing, including the revelation that followed.
story: AN ACCOUNT OF THE DEATH OF 'ABD ALLAH B. UBAYY, MAY GOD FIND HIM REPULSIVE!
lessons: Not all those who claim membership in a community of faith are sincere — wisdom requires discernment without becoming cynical toward everyone.
The Prophet (SAAS) continued to act with dignity and compassion even toward those who had worked against him, demonstrating that principled conduct does not depend on the other's merit.
Divine revelation can clarify boundaries that human compassion alone finds difficult to navigate.
practical_advice: When dealing with people who have been insincere or harmful, maintain your own integrity and compassion without naivety — you can be both principled and aware.
Do not let the existence of insincerity in others harden your heart against genuine connection and trust.
Seek clarity from your values and faith when you are unsure how to respond to someone who has wronged you — the answer is rarely to simply match their energy.

title: Abu Bakr Leads the Pilgrimage in 9 AH and the Revelation of Surah Tawba
summary: The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched Abu Bakr to lead the pilgrimage in 9 AH, after which the revelation of Surah Bara'a (al-Tawba) was sent down, establishing new boundaries with the polytheists.
story: The Dispatch by the Messenger of God (SAAS) of Abu Bakr, 'The Trusting', in command of the Pilgrimage in the year 9 AH and the revelation of Surat Bara'a (i.e. al-Tawba; IX).
lessons: Trusting capable, faithful people with significant responsibilities is itself an act of wisdom and confidence in God's provision.
Divine guidance sometimes arrives to resolve situations that human judgment alone cannot fully navigate.
Major transitions — including the end of old covenants — require clarity, courage, and public proclamation.
practical_advice: Identify the most trustworthy people in your life and consciously invest in those relationships — they will be the ones you can rely on in critical moments.
When facing a major transition or ending in your life, seek clarity about what must be left behind and what must be carried forward.
Approach announcements of significant change with transparency and courage — ambiguity tends to breed anxiety, while clarity, even when difficult, enables resolution.

title: The Delegation from Banu Abd al-Qays
summary: A delegation from Banu Abd al-Qays came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), accepted Islam, and received his guidance.
story: The Delegation from Banu 'Abd al-Qays.
lessons: Seeking knowledge and guidance from those who possess it is a mark of wisdom and intellectual humility.
The willingness to travel and make effort in pursuit of truth is itself a form of devotion.
Every individual and community that comes sincerely to the truth deserves full engagement and welcome.
practical_advice: Make the effort to seek out genuine sources of knowledge and wisdom, even when it requires significant investment of time or energy.
Welcome those who come to you sincerely seeking guidance — your reception of them may determine whether they continue their journey.
Celebrate the moments when communities or individuals make significant positive choices, however small they may seem from the outside.

title: The Story of Thumama and the Delegation from Banu Hanifa, Including Musaylima the Impostor
summary: The delegation from Banu Hanifa included Musaylima, who later falsely claimed prophethood, and the story of Thumama, whose remarkable transformation after capture became one of the early testimonies to the power of dignified treatment.
story: The Story of Thumama and the delegation from Banu Hanifa, among whom was Musaylima, 'The Impostor', may God damn him!
lessons: Dignified, compassionate treatment of even an adversary can transform a hostile heart in ways that force never could.
False claims to spiritual authority are one of the most destructive forms of deception a community can face.
Not every person who enters a community of faith does so with sincere intention — and such individuals can cause lasting harm.
practical_advice: Treat people with dignity even when they have been hostile — you never know which act of compassion will be the one that reaches them.
Be discerning about those who claim spiritual authority or insight; test their claims against consistency, character, and the wellbeing they produce in others.
When someone in your community makes claims that seem self-serving or divisive, address them clearly and calmly rather than ignoring the danger they pose.

title: The Delegation from the People of Najran
summary: A delegation from the Christian community of Najran came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) for dialogue about faith and established terms of coexistence.
story: The Delegation from the people of Najran.
lessons: Genuine dialogue between communities of different faiths is possible and necessary for peaceful coexistence.
Truth can be presented with patience and clarity without requiring coercion or hostility.
Agreements of coexistence grounded in mutual respect and defined terms have been part of Islamic governance from the very beginning.
practical_advice: When engaging with people of different beliefs, lead with curiosity and respect rather than defensiveness or aggression.
Seek to understand what the other person genuinely believes before attempting to persuade or correct.
Clear, fair agreements between parties with different worldviews are the foundation of peaceful community life — invest in making and honoring them.

title: The Delegation of Banu Amir and the Story of Amir b. al-Tufayl and Arbad b. Qays
summary: The delegation from Banu Amir included Amir b. al-Tufayl and Arbad b. Qays, both of whom plotted against the Prophet (SAAS) and met their ends as a consequence of their treachery.
story: The Delegation of Banu 'Amir and the story of 'Amir b. al-Tufayl and Arbad b. Qays, may God damn them both!
lessons: Those who plot treachery against the righteous ultimately encounter consequences that their own cunning cannot prevent.
Arrogance and pride can blind a person to the truth standing directly before them.
The protection of sincere people and communities is ultimately in God's hands, not only in human strategies.
practical_advice: When you sense that someone is engaging with you in bad faith, trust your discernment and take appropriate precautions rather than dismissing the feeling.
Do not allow your desire to bring others to good to make you naive about their actual intentions.
Place your ultimate trust in God's protection while taking all reasonable and wise precautions in difficult situations.

title: The Arrival of Dimam b. Tha'laba on Behalf of Banu Sa'd b. Bakr
summary: Dimam b. Tha'laba arrived as representative of Banu Sa'd b. Bakr, asked the Prophet (SAAS) direct and unambiguous questions about Islam, and returned to his tribe as a sincere Muslim.
story: The Arrival of Dimam b. Tha'laba to see the Messenger of God (SAAS), on behalf of his tribe of Banu Sa'd b. Bakr.
lessons: Asking direct, sincere questions — even bold ones — is a sign of a truth-seeking mind.
One sincere person returning to their community with genuine knowledge can transform the entire community.
The simplicity and directness of Islam's foundational message is itself a form of its power.
practical_advice: Do not be afraid to ask the direct, foundational questions about what you believe and why — vagueness in your own convictions leaves you vulnerable to confusion.
When you gain genuine insight or knowledge, share it with your community rather than keeping it private.
Approach important decisions with the same directness that Dimam showed — get to the essential questions quickly rather than circling indefinitely.

title: The Delegation of Ta'if with Zayd of the Horses
summary: A delegation from Ta'if arrived, including the companion known as Zayd of the Horses, and the community entered into the fold of Islam.
story: The Delegation of Ta'if with 'Zayd of the Horses' may God be pleased with him.
lessons: Even communities that previously resisted or rejected the truth can come around when the time is right.
Persistence and patience in presenting the truth ultimately bear fruit, though the timeline is in God's hands.
Every person who comes sincerely to faith brings with them gifts, histories, and strengths that enrich the community.
practical_advice: Do not give up on people or communities who seem resistant to change — circumstances shift, and what was impossible yesterday may become possible tomorrow.
When someone who previously opposed you comes around sincerely, receive them with full generosity and without dwelling on the past.
Celebrate the moments when long-hoped-for change finally arrives — gratitude for such moments renews your energy for continued effort.

title: The Story of Adi b. Hatim al-Ta'i
summary: Adi b. Hatim al-Ta'i, son of the famous generous Arab chieftain Hatim al-Ta'i, came to the Prophet (SAAS) and embraced Islam after a journey of doubt, inquiry, and ultimately conviction.
story: The Story of 'Adi b. Hatim al-Ta'i.
lessons: An honest intellectual journey, even one that begins in doubt and resistance, can lead to genuine and deep conviction.
The prestige of family and tradition, while meaningful, should not be the ultimate arbiter of what one believes to be true.
The Prophet (SAAS) engaged inquirers with patience, intelligence, and a willingness to address their real concerns.
practical_advice: If you are in a period of genuine doubt or questioning, stay in the inquiry rather than resolving it prematurely in either direction.
Seek out conversations with those who can engage your real questions thoughtfully rather than dismissing or overwhelming you.
Be willing to follow the truth wherever it leads, even if it means departing from what is familiar and comfortable.

title: The Arrival of Tamim al-Dari and the Account of Al-Jassasa and Al-Dajjal
summary: Tamim al-Dari came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and related a remarkable account involving a mysterious creature called al-Jassasa and the Dajjal (the Deceiver), which the Prophet (SAAS) shared with the community.
story: The Arrival of Tamim al-Dari to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and his informing him about al-Jassasa and about what he heard from al-Dajjal, 'The Imposter,' concerning the mission of the Prophet and the faith of those believing in him.
lessons: There are dimensions of reality and future events that transcend ordinary human experience, and humility before the unseen is a mark of wisdom.
When a person of established integrity brings remarkable information, it deserves serious consideration rather than dismissal.
Awareness of future trials, including the trials of deception, strengthens the community's ability to face them with clarity.
practical_advice: Cultivate a grounded awareness of the possibility of deception in the world — this is not paranoia but wisdom that keeps you discerning.
When confronted with information that is extraordinary or difficult to verify, apply both open-mindedness and careful evaluation.
Use reminders of ultimate accountability and cosmic significance to reorient yourself when daily life feels small or purposeless.

title: The Farewell Pilgrimage — Hajjat al-Wada — in 10 AH
summary: The Messenger of God (SAAS) performed his only complete pilgrimage from Medina in 10 AH, known as the Farewell Pilgrimage, accompanied by vast numbers of companions in what would be his final pilgrimage before his death.
story: BOOK ON THE HIJJAT AL-WADA', 'THE FAREWELL PILGRIMAGE', IN 10 AH. Chapter: Information on the fact that the Prophet (SAAS), made only one full pilgrimage from Medina, having previously departed for three 'umra, 'lesser', pilgrimages. Chapter: On the date of the departure of the Prophet (SAAS), from Medina on the Hijjat al-wada'. Chapter: A description of the departure of the Messenger of God (SAAS), from Medina to Mecca for the pilgrimage.
lessons: Some moments in life are singular and unrepeatable — being fully present to them is itself a form of gratitude.
Great endings carry within them the seeds of everything that was built — the Farewell Pilgrimage gathered the entire fruit of the Prophet's mission.
The knowledge that a significant chapter is closing can deepen one's appreciation for every moment remaining within it.
practical_advice: When you recognize that you are in a significant, perhaps unrepeatable moment, set aside distraction and be fully present.
Take time to mark and honor significant transitions in your life rather than letting them pass without acknowledgment.
Let awareness of endings inspire you to say what needs to be said and do what needs to be done, while you still have the opportunity.

title: The Recitation of the Talbiyya by the Messenger of God (SAAS)
summary: Accounts of how and where the Messenger of God (SAAS) recited the talbiyya — the sacred declaration of pilgrimage — and the differing scholarly views on the precise moment and location of this recitation.
story: AN ACCOUNT OF THE RECITATION OF THE TALBIYYA MADE BY THE MESSENGER OF GOD (SAAS). The Hadith of Jabir B. 'Abd Allah, may God be pleased with him, on the hijja of the Messenger of God (SAAS). AN ACCOUNT OF THOSE PLACES IN WHICH THE MESSENGER OF GOD (SAAS) PRAYED WHILE TRAVELLING FROM MEDINA TO MECCA ON HIS 'UMRA AND HIS HAJJ.
lessons: Sacred words and rituals carry power not only in their content but in the intention and awareness with which they are spoken.
Scholarly care in preserving the details of the Prophet's practice reflects the community's love for him and its commitment to following his example with precision.
The journey toward a sacred destination is itself sacred — the path and the arrival are both worthy of full presence.
practical_advice: Pay attention to the words you recite regularly in worship — their meaning, spoken with awareness, can transform routine into genuine connection.
On journeys of any kind — physical or spiritual — mark meaningful moments along the way rather than rushing only toward the destination.
When you encounter differences of scholarly opinion on matters of practice, approach them with curiosity rather than anxiety — they reflect the richness of the tradition.

title: The Entry of the Prophet (SAAS) into Mecca and His Circumambulation
summary: Detailed accounts of the Messenger of God's (SAAS) entry into Mecca during the Farewell Pilgrimage, including his circumambulation of the Ka'ba and his movement between al-Safa and al-Marwa.
story: Chapter: The entry of the Prophet (SAAS) into Mecca, may God the Almighty and Glorious, honour it well. Descriptions of the Circumambulations made by the Messenger of God (SAAS). AN ACCOUNT OF HOW THE MESSENGER OF GOD (SAAS) JOGGED AND MOVED AT A FAST PACE ON HIS CIRCUMAMBULATION AND THE IDTIBA', 'TUCKING UP ONE'S GOWN UNDER THE RIGHT ARM'. ACCOUNTS OF THE CIRCUMAMBULATION OF THE PROPHET (SAAS) BETWEEN AL-SAFA AND AL-MARWA.
lessons: Embodied worship — involving the full person, body and soul — is a distinctive and profound dimension of Islamic practice.
The details of how the Prophet (SAAS) performed worship were preserved with extraordinary care, enabling every subsequent generation to follow his example.
Returning to a sacred place after a long journey carries a particular emotional and spiritual power — gratitude, memory, and presence converge.
practical_advice: Engage in your acts of worship with your whole body and attention, not only your mind — the physical dimension of devotion anchors and deepens the spiritual.
When you return to a meaningful place after time away, allow yourself to feel fully the significance of that return rather than rushing through it.
Study the details of your worship practices so that they are performed with both correctness and genuine understanding of their meaning.

title: The Address at Mount Arafat and the Sublime Revelation
summary: The Messenger of God (SAAS) delivered his renowned address while standing at Mount Arafat during the Farewell Pilgrimage, and the sublime revelation of the completion of the religion was sent down at that honored station.
story: Chapter: Those parts of the prayer delivered by the Messenger of God (SAAS), while he stood upon Mt. 'Arafat that are recorded. AN ACCOUNT OF THE SUBLIME REVELATION AT THAT HONOURED STATION.
lessons: The completion of a great mission is an occasion for profound gratitude, not personal pride.
The words spoken at a moment of completion carry a particular weight — they summarize, they warn, they exhort, and they release.
The revelation that the religion had been completed was both a gift and an implicit farewell — carrying within it both joy and the quiet sadness of an ending.
practical_advice: When you reach a significant milestone after long effort, pause to express genuine gratitude before moving on to the next goal.
Use moments of completion and transition to clarify and communicate your most essential values and commitments to those around you.
Allow yourself to hold simultaneously the joy of completion and the grief of endings — they are not contradictory but are the twin faces of meaningful experience.

title: Foreword: The Interrelationship of Human Cultures and Civilizations
summary: The foreword of 'The Life of the Prophet Muhammad' describes the establishment of the Center for Muslim Contribution to Civilization and its Great Books of Islamic Civilization Project, aiming to make classical Islamic works accessible in English to both specialists and general readers.
story: THE interrelationship and interaction of human cultures and civilisations has made the contributions of each the common heritage of men in all ages and all places. Early Muslim scholars were able to communicate with their Western counterparts through contacts made during the Crusades; at Muslim universities and centres of learning in Muslim Spain (al-Andalus, or Andalusia) and Sicily to which many European students went for education; and at the universities and centres of learning in Europe itself (such as Salerno, Padua, Montpellier, Paris, and Oxford), where Islamic works were taught in Latin translations. Among the Muslim scholars well-known in the centres of learning throughout the world were al-Razi (Rhazes), Ibn Sina (Avicenna), Ibn Rushd (Averroes), al-KhwarizmI and Ibn Khaldun. Muslim scholars such as these and others produced original works in many fields. Many of them possessed encyclopaedic knowledge and distinguished themselves in many disparate fields of knowledge. In view of this, the Center for Muslim Contribution to Civilization was established in order to acquaint non-Muslims with the contributions Islam has given to human civilisation as a whole. The Great Books of Islamic Civilization Project attempts to cover the first 800 years of Islam, or what may be called Islam's Classical Period. This project aims at making available in English a wide selection of works representative of Islamic civilisation in all its diversity. It is made up of translations of original Arabic works that were produced in the formative centuries of Islam, and is meant to serve the needs of a potentially large readership. Not only the specialist and scholar, but the non-specialist with an interest in Islam and its cultural heritage will be able to benefit from the series. Together, the works should serve as a rich source for the study of the early periods of Islamic thought. In selecting the books for the series, the Center took into account all major areas of Islamic intellectual pursuit that could be represented. Thus the series includes works not only on better-known subjects such as law, theology, jurisprudence, history and politics, but also on subjects such as literature, medicine, astronomy, optics and geography. The specific criteria, used to select individual books, were these: that a book should give a faithful and comprehensive account of its field; and that it should be an authoritative source. The reader thus has at his disposal virtually a whole library of informative and enlightening works. Each book in the series has been translated by a qualified scholar and reviewed by another expert. While the style of one translation will naturally differ from another, the translators have endeavoured, to the extent it was possible, to make the works accessible to the common reader. As a rule, the use of footnotes has been kept to a minimum, though a more extensive use of them was necessitated in some cases. This series is presented in the hope that it will contribute to a greater understanding in the West of the cultural and intellectual heritage of Islam and will therefore provide an important means towards greater understanding of today's world. May God Help Us! Muhammad bin Hamad Al-Thani, Chairman of the Board of Trustees.
lessons: Knowledge and scholarship are a shared human heritage that transcend cultural and religious boundaries, reminding us that no one community has a monopoly on wisdom.

Dedicated effort to preserve and transmit knowledge across generations and languages is itself an act of service to humanity, reflecting the Islamic value of seeking and spreading beneficial knowledge.
Understanding others' contributions fosters respect and peaceful coexistence, which is itself a spiritual and moral goal.
practical_advice: When feeling disconnected or isolated, explore the intellectual and spiritual contributions of your own tradition to find a sense of pride, rootedness, and belonging.
Seek out translated or accessible versions of classical works from your tradition as a means of deepening your faith and emotional grounding.
Practice gratitude by reflecting on the scholars and individuals throughout history whose efforts have made your access to knowledge and faith possible today.

title: About the Great Books of Islamic Civilization Series
summary: The editors of the Great Books of Islamic Civilization series describe their methodology and objectives for translating foundational Islamic texts into English, aiming to bridge Islamic and Western intellectual traditions for a global readership.
story: THIS series of Arabic works, made available in English translation, represents an outstanding selection of important Islamic studies in a variety of fields of knowledge. The works selected for inclusion in this series meet specific criteria. They are recognized by Muslim scholars as being early and important in their fields, as works whose importance is broadly recognized by international scholars, and as having had a genuinely significant impact on the development of human culture. Readers will therefore see that this series includes a variety of works in the purely Islamic sciences, such as Qur'an, hadith, theology, prophetic traditions (sunna), and jurisprudence (fiqh). Also represented will be books by Muslim scientists on medicine, astronomy, geography, physics, chemistry, horticulture, and other fields. The work of translating these texts has been entrusted to a group of professors in the Islamic and Western worlds who are recognized authorities in their fields. It has been deemed appropriate, in order to ensure accuracy and fluency, that two persons, one with Arabic as his mother tongue and another with English as his mother tongue, should participate together in the translation and revision of each text. This series is distinguished from other similar intercultural projects by its distinctive objectives and methodology. These works will fill a genuine gap in the library of human thought. They will prove extremely useful to all those with an interest in Islamic culture, its interaction with Western thought, and its impact on culture throughout the world. They will, it is hoped, fulfil an important role in enhancing world understanding at a time when there is such evident and urgent need for the development of peaceful coexistence. This series is published by the Center for Muslim Contribution to Civilization, which serves as a research centre under the patronage of H.H. Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa al-Thani, Amir of Qatar. It is directed by a Board of Trustees chaired by H.E. Sheikh Muhammad bin Hamad al-Thani, the former Minister of Education of Qatar. The Board is comprised of a group of prominent scholars. These include H.E. Dr Abul-Wafa al-Taftazani, Deputy Rector of Cairo University, and Dr Yusuf al-Qaradhawi, Director of the Sira and Sunna Research Center. At its inception the Center was directed by the late Dr Muhammad Ibrahim Kazim, former Rector of Qatar University, who established its initial objectives. The Center was until recently directed by Dr Kamal Nagi, the Foreign Cultural Relations Advisor of the Ministry of Education of Qatar. He was assisted by a Board comprising a number of academicians of Qatar University, in addition to a consultative committee chaired by Dr Ezzeddin Ibrahim, former Rector of the University of the United Arab Emirates. A further committee acting on behalf of the Center has been the prominent university professors who act under the chairmanship of Dr Raji Rammuny, Professor of Arabic at the University of Michigan. This committee is charged with making known, in Europe and in America, the books selected for translation, and in selecting and enlisting.
lessons: Collaborative effort between people of different linguistic and cultural backgrounds can produce something greater than any individual could achieve alone, modeling the Islamic ethic of cooperation for the greater good.
Preserving and transmitting sacred and intellectual knowledge is a communal responsibility that honors those who came before and serves those who come after.
Scholarship conducted with integrity, peer review, and humility is a form of worship and service, reflecting the Prophetic tradition of doing all things with excellence (ihsan).
practical_advice: When overwhelmed by the breadth of your tradition, start small — pick one accessible, well-translated classical text and commit to reading it slowly and reflectively.
If you feel lost in your faith journey, reconnecting with the intellectual depth of Islamic scholarship can restore a sense of wonder, confidence, and direction.
Practice the spirit of collaborative learning by joining a study circle, reading group, or online community dedicated to exploring Islamic texts together.

title: Introduction to Hadith Methodology and Islamic Historical Scholarship
summary: An academic discussion of the methodology used by early Muslim scholars in evaluating Hadith literature, including debates between Western and Muslim scholars on the reliability of early Islamic historical sources.
story: practised by early Muslim scholars, with its close scrutiny of the isnitd and the mutun of prophetic traditions, has, in the main, not been discredited, or even questioned, by Muslim scholars. If anything, that methodology has today been given a new lease of life by scholars such as Na~ir ai-Din al-Albam, who, for example, regard the re-evaluation of the early sources as integral to what they call the Islamic renaissance (al-Nah4a al-Islamiyya). Such a renaissance, Albam argues, will fall far short of its goals, without a thoroughgoing purge of what remains of the spurious material that had crept into [ladith and sira works during the turbulent epoch of early Islamic history22 He thus set himself the task of appraising scholars and the Muslim laity alike to those traditions that were deemed spurious by the regimen of classical [ladith studies. His findings, which were first published under the title "al-A1;Iaduh al-J;)a'ifah wa al-Mawdil'ah" in a weekly column in the magazine al- Tamaddun al-Islami, now comprise a multi-volume work, appropriately titled Silsilah al-A[ladith al-l)a'ifah wa al-Mawdu'ah. 23 18. See Goldziher's chapter on the development of the law in Islam in Introduction to Islamic Law and Theology, (Princeton: Princeton University Press, 1981). 19. J. Schacht, The Origins ofMuhammadan Jurisprudence (Oxford University Press, 1959). 20. Crone, Slaves on Horses, 14-15. 21. W. G. Watt, Muhammad at Mecca (Oxford Univesity Press, 1953), xiii. 22. M. N. AI-Albani, Silsilah al-Al;tadith al-l)a)ifah wa al-Mawducah. Vol. I. Damascus?: Manshurat a1-Maktab a1-Islami, 1376 h. 23. Ibid., 6.
lessons: Preserving the integrity of religious knowledge requires ongoing scholarly effort and rigorous critical evaluation.
A true renaissance of any community begins with purifying its foundational sources from distortion and error.
Intellectual humility and honest self-assessment are signs of a mature and sincere scholarly tradition.
practical_advice: When seeking knowledge, trace information back to its sources and evaluate the credibility of those who transmitted it.
Do not be discouraged by criticism of your tradition; engage with it thoughtfully and use it as motivation to strengthen your understanding.
Invest time in learning the methodology behind religious knowledge, not just the content, so you can distinguish reliable guidance from unreliable claims.

title: The Debate Between Western and Muslim Scholars on the Reliability of Hadith
summary: Scholars such as Azami defend the richness of Hadith literature as a historical source, while Western academics like Crone and Schacht challenge its reliability, with figures like Juynboll seeking a middle ground.
story: Oearly not all contemporary scholars are as eager as Schacht et al. to ring the death knell on /ladith literature as a tool for unravelling early Islamic history. Azarni for one, in his studies on early /ladith literature has attempted to show that /ladith literature is indeed the richest source for the investigation of that era, for it provides, among other things, material for the understanding of the legal, cultural and religious ideas of those early centuries. He maintains that the theories of Margoliouth, Goldziher and more recently, Schacht can no longer be incontestably accepted given the recent discoveries of manuscripts or research. According to him: "In the period referred to, works on the biography of the Prophet and on other historical topics were in a very advanced stage. We find that work on the biography of the Prophet was begun by the Companions. cAbd Allah b. cAmr b. aI-cAs recorded many historical events. It is possible still to trace his work in the a/ladtth narrated by cAmr b. ShnCayb (d. llS AH) as he utilized his great grandfather cAbd Allah b. cAmr's books. Vrwah (d. 93 AH) in his biography of the Prophet names his authority and most probably he had obtained the information in writing. There are works mentioned here and there on a single topic of the Sirah, e.g. Memorandum on the Servants of the Prophet, a book on the ambassadors of the Prophet to different rulers and chieftains with their negotiations. There are references to the collections ofthe Prophet's letters in a very early period."Z4 But it is, in fact, these very sources that Azami cites that have, through the use of contemporary literary and hermeneutical tools, been relegated to no more than "the rubble of early Muslirnhistory". For Patricia Crone therefore, the "inertia" of material such as appears heretofore "comes across very strongly in modem scholarship on the first two centuries ofIslam." 25 "The bulk of it", she argues, "has an alarming tendency to degenerate into mere rearrangements of the same old canon - Muslim chronicles in modem languages and graced with modern titles." 26 Others, such as Juynboll, have strived to arrive at the inevitable solution intermediaire, "a conceivable position that could be taken between the two points of .view represented respectively by Muslim and Western scholarship."27 For him therefore, the /ladith traditions "taken as a whole" do provide a fairly reliable rendition of early Islamic history, and "a judiciously and cautiously formulated overall view of what all those early reports ... collectively point to, may in all likelihood be taken to be not very far from the truth of 'what really happened'."28 24. Azami, Early Hadith, 7--8. 25. See in this regard the introduction to her work, Slaves on Horses: The Evolution of the Islamic Polity. (Cambridge University Press, 1980). 26. Ibid., 13. 27. See G. H. A. Juynboll, Muslim Tradition: Studies in chronology, provenance and authorship of early hadith. (Cambridge University Press, 1983), 1. 28. Ibid., 7.
lessons: Disagreement among scholars is a natural part of the search for truth, and engaging with different perspectives strengthens one's own understanding.
The early Muslim Companions took great care to document and preserve the Prophet's life, reflecting their deep love and sense of responsibility.
Seeking a balanced and evidence-based position in complex debates reflects intellectual maturity and fairness.
practical_advice: When confronted with doubt or criticism about your beliefs, research the topic carefully before drawing conclusions.
Learn to hold complexity — not every question has a simple answer, and sitting with nuance is a sign of intellectual growth.
Appreciate the scholars who dedicated their lives to preserving and transmitting knowledge, and honor their work by seeking it sincerely.

title: The Translator's Preface: Challenges of Rendering Ancient Arabic into Modern English
summary: Translator Trevor Le Gassick reflects on the enormous challenge of translating the ancient Arabic Seerah text into contemporary English while preserving its meaning, authenticity, and scholarly integrity.
story: As has often been observed, translation is impossible, since the associations and emotive content of words in one language and culture differ from those of all others. Attempts at translation, therefore, inevitably represent strivings for compromise. While accuracy and precision are prime objectives, the ultimately necessary requirements for clarity and comprehension in the host language may require simplification or even omission from the original text. The dilemmas inherent in these conflicting objectives are at times irreconcilable, and this is particularly true when one is dealing, as here, with languages and cultures so far removed as ancient Arabic and modern English. This translation, composed in everyday, contemporary English, gives no impression of the ubiquitous rarities, oddities and archaisms of vocabulary and syntax that make the original extremely challenging to comprehend. It is hoped, of course, that the innumerable compromises that this translation represents will be accepted as good-faith attempts to convey the spirit and purpose of the original in a form that readers of English will not find impossibly daunting. In some instances Ibn Kathlr repeats anecdotal al;adith with differing chains of authority that are almost identical in content; often, as will be seen, the accounts differ in only very few of their words and these are typically vocabulary rarities. While such variations between accounts may seem of scant interest to the Western reader, they have nevertheless been left complete and intact in this translation. Including them in full, as in the original work, gives a strong impression of the care with which these anecdotes have been handed down and the impression of their likely authenticity is therefore enhanced. This seems especially the case where the discrepancies involve vocabulary rarities that are synonymous. It seems that it would be just such words that would have been subjected to dispute, change or loss from memory. Ibn Kathlr's objective was to appear authoritative and discriminating in his choices of inclusion and discussion of specific al;iidith; to him the listing of all the names of his authorities and his comments on their reputations was an essential component of this lectures. The give-and-take of oral lecturing - of which this work is essentially a record - would have enabled immediate verbal clarification. Our English text, in contrast, has to stand by itself, and to present an inherent and visible logic and clarity; it must also give some impression of the reliability of the Arabic text that is indicated by its complexity, and by the care with which the names of quoted sources are given and at times evaluated. A perpetual challenge in presenting this text has therefore been to leave the essential narratives clear and succinct while including yet simplifying the lines of authority on which their authenticity is based. The names of authorities quoted have been included in full, since their identities were of prime importance for the initial 'readership' of this work as well as to students and researchers today. However, the exact nature and relative value of the means of transmission from authority to authority and the suggestions implied ofIbn Kathlr's preference for certain sources over others, have not been conveyed with exactitude, since com- mon English vocabulary is unable to convey some of the subtleties ofthe Arabic technical terms employed for this purpose. The essential completeness of the original text in this translation does, however, enable serious students of early Islamic materials to bring their own differentiation to·bear by their knowledge of the reputations of the persons quoted. Certain words common in this text - such as Abu and surat - change in their form in Arabic to accord with basic grammatical rules. Here, however, to avoid confusion for those readers who do not know Arabic, they have been left in the form in which they are most commonly met. Initial hamza, moreover, has been omitted. Since early Arabic manuscripts, like the Arabic printed version of this text, are devoid of quotation marks, the identity of the narrator is sometimes unclear. Similarly, it is occasionally difficult to discern whether comments at the end of an account are those of the transmitting authority or of Ibn Kathlr himself. Footnotes referring to these and similar textual difficulties have been kept to a minimum, while brief parenthetical explanatory comments have sometimes been inserted to aid the general reader. Discriminating and knowledgeable readers and reviewers will no doubt find discrepancies and perhaps inaccuracies in this lengthy and demanding text, especially in the extensive poems quoted. For these the translator - and his reviewers, text editors and typesetters - apologize. But since this work offers intimate details not elsewhere available in English about Arabian history and the inspiration and leadership ofIslam in its earliest formative period, it would seem unsatisfactoty to leave it in a language and form accessible only to a small coterie of scholars. The evident religious historical and philosophical interest of this text suggests that all those associated with its production may properly take refuge and find consolation from criticism in the knowledge that 'to err is human'. To attempt the impossible, moreover, while perhaps foolhardy, is surely more laudable than to make no attempt at all. Trevor Le Gassick Ann Arbor, 2000
lessons: Attempting a great and difficult task sincerely, even imperfectly, is more honorable than never trying at all.
Humility before the weight of a task — and before those who came before us — is a mark of genuine scholarship and devotion.
Making sacred knowledge accessible to more people is itself an act of service, even when the work involves inevitable compromise.
practical_advice: When facing an overwhelming task, focus on your sincere intention and do your best rather than waiting for perfect conditions.
Accept that imperfection is part of every human effort, and let that reality free you to begin rather than paralyze you.
Be grateful to those who worked hard to make knowledge available to you in your own language and context, and honor their effort by engaging seriously with what they produced.

title: The Quranic Decree Barring Polytheists from the Sacred Mosque and the Command to Battle the Byzantines
summary: God decreed that polytheists be barred from the Sacred Mosque, and when the Quraysh feared financial loss from this, God compensated them by commanding them to battle the People of the Scripture — leading the Prophet to resolve to march against the Byzantines.
story: God Almighty stated, "0 you who believe, the polytheists are nothing but unclean; and so they shall not come near the Sacred Mosque after this year. If you should fear poverty, (then remember that) God will enrich you from His bounty, if He so wishes. God is Omniscient, Wise. Battle against those who do not believe in God or in the Last Day, who do not prohibit what God and His Messenger prohibited and who do not follow the religion of the truth. (Battle against) those to whom the (Holy) Book was brought until they pay the jizya tax 'an yadin/ 'being in a state of submission'" (siirat al- Tawba, (or al-Bara'a); IX, v.28-29}. It is related from Ibn 'Abbas, Mujahid, qkrima, Sa'ld b. ]ubayr, Q;!tada, al-J;)al;il;tak and others that when God Almighty decreed that the polytheists sl)ould be prevented from approaching the Sacred Mosque, whether in the pilgrimage or at other times, that Quraysh said they would be deprived of the commercial activity that took place during the pilgrimage, and that they would therefore suffer financial loss. And so God compensated them for that by ordering them to battle the people of the scriptures so that they either accepted Islam or paid the jizya tax (an yadin, "being in a state of submission". I comment that the Messenger of God (SAAS) therefore decided to battle the Byzantines. This was because they were the people nearest to him and those most appropriate to invite to the truth because of their proximity to
lessons: When God closes one door, He opens another — fear of loss must be replaced with trust in divine provision.
Every divine command, even one that appears difficult, carries within it wisdom and a form of compensation for those who obey.
The Prophet's resolve to face the greatest power of his time demonstrates that true leadership requires courage in the face of overwhelming odds.
practical_advice: When you face the fear of financial loss or material hardship, remind yourself that God is the ultimate provider and that trust in Him is itself a form of wealth.
Do not let fear of difficulty cause you to abandon what is right; face your challenges with the resolve modeled by the Prophet.
When you feel that circumstances are working against you, look for the alternative path or provision that God may be opening — it is often not where you expected.

title: The Decision to March on the Byzantines: The Expedition of Tabuk
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) decides to lead a large expedition against the Byzantines during a time of extreme heat, poverty, and drought, openly explaining the purpose of the campaign unlike his usual practice of concealing military objectives.
story: times, that Quraysh said they would be deprived of the commercial activity that took place during the pilgrimage, and that they would therefore suffer financial loss. And so God compensated them for that by ordering them to battle the people of the scriptures so that they either accepted Islam or paid the jizya tax (an yadin, "being in a state of submission". I comment that the Messenger of God (SAAS) therefore decided to battle the Byzantines. This was because they were the people nearest to him and those most appropriate to invite to the truth because of their proximity to Islam and to those who believed in it. God Almighty had stated, "0 you who believe, fight those unbelievers who are near you. Let them see severity in you; and know that God is with those who are pious" (siirat al-Tawba (or al-Bara'a); IX, v.123}. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) determined to send an expedition against the Byzantines - this being the time of the battle of Tabfik - it was at a period of extreme heat and discomfort. He explained the expedition to people and invited the bedouin tribes around him to go forth with him and a large force of, as we shall see, some 30,000 men did join him. Others, however, held back, and God criticized those who were hypocritical and neglectful. He expressed His extreme displeasure and censure to them and brought them great disgrace, giving revelation of verses for recitation about them. It was in siirat al-Tawba IX (or al-Bara'a) that He made clear reference to them; we explained this at length in our Taft;r (Exegesis). He ordered the Muslims to go forth no matter how, saying, "Proceed forth, whether light or heavy, and put your possessions and your persons vigorously to work in God's cause. That is best for you, if you but know it. Had it been an objective close by and a short journey, they would have followed you. But the long journey was too far for them. (Yet) they will swear by God, 'Had we been able, we would have departed with you!' They destroy themselves; God knows that they are liars!" (surat al- Tawba; IX, v.41, 2) and other verses follow these. The Almighty went on to state, "It was not for the believers to proceed forth all together. Why then should not a group from every party among them go forth to seek expertise in the faith and to warn their people when they return to them, so that they may be on their guard?" (surat al-Tawba: IX, v.122). It is said by some that this verse abrogates the fonner; others say not. God knows best. Ibn Isl:Iaq stated, "Thereafter the Messenger of God (SAAS) remained In Medina between the months of Dhu al-I:Iijja and Rajab - of year 9 AH that is - and then ordered the men to prepare for an expedition against the Byzantines. "Al-Zuhri, Yazid b. Ruman, 'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr, 'A~im b. 'Vmar b. Q,ttada and others of our scholars relate as follows of what was told them concerning the expedition to Tabuk; some give information not presented by others. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered his men to make an expedition against the Byzantines. This was at a time when there was considerable suffering from poverty, extreme heat and drought. The fruit was now ripe and people wanted to enjoy it in the shade; they hated the idea of leaving right away at that season. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) rarely went forth on an expedition without disguising its purpose. The expedition to Tabuk, however, he explained fully because of the great distance, the severity of the season and the large size of the enemy force he intended to battle; he gave this explanation so that his men could make proper preparations. He ordered them to ready their equipment, telling them he was going to attack the Byzantines."
lessons: When facing a difficult and demanding task, transparency and honest communication about its challenges is itself a form of leadership and respect for others.
True commitment to a cause is tested most severely when conditions are uncomfortable and the cost is high — ease never reveals the depth of one's faith or character.
God does not demand the impossible; He acknowledges hardship while still calling believers to rise to their responsibilities as best they can.
practical_advice: When you are facing a daunting obligation, resist the urge to downplay the difficulty to yourself or others — acknowledge how hard it is while still taking the first step forward.
In times of collective stress, look to the example of those who served with full commitment despite personal hardship, and let their resolve strengthen your own resolve.

If you feel overwhelmed by the scale of what is being asked of you, break the preparation into smaller steps, as the Prophet did by giving his companions time and clear instruction to prepare properly.

title: Jadd ibn Qays Makes an Excuse to Avoid the Expedition
summary: A man named Jadd ibn Qays uses the excuse of his weakness for women to avoid joining the Tabuk expedition, and God reveals a Qur'anic verse exposing his hypocrisy.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS), while readying his own equipment, asked Jadd b. Q,tys, a man of Banu Salama, 'Jadd, would you like now to attack the banu agar, the "yellow skins"?' He replied, 'Messenger of God, do excuse me and not tempt me; I swear by God, my people well know there's not a man more attracted to women than myself. I fear that if I saw the banu agar women, I'd not be able to restrain myself!' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) turned away from him, saying, 'I excuse you then'. "It was about Jadd that God revealed the verse, 'Among them are those who will say, "Do not tempt me!". But they have already fallen to temptation. Hell encompasses those who disbelieve' (surat al-Tawba; IX, v.29)."
lessons: Rationalizing avoidance of our responsibilities with seemingly reasonable excuses does not change the reality of our choices — and we are ultimately accountable for those choices.
Self-deception is among the most spiritually dangerous conditions; the person who deceives themselves about their own motivations closes the door to growth and sincere repentance.
Accountability before God is not based on the excuses we present to others but on the true state of our hearts and intentions.
practical_advice: When you notice yourself constructing elaborate justifications for avoiding a difficult duty, pause and ask honestly whether the excuse reflects a real limitation or simply a reluctance to step up.
Practice recognizing the difference between a genuine constraint and a convenient story — journaling about your true motivations in difficult decisions can bring surprising clarity.
If you have avoided a responsibility using an excuse you know was not fully honest, acknowledge it privately and recommit to acting with more integrity the next time a similar challenge arises.

title: The Hypocrites Discourage the Army at Suwaylim's House
summary: A group of hypocrites gather at the house of Suwaylim the Jew to discourage Muslims from joining the Tabuk expedition, prompting the Prophet to send men to burn down the house.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was informed that some hypocrites were meeting in the home of Suwaylim the Jew - his dwelling being at Jasum - and that they were dissuading people from accompanying the Messenger of God (SAAS) on his expedition to Tabuk. The latter then dispatched Tal1;la b. 'Ubayd Allah and some men with orders to burn down Suwaylim's house about them. Tal1;la did this. Al-J;>al;J.l;J.ak b. Khabfa jumped down from the roof of the house, breaking his leg. His companions leaped down too, but escaped. Al-J;>al;J.l;J.ak spoke the following verses about this: 'By God's house, Mul)ammad's fire almost burned al-Oa!)!):tak and Ibn Ubayriq! Having climbed atop Suwaylim's house, I crawled away on a broken leg and an elbow. Greetings to you! I'll not do the same again. I am afraid; he whom fire encompasses is burned.'"
lessons: Those who actively work to undermine the collective good of their community bear a heavier moral responsibility than those who simply fail to contribute.
There are natural consequences — in this world and the next — for those who use their influence to sow doubt and discourage others from righteous action.
A leader's responsibility sometimes includes taking firm corrective action against those whose negativity actively harms the community's resolve and welfare.
practical_advice: Be mindful of whose company you keep when facing a difficult commitment — surrounding yourself with people who discourage your best efforts can quietly erode your resolve before you even realize it.
If someone in your life consistently discourages you from doing what you know is right, create healthy distance from that influence and seek out companions who strengthen your commitment instead.
When you recognize that your own words are discouraging others from doing good, take that as a serious prompt for self-examination and course correction.

title: The Hypocrites Say 'Do Not March in the Heat' and God's Response
summary: The hypocrites urge one another not to go forth in the heat of summer to avoid the Tabuk expedition, and God reveals verses rebuking them and warning of a far greater heat — the fire of Hell.
story: "The hypocrites told one another, 'Don't go forth in the heat'. They did this to avoid the warfare and doubting the truth and discrediting the Messenger of God (SAAS). And so God revealed about them, 'They say, "Don't go forth in the heat!" Say: "The heat of the fires of hell is more severe!" If only they understood! Let them laugh a little and weep a lot for the punishment they have earned'" (surat al- Tawba; IX, v.81)."
lessons: Short-term comfort sought at the cost of long-term obligation is never truly a bargain — what we avoid in this life often returns to us in a far more demanding form.
The hypocrite's logic is always inward-looking, measuring cost only in terms of personal comfort; the believer's logic is outward and forward-looking, measuring cost in terms of ultimate consequence.
God's warnings in scripture are not expressions of anger alone but of deep care — a reminder that the minor discomforts of righteous action are nothing compared to the weight of neglect.
practical_advice: The next time discomfort tempts you to abandon a commitment, consciously compare the short-term discomfort of doing the right thing against the long-term weight of having not done it.
Notice when you are rationalizing avoidance by focusing on immediate hardship rather than ultimate purpose — reframe the situation by asking what your future self will be grateful you chose today.
Cultivate a practice of reflecting on the long-term consequences of your choices, not just their immediate costs, so that your decisions are guided by wisdom rather than momentary ease.

title: Uthman ibn Affan's Extraordinary Generosity in Equipping the Tabuk Army
summary: Uthman ibn Affan donates an extraordinary amount of wealth — including 1,000 dinars, hundreds of camels with full equipment — to fund the Tabuk expedition, earning the Prophet's heartfelt praise and God's forgiveness.
story: Ibn Isl;J.aq continued, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) made energetic preparations for his trip, ordering the men to get ready quickly. He urged the wealthy to provide funds and mounts for God's cause. Some of them did so and thus stored away (later reward) for themselves. 'Uthman b. 'Affan made a greater outlay than anyone had done." Ibn Hisham stated, "Someone I trust related to me that 'Uthman paid out 1,000 dinars for the army going on the al-'usYa expedition to Tabuk. The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, '0 God be pleased with 'Utbman, for I am pleased with him.'" Imam Al;J.mad stated that Harun b. Ma'rfif related to him, quoting J;>amra b. Shawdhab, from 'Abd Allah b. al-Q!isim, from Kaththa, the freed-men of 'Abd al-Ral;J.man b. Samra, who said, "'Uthrnan b. 'AfIan took 1,000 dinars to the Messenger of God (SAAS) in his clothing when the latter was preparing the army for the al-'usra expedition. He poured out the money into the lap of the Prophet (SAAS), who began turning the coins over in his hand, saying, 'Affan's son can do no wrong, after what he has done today!'" Al-Tirmidhl related this from Mu1;lammad b. Isma'll, from al-I;Iasan b. Waqi', from J;:>amra. He categorized it as basan gharib, "good but unilateral". 'Abd Allah b. A1;Imad stated, in his father's badith collection, "Abu MUsa al-'Anazi related to me, quoting 'Abd al-Samad b. 'Abd al-Warith, quoting Sakin b. al-Mughira, quoting al-Walid b. Abu Hisham, from Farqad Abu Tal1;la, from 'Abd al-Ral:tman b. Khabbab al-Sulami, who said, 'The Prophet (SAAS) made an address urging support for the army for the al-'usra expedition and 'Uthman b. 'Affan said, "I take responsibility for providing 100 baggage camels with their saddle blankets and pack-saddles." He (the Prophet (SAAS» then came down one step on the minbar, the pulpit, and again urged support. At this 'Uthman said, "I take responsibility for another 100 baggage camels complete with their blankets and pack-saddles."'" 'Abd al-Ra1;lman b. Khabbab al-Sulami went on, "I then saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) use his hand to speak with, moving it thus." And Abu Musa al-'Anazi stated that 'Abd al-Samad put out his hand and moved it as though in delight (and saying), "For what he has done, 'Uthman has no more responsibility hereafter." Al-Tirmidhi related this from Mu1;lammad b. Bashshar, from Abu Da'ud al-Tayalisi, from Sakan b. al-Mughira Abu Mu1;larnmad, the freed-man of 'Uthman's family. AI-Tirmidhi categorized the hadith as gharib minhadha al-wajh, "unique from this line". AI-Bayhaqi related it through 'Amd b. Marzuq, from Sakan b. al-Mughira. He said it happened three times and that 'Uthman took responsibility for providing 300 baggage camels with their blankets and saddles. 'Abd al-Ra1;lman stated, "I witnessed the Messenger of God (SAAS) say while he was upon the minbar, ''Uthman did no wrong after this'. Or he may have said, ... 'after today'." Abu Da'ud al-Tayalisi stated, "Abu 'Awana related to us, from I;Iu~ayn b. 'Abd al-Ra1;Iman, from 'Amr b. lawan, from al-A1;Inafb. Q;1ys, who said, 'I heard 'Uthman b. 'AfTan say to Sa'cl b. Abu Waqqa~, 'Ali, al-Zubayr and Tal1;la, "I adjure you by God to say whether you know that the Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'Whoever equipped the army for the al-'usra expedition has been forgiven by God', and that I equipped them so that they did not lack so much as a halter or a headband?" They responded, "Yes, indeed, by God!"''"
lessons: True generosity in times of communal need is among the most honoring acts a person of means can perform — it lifts not only the material burden but the moral spirit of an entire community.
When we give wholeheartedly in a moment of genuine need, we move beyond mere obligation into an expression of love and faith that leaves a lasting mark on both the giver and those who witness it.
The Prophet's public acknowledgment of Uthman's giving teaches that expressing heartfelt gratitude and recognition to those who give generously is itself a righteous and encouraging act.
practical_advice: When a cause you believe in is struggling for resources, ask yourself honestly what you have the capacity to give — and then consider whether you are giving at that level or holding back out of habit or hesitation.
Do not underestimate the ripple effect of your generosity in a moment of collective need; one person stepping forward wholeheartedly can inspire and embolden many others to do the same.

If someone in your life gives generously and sacrificially, make the effort to acknowledge it sincerely and specifically — recognition from someone whose opinion matters deeply is itself a form of nourishment.

title: Those Who Wept Because They Could Not Afford to Join the Expedition
summary: God reveals Qur'anic verses distinguishing between those wealthy hypocrites who made excuses to avoid the Tabuk expedition and those sincere believers who wept out of genuine grief because they lacked the means to participate.
story: God Almighty stated, "And when a chapter (of the Qur'an) is revealed saying, 'Believe in God and strive along with His Messenger', those among them of ample means seek your permission (to remain) saying, 'Leave us behind with those who remain.' They are pleased to be with those who stay back; their hearts have been sealed over, and they do not comprehend. However, the Messenger and those who believe with him dedicate fully their property and themselves. These persons shall have the benefits, and it is they who shall succeed. God has prepared for them gardens beneath which rivers flow; they shall rest therein for eternity. That is the great success. Then the excuse--makers from among the bedouin came seeking pennission (to stay behind) and those who denied the veracity of God and His Messenger also stayed. Those of them who disbelieved shall suffer painful punishment. There shall be no blame for the weak and the sick or those without the means to expend, provided that they are sincere with God and His Messenger; there is no cause to blame those who do good. And God is Forgiving and Beneficent. Nor is there blame upon those who, coming to you for mounts, have you tell them, 'I have nothing on which to transport you', with the result that they turn away, their eyes welling tears of regret that they do not have the means to expend. Cause for blame rests with those who seek your pennission to stay back even though they are wealthy, preferring to be with those who remain behind. God has placed a seal over their hearts, and they do not know" (surat al-Tawba; IX,v. 86-93)."
lessons: God distinguishes with great care between those who cannot contribute due to genuine incapacity and those who choose not to contribute despite having the means — intention and sincerity are at the very heart of accountability.
Sincere grief over one's inability to do good is itself a form of devotion — the tears of those who wanted to give but could not were honored and recorded by God.
Our worth before God is not measured by what we were able to accomplish but by the sincerity and wholeness of heart with which we wished to serve.
practical_advice: If you are going through a season in which your circumstances genuinely prevent you from contributing to something meaningful, do not allow that limitation to become a source of shame — sincerity of heart and longing to do good are themselves of real value.
Resist comparing your capacity to contribute with that of others who have more resources or opportunity; focus instead on giving fully and faithfully from what you actually have.

When you find yourself weeping over a good you wished you could do but could not, receive that grief as a sign of your sincerity rather than a mark of failure — and channel it into gratitude for whatever you are able to do.

title: The Weeping Seven: Those Who Could Not Afford to Join the Tabuk Expedition
summary: Seven companions came to the Prophet weeping because they lacked the means to join the Tabuk expedition. One companion, Ibn Yamin, gave them his own camel and provisions so they could participate.
story: Ibn Isḥaq stated, 'These totalled seven persons, consisting of anṣār and others. Of Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf there were: Salim b. 'Umayr; 'Ulba b. Zayd, brother of Banu Ḥāritha; Abu Layla 'Abd al-Raḥman b. Ka'b, brother of Banu Mazin b. al-Najjar; 'Amr b. al-Ḥumam b. al-Jumuḥ, brother of Banu Salama; 'Abd Allah b. al-Mughaffal al-Muzanī. Some maintain that this last-mentioned was 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr al-Muzanī. (They also include) Harami b. 'Abd Allah, brother of Banu Waqif and 'Irbaq b. Sariya al-Fazzārī. Ibn Isḥaq went on, 'I have been told that Ibn Yamīn b. 'Umayr b. Ka'b al-Naqrī met Abu Layla and 'Abd Allah b. Mughaffal who were both weeping. Ibn Yamīn asked them, 'What is making you weep?' They replied, 'We have been to the Messenger of God (SAAS) to provide us mounts but he had none to carry us. And we lack the means to proceed out with him.' So he (Ibn Yamīn) gave them a water-transport camel he owned and they saddled it. He also provided them with some dates and so they proceeded forth with the Prophet (SAAS).'
lessons: Genuine love for God's cause can move a person to tears when they are unable to contribute, reflecting sincerity of faith.
The generosity of fellow believers can be the means through which God fulfills the wishes of those who are sincere but lack resources.
Being unable to fulfil a religious duty due to genuine inability is not a failing — God judges by intention and effort, not outcome alone.
practical_advice: When you feel helpless in a situation you deeply care about, recognize that your sincere desire itself has value — channel that feeling into whatever small contribution you can make.
If you see someone grieving over an inability to do good, consider whether you have resources or means to help them fulfill their goal.
When overwhelmed by what you cannot do, focus on what you can offer — even a small act of generosity or support for others can transform your feelings of helplessness into purpose.

title: 'Ulba b. Zayd's Night Prayer and Charitable Donation
summary: 'Ulba b. Zayd, unable to join the Tabuk expedition due to lack of a mount, spent the night weeping in prayer and donated to all Muslims whatever wrongs had been done to him, and was praised by the Prophet the next morning.
story: Yūnus b. Bukayr added the following from Ibn Isḥaq, ''Ulba b. Zayd went outside at night, spending that part of it in prayer that God wished. He then wept and said, 'O God, You have ordered to battle and inspired enthusiasm for it, but then not provided me with the means for it, not given to Your Messenger a mount to transport me. I wish to donate to all Muslims (the compensation I received for) whatever wrongs may have befallen me, whether in money, in bodily harm or in honour.' 'Next morning he arose with the rest and the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, 'Where is he who spent the night making donation?' No one got up. He next said, 'Let the man who made the donation get up now.' He ('Ulba) then went over to him and told him and the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Rejoice! By Him in whose hand is my soul, you have been inscribed among those having made a zakat donation that is found acceptable (by God)!'''
lessons: When external action is impossible, turning inward through prayer and sincere worship can itself become a great act of devotion recognized by God.
Forgiving others and releasing grievances, even when done quietly and alone, is a form of charity that God sees and values.
The Prophet's recognition of 'Ulba's private night act shows that God is aware of every sincere deed done in secret, and no genuine effort goes unnoticed.
practical_advice: When you feel unable to contribute in the way you wish, turn your helplessness into prayer and private devotion — your sincerity in those moments holds great worth.
Consider practicing emotional generosity by mentally releasing grievances you hold against others; this act of letting go can bring inner peace and spiritual reward.
Remember that recognition does not always come immediately or from expected sources — trust that sincere private acts of goodness are never lost or forgotten.

title: Abu Musa al-Ash'ari Asks for Mounts and Is Initially Refused
summary: Abu Musa al-Ash'ari went to the Prophet on behalf of his companions to request mounts for the Tabuk expedition, was initially refused due to the Prophet's angry mood, but was later called back and given six camels — an experience that tested his trust and integrity.
story: At this point the ḥāfiẓ al-Bayhaqī gave the ḥadīth of Abu Musa al-Ash'arī, stating, 'Abu 'Abd Allah al-ḥāfiẓ related to us, quoting Abu al-'Abbas Muḥammad b. Ya'qub, quoting Aḥmad b. 'Abd al-Ḥamid al-Mazinī, quoting Abu Usāma, from Burayd, from Abu Burda, from Abu Musa, who said, 'My companions sent me to the Messenger of God (SAAS), to ask him for mounts for them to accompany him with the army for the al-'usra expedition to Tabūk. I said, "Prophet of God, my companions have sent me to you to provide them mounts." He replied, "By God, I won't mount you on anything." I had happened to go to him, without realizing it, when he was in an angry mood. 'So I left, sad that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had rejected me and fearful that he bore me some ill-will. When I returned to my friends, I told them what he had said. Very soon after that, I heard Bilal calling out, "Where is 'Abd Allah b. Qays?" When I responded, he said, "Make answer to the Messenger of God (SAAS) who is calling for you." When I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) he said, "Take these two camels tied together, and these two and these two." He was referring to six baggage camels he had just bought from Sa'd. Then he said, "Take these to your companions and say, 'God' (or 'the Messenger of God') 'will mount you upon these'." 'So (I went to them and) I said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) mounts you upon these. But, I swear by God, I'll not allow you this until some of you accompany me to someone who heard what the Messenger of God (SAAS) said when I asked him on your behalf and how he at first refused, then later gave them to me. I don't want you to think I reported to you something he did not say." They replied, "By God, we consider you entirely credible; but we'll do whatever you wish." 'Abu Musa then left with a group of them and they went to those who had heard what the Messenger of God (SAAS) said — how he had at first refused and then had given to them. They confirmed to them exactly what Abu Musa had said.' Both al-Bukhārī and Muslim gave this from Abu Kurayb, from Abu Usāma.
