In one account of theirs they both quote Abu Musa as saying, 'I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) along with a group of Ash'aris to provide us mounts and he replied, "I swear by God, I'll not give you mounts; I have none on which to transport you."' He went on, 'Some camels taken as booty were then brought to him, and he ordered that we be given six young ones. We took them, but then we said, "We caused the Messenger of God (SAAS) to break his oath, and God will not bless us." So we went back to him and told him this. He replied, "It is not I who gave you mounts; it is God who did so." He then said, "I swear by God, if God wills it, I will never swear an oath then find that its opposite is better without then nullifying that first oath."'
lessons: Initial rejection from those we trust and love can feel devastating, but patience and continued trust often reveal a greater good waiting just ahead.
Integrity in reporting what was truly said, even when it may reflect poorly, is a mark of trustworthy character that others instinctively respect.
The Prophet's statement that 'it is God who gave you mounts' is a reminder that provision and relief ultimately come from God, and human intermediaries are only instruments.
practical_advice: When you receive an unexpected refusal from someone you rely on, resist the urge to catastrophize — give the situation time and space before concluding the worst.
Practice honesty even when it is inconvenient; if you are delivering news or reporting an event, commit to accuracy over impression management.
When something you needed arrives after difficulty, pause to acknowledge gratitude rather than moving straight to relief — recognizing the source of blessings deepens trust in God.

title: The Three Men Left Behind and Those Who Lagged Without Doubt
summary: Several companions, including the honourable Ka'b b. Malik, Marara b. Rabi', and Hilal b. Umayya, lagged behind the Prophet during the Tabuk expedition through personal weakness rather than doubt, and God revealed verses about their inner anguish.
story: Ibn Isḥaq went on, 'There were a number of Muslims whom absence delayed and so they lagged behind the Messenger of God (SAAS), but not because of any doubt or indecision on their part. Among these were Ka'b b. Malik b. Abu Ka'b, a brother of Banū Salama; Marara b. Rabī', brother of Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf; Hilal b. Umayya, brother of Banū Waqif, and Abu Khaythama, brother of Banu Salim b. 'Awf. These were men of high honour against whose adherence to Islam no one could make any charge.' I note that the story of the first three of these men will be related at length shortly hereafter, if God Almighty wills it. It is these men about whom God gave the following revelation: 'As for the three who were left behind so that the earth became constricted for them, though it was spacious. And their spirits also felt constrained, and they thought there to be no refuge from God, except in Him' (surat al-Tawba; IX, v.118).
lessons: A temporary failure to act on one's good intentions does not necessarily reflect the depth of one's faith — God distinguishes between doubt and human weakness.
The Quranic description of the earth feeling constricted despite its spaciousness captures perfectly the inner experience of guilt and estrangement from one's values.
God's mercy encompasses those who stumble while their hearts remain attached to Him, as long as they do not flee from accountability.
practical_advice: When you fail to follow through on a commitment you genuinely care about, acknowledge it honestly rather than rationalising it away — accountability is the beginning of recovery.
If you feel spiritually constrained and as though no path forward is open to you, remember that turning back to God is always available, regardless of the failure.
Distinguish between doubt about your path and weakness in following it — the first needs reflection and clarity, the second needs compassion for yourself and renewed resolve.

title: The Departure from Madinah and 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy's Betrayal
summary: The Prophet departed for Tabuk with over 30,000 men, but the hypocrite 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy and a group of hypocrites and doubters stayed behind, undermining the expedition from within.
story: Yūnus b. Bukayr quoted Ibn Isḥaq as stating, 'Having made his arrangements, the Messenger of God (SAAS) decided to set off. When, on a Thursday, he departed, he made camp at Thaniyyat al-Wadā'; with him there were more than 30,000 men. That enemy of God 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy pitched his camp lower down, his being, so they say, no smaller in number. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) set forth again, 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy remained behind, along with a group of the hypocrites and doubt-mongers.' Ibn Hishām stated, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) placed Muḥammad b. Maslama, the anṣārī, in command of Medina. Al-Darawardi related that it was Sibā' b. 'Urfuta whom he left in command at the time of the expedition to Tabūk.'
lessons: Leadership requires wise delegation — the Prophet's appointment of a trusted person to guard Madinah reflects the importance of securing what is left behind when moving forward.
The presence of hypocrites and doubt-mongers within a community is a recurring trial that sincere believers must navigate with discernment rather than naivety.
A large visible show of strength or numbers does not always indicate genuine commitment — true solidarity is proven by action, not proximity.
practical_advice: When undertaking a major commitment or challenge, take time to ensure that what you are leaving behind — relationships, responsibilities, safeguards — is properly cared for.
Be aware that discouragement often comes from those closest to us who have chosen comfort over commitment; do not let their withdrawal weaken your own resolve.
Identify and build relationships with people of proven reliability who can hold things steady while you focus your energy on a primary goal.

title: Ali b. Abu Talib Left Behind to Guard the Prophet's Family
summary: The Prophet left Ali behind to care for his family during the Tabuk expedition; when hypocrites spread lies implying the Prophet found Ali burdensome, Ali rushed to confront this, and the Prophet reassured him with the famous comparison to Aaron and Moses.
story: Ibn Isḥaq went on, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) left 'Ali b. Abu Talib behind to care for his family, ordering him to stay with them. The hypocrites spread lies about 'Ali, maintaining that the Messenger of God (SAAS) found his presence onerous and wished to alleviate this. 'When they said this, 'Ali took up his weapons and proceeded forth, catching up with the Messenger of God (SAAS) while the latter was making a halt at al-Jurf. 'Ali told him what people were saying and he responded, "They lie; I left you there to care for those I have left behind. Go back and act on my behalf with my family as well as your own. Are you not content, 'Ali, to have the same status with me as Aaron had with Moses? There will, however, be no prophet after myself." 'Ali did return and the Messenger of God (SAAS) departed on his journey.' Ibn Isḥaq went on, 'Muḥammad b. Ṭalḥa b. Yazid b. Rukana related to me, from Ibrāhīm b. Sa'd b. Abu Waqqāṣ, from his father Sa'd, (who said) that he heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) make this comment to 'Ali.' Al-Bukhārī and Muslim both related this through Shu'ba, from Sa'd b. Ibrāhīm, from Ibrāhīm b. Sa'd b. Abu Waqqāṣ, from his father. Abu Dā'ud al-Ṭayālisī stated in his ḥadīth compendium, 'Shu'ba related to us, from al-Ḥakam, from Muṣ'ab b. Sa'd, from his father, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) left 'Ali b. Abu Talib behind from the expedition to Tabūk. 'Ali then asked him, 'Messenger of God, would you leave me behind with the women and children?' He replied, 'Are you not content to have with me the status Aaron had with Moses? Although there will be no prophet after myself.'"' Both authorities gave this on lines of transmission from Shu'ba in similar form. Al-Bukhārī also gave it on a line from Abu Dā'ud, from Shu'ba. Imam Aḥmad stated that Qutayba b. Sa'id related to him, quoting Ḥātim b. Ismā'il, from Bukayr b. Mismār, from 'Āmir b. Sa'd, from his father, who quoted the Messenger of God (SAAS) as follows. 'Ali, having been left behind by him at one of his expeditions asked, 'Messenger of God, would you leave me behind with the women and children?' He replied, ''Ali, are you not content that you have the same status with me that Aaron had with Moses? However, there will be no prophet after myself.' Muslim and al-Tirmidhī related this from Qutayba. Muslim and Muḥammad b. 'Abbād both added to this, quoting Ḥātim b. Ismā'īl. Al-Tirmidhī categorized it as being ḥasan, ṣaḥīḥ, gharīb, good and authentic, and unique from this line.
lessons: Lies and rumours from ill-intentioned people can make a person doubt even a clear and honourable assignment; going directly to the source for clarification is the right response.
Being assigned to protect and care for others is not a lesser role — the Prophet elevated it by comparing Ali's station to that of Aaron beside Moses.
Trust in the one who has placed you somewhere, rather than in the whispers of those who seek to diminish your role, is a source of dignity and peace.
practical_advice: When others try to reframe your role or responsibilities in a way that makes you feel undervalued or unwanted, seek direct clarification from the person who assigned you rather than acting on rumour.
Recognise that supporting roles — caring for family, holding things together at home, maintaining what others overlook — carry real honour and deserve to be embraced rather than resented.
If you feel left behind in life while others seem to move forward, reflect on whether the place you are in right now might be exactly where you are needed most.

title: Abu Khaythama's Return to Join the Prophet at Tabuk
summary: Abu Khaythama returned home after the Prophet's departure and, confronted by the comfort of his home compared to the Prophet's hardship in the field, felt deep shame and immediately set off to rejoin the army.
story: Ibn Isḥaq stated, 'Abu Khaythama returned on a hot day to his family several days after the departure of the Messenger of God (SAAS). He found two wives he had each in a bower in his garden; each one had sprinkled and cooled her bower with cold water and had prepared him food. 'When he entered, Abu Khaythama stood at the door of a bower, gazed at his two wives and at what they had done, and said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) is out there in the sun, wind and heat, while Abu Khaythama is in the cool shade with food prepared for him with beautiful women and on his own property! This is not fair. By God, I swear I'll not enter the bower of either one of you before joining up with the Messenger of God (SAAS)! Prepare provisions for me!" And they both did so. 'He then brought up his camel, saddled it, and left to seek the Messenger of God (SAAS). He reached...'
lessons: A moment of honest self-confrontation — comparing one's comfort to the sacrifice of those who lead and serve — can be the catalyst for decisive and courageous action.
Genuine moral conscience does not require external pressure or rebuke; it operates from within when a person is truly connected to their values.
Acting immediately on a conviction, without waiting for the feeling to fade or for circumstances to change, is what separates remorse from transformation.
practical_advice: When you notice a gap between how you are living and how you believe you should be living, use that discomfort as fuel rather than letting guilt paralyse you — let it move you to act.
Practice the habit of honest self-comparison not against others' comfort but against your own stated values and commitments.
When you feel the pull of ease and comfort tempting you away from something important, name it clearly to yourself as Abu Khaythama did — sometimes speaking the conflict aloud is what breaks it.

title: Abu Khaythama Leaves Comfort to Join the Prophet at Tabuk
summary: Abu Khaythama, having stayed behind while the Prophet marched to Tabuk, felt ashamed sitting in comfort while the Prophet endured hardship, and rushed to join him, arriving at Tabuk where the Prophet recognized and blessed him.
story:
done, and said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) is out there in the sun, wind and heat, while Abu Khaythama is in the cool shade with food prepared for him with beautiful women and on his own property! This is not fair. By God, I swear I'll not enter the bower of either one of you before joining up with the Messenger of God (SAAS)! Prepare provisions for me!' And they both did so.
"He then brought up his camel, saddled it, and left to seek the Messenger of God (SAAS). He reached him after he had encamped at Tabuk. 'Umayr b. Wahb al-Jumahi, himself heading for the Messenger of God (SAAS), had overtaken Abu Khaythama on the way and they had travelled on together until they neared Tabuk. Abu Khaythama then said to 'Umayr b. Wahb, 'I am at fault. Best for you to stay back from me until I go on to the Messenger of God (SAAS).' And he did so.
"When Abu Khaythama approached the Messenger of God (SAAS), people told the latter, 'There's a rider approaching along the road.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, 'That will be Abu Khaythama!' They commented, 'By God, Messenger of God, it is indeed Abu Khaythama!'
"When he arrived, he came up and greeted the Messenger of God (SAAS) who responded, 'Woe upon you, Abu Khaythama!' He then explained to the Messenger of God (SAAS) what had happened and the latter blessed him."
Ibn Hisham stated, "Abu Khaythama - his given name being Malik b. Quys - spoke the following verses on this. 'Having seen how people were hypocritical in their faith, I kept to what was more fine and noble. I gave forth my hand in fealty to Muhammad and did no wrong nor committed any sin. I left a woman wearing make-up inside the bower, amidst the palms, along with a feast of ripened dates. While the hypocrites were in doubt, my soul flowed onwards to the faith, its path wheresoever that would lead.'"
lessons: Genuine faith compels action even when comfort and ease tempt us to stay behind — Abu Khaythama's conscience would not let him enjoy luxury while those he loved suffered.
Acknowledging our fault openly and seeking to correct it is a sign of spiritual maturity; Abu Khaythama told 'Umayr to stay back so he could face the Prophet honestly and alone.
The Prophet's foreknowledge of who was approaching reflects his blessed insight, and arriving with humility and truthfulness earns divine blessing even after a mistake.
practical_advice: When you notice a gap between your stated values and your actual behaviour, treat that discomfort as a call to act rather than a reason to feel paralysed — make a practical step toward alignment today.

If you have fallen short of a commitment, resist the urge to hide or minimise it; approach the person or situation you let down with transparency, as Abu Khaythama did, and allow the truth to open the door to forgiveness.
Use moments of physical or material comfort as a check-in: ask yourself whether that comfort is earned or whether it is keeping you from a duty you know you should fulfil.

title: Abu Dharr Falls Behind on the Way to Tabuk and Walks Alone
summary: Abu Dharr's camel slowed him down on the march to Tabuk; rather than give up, he carried his own gear on his back and walked until he rejoined the Prophet, who prophesied that Abu Dharr would live, die, and be resurrected alone — a prophecy fulfilled years later in exile.
story:
"Eventually he was told, 'Messenger of God, Abu Dharr has fallen behind, his camel having slowed him down.' He replied, 'Leave him; if there is good in him, God will have him catch up with you. If otherwise, then God will have relieved you of him.'
"Abu Dharr gave his camel time to recover, but when it still moved only slowly, he took from it his equipment, placed it on his own back and walked on after the Messenger of God (SAAS). The latter made one of his halts and a Muslim on look-out said, 'Messenger of God, I can see this man walking along the road.'
"The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'That will be Abu Dharr.' When they had observed him closely, people said, 'Messenger of God, it is Abu Dharr, by God!' At this, the Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'May God have mercy on Abu Dharr! He walks alone, will die alone and will be resurrected alone!'
"And fate did strike its blow at him. Abu Dharr was (ultimately) sent to al-Rabdha. When his death was near, he gave instructions to his wife and a slave, saying, 'When I die, wash me and wind me in my shroud at night, then lay me on the crest of the road and tell the first passer-by that this is Abu Dharr.'
"When he died, they did this. Riders came along and unwittingly trod him underfoot. When Ibn Mas'ud came past in a group of men of Kufa, he asked 'What is this?' He was told it was the bier of Abu Dharr. Ibn Mas'ud began weeping upon hearing this and said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) spoke the truth! He said, "May God have mercy on Abu Dharr! He walks alone, will die alone and will be resurrected alone!"'
"He then dismounted and he himself buried him."
lessons: True determination is shown not when circumstances are favourable but when every support falls away — Abu Dharr carried his own load on his back and kept walking rather than turn back.
Loneliness and isolation, even when painful, do not diminish a person's worth before God; the Prophet's words about Abu Dharr were full of mercy, not condemnation.
The fulfilment of the Prophet's prophecy decades later is a reminder that divine wisdom encompasses our entire life journey, including its end — trusting that wisdom brings peace even in solitude.
practical_advice: When you feel abandoned or left behind — by circumstances, illness, or people — resist the impulse to give up; identify the one practical step still within your power and take it, as Abu Dharr did when he shouldered his own burden and walked.

If loneliness is a recurring experience in your life, reframe it not only as loss but also as an opportunity for a deeper and more direct relationship with God, who sees you even when no one else does.
Reflect on how you treat people who are isolated, exiled, or forgotten by others — Ibn Mas'ud dismounted to personally bury Abu Dharr; small acts of dignity for the lonely carry enormous weight.

title: The Army of Tabuk Endures Extreme Thirst and Hardship
summary: During the expedition to Tabuk, the Muslim army suffered severe thirst and heat to the point that men feared dying; Abu Bakr asked the Prophet to pray for rain, and a miraculous downpour fell precisely over their camp.
story:
'Umar replied, "We went out to Tabuk when the weather was extremely hot. At one stop we made, we suffered such severe thirst that we feared our necks would come off! Whenever one of us went out to locate his baggage, he'd come back fearing his neck would come off! Men would slaughter their camels and compress the contents of their stomachs to drink, then place the remainder over their livers. Abu Bakr, 'the trusting', asked, 'Messenger of God, God responds favourably to your prayers. Pray to God for us.' 'You would like that?' he asked. 'Yes,' he replied. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then raised up his hands towards heaven and did not bring them back down until it had prepared to give rain. A drizzle fell and then a torrent, and everyone filled everything available. We then went out and discovered that the rain had not fallen beyond our camp!"
The line of transmission for this is excellent. But the authorities did not give it from this line. Ibn Ishaq narrated from 'Asim b. 'Umar b. Qitada, from some men of his tribe, that this incident related to when they were at al-Hijr. They had then asked a man accompanying them who was a hypocrite, "Well, do you want still more (proof) after this?" He replied, "It was just a passing cloud!"
lessons: Extreme hardship that strips away every worldly resource can become the very moment that reveals the reality of divine provision — the rain fell precisely on the camp, leaving no doubt about its source.
Abu Bakr's request to the Prophet models the proper response to crisis: turn to the one closest to God and ask for supplication, rather than despairing or complaining.
The hypocrite's dismissal — 'it was just a passing cloud' — warns us that a closed heart will always find a rational explanation to avoid gratitude, even in the face of clear signs.
practical_advice: In moments of extreme stress when every practical option seems exhausted, make supplication your primary rather than your last resort — turn to God before your situation reaches the point of slaughtering your last camel.
Identify people in your life whose connection to God you trust and who pray sincerely; during crises, asking them to make du'a alongside you is a sound, prophetically modelled strategy.

After a difficulty is relieved, take time to acknowledge the relief explicitly and specifically, as the companions filled every available container — gratitude expressed through concrete action cements tawakkul in the heart.

title: The Miracle of the Blessed Food at Tabuk
summary: When the army at Tabuk faced starvation and wanted to slaughter their transport camels, 'Umar advised the Prophet to gather all remaining food and pray over it; the small pile miraculously filled every container in the camp and satisfied all the men.
story:
"At the expedition to Tabuk, the men were greatly afflicted by hunger. They asked, 'Messenger of God, with your permission, we would like to slaughter our water-transport camels; we could eat them and gain some weight.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) told them to do so.
"Then 'Umar came and asked, 'Messenger of God, if you do this, we'll have a shortage of transport. Invite them instead to bring the remnants of their supplies, then pray to God to bless it for them; perhaps God will place His blessings in it.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Yes, I will.' He then called for a leather coverlet, laid it out and called for the remnants of their food. One man would bring an ear of maize, another a handful of dates, a third a piece of bread. He made a small pile of this on the piece of leather and spoke a prayer blessing it. Then he told them, 'Put this into your containers.' They kept on doing this until there was not a single container in the camp that was not filled with it. They also ate from it until satisfied and still some remained. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then called out, 'I testify that there is no god but God and that I am the Messenger of God! No doubt-free worshipper who addresses God with this shall be kept from paradise.'"
lessons: When we are on the verge of a decision that would sacrifice long-term capacity for short-term survival, wise counsel — like 'Umar's — and trust in God's blessing can open an entirely different path.

Barakah, divine blessing, multiplies what is small when it is gathered with sincerity and offered to God; the companions contributed even their last handful, and that act of surrender was the precondition for the miracle.
The Prophet's declaration of faith at the moment of abundance connects material provision directly to spiritual testimony — gratitude and witnessing are inseparable.
practical_advice: When resources feel desperately scarce, resist the impulse to immediately consume your remaining reserves; pause, consult someone with wisdom, and consider whether what you have, offered with sincerity, could be multiplied through God's blessing.

Practice pooling small contributions with others in times of communal hardship rather than hoarding individually — the companions' willingness to bring even a single date created the conditions for collective relief.

Tie your expressions of gratitude to your declaration of faith: when something good happens, let your first words be words of tawhid and witnessing, as the Prophet modelled after the food was blessed.

title: The Prophet Warns Against Drinking from the Wells of the Thamud at al-Hijr
summary: When the army passed the ruins of the Thamud people at al-Hijr, the Prophet forbade the men from drinking the water of those wells, ordered that any dough made with it be given to the camels, and covered his face with his cloak out of sorrow as he rode through.
story:
Ibn Ishaq stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) passed al-Hijr, he made a halt there and the men took water from its well. When they left, he told them, 'Do not drink at all from its waters; do not use its water for your ablutions. Any dough you have made using it you should give to the camels without eating any of it yourselves.'"
Imam Ahmad stated that Ya'mur b. Bishr related to him, quoting 'Abd Allah b. al-Mubarak, quoting Ma'mar, from al-Zuhri, who said, "Salim b. 'Abd Allah quoted to me his father as having said that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) passed by al-Hijr, he said, 'Do not enter the houses of those who harmed themselves unless you do so weeping, lest there befall you what did them.' And he masked himself with his cloak while he was on his mount."
Al-Bukhari related this from a hadith of 'Abd Allah b. al-Mubarak and 'Abd al-Razzaq, both of whom cited Ma'mar and gave a similar line of transmission.
lessons: The Prophet's command to weep — or at least to cultivate the attitude of weeping — when passing the ruins of destroyed nations is a lesson in using history as a mirror for self-examination, not as a tourist attraction.

Covering one's face and heart from the sites of divine punishment reflects a deep awareness that arrogance and wrongdoing have real, lasting consequences — a sobriety that protects the soul from complacency.

Practical obedience in seemingly small matters (not drinking the water, not eating the dough) is itself an act of faith and a form of protection; the Prophet's instructions were precise because care and mindfulness matter.
practical_advice: When you encounter the consequences of poor decisions — whether in history, in others' lives, or in your own past — approach them with humility and reflection rather than curiosity or detachment; ask what they reveal about your own choices.
Cultivate the practice of using visible reminders of impermanence and consequence (ruins, illness, loss) as prompts for honest self-assessment and renewed commitment to what is right.

Pay attention to the specific, practical guidance given by those with wisdom even when the reason is not immediately obvious — the companions may not have fully understood why the water was forbidden, but their obedience was itself an act of trust and protection.

title: The Prophet's Warning at al-Hijr (Dwellings of Thamud) - Passing with Weeping
summary: During the expedition to Tabuk, the Prophet passed by al-Hijr, the ancient dwellings of the destroyed people of Thamud, and warned his Companions not to enter unless they were weeping, fearing the same punishment would befall them.
story: who said, "Sdim b. (Abd Allah quoted to me his father as having said that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) passed by al-Hijr, he said, 'Do not enter the houses of those who harmed themselves unless you do so weeping, lest there befall you what did them.' And he masked himself with his cloak while he was on his mount." Al-Bukhari related this from a hadith of 'Abd Allah b. al-Mubarak and 'Abd al-Razzaq, both of whom cited Macmar and gave a similar line of transmission. Malik stated, from (Abd Allah b. Dinar, from Ibn CUmar, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) told his Companions, "Do not enter upon these persons who suffered punishment unless you are weeping; if you do not weep, then do not enter upon them, otherwise there will afflict you what afflicted them." Al-Bukhari related this from a hadith of Malik, and from a hadith of Sulayman b. Bild, both of whom quoted 'Abd Allah b. Dinar. Muslim related it from another line from 'Abd Allah b. Dinar in similar form.
lessons: Humility before God's power means approaching signs of divine punishment with grief and reflection, not curiosity or tourism.
The Prophet himself veiled his face as a mark of solemnity, showing that leaders set the tone for reverence and mindfulness.
Remembering the fate of those who disobeyed God is a powerful tool for strengthening one's own commitment to righteousness.
practical_advice: When you encounter reminders of worldly consequences for wrongdoing — in history, news, or personal experience — pause and reflect rather than treating them as entertainment.
Cultivate a habit of pausing at moments that invite spiritual reflection, even briefly, to reconnect with your sense of accountability before God.
If you feel spiritually numb or distant, recall stories of those who faced consequences for their choices; let that awareness soften your heart and renew your intention.

title: The Expedition to Tabuk: Halt at al-Hijr and the Discarded Water and Dough
summary: During the Tabuk expedition, the Prophet's army camped at al-Hijr and drew water from the wells of Thamud; the Prophet ordered them to discard the water, feed the dough to the camels, and not eat any of it, out of reverence for the punishment that had befallen that people.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that it was related to him by 'Abd al-Samad, quoting Sakhr - Ibn Juwayriyya, that is - from Nafic, from Ibn 'Umar, who said, "At the time of the expedition to Tabak, the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his men made a halt at al-Hijr, at the dwellings of Thamad. The men drew water from the wells from which Thamnd used to drink, made dough and set up cooking pots with meat. The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered them to empty out the pots and to feed the dough to the camels. He then left with them and proceeded on, making another halt at the well from which the camel used to drink. He forbad them entering the dwellings of those who had been afflicted with punishment, saying, 'I fear that there will befall you what befell them; do not enter in upon them.'"
lessons: Obedience to prophetic guidance, even when the reason is not immediately obvious, is a mark of true faith and wisdom.
The Prophet's caution about using the resources of a punished people teaches us that some boundaries exist for our spiritual and physical protection.
Trusting the guidance of those wiser and more spiritually aware than ourselves is an act of humility that protects us from unseen harm.
practical_advice: When you receive sound religious or moral guidance that asks you to give something up, practice trusting the wisdom behind it rather than demanding a full explanation before complying.
Reflect regularly on whether the sources of your sustenance and comfort are spiritually wholesome, and be willing to let go of what may be harmful even if it is convenient.
Develop the habit of deferring to established wisdom in moments of uncertainty rather than acting purely on curiosity or impulse.

title: The Story of Abu Righal: The Man Who Left the Temple and Was Destroyed
summary: The Prophet recounted the story of Abu Righal, the one man of Thamud who was in God's temple when the punishment descended, but was struck down as his people were once he left the sanctuary.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that 'Abd al-Razzaq related to him, quoting Macmar, from CAbd Allah b. CUthman b. Khuthaym, from Aba al-Zubayr, from Jabir, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) passed by al-Hijr, he said, 'Do not ask for signs. Salih's people asked for them and they would come from this cleft.' They disobeyed the order of their Lord and hamstrung it (the holy camel). It would one day drink their water and on another they would drink its milk; God destroyed every one of them beneath the surface of the sky, except for one man who was inside God's temple.' "Someone asked, 'And who was he, Messenger of God?' 'That was Abn Righ~l,' he replied. 'And when he left the temple, he was struck down as his people had been.'"
lessons: Proximity to a place of worship does not protect a person whose heart is not aligned with God's commands; righteousness must be internal, not merely locational.
The story of Abu Righal reminds us that God's mercy and protection require sincere engagement, not passive association with sacred spaces or communities.
Disobedience to divine command — even among a people who had witnessed miracles — shows that signs alone do not produce faith without a willing heart.
practical_advice: Examine whether your religious practice is rooted in genuine commitment or merely in outward association with sacred spaces, communities, or rituals.
Do not rely solely on your environment or community to keep you spiritually safe; cultivate internal accountability and personal connection with God.
When you witness clear guidance being ignored by those around you, reflect on your own response to divine instruction rather than focusing only on others' failures.

title: The Companions' Curiosity About the People of al-Hijr and the Prophet's Rebuke
summary: When the Companions rushed to visit the ruined dwellings of al-Hijr out of curiosity, the Prophet rebuked them and redirected their attention to something far more wondrous — his own prophethood and the knowledge he carried.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Yazid b. Harnn related to him, quoting al-Mascadi, from Ismacil b. Wasit, from Muhammad b. Aba Kabsha al-Anmari, who quoted his father as saying, "On the expedition to Tabnk, the men raced off to visit the people of al-Hijr, to enter their dwellings. This reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he had a call made for a general assembly for prayer. "I came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), as he was there holding his camel, saying, 'What? Would you enter in upon a people with whom God was angry?' One man called out, 'We're curious about them.' 'Do I not tell you of matters more curious than that? A man from among yourselves informs you of what happened before you and what will be after you? Be righteous, and see clearly; God cares nothing for your punishment. A people will come who will not be able to protect themselves at all.'"
lessons: Curiosity is a natural human trait, but it must be directed toward what is beneficial and spiritually enriching rather than toward what is harmful or forbidden.
The Prophet redirected the Companions' sense of wonder toward the miracle of prophethood itself — a greater and more productive object of reflection.
God's warnings are given out of mercy, not to punish arbitrarily; staying within those boundaries is an act of self-care as much as obedience.
practical_advice: When you find yourself drawn toward things that are spiritually risky out of curiosity, consciously redirect that energy toward learning something beneficial instead.
Cultivate a sense of wonder about the gifts God has already given you — guidance, community, the gift of reflection — rather than seeking novelty in forbidden or dangerous directions.
If you feel that religious boundaries are restrictive, reframe them as expressions of divine care for your wellbeing, and ask yourself what God may be protecting you from.

title: The Two Men of Banu Sa'ida Who Disobeyed the Prophet's Order at al-Hijr
summary: Two men from Banu Sa'ida disobeyed the Prophet's instruction not to go out alone at al-Hijr; one was choked and the other was carried away by the wind, but the Prophet prayed for the first and he was cured.
story: Yunus b. Bukayr quoted Ibn Isl;J.aq as stating, "'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr b. !:Iazam related to me, from al-'Abbas b. SaW b. Sa'd a1-Sa'idl - or from al-'Abbas b. Sa'd, I am not sure which - who said that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) passed by al-!:Iijr, where he made a halt, the men took its water. And when they left there, the Messenger of God (SAAS) told them, 'Do not drink aoy of their water, nor use it for ablutions before the prayer. Feed the dough you have prepared to the camels, eating none of it yourselves. And let none of you go out this night unless accompanied by another.' "They did as the Messenger of God (SAAS) had ordered them, except for two men of Banu Sa'ida. One of them went out to relieve himself aod the other went to look for a camel of his. The mao who relieved himself was choked at the place where he had gone. The one searching for his camel was carried away by the wind and thrown up on Mt. Tayyi'. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was told of this aod commented, 'Didn't I tell them not to go out unless accompanied by another?' "He then spoke a prayer for the mao who had been afflicted at the place where he relieved himself, and the man was cured. The other man reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) (on his return) from TabUk." In one account there is additional information given from Ibn Isl;J.aq to the effect that a man of Banu Tayyi' presented him (the second mao) to the Messenger of God (SAAS) when he returned to Medina. Ibn Isl;J.aq stated, "'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr related to me that al-'Abbas b. Sahl named these two men to him but in confidence aod so he did not tell me who they were."
lessons: Disobeying clear and specific guidance, even for seemingly minor or necessary reasons, can expose us to real harm that obedience would have prevented.
The Prophet's compassionate prayer for the man who was harmed — rather than abandoning him — demonstrates that divine mercy follows sincere repentance and the intercession of the righteous.
Being honest about the consequences of our choices, without hiding behind excuses, is the first step toward healing and recovery.
practical_advice: When you have been given clear guidance by a trusted authority — religious, medical, or otherwise — resist the temptation to make small exceptions, as those small deviations often carry the greatest risk.
If you have acted against your better judgment and suffered consequences, do not despair; seek help, pray, and trust that mercy is still available to you.
In times of difficulty or travel, stay close to your community and do not isolate yourself unnecessarily; companionship is both a practical and spiritual protection.

title: The Garden at Wadi al-Qura and the Prophet's Correct Guess of Its Produce
summary: On the way to Tabuk, the Prophet and his Companions passed a garden at Wadi al-Qura; the Prophet correctly guessed its produce would be ten camel loads, and this was confirmed when they returned from Tabuk.
story: Imam Al;J.mad stated that 'Affan related to him, quoting Wahib b. Khalid, quoting 'Amr b. Yal;J.ya, from al-'Abbas b. Sahl b. Sa'd al-Sa'idI, from Abu !::Iumayd al-Sa'idI, who said, "We left with the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the Tabuk expedition and travelled to WadI al-QjIra. There we carne across a woman in a garden. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said to his Compaoions, 'Guess!' The men made their guesses, as did the Messenger of God (SAAS); he guessed that the produce from the garden would equal ten awsaq, ten camel loads. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked the woman, 'Keep a total of the produce that comes from it until, if God wills it, I come back.' "He then travelled on to Tabuk... eventually we were back in Wadi al-(~lfa. He asked the woman, 'How much did your garden produce?' 'Ten carnelloads,' she replied. He had guessed correctly."
lessons: The Prophet's knowledge and perception were a sign of his prophethood, reminding believers that divine guidance is accurate and trustworthy even when it seems unlikely.
Returning to verify a promise or a word — as the Prophet did when he asked the woman about her garden — models integrity and follow-through in our own commitments.
Small, seemingly ordinary encounters on a journey can contain profound signs of God's wisdom and the Prophet's special connection to divine knowledge.
practical_advice: Practice being a person of your word: if you make a commitment or statement, follow up on it, even when time has passed or circumstances have changed.
Look for signs of God's wisdom and blessing in the ordinary details of your daily life — the small moments of accuracy, provision, or guidance that are easy to overlook.
When others around you demonstrate trustworthy judgment consistently, reflect on what you can learn from their approach rather than dismissing it as coincidence.

title: The Severe Gale During the Night at Tabuk and the Man Carried to Mt. Tayyi'
summary: The Prophet warned the army at Tabuk of a severe overnight gale and instructed them to stay put and secure their camels; one man disobeyed and was carried away by the wind to Mt. Tayyi'.
story: Abu !::Iumayd said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) told the men, 'Tonight there will be a severe gale. No one should get up during it. Those of you with camels should fasten down their halters.' "Abu !:Iamid said, 'We did and during the night a severe gale swept over us. One of our men did get up and it carried him away on to Mt. Tayyi'.'"
lessons: Heeding prophetic warnings — even when we cannot see the danger ourselves — is an act of faith that protects us from foreseeable harm.
The one man who disobeyed serves as a reminder that collective instructions exist for collective protection, and individual exceptions break that protection.
Preparing practically for what we are warned about (fastening the camels' halters) alongside spiritual trust shows that faith and practical action work together.
practical_advice: When you receive clear warnings from trustworthy sources — whether about physical, spiritual, or emotional risks — act on them promptly rather than waiting to see if the danger materializes.
In stressful or dangerous situations, resist the urge to act independently out of restlessness or impatience; sometimes stillness and obedience are the wisest response.
Pair your trust in God with concrete preparation: faith does not mean passivity, but it also does not mean ignoring wise counsel.

title: The Governor of Ayla's Gift and the Prophet's Robe of Honour at Tabuk
summary: During the Tabuk expedition, the Governor of Ayla came to the Prophet, presented him with a white mule, and received in return a robe of honour and a written document of protection.
story: "Then the Governor of Ayla came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and presented to him a white mule. The Messenger of God (SAAS), presented him with a robe of honour and wrote a document for him giving them protection."
lessons: The Prophet's practice of reciprocating generosity and formalizing agreements in writing models dignified and trustworthy leadership.
Extending protection to those who come in peace — even non-Muslims — reflects the Islamic ethic of justice and coexistence.
Acts of generosity exchanged between leaders can build bridges of peace that protect entire communities from harm.
practical_advice: Respond to others' generosity with genuine gratitude and reciprocity; do not take kindness for granted or leave it unacknowledged.
When you make agreements or commitments, consider putting them in writing to honor both parties and prevent misunderstanding.
Look for opportunities in your relationships and community to extend protection, support, and dignity to those who are vulnerable or who approach you in goodwill.

title: The Prophet Combining Prayers During the Tabuk Expedition and the Miracle of the Spring
summary: During the Tabuk expedition, the Prophet combined prayers due to travel, then warned the army about the spring of Tabuk; two men touched its water before being told not to, and after the Prophet washed his face and hands in it and returned the water, the spring miraculously overflowed.
story: Imam Malik, may God have mercy on him, stated from Abu al-Zubayr, from Abu al-Tufayl 'Amir b. Wathila, that Mu'adh b. Jabal informed him that they went forth with the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the Tabuk expedition. (Mu'adh stated), "He would combine together the prayers ofthe al-,?ukr, 'noon', and the al-'a~r, 'late afternoon', and those of the al-magkrib, 'early evening', and the al-'iska, 'late evening'. One day he postponed the prayer and came out and prayed the al-,?ukr and the al-'a~r together. He then went back in and carne out again and prayed the al-magkrib and the al-'iska together. He then said, 'Tomorrow, if God wills it, you will arrive at the spring at Tabuk. You'll not reach it until forenoon. Those who reach there should not touch any of the water before I arrive.' "When we arrived there, we had been preceded by two men. The well looked like skirak, 'sandal straps', just oozing a little water. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked the two men, 'Did you touch any of the water?' 'Yes,' they replied. He berated them and said to them what God wished him to speak. They then little by little scooped out water from the spring and put it all in a water-skin. The Messenger of God (SAAS) washed in it his face and hands then poured it back into the spring. Now the spring produced much water and the men drew from it. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'Mu'adh, if you live long enough, you'll see that what's here will be enough to fill several gardens!'" Muslim did include this in a /.tadilk from Malik.
lessons: Islamic law provides accommodations for hardship — such as combining prayers during travel — showing that the religion is built on mercy and ease, not unnecessary burden.
The miraculous overflowing of the spring after the Prophet's blessed touch is a reminder that divine provision is not limited by apparent scarcity.
Even when others disobey instructions and we feel the consequences collectively, prophetic leadership responds with correction and then with action to restore what was damaged.
practical_advice: When you are going through a demanding season of life — travel, illness, heavy responsibility — remember that Islam's accommodations for hardship are gifts; use them without guilt.
When you face situations of apparent scarcity or lack, do not panic; take the spiritually grounded action available to you and trust that God's provision can exceed what you see.

If you have acted hastily and made a situation more difficult for yourself or others, do not freeze in shame; acknowledge what happened honestly, accept correction, and focus on what can be done now to move forward.

title: The Prophet's Address at Tabuk Leaning Against a Palm Tree: The Best and Worst of Men
summary: At Tabuk, the Prophet gave an address leaning against a palm tree, describing the best of men as those who strive in God's cause until death, and the worst as those who are profligate, insolent, and recite God's Book without heeding it.
story: Imam Al;J.mad narrated from Abu al-Naqr Hashim b. al-Qasim, YUnus b. Mu1).ammad al-Mu'addib and I:Iajjaj b. Mu1).ammad, all three quoting al-Layth b. Sa'd, from Yazid b. Abu !::Iabib, from Abu al-Khayr, from Abu al-Khanab, from Abu Sa'id al-Khudari, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave an address at Tabuk while leaning his back against a palm tree. He spoke as follows: 'Shall 1 tell you who is the best of men and who is the worst? Of the best of men is one who works in God's cause on the back of his horse and on the back of his mule and on his two feet until death comes to him. Of the worst of men is one who is profligate and insolent, a man who recites God's Book but pays no attention to any of it.'" Al-Nasa'i narrated this from Qutayba, from al-Layth.
lessons: True virtue is not a moment of inspiration but a sustained commitment — working in God's cause by whatever means available until the very end of one's life.
Reciting or knowing scripture without internalizing or acting upon it is identified by the Prophet as among the worst of qualities, warning us against hollow religious performance.
The Prophet delivered this profound guidance not from a grand platform but leaning against a palm tree in the field — a reminder that wisdom and leadership do not require ceremony.
practical_advice: Audit your own religious practice: are you engaging with Quran, prayer, or worship in a way that genuinely shapes your character and choices, or has it become routine and empty?
Commit to consistency in your good deeds rather than dramatic but short-lived efforts; God loves deeds that are sustained, even if they are small.
Resist the temptation to measure your faith by your knowledge or recitation alone; ask yourself regularly how your beliefs are actually changing the way you treat people and make decisions.

title: Bilal and the Prophet Both Oversleep at Tabuk
summary: During the expedition to Tabuk, both the Prophet and his caller to prayer Bilal overslept past the dawn prayer, and when questioned, Bilal replied that the same God who caused the Prophet to sleep had caused him to sleep as well.
story: AI-Bayhaqi narrated through Ya'qub b. Mu!)ammad al-Zuhri, from 'Abd al-'Aziz b. 'Umran, that Mu~'ab b. 'Abd Allah related to him, from Man?ur b. Jamil b. Sinan, who quoted his father as saying to him, "I heard 'Uqba b. 'Amir al-Juhani relate as follows, 'We went forth with the Messenger of God (SAAS), on the expedition to TabUk. (One night) the Messenger of God (SAAS) remained in bed without waking up until the sun had risen a spear's length high. He called, 'Bilal, did 1 not tell you to announce dawn to us?' He replied, 'Messenger of God, He who bore you away in sleep bore me also!' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) moved a little away from his tent and prayed. He travelled on the rest of that day and night and arrived next morning in Tabuk."
lessons: Even the Prophet and his companions were subject to human limitations such as sleep and forgetfulness — acknowledging our humanity is not a failure of faith but a reminder of our dependence on God.
Bilal's response reflects profound theological insight: all affairs, including our limitations, are ultimately in God's hands, and this awareness brings peace rather than shame.
When we miss an obligation due to circumstances beyond our control, the correct response is to fulfill it as soon as possible without excessive guilt, as the Prophet simply moved away and prayed.
practical_advice: When you miss a prayer, an appointment, or an obligation due to circumstances outside your control, do not spiral into guilt — acknowledge what happened, make it up promptly, and move forward.
Use moments of human limitation as reminders of your dependence on God, turning them into opportunities for humility and gratitude rather than self-criticism.
If someone in your care makes a mistake that you also made, respond with understanding rather than blame — Bilal's honest answer modeled accountability without shame.

title: The Crippled Man Whose Feet Were Affected by the Prophet's Prayer at Tabuk
summary: A man recounted how as a youth he ran between the Prophet and the direction of prayer during the expedition to Tabuk, prompting the Prophet to say 'May God cut out his footprint,' after which the man was unable to walk normally for the rest of his life.
story: Abu Da'ud stated that both Al)mad b. Sa'ld al-Hamadanl and Sulayrnan b. Da'ud related to him, quoting Ibn Wahb, quoting Mu'awiya, from Sa'ld b. Ghazwan, from his father (who said) that he passed the night at Tabuk while on his way to the pilgrimage. There he noticed a cripple. (Ghazwan's account goes on), "I asked him about himself and he replied, 'I will give you an account, but don't tell anyone of it for so long as I live. The Messenger of God ($AAS) was encamped at Tabuk near a palm tree. He said, "This is our qibla." He then performed the prayer towards it. I, a youth at the time, came running along and passed between him and the tree. He then said, "He has severed our connection! May God cut out his footprint!" And I've not stood up on it to this very day!''' Abu Da'ud then related it from a [tadith of Sa'jd b. 'Abd al-'Azlz al-Tanukhl, from a freed-man of yazjd b. Nimran, from yazjd b. Nimran, who said, "At Tabuk, I saw a cripple. He told me, 'While riding my donkey, I passed in front of the Messenger of God ($AAS) as he was praying. He said, "0 God, cut off his footprint!" And I have not been able to walk on it ever since.'" In another account, the words are, "He cnt off our connection! May God cut out his footprint!"
lessons: Actions taken in haste or ignorance can carry lasting consequences — mindfulness and awareness of sacred moments and spaces is a form of respect and care.
Carrying the burden of a past mistake privately for years, as this man did, is not the healthiest path — acknowledging one's errors and seeking forgiveness brings more peace than concealment.
The sacredness of worship and the space of prayer deserves our deepest attentiveness; disruptions, even unintentional ones, reflect a lack of presence that we should strive to cultivate.
practical_advice: If you carry long-standing guilt over a past mistake, consider that concealing it indefinitely may increase the burden — seek forgiveness from God sincerely and allow yourself to heal.
Cultivate awareness and presence in sacred moments such as prayer — put away distractions, slow down, and treat the time of worship as a protected space.
When you witness others engaged in worship, honor that space by waiting patiently rather than rushing through — small acts of respect for others' devotion strengthen community bonds.

title: The Angels Pray Over Mu'awiya b. Mu'awiya at Tabuk
summary: While at Tabuk, the Prophet was informed by Gabriel that a man in Madinah named Mu'awiya b. Mu'awiya had died and been honored by 70,000 angels praying over him due to his constant recitation of Surah Al-Ikhlas.
story: AI-Bayhaql narrated from a [tadith of Yazjd b. Harun, quoting al-'Ala' Abu Mul)ammad al-Thaqafj, who quoted Anas b. Malik as saying, "We were with the Messenger of God ($AAS), at Tabuk. The sun came up with a brightness and with rays oflight such as I had never seen before. Gabriel came to the Messenger of God ($AAS), who asked, 'Gabriel, why do I see the sun today with a brightness and rays oflight such as I never before saw it arise?' He replied, 'That is because Mu'awiya b. Mu'awiya al-Laythj died today in Medina. God sent to him 70,000 THE LIFE OF THE PROPHET MU!:IAMMAD 18 angels to pray over him.' 'And why was that?' he asked. 'It was due to the frequency of his reciting the words: "Say: He is God, One" (surat al-Ikhla,; CXIl, v.l). He did so by night and by day, when walking and upon rising or sitting down. Would you like me, Messenger of God, to take hold of the earth for you so you can pray for him?' 'Yes,' he replied. And he then prayed for him and returned." This badtth is extremely strange and objectionable. People base it upon this al-'Ala' b. Zayd, but he is the object of controversy. AI-Bayhaqi then stated, "'Ali b. A]:lmad b. 'Abdan related to us, quoting A]:lmad b. 'Ubayd aI-Saffar, quoting Hisham b. 'Ali, quoting 'Uthman b. aI-Haytharn, quoting Ma1)bub b. Hila!, from 'Ata' b. Abu Maymuna, from Anas, who said, 'Gabriel carne and said, "MuQ.ammad, Mu'awiya b. Abu Mu'awiya al-Mazani has died. Would you like to pray for him?" "Yes," he replied. Gabriel flapped his wings and every tree and mountain top bent over in submission. He then prayed while behind him there stretched two columns of angels in each of which there were 70,000 angels. 'I (the Prophet (SAAS)) then asked, "Gabriel, how did he achieve this status with God?" He replied, "By his love for the words, 'Say: He is God, One.' He would recite it standing or sitting, going or corning, and at all times."' 'Uthman went on, "I asked my father where the Prophet was at the time. He replied that he was on the expedition to Tabuk in Syria, that Mu'awiya had died in Medina and that his bed had been raised up so he could see it and pray for him."
lessons: Consistency in small acts of worship — such as frequently reciting a short chapter of the Quran — can carry immense weight with God, reminding us that spiritual elevation comes through sustained devotion rather than grand gestures.
God's honor for a person may be invisible to those around them in life but made manifest in ways beyond ordinary sight — this encourages us not to measure a person's worth by worldly status.
The Prophet's willingness to pray for someone even during a military expedition shows that compassion and spiritual connection transcend physical distance and circumstance.
practical_advice: Incorporate the regular recitation of Surah Al-Ikhlas into your daily routine — in the morning, before sleep, while walking — as a simple yet profound act of remembrance that costs nothing but builds a powerful spiritual habit.
When feeling spiritually insignificant or that your small acts of worship don't matter, remember this account and let it renew your motivation to be consistent even in modest devotions.

Do not wait for grand occasions to express your faith — the most honored acts in this account were performed in ordinary moments: sitting, standing, walking — weave worship into the fabric of everyday life.

title: Heraclius Receives the Prophet's Invitation and Sends a Reply via the Tanukhi Envoy
summary: The Prophet sent a message to Heraclius the Byzantine emperor from Tabuk, who after consulting his priests and facing their outrage, sent back a reply with an Arab envoy whom the Prophet invited to Islam.
story: Imam AQ.mad stated that it was related to him by IsQ.aq b. '1sa, quoting YaQ.ya b. Salim, from 'Abd Allah b. 'Uthman b. Khuthaym, from Sa'id b. Abu Rashid, who said, "In I;Iims I met al-Tanukhi, the envoy ofHerac1ius to the Messenger of God (SAAS); he was a neighbour ofmine and a very old man who had reached 90 or so. I asked, 'Won't you tell me about the message of Herac1ius to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and of his message to Herac1ius?' 'Certainly,' he replied. "(He narrated) The Messenger of God' (SAAS) reached Tabuk and sent DiQ.ya al-Kalbi to Heraclius. When the message from the Messenger of God (SAAS) reached him, Herac1ius summoned the priests and patriarchs of Rome and locked himself and them inside a building. He told them, 'This man is encamped where you are aware, and he has sent me offering me three alternatives. He invites me to follow him in his religion, or that we give him what we have on this our land, this land remaining ours, or that we go to war with him. By God, you well know from what you read in the books that he will definitely IMAM ABO AL-FIDA' ISMA'IL IBN KATHIR 19 take our land, so let us either follow him in his faith or give him what we have on our land.' "They snorted in disgust as one man so hard as almost to burst from their hooded gowns, saying, 'You are inviting us to abandon Christianity or have us be slaves to a bedouin from I;Iijiiz?' "When he realized that when they left him they would tum the Byzantines against him, he made peace with them immediately, saying, 'I only said that to find out how dedicated you are.' "He then summoned an Arab of Tajib who had power over the Christian Arabs and said, 'Find me some man who can well remember speech and whose native tongue is Arabic whom I can send to this man with a reply to his message.' "He brought me to him and Heraclius gave me a letter, saying, 'Take this letter of mine to this man. Commit to memory what he says and note three specifics: take note of any comment he makes about the letter he wrote me; observe whether he reads my letter and whether he makes mention of "night". and observe whether there is anything you find curious about his back.' "So I set off with his letter and reached Tabuk where I found him seated among his men, his legs drawn up and wrapped in his garment, over near the spring. I asked, 'Where is your leader?' 'This is he,' I was told. "I went over and sat down before him, handed him my letter which he placed on his lap. He then asked, 'From whom have you come?' 'I am a brother ofTanukh,' I answered. He asked, 'Would you like to join Islam, the banafi faith ofyour father Abraham?' I replied, 'I am the messenger ofmy people and belong to the faith of my people; I cannot turn from it before I return to them!' He laughed and said, 'You cannot lead aright whomever you wish; but God leads aright whomever He wishes; He knows best those who will be led aright' (surat al-Qa,a,; XXVIII, v.56). Brother of Tanukh, I wrote a message to Chosroe
lessons: The Prophet's da'wah — his invitation to faith — was extended with wisdom and without compulsion even to the most powerful rulers of the world, demonstrating that truth is offered freely and accepted only by God's will.

Heraclius privately acknowledged the truth of the Prophet's message but allowed social pressure and fear of losing power to prevent him from acting on it — a powerful lesson about how ego and social standing can obstruct genuine guidance.

The Prophetic response 'You cannot lead aright whomever you wish; but God leads aright whomever He wishes' reveals a deep inner peace about outcomes — the Prophet's role was to convey, not to control results.
practical_advice: When you share truth, good advice, or an invitation with someone and they decline, release the outcome — your responsibility is to convey sincerely, not to control the response, and this detachment protects your peace.

Reflect on whether there are truths you privately acknowledge but resist acting on due to fear of social consequences or loss of status — examine what is holding you back and whether it is worth the cost.

When delivering an important message or invitation to someone, lead with genuine care and an open question rather than pressure — the Prophet's gentle, inquiring approach created space for honest dialogue even across vast cultural divides.

title: The Messenger's Encounter with the Brother of Tanukh
summary: A messenger from the Tanukh tribe visits the Prophet Muhammad and recounts their exchange, including discussions about Islam, the fate of various rulers, and the Prophet's miraculous seal.
story: he placed on his lap. He then asked, 'From whom have you come?' 'I am a brother ofTanukh,' I answered. He asked, 'Would you like to join Islam, the banafi faith ofyour father Abraham?' I replied, 'I am the messenger ofmy people and belong to the faith of my people; I cannot turn from it before I return to them!' He laughed and said, 'You cannot lead aright whomever you wish; but God leads aright whomever He wishes; He knows best those who will be led aright' (surat al-Qa,a,; XXVIII, v.56). Brother of Tanukh, I wrote a message to Chosroe (and he tore it up) and God will tear him up and tear up his realm. I wrote a letter to the Negus and he burned it; and God will burn him up along with his realm. I wrote a letter to your leader and he kept it; the people will continue to experience courage from him so long as there remains goodness in life.' "I told myself that this was one of the three things my master had advised me about. So I took an arrow from my quiver and used it to write on the side of my sword. He then took the letter and gave it to a man on his left. I asked, 'Who is the man with your letter who reads to you?' 'That is Mu';;wiya,' they replied. In my master's letter it said, 'You invite me to a paradise as big as the sky and the earth that is prepared for the pious; then where is hell-fire?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, 'Glory be to God! And where is night when daytime comes?' "I took an arrow from my quiver and noted this on the leather scabbard of my sword. "When he had fmished reading my letter, he said, 'You are right; you are indeed a messenger. IfI had some reward, I would give it to you. We are travellers out of supplies.' One of the gronp of men then called out to him, 'I will give him a reward.' And he opened his pack and brought me a gold~threaded garment which he placed in my lap. I asked who was the donor of the reward, and I was told it was 'Uthman. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked, 'Which ofyou will accommodate this man?' One ofthe young an$iir said, 'I will.' The an$iir got up and I arose with him. When I had left the group of men, the Messenger of God (SAAS) called out to me, 'Come here, brother of Tanukh!' I hurried over to where I had been sitting in front of him and he lifted off the shirt from his back and said, 'Over here; carryon and do as you were ordered.' I looked over at his back and saw a seal in the place of the fold of his shoulder blades, like a large mole." This is a /:tadith that is gharib, unilateral; its line of authorities is not bad. Imam AQmad is alone in giving it.
lessons: Guidance is ultimately from God alone; no human can force faith into another's heart, so focus on sincere effort rather than guaranteed outcomes.
Responses to truth reveal character: those who honor a message of goodwill receive its blessings, while those who reject it face consequences of their own choosing.
Wisdom and generosity, even toward a stranger or potential adversary, reflect the highest moral character.
practical_advice: When you feel frustrated that your sincere efforts to help others are not accepted, remind yourself that your role is to try sincerely, not to control outcomes — release the burden of responsibility for others' choices.

When engaging with those who hold different beliefs, approach them with curiosity and kindness rather than confrontation, as the Prophet's calm laughter and reasoned reply opened more doors than arguments could.
Observe how people respond to truth and generosity in your own life; surround yourself with those who honor goodness, as their character will uplift you.

title: The Peace Pact with the Governor of Ayla and the People of Jarba' and Adhruh at Tabuk
summary: At Tabuk, the Prophet Muhammad made peace treaties with the Governor of Ayla and the people of Jarba' and Adhruh, granting them protection and safe passage in exchange for the jizya poll-tax.
story: Ibn ISQaq stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had reached Tabuk, YUQanna b. RU'uba, the Governor of Ayla came to him. He made peace with the Messenger of God (SAAS) and paid him the jizya, the alms poll~tax. People also came from Jarbi!' and AdhruQ and also gave him thejizya. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) wrote a document for them which they retain. He wrote the following to Yui).anna b. Ru'uba and the people of Ayla: 'In the name of God, the most Mercifnl and Beneficent. This is a guarantee ofsafe passage from God and Mui).ammad the Prophet, the Messenger of God, to Yui).anna b. Ru'uba and the people of Ayla and their transports by land and sea. They have the protection of God and that of Mui).ammad, the Prophet and those with him, of the people of Syria, Yemen and the sea. Those of them who cause any incident (breaking this treaty) shall not find that their wealth protects them; (their property) shall be fair game for whoever seizes it. It shall not be permissible to prevent their passage to any well to which they go or any path they take, whether by land or sea.' Yunus b. Bukayr added the following from Ibn Isi).aq: "This is the document of Juhaym b. al~Salt and Shura1).bil b. I:Iasana, (made) by permission of the Messenger of God (SAAS)." Yunus quoted Ibn ISQaq as stating, "He also wrote to the people ofJarba' and Adhrui)., as follows, 'In the name of God, the most Merciful and Beneficent; this is a document from Mui).ammad the Prophet, the Messenger of God, to the people ofJarbi!' and Adhrui).: that they are secure under the protection of God and Mui).ammad. That they shall pay 100 dinars each Rajab and 100 awqiyya weight of iiba, fine perfume. Moreover, God holds them responsible for providing friendship and kindness to Muslims, and to any Muslims who might seek refuge with them.' "The Prophet (SAAS) awarded the people of Ayla his cloak, along with his treaty for them." He went on, "Abu a1-'Abbas 'Abd Allah b. Mu1).ammad purchased it (the cloak) thereafter for 300 dInars."
lessons: Peaceful coexistence and formal agreements can protect communities far more effectively than ongoing conflict, demonstrating that strength can be expressed through mercy and order.
Honoring written commitments and upholding the rights of those under one's protection is a mark of trustworthy leadership.
True security comes from God's protection, and acknowledging this in formal agreements reflects humility and faith.
practical_advice: When navigating conflict or tension with others, consider whether a clear, written agreement outlining mutual rights and responsibilities could resolve the uncertainty and build lasting trust.

Practice extending protection and goodwill even to those who are different from you; the Prophet's treaty guaranteed the rights of non-Muslim communities, modeling inclusive and principled leadership.
In your own dealings, strive to honor your commitments in writing and in spirit — reliability is the foundation of trust in any relationship.

title: The Burial of Dhu al-Bijadayn
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) personally helped lower Dhu al-Bijadayn into his grave at Tabuk and prayed for God's pleasure upon him, moving Ibn Mas'ud to wish the grave had been his own.
story: was saying, "Let down your brother close to me!" And they did so. When he placed him in position into his niche, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "0 God, I was pleased with him; may You be pleased with him!" '''And 'Abd Allah b. Mas'ud would say, "How I wish that grave had been my own!"''' Ibn Hisham stated, "The reason he was known as 'Dhu al-Bijadayn', 'he of the two pieces of coarse cloth', is because when he wanted to accept Islam, his people prevented him. They persecuted him, and when he eventually escaped them, all he had on was one piece of bijad, 'coarse cloth'. He ripped it into two pieces and used one as a waist-wrapper and the other as a shirt. He then went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and became known as Dhu al-Bijadayn."
lessons: Sincerity and sacrifice for one's faith, even when one has nothing material to offer, can earn the highest honor in the sight of God and His Messenger.
The Prophet's personal care for even the humblest of his Companions demonstrates that true leadership is built on love and service, not status.
The longing of Ibn Mas'ud to have been in that grave reflects how the righteous measure honor not by worldly success but by closeness to God and His approval.
practical_advice: When you feel you have little to offer — in wealth, status, or ability — remember that sincerity of heart and willingness to sacrifice are what matter most to God.
When you are going through persecution or loss, focus on what you are moving toward (your faith, your community, your purpose) rather than what you are leaving behind.
Reflect regularly on what kind of legacy you are building — not what people will say about you, but whether God will be pleased with you.

title: Abu Ruhm's Night Journey Beside the Prophet at Tabuk
summary: During the Tabuk expedition, Abu Ruhm al-Ghifari recounts a tense night ride beside the Prophet (SAAS), during which his mount kept drifting dangerously close, and the Prophet questioned him about companions who had fallen behind.
story: Ibn Is!:taq continued, "Ibn Shihab al-Zuhri related, from Ibn Ukayrna al-Laythi, from his nephew Abu Ruhm al-Ghifari, who stated he heard Abu Ruhm Kulthum b. al-Husayn, who was one of those who pledged allegiance at the tree, say, 'I went on the expedition to Tabuk with the Messenger of God (SAAS). One night I was travelling with him at al-Akhdar, when God sent sleep down upon me. I awoke to find that my mount was very close to that of the Prophet (SAAS). I was shocked to find it so close to him, afraid I might strike against his leg in the stirrup. I therefore set about moving my mount away from him. Eventually, some distance further on, my eyes were again overcome and my mount approached him and his leg in the stirrup. This time I only awoke when he shouted, "Huss!", "careful!". I exclaimed, "Please forgive me, Messenger of God!" He replied, "Ride on!" "'He then began asking me about those men of Banu Ghifar who had fallen behind, and I told him. He then asked me, "What happened to those men with long, red, thin beards who had no other facial hair?" I told him they had dropped behind. "'He went on, "And what about those men with short, black, curly hair?" I replied, "By God, I don't know any like that among us." "Yes," he insisted, "they're the ones who own camels at Shabakat Shadakh." I then remembered them to have been among the Banu Ghifar, but I could not identify them until I recalled them to have been a clan of Aslam who were allied to us. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked, "What prevented one of them, when he fell behind, from giving transport on one of his mounts, to some man eager to participate in God's cause? It troubles me greatly to have muhajiriin and ansar, along with men of Ghifar and Aslam, fall behind."'"
lessons: The Prophet's gentle response — 'Ride on' — after Abu Ruhm's fearful apology shows that true leadership responds to honest mistakes with patience and mercy rather than rebuke.
The Prophet's detailed concern for those who fell behind the expedition reveals that a true community leaves no one behind, and that leadership means accounting for every individual.
Communal responsibility is a spiritual obligation — those with resources (like extra mounts) are expected to assist others striving in God's cause.
practical_advice: When you make an embarrassing mistake in front of someone you respect, resist the urge to spiral into excessive guilt — apologize, accept their grace, and move forward as the Prophet instructed: 'Ride on.'
If you are in a position of relative ease or resources, ask yourself who around you is struggling and whether you could offer practical support to help them continue their journey.

When you feel overwhelmed by a long and difficult path (physically, spiritually, or emotionally), take comfort in knowing that those around you — and God — notice your effort and care about whether you are keeping up.

title: The Hypocrites' Plot to Assassinate the Prophet on the Ridge at Tabuk
summary: On the return from Tabuk, a group of hypocrites plotted to assassinate the Prophet (SAAS) by pushing him off a steep ridge, but the plot was foiled by Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman, and the Prophet chose not to have them killed to protect Islam's reputation.
story: Ibn Lahi'a quoted Abu al-Aswad as telling him that 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) set out to return from Tabuk to Medina, a group of hypocrites plotted to assassinate him by casting him down from a steep ridge on to the path below. He was informed of this and ordered the men to pass through the valley while he mounted the ridge. Those who had plotted accompanied him, having muffled their faces. The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered 'Ammar b. Yasir and Hudhayfa b. al-Yaman to proceed along with him, 'Ammar holding the bridle of his camel, while Hudhayfa urged it forward. "While they were proceeding, they heard the group (of assassins) descending upon them. The Messenger of God (SAAS) became angry and when Hudhayfa saw this, he went back towards the assassins carrying a bent cudgel that he waved in the faces of their mounts. When they saw Hudhayfa, they realized that the terrible deed they planned had been discovered, so they hurried away and mingled with the rest of the force. "Hudhayfa returned and caught up with the Messenger of God (SAAS). He ordered his two guides to hurry ahead and they traversed the ridge trail and halted, waiting for the others. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked Hudhayfa, 'Did you recognize those men?' 'No,' he replied, 'all I could see was their mounts as I came down at them in the dark of night.' He then asked, 'And do you two men know anything about what these men were doing?' 'No,' they replied. And he told them both of what they had conspired to do to him; he named them, and asked the two men to keep this confidential. "They asked, 'Messenger of God, won't you order us to kill them?' He replied, 'I dislike to have people say that Muhammad kills his own men.'" Ibn Ishaq gave this account, except for relating that the Messenger of God (SAAS) told the names of the conspirators only to Hudhayfa b. al-Yaman. This is more likely; but God knows best. Evidence for this is in the words of Abu al-Darda' to 'Alqama, the friend of Ibn Mas'ud: "Do you not have among you" — meaning the people of Kufa — "the sahib al-sawad wa al-wisad?" (By this he meant Ibn Mas'ud.) "And do you not have among you the sahib al-sirr, 'he in charge of the secret'?" (By this he meant Hudhayfa.) "And do you not have among you he whom God protected from the devil through the tongue of Muhammad?" (By this, he meant 'Ammar.) And the Commander of the Believers, 'Umar b. al-Khattab, may God be pleased with him, has been quoted as having said to Hudhayfa, "I adjure you by God to say whether I was one of them!" "No," he replied, "But I'll not declare innocent anyone else after you!" Meaning, in order not to disclose the secret of the Prophet (SAAS). I observe that they were 14 men, though some say they were 12. Ibn Ishaq stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent Hudhayfa b. al-Yaman to assemble them and that the former then told them what they had conspired to do. Ibn Ishaq proceeded to list their names and said, "And it was about them that God, the Almighty and Glorious, revealed the words, 'And they set out to do what they did not accomplish.' (surat al-Tawba; X, v.74).
lessons: The Prophet's refusal to execute the hypocrites — despite their treachery — demonstrates that protecting the integrity and reputation of the Muslim community can sometimes take precedence over personal justice.
Hudhayfa's courage in advancing alone into the dark toward armed conspirators shows that standing between a leader and harm, even at personal risk, is among the highest acts of loyalty.
The Prophet's choice to keep the names of the conspirators secret, entrusting them only to Hudhayfa, reflects a wisdom that valued communal stability over personal vindication.
practical_advice: When someone betrays your trust, resist the immediate impulse toward public retaliation — consider whether a measured, private response might better serve the larger good you are trying to protect.

In moments of real danger, remember that even the Prophet (SAAS) was protected through the loyalty and quick action of those around him; do not underestimate the power of having a few trustworthy companions.

If you are entrusted with a difficult secret — especially one involving others' failings — take seriously the responsibility of that trust, as Hudhayfa did, and guard it unless absolutely necessary to disclose.

title: Hudhayfa's Account of the Twelve Hypocrites on the Ridge
summary: Hudhayfa ibn al-Yaman recounts leading the Prophet's camel on the ridge trail at Tabuk when twelve muffled hypocrites appeared intending to push the Prophet off, and the Prophet revealed their ultimate fate — that they would make war on God and His Messenger and be consumed by divine punishment.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related through Muhammad b. Salama, from Abu Ishaq, from al-A'mash, from 'Amr b. Murra, from Abu al-Bakhtari, from Hudhayfa b. al-Yaman, who said, "I was holding the halter of the camel of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and leading it ahead, while 'Ammar drove it" — or his words were, "I was driving it while 'Ammar was leading it". "Suddenly, there on the ridge trail were 12 men blocking our way along it. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was startled at this and shouted out at them, and they turned and went back. "He then asked us, 'Did you recognize them?' We replied, 'Messenger of God, they were muffled. We did recognize their mounts, however.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) went on, 'Those are men who will be hypocrites right on until Judgement Day! Do you know what they intended?'. 'No,' we replied. 'They wanted to crowd out the Messenger of God, on the trail, to cast him down from it!'
