There aI-Mugh!ra wished to send Abu Sufyan on ahead, but Abu Sufyan refused this. He responded, 'You go on in to your people.' And Abu Sufyan remained on a property he owned at Dhu al-Hadm. "When al-Mugh!ra entered the town, he went up to the idol and struck it with a pickaxe. His people, Banu Mu'attib, stood in front of him fearing that arrows would be fired at him, or that he would be struck down, as happened to 'Urwa b. Mas'ud. "The Thaq!f women came out with their heads bare, weeping for the idol and chanting, 'We weep for a protector betrayed by those accursed They have not done well in striking her.' "While al-Mughrra struck her with the pickaxe, Abu Sufyan was saying, 'Alas! Alas for you!' When aI-Mugh!ra had finished destroying her, he took all she had, including her jewels, and sent a message to Abu SufYan. He also told him, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered us to settle with 'Urwa b. Mas'ud and his brother al-Aswad b. Mas'Ud, father of QIrib b. aI-Aswad, for the debts owed them, using the money from the idol.' And this they did."
lessons: The transition from falsehood to truth can be painful for those attached to old ways, but the grief of letting go is part of growth.
Acting with courage to dismantle harmful beliefs and practices, even in the face of communal resistance, is a mark of sincere conviction.
Justice requires settling debts and honouring obligations even in the midst of great change.
practical_advice: When you are called to make a significant change in your life, expect grief and resistance from those around you, and respond with steadiness rather than aggression.
Honour your financial and moral obligations to others even as your life circumstances change; integrity is not conditional.
Approach the end of harmful habits or attachments with decisiveness, understanding that the discomfort of change is temporary.

title: The Thaqif Delegation's Initial Deception and Their Community's Eventual Acceptance of Islam
summary: The Thaqif delegation, after secretly accepting Islam's conditions, initially misrepresented the Prophet to their people to test their reaction; when their community prepared for war and then felt terror, they finally accepted Islam fully.
story: Musa b. 'Uqba related that the Thaq!f delegation consisted of some ten men, and that when they arrived, the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave them accommodation in the mosque so that they would hear the Qur'an. They questioned him about the taking of interest, fornication and wine, and he forbad them all of these. They asked him about the goddess and what he would do with her. He replied, "Destroy her!" They responded, "How terrible! If the goddess knew that you intend to destroy her, she would execute her people!" 'Umar b. aI-Khanab commented, "Really! Ibn 'Abd Yalrl, how ignorant you are! The goddess is nothing but stone." They replied, "We did not come here to see you, Ibn al-Khanab!" They then said, "Messenger of God, you undertake destoying her. We'll never do it." He replied, "I'll send someone to take care of her destruction for you." They gave him a document specifying this and asked him to permit them to arrive back home before his messengers arrived. When they reached their own people, they met with them and were asked what had happened. They expressed sadness, saying that they had just come from an uncouth and peculiar man who had appeared bearing a sword, and made whatever judgements he wished. Having dominated the Arabs, he had forbidden usury, fornication and wine. He had ordered the destruction of the goddess. Thaqif expressed their aversion, saying, "We'll never obey this!" He then told them, "Then prepare for battle; prepare your weapons." They did this for two or three days, and then God cast terror into their hearts. Once more they sent representatives, instructing them, "Go back to him and agree to his conditions; make peace with him." The delegation members replied, "We have already done this! We find him to be the most God-fearing of men, very trustworthy, merciful and truthful. We were blessed in having gone to him and in having reached a settlement with him!" They then informed them of what had been agreed, and they accepted what God had favoured them with. They asked, "Why did you conceal this from us at first?" The delegation responded, "We wanted for God to remove Satan's arrogance from your hearts!" They accepted Islam right then and there.
lessons: God can turn hearts that were filled with arrogance and resistance to humility and acceptance in an instant.
Those who encounter truth firsthand often become its most sincere advocates once their own hearts are clear.
Pride and communal pressure can delay the acceptance of truth, but genuine conviction eventually breaks through.
practical_advice: When someone you care about is resistant to a positive change, give them time and space rather than forcing the matter; change of heart often comes when external pressure is removed.
Reflect on whether pride or social conformity is preventing you from accepting something you know in your heart to be right.
Trust that sincere, truthful engagement with others plants seeds that can bear fruit even after a period of resistance.

title: The Destruction of al-Lat Witnessed by All of Thaqif
summary: When the Prophet's emissaries arrived at al-Ta'if to destroy al-Lat, the entire tribe gathered to watch, most believing the idol was invincible; al-Mughira struck her down, fell and rose again, and declared her to be nothing but stone and clay.
story: They remained thus for several days, and then the messengers of the Messenger of God ($AAS) arrived. He had placed them under the command of Khlilid b. al-Walid. Al-Mughira b. Shu'ba was among them. They went to al-Llit; all Thaqif, their men, women and children, stood there watching, even the old men having emerged from their pavilions. Most ofThaqifdid not believe that she was to be destroyed, thinking her invincible. Al-Mughira b. Shu'ba took up the al-karz;n - meaning the m;'wal, "the pickaxe", and said to his companions, "I'm really going to make you laugh at al-Thaqifl" So he struck out with the pickaxe, then fell, his legs giving way. The people of al-Tli'if were shaken by this, all crying out in one voice. They were delighted and said, "May God remove al-Mughira! The goddess has killed him! They then told the others, "Those of you who wish may go close up." Al-Mughira then arose, saying, "It's nothing but a pile of stone and clay! Accept God's munificence and
lessons: What people fear as invincible often turns out to be nothing when confronted with genuine conviction and trust in God.
Falsehood, no matter how long it has been venerated, cannot withstand the determination of those who are committed to truth.
Moments of apparent setback, like al-Mughira's fall, do not determine the final outcome for those who persevere.
practical_advice: When confronting a deeply ingrained fear or a long-held false belief in your own life, take the first decisive action even if you stumble; rising again is what matters.
Do not be discouraged when people around you celebrate your stumble; stay focused on your purpose and let your perseverance speak louder than their doubt.
Recognize that things you once considered unshakeable sources of security may be nothing more than stone and clay; place your ultimate trust in God alone.

title: Destruction of the Idol Al-Lat at Ta'if
summary: Al-Mughira b. Shu'ba destroys the idol al-Lat in Ta'if after the surrender of Thaqif, levelling it to the ground despite initial panic among the people, after which the Prophet distributed its wealth.
story: Al-Mughira b. Shu'ba took up the al-karz;n - meaning the m;'wal, "the pickaxe", and said to his companions, "I'm really going to make you laugh at al-Thaqifl" So he struck out with the pickaxe, then fell, his legs giving way. The people of al-Tli'if were shaken by this, all crying out in one voice. They were delighted and said, "May God remove al-Mughira! The goddess has killed him! They then told the others, "Those of you who wish may go close up." Al-Mughira then arose, saying, "It's nothing but a pile of stone and clay! Accept God's munificence and worship Him!" He struck the door and broke it, then scaled its walls, as did other men with him, and they kept on destroying it stone by stone until they had levelled it to the ground. Its guardian then began shouting, "Its foundations will still be angry! They will swallow them up!" When al-Mughira heard this, he said to Khlilid, "Let me dig up its foundation!" So they dug it all up, collecting all its water and structures. At this Thaqif were truly amazed. Then they all returned to the Messenger of God ($AAS), and he divided up the wealth from it, that very day. They gave praise to God Almighty for inspiring pride in His religion and for aid given to His Messenger.
lessons: True courage means rising after a fall and continuing the mission even when others mock or doubt you.
Falsehood, no matter how long it has been venerated, has no real power — it is stone and clay before the truth.
Victory over deeply entrenched falsehood inspires gratitude and communal praise of God.
practical_advice: When you stumble publicly and feel embarrassed, remember that rising and continuing is what defines you, not the fall itself.
When facing a fear or a deeply held false belief, remind yourself that it holds no intrinsic power — examine it rationally and act accordingly.
After achieving a difficult goal, take a moment to express gratitude, as this grounds you and prevents arrogance.

title: The Prophet's Covenant for the Protection of Wajj Valley
summary: Before besieging Ta'if, the Prophet Muhammad declared the valley of Wajj and its wildlife and trees protected by divine command, issuing a written covenant to that effect for Thaqif.
story: Ibn Isl:Iliq stated, "The document written for Thaqif by the Messenger of God ($AAS) stated, 'In the name of God the most Compassionate, the most Merciful, from Mul:Iammad, the Prophet, the Messenger of God, to the believers. The acacia trees ofWajjl6 and the game there are not to be harmed. Whoever is found doing this shall be whipped and his clothing shall be stripped from him. If he should do this a second time, he will be taken to the Prophet Mu!).ammad. This is the command of the Prophet, Mu!).ammad. Khalil b. Sa'id has written (this) by order of the Messenger, Mu!).ammad b. 'Abd Allah. Let no one transgress this; by so doing, he will harm himself in such as Mu!).ammad, the Messenger of God, has ordered.' Imam A!).mad stated, "'Abd Allah b. al-I:Iarith al-Makhziimi, of Mecca, related to me, quoting Mu!).ammad b. 'Abd Allah b. Insan - whom he praised highly - from his father, from 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr, who said, 'We advanced with the Messenger of God (SAAS), from Mt. Liyya until we reached Mt. al-Sidra. There he made a halt by the side of the peak, facing towards it. He directed his gaze towards a mabbas - a valley, that is. He stayed there until everyone arrived and then he said, "Hunting in Wajj or (cutting down) its trees is forbidden, made so by God." This was prior to his arrival at al-Ta'if and his siege of Thaqif.'"
lessons: The Prophet's concern for the environment demonstrates that stewardship of nature is a spiritual and moral responsibility.
Divine law encompasses the protection of all living things, not only human welfare.
Written covenants and clear rules are a sign of just and organised governance.
practical_advice: Extend your sense of ethical responsibility beyond people to include your environment and the living things around you.
When you are in a position of authority, establish clear and fair rules and communicate them to those under your care.
Recognise that small acts of environmental protection are acts of worship and moral integrity.

title: The Death of 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy, the Chief Hypocrite
summary: The leader of the hypocrites in Madinah, 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy, falls gravely ill and dies, with the Prophet visiting him repeatedly during his illness despite his history of treachery and hypocrisy.
story: Ibn Is!).aq stated, "Al-Zuhri related to me, from 'Urwa, from Usama b. Zayd, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) went in to visit 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy as he lay sick during the illness from which he died. When he saw him to be close to death, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said to him, "Well, by God, I used to warn you against loving the Jews!" 'Abd Allah replied, "And yet did As'ad b. Zurara 17 not express his hatred of them?"' Al-Waqidi stated, "'Abd Allah b. Ubayy fell ill some days before the end of Shawwal. He died in Dhu al-Qa'da. His illness lasted for 20 days. The Messenger of God (SAAS) would constantly visit him during it. The day of his death, the Messenger of God (SAAS), who was being very generous to him with his time, came in to see 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy and commented, 'I advised you against loving the Jews.' He replied, 'As'ad b. Zurara treated them as enemies; what good did it do him?' He then went on, 'Messenger of God, this is no time for complaint; this is my death. Be present when my body is washed and give me your shirt damp from your own skin to wrap me in. And pray and seek forgiveness for me.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) did all this for him."
lessons: Consistent kindness and visiting the sick, even those who have wronged you, is a mark of the highest moral character.
Death is a leveller that strips away all pretence; even the hardened hypocrite sought the Prophet's mercy at his end.
Warning others against harmful paths is an act of care, even if they do not heed the warning during their lifetime.
practical_advice: Choose to show compassion to those who have wronged you, especially in their moments of vulnerability — this is a sign of strength, not weakness.
Do not withhold kindness from difficult people in your life; you never know when your consistent goodness will matter most.
When someone close to you is dying or in crisis, set aside grievances and be present for them.

title: The Prophet Gives His Shirt to Wrap 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy
summary: The Prophet gives his own shirt to be used as a burial shroud for 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy, the chief hypocrite, honouring a favour the man's family had once done for the Prophet's uncle al-Abbas.
story: Sufyan b. 'Uyayna stated, from 'Amr b. Dinar, who heard Jabir b. 'Abd Allah say, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) came to the grave of 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy after he had been placed in it. He ordered that he be brought out and placed upon his knees - or his thighs - and he then discharged some of his spittle upon him and dressed him in his own shirt. But God knows best." In the ,a/;i/; collection of al-Bukhari, this is given with the same chain of authorities. According to him, he only dressed him in his shirt in exchange for 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy having given al-'Abbas his shirt when he had come to Medina; the only shirt appropriate for him they could find was that of 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy.
lessons: Reciprocating kindness, even to those who have harmed you, reflects the highest standard of gratitude and noble character.
Acts of generosity done in the past are never forgotten by the truly noble, who repay them even when it is difficult.
The Prophet's action teaches that recognising another's goodness, however small, is a virtue to be emulated.
practical_advice: Make it a habit to remember and acknowledge past kindnesses people have done for you, even when your relationship with them has soured.
When someone who has wronged you has also done something good for you, find an appropriate way to acknowledge that goodness.
Practice separating people's wrongdoings from their good deeds in your assessment of them — people are rarely entirely one or the other.

title: 'Umar's Challenge and the Revelation Prohibiting Prayer for Hypocrites
summary: When the Prophet prepares to pray over the deceased hypocrite 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy, 'Umar challenges him and a Quranic verse is subsequently revealed prohibiting prayer over hypocrites.
story: Is1)aq b. Ra1)awayh stated, "I asked Abu Usama, 'What was it that 'Ubayd Allah related to you from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar?' He replied, 'When 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy b. Salul died, his son 'Abd Allah came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and asked him to give him his shirt to wrap his body in. He gave it to him. He then asked him to pray for him.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) arose to pray over him, but 'Umar b. al-Khattab took hold of his gown, saying, 'Messenger of God, are you going to pray for him even though God prohibited you from keeping company with him?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'My Lord gave me a choice: "Seek forgiveness for them or do not seek forgiveness for them. Even if you do seek forgiveness for them 70 times, God will still not forgive them." I will do so more than 70 times.' He ('Umar) objected, 'But he was a hypocrite! Will you really pray for him?' Then God, Almighty and Glorious is He, revealed, 'And never offer prayer for anyone of them who dies, and do not stand by their graves. They disbelieved in God and His Messenger' (surat al-Tawba; IX, v.84). Abu Usama confirmed this, saying, 'Yes.' They included this in both ,a/;i/; collections, from a /;adith of Abu Usama. In one account, given by al-Bukhari and others, 'Umar said, 'So I asked, "Messenger of God, would you pray for him, though once he said this-and-that? And then on another occasion he said so-on-and-so-forth?"' The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'Leave me alone, 'Umar. I have two choices. If I knew that forgiveness would be granted for him if I spoke more than 70 prayers, I would do so.' He then did pray for him. And so God the Almighty and Glorious revealed, 'And never offer prayer ...' 'Umar went on, 'I was myself surprised at my courage with the Messenger of God (SAAS); God and His Messenger know best!'"
lessons: Divine guidance refines even the most sincere intentions; what feels like mercy may sometimes need to be bounded by wisdom.
Righteous companions who speak truth to leadership, as 'Umar did, are a great blessing even when they cause discomfort.
Sincere humility — as 'Umar showed by reflecting on his own boldness — is a mark of true faith and self-awareness.
practical_advice: When you feel torn between compassion and wisdom, seek clarity through reflection and, if you are a believer, through prayer and divine guidance.
Value people in your life who are willing to respectfully challenge your decisions — their honesty is a gift.
After acting boldly or confrontationally, reflect on your motivations with humility, as 'Umar did, rather than justifying yourself unconditionally.

title: Hassan b. Thabit's Poem Commemorating the Ansar's Battles
summary: A poem attributed to Hassan b. Thabit or his son enumerates the battles and expeditions in which the Ansar fought alongside the Prophet, honouring their courage, loyalty, and sacrifice.
story: Ibn Is!).aq stated, "The expedition to Tabuk was the last on which the Messenger of God (SAAS) went out." "I:Iassan b. Thabit, may God be pleased with him, spoke the following verses enumerating the battles and expeditions in which the a~iir participated with the Messenger of God (SAAS)." Ibn Hisham attributed these verses to I:Iassan's son 'Abd al-Ra!).man: "Am I not the best of Ma'ad in family and clan, if all were collected and brought together? These are men who all fought at Badr along with the Messenger and they did not disappoint or desert. They pledged allegiance to him and none of them reneged, and there was no falseness in their faith. That morning at the defile at Ul;md hard blows fell on them like the burning heat of a fire. And that day at Dhu ~rad, when fighting arose while on their mounts, and they did not betray or abstain. And at Dhu al-'lJshayra they charged ahead on their horses with the Messenger, armed with swords and lances. And at Waddan they drove its people out, their horses charging ahead until rugged ground and mountain intervened. And there was that night when they pursued their enemy for God; and God will reward them for their action! And that night at l:Iunayn they fought hard with him; that night he gave them new energy for fighting when they became tired. And the attack to Najd, where, with the Messenger, they gained booty and spoils. And at the expedition to al-( we scattered the enemy like camel herds to a water-hole. And that day when allegiance was pledged, it was they who agreed to go to war; they aided him, and did not retreat. And there was the Conquest wherein they were enrolled in his personal guard, and they were not reckless or hasty. And at Khaybar they were in his squadron, all marching ahead, death-defying heroes, Their bare swords brandished in their right hands, brought down to strike, then raised again. And that day the Messenger of God went forth to Tabuk, willing to suffer for reward, they were his prime standard-bearers. And leaders into battle when warfare faced them, either advancing or withdrawing, as they thought best. Those are they - the Prophet's an$ar; these are my people, those to whom I refer in tracing my descent. They die with nobility, and do not break their oaths; if they are killed, it is in God's cause."
lessons: Commemorating the sacrifices of those who came before us preserves their legacy and inspires future generations.
True loyalty is demonstrated through consistent action across many trials, not just a single heroic moment.
Poetry and art can be powerful vehicles for expressing gratitude, honour, and communal identity.
practical_advice: Take time to honour and acknowledge the sacrifices of people who have stood by you through difficult times — express this in words, written or spoken.
Reflect on the many small moments of loyalty and perseverance in your own life and recognise them as part of a larger story of growth.
Find creative outlets — writing, poetry, storytelling — to process and celebrate meaningful experiences and relationships.

title: Abu Bakr Appointed to Lead the Pilgrimage in Year 9 AH
summary: Following his campaigns, the Prophet appointed Abu Bakr to lead the Muslim pilgrimage in 9 AH while polytheists performed their own rites, marking a pivotal transition in the governance of the sacred rites.
story: Following his account of the arrival of the delegation from al-Ta'if in Ramaqan, as detailed above, Ibn Isl:Jaq proceeded to state that the Messenger of God (SAAS) remained there for the remainder of Ramaqan, Shawwal and Dhu al-Q:I'da. He then dispatched Abu Bakr in command of the pilgrimage the Muslims could undertake that year, 9 AH, while the polytheists were at the stations for their own pilgrimage. The Muslims were no longer to be prevented access to the ka'ba; some of them (the Arab tribes) had agreements with him (the Prophet (SAAS)), for specific and temporary periods of time. When Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, and the Muslims with him departed, and he departed from home, God, the Almighty and Glorious,
lessons: Delegating important responsibilities to trusted, capable people is a sign of wisdom and strong leadership.
Even in times of political complexity and coexistence with opponents, the community's spiritual obligations must be upheld.
The gradual transition of sacred responsibilities to the Muslim community reflects the wisdom of building capacity before full transformation.
practical_advice: When you are unable to fulfil an important responsibility yourself, identify the most trustworthy and capable person to represent you and delegate clearly.
Do not let complicated circumstances become an excuse to neglect your spiritual or communal obligations — find the way to fulfil them.
Recognise that major transitions in life rarely happen all at once — be patient with gradual, principled change.

title: Abu Bakr's Command of the Pilgrimage in 9 AH
summary: The Prophet dispatched Abu Bakr to lead the Muslims in pilgrimage in 9 AH, while polytheists also gathered at their stations. This marked a transitional moment before the final exclusion of polytheists from the sacred precincts.
story: of God (SAAS) remained there for the remainder of Ramaqan, Shawwal and Dhu al-Q:I'da. He then dispatched Abu Bakr in command of the pilgrimage the Muslims could undertake that year, 9 AH, while the polytheists were at the stations for their own pilgrimage. The Muslims were no longer to be prevented access to the ka'ba; some of them (the Arab tribes) had agreements with him (the Prophet (SAAS)), for specific and temporary periods of time. When Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, and the Muslims with him departed, and he departed from home, God, the Almighty and Glorious, revealed the following verses at the beginning of surat al-Tawba (IX): "(This is a declaration) of immunity by God and His Messenger to those polytheists with whom you made an agreement: move out into the countryside for four months ..." to the words "... and a declaration from God and His Messenger to the people at the time of the Greater Pilgrimage, that God and His Messenger are free of obligation to the polytheists" (surat al-Tawba; IX, v.l-3). Ibn Isl:Jaq went on to discuss these verses. We have expounded upon them at length in our Exegesis; and to God belong all praise and credit.
lessons: Leadership is a trust; Abu Bakr accepted the mission with full commitment, modeling how responsibility should be embraced with sincerity.
Divine guidance can arrive at any moment, even mid-journey; remaining open and responsive to God's direction is essential.
Transitions in life, even difficult ones, carry divine wisdom — the phasing out of old agreements reflected God's mercy in giving people time to adjust.
practical_advice: When given a responsibility, focus on fulfilling it faithfully rather than worrying about what others are doing around you.
If circumstances change unexpectedly while you are working toward a goal, pause, reassess with gratitude, and adapt rather than resist.
Trust that timelines and transitions in your life, even confusing ones, often carry a wisdom that becomes clearer in hindsight.

title: The Revelation of Surat al-Tawba and the Declaration of Immunity
summary: God revealed the opening verses of Surat al-Tawba while Abu Bakr was already en route to lead the pilgrimage, declaring a formal immunity from obligation toward the polytheists and giving them four months to find safety.
story: God, the Almighty and Glorious, revealed the following verses at the beginning of surat al-Tawba (IX): "(This is a declaration) of immunity by God and His Messenger to those polytheists with whom you made an agreement: move out into the countryside for four months ..." to the words "... and a declaration from God and His Messenger to the people at the time of the Greater Pilgrimage, that God and His Messenger are free of obligation to the polytheists" (surat al-Tawba; IX, v.l-3). Ibn Isl:Jaq went on to discuss these verses. We have expounded upon them at length in our Exegesis; and to God belong all praise and credit. What is referred to here is the dispatch of 'AIJ following that of Abu Bakr so that the former could himself make this declaration of immunity to the polytheists on behalf of the Messenger of God (SAAS); he did this in his capacity as his cousin and relative on his father's side.
lessons: God's decrees, even when they bring endings, are accompanied by mercy — the four-month grace period shows that closure is given with care.
Divine revelation can reframe the meaning of events already in motion; remaining spiritually attentive allows us to receive new understanding.
Clarity about boundaries — whether in relationships or commitments — is not cruelty but a necessary step toward a healthier order.
practical_advice: When a long-standing arrangement in your life must end, give yourself and others adequate time and clear communication rather than abrupt severance.
If you receive new guidance or clarity while already committed to a course of action, do not ignore it — integrate it honestly.
Reflect on areas of your life where unclear obligations are causing confusion, and seek to bring honesty and defined boundaries to those relationships.

title: Ali ibn Abi Talib Sent to Deliver the Declaration of Immunity
summary: The Prophet sent Ali ibn Abi Talib after Abu Bakr, directing him to personally deliver the proclamation of immunity to the polytheists at Mina, explaining that only a man from his own family could properly convey this message.
story: Ibn Isl:Jaq went on, "I:Iakim b. I:Iakim b. 'Abbad b. I:Iunayf related to me, from Abu Ja'far Mul:Jammad b. 'AIJ, who said, 'When the surat al-BariJ?a (al-Tawba; IX) was revealed to the Messenger of God (SAAS), after he had dispatched Abu Bakr to oversee the pilgrimage of the Muslims, he was asked, "Should you not send news of the (revelation) to Abu Bakr?" He replied, "Only a man from my own family could transmit this for me." "'He then summoned 'Ali b. Abu Talib and told him, "Take this part from the beginning of surat al-Barif'a, and announce it to the people when they gather on the day at Mina to make sacrifice there. Tell them (also) that no unbeliever will enter paradise, and that after this year no polytheist shall make the pilgrimage. Nor shall anyone circumambulate the ka'ba naked. Those with agreements made (individually) with the Messenger of God, can consider them valid until their term expires." ""'Ali b. Abu Talib therefore left on ai-'Adba', the camel of the Messenger of God ($AAS), and travelled to Abu Bakr, "the trusting". When Abu Bakr saw him, he asked, "(Do you come) as a commander, or as one to be commanded?" 'Ali replied, "The latter". "'They proceeded on together and Abu Bakr maintained charge of the pilgrimage. That year the Arabs were keeping to their stations for the pilgrimage as they had previously done before the coming of Islam. "'When it was the day to make the sacrifices, 'Ali b. Abu Talib arose and made the proclamation as ordered by the Messenger of God ($AAS). He gave them a period of four months from the day of that declaration for them all to return to some place of safety, or to their own lands. Thereafter there was to be no pact or protection for any polytheists, except for any individual who had a personal agreement with the Messenger of God ($AAS); that would remain in force until its expiration. "'After that year, no polytheist made the pilgrimage and no one circumambulated the ka'ba naked. Thereafter the two men returned to the Messenger of God ($AAS).'"
lessons: Humility in leadership is demonstrated when Ali, a man of great stature, willingly accepted a supporting role under Abu Bakr without complaint.
Being entrusted with an important mission requires setting aside personal comfort and acting with courage — Ali rode out immediately without hesitation.
True brotherhood does not require competition; Ali and Abu Bakr worked together seamlessly, each fulfilling their designated role.
practical_advice: When you are given a difficult message to deliver, prepare yourself spiritually and mentally beforehand, as Ali did by receiving clear instructions before departing.
If you find yourself in a secondary role to someone else on a shared task, focus on fulfilling your own responsibility excellently rather than resenting the arrangement.
Model the kind of cooperation Ali and Abu Bakr demonstrated: acknowledge others' authority in their domain while confidently exercising your own.

title: Abu Hurayra Among the Criers at Mina
summary: Abu Bakr dispatched Abu Hurayra and other criers at Mina on the day of sacrifice to publicly announce that polytheists would no longer be permitted to make pilgrimage and that no one should circumambulate the Ka'ba naked.
story: Al-Bukhari stated as follows in his section entitled: Chapter on the pilgrimage made by Abu Bakr, may Cod be pleased with him, with the men in the year 9 AH: "Sulayman b. Da'ud Abu al-Rabi' related to me, quoting Fulayl:>, from al-Zuhri, from I:Iumayd b. 'Abd al-Ral:>man, from Abu Hurayra, who said, 'Abu Bakr, "the trusting", was sent out by the Messenger of God ($AAS), in command of the pilgrimage that preceded the !tijjat al-wada', "the farewell pilgrimage". With him went a group who were to announce to everyone, "No polytheist shall make the pilgrimage after this year. And no one shall circumambulate the ka'ba naked."' " In another section, al-Bukhari stated that it was related to him by 'Abd Allah b. Yiisuf, quoting al-Layth, quoting 'Aqil, from Ibn Shihab, who said, "I:Iumayd b. 'Abd al-Ral:>man informed me that Abii Hurayra stated, 'On that pilgrimage, Abii Bakr, "the trusting", sent me out along with those criers he dispatched on the day of the making of the sacrifice, to announce at Mina, "After this year no polytheist shall make the pilgrimage. And no one shall circumambulate the ka'ba naked."'" "Bumayd stated, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) then sent 'Ali forth to make the declaration concerning the al-bartt'a, "the immunity".' "Abu Hurayra stated, 'He sent out 'Ali along with us to make the announcement to those at Mina on the day of the sacrifice, concerning the declaration about the immunity, (and) that, "After this year no polytheists shall make the pilgrimage", and "no person shall circumambulate the ka'ba naked".'"
lessons: Every person in a community has a role to play in upholding what is sacred; Abu Hurayra's willingness to be a public crier shows that important work is not always glamorous but is always meaningful.
Speaking truth in a public space, especially when the message may be unwelcome, requires moral courage rooted in a higher purpose.
Sacred spaces deserve sacred conduct; the proclamation about circumambulation reflects how the inner state should match the outward act of worship.
practical_advice: If you are called to deliver a difficult or unpopular message in your community or workplace, ground yourself in the righteousness of the cause rather than fear of reaction.
Reflect on whether your outward religious or spiritual practices genuinely reflect your inner state, and seek to close that gap.
Be willing to do the unglamorous but necessary work in service of a good cause, as Abu Hurayra did by raising his voice repeatedly in the crowds.

title: Ali's Voice Grows Hoarse from Repeated Proclamation
summary: Ali ibn Abi Talib recounted that he continuously proclaimed the four key messages of the declaration of immunity throughout the pilgrimage until his voice grew hoarse from the effort.
story: Imam Al;1mad stated that Mul;1ammad b. Ja'far related to him, quoting Shu'ba, from Mughira, from al-Sha'bi, from Mul;1riz b. Abu Hurayra, who quoted his father as saying, "I was with 'Ali b. Abu Talib when the Messenger of God (SAAS) had dispatched him. 'Ali asked, 'What was the announcement you were making?' "He replied, 'We would announce that no one but a believer would enter paradise, that no one naked should circumambulate the ka'ba, and that those having an agreement with the Messenger of God (SAAS) would have their pact valid for a term - or a period - of four months. After those four months were over, then God and His Messenger would have immunity from it. Also, that no polytheist would make the pilgrimage after this year.' He added, 'I kept making this announcement until my voice became hoarse.'" This chain of transmission is excellent.
lessons: Dedication to a mission sometimes means exhausting yourself in its service — Ali's hoarse voice is a testament to wholehearted commitment.
Even when a task is repetitive or physically taxing, doing it with full effort honors the trust placed in you.
Persistence in conveying truth, even when it is tiring, is a form of sincerity and worship in itself.
practical_advice: When working toward an important goal, be prepared to give more than you initially planned and to keep going even when you feel depleted.
If you find yourself exhausted by a responsibility, take that as a sign of your dedication rather than a reason to quit — rest, then continue.
Reflect on how much of yourself you are genuinely investing in the causes and commitments that matter most to you.

title: Ali Proclaims the Immunity Verses at Arafat and Goes Around the Camps
summary: Ali recited the opening verses of Surat al-Bara'a at Arafat during Abu Bakr's address, then traveled through the pilgrims' camps to ensure those who missed the main address also heard the proclamation.
story: Ibn Jmr stated that Mul;1ammad b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd al-I;Iakam related to him, quoting AbU Zur'a Wahb Allah b. Rashid, quoting I;Iaywa b. Shurayl;1, quoting Ibn Sakhr (who said) that he heard Abu Mu'awiya al-Bajali, from Kufa, say, "I heard Abu al-Sahba' al-Bakri say, 'I asked 'Ali b. Abu Talib about the day of the "greater pilgrimage". He replied, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched Abu Bakr b. Abu Qul;1afa to conduct the people on pilgrimage, and then sent me as well bearing 40 verses of siirat a/-Baril'a. When he was at Mt. 'Arafat, he made a public address on the day of the assembly there. When he had completed his address, he turned to me and said, 'Now you rise, 'Ali, and deliver the message of the Messenger of God (SAAS).' ""'I got up and recited to them the 4 verses from siirat a/-Baril'a. We then proceeded on up to Mina, where I cast stones and slaughtered a sacrificial camel. I then shaved my head. Having learned that not all those present had attended the address given on Mt. 'Arafat by Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, I went all around to the camps reciting the verses to them." "'Ali went on, "From that, I believe you imagined it to have been the day when sacrifice is made, when in fact it was the day of Mt. 'Arafat."'"
lessons: Going the extra mile to ensure no one is left without access to important guidance reflects a deep sense of care and responsibility.
Performing one's rituals faithfully — casting stones, slaughtering, shaving — grounds a person even amid a demanding mission.
Correcting misunderstandings with gentleness, as Ali did when clarifying which day the proclamation occurred, is a mark of intellectual honesty.
practical_advice: After completing a major task, take the time to check whether anyone who needed the information or help actually received it — do not assume the work is done.
When carrying out multiple obligations simultaneously, stay grounded by fulfilling each one properly rather than rushing through them.
When someone misremembers or misunderstands an event you were part of, correct them kindly and with specifics rather than letting the error stand.

title: Abu Bakr Leads the Pilgrimage and Ali Announces the Immunity Declaration
summary: Abu Bakr leads 300 men on pilgrimage in 9 AH, while the Prophet sends Ali to announce the declaration of immunity (bara'a) to the pilgrims at Hajj.
story: Al-Waqidi stated, "Three hundred of the men departed with Abu Bakr on this pilgrimage, including 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Awf. Abu Bakr took with him five camels for sacrifice. Along with him the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent twenty animals for sacrifice, then sent 'Ali out to follow him; he caught him up at al-'Arj. He made the announcement concerning bara'a, 'immunity', in the presence of those on the pilgrimage."
lessons: Divine plans unfold in stages; the announcement of a new covenant required proper preparation and the right messenger at the right moment.
Leadership sometimes means delegating critical tasks to those best suited for them, even mid-mission.
Clarity in religious obligations must be communicated broadly so that no one is left uninformed.
practical_advice: When facing an important announcement or difficult conversation, ensure it reaches everyone who needs to hear it, not just those immediately present.
Be willing to adjust your plans when a higher priority emerges, as Ali did in rushing to catch up with Abu Bakr.
Trust that responsibilities given to you by those in authority carry weight and fulfill them completely.

title: The Death of the Negus of Abyssinia
summary: The Negus, the Christian king of Abyssinia who had sheltered early Muslim refugees, died in Rajab of 9 AH, and the Prophet made a public address in his remembrance.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that in Rajab of that year the Negus, the leader of Abyssinia, died and the Messenger of God (SAAS) made a public address in remembrance of him.
lessons: Goodness and justice transcend religious and cultural boundaries; the Prophet honored a non-Muslim ruler who had shown compassion to the early Muslims.
Those who protect the vulnerable and uphold justice deserve to be remembered and honored.
Gratitude must be expressed even when someone who helped us is no longer alive.
practical_advice: Honor the memory of those who showed you kindness, regardless of their background or beliefs.
Take time to publicly acknowledge the contributions of people who supported you during difficult times.
Let gratitude motivate you to pay kindness forward to others who may be in need.

title: The Death of Umm Kulthum, Daughter of the Prophet
summary: Umm Kulthum, a daughter of the Prophet, died in Sha'ban of 9 AH; her husband Uthman stepped back from entering her grave following the Prophet's guidance.
story: In Sha'ban that year Umm Kulthum, the daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS) died. Her body was washed by Asma', daughter of 'Omays and by Safiyya, daughter of 'Abd al-Munalib. It is also said that she was washed by other wives, including Umm 'Atiyya. I note that this is established in both Sahih collections as well as in other hadith accounts, that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) prayed over her and was about to bury her, he said, "Let no one enter her grave who has tonight experienced desire for his family." Her husband, 'Othman, therefore abstained (from entering the grave) and she was buried by Abu Talha al-Ansari. May God be pleased with her.
lessons: Even the Prophet experienced the grief of losing his children, reminding us that loss and sorrow are part of every human life.
Ritual purity and adherence to religious guidance are maintained even in the most emotionally difficult moments.
Community comes together to care for one another in times of death and grief.
practical_advice: In times of personal loss, lean on your community and allow others to help carry the burden of grief.
Observe religious practices and guidance even when emotions are overwhelming, as they provide structure and comfort.
Honor the deceased by fulfilling the rites due to them with care and sincerity.

title: The Destruction of the Dirar Mosque Built by Hypocrites
summary: A group of hypocrites built a mosque in 9 AH that was secretly intended as a base for war against the Muslims; the Prophet ordered it burned down.
story: That year the Dirar mosque had been built by a group of hypocrites; it had the form of a mosque, but secretly was a house for war. The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave orders and it was burned down.
lessons: Deception that uses sacred symbols to disguise harmful intent is among the most dangerous forms of corruption.
Leadership sometimes requires difficult and decisive action to protect the integrity and safety of the community.
True worship cannot coexist with hidden agendas of harm; sincerity is the foundation of all religious practice.
practical_advice: Be discerning about the true intentions behind outward appearances of goodness in your environment.
When you identify something in your life that appears righteous but serves a harmful purpose, have the courage to remove it.
Prioritize sincerity in your own worship and actions, ensuring your outward deeds match your inner intentions.

title: The Delegation of Thaqif and the Destruction of Al-Lat
summary: In Ramadan of 9 AH, a delegation from the tribe of Thaqif came to the Prophet, made peace, and returned with a security agreement; the idol al-Lat was subsequently destroyed.
story: In Ramadan that year, a delegation came from Thaqif and made peace on behalf of their people, then returned with a security agreement. Also, al-Lat was destroyed, as has been related above.
lessons: Even long-resistant communities eventually open their hearts when the truth is made clear and patient outreach continues.
The removal of false objects of worship is a necessary step toward spiritual clarity and freedom.
Peace agreements built on mutual respect and sincerity can bring lasting benefit to entire communities.
practical_advice: Do not give up on people or situations that seem resistant; persistence and good character can lead to breakthroughs.
Identify what false attachments or 'idols' in your own life — habits, fears, or dependencies — need to be removed for your spiritual growth.
Approach reconciliation and peace-making with sincerity, ensuring agreements are honored on your side.

title: The Death of Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the Chief Hypocrite
summary: Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul, the leader of the hypocrites in Madinah, died at the end of 9 AH, marking the end of a prolonged source of internal strife for the Muslim community.
story: At the end of that year 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy b. Salul, the chief hypocrite, may God curse him, died. A month prior to that occurred the death of Mu'awiya b. Mu'awiya al-Laythi - or al-Muzani. It was he for whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) spoke prayers while he was at Tabuk, if the report about that is true.
lessons: Those who persistently sow discord and hypocrisy within a community ultimately meet their end, and God's plan prevails.
The Prophet's compassion extended even to those who had wronged him, as he prayed for individuals even during the Tabuk expedition.
Patience in the face of internal opposition is a form of strength; communities can endure and outlast those who seek to harm them from within.
practical_advice: When dealing with persistently toxic people in your life, maintain your integrity and trust that their influence will not last forever.
Do not let the negativity of others cause you to abandon your values or reduce your compassion.
Focus your energy on building and strengthening the positive elements of your community rather than being consumed by those who undermine it.

title: The Year of Delegations: Arabs Enter Islam in Droves After the Conquest of Makkah
summary: After the conquest of Makkah, Arab tribes from across the peninsula sent delegations to the Prophet in 9 AH, accepting Islam en masse — a year known as the Year of the Delegations.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had conquered Mecca and completed the expedition to Tabuk, Thaqif accepted Islam and pledged allegiance, and delegations of Arabs came to him from all directions." Ibn Hisham stated, "Abu 'Ubayda related to me that that occurred in the year 9 AH, which was known as the sanat al-wufud, 'the year of the delegations'." Ibn Ishaq continued, "The Arabs had only been holding back their acceptance of Islam to observe what became of this tribe of Quraysh. Because it was Quraysh whom the others considered as their leaders and guides; they had charge of the ka'ba and the sacred area, and the pure line of descent from Isma'il, son of Abraham. And the leading Arabs did not dispute that. "It was Quraysh who had set up the warfare and conflict with the Messenger of God (SAAS). When Mecca was conquered, Quraysh accepted his leadership. Islam having conquered them, the Arabs realized that they did not have the power either to go to war against the Messenger of God (SAAS) or to be antagonistic towards him. And so they entered into God's faith, as God the Almighty and Glorious stated, afwajan, 'in droves', coming to him from every side. "God Almighty stated to His Prophet (SAAS), '(Remember) when God's aid came and the conquest. And you saw people entering in droves into God's religion. And so celebrate in praise of your Lord and seek His forgiveness; He is very ready to forgive'" (surat al-Nasr; cx, v.1-3). That is, give praise to God for having given victory to your religion; and seek His forgiveness, for He is very ready to forgive.
lessons: Divine promises are fulfilled in their appointed time; the Prophet endured years of rejection before witnessing this mass acceptance of truth.
Patience and steadfastness in the face of overwhelming opposition can ultimately lead to outcomes beyond what one imagined.
The conquest of hearts is more significant than the conquest of land; when truth becomes evident, people naturally incline toward it.
practical_advice: When your efforts seem slow to bear fruit, remember that transformation — in people and communities — often happens suddenly after long preparation.
Respond to success with gratitude and humility, as the Quran instructed the Prophet to praise God and seek forgiveness at the moment of victory.
Use moments of triumph as opportunities for reflection and renewed spiritual commitment, not pride or complacency.

title: The First Delegation from Muzayna and the Declaration of Hijra in Their Homes
summary: The first delegation from the Mudar tribes to come to the Prophet was 400 men of Muzayna in Rajab of 5 AH; the Prophet declared their hijra valid in their own homes.
story: Muhammad b. 'Umar al-Waqidi stated, "Kathir b. 'Abd Allah al-Muzali related to us, from his father, from his grandfather, who said, 'The first delegation to come in to the Messenger of God (SAAS) from Mudar were 400 men of Muzayna; this occurred in Rajab of 5 AH. The Messenger of God (SAAS) declared the hijra as valid for them (while staying) in their own homes. He stated, "You are muhajiroon where you were; and so return to your properties." And they went back home.'"
lessons: Islam adapted its requirements to the realities of its followers; spiritual commitment is not confined to physical migration.
True belonging to a faith community is a matter of the heart and intention, not merely of geography.
The Prophet's wisdom in affirming people's status in their own context helped them embrace Islam without unnecessary hardship.
practical_advice: Recognize that spiritual growth does not always require dramatic external changes; you can transform from where you are.
Affirm others in their faith journey without imposing unnecessary conditions or burdens on them.
Understand that your commitment to your values can be expressed fully within your existing circumstances.

title: Khuza'i ibn Abd Nahm Rallies His Tribe to Islam Through Gentle Pressure
summary: Khuza'i of Muzayna pledged allegiance on behalf of his tribe but returned to find them unwilling; Hassan ibn Thabit composed subtle verses that stirred their honor until they accepted Islam.
story: Al-Waqidi then recounted from Hisham b. al-Kalbi, with his chain of transmission, that the first person to arrive from Muzayna was Khuza'i Ibn 'Abd Nahm, accompanied by others of his tribe of Muzayna. He pledged to him the allegiance of his tribe, but when he returned to them he did not find them to be as he had thought; they put it off. The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered Hassan b. Thabit to make negative allusions to Khuza'i without actually satirizing him. He did speak some such verses and when they reached Khuza'i he complained of this to his people and they rallied around him and did accept Islam along with him. He then brought them to the Messenger of God (SAAS). On the day of the conquest (of Mecca) the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave the banner of Muzayna — a force of 1,000 men at that time — to this Khuza'i.
lessons: Rejection after making a commitment on behalf of others is painful, but perseverance can transform that rejection into eventual success.
Creative and non-confrontational approaches can sometimes move people more effectively than direct pressure.
Those who remain steadfast despite initial failure are often rewarded with greater responsibility and honor.
practical_advice: When people do not respond as you hoped after you advocated for them, do not abandon your mission — find creative ways to re-engage.
Use words and communication thoughtfully to awaken a sense of dignity and responsibility in others, rather than shaming or attacking them.
If you feel let down by a group you stood up for, channel that disappointment into renewed effort rather than bitterness.

title: The Delegation of Banu Tamim and the Disagreement Between Abu Bakr and Umar
summary: When the delegation of Banu Tamim arrived, Abu Bakr and Umar disagreed loudly over who should be appointed their leader, leading to the revelation of a Quranic verse about conduct in the Prophet's presence.
story: Al-Bukhari also related this from a different chain of authorities, from Ibn Abu Mulayka, and with different phraseology. We have made reference to this in our Tafsir (Exegesis) regarding the words of the Almighty, "Do not raise your voices above that of the Prophet (SAAS)" (surat al-Hujurat; XLVIII, v.2). Al-Bukhari stated that Ibrahim b. Musa related to him, quoting Hisham b. Yusuf (who said) quoting Ibn Jurayj, from Ibn Abu Mulayka, that 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr informed them that, "Some men of Banu Tamim rode in to the Prophet (SAAS), and Abu Bakr said (to the Prophet (SAAS)), 'Appoint al-Qa'qa' b. Ma'bad b. Zurara as leader.' 'Umar said, 'No! Appoint al-Aqra' b. Habis instead.' Abu Bakr commented, 'You just want to oppose my view.' 'Umar responded, 'I don't just want to oppose you.' They argued, their voices raised. Then there was revealed the verse, 'O you who believe! Do not be forward in the presence of God and His Messenger'" (surat al-Hujurat; XLVIII, v.1).
lessons: Even the closest and most righteous companions of the Prophet were human and could fall into disagreement; what matters is how divine guidance redirects us.
Public disagreements, especially in sacred spaces or in the presence of authority, require humility and self-restraint.
Divine revelation responded to real human situations, showing that guidance comes precisely when and where it is needed.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in a heated disagreement, pause and ask whether the setting and manner of your dispute is appropriate.
Be willing to receive correction gracefully, even when you believe you are right; humility in conflict is a sign of strength.
Reflect on whether your disagreements with others are truly about the matter at hand or about personal ego and the desire to have your view prevail.

title: Banu Tamim's Indifference to Good Tidings Contrasted with Yemen's Acceptance
summary: When the Prophet gave glad tidings to Banu Tamim they responded with ingratitude, but when a Yemeni delegation arrived and accepted the same good news, the Prophet's face brightened.
story: Al-Bukhari, may God forgive him, stated, in a section of his work entitled: Chapter on the Delegation of Banu Tamim "Abu Nu'aym related to us, quoting Sufyan, from Abu Sakhra, from Safwan b. Muhriz al-Mazini, from 'Imran b. Husayn, who said, 'A group of men came in from Banu Tamim to the Prophet (SAAS), and he told them, "Accept the good tidings, Banu Tamim!" They replied, "Messenger of God, you have already given it to us, so give us (something else now)." Displeasure was to be seen in his face. A group from Yemen then arrived, and he told them, "Accept the good tidings, for Banu Tamim did not do so." They replied, "We do accept them, Messenger of God."'"
lessons: Gratitude for blessings opens the heart to receive more; ingratitude and entitlement close it.
The way we receive good news and generosity reflects the state of our character and spiritual readiness.
Simple, sincere acceptance of what is offered can be more valuable than demanding more.
practical_advice: Cultivate genuine gratitude for the blessings, opportunities, and good news you receive rather than immediately seeking more.
Notice when you respond to generosity with entitlement, and consciously shift to an attitude of appreciation.
When someone offers you kindness or encouragement, receive it fully and sincerely before asking for anything else.

title: The Delegation of Banu Tamim: Oratory and Poetry Contest Before the Prophet
summary: A large delegation of Banu Tamim arrived at the mosque and challenged the Prophet's community to a contest of oratory and poetry. After exchanges by both sides, the delegation accepted Islam and received gifts from the Prophet.
story:
God (SAAS), 'Utarid b. Ḥajib b. Zurara b. 'Adas al-Tamimi arrived accompanied by leaders of Banu Tamim. These included al-Aqra' b. Ḥabis al-Tamimi, al-Zibriqan b. Badr al-Tamimi - a man of Banu Sa'd - 'Amr b. al-Ahtam, al-Ḥabḥab b. Yazid, Nu'aym b. Yazid, Qays b. al-Ḥarith, and Qays b. 'Āṣim, a brother of Banu Sa'd, along with a large delegation of Banu Tamim.

"With them was 'Uyayna b. Ḥuṣn b. Ḥudhayfa b. Badr al-Fazari. Al-Aqra' b. Ḥabis and 'Uyayna had been present with the Messenger of God (SAAS) at the conquest of Mecca, and at the battles of Ḥunayn and al-Ta'if. When the delegation of Banu Tamim arrived, these two men were with them.

"When all these men entered the mosque, they called out to the Messenger of God (SAAS), from behind his apartments, saying, 'Come on out to us, Muḥammad!' This shouting of theirs disturbed the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he came to them. They then said, 'Muḥammad, we have come to express our admiration for you; give permission to both our poet and our orator.' He replied, 'I give permission to your orator; he may speak.'

"'Utarid b. Ḥajib then arose and said, 'Praise be to God who provides us His grace and favour, for He is worthy of such praise. It is He who made us kings and awarded us great wealth which we donate to charity. It is He who made us the most powerful men of all the East, the greatest in number and equipment.

"'Who is our equal? Are we not at the leaders of the people, their very best? Any who would challenge us should enumerate their qualities, as we have done. We could, if we wished, speak at greater length, but we are reluctant to speak over much of what He has given us. We are well known for this (reluctance). I say this so that you may speak of similar or even better.' He then sat down.

"The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said to Thabit b. Qays b. Shammas, brother of Banu al-Ḥarith b. al-Khazraj, 'Arise and respond to this man's address.'

"Thabit got up and said, 'Praise be to God, Creator of both the heavens and the earth. In these He expressed His will; His throne encompasses all He knows. Nothing ever existed except by His favour.

"'It was, moreover, by His power that He made kings of us and chose as His Messenger the best of His creation, its most noble in lineage, its most truthful in speech and most respected man. It was to him that He revealed a book, entrusting it to him above all others of His creation. He is the best of God's creation in the worlds.

"'He then called upon the people to believe in him. And the muhajirūn from his tribe did so, along with his relatives, those most noble in line, those best of all in person and deed. It was we who were foremost in responding to God when the Messenger made his call. We are the anṣār "the Helpers", of God and deputies of His Messenger. We will fight people until they believe. Whoever believes in God and His Messenger is also protecting his own possessions and life. Whoever disbelieves, we will battle for God for ever. And executing them will be a minor matter for us. In speaking this, I ask forgiveness for myself and for you all and for all believers. Peace be upon you.'

"Al-Zibriqan b. Badr arose and spoke the following verses: 'It is we who are the nobles; no clan can match us. Kings come from us and among us places of worship are built. Many are the clans we have defeated, extracting plunder from all; and the benefits of might are to be pursued. Even in famines we entertain, our food fine meat stew, even when no trace of a rain-cloud is visible. And so you see chieftains arrive from all around, hurrying for us to serve them a banquet. We slaughter large, fat, young camels for our guests; when they are accommodated, they are well satisfied. You know that every tribe we challenge submits and becomes as though leaderless. And whoever may challenge us, we know what will happen; his people will retreat and that news will spread abroad. We may decline, but no one declines us; and so it is that we rise in pride.'

Ibn Isḥaq went on, "Since Ḥassān b. Thabit was absent, the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent for him. He (Ḥassān later) said, 'When I reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), and their poet spoke as above, I challenged what he had said and spoke in the same manner as he had.'

"When al-Zibriqan had finished speaking, the Messenger of God (SAAS) told Ḥassān b. Thabit, 'Get up and respond to the man.'

"Ḥassān then recited, 'The chieftains of Fihr and their brothers have clarified a way of life for the people that should be followed Such as to please any man whose inner purpose is to fear God and who desires to do good, A people who, when they fight, destroy their enemy, or, if seeking advantage to their allies, achieve this. This is their natural disposition, no acquired trait; the worst of characteristics - you well know - are those innovated. Even if, after they are gone, others surpass them, (it is true that) anyone who leads still follows those who preceded him. Men cannot mend what their own hands have ruined in battle, nor could others ruin what they have mended. When they seek to lead, their effort succeeds; if compared in generosity to other nobles, they tip the scales. These are fine men, their purity mentioned in the revelation; no materialists, these, untainted by greed. From their generosity to neighbours, they are no misers and they are untouched by any taint of ambition. When we attack people, we do not crawl as does a wild calf to its mother; We get right up when the talons of war attain us, though the riff-raff submit to its claws. They are not boastful when they attain their enemy; and if overcome, they do not turn feeble or despair. When engaged in battle, when death is close by, they are like lions of Ḥalya, their claws bent and ready. If they are angry, take whatever you can get submissively, and don't worry after what they are refusing. Do not seek their enmity, for in going to war with them you will have evil laced with poison. Honour a people in whose party is the Messenger of God, at a time when ideas and sects are very diverse. My heart awards them my praise, helped on in its favoured task by a creative and inventive tongue. They are the best of all peoples, whether we talk of what is serious or what is light.'"

Ibn Hisham stated, "A scholar of the poetry of Banu Tamim told me that when al-Zibriqan came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), with the delegation of Banu Tamim, he arose and spoke the following verses: 'We have come to you so that people may know our virtue when they mingle in attendance at the fairs, And that we are the finest in any place, in all the land of Ḥijaz, none the like of Darim. We put the medalled champions to flight when they boast, and strike the heads of warriors seeking fame. To us goes the one-fourth share of every expedition we fight, whether in Najd or abroad.'

"Ḥassān then arose and responded to him as follows, 'Is glory anything but repeated mastery and magnanimity, the glory of kings and the bearing of great responsibility? We give aid and refuge to the Prophet, Muḥammad, regardless of the resentment or pleasure of Ma'ad. In a unique tribe whose origins and wealth stem from a place in Jabiyyat al-Julan, amidst foreigners. We gave aid when he came to live among us, using our swords against all the tyrants and aggressors. We placed our sons and our daughters between us and him, and put him at ease regarding the spoils gained. We strike at men with our fine, sharp swords until they follow into his faith. It was we who fathered the greatest man of Quraysh, we who fathered the Prophet of good from Hashim's line. Banu Darim, do not boast; your boasting seems shameful when noble deeds receive mention. You are fools! Do you pride yourselves over us, when you are our vassals, somewhere between wet-nurses and slaves? If you have come to us to save your blood and property, from being distributed as booty, Then consider none a peer of God and accept Islam; and do not dress yourselves in such garb as foreigners wear.'"
lessons: Truth and sincerity ultimately prevail over mere boasting and tribal pride, as the Companions responded to arrogance with grounded, principled eloquence.
The Prophet's patience and wisdom in responding to provocative behavior — such as being called out loudly from behind his apartments — models graceful composure under social pressure.
Genuine virtue does not need to be loudly proclaimed; when the Ansar's spokesperson spoke with humility and truth, it was more compelling than the boastful speeches of the visitors.
practical_advice: When you feel the urge to prove your worth in a competitive or confrontational situation, pause and ground your response in your values rather than matching the other person's boastfulness.
If someone challenges or belittles you publicly, seek to respond with calm dignity rather than escalating; let the quality of your character speak louder than your words.

Remember that accepting truth, even when it comes from a perceived rival, is a sign of strength — just as al-Aqra' b. Ḥabis acknowledged the superiority of the Muslim orator and poet and ultimately accepted Islam.

title: Qays b. 'Asim Belittles 'Amr b. al-Ahtam Before the Prophet, Who Honors the Young Man Anyway
summary: Qays b. 'Asim attempted to diminish young 'Amr b. al-Ahtam in the Prophet's eyes, but the Prophet gave 'Amr the same gifts as the others. 'Amr then responded to Qays's slander with sharp satirical verses.
story:
Finally they accepted Islam and the Messenger of God (SAAS) presented valuable gifts to them. They had left 'Amr b. al-Ahtam behind with their mounts, he being their youngest. Qays b. 'Āṣim, who disliked 'Amr b. al-Ahtam, said, 'Messenger of God, we do still have one man, a young fellow, out with our mounts.' And he went on to belittle him. However, the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave to 'Amr what he had given to the others.

'Amr b. al-Ahtam recited the following satirical verses when he learned what Qays had said; 'You hurt me, you lazy, hairy dog, by slandering me to the Messenger; you were not truthful or accurate We ruled you in broad authority, but your leadership merely consists of sitting on your tail and displaying your molars!'
lessons: The Prophet's fairness and justice ensured that even the youngest and most overlooked member of a group received equal treatment, teaching us that every person has inherent worth regardless of status or age.
Envy and personal dislike can cause people to publicly undermine others, but God's justice ensures that such attempts do not ultimately harm the person being wronged.
When we are slandered or belittled by others, we can take heart knowing that those in authority who are just and wise will see through unfair characterizations.
practical_advice: If someone undermines you to a person in authority, trust that your true character and efforts will speak for themselves rather than retaliating in kind.
When you feel the sting of being belittled or passed over because of your age or perceived inexperience, remember that genuine merit is recognized by those with wisdom and fairness.

Channel feelings of anger at injustice into constructive expression rather than destructive retaliation, finding healthy outlets such as writing, speaking your truth, or seeking counsel from someone you trust.

title: Al-Zibriqan's Self-Praise Contradicted by 'Amr b. al-Ahtam Before the Prophet
summary: When asked about al-Zibriqan, 'Amr b. al-Ahtam first praised him moderately, then — when al-Zibriqan complained of being undervalued — reversed his assessment and harshly criticized him, prompting the Prophet to observe that oratory carries its own power.
story:
The Ḥāfiẓ al-Bayhaqi related through Ya'qub b. Sufyan, quoting Sulayman b. Ḥarb, quoting Ḥammad b. Zayd, from Muḥammad b. al-Zubayr al-Ḥanẓali, who said, "Al-Zibriqan b. Badr came in the company of Qays b. 'Āṣim and 'Amr b. al-Ahtam to see the Messenger of God (SAAS). The latter asked 'Amr b. al-Ahtam, 'Tell me about al-Zibriqan; I won't ask you about this other man.'

"He thus indicated that he already knew of Qays. 'Amr responded, 'He is obeyed by those closest to him, a good orator and very defensive of those in his care.'

"Al-Zibriqan commented, 'He has spoken very well, knowing me to be better than what he has said!'

"'Amr responded, 'I swear by God, all I know of you is that you are of little manliness, narrow-minded, that your father was a fool and your uncle a rogue!' He then went on, 'Messenger of God, I spoke the truth in both my characterizations of him. When he treated me well, I said of him the best I could, and when he angered me, I said the worst I know!'

"The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'Oratory has its own magic.'"
lessons: The power of words is immense — they can build or destroy a person's reputation in moments, which is why the Prophet recognized that oratory itself carries a kind of enchantment.
Greed for praise and unwillingness to accept a fair, balanced assessment of oneself can backfire severely, as al-Zibriqan's demand for more praise led to harsher criticism instead.

Emotional reactions in the heat of the moment can lead us to say things we might not say in a calmer state; 'Amr's candid admission that his words changed with his mood is a warning about allowing feelings to govern our speech.
practical_advice: Be mindful of the words you speak about others, especially in moments of anger, because once spoken they cannot be unsaid and may cause lasting harm to relationships and reputations.
When someone offers you a fair and balanced assessment of your character, receive it with humility rather than pressing for more flattery, which often produces the opposite result.

If you notice that your emotional state is shaping what you say about someone — whether too positively or too negatively — take a moment to pause and ask whether your words reflect the truth or merely your feelings in that moment.

title: The Delegation of Banu Tamim and the Boasting of Al-Zibriqan
summary: Al-Zibriqan b. Badr boasts of his status before the Prophet, prompting a contradictory response from 'Amr b. al-Ahtam, who first praises then insults him. The Prophet observes that 'oratory has its own magic.'
story: Qitys b. 'A~im, al-Zibriqan b. Badr and 'Amr b. al-Ahtam, all of Banu Tamjm, sat down with the Messenger of God (SAAS). AI-Zibriqan set about boasting, saying, "I am lord of Tamlm, the one they fully obey and respect. It is 1 who defend them from aggression and ensure their rights. This man well knows that." This comment referred to 'Amr b. aI-Ahtam, who responded, "He is a good orator, protective of his own family, and obeyed by those close to him." 'A1-Zibriqan commented, "I swear by God, Messenger of God, he knows of me other than what he has just said. It is merely his envy that prevents him speaking it." 'Amr b. Ahram asked, "What? Me envy you! 1 swear by God, your uncle is a rogne, only recently wealthy, your child is a fool, and you're a nobody in the tribe! 1 swear by God, Messenger of God, 1 spoke the truth in what 1 first said and I've not lied in what 1 just said. However, 1 am a man who will speak the best he can if pleased, but the worst he can when angered. But 1 still tell the truth, both the former and the latter." The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, "Oratory has its own magic."
lessons: Words spoken in anger can contradict and undo words spoken in goodwill, reminding us to guard our tongues even when provoked.
Boasting and seeking status before others often invites conflict rather than the respect one desires.
The Prophet's calm, brief observation rather than lengthy condemnation teaches us that wisdom sometimes speaks in few words.
practical_advice: Before speaking about your own achievements, pause and ask whether your words build community or create division.
When you feel the urge to respond harshly to someone who has angered you, write your words down first rather than speaking them immediately.
If someone boasts in your presence, resist the pull to either falsely validate them or tear them down — a measured, honest response reflects better character.

title: The Prophet's Praise of Banu Tamim
summary: Abu Hurayra relates three statements the Prophet made in praise of Banu Tamim, which inspired lasting love for that tribe in Abu Hurayra's heart.
story: Zuhayr b. I;Iarb related to us, quotingJarJr, from 'Umara b. al-Qt'qa', from Abu Zur'a, from Abu Hurayra, who said, "I have always loved Banu Tamim since I heard three things that the Messenger of God ($AAS) said about them. These were, 'They are the most severe of my people against al-Dajjal, "the imposter", "Satan". 'A'isha had one of them as a slave-girl, and of her he said, 'Free her, for she is of the progeny of Isma'il.' And when their charitable donations arrived, he said, 'These donations are from a people - my people, that is.'''
lessons: Love and respect for others can be built on witnessing their virtues through the eyes of those we trust, such as the Prophet.
Gratitude and recognition of the good in a community — even an imperfect one — is a mark of generous character.
A single kind word from a person of wisdom and authority can shape how an entire community is perceived for generations.
practical_advice: Look for three genuine positive qualities in a person or group you have previously dismissed or judged harshly.
When you hear someone speak well of others, let it soften any preconceptions you hold rather than defending your prior judgment.
Practice expressing sincere appreciation for the contributions of those around you, even in small ways — words of recognition have lasting impact.

title: The Delegation of Banu 'Abd al-Qays and the Four Commandments
summary: A delegation from Banu 'Abd al-Qays traveled through dangerous polytheist territory to reach the Prophet and asked for guidance that would ensure their entry to paradise. The Prophet gave them four commandments to follow and four prohibitions to observe.
story: Abu Is!}aq related to us, quoting Abu 'Amir al-'Aqdi, quoting QUITa, from Abu Jamra, who said, 'I told Ibn 'Abbas, "I have a jar that produces wine for me that I drink when it is sweet. IfI drink much of it and then sit down among the people, I fear I may be discovered." He respouded, "A delegation of Banu 'Abd Qiys came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he told them, 'Welcome! You will suffer no disgrace or regret!' They replied, 'Messeuger of God, in the territory between you and ourselves there are the polytheists of Banu Mugar and so we can only reach you duriug the sacred months. Could you kindly tell us of the actions in which we can engage to ensure our entry into paradise? We will tell those of us back home the same.' He replied, 'I order you to accomplish four things and to avoid four things. Faith in God. Do you know what faith in God means? It means to speak the shahada, "to say La Ilaha illa Allah wa Mubammad rasii! Allah, 'there is uo god but God and Mu!}ammad is the Messenger of God'". Also, perform the prayers, pay the zakat, "alms tax", fast for the month of Ramagan, aud give over the khumus, "one-fifth of the spoils". And I forbid you four things: the wine that is made in al-Dubba', iu al-Naqir, ai-Bantam and al-Muzaffat.'"
lessons: When access to guidance is limited by circumstance or danger, the determination to seek it anyway reflects true sincerity of faith.
The Prophet welcomed this delegation with warmth and reassurance before delivering any instruction, modeling how guidance is best received when the recipient feels safe and respected.
Clear, actionable guidance — knowing both what to do and what to avoid — is a mercy that reduces confusion and empowers people to act with confidence.
practical_advice: Identify the spiritual practices in your life that feel most foundational and make a concrete commitment to maintain them even when circumstances are difficult.
When you are struggling to access support or guidance, take what steps you can during the openings available to you rather than waiting for perfect conditions.
Share what you learn with those in your community who cannot access the same resources — passing on beneficial knowledge multiplies its good.

title: Ashajj 'Abd al-Qays Is Praised for Discernment and Perseverance
summary: The Prophet praised Ashajj 'Abd al-Qays for two qualities — discernment and perseverance — telling him these were gifts from God. Ashajj responded with humble gratitude.
story: In his (Muslim's) account, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said to Ashajj 'Abd al--Q;tys, "You have two qualities that God, the Almighty and Glorious, much likes: discernment and perseverance." In one version, the words are "that God and His Messenger much like". Ashajj asked, "Messenger of God, was it I who developed these qualities, or God who brought me to them?" He replied, "It was God who brought you to them." Ashajj then said, "Then praise be to God who brought me to two qualities that God and His Messenger much like!" Imam Al)mad stated that Abu Sa'Id, the freed-man of Banu Hashim related to him, quoting Matar b. 'Abd al-Ral:tman, who said that he heard Hind, daughter of al-Wazi' report that she heard al-Wazi' say, "I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) in the company of al-Ashajj al-Mundhir b. 'Amir" - "or 'Amir b. al-Mundhir. With their party there was a man who was much aftlicted. Having reached and seen the Messenger of God (SAAS), they jumped down from their mounts. Then they went up to him and kissed his hand. AI-Ashajj also dismounted, hobbled his camel, took out and opened his baggage pack and extracted from it two white garments which he put on. He then went over to the other camels, hobbled them too and proceeded on to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who asked him, 'Ashajj, you have two qualities both God the Almighty and Glorious and His Messenger very much like discernment and perseverance.' AI-Ashajj responded, 'Messenger of God, is it I who developed these, or did God bring them to me?' He replied, 'No, it was God who brought them to you.' AI-Ashajj commented, 'Praise be to God who brought to me two qualities that God the Almighty and Glorious and His Messenger both much like!'"
lessons: Our best qualities are ultimately gifts from God, and recognizing this transforms personal pride into humble gratitude.
Discernment and perseverance are among the most spiritually valued traits a person can possess, worth cultivating intentionally.
When we are affirmed for our good qualities, the ideal response is not self-congratulation but redirection of praise to the One who shaped us.
practical_advice: Reflect on a positive quality others recognize in you and trace it back to the moments, experiences, or divine gifts that shaped it rather than claiming sole credit.
Make a habit of pausing before reacting to difficult situations — ask yourself what discernment and perseverance would look like in this moment.
When you praise someone's character, consider also telling them that such qualities are gifts worth being grateful for, not just proud of.

title: Al-Wazi's Afflicted Companion Is Healed by the Prophet
summary: Al-Wazi' traveled with the delegation to meet the Prophet and brought along an uncle who was seriously afflicted. After the Prophet struck the man on the back and commanded the affliction to leave, the man was restored to full health.
story: "AI-Wazi' then said, 'Messenger of God, I have with me an uncle who is much affiicted. Please pray to God for him.' 'Where is he?' he asked. 'Bring him to me.' AI-Wazi' went on, 'I then did as al-Ashajj had done: I dressed him in two white garments and took him over to him.
