He took hold of his outer garment and lifted up both sides so that we could see the white of his armpits. He then struck the man on his back, saying, "Get out, you enemy of God!" The man then turned around and he was seeing just as does a healthy man.'"
lessons: Bringing our suffering and the suffering of those we love before God and His Prophet reflects reliance on the ultimate Healer rather than despair.
Preparation and proper conduct before seeking help — as shown by dressing the afflicted man respectfully — reflects that how we approach a request matters.
Healing and relief can come suddenly and completely when God wills it, offering hope to those who feel their suffering is permanent.
practical_advice: When you feel helpless in the face of a loved one's suffering, take the concrete step of seeking the best available help rather than remaining passive in grief.
Prepare yourself properly — spiritually and practically — before bringing a serious concern or request before anyone you hope can help you.
Hold onto hope that suffering, no matter how severe or prolonged, is not necessarily permanent; make du'a with the conviction that God is fully capable of complete restoration.

title: The Delegation of Banu Abd al-Qays and al-Ashajj's Praiseworthy Qualities
summary: A delegation of 13 riders from Banu Abd al-Qays arrives in Madinah after the Prophet foretold their coming. Al-Ashajj, who calmly tended to the camels before greeting the Prophet, is praised for possessing two naturally God-given qualities that God and His Messenger love.
story: The Hafiz al-Bayhaqi narrated through Hud b. 'Abd Allah b. Sa'd, that the latter heard his grandfather Muzida al-'Abdi say, "While the Messenger of God (SAAS) was engaged in speaking to his Companions, he told them, 'From over there riders are coming who are the best of the people of the East.' "'Umar arose and went out towards them and met 13 riders. When he asked who they were, they replied, 'We're from Banu 'Abd al-Qays.' 'And what brings you to these parts; business?' 'No,' they replied. 'Well,' 'Umar commented, 'The Prophet (SAAS) just made mention of you and spoke highly of you.' "They proceeded in with him to the Prophet (SAAS), and 'Umar told them, 'This is your master you seek.' The riders threw themselves off their mounts and some walked while others jogged and ran towards him. When they reached him, they took his hand and kissed it. Al-Ashajj remained back until he had hobbled the camels and gathered some of the baggage of the group. He then walked over and took the hand of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and kissed it. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, 'You have two qualities that both God and His Messenger much like.' He asked, 'And is that a natural disposition created within me or something I myself have brought about?' 'It is a natural disposition,' he replied. Al-Ashajj commented, 'Then praise be to God who has created within me what both God and His Messenger much like!'''
lessons: Calm deliberateness and fulfilling responsibilities before rushing to seek reward reflects true character, not indifference.
God-given qualities of patience and forbearance are gifts to be recognized and celebrated with gratitude rather than pride.
The Prophet's ability to foretell good tidings about people demonstrates that honoring and speaking well of others is a prophetic virtue.
practical_advice: When you feel overlooked for not being the most enthusiastic in a group, remember that quiet, responsible action often reflects the deepest character.
Reflect on the natural qualities you possess — patience, calm, deliberateness — and instead of taking them for granted, thank God for them specifically.
When someone praises a quality in you, respond with gratitude toward God rather than self-congratulation, which keeps the heart grounded.

title: Al-Jarid b. Amr Accepts Islam and Remains Firm During the Wars of Apostasy
summary: Al-Jarid, a Christian leader from Banu Abd al-Qays, accepts Islam after the Prophet guarantees him a better path, and later stands firm in his faith during the Wars of Apostasy when his people begin to revert.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Al-Jarid b. 'Amr b. Hanash, a brother of Banu 'Abd al-Qays arrived to visit the Messenger of God (SAAS)." Ibn Hisham stated, "He was al-Jarid b. Bishr b. al-Mu'alla, accompanying a delegation of 'Abd Qays; he was a Christian." Ibn Ishaq continued, "Someone I trust related to me that al-Hasan said, 'When we reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), the latter explained Islam to him, invited him to accept it, and made him very enthusiastic about it. Al-Jarid told him, "Muhammad, I used to follow a faith, but now I am leaving it for yours. Do you guarantee to me your faith?" He replied, "I do indeed guarantee to you that God has led you now to something better than it." And so al-Jarid accepted Islam, as did his companions.' "He then asked the Messenger of God (SAAS) for transport, but he replied, "I swear, I've nothing on which to mount you." Al-Jarid then asked, "Messenger of God, between here and our home there are stray mounts that have wandered away from their owners. May we use them to reach home?" "No," he replied, "beware of them. They would mean the fires of hell." "Al-Jarid left to return to his own people; he was a good Muslim and firm in his faith until he died. He lived on into the ridda, the wars of apostacy. When some of his people reverted to their former religions along with al-Gharur b. al-Mundhir b. al-Nu'man b. al-Mundhir, al-Jarid arose and gave testimony to the true faith and called for them to join Islam. He said, "O people, I bear witness that there is no god but God and that Muhammad is His servant and His Messenger. I consider unbelievers all who do not bear witness."'''
lessons: True faith is proven not at the moment of conversion but in times of communal pressure and social collapse around us.
Standing publicly for truth when those around you abandon it requires a depth of conviction that is built steadily during times of stability.
The Prophet's refusal to allow use of stray animals, even in hardship, teaches that integrity in small matters builds the character needed for great trials.
practical_advice: When people around you are abandoning their values or faith, draw strength from the clarity of your own conviction rather than measuring yourself by the crowd.
Practice speaking your beliefs openly and calmly in low-stakes situations so that when high-stakes moments come, you are prepared.
When facing a difficult journey or hardship, resist the temptation to take shortcuts that compromise your integrity, even when they seem harmless.

title: Thumama b. Uthal: From Enemy to Devoted Muslim
summary: Thumama b. Uthal, a leader of Banu Hanifa who was captured and tied to a mosque pillar, is repeatedly questioned by the Prophet and then released unconditionally, after which he embraces Islam and declares his complete change of heart.
story: Al-Bukhari stated, "'Abd Allah b. Yusuf related to us, quoting al-Layth b. Sa'd, quoting Sa'id b. Abu Sa'id, who heard Abu Hurayra say, 'The Prophet (SAAS) dispatched some cavalry to Najd. They brought back a man of Banu Hanifa named Thumama b. Uthal. They bound him to one of the pillars of the mosque. The Messenger of God (SAAS) went to him and asked, "Well, Thumama, what do you have (in mind I should do with you)?" He replied, "Muhammad, I have (a) good (idea). If you kill me, you'll (only) kill a man who has killed others. But if you act kindly, you will be doing so to someone who will be grateful. If it's money you want, just ask for it." "He left him there till the next day, when again he asked, "Well, Thumama, what do you have (in mind)?" "I have what I told you; if you act kindly, you'll do so to a man who will be grateful." Again he left him, until the following day, when he again asked, "What do you have (in mind), Thumama?" "Just as I told you before," he replied. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said "Release Thumama." "At that the man hurried over to a garden of date-palms near the mosque, washed himself and entered the mosque. He then announced, "I testify that there is no god but God and that Muhammad is the Messenger of God! Muhammad, I swear, formerly there was no one on earth I hated more than you. Now I love you best of all men. And, I swear, there was no faith more hateful to me than yours. Now your faith has become the one I love best. Moreover, there used to be no land more hated by me than your land; now your land has become the one I love best. Your cavalry captured me while I was on my way to make the al-'umra pilgrimage; what do you think (about it now)?" "The Messenger of God (SAAS) congratulated him and told him to proceed on the 'umra. When Thumama reached Mecca, someone asked him, "Have you adopted the Sabian faith, then?" He responded, "No, but I have accepted Islam with Muhammad (SAAS). And I swear, you'll not receive a single grain of wheat from Yamama until he gives his permission for that!"'''
lessons: Unconditional kindness and release, offered without demand for reciprocity, can transform even the deepest enmity into profound love.
A person's heart can change completely when they experience genuine mercy; the best da'wah is often simply dignified, patient treatment.
Gratitude that is genuinely felt transforms one's entire relationship to a person, a faith, and a place — it reshapes our inner world.
practical_advice: When someone wrongs you, consider whether releasing them from obligation — rather than demanding an apology — might open a door that force never could.
If you feel distant from your faith or community, reflect on whether you have truly experienced its mercy and kindness firsthand, as sometimes distance is simply unfamiliarity.
When your feelings about something change completely, take time to articulate that change honestly and specifically, as Thumama did — it deepens the transformation and makes it real.

title: Musaylima the Imposter Arrives in Madinah and the Prophet's Dream of Two Gold Bracelets
summary: Musaylima, the false prophet from Yamama, arrives in Madinah claiming he will follow the Prophet if given authority after him. The Prophet firmly rebukes him and recounts a dream of two gold bracelets representing the two great imposters who would arise after him.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that it was related to him by Abu al-Yaman, quoting Shu'ayb, from 'Abd Allah b. Abu Husayn, quoting Nafi' b. Jubayr, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "During the lifetime of the Messenger of God (SAAS), Musaylima, 'the imposter', arrived and began saying, 'If Muhammad appoints me to rule after him, I will follow him.' He had arrived in the company of a great number of his people. "The Messenger of God (SAAS), holding a stick of date-palm wood in his hand, went out to him, accompanied by Thabit b. Qays. Coming to a stop near Musaylima and his men, the Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, 'If you had asked me for it, I'd not even give you this stick! You will not avoid God's order concerning you. If you do not turn away, God will destroy you. I believe you to be the person I saw in a dream. This man is Thabit, and he will answer your questions about me.' The Prophet (SAAS) then left him. "Ibn 'Abbas stated, 'I asked about the statement of the Messenger of God (SAAS), "I believe you to be the person I saw in a dream." Abu Hurayra told me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "While I slept, I saw on my hand two gold bracelets. This disturbed me. Then I received revelation, in my sleep, that I should blow upon them. I did so, and they disappeared. I interpreted them to be representing two imposters who would emerge after me, one of them being al-Aswad al-Ansi, the other Musaylima."''' Al-Bukhari went on to state that Ishaq b. Nasr related to him, quoting 'Abd al-Razzaq, who said, "Ma'mar informed me that Hammam b. Munabbih told him that he heard Abu Hurayra say that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'While I slept, I was brought treasures from the earth and two gold bracelets were placed on my palm. They disturbed me, and revelation came to me that I should blow upon them. I did so, and they disappeared. I interpreted them as the two imposters between whom I am — the ruler of San'a' and the ruler of al-Yamama.'''
lessons: Those who seek power and status as conditions for following truth reveal that their goal was never truth itself but self-elevation.
The Prophet's calm, direct, and brief response to Musaylima — offering him nothing and warning him clearly — models how to confront manipulation without being drawn into it.
Prophetic dreams and divine foresight remind us that God's plan encompasses even trials and deceptions, and that those who spread falsehood will ultimately face their reckoning.
practical_advice: When someone approaches you with conditional loyalty — 'I will support you if you give me this' — recognize that as a red flag rather than an opportunity to negotiate.
When you feel disturbed by something troubling you have seen or sensed, don't suppress it; reflect on it prayerfully and seek understanding, as the Prophet did with his dream.

If you are ever in a position of leadership or trust and someone tries to pressure or manipulate you, respond with firm clarity and brevity rather than lengthy engagement, which only rewards the manipulation.

title: Musaylima the Imposter Meets the Prophet in Medina
summary: Musaylima, the self-proclaimed false prophet of Banu Hanifa, visited Medina and boldly demanded that the Prophet make him his successor, only to be firmly refused even a palm stick.
story: is given elsewhere as 'Abd Allah ~ who said that 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Utba said, "We learned that Musaylima, 'the imposter', had arrived in Medina and was staying in the home of the daughter of aI-I;Iarith. His wife was the daughter of al-I;Iarith b. Kurayz, and she was the mother of 'Abd Allah b. 'Amir b. Kurayz. The Messenger of God (SAAS) went to him, accompanied by Thabit b. Q;iys b. Shammas; it was he who was known as the orator of the Messenger of God (SAAS). The latter had a stick in his hand. He stood before Musaylima and spoke to him and Musaylima told him, 'If you wish, we will leave the way clear for you to rule, provided that you make me_your successor.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, 'If you were to ask me for this stick, I'd not give it to you. I believe you are the one about whom I had a dream. This is Thabit b. Q;iys; he will answer any questions on my behalf.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then left."
lessons: Truth cannot be bargained away — the Prophet's refusal to grant even a stick to Musaylima shows that principles are not negotiable under pressure or threat.
Recognising deception early is a form of wisdom; the Prophet identified Musaylima as the figure from his dream and acted with clarity rather than hesitation.
Walking away from a confrontation with falsehood, rather than prolonging it, can itself be a powerful and dignified response.
practical_advice: When someone pressures you to compromise your core values for worldly gain, pause and ask whether the concession truly costs 'only a little' — small concessions to falsehood often open large doors to harm.
If you sense that a situation or person is dishonest, trust that instinct and disengage calmly rather than engaging in prolonged argument.
Bring a trusted, articulate friend when facing difficult confrontations so you have moral support and a witness to what is said.

title: The Prophet's Dream of Two Gold Bracelets Foretelling the Two Imposters
summary: The Prophet recounted a dream in which two gold bracelets placed on his hands disturbed him, and when he blew on them they vanished — interpreted as a prophecy of two false prophets, al-Aswad al-Ansi and Musaylima.
story: 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Abd Allah continued, "I asked Ibn 'Abbas about the vision he had mentioned. Ibn 'Abbas replied, 'I was told that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "While I slept, I was given a vision in which two gold bracelets were placed on my hand. I was disturbed by these and disliked them. Then I was given permission to blow on them and when I did so, they disappeared. I interpreted them to represent two imposters who would appear."'" 'Ubayd Allah stated, "One of these was aI-'Ansi, who was killed by Fayruz in Yemen; the other was Musaylima, 'the imposter'."
lessons: Prophetic insight and divine guidance can prepare a person for trials before they arrive, reducing shock and enabling a measured response.
Discomfort and unease in a dream or intuition can sometimes be a signal to pay attention, not a reason to despair.
Every falsehood, no matter how threatening it appears, is ultimately temporary — the bracelets disappeared with a single breath, symbolising the fleeting nature of deception.
practical_advice: When you feel a sense of unease about a situation or person that you cannot fully explain, take time to reflect rather than dismiss the feeling outright.
Use moments of anxiety or foreboding as prompts to prepare practically and spiritually, rather than letting worry paralyse you.
Remind yourself in difficult times that falsehood, by its nature, cannot endure — this perspective can help you remain patient when deception seems to be winning.

title: The Delegation of Banu Hanifa and Musaylima's Hidden Presence
summary: The delegation of Banu Hanifa came to the Prophet, concealing Musaylima among their baggage, and when he made demands the Prophet firmly refused him even a palm frond.
story: Mu1).ammad b. Is1).aq stated, "The delegation of Banu I;Ianifa came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and with them was Musaylirna b. I;Iabib, 'the imposter'." Hisham stated, "His name was Musaylima b. Thumama b. Kathir b. I;Iabib b. al-I;Iarith b. 'Abd aI-I;Iarith b. Haffan b. Dhahl b. aI-DIIl b. I;Ianifa; he was known as Abu Thumarna, and also as Abu Harun. He was also referred to as 'al-Ra1).man' and as 'RaJ.unan of aI-Yamama'. He was 150 years old at the time of his execution. He knew various forms of magic. He knew how to insert an egg into a long-necked bottle and was the first to do this. He would cut off a bird's wing, then reattach it. It is said that there was a gazelle that would come down from the mountain and he would drink its milk." Ibn Is1).aq went on, "They went into residence in the horne of the daughter of a1-l;Iarith, an an~ari woman of Banu ai-Najjar." Al-Suhayli stated, "She was Zaynab; some say her name was Kaysa, daughter of al-l;Iarith b. Kurayz b. l;Iabjb b. 'Abd Shams. Musaylima had married her long before, but then separated from her. This is why they stayed in her horne." Ibn Is1).aq continued, "One of our scholars of Medina related to me that Banu l;Ianjfa hid him in some clothing and brought him to the Messenger of God (SAAS), while the latter was sitting among his Companions, holding a palm frond. When he reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), having been completely concealed by the clothes, and began to make requests, the Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, 'Ifyou were to ask me for this palm frond, I'd not give it to you!'"
lessons: Those who resort to concealment and deception to gain power reveal, by their very secrecy, that they know their cause cannot withstand open scrutiny.
The Prophet's consistent refusal — whether of a stick or a palm frond — demonstrates that firm, principled boundaries do not waver based on how a request is packaged.
Worldly prestige, age, or unusual abilities do not qualify a person for spiritual authority; character and truth are the true measures.
practical_advice: Be cautious of requests that come wrapped in layers of distraction or social pressure — ask yourself what is actually being asked of you beneath the surface.
Practise saying a clear, calm 'no' to unreasonable demands without lengthy justification; a brief refusal is often more powerful than an elaborate one.
When evaluating someone's credibility or authority, look past impressive appearances or skills and ask whether their character and conduct align with their claims.

title: Musaylima's Apostasy and False Prophethood After Returning to Yamama
summary: After receiving the Prophet's generosity, Musaylima returned to Yamama, apostatized, proclaimed himself a prophet, distorted the Prophet's words to claim partnership, and produced blasphemous parodies of the Quran while making unlawful things permissible.
story: "A sheikh of Banu l;Ianjfa from a1-Yamama related to me that the incident concerillng him was quite different. He claimed that the delegation from Banu l;Ianjfa came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), having left Musaylima with their mounts. Having accepted Islam, they remembered him and asked, 'Messenger of God, we have left a companion of ours with our mounts and baggage to care for them for us.' "And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered that what had been given to the men of Banu l;Ianjfa should be given to him too, saying, 'His status is no worse than yours.' That is, due to his having looked after his companions' property. That is what the Messenger of God (SAAS) wanted to imply. "They then all left and took to Musaylima what the Messenger of God (SAAS) had given to each one of them. When they reached Yamama that enemy of God apostatized, proclaimed himself a prophet and told them many lies. He said, 'I have been made his partner in this matter.' "He then told his delegation, who were with him, 'Did he not say to you, "His status is no worse than yours?" The only reason he said this was from his knowledge that I had been made his partner in the matter.' "Thereafter he began to recite doggerel verse to them and to imitate the Qur'an, saying, 'God has bestowed His favour on the pregnant woman; he has extracted from her a being that moves, from between the skin on her womb and her intestines.' "He made wine and fornication permissible for them and abolished the need for prayer. He nevertheless testified for the Messenger of God, God's peace and blessings be upon him, being a prophet. And Banu l;Ianjfa was in agreement with him on that."
lessons: Generosity and good faith can be twisted by those with corrupt intentions — this does not mean generosity was wrong, but it calls for wisdom in whom we trust.
Falsehood often imitates truth closely enough to mislead those who are not grounded; Musaylima's parody of the Quran succeeded because people were not deeply rooted in the real thing.
Communities that abandon their obligations and follow those who make the unlawful permissible are on a path toward collective ruin, no matter how compelling the leader seems.
practical_advice: Ground yourself in authentic sources of knowledge so that imitations and distortions are more easily recognisable to you.
Be alert to leaders or influencers who frame their message as an extension of a respected authority — verify claims independently rather than accepting them on that basis alone.
When someone in your life responds to your kindness with manipulation, recognise that their behaviour reflects their own character, not the unwisdom of your generosity.

title: Al-Rahhal ibn Unfuwa's Apostasy and the Prophet's Prophecy About Him
summary: Al-Rahhal ibn Unfuwa, a companion who had learned the Quran, apostatized to join Musaylima and falsely testified on his behalf, fulfilling the Prophet's ominous warning that one among his companions had his molar in hell-fire like Mount Uhud.
story: Al-Suhayli and others related the following about al-Ra1).1).al b. 'Unfuwa - his other name being Nahar b. 'Unfuwa; he had accepted Islam and learned some of the Qur'an and spent a period as a Companion of the Messenger of God (SAAS). The latter once passed by while al-Ra1).1).al was seated with Abu Hurayra and commented, "One of you has his molar in hell-fire like Mt. U1).ud!" Both men remained apprehensive until eventually al-Ra!Jl:;tal apostatized with Musaylima. He falsely testified that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had made Musaylima his partner in his activities and attributed to the latter some part of the Qur'an he had learned; this Musaylima claimed for himself. All this resulted in great confusion for Banu I:Iamta. AI-Ral:;tl:;tal was killed by Zayd b. al-Khanab at the battle of al-Yamiima.
lessons: Knowledge of scripture without sincere commitment to truth can become a tool for great harm; learning the Quran without internalising its values did not protect al-Rahhal from apostasy.
A person's final choices define them far more than their earlier associations — proximity to righteousness does not guarantee one will remain on the right path.
The anxiety Abu Hurayra and al-Rahhal felt upon hearing the Prophet's warning shows that self-examination in response to such reminders is healthy and appropriate.
practical_advice: Regularly examine your relationship with your faith beyond outward practice — ask yourself whether your knowledge is truly shaping your character and choices.
When you hear or read something that causes you spiritual unease, treat it as an invitation to introspect rather than something to suppress or dismiss.
Guard against situations where your skills or knowledge could be co-opted to serve dishonest ends, even when the pressure to do so is social or material.

title: Musaylima Writes to the Prophet Demanding Partnership in Prophethood
summary: Musaylima sent a letter to the Prophet claiming equal partnership in prophethood and demanding half of all authority, to which the Prophet responded with a brief, dignified refusal affirming that the earth belongs to God alone.
story: Yunus b. Bukayr quoted Ibn Isl:;taq as having stated, "Musaylima b. I:Iabib wrote to the Messenger of God (SAAS) as follows, 'From Musaylima, Messenger of God, to Mul:;tammad, Messenger of God. Peace be upon you. To proceed: I have been appointed as your partner in the task, along with you. Half the authority is ours, the other halfthat ofQuraysh. However, Quraysh are an aggressive people.' Two messengers brought this document and the Messenger of God (SAAS) wrote back to him. He stated, 'In the name of God, the most Merciful, most Beneficent. From Mul:;tammad, Messeuger of God, to Musaylima, the imposter. Peace be upon him who follows the guidance. To proceed: the earth belongs to God. He entrusts it to whomever of His servants He wishes. The outcome will favour the pious.'" This - the receipt of this message - was at the end of 10 AH.
lessons: A response to arrogance need not match its tone — the Prophet's brief, composed reply demonstrated that true authority does not need to argue for itself.
Power and authority ultimately belong to God, not to those who loudly claim them; this principle is both a comfort and a caution.
The outcome always favours the pious — this reminder is as relevant to personal struggles as it is to grand historical confrontations.
practical_advice: When someone makes an outrageous demand or claim against you, resist the urge to respond with equal bluster; a calm, principled reply often communicates more strength.

In moments when others seem to be claiming what is rightfully yours — credit, authority, recognition — anchor yourself in the belief that God is aware and that just outcomes are ultimately in His hands.
Keep your communications dignified and grounded in principle, especially when provoked; how you respond under pressure reveals and shapes your character.

title: The Prophet's Warning to Musaylima's Two Messengers
summary: When Musaylima's two messengers openly declared belief in Musaylima's false prophethood before the Prophet, he stated he would have executed them were it not for the sacred protection afforded to messengers.
story: Yunus b. Bukayr stated, quoting Ibn Isl:;taq, "Sa'd b. Tariq related to me, from Salama b. Nu'aym b. Mas'ud, from his father, who said, 'I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say to the two messengers of Musaylima when they brought the letter from him, "Do you agree with what he said?" "Yes," they replied. "I swear by God", he told them, "that if it were not for the fact that messengers are not to be executed, I would strike off the heads of you both!"'" Abu Da'ud al-Tayalisl stated that al-Mas'udl related to him, from 'A~im, from Abu Wa'l1, from 'Abd Allah b. Mas'ud, who said, "Ibn al-Nawal:;ta and Ibn Uthal came as messengers to the Messenger of God (SAAS), on behalf of Musaylima, 'the imposter', and he asked them, 'Do you testify that I am the Messenger of God?' They replied, 'We testify that Musaylima is the Messenger of God!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then exclaimed, 'I proclaim my belief in God and in His Messengers! If I were someone who executed messengers, I would execute both of you!'" 'Abd Allah b. Mas'ud went on, "And so the practice was in~tituted that messengers would not be executed."
lessons: The Prophet's anger was righteous but governed — he felt the full weight of the provocation yet upheld a principle of international conduct that protected even those who insulted him.
Institutions and ethical principles are sometimes established precisely in the most difficult moments, when upholding them costs the most.
Standing firm in one's beliefs when pressured to affirm falsehood is a mark of personal integrity, even if the social cost is high.
practical_advice: When you are deeply provoked, pause before acting — ask yourself whether the action you are tempted to take would uphold or undermine the principles you believe in.
Identify the ethical rules in your own life that you will not compromise even under emotional pressure, and commit to them in advance so they guide you in difficult moments.
When others around you affirm something you know to be false, hold to what you know is true without aggression, as the Prophet did by simply declaring his own belief.

title: Ibn al-Nawahha's Continued Defiance and Eventual Punishment
summary: Ibn Mas'ud recounted his lasting anger toward Ibn al-Nawahha, one of Musaylima's messengers, and how years later Ibn al-Nawahha was found leading a group reciting fabricated scripture and was executed for it.
story: He continued, "Regarding Ibn Uthal, God ultimately gave him his reward. As for Ibn al-Nawa!.la, I continued to feel much anger against him until God took possession of him." The {taft:;. al-Bayhaqj stated that Usama b. Uthal accepted Islam. And we referred to this above. Regarding Ibn al-Nawa!.la, Abu Zakariyya' b. Abu Is!.laq al-Muzanj, quoting Abu 'Abd Allah Mu!.lammad b. Ya'qub, quoting Mu!.lammad b. 'Abd al-Wahhab, quoting Ja'far b. 'Awn, quoting Isma'jJ b. Abu Khalid, from Qiys b. Abu l:;Iazim, who said that a man came to 'Abd Allah b. Mas'ud and said, 'I passed by a mosque of Banu l;Ianjfa while they were making a recitation of something God did not reveal to Mu!.lammad (~AAS). It was, "By those women who mill flour; by those women who knead dough; by those women who bake bread; by those women who concoct stew; by those women who swallow morsels ..."' "'Abd Allah then sent for them; they were 70 men in number, and their leader was 'Abd Allah b. al-Nawa!.la. 'Abd Allah gave orders and he was executed. He then said, 'We'll never acquire Satan out of these; however, we can drive them off to Syria, so that God may take care of them for us!'"
lessons: Patience does not mean indifference to injustice — Ibn Mas'ud carried righteous anger for years but entrusted the outcome to God, and justice eventually came.
Fabricated religious texts are a serious danger to communities; the willingness to publicly recite false scripture in a mosque shows how deeply false teachings can take root if left unchecked.
Taking decisive action against falsehood when it comes within one's authority is a communal responsibility, not merely a personal one.
practical_advice: When someone wrongs you or a community and the matter is out of your hands, practise entrusting the outcome to God while remaining alert to any action you legitimately can take.
If you encounter content — religious, informational, or otherwise — that claims sacred authority but feels inconsistent with what you know to be true, investigate before accepting it.
Distinguish between holding onto grievance that poisons you and holding onto principled concern for justice that motivates righteous action.

title: The Delegation of Banu Hanifa: Hospitality, Gifts, and Musaylima's Misinterpretation
summary: Al-Waqidi describes how the delegation of Banu Hanifa was hosted generously in Medina, given gifts including silver, and how Musaylima — who had stayed with the mounts — received the same share, which he later twisted into a claim of prophetic partnership.
story: Al-Waqidj stated, "The delegation from Banu l;Ianjfa consisted of approximately ten men, under the leadership of Sulma b. l;Ian?ala. They included al-Ra!.l!.lal b. 'Unfuwa, Talq b. 'Ali, 'Ali b. Sinan, and Musaylima b.l:;labjb, 'the imposter'. They were accommodated in the home of Maslama, daughter of al-l;Iarith, and they were given full hospitality. They were given lunch and dinner - at times consisting of bread and meat, at others of bread and yoghurt, bread and meat fat, and dates, during their stay. "When they came to the mosque, they accepted Islam. They had left Musaylima with their mounts and when they wanted to depart, the Messenger of God (~AAS) gave them each five awqiyya weight of silver. He also ordered the same gift for Musaylima, and when they said he was with their mounts, he commented, 'His status is no worse than yours.' "When they returned to Musaylima and told him what the Messenger of God (~t\AS) had said, he commented, 'He only said that because he knows that power will come to me after him.' "Having said this, Musaylima, may God abhore him, persisted and made claim to the prophethood." Al-Waqidj stated, "The Messenger of God (~AAS) had sent out with them a vessel containing the remainder of water he had used for cleansing himself. Musaylima ordered his men to revoke their pledge of allegiance to the Messenger of God (~AAS) to pour the water over his dwelling, and to adopt it as a masjid, a place of prayer. This they did."
lessons: Good-faith generosity toward those who will later betray it does not make the generosity wrong; the Prophet's conduct remains exemplary regardless of how Musaylima responded.
Those determined to find confirmation for a false belief will twist even neutral words into evidence — this is a feature of self-deception, not of the words themselves.
Sacred things misused for political ends — like the Prophet's blessed water being turned into a symbol of a rival claim — represent a profound corruption of what is holy.
practical_advice: Be aware that when you extend kindness to others, you cannot control how it is received or interpreted — your responsibility is the sincerity of the act, not the other person's response.
When you notice that someone is consistently reinterpreting neutral events to confirm a particular narrative, treat this as a sign of motivated reasoning rather than genuine insight.
Protect the things you hold sacred from being co-opted for purposes that contradict their meaning, whether in personal relationships, communities, or public life.

title: Musaylima the Liar and His False Prophethood
summary: Musaylima, a false prophet, ordered his followers to revoke their pledge to the Prophet Muhammad and turn his dwelling into a place of worship. He was later killed during the caliphate of Abu Bakr.
story: containing the remainder of water he had used for cleansing himself. Musaylima ordered his men to revoke their pledge of allegiance to the Messenger of God (~AAS) to pour the water over his dwelling, and to adopt it as a masjid, a place of prayer. This they did. Hereafter we will recount the death of al-Aswad ai-'Ansj at the end of the life of the Messenger of God (~AAS). Musaylima was killed during the caliphate of Abu Bakr, 'the trusting'.
lessons: Falsehood may gain followers temporarily, but truth ultimately prevails — those who build on deception will eventually be exposed.
The strength of a community depends on the sincerity of its allegiance; pledges made insincerely collapse under pressure.
Patience and trust in God's plan sustains believers when they witness corruption and misguidance spreading around them.
practical_advice: When you encounter misleading influences in your environment, hold firmly to your convictions rather than following the crowd.
Reflect on whether your commitments and loyalties are sincere; renewing your intentions regularly strengthens your integrity.
When injustice seems to prevail temporarily, remind yourself of historical examples where truth was ultimately restored.

title: The Delegation from Najran and the Selection of Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah
summary: The Christian rulers of Najran came to the Prophet intending a ritual oath-taking challenge, but instead requested a trustworthy man be sent with them. The Prophet selected Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah, calling him the most trustworthy man of the nation.
story: AlcBukhari stated that it was related to him by 'Abbas b. al-I:Iusayn, quoting Y~ya b. Adam, from Isra)i~ from Abii Is(laq, from Sila b. Zufar, from I:Iudhayfa, who said, "Ai-'Aqib and 1l1-Sayyad, rulers of Najran, came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), intending to engage in the ti'an" ceremony. "One man said to the other, 'Let's not do this. If he is a prophet, and we engage him in this titan, neither we nor our descendants will have success.' They both then said (to the Prophet (SAAS)), 'We will give you whatever you want. Send a trustworthy man out with us, but do not send anyone who is not worthy of trust.' He responded, 'I will send with you a man who is truly trustworthy.' "The Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) competed to have this honour, and he ultimately said, 'Stand up, Abii 'Ubayda b. al-Jarra\1.' And when he did so, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'This is the (most) trustworthy man of this nation.'"
lessons: True trustworthiness is a rare and noble quality that earns recognition from the highest authority.
Competing for opportunities to serve a righteous cause is praiseworthy; it reflects sincere love for what is good.
Even those who oppose the truth may recognize the importance of integrity and honesty in those they deal with.
practical_advice: Strive to build a reputation for trustworthiness in your daily dealings so that you are the natural choice when reliability is needed.
When you feel competitive with others, redirect that energy toward competing in good deeds and noble qualities rather than worldly status.
Reflect on which qualities those around you associate with you — and if trustworthiness is not among them, take concrete steps to develop it.

title: The Prophet's Letter to the Bishop of Najran
summary: The Prophet Muhammad wrote a letter to the bishop of Najran summoning the people to monotheism, offering them terms of peace, jizya, or warning of warfare. The bishop was shocked and convened his leaders to deliberate on the letter.
story: The !taft'? Abii Bakr al-Bayhaqi stated that he was informed by Abii 'Abd Allah al-I:Iafi~ and Abii Sa'id Mu(lammad b. Miisa ibn al-Facjl, both of whom said, "Abii al-'Abbas Mu(lammad b. Ya'qiib related to us, quoting A(lmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar, quoting Yiinus b. Bukayr, from Salama b. 'Abd Yasii', from his father, from his grandfather. Yiinus, who had been a Christian and had accepted Islam, said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) wrote to Najran before the (siirat) Ta Sirt' of Sulayman (Soloman) had been revealed to him, 'In the name of the God of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob, from Mu(lammad, the Prophet, the Messenger of God, to the bishop of Najran. Are you peaceable? I praise to you the God of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob. To proceed: I summon you to the worship of God, away from worshipping servants of God. Ifyou should refuse, then you will have to pay the jizya. If you refuse, I am warning you of warfare, Peace.' "When this letter reached the bishop, he was shocked and astounded by it. He sent for a man of Najran named Shura(lbil b. WadaCa, who was originally from Hamdan. No one would be consulted in preference to him in the case of some problem - not al-Abham, nor al-Sayyid, nor al 'Aqib. "The bishop gave this letter of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to Shurahbii. When he had read it, they asked him, 'Abii Maryam, what do you think?' Shur~bil replied, 'You well know how God promised Abraham that prophethood would pass on through the progeny oflsma'il; don't you believe that this could refer to this man? I have no personal opinion about the prophethood question. If this were a worldly matter, I would give you my views on it, and would do my very best for you.' The bishop told him, 'Come aside and take a seat.' Shura!).bJ! then went aside and sat down near him. "The bishop sent to a man of Najran named 'Abd Allah b. Shura!).bil, a man of Dhjj Asba!)., from I:Iimyar. He had him read the letter and asked him his opinion. This man spoke as Shura!).bil and the bishop told him, 'Come aside and take a seat', and the man did so and sat near him. "Next the bishop sent for a man of Najran named Jabbar b. Fayq, of Banjj al-I:Iarith, son of Ka'b, a man of Banjj al-I:Iimas. He asked this man to read the letter and to give his opinion of it. Jabbar replied as had Shura1).bil and 'Abd Allah. The bishop then told him to go aside and sit, which he did. "When they had all agreed on the same statement, the bishop gave orders that the bell be struck and lighted lanterns and hair shirts were lifted up into the monks' cells; they would do this if there were an alarm during the day, but if the alarm were by night, they would merely strike the bell and lift up the lights into the cells. "When the bell was struck, they assembled and the people of the valley, both in its upper and its lower parts, raised the hair shirts; the valley was the length ofa day's journey for a fast rider. It consisted of73 villages and had 100,000 warriors. The letter of the Messenger of God (SAAS) was read to them and the bishop sought their opinion of it. Their leaders agreed that they should send Shura!).bJ! b. Wada'a al-Hamadani, 'Abd Allah b. Shura1).bil al-A~ba1).i, andJabbar b. Fayq al-I:Iarithi to seek information about the Messenger of God (SAAS)."
lessons: A message of truth may initially cause shock and discomfort, but it deserves careful and honest deliberation rather than immediate rejection.
Wise leaders seek counsel broadly before making decisions that affect their entire community.
Some people, even outside the faith, possess enough sincerity and fairness to acknowledge the possibility of truth when confronted with it.
practical_advice: When you receive challenging news or a difficult message, resist the urge to react immediately — take time to consult trusted people and reflect carefully.
Surround yourself with advisors who will speak honestly even when the truth is difficult to hear.
Practice intellectual humility by remaining open to the possibility that you may need to revise long-held beliefs when presented with compelling evidence.

title: The Najran Delegation Arrives in Madinah and Is Ignored Due to Their Arrogance
summary: The Najran delegation arrived in Madinah dressed in fine robes and gold rings, but the Prophet did not respond to their greeting all day. Ali ibn Abi Talib advised them to remove their finery, and only then did the Prophet respond to them.
story: "The delegation travelled to Medina and when they arrived there, they took off their travel garments and dressed in fine robes of striped cloth and put on gold rings. They then went to the Messenger of God (SAAS). They greeted him, but he did not respond. They waited there all day long for him to speak to them, but he did not, since they were wearing those garments and gold rings. "The delegation went to fmd 'Othman b. 'Affan and 'Abd al-Ra1)man b. 'Awf, whom they had known previously; they found them in an assembly, meeting with alljar and muhajirtn. They asked them, ''Othman and 'Abd al-Ra!).man, your Prophet wrote us a letter and we have come in response to it. We have greeted him, but he has not returned our greeting. We waited patiently alI day long, but failed to get him to speak to us. What do you both think; do you consider we should return home?' "They also asked 'Ali b. Abjj Talib who was also there in the group. He said to 'Othman and 'Abd al-Ra!).man, 'I think they should take off these fine robes and rings, dress in their travel garments and then return to him.' "They did this and when they greeted him, he responded. He then told them, 'By Him who sent me with the truth, when you first came to me, Iblis (Satan) was certainly with you.'"
lessons: Outward displays of wealth and status can become barriers to genuine connection and sincere engagement.
Seeking advice from wise and honest friends can resolve confusion and open closed doors.
Humility in approach — setting aside pride and pretension — is often the key to being truly heard.
practical_advice: When an important conversation or relationship feels blocked, examine whether your own presentation or attitude may be creating the barrier.
Seek out honest friends who will give you direct advice rather than only telling you what you want to hear.
Before entering a significant meeting or difficult conversation, check your intentions and strip away any desire to impress — sincerity opens more doors than status.

title: The Discussion on Jesus and the Revelation of the Mubahala Verse
summary: The Najran delegation asked the Prophet about Jesus, and God revealed verses comparing Jesus to Adam. The Prophet prepared for a mubahala oath-taking ceremony, arriving with his family, but the delegation ultimately refused to proceed.
story: "This session of questioning continued and they eventually asked, 'What do you say about Jesus? Although we will go back to our people as Christians, it would please us, if you are a prophet, we could hear what you say of him.' "The Messenger of God ($AAS) replied, 'I don't have anything to say about him today; stay here until I tell you what it is God says about Jesus.' "By next morning God, the Ahnighty and Glorious, had revealed this verse, 'God considers Jesus to be the like of Adam; He created him from earth, then said to him: "Be!" And he was. The truth is from your Lord; and so do not be of those who make disputes. To those who could dispute with you, after the knowledge that has come to you, say, "Come, let us call our sons and (you) your sons, we our wives and (you) your wives and we our ownselves and (you) yourselves, and then let us make our appeals to God, and pray that God places his curse upon those who lie' (surat Al 'lmran; III, v. 58-60). "They refused to agree to this. "On the next morning following that when he had told them of this, he arrived for a mula'ana,27 'an oath-taking competition', accompanied by al-l;Iasan and al-l;Iusayn. He was dressed in a khamil, 'a coarse-fibred garment', and Fatima walked at his back; that day he was also accompanied by several of his womenfolk."
lessons: The Prophet did not rush to answer difficult theological questions but waited for divine guidance — a model of patience and humility before God.
Truth does not need to be defended through aggression; the willingness to submit the matter entirely to God is itself a powerful statement of conviction.
Even when facing those who deny clear evidence, the believer's role is to present the truth clearly and leave the outcome to God.
practical_advice: When faced with a difficult question you cannot immediately answer, it is a sign of wisdom and integrity to say 'let me reflect and return to you' rather than rushing to respond.
When you are in a conflict where you know you are on the side of truth, resist the urge to force the other person to accept it — present your case clearly and trust God with the result.
In moments of religious or intellectual confrontation, ground yourself in your core convictions and let your conduct and character speak alongside your words.

title: Shurahbil's Wise Counsel and the Agreement to Accept the Prophet's Judgment
summary: Shurahbil, the chief advisor of the Najran delegation, counseled his companions against the oath-taking challenge, warning of the spiritual and worldly consequences of opposing a true prophet. He proposed instead to accept the Prophet's judgment, which the entire valley agreed to.
story: "Shural;1bil told his two companions, 'You both well know that the upper and lower parts of our valley join in accepting my views and I have, I swear, reached a weighty conclusion. Given that this man is a powerful leader, if we were the first of the Arabs to spite him and to reject his orders, he and his men would maintain such ill-will for us that they would eventually do us great harm. We are the Arabs who are his closest neighbours, and if this man is truly a prophet, and we challenge him as a liar, then every hide and hair of us is sure to perish!' "His companions then asked him, 'Well, Abu Maryam, what should we do?' He replied, 'My opinion is that I should recognize his authority. He is, I think, not a man who would ever make an over-harsh judgement.' Shural;1bil then met with the Messenger of God ($AAS) and told him, 'I have something better to suggest than an oath-taking competition with you.' 'And what is that?' he asked. 'Spend this day till tonight and tonight till tomorrow reaching your judgement and whatever you decide concerning us will be acceptable.' "The Messenger of God ($AAS) asked, 'Is there anyone else back home behind you who will contradict you?' Shural;1bil replied, 'Ask my two companions.' They said, 'Our whole valley will accept his decision.' "The Messenger of God ($AAS) then returned home without making oaths regarding them."
lessons: True wisdom sometimes means stepping back from a confrontation you could win in pride but lose in truth.
Recognizing the authority of justice and fairness, even when it comes from someone outside your own tradition, is a mark of sincere character.
A leader who can guide their community away from a destructive path, even under pressure, serves them far better than one who leads them into ruin out of stubbornness.
practical_advice: Before escalating a conflict, pause and honestly assess: what is the best outcome for everyone involved, and is pride driving you more than wisdom?
When someone in authority has shown themselves to be fair-minded, extend trust rather than assuming the worst — this often leads to better outcomes for all sides.
Practice the courage it takes to counsel others toward de-escalation; being a voice of reason in tense situations is a form of leadership that takes real strength.

title: The Prophet's Covenant with the People of Najran
summary: After the Najran delegation agreed to accept the Prophet's judgment, he wrote them a generous covenant guaranteeing their property and wealth in exchange for a reasonable annual payment, witnessed by leading Companions.
story: "When they went to him next day, he wrote for them the following document, 'In the name of God, the most Merciful, the most Beneficent. This is written by Mul;1ammad, the Prophet, the untutored, the Messenger of God, to Najran. It is his judgement regarding all their produce and all their assets in gold, silver and slaves to be generous to them and to leave them all this in consideration of a payment of 2,000 sets of garments. Of these 1,000 shall be paid each Rajab, and another 1,000 each Safar.' He then went on to give the rest of the conditions. The document ends, 'Wimessed by Abu Sufyan b. Barb, Ghaylan b. 'Amr, Malik b. 'Awf, ofBanu N~r, and al-Aqra' b. Babis al-Banzali, and al-Mughira.' And he (so) wrote it."
lessons: Justice and generosity in leadership builds lasting peace far more effectively than domination or humiliation of those who submit.
Formalizing agreements with clarity and witnesses protects all parties and reflects a commitment to fairness.
The Prophet's dealings with non-Muslims demonstrate that Islam calls for dignified and honorable treatment of those who choose peaceful coexistence.
practical_advice: When resolving conflicts, aim for outcomes where all parties feel their dignity has been respected — sustainable peace requires that no one feels crushed.
Document important agreements clearly and involve witnesses; this is not a sign of distrust but of wisdom and care for all involved.
Reflect on how generosity in resolution — offering more than the minimum required — can transform an adversary into a peaceful neighbor.

title: Bishr ibn Mu'awiya Turns Back to Madinah After Being Moved by the Prophet's Letter
summary: When the Najran delegation returned home with the Prophet's letter, a man named Bishr ibn Mu'awiya was so moved by what he heard that he turned his camel back toward Madinah, determined to go to the Prophet, despite his brother the bishop trying to stop him.
story: "When they received this document, they left for Najran. With the bishop there was a brother of his, on his mother's side, he being his uncle's son, a man named Bishr b. Mu'awiya, known as Abu 'Alqama. The delegation gave the letter of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to the bishop. While he was reading it, in the company of Abu 'Alqama, while they were both riding, Bishr's camel stumbled. Bishr swore an oath, although he did not make reference to the Messenger of God (SAAS). "The bishop-told him, 'You might have spoken a curse against a prophet who has been sent.' Bishr replied, 'Certainly; and I swear I'll not nullify it until I go to the Messenger of God (SAAS).' "He then directed his camel back towards Medina, but the bishop positioned his own in his way and told him, 'Understand from me, that'"
lessons: Sometimes a small, unplanned moment — a stumble, an offhand remark — becomes the turning point that leads a person toward the truth.
When the heart recognizes something as true, it compels a person toward action even when those around them try to hold them back.
Family bonds, though precious, should not be the reason we suppress or abandon our sincere search for truth.
practical_advice: Pay attention to the small, unexpected moments in your life — sometimes what seems like a random disruption is actually an invitation to reflect and change direction.
If you feel a strong inner pull toward something you believe is right, don't let social pressure or the disapproval of loved ones silence it — investigate it honestly.
When someone close to you is on a sincere journey of personal or spiritual growth, resist the urge to block them out of fear; walk alongside them instead.

title: Bishr b. Mu'awiya Accepts Islam After the Najran Delegation
summary: Bishr b. Mu'awiya, traveling with the bishop during the Najran delegation's encounter with the Prophet, swears an oath and refuses the bishop's political reasoning, instead turning back to Medina to accept Islam.
story: God (SAAS) to the bishop. While he was reading it, in the company of Abu 'Alqama, while they were both riding, Bishr's camel stumbled. Bishr swore an oath, although he did not make reference to the Messenger of God (SAAS). "The bishop-told him, 'You might have spoken a curse against a prophet who has been sent.' Bishr replied, 'Certainly; and I swear I'll not nullify it until I go to the Messenger of God (SAAS).' "He then directed his camel back towards Medina, but the bishop positioned his own in his way and told him, 'Understand from me, that I only said that so that the Arabs have that reported to them of me, fearing that they might consider that we had accepted his authenticity, or been swayed by his voice, or agreed to something this man wanted to which the Arabs did not comply, even though we are the most powerful and numerous of all.' Bishr replied, 'No; I swear, I'll never accept this attitude of yours.' "Bishr then spurred his mount into motion, turning his back to the bishop. He also spoke the following doggerel verse: 'To you (my camel) anxiously turns her flank Though a foal inside her belly objects, For her religion opposes that of the Christians.' "When he reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), he accepted Islam and stayed on with him until he was eventually killed."
lessons: When truth becomes clear to us, social pressure and group loyalty should not prevent us from following it — Bishr chose conscience over comfort.
Peer pressure can take sophisticated forms, including appeals to collective reputation or political strategy; discerning the difference between sincere counsel and manipulation is a spiritual skill.
A moment of stumbling or an oath made in haste can become the turning point that redirects an entire life toward a better path.
practical_advice: When you feel pulled between what you know is right and what your social circle expects of you, write down your core values and measure the decision against them rather than against others' opinions.
If someone offers you a 'practical' reason to delay acting on your conscience, ask yourself honestly: is this wisdom or is it an excuse? Bishr's example shows that some delays are simply denials.
Take small, concrete steps toward the truth you recognize — even a physical act like turning your camel around can be the beginning of a life-changing commitment.

title: The Monk Ibn Abu Shamar al-Zubaydi Hears of the Prophet and Brings Gifts
summary: A monk in Najran, upon hearing that a prophet had been sent and that Bishr had accepted Islam, insisted on being brought down from his cell and personally traveled to the Prophet with gifts, yet did not formally accept Islam before the Prophet's death.
story: "The delegation entered Najran and went to the monk Ibn Abu Shamar al-Zubaydi while he was high up in his cell, telling him, 'A prophet has been given a mission in Tihama!' They narrated how the Najran delegation had gone to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and how he had suggested an oath-swearing competition to them, which they had refused, and how Bishr b. Mu'awiya had hurried off to him and had accepted Islam. "The monk replied, 'Bring me down, or else I'll cast myself from this cell!' They did so; he brought with him a present which he took with him to the Messenger of God (SAAS). Part of it is that cloak that the caliphs wear, along with a wooden cup and a staff. "He remained for some time with the Messenger of God (SAAS), listening to the revelation, then returned to his own people. He is not credited with having accepted Islam. He promised to come back but this was not to be until the Messenger of God (SAAS) had died."
lessons: Being moved by truth and even spending time in its presence is not the same as fully committing to it — the monk's story is a reminder that proximity to guidance does not guarantee transformation.
Urgency in seeking truth is admirable — the monk was so stirred he threatened to throw himself from his cell — but urgency must be followed through with decision and commitment.
Procrastination in matters of deep spiritual importance carries real risk; the monk intended to return but the window of opportunity closed before he did.
practical_advice: If you feel a strong pull toward a truth or a change in your life, do not let that initial urgency dissipate into vague intentions — set a specific, time-bound commitment while the feeling is alive.
Recognize the difference between admiring something from a distance and actually living it. Ask yourself: what concrete step can I take today to move from appreciation to practice?
When you find yourself saying 'I will do this later,' examine whether 'later' is a realistic plan or a quiet way of saying 'never.' The monk's example is a sobering lesson about deferred decisions.

title: The Prophet's Covenant of Protection for the Bishop of Najran and the Christians
summary: After the Najran Christian delegation met with the Prophet, he issued a formal written covenant guaranteeing protection of their bishops, monks, priests, rights, and practices for as long as they remained peaceful.
story: "The bishop, Abu al-Harith, went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), along with al-Sayyid, al-'Aqib and important persons from their people. They stayed with him, hearing what God revealed to him. He wrote the document for the bishop and those bishops to come after him: 'In the name of God, the most Merciful, the most Beneficent. From Muhammad, the Prophet, to Bishop Abu al-Harith and the bishops of Najran, and its priests and monks, including all those under their control, few or many. (This gives) protection of God and of His Messenger, which shall not be changed for any of its bishops, monks or priests. Nor shall any of their rights or powers or former practices be changed. The protection of God and His Messenger is for ever, so long as they remain peaceful and sincere and are not subjected to wrongdoing or engage in it.' 'Written by al-Mughira b. Shu'ba."
lessons: True strength and confidence in one's beliefs is demonstrated not through coercion but through extending justice and protection even to those who differ — the Prophet's covenant is a model of principled pluralism.
Documenting agreements and commitments in writing is itself a prophetic practice, ensuring clarity, accountability, and the protection of rights across time.

Security and trust are the foundations of any peaceful relationship between differing communities; guaranteeing people's rights and practices creates conditions for sincere engagement rather than fearful compliance.
practical_advice: In your own relationships and communities, practice the principle of granting others security in who they are — people open up and engage honestly when they do not feel threatened or pressured to change.
When making agreements or commitments — personal or professional — put them in writing with clear terms. This prophetic practice reduces misunderstanding and protects all parties.

Reflect on whether your engagement with people who are different from you is characterized by coercion or genuine respect for their dignity and rights. Let this covenant be a standard to measure yourself against.

title: Abu Haritha b. Alqama Knows the Truth but Chooses Status Over Faith
summary: The Christian bishop Abu Haritha, fully aware that the Prophet was the awaited prophet, admitted this privately to his brother Kurz but chose not to accept Islam out of fear of losing his privileges from the Byzantines.
story: "When they left Najran, Abu Haritha was mounted on a mule and by his side rode a brother of his named Kurz b. 'Alqama. The mule of Abu Haritha stumbled and Kurz said, 'May al-Ab'ad, "the one far away", (meaning the Messenger of God (SAAS)) stumble!' Abu Haritha commented, 'No; it's you who may stumble!' Kurz asked him, 'Why, brother?' He replied, 'I swear by God, he is certainly the prophet whom we have been awaiting!' "Kurz asked, 'Well, what is holding you back, when you know that?' He replied, 'It's because of what these people have done for us. They have honoured us, made payments to us, and given us authority. But they adamantly oppose him; if I were to take action, they would take everything away from us.' "His brother Kurz gave much thought to what he had said and eventually accepted Islam."
lessons: Knowing the truth and acting on the truth are two very different things — worldly attachments, particularly status, wealth, and the approval of powerful patrons, can create a prison that knowledge alone cannot unlock.
The fear of losing what we have often does more to hold us back than any genuine intellectual doubt — Abu Haritha's honesty with his brother reveals that his barrier was not belief but attachment.
Sometimes the person who hears second-hand — Kurz, who heard his brother's confession — responds more freely than the one with direct knowledge, because they have less to lose.
practical_advice: Identify one area of your life where you already know what the right course of action is but have been avoiding it because of what you might lose. Write down what you are protecting and honestly evaluate whether it is worth what it is costing you spiritually.

When you catch yourself making excuses for not acting on your values, practice naming the fear specifically — 'I am afraid of losing X' — because named fears are far easier to address than vague discomfort.

Seek out someone you trust and speak your truth to them, as Abu Haritha did with Kurz. Sometimes articulating what we believe but have not acted on is the first step toward closing the gap between the two.

title: The Delegation of Banu Amir and the Plot to Assassinate the Prophet
summary: A delegation from Banu Amir led by 'Amir b. al-Tufayl arrived with a secret plan to assassinate the Prophet, but the plot failed when the conspirator Arbad hesitated, and the Prophet prayed against 'Amir, who subsequently died of a sudden illness.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Then a delegation from Banu Amir came to visit the Messenger of God (SAAS). It included 'Amir b. al-Tufayl, Arbad b. Qays b. Jaz' b. Khalid b. Ja'far and Hayyan b. Salma b. Malik b. Ja'far. These three men were the leaders and chief mischief-makers of their tribe. "When that enemy of God 'Amir b. al-Tufayl came to see the Messenger of God (SAAS), it was with the intent of betraying him. His people had told him, 'Abu 'Amir, others have accepted Islam, you do so too.' But he replied, 'I swear by God, I made a vow I would never cease striving to get the Arabs to follow me; should I now follow this Quraysh fellow?' "Al-Arbad then said, 'When we go to see this man, I will get him to turn his face away from you. When I do this, you strike him with your sword!' "When they reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'Amir b. al-Tufayl said, 'Muhammad, will you come aside with me?' He replied, 'No, by God, not until you believe in God alone!' 'Amir repeated, 'Please, just come aside with me, Muhammad,' and he began talking to him, waiting for Arbad to act. Arbad, however, did nothing. When 'Amir saw that Arbad was taking no action, he again said, 'Muhammad, do come aside with me,' but he again replied, 'No, not until you believe in God alone Who has no partner.' "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) thus rejected him, 'Amir said, 'Then, by God, I'll fill this place with horses and men to fight you!' When 'Amir had turned away to leave, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'O God, take care of 'Amir b. al-Tufayl for me!' "When they had left the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'Amir b. al-Tufayl asked Arbad, 'Had I not ordered you what to do? I swear by God, there was no man on earth I considered more fearsome than you! I swear, I'll never fear you again, after today!' "He replied, 'I pray you, don't blame me too hastily. Whenever I was about to do as you had asked, you got in the man's way so all I could see was you. Should I have struck you with my sword?' "They headed back home and when they had gone part way, God, the Almighty and Glorious, afflicted 'Amir b. al-Tufayl with a growth in his neck. And God killed him in the home of a woman of Banu Salul. He exclaimed, 'O Banu 'Amir, shall (my death) be from a growth like that on a young camel in the home of a woman of Banu Salul?'"
lessons: Arrogance and the refusal to relinquish the desire for dominance can lead a person to oppose what they know to be true — 'Amir's own people urged him toward Islam but he could not bear to follow rather than lead.
Divine protection operates in ways beyond human calculation; the Prophet remained firm, uncompromising, and turned to God in prayer rather than to human defenses when faced with a mortal threat.

Those who plan harm with cunning and boldness often find their own schemes unraveling from within — Arbad's hesitation, inexplicable to both himself and 'Amir, was itself a form of divine intervention.
practical_advice: When you face a situation where someone is pressuring you to compromise your principles, practice the Prophet's response: remain steady, state your position clearly, and do not be drawn into isolated or ambiguous situations by persistent manipulation.
In moments of genuine threat or overwhelming difficulty, turn to prayer as a primary response rather than a last resort. The Prophet's supplication against 'Amir was his first and sufficient action.

Examine whether pride or the desire to be a leader rather than a follower is preventing you from accepting guidance or changing course in your life. Ask yourself honestly: is my resistance to this change about truth, or about ego?

title: Maw'ala b. Kuthayf Accepts Islam and His Legacy
summary: Maw'ala b. Kuthayf, a young man of 20, came to the Prophet, accepted Islam, pledged allegiance, and lived 100 years in the faith, becoming known for his eloquence as 'he of the two tongues.'
story: al-$a!taba (A Study of the Names of the Companions) concerning (a man named) Maw'ala: "He was Maw'ala b. Kuthayf aI-l)ababi aI-Kilabi al-Cj\mirl, of Banjj 'Amir b. Sa'sa'a. He went to the Messenger of God ($AAS), and accepted Islam when a young man of 20, and he lived on in the faith for 100 years. He was known as 'he of the two tongues' because of his eloquence. His son 'Abd al-'Aziz related materials from him. It was he who recounted the story of 'Amir b. aI- Tufayl and the line, ' ... a growth like that of a baggage camel and a death in the house of Saljjliyya!'" AI-Zubayr b. Bakkar stated, "Zamya', daughter of 'Abd al-'Aziz b. Maw'ala b. Kuthayf b. l;laml b. Khalid b. 'Amr b. Mu'awiya, he being al-I)abbab b. Kilab b. Rabi'a b. 'Amir b. $ac"a'a, said, 'My father related to me, quoting his father, quoted Maw'ala as having recounted that he went to the Messenger of God ($AAS), and accepted Islam when he was 20 years old. He pledged him his allegiance, wiped his right arm (in deference to him), and led his camel to him. The Messenger of God ($AAS) gave him a two-year-old she-camel. After the death of the Prophet ($AAS), he became a companion of Abjj Hurayra and lived on for 100 years into Islam. He was nicknamed "he of the two tongues" because of his eloquence.'"
lessons: Embracing faith at a young age can shape an entire lifetime of spiritual growth and contribution.
True eloquence and wisdom, when rooted in faith, leave a legacy that outlasts the individual.
Generosity from a leader — even something as simple as gifting a she-camel — can deeply bond hearts and inspire lifelong loyalty.
practical_advice: If you feel lost or searching for meaning, consider that committing to your values and faith early gives your life direction and depth.
Cultivate your God-given gifts — whether eloquence, skill, or knowledge — in service of truth and community.
Reflect on the mentors and companions who shaped you; seek to carry their legacy forward as Maw'ala carried the Prophet's legacy.

title: The Prophet's Supplication Against 'Amir b. al-Tufayl
summary: After the treachery of 'Amir b. al-Tufayl at Bi'r Ma'una, the Prophet supplicated against him for 30 mornings, and God afflicted 'Amir with a fatal malignancy.
story: AI-Awza'i stated that Ya!)ya said, "The Messenger of God ($AAS) spoke prayers against 'Amir b. Tufayl for 30 mornings, saying, 'O God, rid me of 'Amir b. al-Tufayl however You wish, and send upon him what will kill him.' And God did infect him with a malignancy."
lessons: When wronged and unable to retaliate, turning to God in sincere supplication is a powerful and dignified response.
Persistent, patient supplication — sustained over 30 mornings — demonstrates that seeking God's help is not a one-time act but a continuous practice.
Divine justice operates on its own timeline; the believer's role is to entrust the outcome to God.
practical_advice: When you feel powerless against injustice or someone who has harmed you, channel your energy into sincere prayer rather than bitterness or despair.
Establish a consistent daily habit of supplication, especially for matters that weigh heavily on you, rather than expecting immediate results.
Trust that God is aware of every harm done to you; releasing the outcome to Him can bring genuine peace of mind.

title: 'Amir b. al-Tufayl's Arrogant Demands Before the Prophet
summary: 'Amir b. al-Tufayl came to the Prophet demanding to be made his successor or ruler over the nomads, and upon refusal threatened war, only to be told that God would prevent him.
story: Anas stated, "'Amir b. al-Tufayl went to the Messenger of God ($AAS) and told him, 'I give you to choose between three options: you shall govern the people on the plains, while I the nomads, or I shall be your successor after you, or I will attack you with Banjj Ghatafan and 1,000 stallions and 1,000 mares.'" He went on, "He was then, while staying in a woman's house, afflicted with a malignant growth. At this, he exclaimed, 'Shall it be a growth like that on a camel and death in the house of a woman of Banjj so-and-so? Bring me my horse!' He rode away and died upon his horse."
lessons: Arrogance and the demand for power as a prerequisite for faith is a spiritual trap that leads to ruin.
The Prophet's calm and principled refusal to bargain away justice or succession illustrates that true leadership cannot be coerced.
Those who approach truth seeking personal gain rather than genuine conviction rarely find peace.
practical_advice: Examine your own motivations when you approach commitments — are you seeking genuine goodness, or are you demanding that life meet your conditions first?
When someone makes threats or ultimatums to pressure you, take strength from the Prophet's example of calm resolve: state the truth and leave the result to God.
Pride in one's status or power can blind a person to what truly matters; regularly practicing humility guards against this spiritual danger.

title: The Assassination Plot of Arbad and 'Amir Against the Prophet
summary: Arbad b. Qays and 'Amir b. al-Tufayl conspired to assassinate the Prophet in Madinah, but when Arbad reached for his sword his hand was paralyzed and he could not draw it, and they fled.
story: "When Arbad and 'Amir left, the latter suggested, 'Arbad, I'll distract Mul;iammad from you by talking to him, and then you strike him with your sword. If you do kill Mul;iammad, they'll want no more than to be satisfied by payment of the blood-wit. They dislike warfare. You'll just give them the blood-wit.' Arbad replied, 'I'll do it.' "They proceeded back to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and 'Amir addressed him, 'Mul;iammad, get up and come with me so I can talk to you.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) arose and went alone with him towards the wall where he stopped to talk to him. Arbad went to draw his sword, but when he touched it, his hand lost its grip on the sword's hilt and he could not unsheath it. Arbad was unable to strike him sufficiently quickly for 'Amir, because the Messenger of God (SAAS) turned and, seeing Arbad and what he intended, walked away from them both."
lessons: God's protection over His Prophet was direct and miraculous; those who plot against truth find their plans undone by forces beyond their control.
Deception and conspiracy, no matter how carefully planned, cannot overcome divine protection.
The Prophet's awareness and composure in the face of danger — simply walking away — is a model of dignified self-protection.
practical_advice: When you feel surrounded by people who wish you harm, take comfort in the Quranic promise that God is the best of protectors; this is not passive resignation but active trust.
If you sense betrayal or hidden ill-intent from someone around you, trust your instincts and create distance calmly, as the Prophet did.
Protect your inner circle by surrounding yourself with people of integrity; the companions Sa'd b. Mu'adh and Usayd b. al-Hudayr immediately stood up to drive away the enemies of the Prophet.

title: Sa'd b. Mu'adh and Usayd b. al-Hudayr Confront 'Amir and Arbad
summary: After the failed assassination attempt, the companions Sa'd b. Mu'adh and Usayd b. al-Hudayr pursued 'Amir and Arbad and drove them away with bold condemnation.
story: "When Arbad and 'Amir left the presence of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and reached the lava plain of Waqim, they halted. But Sa'd b. Mu'adh and Usayd b. al-l;Iudayr went out after them and said, 'Clear off, you enemies of God! God damn you both!' 'Amir responded, 'Who is that, Sa'd?' 'That is Usayd b. l;Iugayr, of the cavalry squadrons,' he replied."
lessons: True companionship includes the courage to stand publicly against those who threaten what you love and believe in.
Naming evil directly and confronting it — rather than staying silent out of fear — is a mark of moral courage.
A community that actively defends its values and members is far more resilient than one that remains passive.
practical_advice: When someone you care about is threatened or treated unjustly, do not wait for someone else to speak up; step forward as Sa'd and Usayd did.
Develop the habit of naming harmful behavior directly but without losing your dignity; vague hints rarely protect anyone.
Build relationships with people who will stand beside you when things get difficult, not only in times of ease.

title: The Divine Destruction of Arbad by Thunderbolt
summary: After fleeing Madinah following their failed assassination plot, Arbad b. Qays was struck and killed by a divine thunderbolt at al-Raqm, a sign of God's protection over the Prophet.
story: "They left, and while they were at al-Raqm, God sent a thunderbolt down on Arbad and killed him. 'Amir then left, and when he was in the lava field, God sent a malignant growth to afflict him. That night he spent in the house of a woman of Banu Sali!I. He began feeling the growth on his neck and said, 'A growth like that of a camel, in the house of a woman of Banu Sali!I!' He loathed the idea of dying in her house. "He then rode off on his horse, made it gallop, then died on it as he returned. God revealed about them both, 'God knows what every female ...' and so on, to the words, 'He has those (Helpers) who proceed forth on his behalf...' By this, He was making reference to Mul;iammad (SAAS). He then made a reference to Arbad and how God had killed him, then quoted the verse, 'He sends the thunderbolts and uses them ...'"
lessons: Divine justice can arrive swiftly and unexpectedly; no one who plots against truth is beyond accountability.
The manner of Arbad's death — by the very force of nature — and 'Amir's humiliating end illustrate that arrogance has its own undoing built within it.
Quranic revelation responding directly to historical events demonstrates that God is fully aware of every plot and every soul.
practical_advice: When you observe injustice going apparently unpunished, resist despair; accountability in God's plan is not always immediate but it is certain.
Reflect on how the consequences people fear most — like 'Amir's dread of an undignified death — often come to them in exactly the form they feared; this is a reminder to live with integrity.
Read Surah al-Ra'd (Chapter 13) and reflect on the verses revealed about this event as a source of reassurance that God's knowledge and power encompass all things.

title: Labid's Elegy for His Brother Arbad b. Qays
summary: The poet Labid b. Rabi'a composed a moving elegy mourning his half-brother Arbad b. Qays after Arbad was killed by a thunderbolt, expressing grief, admiration, and reflection on mortality.
story: Ibn Isl:taq stated, "Arbad b. Qtys was a brother of LabId b. RabI'a on his mother's side. LabId said, mourning Arbad, 'Death spares no one - not a kindly father, nor a son. I fear much that death of ArOOd more than I do the influence of Pisces or Leo. Eye, why weep for Arbad, now that both we and the women have stood in mourning? If others threatened, he ignored their threats; if they were reasoned in judgement, so was he. A sweet and clever man, though with some bitterness in his sweetness; a man of calm and healthy constitution.
