Eye, would you weep for Arbad now that the winter winds sweep the bare trees And they have become wind-tossed and fruitless, their remnants of fruit revealed? He was braver than a meat-eating forest-lion, staring forth and determined to prevail. The eye does not see to its full extent on a night when horses return frail and worn. He inspired the mourning women to attend his wake, "(as evident) as young gazelles on barren terrain. The lightning and thunderbolt terrified me when they struck this bold warrior that terrible day. He took from those who robbed and gave back to their victims; if they came in distress, he gave them again and again. He was generous in bad times, as when spring rain produces growth. All the sons of a free-born woman must diminish in number, no matter how many they were. Even though once envied, all shall be brought down; though once leaders, all shall perish and fade.'"
lessons: Grief is a universal human experience; even those on the wrong side of history are mourned by those who loved them, reminding us of our shared humanity.
Poetry and expressive language can be a healthy channel for processing the pain of loss and honoring those we have loved.
Death is the great equalizer — the brave, the clever, and the powerful all ultimately submit to it — a reality that invites reflection on how we live.
practical_advice: When grieving a loss, allow yourself to honor the good you genuinely saw in the person, even while holding complexity about their choices, as Labid did.
Find a creative outlet — writing, poetry, art — to express grief that feels too large for ordinary words; structured expression can help process overwhelming emotion.
Meditate on the line 'all shall be brought down; though once leaders, all shall perish and fade' as a reminder to focus on what genuinely matters and endures beyond this life.

title: Quranic Revelation of Surah al-Ra'd Verses Concerning 'Amir and Arbad
summary: Ibn Abbas recounted that God revealed verses of Surah al-Ra'd specifically in response to the plotting of 'Amir b. al-Tufayl and Arbad, affirming divine protection of the Prophet and announcing God's power over all things.
story: Ibn Hisham stated that Zayd b. Aslam recounted, from 'Ala' b. Yasar, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "And God, the Almighty and Glorious, revealed the following concerning 'Amir and Arbad: 'God knows what every female bears and what falls short and what comes to fruition in the womb. And with Him everything has its measure. (He it is who is) Knowledgeable of the unseen and what is witnessed; He is the Great, the Most High. Alike (for Him) are those of you who conceal their words and those who speak openly, those who hide by night, and those who go forth by day. He has those (Helpers) who proceed forth on his behalf, both ahead and behind him, who protect him, by God's command' (siirat al-Ra'd; XIII, v.9-ll). By this latter wording, He is referring to Mul;iammad (SAAS). "He proceeded to make reference to Arbad and to his execution. God Almighty stated, 'And when God intends harm for a people there is no averting it and from Him they have no protector. He it is who shows you lightning bringing fear and expectation, He who summons the heavy clouds. The thunder proclaims His praise and the angels too are in awe of Him. He sends the thunderbolts and uses them to strike whomever He wishes. Yet they dispute concerning God, and He is Mighty in power'" (siirat al-Ra'd; XIII, v.ll-13)."
lessons: The Quran was revealed in direct response to real events, showing that God's guidance is intimately connected to the lived reality of the community.
God's knowledge encompasses everything — the secret and the open, the night and the day — so there is no hiding from divine awareness, and equally no reason to feel unseen in one's struggles.
Divine protection of the Prophet and, by extension, of the truth he carried, is a recurring Quranic theme that offers deep reassurance to believers.
practical_advice: When you feel that evil or injustice is going unnoticed, recite or reflect on these verses of Surah al-Ra'd as a reminder that nothing is hidden from God.
Take comfort in the Quranic affirmation that God surrounds His servants with protection; this is not a reason to be reckless, but a foundation for inner security.
Study the context of Quranic revelation — knowing why verses were revealed deepens your understanding and makes the guidance feel immediate and alive.

title: Dimam b. Tha'laba's Delegation to the Messenger of God on Behalf of Banu Sa'd b. Bakr
summary: Dimam b. Tha'laba arrives as a delegate of Banu Sa'd b. Bakr, boldly questions the Prophet about the pillars of Islam, accepts the faith, and returns to convert his entire tribe overnight.
story:
Ibn Isl;iaq stated, "Mul;iammad b. al-WalId b. Nuwayfi' related to me, from Kurayb, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'Bani! Sa'd b. Bakr sent Oimam b. Tha'iaba with a delegation to the Messenger of God (SAAS).
'''When he arrived, he made his camel kneel at the door of the mosque, hobbled it and entered the mosque while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was seated inside with his men. J;>imam was a burly and hairy man who had two pigtails.
'''He advanced, stood over the Messenger of God (SAAS) and asked, 'Which one of you is 'Abd al-Munalib's son?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "I am 'Abd al-Munalib's son."
'''J;>imam then asked, "Mu/:1ammad?" "Yes?" he asked. "Son of 'Abd alMunalib, I've got some tough questions to put to you; don't be offended." '" "I'll not be offended," he replied. "Ask what you want."
'" "I adjure you by God, your God, and the God of those who came before you, and the God of those who will corne after you, is it God who orders you that we worship Him alone, and associate none other with Him, and that we take down those idols that our fathers worshipped?" "Yes, by God," he replied.
'" "I again adjure you by God, your God, and the God of those before and after you, is it God who ordered you that we pray those five prayers?" "Yes," he replied.
'''J;>imam then began referring to the religious duties of Islam, one by one - alms-giving, fasting, the pilgrimage and all the other ordinances of Islam. He went on to refer to each one specifically. When he had finished, he said, "I testify that there is no god but God, and I testify that Mu/:1ammad is the Messenger of God. And I shall perform these duties and shall abstain from all that you .prohibit. And I shall not add nor subtract anything."
'''He then went to his camel to go horne, The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "If that man with the two pigtails spoke the truth, he will enter paradise!"
'''When J;>imam reached his camel, he untied it and travelled horne. His people gathered to meet him and the first thing .he said was, "AI-Lat and aI-'Uzza are evil!" They told him, "Take care, J;>imam! You might get leprosy! You might get e1ephantisis! You might go crazy!"
'''He replied, 'Confound you all! Those two can do no harm nor good. God has sent a Messenger and revealed to him a book with which I will try to save you from your former error. I testify that there is no god but God alone who has no partner, and that Mu/:1ammad is His servant and His Me~senger. I have corne to you from him with orders for what you should do and what he forbids you from doing.'
'''And by God, that night and by next morning every man and woman there had become Muslims. And Ibn 'Abbas said, "We never heard of anyone who carne as a finer representative of any tribe than J;>imam b. Thaqaba."'"
lessons: Sincerity in seeking truth, even when asking bold or direct questions, is honoured rather than punished — the Prophet's patience with Dimam's blunt questioning shows that genuine inquiry is always welcome.
One courageous person returning to their community with truth can transform an entire people overnight; individual conviction, when shared clearly and fearlessly, carries enormous power.
True faith is expressed in commitment to action — Dimam's declaration that he would 'not add nor subtract anything' reflects the Islamic ideal of wholehearted, undivided devotion.
practical_advice: When you feel anxious about asking an important question, remember that honest inquiry is a sign of intelligence and sincerity — approach the conversation with respect and directness rather than holding back out of fear.
If you have experienced a positive change in your life, share it with those closest to you openly and clearly; your personal conviction can be the spark that guides others.

When you make a commitment — to God, to yourself, or to others — write it down simply and completely, then resolve to neither exaggerate nor diminish it, just as Dimam pledged to add nothing and subtract nothing.

title: The Desert Man's Direct Questioning of the Prophet Witnessed by Anas b. Malik
summary: A man from the desert arrives at the mosque, adjures the Prophet by God to confirm each pillar of Islam one by one, then departs declaring he will add nothing and subtract nothing, prompting the Prophet to say he will enter paradise.
story:
Imam Al,unad stated that it was related to him by Hashim b. aI-Qasim, quoting Sulayman - meaning Ibn aI-Mughira - from Thabit, from Anas b. Malik, who said, "We had been forbidden to pose any questions to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and it would amaze us to have some intelligent man come in from the desert and ask him questions while we listened.
"One man arrived from the desert and asked, 'Mui;Iammad, your messenger' came to us and claimed that you claim that God sent you.' 'He spoke the truth,' he replied.
"'So who created the sky?' he asked. 'God.' 'And who created the earth?' 'God.' 'And who set up these mountains and placed things upon them?'
"He went on, 'By Him who created the sky and the earth and set up these mountains, w~s it God who sent you?' 'Yes.'
"'Your messenger claimed that we are required to perform five prayers each day and night.' 'He spoke the truth.' 'By Him who sent you, was it God who gave you this order?' 'Yes.'
"'Your messenger claimed that we are to give zakiU payments from our assets.' 'He spoke the truth.' 'By Him who sent you with the truth, did He really give you this order?' 'Yes.'
"'Your messenger claimed that we are required to fast for one month each year.' 'He spoke the truth.' 'By Him who sent you, was it God who gave you this order?' 'Yes,' he replied.
"'And your messenger claimed that everyone with the necessary means is required to make a pilgrimage to the ka'ba.' 'He spoke the truth.'
"The man then turned aside, saying, 'By Him who sent you with the truth, I will not add to nor subtract from these at all.'
"The Prophet (SAAS) said, 'If he has spoken the truth, he will enter paradise.'"
lessons: Clarity and simplicity in faith are virtues — the desert man's approach of verifying each obligation methodically before committing demonstrates that deliberate, reasoned acceptance of truth is deeply valued.

The Companions' observation that they benefited from the questions of outsiders reminds us that we can learn from those who come to a subject fresh, without the inhibitions that familiarity sometimes creates.

A complete and unadorned commitment — 'I will not add nor subtract' — is recognised as the highest standard of sincerity, suggesting that integrity lies in doing exactly what is required, no more and no less.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by religious or moral obligations, try addressing them one at a time, confirming your understanding and commitment to each before moving to the next, rather than taking on everything at once in a vague way.

If you find yourself unable to ask questions in a group setting due to social pressure, seek out one-on-one opportunities to ask the questions you genuinely need answered — clarity of understanding is worth pursuing.
Write out your core commitments plainly and revisit them regularly to make sure you are neither over-burdening yourself with extras nor neglecting what is truly required of you.

title: Zayd al-Khayr's Conversion, Renaming, and Death from Fever
summary: The Prophet renamed Zayd al-Khayl to Zayd al-Khayr, gifted him land, and predicted he would catch the Medina fever. Zayd died from fever on his return journey, and his wife burned all his documents out of ignorance.
story: God (SAAS) then renamed him Zayd al-Khayr, 'Zayd the good', instead of Zayd al-Khayl, 'Zayd of the horses'. He then deeded to him Fayd and two properties along with it, giving him a document to that effect. "Zayd then left to travel back to his people; as he did so, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Let's hope Zayd avoids catching the Medina fever.' "The source for this report said that he did not actually use the term zumma, 'fever', nor did he use its commonly used nickname Umm Maldam; however, my source did not actually give the term he used." Ibn Ishaq continued, "When Zayd travelled on and reached one of the wells in Najd called Farda, he was fatally stricken by the fever. As he sensed the approach of death, he said, 'Shall my people move on in the morning to the East while I am left in a house in Farda in Nejd? Many was the day that women would visit with me, even when sick, or tired but not exhausted by journeying.' "After his death, his wife, due to her ignorance and the scant nature of both her intellect and her faith, took all the documents Zayd had with him and burned them."
lessons: Even the most promising new believers may face tests and trials that cut their journey short, reminding us that the length of one's life matters less than the quality of one's faith and deeds.
The Prophet's concern for Zayd's wellbeing, even as he departed, reflects the Islamic value of caring for others and praying for their protection from harm.
Ignorance and weak faith can lead people to destructive actions — a reminder of the importance of seeking knowledge and strengthening one's connection to God.
practical_advice: When you welcome a new beginning or fresh start, hold it with gratitude but also prepare spiritually for the trials that may accompany it.
Pray for those you care about as they depart from your company, as the Prophet did for Zayd, making du'a a habit of farewell.
Seek knowledge actively, because ignorance — even well-intentioned — can lead to harm for yourself and those around you.

title: The Story of 'Adi b. Hatim al-Ta'i: From Fleeing the Prophet to Embracing Islam
summary: 'Adi b. Hatim, a Christian chieftain who initially fled the Prophet out of hatred and fear, was moved to accept Islam after witnessing the Prophet's humility, prophetic knowledge, and compassionate treatment of his captive sister.
story:
Ibn Ishaq stated, "'Adi b. Hatim, so I have been told, used to say, 'No Arab more disliked the Messenger of God (SAAS), when he first heard of him than did I. Of high birth, I was also a Christian. I used to move among my people collecting my fourth share. I was content about my religion and was treated by my subjects like a king. '
"'When I heard about the Messenger of God (SAAS), I hated him. I told an Arab slave of mine who was herdsman for my camels, "Get some of my camels well trained and fattened and keep them close by me. If you hear of Muhammad's army setting foot in this area, then warn me." And he did so.
"'He came to me one morning and said, "'Adi, do now whatever you intended if Muhammad's cavalry were to come; I've seen banners, and when I asked about them I was told they were Muhammad's armies." I told him, "Bring up my camels!" And he did so. Having mounted up my family, I announced, "I'm going to join my fellow Christians in Syria."
"'I travelled to al-Jawshiyya, at which town I left a daughter of (my father) Hatim. When I reached Syria, I took up residence there. Muhammad's cavalry came on after me and among the prisoners they took was Hatim's daughter. She was conducted in to the Messenger of God (SAAS), along with other prisoners of Banu Tayyi'; he had heard of my flight to Syria.
"'Hatim's daughter was placed in an enclosure near the door of the mosque where prisoners were kept. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) happened to pass by there, Hatim's daughter, a woman of fine intellect, arose to greet him and said, "Messenger of God, my father is dead and my wafid, 'guardian', has gone. So please spare me - and may God spare you!"
"'He asked, "And who is your guardian?" "'Adi b. Hatim," she replied. "The one who fled from God and His Messenger?" he asked.
"'She (later) stated, "He then went away, leaving me there. Next morning, he again passed by; I said as I had before, and he made the same response as the previous day."
"The following morning, he again passed and I was in despair. A man following behind him gestured to me to get up and speak to him. I did so, saying, 'Messenger of God, my father is dead and my guardian absent; please spare me. And may God spare you!'"
"He responded, 'So be it. But do not hasten to leave before you find someone of your people whom you trust who can conduct you home. Let me know.' I asked about the man who had gestured to me to speak to him and I was told that he was 'Ali b. Abu Talib."
"'She went on, "I remained until riders came from Bali or Quda'a. All I wanted was to go to my brother in Syria. So I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him, 'Messenger of God, a group of my people have arrived and I consider them trustworthy.'" She went on, "He gave me clothing and a mount and expense money; I then left with them and arrived in Syria."'"
'Adi went on, "There I was, I swear, sitting with my family when I saw a howdah heading for our people. I said, 'That will be Hatim's daughter.' And there she was! When she stood there before me, she burst out, 'You absolute villain! You carried off your wife and family, yet left your father's daughter unprotected!' I responded, 'Come on now, sister; speak only good! I swear, I have no excuse. I did just as you say.'
"She then dismounted and remained there with me. I asked her, knowing her to be a sensible woman, 'What is your view of this man?' She replied, 'In my opinion you should join him quickly. If the man is a prophet, then all the more virtue to those who go to him first. If he is a king, then you'll not be bringing any shame to the glory of Yemen, being who you are.'
"I said, 'By God, that's good advice.' I then left and went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) in Medina. I went to him in his mosque and greeted him. He asked, 'Who is this man?' I replied, ''Adi b. Hatim.'
"The Messenger of God (SAAS) arose and took me home with him. On the way with me there an infirm old woman came up and stopped him. He stood there talking with her for a long time about her problem. I told myself, 'By God, this is no king!'
"The Messenger of God (SAAS) then took me to his home. When I went inside, he picked up a leather cushion stuffed with palm leaves and threw it to me, saying, 'Here, sit down on this.' I replied, 'No, you sit on it.' 'No, you,' he insisted.
"So I did sit, while he seated himself on the floor. Again I told myself, 'This is not the action of a king!'
"He then said, 'Well then, 'Adi b. Hatim, so you're a rakusi, right?' 'That's right,' I agreed. 'You've been taking a quarter share from your people, haven't you?' he asked. 'Yes,' I agreed. 'But that's not appropriate for you in your religion,' he commented. 'I swear, you're quite right,' I said, conscious of him being a prophet, aware of things not well known.
"He then said, 'Maybe, 'Adi, the only thing preventing you joining the faith is the poverty of our people. I swear by God, wealth is about to flood over them so that there won't be enough to take it! Or perhaps you don't enter the faith because of the large number of our enemies. I swear by God, you'll soon be hearing of women who come riding camels from Qadisiyya to visit this house, and without being afraid. Or maybe what's holding you back is your seeing that others have control and sovereignty over them. I swear by God, you'll soon hear of the white castles of Babylon being conquered by them.'"
'Adi concluded, "At that I accepted Islam."
Ibn Ishaq's account continues, "'Adi used to say, 'Two of these (predictions) came to pass, but the third remained unfulfilled. But, I swear, it too was to be fulfilled! I did see the white castles of Babylon conquered. And I also saw women borne on howdahs from al-Qadisiyya and make pilgrimage to the ka'ba without fear. And, I swear, the third will happen too; wealth will flood in until there won't be those to take it!'"
lessons: Pride, status, and attachment to one's inherited religion can be barriers to truth, but sincere inquiry and openness of heart can overcome even the deepest resistance.
The Prophet's humility — stopping for an elderly woman in the street, sitting on the floor so a guest could have the cushion — was itself a form of da'wah more powerful than any argument.
Wise counsel from those closest to us, even when it stings, can be the turning point that changes the course of our lives, as 'Adi's sister's honest words changed his.
practical_advice: When you feel resistant to a truth or a change, pause and ask yourself honestly: is your resistance based on evidence, or on fear of losing status, comfort, or familiar identity?
Practice humility in your daily interactions — giving others precedence, listening patiently to those who are struggling — as these acts speak louder than any claim of good character.
Seek the honest counsel of people you trust, especially when you are at a crossroads, and be willing to hear hard truths from them with an open heart.

title: Hatim's Aunt Pleads Before the Prophet and Urges 'Adi to Embrace Islam
summary: 'Adi b. Hatim's aunt was taken prisoner and pleaded before the Prophet, who honoured her and provided her a howdah; upon her return she rebuked 'Adi and urged him to go to the Prophet, which ultimately led to his conversion.
story:
Imam Ahmad stated that it was related to him by Muhammad b. Ja'far, quoting Shu'ba, quoting Simak b. Harb, quoting 'Abbad b. Hubaysh, who related the following hadith from 'Adi b. Hatim, who said, "The cavalry of the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived while I was at 'Aqrab. They took prisoner my aunt and others. When these were later lined up before the Messenger of God (SAAS), she said, 'Messenger of God, my guardian is far away, and I am separated from my children. I'm just an elderly woman with no servants. Spare me - and may God spare you!'
"He asked her, 'Who is your guardian?' ''Adi b. Hatim,' she replied.
"He asked, 'The man who fled from God and His Messenger?' 'Spare me,' she asked.
"When he next came back, there was another man at his side who I think to have been 'Ali, who said, 'Ask him for a howdah.' She did so, and he ordered one."
'Adi went on, "Then she came to me and said, 'Your father would never have done as you have! Go to him, whether hoping for gain or pity. So-and-so went and were rewarded, as others have been.'
"I did go to him and he had with him a woman with two children and another boy. He mentioned their relationship to him. I then knew that this was not the domain of some Chosroe or Caesar.
"The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked, ''Adi b. Hatim, what made you flee? Was it having the words, "There is no god but God" spoken? Is there any god but God?"
lessons: Compassion toward the vulnerable — even those connected to one's opponents — is a mark of true moral greatness and can be a more effective call to faith than any debate.
Family members who care for us enough to speak honestly, even harshly, are a blessing; their words, though difficult to hear, may be the catalyst for our most important decisions.
Flight from truth is ultimately futile; what we run from has a way of finding us through the people and circumstances we love most.
practical_advice: When you are in a position of authority or strength, look for opportunities to show mercy and generosity to those who are vulnerable, regardless of their background or the actions of their relatives.
If a trusted family member or friend confronts you about a wrong you have committed, resist the urge to be defensive and instead reflect honestly on whether their words are true.
When you are avoiding a difficult truth or a necessary change, ask yourself what you are truly afraid of losing, and whether holding onto it is worth the cost to your integrity and relationships.

title: Adi ibn Hatim's Journey to the Prophet and Acceptance of Islam
summary: Adi ibn Hatim, a Christian Arab chieftain, flees to Byzantine territory upon hearing of the Prophet's mission but eventually visits him and accepts Islam after the Prophet addresses his doubts and makes remarkable prophecies.
story:
and said, 'Your father would never have done as you have! Go to him, whether hoping for gain or pity. So-and-so went and were rewarded, as others have been.' "I did go to him and he had with him a woman with two children and another boy. He mentioned their relationship to him. I then knew that this was not the domain of some Chosroe or Caesar. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked, "Adi b. Hatim, what made you flee? Was it having the words, 'There is no god but God' spoken? Is there any god but God? What made you flee? Was it due to the words, 'Allahu Akbar! God is most Great!' spoken? Is there anything greater than God, Almighty and Glorious is He?' So I accepted Islam and saw his face rejoice. He then said, 'The ones al-maghdub alayhim, who have incurred (God's) wrath, refers to the Jews; the al-dalin, those who are straying refers to the Christians.' "Then questions were asked of him, and he gave praise and thanks to God. He went on to say, 'To proceed: people, it is up to you to give from (your) surplus. Give a man (the measure of) a sa'; or one part of a sa'; or a handful; or part of a handful.' (Shu'ba stated, 'As far as I know, he also said, "or a date; or half a date".')
"(The Prophet (SAAS) went on) 'Each of you will meet God, saying as I am, "Did I not make you hearing and seeing? Did I not give you wealth and children? Yet what have you offered?" Each person will then look before and behind him, to his right and to his left and find nothing but hell-fire wherever he looks. Fear the fire, even if by giving only half a date! And if you do not possess that, then give a kind word. I fear not poverty for you. God will certainly aid you until a howdah-borne woman can travel between al-Hira and Yathrib (Medina) with the worst fear she has being to be robbed while travelling.'"
lessons: Hesitation and doubt before embracing truth is a natural human experience, but sincere seeking leads one to clarity and certainty.
True leadership is not measured by worldly power or wealth — the Prophet's humble household revealed to Adi the spiritual nature of this mission.
Even small acts of generosity, as little as half a date or a kind word, carry immense weight before God and can protect a person from harm in the Hereafter.
practical_advice: When you feel torn between the familiar and what you sense may be true, take the courageous step of investigating with an open heart rather than fleeing from uncertainty.
In moments of spiritual confusion, look beyond outward appearances — true guidance often comes from humble, sincere sources rather than grand displays of power.
Practice giving generously even in small ways; a kind word, a small donation, or a moment of your time can create ripple effects far beyond what you can see.

title: The Prophet's Prophecies to Adi ibn Hatim — Conquest of Chosroe and Safe Travel for Women
summary: The Prophet foretells to Adi ibn Hatim that women will travel safely from al-Hira to circumambulate the Ka'ba, the treasuries of Chosroe will be conquered, and wealth will be so abundant no one will accept charity — all of which Adi later witnessed being fulfilled.
story:
"So I told myself, 'If I go to this man, it will not harm me if he is a liar; and if he is being truthful, then I will have learned.' So I did go to him and when I arrived, people said, 'It's Adi b. Hatim!'
"'When I went in to see the Messenger of God (SAAS), he asked me, Adi b. Hatim, aslim, taslim!, "accept Islam and be safe!" He said this three times.'
"I replied, 'But I already have a faith.' He commented, 'I know more about your faith than you do!'
"'You know more of my faith than I?' I asked. 'Yes,' he said. 'Aren't you of the al-rakusiyya? And yet you take a fourth of your people's produce?' 'Yes,' I acknowledged. 'Well,' he commented, 'that's not permissible for you in your faith.' 'Yes,' I agreed. And as soon as he said this, I came to agree about it.
"He went on, 'And I know what is keeping you from Islam. You say, "It's only those with the least power who follow him, those with no power, and the Arabs attack them." Do you know al-Hira?' 'I've not seen it; but I have heard of it,' I replied. 'I swear by Him who holds my soul in His hand,' he went on, 'God will so conclude all this that women borne on howdahs will come out of al-Hira and circumambulate the ka'ba without having any to protect them! And He will conquer the treasuries of Chosroe, son of Hunnuz! The treasures of Chosroe, son of Hunnuz, and the money will be distributed bountifully until there will be no one to accept it!'
"And Adi b. Hatim went on, 'these women borne on camels did come and circumambulate the ka'ba without (needing) any to protect them. Moreover, I was one of those who conquered the treasuries of Chosroe and the third prediction will come about because it was the Messenger of God (SAAS) who said it!'"
lessons: The fulfillment of prophecy is among the clearest signs of divine guidance; witnessing predicted events come true strengthens faith profoundly.
Social weakness and lack of worldly power are not indicators of falsehood — many great movements began among the humble and dispossessed.
God's promise of ease and prosperity after difficulty is real; what appears impossible from a position of fear or weakness can become reality through sincere faith and effort.
practical_advice: When doubt arises about whether your faith or effort will lead anywhere, recall historical examples of seemingly impossible promises that were fulfilled through trust in God.
Do not judge the truth of a message by the social status of those who carry it — investigate the substance and character of the message itself.
Keep a record of moments when you witnessed God's promises fulfilled in your own life; revisiting these strengthens your trust during difficult periods.

title: The Captive Daughter of Hatim al-Ta'i Pleads Before the Prophet
summary: When women of the Tayyi' tribe are brought as captives, the daughter of the famous generous Arab chieftain Hatim al-Ta'i eloquently pleads her case before the Prophet, who releases her in honor of her father's noble character despite him having died as a non-believer.
story:
"'Ali b. Abu Talib stated, 'Glory be to God! How many a man who acts pious for gain! How strange is someone who is approached by his Muslim brother in need yet does not see his way to doing good. For even if he did not hope for reward or fear punishment, he ought to make haste in acting with nobility, for it is such deeds that lead to success.'
"A man thereupon arose to ask him, 'O Commander of the Believers, I pledge you my father and my mother, did you hear that from the Messenger of God (SAAS)?' 'Ali replied, 'Yes. And I have a tale even better than that. When the Tayyi' women were brought in as captives, one of them was red-haired, cherry-lipped, smooth-skinned, slender-necked, fine-nosed, with an erect figure, raised head, full ankles, plump legs, rounded thighs, slim waist, slender sides and well-shaped body. I was much struck by her when I saw her and said I would request the Messenger of God (SAAS), to award her to me in my portion of the booty. But when she spoke, I forgot her beauty for the eloquence I heard. She said, "O Muhammad, will you not release me and spare me the malicious gloating of the Arab tribes, for I am the daughter of the leader of my people. My father was guardian of our sacred objects; he relieved the distressed, fed the hungry, clothed the naked, gave generous hospitality, provided the best of food, spread peace abroad, and never refused the request of the needy. I am the daughter of Hatim al-Ta'i."
"'The Prophet (SAAS) replied, "O girl, all that truly describes the Believers. Had your father been a believer, we would certainly have been merciful to him. Release her, for her father was a man who loved noble character traits, and God Almighty loves noble character traits."
"'At that Abu Burda b. Niyar arose and asked, "Messenger of God, does God really love noble character?"
"'The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "By Him in whose hand is my soul, no one will enter heaven except by noble character."'"
lessons: Noble character — generosity, feeding the hungry, relieving the distressed — is recognized and honored by God even across the boundaries of creed and time.
Speaking truth with dignity and eloquence in a moment of vulnerability can move hearts and change outcomes in ways that silence cannot.
The Prophet's mercy extended even to those outside his community, demonstrating that Islam's values of compassion and nobility are universal in their application.
practical_advice: When you face a situation where you feel powerless or at the mercy of others, speak your truth with calm dignity and let your character and lineage of goodness speak for you.
Cultivate noble character traits — generosity, compassion, hospitality — not for reward or recognition but because these are qualities that God Himself loves.
If you are in a position of authority or judgment over others, look beyond group identity or circumstance and recognize virtue wherever it genuinely exists.

title: The Prophet's Prayer for Banu Daws Instead of Against Them
summary: Al-Tufayl b. Amr came to the Prophet asking him to pray against Banu Daws for their disobedience and rejection of Islam, but the Prophet instead prayed for their guidance.
story: Al-Bukhari, may God have mercy upon him, also gave a section under the heading, The Story of (Banu) Daws and al-Tufayl b. 'Amr. He stated that it was related to him by Abu Nu'aym, quoting Sufyan, from Ibn Dhakwan - he being 'Abd Allah b. Ziyad - from 'Abd al-Rahman al-A'raj, from Abu Hurayra, who said, "Al-Tufayl b. 'Amr came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and told him, '(Banu) Daws are done for; they have been disobedient and have refused (Islam); so pray to God against them.' However, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'O God, give Daws guidance and bring them.'"
lessons: Responding to hostility and rejection with prayers for guidance rather than punishment reflects the highest moral character.
Despair about others' spiritual state should not lead us to give up on them; the Prophet's mercy extended even to those who rejected him.
True strength lies in seeking good for one's enemies, not in seeking their destruction.
practical_advice: When someone wrongs or rejects you, resist the impulse to wish harm upon them and instead pray for their wellbeing and guidance.
If you feel hopeless about a difficult relationship or situation, reframe your prayers toward positive outcomes rather than punitive ones.
Practice mercy as an active choice, especially when frustration tempts you toward anger or retribution.

title: Abu Hurayra's Journey to the Prophet and Freeing His Escaped Slave
summary: Abu Hurayra recounts his emotional journey traveling to pledge allegiance to the Prophet, during which a slave escaped, but upon arriving Abu Hurayra freed the slave for God's sake.
story: Muhammad b. al-'Ala' related to us, quoting Abu Usama, quoting Isma'il, from Qays, from Abu Hurayra, who said, 'As I made my way to the Messenger of God (SAAS), I recited to myself, "O night, how long and difficult you are; however, you have freed me from the abode of disbelief!" On the way, a slave of mine escaped and, having reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), and pledged my allegiance to him, I was still there with him when the slave suddenly appeared. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told me, "Abu Hurayra, this is your slave." I replied, "He is freed for God's sake." And I manumitted him.'
lessons: The journey toward faith often involves hardship and loss, but arriving at truth makes those sacrifices feel worthwhile.
Acts of generosity and liberation become most meaningful when done purely for God's sake, without expectation of reward.
Even in moments of personal achievement, looking outward and acting with compassion toward others elevates the soul.
practical_advice: When facing a long or difficult personal journey, remind yourself of what you are moving toward, not just what you are leaving behind.
Look for opportunities to act generously at moments of personal joy or success, as these acts are especially powerful.
Reflect on what 'freedom' means in your own life — what habits, relationships, or beliefs are you being freed from as you grow?

title: The Prophet's Joy at the Arrival of Ja'far and the Ash'ari People from Yemen
summary: When the Ash'ari people arrived from Yemen alongside Ja'far b. Abu Talib returning from Abyssinia, the Prophet expressed that he did not know which event made him happier — their arrival or the conquest of Khaybar.
story: They came in the company of Abu Musa al-Ash'ari, accompanied by Ja'far b. Abu Talib and his fellow muhajirin who had been in Abyssinia. And this all happened while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was engaged in the conquest of Khaybar, as we have detailed in its appropriate place above. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had then made his statement, 'I swear by God, I don't know which makes me happier; the arrival of Ja'far, or the conquest of Khaybar!'
lessons: The reunion of loved ones and the return of those who sacrificed for faith can bring joy equal to great worldly victories.
Human connection and community are among the greatest gifts, cherished even by the Prophet above material conquest.
Expressing heartfelt joy and gratitude for the people around us reflects a generous and loving spirit.
practical_advice: Do not underestimate how much your presence and return means to those who love you — reach out and reconnect with people you have been separated from.
Cultivate the ability to feel and express genuine joy at the good fortune and arrival of others, as this strengthens community bonds.
Reflect on whether you are placing too much value on external achievements and too little on relationships and reunion.

title: The Prophet's Promise to Jabir and Abu Bakr's Fulfillment of It After the Prophet's Death
summary: The Prophet had promised Jabir b. Abd Allah a gift from the money expected from al-Bahrayn, but it arrived only after the Prophet's death; Caliph Abu Bakr honored the promise and gave Jabir the full amount.
story: Qutayba b. Sa'id related to him, quoting Sufyan, quoting Muhammad b. al-Munkadir, quoting Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) told me, 'When the money arrives from al-Bahrayn, I'll give you so-much and so-much and so-much.' He gave three figures. But the money from al-Bahrayn did not arrive before the Messenger of God (SAAS) died. When it came in for (the Caliph) Abu Bakr, he ordered a crier to announce, 'Anyone having an outstanding debt or payment due him from the Messenger of God (SAAS) should come to me.' Jabir went on, 'So I went to Abu Bakr and told him that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had said, "When the money arrives from Bahrayn, I'll give you so-much and so-much," mentioning three figures. But he turned away from me. So thereafter I again met Abu Bakr and asked him, but again he gave me nothing. Once more I went and he still did not give to me. And I did this yet a third time, with him still not giving to me. I then told him, "I have repeatedly come to you and asked you to give me payment but you have not done so. Either you pay me, or you will be treating me in a miserly manner. Are you being a miser?" He replied, "What curse would be worse than miserliness!" He said this three times, then went on, "Every time I refused you, I really did want to give to you."' And Abu Bakr gave him the money and said, 'So count it.' I did so and found it to be 500 dirhams. He then said, 'Now, take the same, twice over.'"
lessons: Promises made in good faith must be honored even when circumstances make fulfillment delayed or difficult.
Persistence in seeking what is rightfully yours is not shameful; patient and repeated advocacy can be a form of trust in justice.
True generosity is not diminished by delay — what matters is the sincerity of intent and the eventual fulfillment of one's word.
practical_advice: If you have made a commitment you have not yet been able to fulfill, make a sincere plan to honor it rather than letting it quietly fade.
When you feel your legitimate needs are being overlooked, it is appropriate to advocate for yourself calmly and persistently.
Reflect on any unfinished promises or obligations in your life and take one concrete step today toward fulfilling them.

title: Farwa b. Musayk Leaves the Kings of Kinda and Comes to the Prophet
summary: Farwa b. Musayk, after his tribe suffered defeat at the battle of al-Radm and after becoming disillusioned with the kings of Kinda, made his way to the Prophet and was appointed governor over his people.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Farwa b. Musayk al-Muradi came in to the Messenger of God (SAAS), having abandoned and separated from the kings of Kinda. A conflict had arisen between his people of Murad and (Banu) Hamdan. In that engagement, known as the battle of Radm, Hamdan had severely beaten his people; the leader of the Hamdan force had been al-Ajda' b. Malik." Ibn Ishaq stated, "Farwa b. Musayk spoke the following verses on that day: 'They passed by Lafat, their eyes fatigued, pulling at their reins, turning aside. Should we be conquered, well, we were conquerors once, and if (now) conquered, then by others than those we conquered. It is not our way to be cowards, but our fate and the wishes of others (influence the outcome). Thus it is that fate revolves, its revolutions going around and around. We may be pleased and contented with our lot and may have enjoyed its bounties for years. The cycles of fate may reverse their course and those formerly envied are suddenly crushed. Those who are envied for bounty from fate may find the turns of time treacherous. If kings were immortal, so would we be; and if men of nobility lived for ever, so would we. The leaders of my people pass away, just as have those of former times.' Ibn Ishaq went on, 'And as Farwa left the kings of Kinda on his way to the Messenger of God (SAAS), he said: "Having seen how the kings of Kinda went astray, like a man betrayed by his thigh joints, I brought up my mount to head for Muhammad, hoping for his favours and all they entail."' When Farwa reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), the latter asked him, so I have been told, 'Farwa, did what befell your people at the battle of al-Radm truly upset you?' He replied, 'Messenger of God, what man would not be upset to have happen to his people what happened to mine?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then told him, 'That can only compound for your people the benefit Islam will bring them.' He then appointed Farwa as governor over Murad, all of Zubayd and Madhij, sending out with him Khalid b. Sa'id b. al-'As to collect the alms; he remained there with him."
lessons: Defeat and loss can become the turning point that redirects a person toward a greater and more meaningful path.
Acknowledging grief honestly — 'what man would not be upset?' — is not weakness; it is the beginning of healing.
Faith has the power to transform the meaning of past suffering, turning what seemed like pure loss into the foundation for future good.
practical_advice: When you experience a significant defeat or setback, allow yourself to grieve it honestly rather than suppressing the pain.
Consider whether a current disappointment or failure might be redirecting you toward something more aligned with your values and purpose.
Look for a trusted person or mentor who can help reframe your losses as potential sources of strength and new direction, just as the Prophet reframed Farwa's defeat.

title: Farwa b. Musayk's Appointment as Governor After the Battle of al-Radino
summary: Farwa b. Musayk expressed grief over what befell his people at the battle of al-Radino, and the Prophet reassured him that this would compound the benefit Islam would bring them, then appointed him as governor over Murad, Zubayd, and Madhij.
story: of God CSAAS), the latter asked him, so I have been told, 'Farwa, did what befell your people at the battle of al-Radino truly upset you?' "He replied, 'Messenger of God, what man would not be upset to have happen to his people what happened to mine?' "The Messenger of God CSAAS) then told him, 'That can only compound for your people the benefit Islam will bring them.' He then appointed Farwa as governor over Murad, all of Zubayd and Madl.tij, sending out with him KhaIid b. Sa'id b. a1-'A~ to collect the alms; he remained there with him in his territory until the death of the Messenger of God CSAAS).'
lessons: Grief and loss can be transformed into a foundation for greater purpose and benefit.
A true leader acknowledges the pain of those under his care before offering perspective or guidance.
Trusting in God's plan does not mean suppressing sorrow, but rather holding both grief and hope simultaneously.
practical_advice: When you experience a painful loss, allow yourself to grieve honestly rather than suppressing your feelings, knowing that difficulty can become the root of deeper blessings.
Seek out a trusted mentor or spiritual guide when you are struggling with grief, as a wise word of perspective can help reframe suffering without dismissing it.
Reflect on past hardships in your life and consider how they may have ultimately led to growth, strengthened character, or opened doors you would not have otherwise found.

title: The Arrival of 'Amr b. Ma'di Karib and His Acceptance of Islam
summary: 'Amr b. Ma'di Karib urged his tribal leader Qitys to investigate the Prophet's claim, and when Qitys refused, 'Amr travelled alone to the Prophet, accepted Islam, and later composed verses defending his decision.
story: Ibn Isl.taq stated, "When news of the Messenger of God CSAAS) had reached them, 'Amr b. Ma'di Karib had said to Qitys b. Makshul.t a1-Muradi, 'Qitys, you are your people's leader; we have been told that a man of Quraysh named Mul.tammad has come forth in I:Iijaz saying that he is a prophet. Let's go to him and gain knowledge of him. If a prophet, as he says, we'll find out, and if we decide him to be one, we'll follow hino. If not, we'll know that too.' But Qitys refused to do this and ridiculed his view. "'Amr b. Ma'di Karib then left and rode to the Messenger of God CSAAS); he accepted Islam, believing and having faith in him. When news of that reached Qitys b. Makshul.t, he made threats against 'Amr for having acted against his own wishes and views. "On that subject, 'Amr b. Ma'di Karib spoke the following verses: 'I advised you on that day at San<a in a manner that was plainly wise I advised you to fear God, to act with charity and to subject yourself to Him. But you went off out of your lust, like an ass led on by his member! He wanted me to be on a horse, seated on it like a lion. Dressed in a coat of mail (shining) like a pool of water, its water clear, lying on rocky, flat ground. Armor that will fend off lances having bent their heads, making them fly off in pieces. Had you met me in battle, you would have found me like a lion with a towering mane, A fierce lion, its claws raised about its shoulders Facing its opponent; if his rival makes for him, he throws him over, Grips him, raises him, brings him down and kills him, Crushing his brains, smashing him, biting him and devouring him, Refusing to share what his own fangs and claws grip fast.'"
lessons: Seeking truth requires personal courage, especially when those around you mock or refuse the journey.
The willingness to investigate sincerely, rather than follow the crowd, is a mark of spiritual maturity.
Social pressure and ridicule from peers should not prevent a person from pursuing what their conscience guides them toward.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually drawn to seek truth or make a meaningful change, do not wait for others to validate your decision before you act.
If those around you mock your sincere spiritual inquiries, recognise that their resistance often reflects their own fears rather than a flaw in your search.
Write down or articulate the reasons behind your most important life decisions so that you can defend them clearly and with confidence, as 'Amr did through his poetry.

title: 'Amr b. Ma'di Karib's Apostasy After the Prophet's Death and His Return to Islam
summary: After the death of the Prophet, 'Amr b. Ma'di Karib apostatized along with others but later returned to Islam, going on to become a celebrated warrior and poet who participated in the great conquests.
story: Ibn Isi).aq proceeded, "'Amr b. Ma'di Karib remained there among his people of Banu Zubayd while Farwa b. Musayk was their governor. Following the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'Amr b. Ma'di Karib apostatized along with the others and spoke the following verses satirizing Farwa b. Musayk: 'We found the rule of Farwa the worst ever - an ass, his nose sniffing at a female organJ When one looks at Abu <Umayr, one is reminded of the disgusting afterbirth from a carnel!'" I note that he returned to Islam thereafter and was a good Muslim. He participated in many of the conquests made during the rule of Abu Bakr and that of'Vmar, may God be pleased with them both. He was a brave and renowned warrior and hero, as well as a fine poet. He died in 21 AH after having taken part in the battle of Nahawand; it is alternatively said that he took part in the battle of al-Qiidisiyya and was killed On that day.
lessons: Returning to the right path after going astray is always possible, and one's later good deeds can define their legacy more than their moments of failure.
The death of a beloved leader or spiritual anchor can cause a profound crisis of faith; this is a known and human vulnerability, not an unforgivable one.
A person's worth is not permanently defined by their lowest moments; sincere return and renewed action can transform the narrative of a life.
practical_advice: If you have strayed from your values or faith during a period of grief or instability, know that the door to return is always open and your previous good is not erased.
During times of communal loss or upheaval, be especially watchful of the company you keep, as collective despair can pull even sincere people toward poor decisions.
Reflect on people in history who fell and then rose again; let their stories remind you that a stumble does not have to be a permanent fall.

title: The Verses of 'Amr b. Ma'di Karib: Belief in the Prophet Without Having Seen Him
summary: A version of the account holds that 'Amr b. Ma'di Karib never personally visited the Prophet, yet composed moving verses expressing his faith, describing the Prophet as the master of all men and a light that guided people from blindness.
story: Yunus stated, quoting from Ibn Isi).aq, who said, "It has been said that 'Amr b. Ma'di Karib did not actually go to see the Prophet (SAAS). On that subject he ('Amr b. Ma'di Karib) is quoted as having spoken the following verses: 'I am in myself fully convinced by the Prophet, even if I have not seen him with my own eyes. He is the master of all men, and the closest to God when his-stature became manifest. He brought the law from the preseuce of God, and he was al-Amin, "the trustworthy", given help thereby. In wisdom after wisdom and in light by which we were led aright out of our blindness. We rode along the (right) path when we rode with him, new in both what we dislike and liked. We worshipped God in truth, whereas before having worshipped idols, in our ignorance, We became allied with him, whereas we had been enemies, and we came back with him, as friends. Peace be upon him, and peace from us (to him) wherever we were and wherever he be. Even if we did not see the Prophet, we followed his path in faith.'"
lessons: Faith can be deep and sincere even without direct personal experience; conviction rooted in reflection and testimony is a legitimate and powerful form of belief.
The transformation from enmity to friendship, and from ignorance to guidance, is one of the most profound gifts that faith can offer.
Expressing gratitude and love for what has guided you, even through poetry or personal reflection, strengthens and roots that connection.
practical_advice: If you feel distant from your faith because you cannot see or physically connect with its sources, focus on the wisdom and transformation that faith has produced in your life and in others as tangible evidence.
Try expressing your spiritual journey in your own words, whether through journaling, poetry, or conversation, as articulating your path can deepen your conviction.
Reflect on the specific ways your worldview has changed for the better through faith, and let those changes serve as your personal testimony.

title: The Delegation of al-Ash'ath b. Qays from Kinda and the Question of Ancestry
summary: Al-Ash'ath b. Qays arrived with 80 riders from Kinda dressed elaborately, and after removing silk from their robes at the Prophet's request, claimed shared ancestry with him, which the Prophet gently but firmly corrected by affirming the true lineage of Quraysh.
story: Ibn Isi)aq stated, "Al-Ash'ath b. Q;1ys came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) in a delegation from Kinda. "Al-Zuhri related to me that he came as one of a party of 80 riders from. Kinda. They went in to the Messenger of God (SAAS) in his mosque, having curled their long hair, put kul;/, 'mascara', around their eyes and dressed in multi-coloured robes with silken edges. "When they entered in to the Messenger of God (SAAS), he asked them, 'Would you not accept Islam?' 'Why yes,' they replied. 'Then why is there this silk around your necks?' They ripped it off and discarded it. "Al-Ash'ath b. Q;1ys then told him, 'MessengerofGod, we are, just as you are yourself, from the tribe of the 'aki/ a/-murar, "the one who ate bitter herbs".' "The Messenger of God ($AAS) smiled and said, 'People do ascribe this ancestry to al-'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Mut1;alib and Rabi'a b. al-I:Iarith.' "These two men referred to were merchants and when they travelled out among the Arabs and were asked who they were, they would reply, 'We are sons ofAkil al-Murar'; that is, they would attribute their ancestry to Kinda to acquire prestige. This is because Kinda had been kings. And Kinda considered that Quraysh were their descendants, because of this comment made by 'Abbas and Rabi'a. The full name of this Akil al-Murar was al-l;Iarith b. 'Amr b. l;Iijr b. 'Amr b. Mu'awiya b. al-l;Iarith b. Mu'awiya b. Thawr b. Murti' b. Mu'awiya b. Kindi, also known as Ibn Kinda. "The Messenger of God ($AAS) then told them, 'But really we are descendants of aI-Nagr b. Kinana; we don't cast aspersions on our mothers, nor do we deny our fathers.' "Al-Ash'ath b. Q;tys then told his companions, 'By God, people of Kinda, any man I hear say that in future I'll give 80 lashes!'"
lessons: Integrity in identity means neither inflating one's lineage for social prestige nor denying one's true origins.
Worldly adornments and appearances are the first things to be set aside when one sincerely enters into a spiritual commitment.
A leader's gentle correction of false claims, delivered with a smile rather than condemnation, is often more effective than harsh rebuke.
practical_advice: Examine whether any part of your self-presentation is built on exaggeration or borrowed prestige; authentic identity is more durable and more respected.
When you make a new commitment to your values or faith, look honestly at the habits and attachments that are inconsistent with that commitment and be willing to let them go immediately.
When correcting someone who has overstated a claim, aim for clarity and firmness without unnecessary humiliation, as the Prophet modelled by smiling while correcting.

title: The Prophet's Counsel to al-Ash'ath About Children and Cowardice
summary: When al-Ash'ath mentioned his newborn son and expressed a wish for him to be a provider for his people, the Prophet warned him that such framing would make the child cowardly and pitiable, and instead counselled trust in God's reward.
story:
Imam Ai).mad stated that it was related to him by Shurayi). b. aI-Nu'man, quoting Hushaym, quoting MujaIid, from al~Sha'bI, quoting aI-Ash'ath b. Q;tys as saying, "I went to see the Messenger of God ($AAS), in a delegation from Kinda and he asked me, 'Do you have children?' 'A son was born to me as I was leaving to come to you, her mother being the daughter ofJamad. I want his role to be that of our people's provider.' "He responded, 'Don't say that; they will be made content and given reward, if they should die. And if you say as you have, then they will become cowardly and pitiable; yes, cowardly and pitiable!'"
Ai).mad is alone in giving this; it is a {zadith that is {zasan, "good", and it has a fine line of transmission.
lessons: The intentions and expectations we set for our children shape their character; framing them primarily around worldly provision can instil fear rather than courage.
Placing children in God's care, rather than defining them by their utility to the tribe or family, is a form of deep trust and good parenting.
A wise teacher addresses not just what is said but the underlying worldview it reveals, offering correction that transforms how a person thinks, not just what they say.
practical_advice: Reflect on the expectations you have for your children or those under your care and ask whether those expectations cultivate courage and faith or anxiety and dependency.
When you find yourself defining success for your loved ones primarily in material or social terms, consciously reframe their purpose around character, integrity, and trust in God.
Practice releasing outcomes for those you love to God's care; this does not mean passivity but rather a grounded confidence that is free from the paralysing fear of loss.

title: Al-A'sha Seeks the Prophet's Help to Recover His Runaway Wife Mu'adha
summary: A man named al-A'sha came to the Prophet complaining that his wife Mu'adha had fled to a more powerful tribesman named Mutarrif who refused to return her; the Prophet wrote a letter to Mutarrif commanding him to return her, and she was restored to her husband after securing a pledge that he would not punish her.
story: 'Abd Allah, son of Imam Ai).mad, stated that it was related to him by aI-'Abbas b. 'Aba aI-'~Im aI-'AnbarI, quoting Abii Salama 'Ubayd b. 'Abd al-Rai).man al-I:JanafI, who said, "AI-Junayd b. Amln b. Dhirwa b. Nagla b. Tarlfb. Nah~il aI-I:Jirmilazi related to me, quoting Abii AmIn, quoting his father Dhirwa, from his father Nagla, that one of their men, called aI-A'sha, his actual name being 'Abd Allah aI-A'mmar, had a wife named Mu'adha. He went forth in Rajab to seek supplies from his family from Hijr. After he had left, his wife fled, in defiance of him, and took refuge with a man of their tribe called Mu\arrif b. Nahshal b. Na'b b. Qumaytha' b. Dulafb. AhQam b. 'Abd Allah b. al"I:Iirmaz who hid her. When al"A'sha returned home and did not fmd his wife there, he was told that she had left him and had sought refuge with Mu\arrif b. Nahshal. Al"A'sha went to this man and asked him, 'Cousin, if my wife Mu'adha is with you, hand her over to me.' 'She is not here,' he replied. 'And even if she were, I'd not give her over to you!' "Now Mu\arrifwas more powerful than him. So al"A'sha went to the Prophet ($AAS), to seek his help and recited the following verses: 'Lord of men, he who gives religion to the Arabs, I complain to you about someone sharp of tongue Like a long"haired female wolf in the shade of her lair; I went out to find food for her in Rajab She left me in dispute and fled; she broke the promise and hid in disgrace. She defamed me among the crowd of impure lineage. And women can be overwhelmingly evil to those they overcome.' "At this the Prophet ($AAS) said, 'They can be overwhehningly evil for those (evil spirits) who control them.'" "Al"A'sha went on to complain to him about his wife's behaviour and how she had gone off to a man of their tribe named Mu\arrifb. Nahshal. The Messenger of God ($AAS) then wrote a letter to this Mutarrif saying, 'As for Mu'adha, the wife of this man; return her to him!' "When he received this note and it was read to him, he told her, 'Mu'adha, this is a letter from the Prophet ($AAS) about you. I'm giving you over to him.' She replied, 'Get a firm pact from him and the protection from his prophet that he will not punish me for what I did.' He obtained this commitment and Mu\arrif gave her back to him, whereupon he (al"A'sha) spoke the following verses: 'Neither my love for Mu'adha, I swear, nor time itself, will bring change'"
lessons: Those who are wronged and have no worldly recourse can seek justice through legitimate authority, and such appeals are honourable rather than weak.
Even in personal and domestic disputes, the presence of a just authority can bring resolution without violence or prolonged enmity.
Seeking a formal pledge or assurance before returning to a difficult situation is a sign of wisdom and self-protection, not distrust.
practical_advice: When you feel powerless in the face of an injustice being perpetrated by someone stronger than you, seek out the appropriate channels of legitimate authority rather than resorting to aggression or despair.
If you are trying to restore a broken relationship or resolve a conflict, consider involving a trusted mediator whose authority both parties respect.
Before returning to a situation that previously caused you harm, it is wise to establish clear agreements and mutual commitments so that both parties have a foundation of security.

title: Mu'adha Returned to Her Husband After Prophet's Intervention
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) intervened on behalf of al-A'sha, writing a note to Mutarrif to return his wife Mu'adha to him. Mu'adha requested a firm pact of protection before agreeing to return, and al-A'sha composed verses about the matter.
story: Mutarrif saying, 'As for Mu'adha, the wife of this man; return her to him!' "When he received this note and it was read to him, he told her, 'Mu'adha, this is a letter from the Prophet ($AAS) about you. I'm giving you over to him.' She replied, 'Get a firm pact from him and the protection from his prophet that he will not punish me for what I did.' He obtained this commitment and Mu\arrif gave her back to him, whereupon he (al"A'sha) spoke the following verses: 'Neither my love for Mu'adha, I swear, nor time itself, will bring change to the slanderer The evil she wrought is nothing, made trivial by the lustful calls of men (to her) after me.'"
lessons: The Prophet's compassion extended to resolving personal and family disputes with wisdom and fairness.
Seeking protection and clear terms before reconciliation shows wisdom rather than weakness.
Love can endure betrayal when one chooses to focus on mercy and forgiveness over resentment.
practical_advice: When seeking reconciliation in a relationship, clearly define the terms and expectations before returning, as Mu'adha did.
If you feel wronged in a relationship, seek the guidance of a trusted authority or counselor to facilitate a fair resolution.
When processing hurt from someone you love, find a healthy outlet such as writing or poetry, as al-A'sha did, to express and release those emotions.

title: Surad b. Abd Allah al-Azdi Appointed Governor and His Military Campaign
summary: Surad b. Abd Allah al-Azdi came to the Prophet with a delegation, accepted Islam, and was appointed governor over his people. He was ordered to fight polytheists in neighboring Yemeni tribes and besieged Jurash for about a month before using a strategic retreat to defeat the enemy.
story: Ibn Isl:>aq stated, "$urad b. 'Abd Allah al"Azdl then came in to the Messenger of God ($AAS), among a delegation of Banu al"Azd. He accepted Islam and was a good Muslim. The Messenger of God ($AAS) appointed him as governor over those of his people who had accepted Islam. He gave him orders to fight alongside those who had accepted Islam against the polytheists of the neighbouring tribes of Yemen. "He proceeded to besiege Jurash, wherein were some of the Yemeni tribesmen. Banu Khath'am had taken refuge with them when they had heard of his approach. He besieged them for approximately one month but they held out against him. Surad then retired to a place close by a mountain called Shakar. The enemy, thinking that he had retired in defeat, went out to chase him. He then turned on them and engaged them in fierce battle."
lessons: Accepting Islam and committing to its demands wholeheartedly leads to being entrusted with greater responsibility.
Strategic patience — appearing to retreat — can be more effective than a direct confrontation with an entrenched enemy.
Leadership in Islam comes with serious obligations to guide and protect those under one's care.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult challenge that refuses to yield to direct effort, try stepping back strategically rather than forcing a confrontation — patience can create an opening.
Accept roles of responsibility with sincerity and a willingness to serve, as Surad did, rather than seeking status for its own sake.
When overwhelmed by a seemingly unwinnable situation, reflect on whether a temporary withdrawal to regroup might lead to a better outcome than persisting with a failing approach.

title: The Prophet's Miraculous Knowledge of the Battle at Shakar
summary: Two men from Jurash were in Madinah when the Prophet, after prayer, described a battle happening in real time at a mountain called Shakar. He told them that sacrifices were being offered to God there and then, and upon returning home they confirmed that their people had suffered casualties exactly as the Prophet had described.
story: "Meanwhile, the people of Jurash had sent out two of their men to Medina to observe the Messenger of God (SAAS). One day, following the al-'a" prayer, he asked, 'In which part of God's earth is Shakr?' The two men of Jurash arose and responded, 'Messenger of God, in our territory there is a mountain called Kashar. That is the name the people of Jurash give it.' He commented, 'Well, it's not named "Kashr"; it's "Shakr".' 'Why do you refer to it, Messenger of God?' they asked. 'Bodies are being offered in sacrifice to God there and now.' "The two men sat down next to Abu Bakr [or 'Uthman] and he told them, 'Woe on you both! The Messenger of God (SAAS) has just been informing you of the death of your people; go and ask him to pray to God to spare them.' The two men did so and he replied, 'O God, spare them.' "They then returned home and discovered that their people had indeed suffered casualties on the day the Messenger of God (SAAS) had so informed them."
lessons: The Prophet's knowledge, given by God, transcended time and distance, affirming his prophethood to those who witnessed it.
When a person of wisdom speaks a difficult truth, the wise response is to act on it immediately, as Abu Bakr urged the two men.
Turning to God in supplication, even when a situation already seems dire, is never futile — His mercy can still intervene.
practical_advice: When trusted advisors or mentors point out a serious warning in your situation, do not delay — act immediately rather than sitting in confusion.
In moments of shock or bad news, redirect your energy from paralysis to prayer and constructive action.
Trust that sincere supplication has real effect, even when circumstances appear to have already unfolded — keep turning to God.

title: The Delegation of Jurash Accepts Islam After Their Defeat
summary: Following the military defeat of their people, a delegation from the remaining people of Jurash came to the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) in Madinah, accepted Islam, and were thereafter considered good Muslims. The Prophet placed a protective zone around their village.
story: "A delegation of those of the people of Jurash who were still left then came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and accepted Islam. They were good Muslims thereafter; he placed a protective zone around their village."
lessons: Calamity and defeat can become a doorway to guidance and a better way of life when one responds with humility and openness.
The Prophet's mercy extended to former enemies once they sincerely turned to Islam.
True protection and security comes from aligning oneself with God and His Messenger, not from military strength alone.
practical_advice: In the aftermath of loss or failure, resist the urge to rebuild the same structures that led to your downfall — instead, seek a fundamentally better path.
Approach periods of defeat as potential turning points rather than final endings; many great transformations begin in moments of loss.
When you have been humbled by life's circumstances, use that vulnerability as an opportunity to seek guidance and connect with a community that can offer you protection and belonging.

title: The Kings of Himyar Send Messengers Announcing Acceptance of Islam
summary: After the Prophet's return from Tabuk, messengers arrived from the kings of Himyar in Yemen announcing their acceptance of Islam and abandonment of polytheism. The Prophet responded with a detailed letter outlining religious obligations, tax requirements, the treatment of non-Muslims, and instructions for his envoys sent to Yemen.
story: According to al-Waqidi, this took place in Ramadan, 9 AH. Ibn Isl:>aq stated, "Following his return from TabUk, a document was brought by messengers on behalf of the kings of I:Iimyar announcing their acceptance of Islam. Their names were al-I:Iarith b. ('Abd) Kulal, Nu'aym b. 'Abd Kulal, al-Nu'man, the prince of Dhu Ru'ayn, Ma''afir and Hamdan. Zur'a Dhu Yazan sent to him Malik b. Murrat al-Rahawi with news of their acceptance of Islam and their abandonment of polytheism and those accepting it. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) wrote them the following document: 'In the name of God, the most Merciful and Beneficent. From Muhammad, the Messenger of God, the Prophet, to al-I:Iarith b. 'Abd Kulal, Nu'aym b. 'Abd Kulal, al-Nu'man, prince of Dhu Ru'ayn, Ma'afir and Hamdan. To proceed: to you I give praise to God, the One and only God, Your Messenger came to us upon our return from Byzantine territory. We met in Medina; he informed us of your message, of your wishes, of your acceptance of Islam, of your battling against the polytheists, and of your having been given guidance by God. If you have indeed reformed and have given obedience to God and to His Messenger, then perform the prayers and give the alms. You must also give one-fifth of booty to God, along with the share due to the Prophet (SAAS), that portion he selects, along with the charitable donation required of Muslims. (This consists of) one-tenth of the produce of land watered by wells or rain and one-twentieth of that watered by bucket. For every forty camels you must give one young female foal of a milch-camel and for every thirty camels you must give one young male born to a milch-camel. For every five camels you must give one sheep; for every ten camels, two sheep. For every forty cattle, you must give one cow. For every thirty cattle you must provide a calf that can be either a male or a female. For every forty goats (or sheep) at pasture, you must give one sheep. This donation is a requirement God has made incumbent upon believers; those who do even greater good will benefit thereby. "'Those who fulfil all this, give testimony to their acceptance of Islam, and assist Muslims against polytheists, shall be considered Muslims with all the attendant rights and obligations they have. They shall have the protection of God and that of His Messenger. Those Jews and Christians who accept Islam become Muslims with all the rights and obligations due them. "'Those who remain within Judaism or Christianity shall not be turned away from either, but they must pay the jizya, "the poll-tax", for each adult, whether male or female, free or slave, one full dinar by the value of (gold) dust, or its equivalent value in clothing. Those who pay this to the Messenger of God (SAAS) shall have the protection of God and of His Messenger; those who refuse it will be enemies of God and of His Messenger. "'Further, the Messenger of God, Muhammad, the Prophet, has sent to Zur'a Dhu Yazan the following: "When my envoys, Mu'adh b. Jabal, 'Abd Allah b. Zayd, Malik b. 'Ubada, 'Uqba b. Nimr, Malik b. Murra and their men come to you, I require you to treat them well. Collect the alms and the poll-tax from your territories and deliver all this to my envoys. Their commander is Mu'adh b. Jabal; he is not to return until fully satisfied." "'To proceed further, Muhammad testifies that there is no god but God and that he is His servant and His Messenger. Moreover, Malik b. Murrat al-Rahawi has told me that you are the first men of Himyar to have accepted Islam, and to have fought against the polytheists. I commend you and command you to treat Himyar well; do not betray or forsake them. The Messenger of God (SAAS) is the ally of your rich and of your poor. The alms payments may not legally go to Muhammad or to his household; they are alms payable to poor Muslims and to wayfarers. Malik delivers information and withholds what is confidential. I order you to treat him well. I have dispatched to you some of my finest people, most religious and learned men; I order you to treat them well. They are being watched over. May peace be upon you along with the mercy and blessings of God.'"
lessons: The acceptance of Islam by entire peoples and their rulers demonstrates that guidance can come to anyone at any time, and its arrival should be met with gratitude and immediate commitment to its obligations.
The Prophet's detailed letter shows that true leadership involves clarity — spelling out rights, obligations, and expectations so that no one is left uncertain of what is required.
The protection extended to non-Muslims who lived peaceably under Islamic governance reflects the principle that justice in Islam encompasses all people regardless of faith.
practical_advice: When making a significant life commitment — whether to a faith, a community, or a cause — seek to understand the full scope of its obligations rather than only its benefits.
As a leader or person of influence, follow the Prophet's example of clear written communication: specify roles, expectations, and rights so that those under your guidance are never left in ambiguity.

When you are praised for being among the first to do something good, use that recognition as motivation to continue excelling and to set an example for others, not as a reason to rest on past achievements.

title: The Gift of a Ceremonial Robe from Malik Dhu Yazan to the Prophet
summary: Malik Dhu Yazan, from the kings of Himyar, presented the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) with a valuable ceremonial robe for which he had paid either 33 baggage camels or 33 riding camels, a gesture marking the newfound bond between the kings of Himyar and the Prophet.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Hasan related to him, quoting 'Umara, from Thabit, from Anas b. Malik, that Malik Dhu Yazan presented to the Messenger of God (SAAS) a ceremonial robe for which he had paid either 33 baggage camels or 33 riding camels. Abu Da'ud related it from 'Amr b. 'Awn al-Wasiti, from 'Umara b. Zadhan al-Saydalani, from Thabit al-Bunani.
lessons: Generous gifts given to honor a righteous leader reflect the giver's sincerity and the depth of their new commitment.
Material generosity, when offered with pure intentions, strengthens bonds of trust and community.
The honoring of the Prophet by kings and rulers affirms that true greatness transcends worldly power.
practical_advice: Express gratitude and commitment to those who have profoundly guided or changed your life through a meaningful and generous gesture.
Do not underestimate the power of a sincere, well-timed act of generosity to solidify a new relationship or alliance.
When building a new relationship with a community or leader, find culturally appropriate ways to demonstrate your commitment and respect.

title: The Letter of Amr b. Hazam: Comprehensive Instructions for Yemen's Religious Governance
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) wrote a detailed letter appointing Amr b. Hazam as his envoy to Yemen, providing comprehensive instructions on religious education, proper conduct, prayer times, almsgiving, the Hajj, and rules of personal conduct — serving as a complete guide for a newly Muslim population.
story: The hafiz al-Bayhaqi related at this point the story of the letter of 'Amr b. Hazam. He stated, "Abu 'Abd Allah al-Hafiz related to us, quoting Abu al-'Abbas al-Asam, quoting Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar, quoting Yunus b. Bukayr, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr, from his father Abu Bakr b. Muhammad b. 'Amr b. Hazam, who said, 'The following is the letter we have that the Messenger of God (SAAS) wrote for 'Amr b. Hazam when he sent him to Yemen to give religious instruction to its people, to teach them the sunna, the orthodox practices of Islam, and to collect their alms payments. The letter he wrote for him gives him his appointment and his instructions. "'He wrote, "In the name of God, the most Merciful and Beneficent. This is a document from God and His Messenger. 'O you who believe, keep to your agreements!' (This represents) a commission from the Messenger of God (SAAS), to 'Amr b. Hazam upon his being sent to Yemen. """He commands him to fear God in all he does, for God stays with those who fear Him and who do good. He orders him to act in accord with the truth as God so commanded it, and to give people glad tidings of what is good and to tell them to act in accord with it. He shall teach people the Qur'an and give them religious instruction. He must warn people that no one may touch the Qur'an unless that person is undefiled. He shall inform people of their rights and of their obligations. He shall treat them with liberality in matters relating to good, but with severity in cases of injustice; for God proscribed and forbad injustice, saying, 'The curse of God shall be upon the unjust, those who turn others aside from the path of God' (surat Hud; XI, v.18, 19). """'He shall tell people the good tidings of paradise and what happens there, and will warn people of hell-fire and what happens there. He will court people's friendship so that they acquire knowledge of the faith. He will teach them about the conduct and practices and obligations required by God that relate to the hajj, "the pilgrimage", and it consists of al-hajj al-akbar, "the greater pilgrimage", and the al-hajj al-asghar, "the lesser pilgrimage", which is known as the al-'umra. """'He shall order people that a man should not pray in one flimsy garment unless it be loose-fitting and hangs down over both shoulders between both sides. He will forbid a person from sitting in a single garment with his legs drawn up, thus exposing his private parts to the sky. And a person's hair should not be plaited if it hangs over the neck. He is to forbid people from calling upon clans or tribes in the case of conflict; their call should be directed solely to God alone Who has no associate.
