Those who do not call to God but call instead to clans or tribes are to be put to the sword until their calls are to God alone Who has no associate. """'He shall command people to perform the ablutions properly, washing their faces, their hands up to their elbows and their feet up to their ankles. And they shall wipe over their heads just as God the Almighty and Glorious, commanded. They are commanded to say the prayers at the correct times and to perform properly the bowings and the prostrations at them. They are to arise for the al-subh, "pre-dawn", prayer while it is still dark, pray at midday until the sun is past its zenith, pray the al-'asr, "the late afternoon", prayer while the sun is hurrying towards earth, and thereafter the al-maghrib, "the post-sunset prayer", as night approaches, but it should not'"
lessons: True Islamic governance begins with taqwa — God-consciousness — as the foundation for every action and every decision.
Teaching people their rights alongside their obligations is essential to building a just and harmonious community.
The Prophet's instructions balance encouragement with warning, generosity with firmness, showing that good leadership is never one-dimensional.
practical_advice: When taking on a new role of responsibility, begin by identifying what God-consciousness and integrity require of you in that position, before focusing on strategy or outcomes.
When educating others about faith or values, balance the sharing of positive incentives and hopeful outcomes with honest discussion of consequences — both are necessary for genuine understanding.

Establish consistent daily rituals, as the prayer times prescribed by the Prophet demonstrate — anchoring your day with structured moments of reflection and devotion brings order and resilience to even the most overwhelming schedule.

title: Instructions on Prayer Times and Alms (Zakat) in the Letter to Amr b. Hazm
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) sent detailed instructions outlining the correct times for the five daily prayers and the precise alms (zakat) obligations for various types of agricultural produce, livestock, and arrangements for people of other faiths under Islamic governance.
story: up to their elbows and their feet up to their ankles. And they shall wipe over their heads just as God the Almighty and Glorious, commanded. They are commanded to say the prayers at the correct times and to perform properly the bowings and the prostrations at them. They are to arise for the al-subh, "pre-dawn", prayer while it is still dark, pray at midday until the sun is past its zenith, pray the al-'asr, "the late afternoon", prayer while the sun is hurrying towards earth, and thereafter the al-maghrib, "the post-sunset prayer", as night approaches, but it should not be delayed until when the stars appear in the sky. And then is the al-'isha, "the evening prayer", at the first part of the night. He is ordered to take God's share of the booty as alms as is required from Muslims. From the produce of land watered by springs, the alms payments shall be one-fifth. From land watered by rain, it shall be one-tenth. From land watered by the bucket, it shall be one-twentieth. For every ten camels, two sheep shall be paid, and for twenty camels, the payment shall be four sheep. For every forty cattle, one cow. For every thirty cattle, whether male or female, one calf, whether male or female. For every forty sheep (or goats) at pasture, one sheep shall be paid. This is all an obligation made incumbent by God upon Muslims. Anyone paying more will gain benefit thereby. Any Jew or Christian who genuinely accepts Islam and properly performs in accord with the faith of Islam shall be (considered) a believer, and shall enjoy all the rights and obligations they have. Those who remain in their Judaism or Christianity shall not be made to change their faith. But each of their adults, whether male or female, free or slave, shall pay one full dinar, or clothing in value thereto. Those who fulfil this shall have the protection of God and His Messenger; those who refuse shall be considered the enemies of God, of His messenger and of all believers.
lessons: Clear and structured guidance reduces confusion and anxiety; the Prophet's detailed instructions gave communities a firm framework to follow without ambiguity.
Obligations calibrated to one's means reflect divine mercy — those with more resources contribute more, ensuring fairness and reducing resentment.
Religious diversity was acknowledged and protected within a framework of mutual responsibility, teaching that coexistence requires both rights and duties.
practical_advice: When feeling overwhelmed by responsibilities, break them down into clear, ordered steps as the Prophet structured the prayer times and alms — clarity transforms anxiety into action.
Reflect on your own acts of giving: are they proportional to what you have been blessed with? Calibrating generosity to your means is both spiritually sound and psychologically sustainable.
When navigating a community with diverse beliefs, focus on shared responsibilities and mutual respect rather than differences, following the model of protected coexistence.

title: The Arrival of Jarir b. Abd Allah al-Bajali and His Acceptance of Islam
summary: Jarir b. Abd Allah al-Bajali travelled to Madinah to accept Islam, and the Prophet (SAAS) honoured him publicly, having already foretold his coming with a description of authority on his face, and thereafter always greeted him with a smile.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that it was related to him by Abu Qatan, quoting Yunus, from al-Mughira b. Shibl, who quoted Jarir as saying, "When I approached Medina, I made my camel kneel, untied my leather bag, put on my ceremonial garments and went inside. There I found the Messenger of God (SAAS) making an address. The people present stared hard at me and I asked the person sitting next to me, 'Abd Allah, did the Messenger of God (SAAS) make some reference to me?' 'Yes,' he replied, 'he did make a very nice reference to you while he was making his address. He said, "There will come in to you through this door" - or this opening - "one of the finest men of Yemen. On his face, however, will be an expression of authority."' Jarir went on, "So I gave praise to God, the Almighty and Glorious, for the esteem he had expressed for me." The hafiz al-Bayhaqi stated that he was informed by Abu 'Abd Allah al-Hafiz, quoting Abu 'Amr and 'Uthman b. Ahmad al-Sammak, quoting al-Hasan b. Sallam al-Sawwaq, quoting Muhammad b. Muqatil al-Khurasani, quoting Husayn b. 'Umar al-Ahmasi, quoting Isma'il b. Abu Khalid, from Qays b. Abu Hazim, from Jarir b. 'Abd Allah, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent for me and said, 'Jarir, why have you come?' I replied, 'To accept Islam at your hands, Messenger of God.'" Jarir went on, "He then threw a mantle over me and went to his men, saying, 'When a people's (most) noble man comes to you, then honour him.'" He went on, "Jarir, I invite you to testify that there is no god but God, that I am God's Messenger, that you believe in God and in the last day, in fate, both its good and its evil, that you will perform the prayers as written, and that you will pay the alms that are required." Jarir said, "I did all that; and thereafter, whenever he saw me, he would smile at me." Imam Ahmad stated that Muhammad b. 'Ubayd related to him, quoting Isma'il, from Qays, from Jarir, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) never ignored me after I had accepted Islam. Whenever he saw me, he would smile directly at me."
lessons: Being honoured and welcomed at a moment of vulnerability — as Jarir was — reminds us that sincere steps toward truth are recognized and rewarded by God and righteous people.
The Prophet's consistent smile toward Jarir after his conversion illustrates how sustained warmth and inclusion help new believers feel secure and valued.
Public recognition of someone's nobility and sincerity, as the Prophet demonstrated, fosters dignity and belonging — qualities essential to emotional wellbeing.
practical_advice: When entering a new community or starting a new chapter of faith, prepare yourself with sincerity and intention, as Jarir dressed in his best garments before entering Madinah — outward preparation can strengthen inner resolve.
If you are in a position of leadership or community, greet newcomers warmly and consistently; a simple, sincere smile can be the foundation of a person's sense of belonging.
When you receive unexpected honour or recognition, follow Jarir's example: direct your gratitude to God first, recognizing that such blessings are from Him.

title: Jarir's Pledge to the Prophet and the Conditions of His Islam
summary: Jarir b. Abd Allah asked the Prophet (SAAS) to set the conditions of his pledge himself, and the Prophet laid out the core obligations of Islam including worship, prayer, alms, loyalty to Muslims, and disavowal of idolatry.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that it was related to him by Abu Sa'id, quoting Za'ida, quoting 'Asim, from Sufyan - Abu Wa'il, that is - from Jarir - who said, "I asked, 'Messenger of God, make stipulations; you know best what they should be.' He replied, 'I ask you to pledge that you will worship God alone and that you will not associate any other with Him; that you will perform the prayers, pay the alms, be loyal to Muslims and disavow idolatry.'" Imam Ahmad stated that Yahya b. Sa'id al-Qattan related to us, quoting Isma'il b. Abu Khalid, from Qays b. Abu Hazim, from Jarir b. 'Abd Allah, who said, 'I pledged to the Messenger of God (SAAS) to perform the prayer, to pay the alms and to be loyal to all Muslims.'
lessons: Surrendering the terms of your commitment to someone wiser — as Jarir did by asking the Prophet to set the conditions — is a sign of humility and trust, not weakness.

The core pledges of Islam — worship, prayer, giving, loyalty, and disavowal of falsehood — address the full spectrum of human spiritual, social, and moral life, offering a comprehensive path to wellbeing.
Loyalty to one's community is listed alongside worship as a fundamental obligation, reminding us that individual faith is inseparable from communal responsibility.
practical_advice: When you feel lost about where to begin in your spiritual or personal life, ask a trusted guide to help you identify the foundational commitments most needed — clarity from others can cut through inner confusion.
Choose a small number of core commitments and pledge yourself to them sincerely rather than trying to do everything at once; Jarir's pledge was simple, clear, and transformative.
Regularly examine whether your loyalty to your community — family, neighbours, fellow believers — is as active as your private worship; both are essential to a balanced and healthy life.

title: The Prophet Sends Jarir to Destroy the Temple of Dhu al-Khalasa
summary: After accepting Islam, Jarir b. Abd Allah was sent by the Prophet (SAAS) to destroy the pagan temple of Dhu al-Khalasa; after the Prophet prayed for him to be seated firmly on horseback, Jarir successfully led 150 cavalry to demolish the temple and returned with good news.
story: We have referred previously to how the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent him, following his acceptance of Islam, out to Dhu Khalasa, the temple where (Banu) Khat'am and (Banu) Bajila would worship. That was known as the "Southern Ka'ba" with which they would rival the Ka'ba in Mecca. The Ka'ba at Mecca they would refer to as the "Northern Ka'ba". The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked Jarir, "Will you not relieve me of Dhu al-Khalasa?" Jarir then complained to the Messenger of God (SAAS), that he had difficulty in keeping his seat on horses. At this, the Messenger of God (SAAS) struck him in the chest so hard as to make a mark there and said, "O God, seat him firmly and make him a guide who is well guided." Thereafter he never fell from a horse. Jarir then went to Dhu al-Khalasa with a force of 150 cavalry of his own clan of Ahmas and destroyed and burned that temple, leaving it looking like a mangy camel! He sent back a messenger named Abu Artat to tell him this good news. Thereupon the Messenger of God (SAAS) five times spoke blessings upon the horses and men of Ahmas.
lessons: The Prophet's prayer for Jarir — that he be seated firmly and guided well — transformed a personal weakness into a source of strength, reminding us that supplication to God can resolve what human effort alone cannot.
Expressing your limitations honestly to a trusted guide, as Jarir did about his difficulty on horseback, opens the door for support and divine assistance rather than closing it.

When a difficult mission is accomplished through sincere intention and God's help, the recognition given — as the Prophet blessed the men of Ahmas five times — reinforces that communal effort done for God's sake is deeply honoured.
practical_advice: When you feel physically or emotionally unequal to a task you have been assigned, voice that honestly to those who can help, then trust that sincere supplication and effort will be met with divine support.

Before undertaking a challenging responsibility, seek the prayers of righteous people in your life — their supplications, like the Prophet's prayer for Jarir, can give you strength and stability you did not have before.

After completing a difficult mission, take time to acknowledge and celebrate the people who helped you succeed; the Prophet's repeated blessings on the men of Ahmas show that gratitude expressed openly strengthens community bonds.

title: Jarir b. 'Abd Allah and the Prophet's Advice on Lowering the Gaze
summary: Jarir b. 'Abd Allah, a man of large stature and remarkable beauty yet great modesty, asked the Prophet about inadvertent glances at the opposite sex, and the Prophet instructed him to turn his gaze away.
story: Jarir was a man of very large stature. His feet were the length of a fore-arm. His face was also unusually handsome. Despite this, he was the the most modest of men. Thus, it is related of him in the traditions considered authentic that he said, "I asked the Messenger of God (SAAS), about glancing inadvertently (at the opposite sex), and he replied, 'Turn your gaze away!'"
lessons: True character is not defined by outward appearance but by inner modesty and restraint.
Seeking religious guidance on matters of personal struggle is a sign of spiritual maturity and honesty.
The Prophet's concise wisdom shows that the best solutions to moral challenges are often simple and direct.
practical_advice: When you notice your eyes or attention drifting toward something you know is not good for you, immediately redirect your focus rather than lingering.
If you struggle with a recurring personal weakness, seek guidance from a trusted scholar, counselor, or mentor rather than feeling ashamed in silence.
Practice mindfulness in daily interactions by being intentional about where you direct your attention and energy.

title: The Arrival of Wa'il b. Hujr to the Prophet and the Encounter with Mu'awiya
summary: Wa'il b. Hujr, a prince of Hadramawt, arrived to the Prophet who welcomed him with great honor, assigned him authority, and sent Mu'awiya with him on foot; Mu'awiya experienced humiliation during the journey but later, as caliph, graciously acknowledged the memory of that event.
story: Abu 'Umar b. 'Abd al-Barr stated, "He was one of the princes of Hadramawt, his father having been one of their kings. It is said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) announced the news of his coming before his actual arrival, saying, 'A son of a line of kings is coming to you.' When Wa'il entered, he welcomed him heartily, positioned him to sit close to himself and spread out his cloak for him, saying, 'O God, bless Wa'il and his sons and the sons of his sons!' "He then placed him in command of the princes of Hadramawt, sending three letters home with him. One was a letter to al-Muhajir b. Abu Umayya. There was also a letter to the princes and (another) to the hereditary rulers. The Messenger of God (SAAS) assigned him control over a specific territory and dispatched Mu'awiya b. Abu Sufyan along with him on foot. The latter complained to him at the heat of the ground and Wa'il responded, 'Well, step in the shadow of the camel!' Mu'awiya asked, 'What good will that do? Could you not let me ride behind you?' Wa'il replied, 'Be silent! You're not such as to ride behind kings!' "Wa'il b. Hujr lived on to later go to see Mu'awiya when he had become Commander of the Believers. Mu'awiya recognized him, welcomed him and invited him to come close to himself. He then reminded Wa'il of what he had said and offered him an official gift. Wa'il refused it saying, 'Give it to someone more needy of it than myself.'" The hafiz al-Bayhaqi gave some of the above and pointed to the fact that al-Bukhari in his History related it in part. Imam Ahmad stated, "Hajjaj related to us, quoting Shu'ba, from Simak b. Harb, from 'Alqama b. Wa'il, from his father, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) assigned me control over a specific territory. And he sent with me Mu'awiya for me to give it' - the land or its revenues - 'over to him' (or his words were '... to acquaint him with it' (the land). Mu'awiya asked, 'Let me ride behind you.' I replied, 'You're not such as to ride behind kings!' He then asked, 'Then give me your shoes.' I replied, 'Step in the camel's shade.'" Wa'il went on, "After Mu'awiya had become caliph, I went to visit him; he seated me next to him on his couch and reminded me of our conversation." Simak continued, "Wa'il then commented, 'I wished I had in fact borne him in front of myself.'"
lessons: Worldly rank and status are temporary; those who experience hardship may later rise to positions of great honor.
Generosity and self-sufficiency are noble qualities; refusing an unneeded gift and redirecting it to the more deserving reflects strong character.
Regret over past harshness toward others, even when it was within one's rights, is a sign of a reflective and growing soul.
practical_advice: When you recall moments where you treated someone dismissively or harshly, let that reflection motivate kindness and humility in your current interactions rather than guilt that paralyzes you.
Practice generosity by occasionally redirecting resources or recognition to those who need it more than you do.
Remember that people's circumstances change; do not permanently define others by the role or status they held during a difficult period.

title: Laqit b. 'Amir's Journey to the Prophet and the Dialogue on the Unseen, the Resurrection, and the Day of Judgment
summary: Laqit b. 'Amir and his companion travelled to Medina and engaged the Prophet in a profound dialogue about the keys of hidden knowledge, death, resurrection, the Day of Judgment, and how God will gather all of humanity and look upon them.
story: Laqit stated, 'I and my friend travelled until we reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) in Medina at the end of Rajab. We came to him and expressed our allegiance to him as he was leaving the noon prayer meeting. He then stood to address the people and said, "The only reason I have been refraining from addressing you for the past four days was in order to listen to you. Is there any man among you sent out by his people?"' 'People were saying (to one another), "Tell me what the Messenger of God, is saying!" He then said, "What if a person is misled by what he tells himself, or what his friend tells him, or what he is told in error; am I not responsible for delivering my message? Listen carefully, and you will live. Sit down all of you."' 'The people sat down. I and my friend stood, and when he had turned his attention to us entirely, I asked, "Messenger of God, what knowledge do you possess of al-ghayb?"' 'He laughed, I swear by God, and shook his head, realizing that I was seeking to trip him up. He replied, "Your Lord, Almighty and Glorious is He, kept concealed five keys to the future that only God knows." And he made a gesture with his hand.' 'I asked, "And what are those?" He replied, "Knowledge of death; He knows when the death of each of you will occur, though you do not. And (He has) knowledge of the sperm when it is inside the womb; he knows of it, though you do not. And knowledge of what tomorrow will bring and what your sustenance will be. Though you have no sense or knowledge of tomorrow. He knows when heavy rain will fall, and when you will suffer severe drought. And He continues laughing, knowing that your fate is close at hand."' Laqit stated, "We'll never lack from a Lord who laughs in benevolence. And Who knows yawm al-sa'a, the day of the (judgement) hour. "We then said, 'Messenger of God, teach us what people do not know but that you do know. We are of a tribe who never gives credence to anyone; we are a group from (Banu) Madhhij who lead us, from Khath'am who are allied to us, and we ourselves, from our own tribe.' "He replied, 'You will continue on for some time as now, and then your Prophet will die. Again you will continue as before for a time and then the day of lamentation will be sent. By the life of your God, I swear every single thing on earth will die, as well as the angels who are with your Lord. Your Lord, the Almighty and Glorious, will then pass over the earth, alone, over all its parts. Your Lord will have sent down a deluge from His throne. And, by the life of your Lord, every single place on earth where warriors have fallen or the dead have been buried will be split open, revealing them. The dead will be recreated from the head (down) and each one will sit up straight. Your Lord, the Almighty and Glorious, will ask them, "Mayham? 'What did you do?'" - concerning what each had done. Each will reply, "O Lord, it all happened shortly before today." And from his knowledge of life, he will consider it (his death) to have been a recent event for his family.' "I asked, 'Messenger of God, how will He join us back together again once the winds, decay and wild beasts will have scattered our remains?' "He replied, 'I remind you of such similar favours of God; you have looked down at the earth and found clods of soil empty of life and have said, "It will never live again. But then your Lord sends rain from the sky and soon you will look down at it again and see a plant sprouting. By the life of your God, it is easier for Him to compound you from water than the plants from the earth. You shall emerge from your graves wherever you died, and you will look at Him, as He will look at you."' "I asked, 'Messenger of God, how can it be that we (who are so many as to) fill the land while He, Almighty and Glorious is He, is just one person, and yet He can look at us as we look at Him?' "He replied, 'I refer you to some of God's favours that are similar to that. The sun and the moon are a sign from him. You see them as small. They see all of you at one instance, yet you are not harmed by seeing them. By the life of Your God, He is more able to see you and you Him than you are to see both of them, and them to see you.' "I asked, 'Messenger of God, what will our Lord do when we meet Him?' He replied, 'You will turn to him and show him your entire surface; nothing of you shall remain hidden from him. Your Lord will take up a handful of water and splash it towards you. And, I swear by the life of your God, not a drop of it will fail to strike the face of anyone of you. It will fall upon the face of the Muslims like a fine, white cloth, while it will lie upon that of the unbelievers like black pitch. "'Then your Prophet will leave, as will the righteous, following after him. You will pass across a bridge of fire, and when one of you treads upon it, he will cry out in pain. Your Lord, Almighty and Glorious is He, will say, "His time has come!" You will gaze out at the al-hawd of the Messenger as all go towards it, burning with thirst, a sight such as you will never have seen before. And, by the life of your God, every time anyone of you stretches forth his hand, there will fall over it a cup full with which he will cleanse himself from the excrement, the urine and the filth. The sun and the moon will be veiled over.'"
lessons: Uncertainty about the future is not a source of despair but an invitation to trust in the One who holds all knowledge; peace comes from surrendering what we cannot know.
Just as dry earth springs back to life after rain, no hardship or loss is beyond the capacity of God to restore and renew.
Seeking understanding of life's deepest questions is a noble pursuit; the Prophet engaged even challenging questions with patience, clarity, and warmth.
practical_advice: When anxiety about the future overwhelms you, remind yourself that not knowing tomorrow's outcome is not a weakness but the shared human condition; redirect your energy to what you can do today.
When you feel that a situation in your life is dead and beyond recovery, reflect on the image of rain falling on barren earth — renewal is always possible when you least expect it.

If a question or fear about death or the afterlife unsettles you, do not suppress it; engage it honestly through study, reflection, or conversation with a trusted guide, as Laqit did by journeying to ask the Prophet directly.

title: Ziyad ibn al-Harith al-Suda'i Accepts Islam and Negotiates Peace for His People
summary: Ziyad al-Suda'i pledges allegiance to the Prophet, negotiates the peaceful conversion of his entire tribe by sending them a letter, and is appointed their leader and alms collector.
story: I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and pledged to him my allegiance in Islam. I was told that he had sent out an army against my people and I said, 'Messenger of God, bring back the army and I will guarantee to you that my people will accept Islam and pledge obedience to you.' He replied, 'You go and bring them back.' I replied, 'My camel is exhausted.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then sent out a man and brought them back. I wrote a letter to them, and their delegation arrived with news of their acceptance of Islam. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Brother of (Banu) Suda', your people give you obedience.' I replied, 'In fact it is God who led them to Islam.' He then asked, 'Should I then not appoint you to be their leader?' I replied, 'Oh no; you should do so, Messenger of God.' He then wrote a document appointing me their leader and I asked him, 'Messenger of God, entrust me with part of their alms payments.' He replied, 'Certainly'. He then wrote me another document.
lessons: When we attribute our successes to God rather than to ourselves, we reflect true humility and gratitude, which are hallmarks of genuine faith.
Taking personal initiative and responsibility for one's community — even at personal cost — can prevent conflict and bring people together peacefully.
A single sincere individual acting as a bridge between their community and truth can bring entire peoples toward guidance and goodness.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in a position to prevent harm or conflict for people you care about, act decisively and trust that God will support sincere efforts made for the right reasons.
Practice attributing your achievements and successes to God's guidance rather than your own cleverness — this simple habit builds humility and reduces ego-driven stress.
If you are in a leadership role, remember that the best leaders seek responsibility as a means of service, not personal gain, and approach it with that mindset daily.

title: The Prophet's Warning About Seeking Command and Requesting from Surplus Wealth
summary: During a journey, the Prophet publicly states that command brings no benefit to a believer and that requesting donations from surplus wealth is blameworthy, causing al-Suda'i to reflect on his own requests with shame and ultimately renounce his appointments.
story: That occurred during one of his trips. The Messenger of God (SAAS) took up residence in a house and the family of that house came to him and complained about their governor, saying, 'He blames us for some problem there used to be between us and his people in the jāhiliyya days.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, 'He does this, does he?' 'Yes,' we replied. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then turned to his Companions, who included myself, and he said, 'Well, there's no (material) benefit in having command for a man who believes.' Al-Sudā'ī went on, 'His words had great impact on me. Another man then came to him and asked, "Messenger of God, give to me!" The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "Those who request donations from superfluous property are an ache in the head and a pain in the stomach!" The petitioner now asked, "Then give me from the alms." The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "God would not be pleased at alms being disbursed by the decision of a prophet or anyone else, whereas He Himself has decided about it and divided it up into eight parts. If you were of one of those parts, I would give it to you."' Al-Sudā'ī went on, 'That made an impression on me; I was wealthy, yet I had asked for payment from the alms.' ... 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had completed the prayer, I took to him both documents and said, "Messenger of God, please cancel out both of these for me." "What is your reason?" he asked. I replied, "Messenger of God, I heard you say, 'There is no benefit in having command for a man who believes.' And I do believe in God and in His Messenger. I heard you tell that petitioner, 'Those with superfluous property who request donations are an ache in the head and a pain in the stomach.' I made a request to you, though I am wealthy." He replied, "So be it; accept if you wish, or decline." "I decline," I answered. He then told me, "Point out to me a man I can place in command of you." I led him to one man of the delegation who had come to him and he appointed him in command of them.'
lessons: True self-awareness means reflecting honestly on our motivations and being willing to correct ourselves even when no one is demanding it of us.
Guilt felt after genuine reflection is a sign of a living conscience — when acted upon with honesty and correction, it becomes a powerful tool for growth rather than a burden.
Seeking positions of authority or material benefit from a place of self-interest is spiritually harmful; the believer is called to examine their intentions regularly.
practical_advice: When you feel a pang of guilt or embarrassment about something you have done, resist the urge to rationalize — instead, take it as an invitation to reflect and, if needed, make it right.
Periodically review your current responsibilities, roles, and requests to ask honestly: am I doing this for God's sake, or for personal gain? Adjust accordingly.
If you catch yourself seeking something you do not truly need, practice the discipline of voluntarily stepping back from that request as an act of spiritual refinement.

title: The Miracle of Water Springing from the Prophet's Fingers for Al-Suda'i
summary: When water was critically scarce during a journey, Ziyad al-Suda'i brought the Prophet a small amount of water, and water miraculously sprung from between the Prophet's fingers, providing enough for all the Companions present.
story: Early that night the Messenger of God (SAAS) went to the campfire and I kept close by him; his (other) Companions were away from him and late in coming to him, so that there was no one with him except myself. When it was close to the time for the al-ṣubḥ, 'pre-dawn', prayer, he gave me orders and I made the call to prayer. I asked him, 'Shall I (give the) iqāma, then, Messenger of God?' He began looking out to the east for the dawn and said, 'No'. When dawn came, he went off and relieved himself, then came back to me, his Companions with him. He asked me, 'Brother Suda', is there water?' I replied, 'Not except for just a little that won't be enough for you.' He told me, 'Put it in a vessel and bring it to me.' I did so and he placed his palm in the water. And there between his fingers I saw a spring bubbling up! The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'Were it not for my diffidence towards my Lord, Almighty and Glorious is He, we would drink of the water and also draw therefrom. Invite those of my Companions in need of water to come.' I did call out to them and those who wished did take some. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then arose to perform the prayer. Bilal wished to call the iqāma, but the Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, 'Brother Suda' made the call to prayer; he who calls the adhān should also call the iqāma.' And so I did.
lessons: In moments of genuine scarcity and need, placing trust in God's provision — rather than panicking — opens the door to blessings we cannot anticipate.
Being present, attentive, and close to sources of guidance during difficult times positions us to witness and benefit from moments of grace.
The Prophet's humility before God, even in miraculous moments, is a profound lesson that greatness and servitude to the Divine are not in conflict.
practical_advice: When resources feel scarce — whether material, emotional, or spiritual — resist despair and instead bring what little you have and ask God to bless it and multiply it.
Make a habit of staying close to those who are spiritually grounded during times of difficulty; their presence and guidance can transform a crisis into a moment of profound gratitude.
Honor commitments and responsibilities you have taken on in service to others, as al-Suda'i did with the call to prayer — consistency in small acts of service builds character and trust.

title: The Miracle of the Well of Banu Suda'a and the Prophet's Prayer with Seven Pebbles
summary: The tribe of Banu Suda'a, having accepted Islam and now surrounded by enemies, asked the Prophet to pray for their well whose water was insufficient in summer; he prayed over seven pebbles and instructed them to throw these in the well, after which its water became inexhaustible.
story: We then said, 'Messenger of God, we have a well that has enough water for us in the winter and we assemble around it. But in the summer its water is scanty, so we disperse to wells around about us. Now we have accepted Islam, all those around us will be enemies. Say a prayer for us to God about our well so that its water supply will suffice for us and we will not have to disperse.' He called for seven pebbles, rubbed them in his hand and spoke a prayer over them. He then said, 'Take these pebbles and when you go to the well, throw them in, one by one, and invoke the name of God.' Al-Sudā'ī went on, 'We did as he had told us. And thereafter, when we looked down into it' - the well, he meant - 'we could not see its bottom!'
lessons: When we embrace truth and righteousness, we may face new hardships and vulnerabilities — but God does not abandon those who sacrifice for His sake.
Small acts of trust and obedience, like throwing pebbles into a well while invoking God's name, can become the vessel through which extraordinary blessings flow.
Community cohesion and stability are blessings worth praying for; when people can remain together, they are stronger in faith and in life.
practical_advice: When a new commitment to what is right brings unexpected hardships or isolation, remember that God provides for those who trust Him — make your need known to Him specifically and sincerely.
Take practical steps of faith even when they seem small or symbolic; the combination of sincere action and invocation of God's name can shift circumstances in ways you cannot plan for.

If your community faces a shared difficulty — material, social, or spiritual — gather together, name the problem honestly, seek guidance, and act on it with trust rather than allowing fear to scatter you.

title: The Miracle of the Well of the Suda' Tribe
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) performed a miracle by rubbing seven pebbles and praying over them, which caused the water in the well of the Suda' tribe to become so abundant that its bottom could no longer be seen.
story: God about our well so that its water supply will suffice for us and we will not have to disperse.' "He called for seven pebbles, rubbed them in his hand and spoke a prayer over them. He then said, 'Take these pebbles and when you go to the well, throw them in, one by one, and invoke the name of God.'" Al-Suda'i went on, "We did as he had told us. And thereafter, when we looked down into it" - the well, he meant - "we could not see its bottom!" There are many testimonials to this hadith, in the collections made by Abu Da'ud, al-Tirmidhi and Ibn Maja.
lessons: Trusting in God's power can transform even physical limitations into abundance — community preservation begins with faith.
Following the Prophet's instructions precisely, even when they seem simple, opens doors to divine mercy.
When communities face the threat of dispersal and loss, turning to God in prayer and following prophetic guidance can bring about extraordinary relief.
practical_advice: When facing a crisis that threatens to break apart your family or community, gather together, pray sincerely, and trust that God can provide in ways beyond what you can see.
Perform small acts of devotion — such as invoking God's name as instructed — with full sincerity; do not underestimate simple spiritual practices.
If you feel your resources are running dry and you fear your circle of support will scatter, make du'a specifically for sufficiency and barakah in what you have.

title: The Submission of the Suda' Tribe After Military Dispatch
summary: After the Prophet (SAAS) dispatched Qays b. Sa'd with 400 men toward the Suda' territory following the 'umra of al-Ji'rana, the tribe sent a representative to negotiate peace, ultimately joining the Muslim community and participating in the Farewell Pilgrimage.
story: Al-Waqidi narrated that following the 'umra of al-Ji'rana, the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent Qays b. Sa'd b. 'Ubada out with 400 men to subdue the territory of Suda'. Those there dispatched one of their men, who said (to the Prophet (SAAS)), "I have come to you to withdraw your army from my people. And I will bring them over to you." Thereafter, their delegation of 15 men did arrive. And 100 of their men were seen participating in the Hijjat al-wada', "the farewell pilgrimage".
lessons: Peaceful negotiation and willing submission are always preferred over prolonged conflict — the door to reconciliation is always open.
A single courageous representative who steps forward for his people can change the course of their entire community's fate.
Joining the larger community of believers, even after initial distance, brings lasting belonging and spiritual fulfillment.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in conflict with a larger force or authority, consider whether a sincere, honest approach and dialogue could resolve matters better than resistance.
Be willing to be the one who steps forward on behalf of others, even when it feels risky — your courage may spare your entire community from harm.
It is never too late to join a community of goodness; even those who were once opposed became participants in the most blessed of gatherings.

title: Al-Harith al-Bakri's Journey to the Prophet and the Story of the Man Sent to 'Ad
summary: Al-Harith al-Bakri traveled to the Prophet (SAAS) to lodge a complaint, inadvertently transporting an elderly woman from his adversaries along the way, and narrated to the Prophet the ancient tale of the man sent by 'Ad who brought upon them their own destruction through his prayer.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Zayd b. al-Habbab related to him, quoting Abu al-Mundhir Sallam b. Sulayman al-Nahwi, quoting 'Asim b. Abu al-Nujud, from Abu Wa'il, from al-Harith al-Bakri, who said, "I went to make a complaint about al-'Ala' b. al-Hadrami to the Messenger of God. As I passed by al-Zabda, I came across an old woman of Banu Tamim who had been stranded. She asked, 'Abd Allah, I need to see the Messenger of God. Will you take me to him!' "So I carried her into Medina. The mosque was inundated with people; a black banner was flying and Bilal was there wearing his sword, standing in front of the Messenger of God (SAAS). I asked what was going on and people told me that he was about to dispatch 'Amr b. al-As. "I sat down, and he entered his house" - or the narrator used the word, his rafl, "his tent" or "dwelling" - "and I asked permission to go in to him and it was granted. I entered and greeted him, and he asked, 'Has there been something going on between your people and (Banu) Tamim?' 'Yes,' I replied. 'And they lost this time. I passed by an old woman of Banu Tamim who had been stranded. She asked me to transport her to you; she is over there, at the door.' He asked her to enter, and she did so. I asked, 'Messenger of God, if you should decide to make some barrier between us, let it be the desert.' The old woman then became very excited and agitated and said, 'Messenger of God, to what will (your own tribe of) Mudar be reduced!' "I commented, 'Well, I'm like in the ancient saying, "a goat bearing its own fate"! I transported this woman without realizing that she was my adversary! I seek refuge with God and His Messenger! I'm like the man who came to (Banu) 'Ad!' "He asked, 'What's that about, "the man who came to 'Ad"?' "(He was more knowledgeable about the story than was the narrator, but he wanted to test him.) "I replied, ''Ad suffered from lack of rain, and they sent out one of their men, named Qiyl; he passed by Mu'awiya b. Bakr and stayed with him for a month, being given wine to drink and having songs sung to him by two girls, called al-jaradatan, "the two locusts". When the month was over, he went off to the mountains of Muhra. There he called out, "O God, You know I would never visit a sick man (without) giving him medicine, nor to a prisoner without ransoming him! O God, send rain for 'Ad as you never have before!" Black clouds then passed above him, and a voice called out to him, "Choose from among them!" He gestured to one particular black cloud. Then a voice called out from it, "Take it to be ashes, fine and copious! You will not have one of 'Ad survive!" And, from what I have heard, no more wind was sent at them than would pass through this ring of mine, before they had all perished!'" Abu Wa'il commented, "He spoke the truth. Men and women would say, when a man was sent to visit them, 'May you not be like the man sent out by 'Ad!'"
lessons: Our good deeds can unexpectedly place us in difficult situations — intention still matters, and seeking refuge in God is always the right response.
The story of 'Ad warns that being distracted from one's purpose by worldly indulgence can lead a person — and those depending on them — to ruin.
Prayers made with sincerity and a clean conscience carry tremendous weight, but we must take care about what we ask for, for ourselves and others.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in an ironic or self-created difficult situation, resist the urge to despair; acknowledge it openly, seek God's refuge, and maintain your sense of perspective.
Before making heartfelt prayers on behalf of others — especially in anger or frustration — pause and reflect on whether what you are asking for truly serves their good.
Avoid distractions that pull you away from your mission or responsibilities; like Qiyl's month of indulgence, delays in fulfilling your purpose can have far-reaching consequences.

title: The Delegation of 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abu 'Uqayl: From Hatred to Love
summary: A delegation that initially harbored deep hatred for the Prophet (SAAS) visited him and left with him as the most beloved person to them, during which the Prophet revealed that he had reserved his special intercession for his nation on the Day of Judgement.
story: Abu Bakr al-Bayhaqi stated that he was informed by Abu 'Abd Allah Ishaq b. Muhammad b. Yusuf al-Susi, quoting Abu Ja'far Muhammad b. Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah al-Baghdadi, quoting 'Ali b. Ja'd, quoting 'Abd al-'Aziz, as having related, quoting Ahmad b. Yusuf, quoting Zuhayr, quoting Abu Khalid Yazid al-Asadi, quoting 'Awn b. Abu Juhayla, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Alqama al-Thaqafi, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abu 'Aqil, who said, "I set off in a delegation to the Messenger of God (SAAS). When we reached him, we made our camels kneel at the door. There was absolutely no one more hated by us than the man we were going in to see. Yet after we had gone in and come out again, there was absolutely no one more beloved by us than the man we had gone in to see! One of our men asked him, 'Messenger of God, have you not asked your Lord for a domain like that of Solomon?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) laughed and replied, 'Perhaps your companion has even higher status with God than King Solomon! God, Almighty and Glorious is He, never sent any prophet without according him a request. Some of them take theirs here on earth and are awarded it. Some use it to invoke God against their people who disobey and are destroyed thereby. God awarded me a request that I have kept hidden with my Lord: I will intercede for my nation on Judgement Day.'"
lessons: Prejudice and hatred formed without direct encounter can dissolve entirely in the presence of truth and noble character — do not let assumptions prevent you from seeking knowledge.
The Prophet (SAAS) chose intercession for his entire nation over personal reward or worldly dominion, demonstrating the height of love and selflessness.
True greatness is not measured in earthly kingdoms but in closeness to God and care for others — this reorients how we should define success in our own lives.
practical_advice: Approach people — even those you have been taught to distrust or dislike — with an open heart and willingness to be changed by genuine encounter.
When you feel overwhelmed by the thought of Judgement Day, take comfort in knowing that the Prophet (SAAS) has reserved his intercession specifically for his ummah.
Reflect on what 'request' you might make of God in your own life — are you using your du'a for immediate relief, or investing in something of lasting spiritual value?

title: Tariq b. 'Abd Allah Encounters the Prophet at the Market and in Madinah
summary: Tariq b. 'Abd Allah witnessed the Prophet (SAAS) being stoned by his uncle at Dhu al-Majaz market while preaching Islam, and later encountered him again in Madinah where the Prophet miraculously fulfilled a transaction and addressed the community on charity and the end of inherited blood debts.
story: The hafiz al-Bayhaqi narrated through Abu Janab al-Kalbi, from Jami' b. Shaddad al-Muharibi, quoting one of his people, a man named Tariq b. 'Abd Allah, who related, "I was standing at the market at Dhu al-Majaz when a man wearing a jubba, a long robe, arrived and said, 'People, say the words, "There is no god but God" and you will prosper!' There was another man following him, throwing stones at him and saying, 'People, he is a liar!' I asked who it was and was told it was a young man of Banu Hashim who claimed to be a Messenger of God. I also asked who it was treating him that way and was told that he was his uncle 'Abd al-'Uzza. When people accepted Islam and migrated (to Medina), we left al-Rabdha for Medina to purchase dates there. When we neared its walls and palm-groves, I said, 'We should make a halt and change our clothes.' Just then a man dressed in rags came up and greeted us. He asked from where we had come, and we told him from al-Rabdha. He then asked where we were headed, and we told him we had come to that town. When he asked why, we told him we wanted to barter some dates. I told him we had a lady in a litter with us and a red camel with a blaze on its nose. He asked whether we would sell him our camel and we said we would, in exchange for a specific weight of dates. "We received no deposit on the price agreed; the man took hold of the camel's halter and left. When he was out of our sight beyond the city's walls and palms, we asked one another what it was we had done. We realized we had sold our camel to someone we did not know, and we had not been paid for it. "The lady with us commented, 'I swear by God, what I saw was a man whose face was like a slice of a full moon! I'll guarantee the price of your camel!' Just then the man appeared and announced, 'I am the Messenger of God to you; these are your dates. Eat until you are satisfied and take your full measure.' We did eat our fill and take full measure. "Then we entered the town and went in the mosque. He was there, on the minbar making an address. We arrived as he was saying, 'Make charitable donations, for alms-giving is better for you. And the hand that gives is better than one that receives - to your mother and your father, then to your sister and your brother, and then on lower down from there.' "A man of Banu Yarbu'" - or the narrator may have said, "one of the ansar" - "then approached and said, 'Messenger of God, these people have unpaid debts of blood from the jahiliyya, "before Islam"." He replied, 'Sins of the father do not become those of the son.' He repeated this three times."
lessons: The Prophet (SAAS) was mocked and stoned in public markets yet continued to call people to goodness — perseverance in the face of public humiliation is a mark of prophetic character.
Generosity with trust — even when it seems financially risky — can open the door to extraordinary blessings, as the woman's calm certainty about the Prophet's face demonstrates.
Islam brought a profound justice by declaring that no person bears the sins of their ancestors — each soul begins fresh, and transformation is always possible.
practical_advice: When you are ridiculed or dismissed for standing up for what is right, remember that the Prophet himself faced stones and slander in the marketplace — your steadfastness has noble precedent.
Cultivate the habit of charitable giving, beginning with those closest to you — parents, siblings, and immediate family — before extending outward.
If you carry guilt or shame connected to your family's past mistakes or wrongs, hold onto the principle that you are not defined by those before you — your account with God is your own.

title: Farwa b. 'Amr al-Judhami Accepts Islam and Is Imprisoned by the Byzantines
summary: Farwa b. 'Amr al-Judhami, a governor serving the Byzantines, sent an envoy to the Prophet (SAAS) declaring his Islam and offering a gift, but was subsequently arrested and imprisoned by the Byzantines, composing poetry of longing and steadfastness from his cell.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Farwa b. 'Amr b. al-Nafirat al-Judhami, of the Nufathi tribe, sent an envoy to the Messenger of God (SAAS), announcing his acceptance of Islam and presented him with a white mule. "Farwa was governor on behalf of the Byzantines over the Arabs whose territory bordered their own. His residency was centred in Mu'an, and the Syrian lands thereabouts. When the Byzantines learned of his acceptance of Islam, they summoned him, and seized and imprisoned him there with them. While imprisoned, he composed the following verses: 'Sulayma made her way at night to my friends, while the Byzantines were between the gate and the water-tank. The spirit turned away in distress at what it had seen and though I tried to sleep, it had made me cry. Do not decorate your eyes with antimony, Salma, when I am gone, nor subject yourself to intercourse. You have learned, O Abu Kubaysha, that when in the presence of the powerful, my tongue is not tied. If I should die, you will mourn your brother, while if I survive my status...'"
lessons: Faith that costs nothing proves nothing — Farwa's willingness to face imprisonment and loss for his Islam reveals the depth of sincere conviction.
Even in isolation and suffering, the believer's spirit can express itself through prayer, poetry, and dignified resistance, preserving inner freedom when outer freedom is stripped away.
Announcing one's faith publicly before powerful adversaries requires immense courage, and God does not allow such sacrifices to go unwitnessed or unrewarded.
practical_advice: When you face pressure from authority figures, employers, or society to abandon your values or beliefs, draw strength from the example of those who held firm even when it cost them their freedom.

If you are in a season of isolation, confinement, or deep longing, allow yourself to express those feelings honestly — through writing, journaling, or poetry — as a form of processing and spiritual resilience.
Do not wait for a 'safe' moment to declare your values or faith; Farwa sent his declaration even knowing the risks, because the sincerity of the moment mattered more than the consequences.

title: Martyrdom of the Companion at the Qifra Well
summary: A Muslim companion imprisoned by the Byzantines composed verses of courage and devotion before being crucified at a well in Palestine called Qifra, bequeathing his body and bones to his Lord.
story: While imprisoned, he composed the following verses: 'Sulayma made her way at night to my friends, while the Byzantines were between the gate and the water-tank. The spirit turned away in distress at what it had seen and though I tried to sleep, it had made me cry. Do not decorate your eyes with antimony, SaIma, when I am gone, nor subject yourself to intercourse. You have learned, 0 Abu Kubaysha, that when in the presence of the powerful, my tongue is not tied. If I should die, you will mourn your brother, while if I survive my status will be recognized. I have encompassed the finest qualities a hero can have - generosity, bravery and eloquence.' When the Byzantines had decided to crucify him at a well of theirs in Palestine called qfra, he spoke the following: 'I wonder whether Sahna has learned that her husband will be at the qfra well upon a camel whose mother was never served by a stallion, a mount whose limbs were pruned by axes?' Al-Zuhri claimed that when they took him out to kill him, he said, 'Inform the Muslim leaders that I am bequeathing my body and my bones to my Lord.' They then cut off his head and crucified him at that well. May God have mercy on him, be pleased with him and give him pleasure and make paradise his abode.
lessons: True courage is not the absence of fear but the presence of conviction — this companion faced death with poetry, dignity, and unwavering faith.
Even in the darkest moments of captivity and impending death, one's ultimate trust can be placed in God alone, as shown by the companion's final words bequeathing himself to his Lord.
A believer's dignity cannot be taken away by any oppressor; the spirit remains free even when the body is imprisoned.
practical_advice: When facing a situation that feels hopeless or beyond your control, identify the one thing you can still give — your trust, your prayer, your dignity — and offer it fully to God.
If you are going through a painful separation or fear of loss, express your feelings honestly as this companion did through verse; writing or speaking your emotions is a healthy release.
Remind yourself that status and worth in God's eyes is not measured by worldly outcome — whether you survive or are lost, your sincerity and character are what endure.

title: Tamim al-Dari's Account of Al-Jassasa and Al-Dajjal
summary: Tamim al-Dari came to the Prophet and recounted how his ship went off course, landing on an island where they encountered a mysterious creature called al-Jassasa and a chained man who was al-Dajjal, who questioned them about the Prophet's mission.
story: Tamim ai-Dan came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and told him how he had gone to sea. His ship had gone off course and they had been cast upon an island. They had gone ashore seeking water and met a man with exceedingly long hair. Tamim asked him who he was, and the man replied that he was al-Jassasa. When they asked him about himself, he replied, 'I'll tell you nothing! But you can have this island!' Tamim's account went on, 'So we proceeded into the island and there we found a man in chains. He asked us, "Who are you people?" "We're Arabs," we replied. He asked, "What's all this about some prophet who has come forth among you?" We replied, "People believe in him, follow him and consider him truthful." He commented, "That is best for them." He then asked, "Tell me what news there is of 'Ayn Za'r." We did so, and he was so excited he almost jumped right outside! He then asked, "What news is there of the date-palms at Baysan? Are they bearing fruit yet?" We told him they were and again he was most excited. He next said, "If I were allowed to do so, I would travel throughout the land - except for Tayba!"' Fatima continued, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent him (Tamim) out to the people and he recounted this to them. And he said, "This is Tayba, and that man was al-Dajjal, the imposter."'
lessons: Signs and warnings given by God are meant to increase our awareness and preparedness, not to paralyze us with fear — the Prophet shared this account openly so the community could be informed.
The fact that al-Dajjal acknowledged that belief in the Prophet was 'best for them' is a reminder that truth is recognizable even to those who oppose it.
Medina — Tayba — was a place of divine protection, reminding us that there are sanctuaries of safety and goodness that we should seek out and hold close.
practical_advice: When you encounter something confusing or unsettling, bring it to a trusted, knowledgeable person rather than dwelling on it alone — Tamim brought his strange experience directly to the Prophet.
If anxious thoughts or fears about the future are overwhelming you, ground yourself in what is certain: your faith, your community, and the knowledge that God is in control of all outcomes.
Seek knowledge about your faith's teachings on trials and end-times not to fuel anxiety but to build mental and spiritual readiness — informed believers are steadier believers.

title: The Delegation of Banu Asad and the Verse of Obligation
summary: A delegation of ten men from Banu Asad came to the Prophet in a year of drought claiming they had come without being summoned, implying they were doing him a favour, upon which God revealed a verse correcting their attitude.
story: Al-Waqidi also stated that a delegation from Banu Asad came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), early in 9 AH; it consisted of ten men. These included Dirar b. al-Azwar, Wabisa b. Ma'bad, Tulayha b. Khuwaylid, who later claimed to be a prophet, then accepted Islam and became a good Muslim, and Nafada b. 'Abd Allah b. Khalaf. Their leader, al-Hadrami b. 'Amir, commented, 'Messenger of God, we have made our way to you in dark of night in a year of drought without you sending out anyone to us.' And so God revealed about them, 'They think they place you under an obligation by accepting Islam. Say: "Don't place me under an obligation for your accepting Islam; rather, it is God who places you under an obligation by guiding you to the faith, if you are sincere"' (surat al-Hujurat; XLIX, v.17). There was one clan of theirs known as Banu al-Rathya, 'the error clan'; he changed their name to Banu al-Rashda, 'the correct clan'. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had requested Nafada b. 'Abd Allah b. Khalaf to bring him a camel that would be excellent for both riding and milk and that did not have young. The only one Nafada could find belonged to a cousin of his and he did bring it. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told him to draw some of its milk. He drank some and gave the remainder back, saying, 'May God bless her and those who donated her.' Nafada asked, 'Messenger of God, and also those who brought her?' He added, 'And those who brought her.'
lessons: Guidance and faith are gifts from God, not favours we do for others or even for God — true gratitude begins with recognising who the real Benefactor is.
The Prophet's renaming of the 'error clan' to the 'correct clan' shows that identity and reputation can be transformed through sincerity and a fresh start in faith.
Even small acts of effort — like Nafada going out of his way to find the right camel — are noticed and blessed; no sincere contribution goes unacknowledged.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether you approach acts of worship or kindness with a spirit of gratitude or a sense of self-congratulation — genuine sincerity comes when we remember that everything good flows from God.
If you carry a negative self-image or a family/community reputation that has held you back, know that transformation is possible — your present sincerity can define you more than your past.
Practice appreciating those who go out of their way to help you; the Prophet's inclusion of 'those who brought her' in his blessing is a model for how to honour effort at every level.

title: The Delegation of Banu Fazara and the Rain Prayer
summary: A delegation from Banu Fazara arrived on emaciated camels after the Prophet's return from Tabuk, describing drought and starvation, and the Prophet ascended the minbar and prayed for rain, which came immediately and was then redirected away from Medina itself.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) returned from Tabuk, that being in 9 AH, a delegation consisting of some ten men came to him from Banu Fazara. These included Kharija b. Hisn and al-Harith b. Qays b. Hisn; the latter was the youngest of them. The camels they rode were emaciated. They had come to affirm their acceptance of Islam. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked them about their lands. One of them replied, 'Messenger of God, our lands are suffering drought; our cattle are dying. Our whole area is barren, and our families are starving. Pray to God for us!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) mounted the minbar and said, 'O God, water Your land and Your cattle! Extend forth Your mercy and give life to Your dead land! O God, send rain upon us to render a great area fertile, soon and without delay, making it beneficial and without harm! O God, send rain for us as an act of mercy, not for punishment, nor for destruction, without inundation or damage! O God, send us rainfall and give us aid against our enemies!' It did rain and they could see no break in the sky. And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) mounted the minbar and prayed, saying, 'O God, let it be around about us, but not upon us! Let it be upon the hillocks and the knolls, on the valleys and the woodlands.' And the rain was drawn away from Medina as though it were a garment removed.
lessons: When facing hardship, the first and most powerful recourse is sincere supplication to God — the Prophet immediately turned to prayer when his community was in need.
Prayer can be specific and nuanced; the Prophet asked for rain that was merciful, not destructive — this teaches us to ask God for what is truly beneficial, not just what we think we want.
Even answered prayers sometimes need to be guided — the Prophet prayed a second time to redirect the rain, showing that engagement with God in supplication is ongoing, not a one-time event.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by circumstances beyond your control — financial stress, health worries, environmental hardship — make a specific, heartfelt du'a articulating exactly what you need and asking that it come as mercy, not hardship.
Model the Prophet's balance: ask boldly for what your community needs, then also ask that the blessing not overwhelm or harm others — cultivate a prayer life that is both courageous and considerate.
If your first effort at solving a problem creates new challenges, don't despair — revisit, reassess, and pray again with fresh specificity, as the Prophet did when the rain needed redirecting.

title: The Delegation of Banu Murra and the Drought Prayer
summary: Thirteen men from Banu Murra arrived after the Prophet's return from Tabuk describing drought in their land; the Prophet prayed for rain and they discovered upon returning home that it had rained the very day he made the prayer.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that their arrival came in 9 AH when he returned from Tabuk. They consisted of 13 men, including al-Harith b. 'Awf. The Prophet (SAAS) awarded them each 10 awqiyya weight of silver. To al-Harith b. 'Awf he gave 12 awqiyya. They related that their country was undergoing drought and he prayed for them, saying, 'O God, send down rain upon them!' When they returned home they discovered that it had rained that very day the Messenger of God (SAAS) had spoken the prayer for them.
lessons: The immediate correspondence between the Prophet's prayer and the rainfall is a reminder that God's response to sincere supplication can be instantaneous, even when the one praying is far from the place of need.
The Prophet's generosity in giving material gifts alongside spiritual support shows that caring for people means addressing both their physical and spiritual needs.
Sometimes we do not witness the answer to our prayers immediately — the delegation only discovered the rain when they returned home, reminding us that God's responses may unfold beyond our sight.
practical_advice: When someone comes to you overwhelmed by hardship, offer both practical support and sincere prayer — don't treat spiritual and material help as mutually exclusive.
Trust that prayers made sincerely on behalf of others carry real weight; make du'a for those you love who are suffering, even when you are far from them and cannot help in other ways.
When you feel your situation is hopeless, remember that transformation can happen in a single moment — the drought ended the very day the prayer was spoken.

title: The Delegation of Banu Tha'laba Seeking to Affirm Islam
summary: Four men from Banu Tha'laba came to the Prophet as envoys of their people to affirm their acceptance of Islam; they were hosted generously and given silver awards before departing.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that it was related to him by Musa b. Muhammad b. Ibrahim, from a man of Banu Thaqaba, who quoted his father as saying, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) returned from al-Ji'rana in 8 AH four of us went to him and said, "We are envoys from our people who wish to affirm their acceptance of Islam." He ordered us to be accorded hospitality. We stayed there a few days, then went to him to say farewell. He told Bilal, "Give them the awards usual to delegations." He brought large quantities of silver and gave each of our men the weight of five awqiyya, saying, "We don't have any dirham coins." We then returned home.'
lessons: Welcoming those who come seeking guidance with hospitality and generosity creates a positive and lasting impression of faith communities.
The Prophet's honest acknowledgement that he had no dirham coins — offering what he had rather than pretending — is a model of dignified transparency in giving.
Representing your community as an envoy of goodwill is an honourable role; these four men carried the hopes of their people and were received with full dignity.
practical_advice: If someone comes to you seeking a new beginning — in faith, in a relationship, in a community — receive them warmly and without conditions; hospitality opens hearts.
Be honest about what you can and cannot give when helping others; offering what you genuinely have is more honourable than promises you cannot keep.
Consider whether there are people in your life who need you to take the first step toward connection on their behalf, as these men did for their tribe.

title: The Delegation of Banu Muharib at the Farewell Pilgrimage
summary: A delegation from Banu Muharib arrived during the farewell pilgrimage, including a man the Prophet recognized who expressed gratitude for living to believe, and the Prophet miraculously cleared the face of Khuzayma b. Sawa.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that Muhammad b. Salih quoted Abu Wajza al-Sa'di, as saying, 'A delegation from (Banu) Muharib came in 10 AH during the Hijjat al-wada', "the farewell pilgrimage". It consisted of ten men, including Sawa' b. al-Harith and his son Khuzayma. They stayed at the home of Ramia, daughter of al-Harith. Bilal would bring them their lunch and their dinner. They accepted Islam, saying that they were leaders of their people. And in the past the fairs had treated the Messenger of God (SAAS), in a more rough and rude way than they had. In that delegation there was one man whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) recognized. That man told him, "Praise be to God who has allowed me to live on to believe in you." The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, "These hearts are in the hands of God, Almighty and Glorious is He." The Messenger of God (SAAS) wiped the face of Khuzayma b. Sawa' and it turned clear and white. He gave them the awards usual to delegations and they then returned home.'
lessons: The man's gratitude for simply living long enough to embrace faith is a profound reminder to be grateful for every moment we have to grow closer to God.
The Prophet's response — 'these hearts are in the hands of God' — teaches humility; guidance is ultimately a divine gift, not something we can manufacture for ourselves or others.
Even those who once treated the Prophet harshly came to faith; no past of hostility or indifference is too great an obstacle for a sincere heart to overcome.
practical_advice: Pause today and reflect on what you have been given the chance to experience, learn, or believe — gratitude for 'still being here' is one of the most grounding spiritual practices available to you.

If you are worried about someone whose heart seems far from goodness or faith, remember that hearts are in God's hands — your role is to be a sincere presence and to make du'a, not to force transformation.

If past behaviour or words you regret are causing you shame or guilt, know that the door of sincere return is always open — those who once treated the Prophet rudely became among his welcomed companions.

title: The Delegation of Banu Kilab and the Work of al-Dahhak
summary: Thirteen men from Banu Kilab, including the poet Labid b. Rabi'a, came to the Prophet and reported how their emissary al-Dahhak b. Sufyan had taught them God's Book, invited them to faith, and distributed charity from the wealthy to the poor among them.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that they came in 9 AH, their delegation consisting of 13 men. These included Labid b. Rabi'a, the poet, and Jabbar b. Sulma. There was great friendship between the latter and Ka'b b. Malik, who warmly honoured and welcomed Jabbar and made gifts to him. They went with him (Ka'b) to the Messenger of God (SAAS), to whom they spoke with the Islamic words of greeting. They told him that al-Dahhak b. Sufyan al-Kilabi had gone among them with God's Book and with information concerning the religious practices of His Messenger that had been ordained by God. Al-Dahhak had invited them to God and they had responded. He had then taken from their wealthy charitable donations, which he had then distributed to their poor.
lessons: Al-Dahhak's work among Banu Kilab shows that one dedicated individual who carries knowledge and acts with justice can transform an entire community.
The redistribution of charity from the wealthy to the poor within the same tribe is a model of economic justice built on faith — caring for one's own community is a form of worship.
True friendship, like that between Ka'b b. Malik and Jabbar, becomes a bridge to greater goods — their bond led Jabbar all the way to the Prophet's presence.
practical_advice: Consider what it would mean to be an 'al-Dahhak' in your own circle — someone who shares knowledge, invites others to goodness, and practically supports those in need around them.

If you are in a position of relative wealth or stability, identify concrete ways to support those in your immediate community who are struggling — this is both a religious practice and a powerful antidote to feeling helpless.
Nurture your meaningful friendships; a sincere and generous friend can be the very means through which someone finds their path to healing, faith, or a better life.

title: The Delegation of Banu Abs and the Prophet as Their Tenth
summary: Nine men from Banu Abs came to the Prophet, who declared himself their tenth member, had a banner made for them with the motto 'O Ten!', and sent them to intercept a Quraysh caravan from Syria.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that they were nine in number, and he named them. The Prophet (SAAS) said 'And I am the tenth of you.' He then gave orders to Talha b. 'Ubayd Allah, who made up a banner for them and he made its motto, 'O Ten!'. It is said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked them about Khalid b. Sinan al-'Absi, whose biography we mentioned in the account of the jahiliyya. They said he had had no descendants. Al-Waqidi stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent them out to waylay a Quraysh caravan arriving from Syria. This would mean that their acceptance of Islam would have preceded the conquest of Mecca. But God knows best.
lessons: The Prophet's declaration of being 'the tenth' of the delegation was an act of profound inclusion — a leader who joins rather than stands above his people inspires genuine loyalty and belonging.
A community banner and a shared motto, however simple, create a sense of unity and purpose — symbols and shared identity matter in building cohesive groups.
The Prophet's interest in the history of Khalid b. Sinan shows that he honoured the memory of righteous figures from his community's past, teaching us to value our spiritual heritage.
practical_advice: If you lead a group or team, look for opportunities to stand alongside your people rather than above them — participation builds trust that instruction alone cannot.
Create small symbols of shared identity in your family, team, or community — a shared motto, a ritual, a name — these seemingly small things build powerful bonds.
Take time to learn about the righteous figures in your own family or community's history; this kind of rootedness provides stability and inspiration during difficult times.

title: The Delegation from Banu Ru'as b. Kilab
summary: A man named 'Amr b. Malik from Banu Ru'as accepted Islam and returned to invite his people, but after killing a man of Banu 'Aqil he came to the Prophet in manacles, fearing punishment. Through sincere supplication he earned the Prophet's forgiveness.
story: Al-Waqidi went on to relate that a man named 'Amr b. Malik b. Qays b. Bujid b. Ru'as b. Kilab b. Rabi'a b. 'Amir b. Sa'sa'a arrived to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and accepted Islam. He then returned to his own people and invited them into Islam, saying, "We will then gain from Banu 'Aqil the like of what they took from us." He then recounted a battle that took place between them and how this 'Amr b. Malik had killed a man of Banu 'Aqil. He ('Amr) said, "I placed my hands in manacles and went to the Messenger of God (SAAS). He had learned what I had done and had said, 'If he comes to me, I'll strike off his hands above the manacles!' When I came in and greeted him in Islam, he did not return my greeting, but turned away. I then approached him from his right side, but again he turned away. I came to him from his left, and again he turned away. Then I approached him from directly in front and said, 'Messenger of God, when the approval of the Lord, Almighty and Glorious is He, is sought and He approves; now therefore do be pleased with me - may God be pleased with you!' He responded, 'I am so pleased.'"
lessons: Genuine repentance and humble supplication can open doors that seem permanently closed — even after grave wrongdoing, the sincere seeker of forgiveness is not turned away forever.
Persistence in seeking reconciliation, approaching from every angle with humility rather than giving up, reflects the courage that accompanies true remorse.
Invoking God's pleasure as the highest aspiration — rather than merely seeking to escape punishment — is the most powerful frame for sincere repentance.
practical_advice: When you have wronged someone and they are not yet ready to receive you, do not flee in shame; remain present, be patient, and seek the right moment and the right words to sincerely apologize.

Frame your apologies around the other person's wellbeing and your genuine desire for righteousness, not only around relieving your own guilt — this sincerity is felt and often transforms the encounter.
If you carry guilt over a past action, remind yourself that God's mercy is wider than any sin; approach repentance step by step, even if the first attempts are met with silence.

title: The Delegation from Banu 'Aqil b. Ka'b and the Grant of Al-'Aqiq
summary: The delegation from Banu 'Aqil visited the Prophet, who granted them the land of al-'Aqiq with its palm trees and wells in recognition of their prayer, almsgiving, and obedience, recording the grant in a formal document.
story: Al-Waqidi recounted that they came to visit the Messenger of God (SAAS), and that he accorded them the income from al-'Aqiq - the 'Aqiq of Banu 'Aqil - which is land on which there are palm trees and wells. The Messenger of God (SAAS) wrote a document to this effect, as follows, "In the name of God, the most Merciful and Beneficent. This records what Muhammad, Messenger of God, has given to Rabi', Mutrif and Anas - namely al-'Aqiq - for their having performed the prayers, made payment for the alms, and having accepted and obeyed (my) authority. He has not given them any right (to anything) belonging to a Muslim." The document was entrusted to Mutrif. Al-Waqidi went on, "Laqit b. 'Amir b. al-Muntafiq b. 'Amir b. 'Aqil, he being known as Abu Razin, also came to him. He awarded Laqit a well called al-Na'im; Laqit expressed allegiance to him for his people."
lessons: Fulfilling the core obligations of faith — prayer, charity, and obedience to righteous authority — forms the foundation upon which worldly blessings and community standing are built.
A leader who documents agreements and honours commitments in writing models integrity and the importance of clarity in communal affairs.
Formal recognition of a community's faith and practice affirms their dignity and strengthens their bond to the larger community of believers.
practical_advice: Ensure that the commitments you make to God — in prayer, generosity, and ethical conduct — are consistent and reliable, as these are the true currency of trustworthiness in any community.
When entering agreements or making promises, document them clearly to protect all parties and prevent future misunderstanding.
Celebrate and affirm the sincere efforts of others to do good; acknowledgment of faithfulness encourages continued growth and strengthens communal bonds.

title: The Delegation from Banu Qushayr b. Ka'b and the Verses of Qurra
summary: Qurra b. Hubayra from Banu Qushayr accepted Islam before the Farewell Pilgrimage, received gifts and a cloak from the Prophet, and was appointed to collect charitable donations from his people, commemorating the experience in verse.
story: This was prior to the Hijjat al-wada', "the farewell pilgrimage", and also before the battle of Hunayn. Among these men is said to have been Qurra b. Hubayra b. 'Amir b. Salama al-Khayr b. Qushayr. He accepted Islam and the Messenger of God (SAAS) made a payment and also awarded him a cloak. He placed him in charge of collecting the charitable donations from his people. When he returned home, Qurra spoke the following verses, "The Messenger of God defended it when it came down to him, protecting it from attack or criticism. And so it attained lush green pastures, Muhammad having provided for all its needs, Upon it is a hero, who would never allow blame to ride beside him, who encourages the hesitant to overcome indecision."
