lessons: Being entrusted with responsibility within one's community is both an honour and a call to serve others — leadership in charity is among the most dignified roles.
Expressing gratitude through creative means such as poetry or writing is a noble tradition; giving voice to one's blessings deepens appreciation and inspires others.
The Prophet's practice of appointing trusted community members to gather and distribute charitable donations shows the wisdom of decentralised, locally-rooted service.
practical_advice: When you receive a trust or responsibility, approach it as a gift rather than a burden, and let it motivate you to grow into the role with sincerity.
Find your own creative outlet — writing, art, conversation — to express gratitude for the blessings in your life; externalising thankfulness strengthens it.
Look for ways to serve your immediate community in practical terms, such as organising charity drives or supporting those in need, as acts of collective care build lasting bonds.

title: The Delegation from Banu al-Bakka' and the Blessing on the Goats
summary: A one-hundred-year-old man named Mu'awiya from Banu al-Bakka' asked the Prophet to stroke his dutiful son Bishr's face for blessing; the Prophet did so and gave them ash-coloured goats whose blessing was so great that the family never suffered drought or poverty afterward.
story: It is said that they arrived in 9 AH and that they were 30 men in number. Among them was Mu'awiya b. Thawr b. Mu'awiya b. 'Ubada b. al-Bakka', he at that time being 100 years old. He had a son with him named Bishr. Mu'awiya asked, 'Messenger of God, I would receive blessing from your touch. I am old, and this son of mine is dutiful towards me. Please stroke his face.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) did stroke his face and gave him some ash-coloured goats. He spoke a blessing over them. And thereafter they were never afflicted by drought or poverty. Muhammad b. Bishr b. Mu'awiya spoke the following verses on this: "It was my father whose head the Messenger stroked, praying for good and blessings for him. When he went to him, Ahmad gave him ash-coloured goats, lean but not emaciated like snakes. They would satisfy the delegation every evening, and return to do the same during the day. Blessed is the gift, and so too the donor, and salutations to him from me for as long as I live."
lessons: A parent's love that seeks blessings for their child, rather than material gain alone, is one of the most beautiful expressions of parenthood.
Blessings can arrive in humble forms — a touch, a prayer, a modest gift — and their spiritual weight far exceeds their outward appearance.
Gratitude transmitted across generations, as shown by the son's verses honouring his father's encounter with the Prophet, keeps the memory of grace alive and inspires descendants.
practical_advice: When you pray for your children or loved ones, be specific and sincere — a heartfelt du'a (supplication) made at the right moment carries extraordinary weight.
Do not underestimate small acts of kindness or simple gifts; the blessing within them depends on the sincerity and love with which they are given and received.
Preserve and share the stories of grace and blessing in your family history; these narratives become anchors of faith and identity for future generations.

title: Wa'ila b. al-Asqa' Joins the Expedition to Tabuk
summary: Wa'ila b. al-Asqa' from the Kinana tribe accepted Islam, was rejected by his father but supported by his sister, and joined the Prophet on the Tabuk expedition; upon return he offered his share of the booty to Ka'b b. 'Ujra, whose generous reply reminded him that the camel's real service had been to God.
story: Al-Waqidi related, with complete lines of transmission, that Wa'ila b. al-Asqa' al-Laythi arrived to see the Messenger of God (SAAS), while he was equipping himself to leave for Tabuk. He performed the al-Fubh prayer with him, then returned to his people. He invited them to accept Islam, telling them about the Messenger of God (SAAS). His father told him, "I swear by God, I'll never forgive you!" His sister heard what he had said and accepted Islam. She equipped him for travel and he went with the Messenger of God (SAAS), to Tabuk, mounted upon a camel belonging to Ka'b b. 'Ujra. The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent him with Khalid to Ukaydir Duma. When they returned, Wathila offered to Ka'b b. 'Ujra the share of the booty that had been apportioned for him. Ka'b replied, "But it was to God the Almighty and Glorious that it carried you."
lessons: Faith sometimes costs us relationships we hold dearest, but God often provides unexpected support from surprising sources — here, a sister stepped forward when a father stepped away.
Acts of generosity and support, like a sister equipping her brother for a spiritual journey, can be the turning point that enables someone to fulfil their purpose.
When we serve others in the path of God, the reward ultimately belongs to God — Ka'b's gracious refusal of the booty reminds us that sincerity of intention, not material return, is what matters.
practical_advice: If a family member rejects you for your beliefs or values, do not let that rejection define your path; look for the supporters God places in your life, often in unexpected people.
When someone has helped you, express your gratitude practically and concretely, as Wa'ila did by offering his share of the booty — the gesture itself honours the relationship.
Reflect on the service you perform for others: is it done for recognition and repayment, or for God's sake? Reorienting your intention transforms ordinary acts into profound ones.

title: The Delegation from Ashja'
summary: The tribe of Ashja' arrived during the year of the Battle of al-Khandaq, numbering either 100 or 700 men, and camped at the Sal' defile; the Prophet provided them with supplies of dates, reached an understanding with them, and they eventually accepted Islam.
story: Al-Waqidi related that they arrived the year of the battle of al-khandaq. They consisted of 100 men; their leader was Mas'ud b. Bukhayla, and they made camp at the Sal' defile. The Messenger of God (SAAS) went out to them and ordered that they be provided supplies of dates. It is also said that they arrived after he had finished with Banu Qurayza, and that they consisted of 700 men. He reached an understanding with them and they returned home. They accepted Islam thereafter.
lessons: Meeting people where they are — going out to them, providing for their immediate physical needs — is a timeless model of dignified, compassionate outreach.
Faith is often a gradual process; the Ashja' departed, reflected, and then accepted Islam later, reminding us that seeds planted with sincerity bear fruit in their own season.
A leader who prioritises practical hospitality and genuine dialogue over coercion creates the conditions for lasting, freely-chosen commitment.
practical_advice: When trying to share something important with others — a belief, an idea, a cause — begin by attending to their immediate needs and comfort; receptivity follows dignity.
Do not be discouraged if your outreach does not yield immediate results; stay consistent, maintain goodwill, and trust that sincere engagement leaves an impression that grows over time.
In tense or uncertain situations, focus on what you can offer rather than what you can demand; generosity de-escalates conflict and builds the ground for understanding.

title: The Delegation from Bahila and the Peace Pact
summary: After the conquest of Mecca, the leader of the Bahila delegation, Mutarif b. Kahin, accepted Islam and brought a peace pact to his people, with 'Uthman b. 'Affan writing the document recording their acceptance of Islamic obligations.
story: Their leader, Mutarif b. Kahin arrived after the conquest of Mecca and accepted Islam. He took a peace pact to his people and signed a document to the Messenger of God (SAAS), accepting the laws and obligations of Islam. 'Uthman b. 'Affan, may God be pleased with him, wrote it.
lessons: A leader who personally accepts a new commitment before bringing it to their people models integrity; they do not ask others to carry what they themselves are unwilling to bear.
Formalising agreements — recording obligations in writing with witnesses — reflects the Islamic emphasis on transparency, accountability, and the sanctity of covenants.
The conquest of Mecca was a moment of spiritual opening that drew many toward Islam; moments of profound communal change can be catalysts for personal transformation.
practical_advice: If you are in a position of leadership, be the first to model the behaviour or commitment you are asking of others — your example carries more weight than your words.
When entering into significant agreements or commitments, write them down and have them witnessed; this protects everyone involved and honours the seriousness of the pledge.
Pay attention to historical or communal turning points around you — they may be precisely the moments when God opens a door you had not previously considered entering.

title: Qays b. Nushba of Banu Sulaym and His Testimony on the Prophet's Words
summary: Qays b. Nushba from Banu Sulaym came to the Prophet, accepted Islam after being deeply impressed, and returned to persuade his people by declaring that the Prophet's words were unlike anything in Byzantine history, Persian legend, Arab poetry, or Himyari divination.
story: He (al-Waqidi) stated, "A man named Qays b. Nushba came from Banu Sulaym to the Messenger of God (SAAS). The latter listened to what he had to say and asked him various questions to which he replied, being fully aware of everything. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then invited him to accept Islam, and he did so. "Qays returned to his people of Banu Sulaym, and told them, 'I've heard the history of the Byzantines, the legends of Persia, the poetry of the Arabs, the divinations of Himyar, but the words of Muhammad are not at all similar to theirs. Obey me and seek your fortune with him.' "The year of the conquest of Mecca, Banu Sulaym went out and joined the Messenger of God (SAAS), at Qadid; they consisted of 700 men, though this figure is also given as 1,000. They included al-'Abbas b. Mirdas and a number of their leading men. They accepted Islam, saying, 'Place us at your fore; make our banner red and our motto muqaddaman, "ahead!" He agreed to this and they were present with him at the conquest, and at the battles of al-Ta'if and Hunayn."
lessons: A person who has genuinely encountered something transformative becomes its most compelling advocate — Qays did not merely recite doctrine but spoke from direct personal comparison and conviction.
Intellectual honesty — acknowledging that something is genuinely different and better than what one already knows — is the beginning of true learning and growth.
When one sincere person acts with courage and clarity, they can move an entire community; Qays's testimony brought hundreds to a new path.
practical_advice: Draw on your own knowledge and experience when explaining your beliefs or values to others; a personal testimony grounded in lived experience is far more persuasive than abstract argument.
Cultivate genuine intellectual curiosity — read widely, listen carefully, and be willing to recognise when something you encounter surpasses what you have known before.
Do not underestimate the ripple effect of your own choices; when you commit to something good with sincerity, those around you observe, and many will follow in their own time.

title: Rashid b. 'Abd Rabbihi: From Idol Worshipper to New Name and New Life
summary: A man of Banu Sulaym who worshipped an idol witnessed two jackals urinating on it, was disgusted, destroyed it himself, then went to the Prophet and accepted Islam, receiving a new name reflecting his transformation from misguidance to right-guidance.
story: "Rashid b. 'Abd Rabbihi al-Sulami used to worship an idol. One day he saw two jackals urinating over it and he spoke the line, 'Is this a lord upon whose head two jackals urinate? Whoever has jackals urinate upon him is disgraced!' "He then attacked and broke it. After that he went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and accepted Islam. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked him, 'What is your name?' He replied, 'Ghawi b. 'Abd al-'Uzza.' 'No,' he told him, 'now your name is Rashid b. 'Abd Rabbihi.' He awarded him the revenue of a place called Ruhat where there is a running spring; it is known as the 'ayn al-rasul, 'the well of the Messenger'. He was the best man of Banu Sulaym, over whom he was placed in command. He was present at the conquest of Mecca and later actions."
lessons: Sometimes disillusionment with what we have been devoted to — when we see it clearly for what it truly is — is God's mercy, not a crisis; it is the first step toward something real.
A new name, a new identity, a new chapter: Islam teaches that genuine conversion is a genuine rebirth, not merely a change of label, and that the past does not have to define the future.
God often uses ordinary, even mundane moments — two jackals, an absurd sight — to shatter illusions that elaborate arguments could never reach.
practical_advice: If something you once believed in or depended on has revealed itself to be hollow or unworthy, do not mourn too long — let the clarity of that disillusionment propel you toward what is real and worthy of your devotion.
Consider whether the names you give yourself — the labels, the identities, the narratives — are ones that lift you toward your best self or keep you anchored to who you used to be.
Transformation is available at any point in life; do not let the length of time you spent on a wrong path convince you that it is too late to take a right one.

title: The Delegation of Banu Hilal: Ziyad's Visit and the Blessing on His Face
summary: Ziyad b. 'Abd Allah from Banu Hilal entered Medina and stayed at his aunt Maymuna's home; when the Prophet found him there he was initially displeased, but after his aunt's intercession he prayed for Ziyad, placed his hand on his head, and touched the tip of his nose — a blessing the family recalled as visibly luminous for the rest of Ziyad's life.
story: He (al-Waqidi) mentioned 'Abd 'Awf b. Ahram in their delegation. He accepted Islam, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) renamed him 'Abd Allah. Also there was Qilbisa b. Mukhariq, who is the source for a hadith about charitable donations. Amongst those he (al-Waqidi) mentioned as in the delegation of Banu Hilal were Ziyad b. 'Abd Allah b. Malik b. Bujayr b. al-Hadm b. Ruwayba b. 'Abd Allah b. Hilal b. 'Amir. When he entered Medina, he made his way to the home of his aunt Maymuna, daughter of al-Harith. He went inside and when the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered his home, he saw him, became angry and went out again. She called to him, "Messenger of God, he's my sister's son!" He then went inside, came out again and went to the mosque, accompanied by Ziyad. He performed the al-Zuhr prayer, then prayed for him. He placed his hand on his head, then touched the tip of Ziyad's nose. Banu Hilal used to say, "We could always recognise the blessing in Ziyad's face." A poet spoke the following verses to Ziyad's son 'Ali: "O Son of him whose head the Messenger touched and for whom he prayed for good at the mosque, I mean Ziyad, referring to none but him, to no passer-by, not to one accused or one seeking aid. That light on the bridge of his nose was always visible, until his body went down to the grave."
lessons: Intercession by someone who loves you — a family member who speaks up on your behalf — can open doors that your own efforts could not; cherish those who advocate for you.
A blessing received can mark a person in ways that are visible and lasting, not only inwardly spiritual but outwardly radiant — the community around Ziyad recognised something real in him.
Even moments of initial displeasure can be transformed by honest, gentle explanation; the Prophet's anger gave way to prayer and blessing once context was provided.
practical_advice: When someone you care about is in a tense situation with another person, a gentle, truthful word of context or advocacy can change the entire atmosphere — do not hesitate to speak up with kindness.
Reflect on the blessings you carry from those who prayed for you — parents, teachers, mentors; let those prayers be a source of confidence and a responsibility to live up to.

When you feel initial disapproval or rejection from someone, do not assume it is the final word; remain present, allow truth to emerge, and let the relationship develop beyond its difficult beginning.

title: The Delegation from Banu Bakr b. Wa'il and the Story of Qiss b. Sa'ida
summary: The delegation from Banu Bakr b. Wa'il came to the Prophet and asked about Qiss b. Sa'ida, the famous pre-Islamic orator; the Prophet clarified that Qiss was of Iyad, not of their tribe, and described him as a hanif who had spoken memorable words at the 'Ukaz gathering.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that when they arrived, they asked the Messenger of God (SAAS) about Qiss b. Sa'ida. He replied, "That man was not one of yours. He was of Iyad. He became a hanif in the jahiliyya. He was present at 'Ukaz while the people were gathered there. He spoke there the words since passed on as his. "In the delegation there were Bashir b. al-Khasasiyya, 'Abd Allah b. Marthad and Hassan b. Khut. A son of Hassan spoke the following verse: 'I am the son of Hassan b. Khut; my father was the messenger of all Banu Bakr to the Prophet.'"
lessons: Seeking knowledge and clarification — even about historical and cultural figures — from those who know best is a sign of intellectual humility and seriousness.
The pre-Islamic hanifs like Qiss b. Sa'ida remind us that the human heart has always been capable of reaching toward the One God, even without formal revelation — fitrah, the innate nature, is real.
Knowing your lineage and history accurately, and being corrected when mistaken, is a gift; clarity about origins helps communities build honest identities.
practical_advice: When you are unsure about something — a historical fact, a cultural claim, an attributed saying — go to a reliable source and ask directly rather than assuming or repeating what is merely tradition.
Reflect on those in history who sought truth before it was made easy or socially accepted — their example can inspire you in moments when following truth feels isolating.
Take pride in your honest heritage and history, neither inflating it with false claims nor diminishing it out of insecurity; accurate self-knowledge is the foundation of dignified identity.

title: The Delegation from Taghlib: Muslims and Christians Together
summary: A delegation of 16 from Taghlib arrived, comprising both Muslims and Christians wearing gold crosses; the Prophet made a peace pact with the Christians on condition they would not baptize their children into Christianity, and gave payments to the Muslim members.
story: He (al-Waqidi) stated that they consisted of 16 men, Muslims, and Christians wearing crosses of gold. They stayed at the house of Ramla, daughter of al-Harith. The Messenger of God (SAAS) made a pact of peace with the Christians on the understanding that they would not baptize their children into Christianity. He gave payments to the Muslims among them.
lessons: The Prophet's engagement with non-Muslim communities through pacts of peace rather than coercion reflects Islam's foundational respect for human dignity and freedom of conscience.
Navigating religiously mixed delegations with fairness — honouring each group distinctly without erasing difference — models the kind of principled pluralism that sustains peaceful coexistence.

Conditions in peace agreements that protect future generations — such as the clause about children — show far-sighted wisdom; the wellbeing of those not yet born deserves consideration in present decisions.
practical_advice: When working or living in religiously or culturally diverse settings, seek agreements and understandings that respect difference without requiring anyone to abandon their dignity — coexistence is built on clarity, not erasure.
Be willing to engage respectfully with people whose beliefs differ significantly from yours; respectful dialogue and clear agreements are more durable than either avoidance or confrontation.
Think about the long-term implications of the agreements and choices you make today, especially regarding children and future generations who will inherit the world you are shaping now.

title: The Delegation from Taghlib
summary: A delegation of 16 men from Taghlib, including both Muslims and Christians, visited the Prophet. He made a peace pact with the Christians and gave payments to the Muslims among them.
story: He (al-Waqidi) stated that they consisted of 16 men, Muslims, and Christians wearing crosses of gold. They stayed at the house of Ramila, daughter of al-Harith. The Messenger of God (SAAS) made a pact of peace with the Christians on the understanding that they would not baptize their children into Christianity. He gave payments to the Muslims among them.
lessons: Peaceful coexistence with people of other faiths is possible through clear agreements and mutual respect.
Leadership involves addressing the needs of different groups with wisdom and fairness.
Establishing boundaries with compassion preserves both relationships and religious integrity.
practical_advice: When navigating relationships with people of different beliefs, seek common ground and establish clear, respectful boundaries.
Practice fairness by addressing each person's needs individually rather than applying a one-size-fits-all approach.
Honor agreements you make with others, even across religious or cultural differences.

title: The Delegation from the People of Yemen and the Pious Youth
summary: A delegation of 13 men from Yemen visited the Prophet in 9 AH. A youth among them asked the Prophet to pray that God place his wealth in his heart rather than his hands, and thereafter became one of the most pious and ascetic of men.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that they came in 9 AH and that they consisted of 13 men. He gave them larger payments than he had to others. Al-Waqidi also said that there was a youth among them whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked what gift he wanted. He replied, "Messenger of God, pray to God to forgive me, to have mercy on me and to place my wealth in my heart." "The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, 'O God, forgive him, have mercy on him, and place his wealth in his heart.' And thereafter he was one of the most pious and ascetic of men."
lessons: True wealth lies in contentment of the heart, not in the accumulation of material possessions.
Asking for forgiveness and mercy is among the greatest gifts one can seek.
A sincere supplication made with the right intention can transform a person's entire character and life path.
practical_advice: When you feel consumed by material concerns, redirect your focus by asking yourself: am I seeking wealth for its own sake or for the contentment it might bring?
Practice making du'a (supplication) for inner peace and contentment rather than only for external gains.
Reflect on what 'placing wealth in your heart' might mean for you — cultivating gratitude, generosity, and freedom from greed.

title: The Delegation from Khawlan and the Destruction of Their Idol
summary: A delegation of ten men from Khawlan arrived in 10 AH. After learning Islam from the Prophet, they returned home and destroyed their idol 'Amm Anas, embracing what God permitted and forbidding what He prohibited.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that they were ten in number, and that they arrived in Sha'ban of 10 AH. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked them about their idol known as "'Amm Anas". They replied that they had exchanged him for a better one and that they intended to destroy it on their return. They learned the Qur'an and the orthodox practices of Islam and when they returned home, they did destroy the idol. They made permissible what God had rendered so, and they forbad that which God had forbidden.
lessons: When one sincerely embraces truth, they find the courage to leave behind old habits and practices that no longer serve their soul.
Learning is only complete when it is followed by action and implementation in daily life.
Inner transformation precedes and enables outward change.
practical_advice: Identify one habit or attachment in your life that you know is harmful, and make a concrete plan to leave it behind as an act of commitment to your values.
After learning something new about your faith or values, ask yourself: what is one practical step I can take today to act on this?
Do not delay positive change — the delegation did not wait; they returned and acted immediately.

title: The Delegation from Ju'fi and Overcoming a Deep Aversion
summary: The delegation from Ju'fi had a deeply held prohibition against eating heart. When they accepted Islam, the Prophet ordered them to eat it to complete their faith, and their leader ate it with trembling hands, overcoming his fear.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that they forbad eating heart. When their delegation accepted Islam, the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered them to eat heart. He had some cooked and handed it to their leader, saying, "Your faith will not be complete until you eat it." He did take it, his hand trembling, and he did eat it, saying, 'I did eat the heart unwillingly, my body trembling when I touched it.'
lessons: Faith sometimes calls us to confront deep-seated fears and ingrained habits that hold us back from wholeness.
Doing something difficult despite fear and trembling is itself an act of courage and devotion.
Completion of commitment requires moving beyond cultural conditioning when it conflicts with truth.
practical_advice: Identify a fear or aversion that may be rooted in habit or culture rather than genuine harm, and gently challenge yourself to face it.
When you feel your hands trembling before a necessary step, take that step anyway — courage is not the absence of fear but action in spite of it.
Seek guidance from those you trust when you are unsure whether an avoidance is wisdom or merely conditioning.

title: The Delegation of Banu Azd and the Twenty Essentials of Faith
summary: A delegation of seven men from Banu Azd visited the Prophet and described fifteen essentials of belief and conduct they already practiced. The Prophet praised their wisdom and added five more, completing twenty essentials of a meaningful life.
story: Abu Nu'aym stated, in his book Ma'rifat al-Sahaba (Knowledge about the Companions) as did the hafiz Abu Musa al-Madini, from a hadith of Ahmad b. Abu al-Hawari, who said, "I heard Abu Sulayman al-Darani, who said, 'Alqama b. Yazid b. Suwayd al-Azdi said, "My father related to me, from my grandfather, from Suwayd b. al-Harith, who said, 'I was the seventh of a delegation of seven men from my people who went to see the Messenger of God (SAAS). When we went in to him, we spoke with him and he was surprised by our appearance and our dress. He asked, "And what are you?" We replied that we were Believers.' "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) smiled and said, "There is truth in every statement; what is the truth in your statement and your faith?"' "'We replied, "There are fifteen essentials, five of which your emissaries ordered us to believe. Five you ordered us to act upon and five we ourselves developed while during the jahiliyya and still maintain — unless you should dislike any of them."' "'He asked, "What are the five my envoys ordered you to believe?"' "'We replied, "They told us to believe in God and in His angels, His books, His messengers and rebirth after death."' "'He then asked, "And what are the five I ordered you to act upon?" We replied, "You ordered us to say, 'There is no god but God', to perform the prayers, to pay the zakat, 'the alms', to fast at Ramadan, and to make pilgrimage to the ka'ba if we are able."' "'He asked, "And what are the five you developed during the jahiliyya?" We replied, "Being thankful when prosperous, being stoical in the face of difficulty, being pleased with whatever fate brings, being truthful at social gatherings, and refraining from cursing enemies."' "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, "Why, such wise and learned men! Their understanding is such as to almost make them prophets!"' "'He then said, "I'll give you five more; then you'll have twenty essentials. If you are as you say, then do not accumulate what you cannot yourselves consume, do not build what you do not inhabit, do not compete for something you will shortly leave behind, fear God to Whom you will be returned and before Whom you will be exposed, and desire strongly that to which you head and wherein you will be forever."' "'The party then left him, having memorized his advice and acting upon it.'"
lessons: Wisdom can be found in all peoples and traditions; the Prophet recognized and honored the good that existed even before Islam.
True faith is lived in practice — in gratitude, patience, contentment, honesty, and restraint — not only in belief.
Detachment from excess and focus on the eternal are cornerstones of a spiritually grounded life.
practical_advice: Reflect on the five qualities the Azd people brought from their pre-Islamic tradition: gratitude in prosperity, patience in difficulty, contentment with fate, truthfulness in gatherings, and restraint from cursing enemies — and honestly assess which of these you practice and which need cultivation.

Apply the Prophet's five additions as a practical guide: avoid accumulating what you cannot use, don't over-invest in temporary things, remember that all you compete for will be left behind, remain conscious of accountability to God, and orient your deepest desires toward the eternal.
Write down your own personal 'essentials of faith and character' — what values do you already hold? What do you need to add?

title: The Delegation from Al-Sadif and the Importance of Greeting
summary: The delegation from Al-Sadif arrived while the Prophet was delivering a sermon and sat down without giving the Islamic greeting. The Prophet gently corrected them and they rose to give the proper greeting.
story: They arrived in a group of some ten riders. They came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), as he was on the minbar delivering an address. They sat down without giving the usual Muslim greeting. He asked, "Are you Muslims?" "Yes," they replied. "Will you not give the Muslim greeting, then?" They all arose and said, "Peace be upon you, Prophet, and the mercy and blessings of God." He responded, "And upon you all be peace. Sit down." They did so and then asked the Messenger of God (SAAS) about the times for the prayers.
lessons: The outward expressions of faith, such as greetings, are not mere formality but carry real spiritual and communal significance.
A gentle correction given without humiliation preserves the dignity of the person being guided.
New Muslims and those learning should be corrected kindly so that they are drawn closer to practice, not pushed away.
practical_advice: When you are new to a practice or community, do not let embarrassment over mistakes keep you from asking questions and continuing to learn.
When you witness someone making an innocent mistake in religious or social etiquette, correct them with kindness rather than judgment.
Consistency in small practices — like greetings — builds the daily fabric of faith and community belonging.

title: The Jackal Who Spoke and the Signs of the Day of Judgment
summary: A shepherd encountered a speaking jackal who told him about the Prophet in Yathrib. When the shepherd reported this to the Prophet, he confirmed it and spoke about the signs of the Day of Judgment, including animals speaking to humans.
story: This jackal resembles the one referred to in the hadith related by Imam Ahmad: "Yazid" — he being Ibn Harun — "related to us, quoting al-Qasim b. al-Fadl al-Huddani, from Abu Nadra, from Abu Sa'id al-Khudari, who said, 'The jackal attacked a sheep. The shepherd went after it and took it from him. The jackal sat up on its tail and asked, "Don't you fear God, taking from me bounty to which God led me?" The shepherd exclaimed, "Amazing! A jackal sitting up on its tail and speaking to me like a human being!"' "'The jackal went on, "Would you like me to tell you something even more amazing than that? Muhammad, the Messenger of God (SAAS) is in Yathrib telling people of what happened long ago."' "'The shepherd then led his herd away into the town; there he left them in a corral and went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and told him about the jackal.' "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) had a call made for a general prayer meeting and went out and said to the bedouin, "Tell them." He did so. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, "He spoke the truth, I swear by Him who has the soul of Muhammad in His hand. Judgement Day will not come until animals speak to humans, and a man's strap on his whip and his sandal straps talk to him, and his thigh informs him of what his family does behind his back."'" Al-Tirmidhi related it from Sufyan b. Waki' b. al-Jarrah, from his father, from al-Qasim b. al-Fadl. He characterized it as hasan, gharib, sahih, "good, unilateral and authentic". We know of this only from the hadith of al-Qasim b. al-Fadl, whom scholars consider a trustworthy and reliable source. Yahya and Ibn Mahdi consider him reliable.
lessons: The miraculous is woven into ordinary life; remaining open to wonder can renew and deepen faith.
Bearing witness to the truth, even when it seems extraordinary, is a responsibility of the believer.
Awareness of the signs of the Day of Judgment is meant to cultivate mindfulness and accountability in daily life.
practical_advice: When you encounter something that challenges your understanding of reality, resist the urge to dismiss it immediately — reflect on what it might be pointing you toward.
Practice sharing remarkable experiences of faith with your community, as the shepherd did, so that others may also benefit and reflect.
Use awareness of accountability — that nothing is truly hidden — as a motivation to act with integrity in both public and private life.

title: The Delegation of the Jinn Seeking Guidance at Mecca
summary: Before the Hijra, delegations of jinn came to Mecca seeking guidance and listened to the Quran. The story of Sawad b. Qarib, a monk who accepted Islam through messages from a jinn channel, is recounted along with poetry describing the jinn's journey toward truth.
story: Material concerning the delegations of the jinn, "the spirits", at Mecca prior to the Hijra has been given above. We have examined this also with regard to the words of the Almighty, in surat al-Ahqaf (XLVI, v.29): "And when we sent towards you a group of the jinn who listen to the Qur'an". We gave commentary on those ahadith and ancient materials referring to this and gave the hadith of Sawad b. Qarib who had been a monk but accepted Islam. We wrote of his channel, who would bring him information, as when he told him, "I was surprised at the jinn and their knowledge, and their gripping their baggage on their mounts Heading for Mecca, seeking guidance, for believing spirits are not like those that disbelieve. So head for the finest men of Hashim, amidst their hills and rocks." He went on to say, "I was surprised at the jinn and their perceptions, and their gripping their cloth blankets on their mounts Heading for Mecca, seeking guidance, for the gracious of the spirits are not like the foul ones. So head for the finest men of Hashim, and raise your eyes to their head." He later continued, "I was surprised at the jinn and their knowledge, and their gripping their baggage on their mounts, Heading for Mecca, seeking guidance, for those that are evil are not like those who are good. So go to the finest men of Hashim; the jinn who believe are not like those of them who disbelieve."
lessons: Guidance is sought by all of creation — the human search for meaning and truth is universal and profound.
Even those who seem far from faith, like a monk steeped in another tradition, can be guided to truth through sincere seeking.
The distinction between the believing and the disbelieving — among jinn and humans alike — is a reminder that choice and orientation of the heart matter deeply.
practical_advice: If you feel lost or spiritually adrift, take comfort in knowing that seeking guidance is itself a sign of a living, open heart.
Do not discount unexpected sources of insight or moments of clarity — guidance can come through remarkable and surprising means.
Reflect on which direction your own 'mount' is heading: are your daily choices and habits orienting you toward truth and goodness, or away from them?

title: The Story of Hama b. al-Haytham b. Laqis b. Iblis and His Acceptance of Islam
summary: An ancient jinn named Hama, descended from Iblis, visits the Prophet Muhammad and recounts his encounters with previous prophets including Noah, Hud, Salih, Moses, and Jesus, ultimately accepting Islam and being taught Quranic chapters by the Prophet.
story: Al-Bayhaql stated in his Dalii'il al-Nubuwwa: "Chapter on the arrival of Hama b. al-Haytham b. Laqls b. Iblls to the Prophet (SAAS), and his acceptance of Islam" as follows: "Abu aI-I:Iasan Mul).ammad b. aI-I:Iusayn b. Da'ud aPAlawl, may God have mercy on him, informed us, quoting Abu Na~r Mul).ammad b. I:Iamdawayh b. Sahl al-Q!iri' al-Marwazl, quoting 'Abd Allah b. I:Iammad aI-Ami1l, quoting Mu\1ammad b. Abu Ma'shar, who quoted his father, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar, who quoted 'Umar, may God be pleased with him, as saying, 'While we were seated with the Messenger of God (SAAS), on one of the mountains of Tihama, an old man holding a staff arrived. He greeted the Messenger of God (SAAS), who returned the greeting, and asked, "By the sing-song and prattling of the ]irm, who are you?" He replied, "I am Hama b. aI-Haytham b. Laqls b. Ibhs." The Prophet (SAAS), asked "There are only two fathers in your line from Ibhs (Satan); how long have you lived, then?" I've been living ahnost as long as the world itself. Back when Cain killed Abel, I was still a boy, learning to understand words, moving across the hills and ordering food to turn bad and breaking up families. The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, "Evil was the work of both the blameworthy old man and the reprehensible boy!" The old man went on, "Spare me from destruction! I turn in repentance to God, Ahnighty and Glorious is He! I was with Noah in his mosque, along with those ofhis people who believe in him. I kept on decrying him for his call to his people until he wept and made me do so too. He said, 'Surely, I am one of those who repent for all that; and I seek refuge with God from being one of those who are foolishly ignorant.' I said, 'Noah, I was of those who took part in shedding the blood of that fortunate martyr Abel, son ofAdam. Can you find forgiveness for me in yourself?' He replied, 'Ham, hurry and find good to perform before you suffer grief and remorse. I have read in what God has sent down to me that every worshipper who turns in repentance to God and communicates this will have God turn to him in forgiveness. Arise, perform the ablutions and make two prayer prostrations to God.' Ham went on, 'I did at once what he told me. He then called out to me, Lift up your head; forgiveness of you has been revealed from heaven. And so I prostrated in worship to God.' He continued, 'I was also with Hud in his mosque, along with those of his people who believed. I kept on criticizing him for his call to his people until he wept over them and made me weep too. He said, Surely, I am one of those who repent for all that. And I seek refuge with God from being one of those who are foolishly ignorant.' Also I was with Sali!:I in his mosque with those of his people who believed. I kept on criticizing him for his call to his people until he wept over them and made me weep too. He said, I am one ofthose who repent for all that. And I seek refuge with God from being one of those who are foolishly ignorant. I used to visit Jacob, too; I was with Joseph in the secure place; and I used to meet Elijah in the valleys; I meet him now. I met Moses, son of 'lmran, and he taught me from the Torah. He told me, If you meet Jesus, son of Mary, greet him for me. I did meet Jesus, son ofMary, and greeted him from Moses. Jesus said, If you meet Mu!:Iammad (SAAS), greet him for me. The eyes of the Messenger of God (SAAS) flowed abundantly in tears as he wept at this. Then he said, And may peace be upon Jesns for as long as the world exists. And may peace be upon you too, Ham, for your keeping to the faith. Ham went on, Messenger of God, do with me as Moses did. He taught me from the Torah. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then taught him the words, When the awesome event occurs (silrat al-Witq;'a; LVI, v.I), And those who are sent forth (silrat al-Mursalitt; LXXVII, v.l), What do they ask one another (silrat al-Nabt; LXXXVIII, v.I), When the sun is covered over (silrat al-Takwtr; LXXXI, v.I), Say: I seek refuge with the Lord of the dawn (silrat al-Falaq; CXIII, v.I), Say: I seek refuge with the Lord of men (silrat ai-Nits; CXIV, v.I), and Say: He, God, is One (silrat al-IkhlitF; CXII, v.I). He then told him, Tell us your needs, Ham. And don't stop visiting us. Umar concluded, He shook hands with the Messenger of God (SAAS) in farewell and never returned to us. We don't know whether he is alive or dead."
lessons: No matter how great one's sins or how long one has lived in wrongdoing, sincere repentance is always accepted by God — this is a source of profound hope for anyone burdened by guilt.
The chain of prophets throughout history all shared the same message of repentance, humility, and seeking refuge in God, demonstrating the timeless consistency of divine guidance.
Weeping out of remorse and empathy, as seen with Noah, Hud, Salih, and the Prophet himself, is a sign of spiritual depth and compassion, not weakness.
practical_advice: If you carry guilt from your past, remind yourself that God's mercy is limitless — make sincere repentance, perform acts of worship, and trust that forgiveness is within reach no matter what you have done.
When you feel spiritually disconnected or lost, seek out sacred knowledge and righteous company, just as Hama sought out the prophets across generations to find guidance.
Use the short protective surahs taught by the Prophet (such as Al-Falaq, An-Nas, and Al-Ikhlas) as a daily practice to ground yourself spiritually and find peace during moments of anxiety or fear.

title: Khalid b. al-Walid's Mission to Banu al-Harith b. Ka'b in Najran
summary: The Prophet Muhammad sent Khalid b. al-Walid to the tribe of Banu al-Harith b. Ka'b in Najran, instructing him to invite them to Islam peacefully for three days before any fighting; the tribe accepted Islam without battle and sent a delegation to the Prophet in Madinah.
story: Ibn Is!:Iaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent forth Khalid b. al-Walid in Rabi' al-Akhir, or Jumada al-Uwla of 10 AH to Banu a1-Barith b. KaCh in Najran. He ordered him to invite them to Islam for three days before attacking them. If they responded positively, he was to accept them, and if they did not, he was to attack them. Khalid went forth and when he reached them, he sent out riders in all directions inviting them to Islam, telling them, 'Accept Islam and you will be safe.' They did accept Islam and accepted what they had been asked to do. Khalid remained with them, instructing them in Islam, in God's Book and in the practices of His Prophet CSAAS), as the latter had ordered if they should accept Islam and not do battle. Khalid b. al-Walid then wrote to the Messenger of God CSAAS), as follows, 'In the name of God the most Merciful and Beneficent. To Mu1).ammad, the Prophet, and Messenger of God, from Khalid b. ai-Wand. Peace be upon you, Messenger of God, along with the mercy and blessings of God. I express to you praise for God, other than Whom there is no god. To proceed, Messenger ofGod, may God's blessings be upon you, you dispatched me to Bano al-I:J:arith b. Ka'b and ordered me not to fight them for three days, to invite them into Islam and if they accepted I was to go and teach them the essentials ofIslam, God's Book, and the practice of His Prophet; if they did not accept Islam, I was to fight them. I did go to them and invited them into Islam for three days, as the Messenger of God CSAAS) ordered me. I sent riders out to them who said, Bano al-I:J:arith, accept Islam and be safe. They did accept Islam and did not fight. I am staying among them telling them of what God has ordered them and forbidding them from what God forbad them. I am teaching them the essentials of Islam and the practice of the Prophet CSAAS), until the Messenger of God CSAAS) writes to me. Peace be upon you, Messenger of God, along with His mercy and His blessings.' The Messenger of God CSAAS) wrote back to him as follows, 'In the name of God, the most Merciful and Beneficent. From Mu1).ammad, the Prophet, the Messenger of God, to Khalid b. al-Walid. Peace be upon you. I express to you praise for God other than Whom there is no other god. To proceed: your letter came to me with your messenger informing me that Bano al-I:J:arith b. Ka'b accepted Islam before you did battle with them, that they responded positively to the requirements ofIslam of which you informed them, that they testified that there is no god but God, that Mu1).ammad is His servant and His Messenger, and that God has given them His guidance. Congratulate them, give them warning and return; and let their delegation come with you. Peace be upon you and the mercy and blessings of God.' Khalid did return to the Messenger ofGodCSAAS), and the delegation ofBano al-I:J:arith b. Ka'b came with him. Qiys b. al-I:J:u~ayn Dho al-Ghu~~a, Yazid b. 'Abd ai-Madan, Yazid b. al-Mu1).ajjal, 'Abd Allah b. Qurad al-Ziyyadi, Shaddad b. 'Ubayd Allah al-Qanani and 'Amr b. 'Abd Allah al-l)ibabi were among them. When they came to the Messenger of God CSAAS), and he saw them, he asked, 'Who are these men who seem to be Indians?' Someone told him, 'These men are Bano al-I:J:arith b. Ka'b.' When they stood before the Messenger of God CSAAS), they greeted him and said, 'We bear witness that you are the Messenger of God, and that there is no god but God.' He replied, 'I bear witness that there is no god but God, and that I am the Messenger of God.' He then said, 'You are the people who, if forced back, would promptly advance again.' They remained silent; none of them offered him any response. He said the same a second and a third time and still no one responded. Then he said it a fourth time, and Yazid b. 'Abd aI-Madan replied, 'Yes, Messenger of God; we are those who advance again if forced back.' He said this four times. The Messenger of God ($AAS) then said, 'If Khalid had not written to me that you had accepted Islam and had not done battle, I would have cast your heads beneath your feet!' Yazid b. 'Abd aI-Madan said, 'In fact, I swear by God, we do not give praise to you or to Khalid.' 'Whom do you praise, then?' he asked. He replied, 'We praise God who led us to you, Messenger of God.' The Messenger of God ($AAS) responded, 'You speak the truth.' He then asked, 'How was it you overcame those you fought in thejahiliyya?' They replied, 'We overcame no one.' 'But you did,' he said. 'You did overcome those you fought.' They replied, 'We did overcome those we fought, Messenger of God, by uniting and not splitting up, and by never initiating any injustice with anyone.' 'You speak the truth,' he said and then appointed Qlys b. a1-I;Illiiayn as their commander." Ibn Isl).aq went on, "They then returned to their people for the latter part of Shawwal or the early part of Dhu al-QlCda."
lessons: Offering peace and invitation before resorting to conflict reflects the Prophet's merciful approach to da'wah — force is a last resort, not a first response.
True praise and gratitude belong to God alone, as Yazid b. Abd al-Madan beautifully demonstrated when he corrected himself before the Prophet, recognizing it was God who guided them.
Unity, consistency, and refraining from initiating injustice were the keys to the strength of Banu al-Harith — these same principles build resilient communities and individuals today.
practical_advice: When approaching a difficult conversation or conflict, follow the prophetic model of offering peace and dialogue first — give the other person multiple chances to respond before escalating.
When you receive good news or achieve something positive, pause and consciously direct your gratitude to God rather than to secondary causes, keeping your heart anchored in the right place.

Reflect on the principle of unity described by Banu al-Harith: examine areas of your life where internal division or inconsistency is weakening you, and work toward greater cohesion and fairness in your relationships and commitments.

title: The Delegation of Banu al-Harith and Their Appointment of Qays b. al-Husayn
summary: The Messenger of God questioned the delegation of Banu al-Harith about how they overcame their enemies in the pre-Islamic era, praised their answer about unity and avoiding injustice, and appointed Qays b. al-Husayn as their commander before dispatching 'Amr b. Hazm to instruct them in the faith.
story: then asked, 'How was it you overcame those you fought in the jahiliyya?' They replied, 'We overcame no one.' 'But you did,' he said. 'You did overcome those you fought.' They replied, 'We did overcome those we fought, Messenger of God, by uniting and not splitting up, and by never initiating any injustice with anyone.' 'You speak the truth,' he said and then appointed Qays b. al-Husayn as their commander. Ibn Ishaq went on, 'They then returned to their people for the latter part of Shawwal or the early part of Dhu al-Qi'da. After their delegation had left, the Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched 'Amr b. Hazm to give them instruction in the faith, to teach them the orthodox practice and the essential features of Islam, and to take from them their charitable donations. He wrote him a document specifying his charge and his orders to him.'
lessons: Unity and avoiding internal conflict are keys to overcoming adversity — this timeless wisdom applies to families, communities, and individuals facing hardship.
Never being the initiator of injustice is both a moral principle and a practical strategy for earning God's pleasure and the trust of others.
When leaders acknowledge truth and wisdom from others, it strengthens bonds of trust and sets a foundation for lasting guidance.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult situation, ask yourself: 'Am I united with those around me, or am I contributing to division?' Seek reconciliation before taking any external action.
Commit to a personal principle of not initiating harm or injustice toward others, even when you feel provoked — this protects your integrity and your community.
If you are placed in a position of responsibility, seek written clarity on your duties and obligations, as the Prophet did for 'Amr b. Hazm, to avoid confusion and conflict.

title: Abu Musa and Mu'adh Sent to Yemen: Instructions to Be Lenient and Co-operate
summary: The Prophet dispatched Abu Musa al-Ash'ari and Mu'adh b. Jabal to govern the two provinces of Yemen, instructing them to be lenient rather than harsh, to encourage rather than repress, and to co-operate rather than conflict with one another.
story: The Prophet (SAAS) sent Abu Musa and Mu'adh b. Jabal to Yemen. He dispatched each one of them to rule a province; Yemen has two provinces. He told them, 'Be lenient, not harsh; encourage, rather than repress.' In one account the words he used were, 'Co-operate with one another; do not conflict.' Each went off to do his work. They promised to visit and greet one another in their own territory and when close to that of the other.
lessons: Leniency and encouragement are more effective tools of leadership and guidance than harshness and repression, reflecting the mercy at the heart of Islamic conduct.
Co-operation between those in positions of responsibility is a prophetic value — division weakens any effort, while unity strengthens it.
Good leaders intentionally nurture relationships with their colleagues, as Abu Musa and Mu'adh did by pledging to visit one another.
practical_advice: In any position of authority — whether as a parent, manager, or community leader — consciously choose encouragement over criticism as your default approach.
When working alongside others toward a shared goal, establish regular check-ins and mutual support, just as the two governors pledged to visit one another.
If you feel tension with a colleague or partner, reframe your mindset from competition to co-operation, asking: 'How can we help each other succeed?'

title: Mu'adh Refuses to Dismount Until the Apostate Is Executed
summary: When Mu'adh b. Jabal visited Abu Musa in Yemen and found a man who had apostatized from Islam bound before him, Mu'adh refused to dismount from his mount until the man was executed in accordance with Islamic law.
story: Mu'adh was once travelling on his land near that of his colleague Abu Musa and rode further on his mule to meet the latter. He found Abu Musa seated, with his people all around him. There was a man there with him whose hands had been tied up to his neck. Mu'adh asked Abu Musa, ''Abd Allah b. Qays, what's this about?' He replied, 'This is a man who apostatized after accepting Islam.' Mu'adh told him, 'Well, I'll not dismount until he is killed!' Abu Musa assured him, 'But that's exactly why he was brought here. Do dismount.' Mu'adh insisted, 'I'll not dismount until he is killed.' The order was given, and he was killed.
lessons: Firmness in upholding principles, even in the presence of authority figures who may already be acting correctly, reflects a deep commitment to accountability.
Mu'adh's insistence was not stubbornness for its own sake but a conscientious assertion of religious duty — distinguishing principled resolve from mere obstinacy is a mark of wisdom.
True resolve sometimes means refusing comfort — Mu'adh's refusal to dismount was a symbolic act affirming that some matters cannot be set aside for convenience.
practical_advice: When you encounter a situation that conflicts with your values or obligations, be willing to pause and assert your position clearly before proceeding, rather than going along passively.
Develop clarity about which of your principles are non-negotiable so that, when tested, you act from conviction rather than emotion.
After a difficult moment of firmness, as Mu'adh did by then sitting and reciting Qur'an, allow yourself to return to calm reflection and spiritual practice.

title: Mu'adh and Abu Musa Discuss Their Recitation of the Qur'an
summary: After the execution of the apostate, Mu'adh asked Abu Musa how he recited the Qur'an; Mu'adh shared that he sleeps for a portion of the night then rises to recite, seeking God's reward for both his sleep and his worship.
story: Mu'adh dismounted and asked, ''Abd Allah, how do you recite the Qur'an?' He replied, 'I do so at regular intervals and piece-by-piece. How do you, Mu'adh?' Mu'adh replied, 'I sleep for the first part of the night, then get up, having slept for that portion I determined. Then I recite what God wrote for me I should. Thus I seek God's reward for both my sleeping and my being up praying.'
lessons: Every act, including rest, can become worship when approached with the right intention — Mu'adh's approach teaches that balance between rest and devotion is itself a spiritual practice.
There is no single correct way to maintain one's relationship with the Qur'an; sincerity and consistency matter more than the specific method.
Discussing spiritual practices openly with peers and mentors helps us refine and deepen our own connection to worship.
practical_advice: Set a consistent but manageable routine for Qur'an recitation that fits your natural rhythms, even if it is only a few verses — consistency outweighs quantity.
Before sleep, set an intention that your rest itself is an act of taking care of the body God entrusted to you, transforming an ordinary act into worship.
Periodically have honest conversations with a trusted companion or mentor about your spiritual practices, as Mu'adh and Abu Musa did, to gain perspective and encouragement.

title: The Prohibition of All Intoxicants: Abu Musa's Question About Yemeni Drinks
summary: Abu Musa asked the Prophet about alcoholic drinks made in Yemen from honey and barley, and the Prophet declared all intoxicants prohibited without exception.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched him to Yemen and asked him about the drinks made there. Abu Musa told him, 'They make al-bit' and al-mizr.' The narrator then asked Abu Burda, 'What is al-bit'?' 'It's an alcoholic drink made from honey. And al-mizr is an alcoholic drink made from barley,' he replied. The Prophet (SAAS) said, 'All intoxicants are prohibited.'
lessons: The prohibition of intoxicants is comprehensive and does not depend on the substance's ingredients or cultural context — the principle is the effect, not the material.
Seeking clarity on religious rulings when entering a new environment is a mark of conscientious faith — Abu Musa proactively asked rather than assuming.
Clear, unambiguous guidance protects communities from harm; the Prophet's concise ruling left no room for rationalization or exception.
practical_advice: When you encounter substances or practices in a new context whose permissibility is unclear, seek reliable knowledge proactively rather than acting first and questioning later.
If you are struggling with habits involving intoxicants or other harmful substances, remind yourself that the prohibition exists as a mercy to protect your mind, body, and relationships.
Surround yourself with environments and companions that support clarity of mind and strong judgment, since intoxicants impair the very faculties needed to live with purpose and integrity.

title: The Prophet's Instructions to Mu'adh on Dawah: Gradual Invitation to Islam
summary: Before sending Mu'adh b. Jabal to Yemen, the Prophet instructed him to invite the People of the Scripture gradually — first to the testimony of faith, then to prayer, then to zakat — and warned him against taking the best of people's wealth and to fear the supplication of the oppressed.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) said to Mu'adh b. Jabal, when he dispatched him to Yemen, 'You are going to people who have a scripture. When you reach them, invite them to testify that there is no god but God and that Muhammad is the Messenger of God. If they obey you in that, then tell them that God has enjoined upon them five prayers each day and night. If they obey you in that, then tell them that God obligates them to make a charitable contribution that will be taken from their rich and be returned to their poor. If they obey you in that, then refrain from (taking as tax payment) their best properties; be fearful of the curse of the oppressed, for there is no barrier between them and God.'
lessons: Gradual, step-by-step guidance is more effective and compassionate than overwhelming people with demands all at once — wisdom in calling others begins with meeting them where they are.
Zakat is a mechanism of social justice: wealth flows from those who have to those who need, reinforcing community bonds and reducing inequality.
The supplication of the oppressed is a serious warning — those in positions of authority must exercise their power with justice, knowing that God hears those who have no other advocate.
practical_advice: When guiding someone — whether in faith, work, or personal growth — introduce changes gradually and build on each small success before moving to the next, rather than overwhelming them.
Examine your own dealings with others: are there ways you may be taking more than your fair share or using advantages that harm those with less power? Make corrections now.
If you are in a position of collecting dues, taxes, or contributions from others, always take only what is required and protect the dignity and best assets of those you serve.

title: The Prophet's Farewell to Mu'adh: 'You May Not Meet Me Again After This Year'
summary: As the Prophet walked beside Mu'adh's mount giving him final instructions before his departure to Yemen, he told Mu'adh that they might not meet again, causing Mu'adh to weep, and the Prophet consoled him by saying weeping in such a manner comes from Satan.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched him to Yemen, the latter went out to give him instructions, walking beside his mount as he rode. When he had finished, he said, 'Mu'adh, you may well not meet me again, after this year; but perhaps you will pass by this mosque of mine, and my grave.' Mu'adh wept in humility at leaving the Messenger of God (SAAS). The Prophet (SAAS) then turned towards Medina and said, 'Those persons who are foremost with me are the pious; whoever and wherever they may be.' The latter then said, 'Mu'adh, don't weep. There are occasions for weeping; weeping comes from Satan.'
lessons: The Prophet's foresight about his own death, shared gently with a beloved companion, reflects how preparing for endings — even painful ones — is a form of mercy and wisdom.

Grief at parting from those we love is natural, but allowing sorrow to overwhelm us to the point of despair is something to guard against — the Prophet's redirection of Mu'adh is a model of emotional balance.
Piety and closeness to God are not determined by physical proximity to a teacher or leader, but by one's own inner state — the pious are closest to the Prophet wherever they are.
practical_advice: When facing a significant farewell or transition, allow yourself to acknowledge the sadness honestly, but consciously redirect your energy toward gratitude for what you had and hope for what is ahead.

If grief or anxiety about the future begins to paralyze you, gently interrupt that spiral — take a breath, make a du'a, and remind yourself that piety and connection to God travel with you wherever you go.
Use moments of departure and transition as opportunities to reflect on your spiritual priorities: are you among 'those who are foremost' in piety, regardless of your circumstances?

title: Mu'adh's Mission to a People Whose Hearts Are Delicate
summary: The Prophet told Mu'adh he was being sent to people with delicate hearts who would fight twice for the truth, and that his mission was to create tranquillity and acquiescence even amid the social disruptions that loyalty to Islam would cause within families.
story: Mu'adh used to say, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent me to Yemen and said, "You may well pass by my grave and my mosque. I have sent you to a people whose hearts are delicate; they will fight twice for the truth. You must fight along with those who obey you against those of them who disobey you. To the extent that wives will challenge their husbands, sons their fathers and brothers one another, they will be faithful to Islam. And so between the two sides you will create tranquillity and acquiescence."'
lessons: Commitment to truth sometimes creates friction within families and communities — this is not a sign of failure but of the depth of conviction taking root.
A leader or guide's role in times of social disruption is not to intensify conflict but to work toward tranquillity and reconciliation within the bounds of truth.
People with 'delicate hearts' are often those most capable of genuine faith — sensitivity is not weakness but a capacity for deep transformation.
practical_advice: If your commitment to your values has created tension in your relationships, do not interpret that tension as a reason to abandon your principles — instead, work patiently to build understanding with those around you.
When conflict arises in your family or community over matters of faith or values, focus your energy on creating peace and understanding rather than winning arguments.
Reflect on whether your own heart is 'delicate' — open to being moved, to feeling deeply, to genuine transformation — and nurture that sensitivity rather than hardening yourself against it.

title: Mu'adh Returns from Yemen and Asks About Bowing Down to the Prophet
summary: When Mu'adh returned from Yemen and mentioned that the people there bowed to one another as a form of greeting, he asked if they should bow to the Prophet; the Prophet replied that if he were to command any human to bow to another, it would be wives to their husbands.
story: When the latter returned from Yemen, he said, 'Messenger of God, I have seen men in Yemen who bow down to one another. Should we not bow down to you?' He replied, 'If I were to order any human being to bow down to any other, I would order women to do so to their husbands.'
lessons: The Prophet consistently redirected reverence away from himself and toward God alone, guarding the principle of tawheed even in the face of sincere affection.
The comparison to the marital relationship underscores the profound importance of mutual respect and honour in marriage within Islamic ethics.
Cultural practices that resemble worship must be evaluated through the lens of monotheism — sincere admiration for a person does not justify acts reserved for God.
practical_advice: Examine the ways you express deep respect or admiration for others — ensure your acts of honour remain clearly distinct from acts of worship, keeping your tawheed pure.
Reflect on the significance placed on the marital relationship in this hadith: are you fulfilling your responsibilities to your spouse with the seriousness and honour they deserve?
When you encounter unfamiliar cultural practices, approach them with curiosity and discernment, asking whether they align with your core principles before adopting them.

title: The Prophet's Instruction to Mu'adh: 'The Keys to Paradise Are Bearing Witness'
summary: The Prophet told Mu'adh b. Jabal that the keys to paradise lie in bearing witness that there is no god but God.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'The keys to paradise are in bearing witness that there is no god but God.'
lessons: The foundation of all spiritual and moral development rests on the sincere acknowledgment of God's oneness — everything else builds upon this.
A 'key' metaphor reminds us that access to the highest good requires something we must actively carry and use — faith is not passive but an active, living commitment.
Returning to the foundational testimony of faith, especially in moments of confusion or distress, reorients the heart and restores clarity of purpose.
practical_advice: In moments of anxiety, confusion, or feeling lost, return to the simple but profound statement of faith — recite the shahada with full attention and let it ground you.
Reflect regularly on what the shahada actually means to you personally, not just as a formula but as a lived commitment shaping your choices and relationships.
Teach those in your care — children, students, loved ones — to understand and internalize the shahada as a source of strength and identity, not merely as words.

title: The Prophet's Threefold Counsel to Mu'adh: Fear God, Repay Evil with Good, Honour People
summary: Mu'adh asked the Prophet for repeated counsel and received three instructions: fear God wherever you are, respond to a bad deed with a good one that erases it, and treat people with honourable conduct.
story: Mu'adh, (who said that) the Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, 'Mu'adh, respond to something bad by something good; that will erase it. Treat people with honourable conduct.' And from another narration: Imam Ahmad then stated that Isma'il related to him, from Layth, from Habib b. Abu Thabit, from Maymun b. Abu Shabib, from Mu'adh, who said, 'Messenger of God, give me instructions.' He replied, 'Fear God, wherever you may be.' 'Tell me more,' he asked. He replied, 'Respond to something bad by something good that will erase it.' 'Tell me more,' he asked. 'Treat people with honourable conduct,' he replied.
lessons: Consciousness of God in every place and situation is the master principle from which all other good conduct flows — taqwa is not a feeling but a constant orientation.
Responding to harm with good is both a spiritual practice and a psychological one: it prevents cycles of retaliation and can transform adversarial relationships.
Honouring people in our conduct — regardless of how they treat us — is the practical expression of faith in daily social life.
practical_advice: When someone wrongs you, pause before reacting and ask yourself: what is the 'good' response that could erase this harm rather than escalate it? Act from that place.
Practice awareness of God in small, mundane moments — at work, in traffic, in difficult conversations — until taqwa becomes less of a concept and more of a reflex.
Commit to one concrete act of honouring someone each day — a kind word, a patient listening, a generous interpretation of their actions — and notice how it changes your relationships over time.

title: The Ten Commandments the Prophet Gave to Mu'adh Before Sending Him to Yemen
summary: Before dispatching Mu'adh to Yemen, the Prophet gave him ten specific instructions covering monotheism, obedience to parents, prayer, avoidance of wine, courage in battle, steadfastness during plague, generosity to children, and instilling love of God in them.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) instructed me using ten phrases. He said, 'Do not associate anything with God, even though you might be killed or burned. Do not disobey your parents, even though they tell you to give up your property or your family. Do not deliberately forego any prescribed prayer. Whoever deliberately forgoes a prescribed prayer loses God's protection. Do not drink wine, for it is the cause of every vile deed. Beware of insubordination, for it incurs the wrath of God. Beware of flight from an advancing enemy, even if people are being destroyed. If death afflicts (your) people, you are of them, so stay firm. Expend upon your children from your assets and do not raise from them your staff in their upbringing. And inspire in them love of God, the Almighty and Glorious.'
lessons: Monotheism is the non-negotiable foundation that must be protected even at the cost of one's life — every other instruction flows from and depends upon this first commitment.
Prayer is not merely a ritual but a covenant of protection; abandoning it is not a private matter but a severance of one's relationship with God's guardianship.
Raising children with love of God — not merely with rules or discipline — is the deepest and most lasting investment a parent can make in their family's wellbeing.
practical_advice: Review your own practice of the five prayers honestly: is there a prayer you consistently delay or skip? Treat restoring that prayer as a matter of urgent spiritual priority.
If you are a parent or mentor, shift your focus from enforcing compliance to inspiring love — ask yourself what you are doing to help those in your care fall in love with God, not just obey rules.

Identify any habit in your life that could be described as 'the cause of every vile deed' — something that consistently leads you to worse decisions and worse versions of yourself — and make a serious plan to leave it.

title: The Prophet Warns Mu'adh Against Seeking Luxury
summary: Before sending Mu'adh to Yemen, the Prophet warned him to beware of luxury, saying that God's servants do not seek after it.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched him to Yemen, he told him, 'Beware of luxury; God's servants do not seek after luxury.'
lessons: Simplicity and contentment are prophetic values — the pursuit of luxury distracts from purpose, softens resolve, and can corrupt both leaders and communities.
A servant's identity is defined by orientation toward God, not toward comfort — when comfort becomes the goal, servanthood is quietly abandoned.
This warning is especially important for those in positions of authority, where access to resources creates particular temptation toward self-indulgence.
practical_advice: Conduct a periodic audit of your lifestyle: where has comfort or luxury gradually become an expectation rather than a blessing? Reintroduce gratitude and simplicity in those areas.
When making decisions about how to spend money or time, ask whether this choice serves your purpose and your obligations, or whether it is primarily about your own comfort.
Practice voluntary simplicity in one area of your life this week — a meal, a purchase, an entertainment habit — and observe how it affects your sense of clarity and contentment.

title: The Prophet's Instructions to Mu'adh on Zakat Collection from Yemen
summary: The Prophet gave Mu'adh detailed instructions on zakat rates for Yemen: one dinar per adult, specific livestock thresholds, and different tithes for rain-watered versus irrigated land.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched me to Yemen and ordered me to take one dinar or its equivalent in ma'afir, 'provisions', from each adult; he told me to take one mature cow for each forty cattle and one camel for each thirty cattle per annum. He ordered me to take one-tenth of the produce of land watered by rain and one-twentieth of the produce of land watered by buckets.
lessons: The detailed precision of zakat rulings reflects the Islamic principle that obligations must be clear and fair — ambiguity in communal financial duties leads to injustice and resentment.
Differentiating between rain-watered and irrigated land acknowledges human effort: where more labour is invested, the share taken is proportionally reduced — a built-in recognition of toil.
Clear financial responsibilities, communicated precisely to those who implement them, protect both leaders and communities from corruption and misunderstanding.
practical_advice: Take time to understand your own obligatory financial duties — whether zakat, tax, or other communal contributions — with precision rather than vagueness, so you can fulfil them with confidence.
When delegating financial responsibility to others, follow the Prophet's example of written, specific, documented instructions to eliminate ambiguity and protect all parties.

Reflect on the fairness embedded in the zakat system: wealth circulates from those who have to those who need. Ask yourself how your own giving is reflecting this spirit of circulation and community care.

title: Mu'adh ibn Jabal's Instructions on Zakat from Yemen
summary: The Prophet dispatched Mu'adh ibn Jabal to Yemen with detailed instructions on how to collect zakat from cattle and agricultural produce, including specific rulings on intermediate numbers.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched me to Yemen and ordered me to take one dinar or its equivalent in maCafir, 'provisions', from each adult; he told me to take one mature cow for each forty cattle and one camel for each thirty cattle per annum. He ordered me to take one-tenth of the produce of land watered by rain and one-twentieth of the produce of land watered by buckets. They proposed to me that I should take extra payment for between forty and fifty, between sixty and seventy, and between eighty and ninety. But I declined this, telling them, 'I will ask the Messenger of God (SAAS) about that.' When I returned, I told the Prophet (SAAS) about this and he told me to take one camel for thirty cattle and for every forty head I should take one mature cow. For sixty I should take two camels. For seventy, one mature cow and one camel. For eighty, two mature cows. For ninety, I should take three camels. For one hundred, one mature cow and two camels. For one hundred and ten, two mature cows and one camel. For one hundred and twenty, I should take three mature cows and four camels. The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered me not to take anything for intermediate figures; only if it amounted to a mature cow or a camel. He claimed that there was no tax on intermediate numbers.
lessons: When uncertain about a ruling or decision, it is wise to pause and seek proper guidance rather than improvise — as Mu'adh declined to act without clarity from the Prophet.
Integrity in financial and administrative matters is a mark of true faith; Mu'adh refused to overstep his authority even when pressured.
Seeking knowledge before acting protects both the individual and the community from harm and injustice.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult decision at work or in a responsibility you've been given, pause and consult a trusted mentor or authoritative source rather than guessing.
If you are in a position of authority over others' resources, document your actions carefully and remain transparent to build trust.
When overwhelmed by the complexity of a task, break it into clear categories as the Prophet did with zakat brackets — structured thinking reduces stress.

title: Mu'adh ibn Jabal's Debt, Dispatch to Yemen, and Dream
summary: Mu'adh ibn Jabal, burdened by debt that consumed all his wealth, was sent to Yemen by the Prophet to restore his fortune; after the Prophet's death, a dream guided him to submit his earnings to Abu Bakr, who returned everything to him.
story: Mu'adh b. Jabal was a handsome and generous young man, one of the finest of his people. Anything ever asked of him he would give, so that eventually he had a debt that foreclosed all his money. He told this to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and asked him to speak to his creditors, and this he did. But they would not reduce his debt at all; and if anyone should ever have had a debt excused because of the plea of another, it should have been Mu'adh after the appeal made by the Messenger of God (SAAS). The Messenger of God (SAAS) summoned him and immediately Mu'adh sold all his possessions and divided the proceeds between his creditors. Mu'adh had nothing left. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) was going on the pilgrimage, he sent Mu'adh to Yemen. And so Mu'adh was the first to do business with that money. When he returned from Yemen to Abu Bakr, 'the trusting', the Messenger of God (SAAS) had died. 'Umar came to him and said, 'Will you obey me and pay this money over to Abu Bakr? If he gives it to you, you can then accept it.' Mu'adh replied, 'I'll not give it to him! The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent me to Yemen to restore my fortune.' When Mu'adh refused, 'Umar went to Abu Bakr and said, 'Send a message to this man and take it, then pay him.' Abu Bakr replied, 'I'll not do that; the Messenger of God (SAAS) only dispatched him to restore his fortune. I'll not take anything from him.' Next morning, Mu'adh went to 'Umar and said, 'I see no alternative to doing what you said. I saw myself yesterday in a dream — being dragged to hell-fire; and you had hold of my waistband!' He then hurried off to Abu Bakr with everything he had brought; he even took him his whip and he swore he had kept nothing hidden from him. Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, said, 'It is yours; I will take none of it.' Abu Thawr related this, from Ma'mar, from al-Zuhri, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Ka'b b. Malik. He related it as above, except for the words, 'and in the year of the conquest of Mecca, the Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched him as governor of a part of Yemen. Mu'adh remained there until the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Then, in the caliphate of Abu Bakr, he returned and left again for Syria.'
