Al-Bayhaqi then related the story of Mu'adh's dream, giving a line of transmission through al-A'mash, from Abu Wa'il, from 'Abd Allah. In that account it is said that what Mu'adh brought from Yemen included slaves, and that he took them to Abu Bakr. When the latter gave everything back to him, Mu'adh took them back too. He then performed the prayer, and all the slaves prayed with him. When he was done, he asked them, 'To whom did you pray?' 'To God,' they replied. 'Then you are all manumitted to Him.' And he did free them.
lessons: Generosity is a noble quality, but unchecked giving without financial wisdom can lead to personal hardship — balance in charity protects one's own stability.
A vivid dream or inner warning can be a spiritual nudge toward doing what is right, even when the ego resists — Mu'adh's dream moved him to act with integrity.
True leaders like Abu Bakr honor the intention behind an appointment; he refused to take from Mu'adh what the Prophet had intended for his benefit, showing justice and compassion in authority.
practical_advice: If you find yourself in debt due to generosity or poor financial boundaries, seek counsel from someone you trust rather than carrying the burden alone.
When an inner feeling, dream, or recurring thought keeps urging you toward an action you've been avoiding, take it seriously and reflect on whether it is guiding you toward something right.
If you are in a position to help someone financially and have the means, consider releasing them from their obligation as Abu Bakr did — such acts of grace leave a lasting spiritual impact.

title: Mu'adh ibn Jabal Frees the Slaves Who Prayed
summary: Upon returning from Yemen, Mu'adh ibn Jabal prayed alongside the slaves he had brought; when they said they prayed to God, he immediately freed them all for the sake of God.
story: Al-Bayhaqi then related the story of Mu'adh's dream, giving a line of transmission through al-A'mash, from Abu Wa'il, from 'Abd Allah. In that account it is said that what Mu'adh brought from Yemen included slaves, and that he took them to Abu Bakr. When the latter gave everything back to him, Mu'adh took them back too. He then performed the prayer, and all the slaves prayed with him. When he was done, he asked them, 'To whom did you pray?' 'To God,' they replied. 'Then you are all manumitted to Him.' And he did free them.
lessons: Faith, when genuinely shared, has the power to transform relationships and dissolve unjust hierarchies — Mu'adh saw fellow worshippers of God, not property.
Acts of freedom and generosity done purely for God's sake, with no expectation of return, represent the highest expression of human dignity.
Recognizing the spiritual equality of all people before God should move us to treat others with greater justice and compassion in every context.
practical_advice: Reflect on any relationships in your life where a power imbalance exists — consider how you can restore dignity and equity in those dynamics.
When you witness someone else's sincerity in worship or in their values, let it move you toward generosity rather than indifference.
Practice acts of unconditional giving — whether of time, money, or opportunity — not for recognition, but as a conscious offering to God.

title: Mu'adh ibn Jabal's Judicial Method: Quran, Sunnah, then Ijtihad
summary: Before dispatching Mu'adh to Yemen as a judge, the Prophet asked him how he would rule on cases, and was delighted when Mu'adh said he would use the Quran, then the Sunnah, then his own careful reasoning.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched him to Yemen, he asked, 'How will you act if a case is brought to you for judgement?' He replied, 'I will make a judgement according to what is in God's Book.' 'And what if the case is not in God's Book?' 'Then in accord with the practice of the Messenger of God (SAAS),' he replied. 'And what if it is not covered in the practice of the Messenger of God (SAAS)?' 'I will ajtahidu, strive hard, and spare no effort.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then struck me in the chest and said, 'Praise be to God, who made fit for the envoy of the Messenger of God that which pleases the Messenger of God!'
lessons: Sound judgment follows a principled hierarchy: divine guidance first, established practice second, and sincere personal reasoning last — this order protects against error and ego.
The Prophet's joy at Mu'adh's answer shows that intellectual effort (ijtihad) in service of truth is praiseworthy, not presumptuous, when grounded in proper foundations.
When facing difficult decisions in life, beginning with your core values and established principles before improvising leads to wiser and more consistent outcomes.
practical_advice: When making a difficult decision, write down your guiding principles in order of priority — what does your faith say first, what does wisdom and experience say second, and only then reason through the specific situation.
Do not be afraid to think carefully and form your own considered opinion when established guidance does not clearly apply — careful reasoning in good faith is valued.
Before taking on a leadership or responsibility role, clarify your decision-making framework so that when pressure comes, you already know how you will respond.

title: The Dispatch of Khalid ibn al-Walid and then Ali ibn Abi Talib to Yemen
summary: The Prophet first sent Khalid ibn al-Walid to Yemen, then replaced him with Ali ibn Abi Talib, instructing Ali to let Khalid's men choose whether to stay or return; those who stayed with Ali received substantial booty.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent us with Khalid b. al-Walid to Yemen. He later dispatched 'Ali to take his place. He told him, 'Tell those of Khalid's men who wish to remain with you that they may; those who wish should return.' I was among those who stayed with him. And I received a considerable number of awqiyya as booty.
lessons: The Prophet's leadership style respected individual agency — soldiers were given a genuine choice about whether to stay or leave, not coerced into continuing service.
Transitions in leadership, when handled with clarity and respect, do not have to cause confusion or resentment — clear instruction from authority makes the transition smooth.
Sometimes staying committed through a transition of leadership, even when you could leave, brings unexpected reward and blessing.
practical_advice: When a leadership transition happens in your workplace, community, or family, give the new leader a fair chance rather than withdrawing loyalty immediately out of habit or preference.
If you are the one leading a transition, communicate clearly to those affected what their options are — respecting their choice builds trust and morale.
Reflect on moments when staying the course through uncertainty brought unexpected benefit — let those experiences strengthen your resolve the next time you face a similar choice.

title: Burayda's Hatred for Ali and the Prophet's Correction
summary: Burayda ibn al-Husayb harbored intense hatred for Ali ibn Abi Talib and joined a military expedition partly out of that hatred. When he complained to the Prophet about Ali's actions, the Prophet corrected him and commanded him to love Ali, transforming Burayda's hatred into deep love.
story: Imam A!).mad stated that Ya!).ya b. Sa'id related to him, quoting 'Abd al-Jalil, who said, "I was in a circle of people that included Abu Mijlaz and Burayda's two sons. 'Abd Allah b. Burayda said, 'My father Burayda related to me as follows, "I used to hate 'Ali more strongly than I had ever hated anyone. And there was one man of Quraysh whom I liked only because of his hatred for 'All. "'That man was sent out with the cavalry and I went with him, solely because of his hatred for 'All. We took some prisoners. He then wrote to the Messenger of God (SAAS), saying, 'Send someone to us who will divide the booty.' He dispatched 'Ali to us. Among the best of the captives was a black slave-girl. """"'Ali took the booty, divided it up and came out. When he did so, his head was wet. I asked, 'Abii al-Basan, what's this?' He replied, 'Didn't you see that black slave-girl included with the captives? I made the division and she became part of the khums. Then she became part of the household of the Prophet (SAAS), and so one of 'Ali's family. And I had sex with her.' '" "The man" had a message to send to the Messenger of God (SAAS). SO I said, 'Dispatch me.' And he did so, expressing his trust. (When in the presence of the Prophet (SAAS)), I began reading out the message, saying it was true. He (the Prophet) then took hold of my hand and the message and asked, 'Do you hate 'Ali?' 'Yes,' I replied. 'Don't hate him,' he asked. 'Whatever love you have for him, make it stronger. I swear by Him Who holds the soul of Mu\;1ammad in His hand, the share of the khums due the family of 'Ali is better than one black slave-girl.' '" "And following what the Messenger of God (SAAS) had said to me, there was no one I loved more than I did 'Ali." ''''Abd Allah b. Burayda went on, "And I swear by the One and Only God, that the only narrator between myself and the Messenger of God (SAAS) was my father Burayda.'"
lessons: Hatred born from personal bias or envy can blind a person to the virtues of others, and it requires honest self-examination to overcome it.
The Prophet's gentle but firm correction of Burayda shows that transforming negative emotions is possible when guided by wisdom and sincerity.
True love and brotherhood in faith are not merely feelings but choices that can be consciously cultivated through obedience to prophetic guidance.
practical_advice: When you find yourself harboring strong dislike toward someone, pause and honestly examine whether your feelings are based on facts or on the influence of others around you.
Seek out a trusted spiritual mentor or counselor who can help you reframe negative emotions and redirect them toward love and fairness.
Practice consciously recalling the good qualities of those you dislike; repeated reflection on others' virtues can gradually soften hardened feelings.

title: Amr ibn Sha's Harms the Prophet by Complaining About Ali
summary: Amr ibn Sha's complained publicly about Ali's conduct during a Yemen expedition. The Prophet confronted him in the mosque, informing him that harming Ali is equivalent to harming the Prophet himself.
story: Muᴥammad b. Isᴥaq stated, "Aban b. Saliᴥ related to us, from 'Abd Allah b. Niyar aI-Aslami, from his uncle 'Amr b. Sha's al-Aslami, who had been present at aI-Budaybiyya, who said, 'I was with 'Ali b. AbiiTalib in the cavalry party the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent out to Yemen. 'Ali was somewhat rude to me, and I resented his behaviour. 'When I returned to Medina, I complained about him in various meetings and to people I met. One day when I went to the mosque, I found the Messenger of God (SAAS) sitting there. When he saw me look into his eyes, he stared straight back at me until I sat down near him. He then said, "I swear, 'Amr b. Sha's, you have done me harm!" '" "But we all belong to God and to Him do we return!" I exclaimed. "I take refuge in God and in Islam against ever harming the Messenger of God (SAAS)!' He commented, 'Whoever harms ʿAlI harms me!'"
lessons: Spreading complaints about others in public gatherings can cause harm far beyond what we intend, affecting the reputation and unity of the community.
The Prophet's interconnectedness with those he loved teaches us that how we treat others reflects on our relationship with those who care about them.
Feelings of resentment, if left unaddressed privately, tend to grow and spill into harmful public behavior that damages communal bonds.
practical_advice: When you feel wronged by someone, seek to address the grievance directly and privately rather than venting to others, which rarely resolves anything and often escalates conflict.
Before speaking negatively about someone in public, ask yourself whether this speech serves justice or merely vents frustration, and choose silence or constructive dialogue instead.
Reflect on how your words about others might affect people who love or are responsible for them, and let that awareness guide you toward more measured and compassionate speech.

title: Ali Leads the Mission to Yemen and Banu Hamdan Accept Islam
summary: After Khalid ibn al-Walid spent six months in Yemen without success, the Prophet sent Ali ibn Abi Talib in his place. Ali's mission resulted in the entire tribe of Banu Hamdan embracing Islam, and upon hearing the news the Prophet prostrated in gratitude and prayed peace upon them.
story: The ḥafiẓ aI-Bayhaqi stated that Muᴥammad b. 'Abd Allah al-Baft? informed him, quoting Abii Isᴥaq al-Muzakki, relating from 'Vbayda b. Abii al-Safr who said, "I heard Ibrahim b. Yilsuf b. Abil Isl}aq state, from his father, from Abil Isl}aq, from ai-Bar:!' (who said) that, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched Khalid b. al-Walid to invite the people of Yemen into Islam.'" AI-Bara' went on, "I was among those who accompanied KhaIid b. al-Walid. We stayed there six months inviting them into Islam, but they did not respond well to him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then sent 'Ali b. Abil TaIib with orders to replace Khalid; those with KhaIid who wanted to stay there with 'Ali could do so. "I was one of those who stayed with 'Ali. When we approached the people there, they came out to us. We advanced and 'Ali led us in prayer. He later positioned us in one line and went out ahead of us and read to them the message from the Messenger of God (SAAS). At that all (Banil) Hamdan accepted Islam. 'Ali then wrote a message to the Messenger of God (SAAS), teIling of their acceptance of Islam. When he read the message, he prostrated in prayer, then raised his head and said, 'Peace be upon (Banil) Hamdan! Peace be upon Hamdan!'"
lessons: Persistence and the right approach matter enormously; what one person cannot achieve, another with a different style and divine facilitation may accomplish.
Gratitude expressed physically and immediately, as the Prophet demonstrated by prostrating upon hearing good news, deepens our connection to God and the blessings He grants.
Success in calling others toward good is a collective effort requiring patience, strategy, and trust that God will open hearts in His own time.
practical_advice: When your efforts in a worthy cause seem to yield no results, do not despair; consider changing your approach or seeking support from others whose skills may complement yours.
Cultivate the habit of expressing immediate gratitude to God when you receive good news, whether through prayer, prostration, or words of thanks, to anchor your joy in its true source.

Celebrate the achievements of your community sincerely and vocally, as the Prophet's repeated blessing of Banu Hamdan shows that affirming others' acceptance and growth strengthens unity and belonging.

title: Abu Sa'id al-Khudri Complains About Ali's Harsh Treatment During the Yemen Expedition
summary: Abu Sa'id al-Khudri accompanied Ali on the Yemen expedition and felt mistreated. He went to the Prophet intending to complain, but the Prophet firmly rebuked him mid-complaint, reminding him of Ali's toughness for God's sake and causing Abu Sa'id to vow never to speak ill of Ali again.
story: Al-Bayhaqi stated that he was informed by Abil al-I;:lusayn Mul}ammad b. al-Faql al-Q<tnan, quoting Abil saW b. Ziyad al-Q<tnan, quoting Isma'il b. Abil Uways, who quoted his brother, from Sulayman b. Bilal, from Sa'd b. Isl}aq b. Ka'b b. 'Vjra, from his aunt Zaynab, daughter of Ka'b b. 'Vjra, from Abil Sa'id al-Khudri, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched 'Ali b. Abil Talib to Yemen. "I was one of those who accompanied him. When he had taken some camels as ẓadaqa, 'charitable payments', we asked him if we could ride them and rest our own, which we could see were tiring. He refused us, saying, 'You have only a share in them, just like all Muslims.' "When .'Ali had finished his mission and left Yemen to return home, he appointed a certain person in command of us. He himself then hurried off and joined in the pilgrimage. When he had completed his pilgrimage, the Prophet (SAAS) told him, 'Go back and rejoin your men.' "In the meantime we had made to the commander 'Ali had appointed over us the same request he had denied, and this man had agreed. When 'Ali learned that the camels taken as ẓadaqa payment had been ridden, and saw how this had tired them, he surmuoned his deputy and berated him. "(I told myself) 'I swear by God, when I reach Medina, I'll certainly make a report to him (the Prophet (SAAS)), and tell him how rudely and harshly we have been treated! After our arrival in Medina, I went to him early one morning intending to fulfil my oath. I met Abu Bakr coming out from having been with the Messenger of God (SAAS), and when he saw me he stopped and welcomed me, asking me various questions, as I did him. He asked, 'When did you arrive?' 'I got here yesterday,' I replied. He then went back with me to the Messenger of God (SAAS). He went inside and said, 'Sa'd b. Malik b. al-Shahld is here.' 'Invite him in,' he told him. "So I entered and greeted the Messenger of God (SAAS), as he did me. He came over to me and asked me various questions about myself and my family, and did so very kindly. I told him, 'Messenger of God, we suffered greatly from the ill-conduct and harsh treatment of 'All.' "He hesitated and I began enumerating the problems we had had with him and right in the middle of my comments he slapped me on the thigh; I was right next to him. He said, 'Sa'd b. Malik b. a1-Shahld, tone down what you are saying about your brother 'All! I swear by God, I well know how tough he is in God's cause!' I told myself, 'Sa'd b. Malik, your mother is as good as bereaved of you! I'm sure to be in his bad books, no matter what! And I'll surely never make a negative reference to him again, confidentially or openly!'"
lessons: A leader's strict enforcement of rules, though uncomfortable in the moment, reflects integrity and fairness that protects the rights of the broader community over individual convenience.
The Prophet's reception of Abu Sa'id with warmth and kindness before his firm correction models how to address complaints with compassion without abandoning truth.
When we feel wronged, the urgency to complain can override our better judgment; pausing before acting on that urgency often reveals that our perspective is incomplete.
practical_advice: Before lodging a formal complaint about someone's leadership or behavior, take time to cool down and consider whether their actions might have been principled rather than personal.
When you receive feedback that cuts short your grievance, resist the urge to feel dismissed; instead reflect on whether the feedback reveals a blind spot in your own perspective.
Practice the discipline of not speaking negatively about others in private or public, as Abu Sa'id's vow illustrates; such a commitment protects relationships and your own integrity.

title: Ali's Deputy Dresses the Army in Ceremonial Robes Before Meeting the Prophet
summary: During the Yemen expedition, Ali's deputy dressed the soldiers in ceremonial robes without authorization. When Ali returned and found them wearing the robes, he took them away, leading the soldiers to complain to the Prophet upon their arrival.
story: Yunus quoted from Mu!,lammad b. Is!,laq as follows, "Yaᴥya b. 'Abd Allah b. Abu 'Umar related to me, from Yazld b. TaI!,la b. Yazld b. Rukana, who said, 'The army who were with 'All in Yemen became upset that, having made their advance, he then put a deputy in command of them and hurried away to join the Messenger of God (SAAS). "This deputy went ahead and dressed every man in a ceremonial robe. When they were on their way to him, 'All came out to receive them and found them wearing their robes. He asked, 'What's all this then?' They replied, 'So-and-so dressed us in them.' He asked, 'What made you do this before coming to the Messenger of God (SAAS), instead of letting him do as he wished?' And he took away their robes. "When they reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), they complained to him about that. These men had previously made a pact of peace with the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he had only sent 'All to collect thejizya, 'poll-tax', imposed upon them."
lessons: Acting outside the boundaries of one's authority, even with good intentions, can create conflict and undermine trust within a community or organization.
Ali's insistence on deferring honors to the Prophet illustrates the Islamic principle of placing proper authority and order above personal generosity or gesture.
Soldiers and followers who feel slighted by leadership decisions should bring their grievances through proper channels rather than amplifying discontent among peers.
practical_advice: When in a position of delegated authority, be careful not to make decisions that exceed your mandate, no matter how well-intentioned, as overreach can cause confusion and resentment.
When you feel that a leader has treated you unfairly, approach the appropriate authority calmly and factually rather than mobilizing others' emotions around your grievance.
Reflect on whether your desire to honor or please others is aligned with proper guidelines and the wishes of those in authority, and adjust accordingly before acting.

title: The Prophet Defends Ali at Ghadir Khumm After the Yemen Expedition Controversy
summary: Following widespread complaints about Ali's conduct during the Yemen expedition, the Prophet stopped at the stream of Khumm on the return journey, addressed the people, and publicly acquitted Ali of wrongdoing while emphasizing his virtues and importance.
story: What is implied here is that when there arose the controversy about that army, due to 'AlI's having prevented them putting the ẓadaqa camels to work and then his having withdrawn from them the permission granted by his deputy. Even though 'Ali could be excused for his actions, talk arose among the pilgrims about him. Therefore, though God knows best, the Messenger of God (SAAS), having completed the pilgrimage and its associated ceremonials, returned to Medina. Passing the stream at Khumm, he arose among the people and made an address. He acquitted 'Ali of wrongdoing, emphasized his importance and his virtues to remove the negative impression of him held by many. A detailed account of this will follow, in its appropriate place, if God so wills it. And in Him is all trust.
lessons: A true leader does not allow the reputation of a just person to be tarnished by misunderstanding; he intervenes to set the record straight with clarity and authority.
Public misunderstandings about righteous individuals require public correction; private assurances are insufficient when public perception has been damaged.
Trust in God's plan and in those He has appointed means defending their honor even when popular opinion runs against them.
practical_advice: If someone you know has been publicly mischaracterized, consider whether you are in a position to offer a clear and public defense of their character and intentions.
When controversy arises around a person you lead or care for, address it directly and openly rather than hoping it will resolve itself with time.
Build your personal judgments of others on verified information and direct knowledge rather than on the accumulated complaints of those who may have partial or biased perspectives.

title: The Prophet Distributes Gold Sent by Ali and Is Questioned by His Companions
summary: Ali sent a chunk of unrefined gold from Yemen and the Prophet distributed it among four individuals, prompting one of his men to question whether the companions had greater right to it. The Prophet rebuked the questioner and affirmed his trustworthiness as the one entrusted by God in heaven.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Qutayba related to him, quoting 'Abd al-WalᴥId, from 'Umara b. al-Q;t'qa' b. Shabrama, who quoted 'Abd al-RaI:>man b. Abu Nu'm as having said that he heard Abu Sa'ld al-Khudrl say, "'Ali b. Abu Talib sent from Yemen to the Prophet (SAAS), in a leather bag a chunk of gold not yet refined from its ore. "He (the Prophet (SAAS)), divided it up between four persons: 'Uyayna b. Badr, al-Aqra' b. I:Iabis and Zayd al-Khayl. The fourth was either 'A1qama b. 'Ulatha or 'Amir b. al-Tufayl. "One of his men said, 'But we had more right to it than they!' That remark reached the Prophet (SAAS), and he said, 'Don't you trust me! Even though I am the one trusted by the One in heaven, who delivers me news of heaven, morning and evening?' "Thereupon a man arose who had sunken eyes, prominent cheekbones, a...
lessons: Questioning the wisdom of those in authority without knowledge or context can reflect a lack of trust that undermines community cohesion and spiritual growth.

The Prophet's strategic distribution of wealth to newly inclined hearts demonstrates that leadership sometimes requires difficult decisions that prioritize long-term community benefit over short-term fairness to insiders.
Trust in prophetic and divinely guided leadership is foundational; when we do not understand a decision, humility requires we assume wisdom before assuming error.
practical_advice: Before questioning a leader's decisions, ask yourself whether you have full knowledge of their reasoning and the broader context, and practice patience before voicing dissent.
Reflect on moments when you have felt overlooked or passed over; consider whether the decision-maker may have had legitimate reasons that your perspective did not capture.
Cultivate trust in those who have consistently demonstrated integrity and divine guidance, and resist the impulse to measure every decision against your personal expectations of fairness.

title: The Distribution of Gold and the Man Who Challenged the Prophet
summary: The Prophet distributed unrefined gold among four tribal leaders, prompting complaint from his companions. When a man arose and told the Prophet to 'fear God,' Khalid ibn al-Walid asked to behead him, but the Prophet refused, and then warned of a deviant group that would emerge from this man's progeny.
story: He (the Prophet (SAAS)), divided it up between four persons: 'Uyayna b. Badr, al-Aqra' b. Habis and Zayd al-Khayl. The fourth was either 'Alqama b. 'Ulatha or 'Amir b. al-Tufayl. "One of his men said, 'But we had more right to it than they!' That remark reached the Prophet (SAAS), and he said, 'Don't you trust me! Even though I am the one trusted by the One in heaven, who delivers me news of heaven, morning and evening?' "Thereupon a man arose who had sunken eyes, prominent cheekbones, a high forehead, thick beard, shaved head and who was wearing his waist-wrapper tucked up. He said, 'Messenger of God, fear God!' He responded, 'Confound you, am I not the most God-fearing man of all?' The man then walked away. "Khalid b. al-Walid then asked, 'Messenger of God, should I not behead him?' 'No,' he replied, 'he may well be one who prays.' 'Ali commented, 'But there are many who pray, yet speak with their tongues what is not in their hearts.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, 'I have not been commanded to search the hearts of people, nor to cut open their stomachs.' "He stared after the man as he walked away, and said, 'From the progeny of this man there will go out people who will recite the Book of God eloquently, but it will not pass beyond their throats. They will exit the faith like an arrow exiting from its target.' I think he also said, 'If I were alive at their time, I would execute them just as Thamud were killed.'"
lessons: Trusting in divinely guided leadership requires humility, even when we feel we deserve more than we receive.
Judging others by outward appearance or behavior alone is insufficient; only God knows what is in the hearts of people.
Piety that remains on the tongue and does not penetrate the heart is a form of spiritual danger that leaders must warn against.
practical_advice: When you feel overlooked or passed over unfairly, pause before reacting and reflect on whether you have full knowledge of the wisdom behind the decision.
Avoid judging the sincerity of others' faith; focus instead on cultivating your own inner integrity and consistency between word and deed.
If someone in authority makes a decision you disagree with, raise your concern respectfully through proper channels rather than with hostility or public challenge.

title: Ali's Appointment to Yemen and God's Promise to Guide Him
summary: The young Ali ibn Abi Talib expressed his worry about being sent to Yemen to judge disputes, feeling too inexperienced. The Prophet prayed for him, and Ali reported never again doubting any case he judged.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS), dispatched me to Yemen while I was still young. "I said, 'You are dispatching me to a people among whom there will be cases (to decide), but I have no knowledge of making judgement.' He replied, 'God will guide your tongue and make your heart firm.' "And I never had any doubts about judging between any two." Imam Ahmad stated that Aswad b. 'Amir related to him, quoting Sharik, from Simak, from Hanash, from 'Ali, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched me to Yemen. I asked, 'Messenger of God, are you sending me out to people older than myself; I'm still young, and I have no knowledge of reaching judgement.' "He then placed his hand on my chest and said, 'O God, make his tongue firm and guide his heart. 'Ali, if you should have two rivals sit down with you, do not judge between them until you hear from the other what you heard from the first; if you act this way, it will be clear to you.' "And since then I never had doubts about any case."
lessons: When God places us in a role that feels beyond our capacity, He also provides the guidance and strength needed to fulfill it.
Acknowledging your limitations honestly to a mentor or leader is a sign of wisdom, not weakness.
Fairness in judgment requires hearing all sides before reaching a conclusion — this is a timeless principle of justice.
practical_advice: When facing a responsibility that feels overwhelming, ask God sincerely for clarity and firmness, then take the first step with trust rather than waiting until you feel fully ready.
Before making any important decision involving others, make sure you have heard every party's perspective completely before forming your view.
If you feel too young or inexperienced for a task you've been given, seek guidance from those wiser than you and rely on that mentorship rather than declining the opportunity.

title: Ali's Judgement on the Disputed Child in Yemen
summary: While serving as judge in Yemen, Ali resolved a complex dispute in which three men each claimed fatherhood of a child by casting lots and awarding blood-money accordingly. When reported to the Prophet, he laughed and affirmed Ali's ruling.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Sufyan b. 'Uyayna related to him, from al-Ajlah, from al-Sha'bi, from 'Abd Allah b. Abu al-Khalil, from Zayd b. Arqam, who said that several people had intercourse with a woman in the same period. 'Ali asked two of them, "Do you find this appropriate?" "No," they replied. 'Ali then approached the others and asked, "Do you find this appropriate?" They both replied, "No." He then said, "You are quarrelsome partners." He went on, "I will cast lots between you. Whichever of you has his opponent win will receive two-thirds of the blood-wit, and I will award the child to him." "This decision was reported to the Prophet (SAAS), who said, 'I know no more than what 'Ali said.'" Ahmad also stated that Shurayh b. al-Nu'man related to him, quoting Hushaym, quoting al-Ajlah, from al-Sha'bi, from Abu al-Khalil, from Zayd b. Arqam (who said) that before 'Ali, while he was in Yemen, three men were brought who had shared (in the conception of) a child. He drew lots between them and awarded to the one to whom the arrow pointed two-thirds of the diyya, and awarded the child to him. Zayd b. Arqam stated that he went to the Prophet (SAAS), and informed him of 'Ali's decision, and that he laughed so loudly that his molars were visible.
lessons: When established precedents do not cover a new situation, a principled and fair-minded approach guided by wisdom can reach sound conclusions.
Divine guidance can work through human reasoning when the person reasoning is sincere and God-conscious.
Humility in leadership means being willing to affirm a subordinate's good judgment rather than insisting on having the final word.
practical_advice: When faced with a complex problem with no clear precedent, don't be paralyzed — use sound principles you already know and trust God to guide you to a fair outcome.
Be willing to acknowledge when someone else, even someone younger or less experienced, has made the right call.
Approach disputes involving shared responsibility by first asking all parties whether they themselves find their position fair — this often opens the door to resolution.

title: Ali's Judgement on the Four Men Who Fell Into the Lion Pit
summary: During his mission in Yemen, Ali judged a case in which four men fell sequentially into a lion trap and all died of their wounds. He assigned graduated blood-money amounts to each victim's heirs to prevent a tribal war, and the Prophet affirmed his ruling.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Abu Sa'id related to him, quoting Isra'il, quoting Simak, from Hanash, from 'Ali, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent me to Yemen and we went out to a group of people who had dug a pit to trap lions. While they were actively engaged in doing this, one man fell in and grabbed hold of another man. The second took hold of another, and so on until there were four men in the pit. The lion wounded them all. Then a man rushed at it with a lance and killed it. But all the wounded died from their injuries. "The heirs of the first man approached those of the second, who took up arms to do battle with them. 'Ali arrived thereafter and asked, 'Do you really want to fight one another even while the Messenger of God (SAAS) is still alive! I will judge between you. If you accept my decision, then so be it. Otherwise, keep away from one another until you go to the Prophet (SAAS), and then he can be the one to judge between you. Anyone who commits aggression after that will have no rights. Gather from the tribes who had dug the pit camels equivalent to one-quarter, one-third, one-half and one whole diyya. To the first victim he awarded one-quarter of a diyya, since he had died, one-third of a diyya to the second, one-half of a diyya to the third and a full diyya to the fourth. "They refused to accept this judgement and went to the Prophet (SAAS), while he was at the maqam Ibrahim, 'Abraham's station' at the ka'ba, and told him the story. He said, 'I will judge between you.' One man then said, 'Messenger of God, 'Ali has already reached judgement for us.' They recounted this to him and the Messenger of God (SAAS) accepted it."
lessons: Preventing further harm and bloodshed is itself an act of justice — sometimes the most important intervention is stopping a conflict from escalating before a perfect solution is found.
A just leader does not shy away from difficult cases simply because the situation is unprecedented or emotionally charged.
Even when people reject sound guidance out of pride or dissatisfaction, they may eventually find that the original judgment was right all along.
practical_advice: When conflict between groups is about to boil over, prioritize de-escalation first — urge parties to pause, accept mediation, and commit to peaceful resolution before engaging further.
Don't refuse to make a decision simply because it is complex or because you fear rejection; do your best with the knowledge and principles available to you.
If others reject your well-reasoned advice, remain calm and encourage them to seek a second opinion rather than becoming defensive — the truth often vindicates itself.

title: The Farewell Pilgrimage: Introduction and Names
summary: The text introduces the Farewell Pilgrimage of the Prophet in 10 AH, explaining the several names given to it and its unique significance as the only full pilgrimage he performed from Madinah, during which the religion of Islam was declared complete by God.
story: BOOK ON THE HIJJAT AL-WADA', 'THE FAREWELL PILGRIMAGE', IN 10 AH. It is also known as the hijjat al-balagh, "the message pilgrimage". It is known as "the farewell pilgrimage" because during it the Prophet (SAAS), said goodbye to the people and performed no pilgrimage after it. It is also named hijjat al-Islam, "the Islam pilgrimage", because the Prophet (SAAS) left on no other occasion from Medina to make the pilgrimage, though he had performed pilgrimages a number of times before the prophethood and thereafter. It is said that the obligation to make the pilgrimage was revealed that year; it is also said that this was in 9 AH, or in 6 AH, or even before the Hijra, though this last is unlikely. It is called hijjat al-balagh, "the message pilgrimage" because in it the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave, both in word and deed, the message to the people concerning God's laws for the pilgrimage. All the bases and fundamentals of Islam, without exception, were made clear by the Prophet (SAAS). When he explained and laid out for them the prescribed law for the pilgrimage, God revealed to him as he stood there at Mt. 'Ararat, "Today I have perfected your faith for you, fulfilled my grace upon you and favoured you with Islam as a religion" (surat al-Ma'ida; V, v.3). Clarification of all this will follow. The purpose here is to relate the manner of the pilgrimage of the Messenger of God (SAAS). The early transmitters differ considerably in this, depending upon the information they each received; this is especially true of those sources who lived after the Companions of the Prophet, may God be pleased with them. We, thanks be to God and through His help and the success He grants, will relate those accounts given by the Imams in their works; we will co-ordinate them in such a manner as to please and instruct those who contemplate them, while interpreting the various accounts, if God so wills it, and in Him is all trust and reliance.
lessons: The completion of a great mission is marked not just by outward achievement but by divine acknowledgment — when God said 'Today I have perfected your faith,' it was a moment of profound closure and fulfillment.
Even the greatest of missions must eventually come to an end; what matters most is whether you fulfilled your responsibility completely before departing.
The preservation and transmission of knowledge, including religious practice, requires care, coordination, and humility in reconciling different accounts.
practical_advice: When you are approaching a major ending — a farewell, a retirement, a final chapter — take time to pass on what you have learned with the same care the Prophet showed in his final pilgrimage.
Reflect on the milestones in your own life where you felt a sense of completion or divine favor; let those memories strengthen your faith during difficult times.
When studying or researching complex events with multiple accounts, approach discrepancies with patience and a commitment to synthesis rather than confusion or dismissal.

title: The Prophet's Umra Pilgrimages and the One Full Pilgrimage from Madinah
summary: The Prophet performed four lesser pilgrimages (umra), all in the month of Dhu al-Qi'da except the one combined with his major pilgrimage, and made only one full pilgrimage from Madinah — the Farewell Pilgrimage.
story: Chapter: Information on the fact that the Prophet (SAAS), made only one full pilgrimage from Medina, having previously departed for three 'Umra, 'lesser', pilgrimages. This is as both al-Bukhari and Muslim related from Hudba, from Hammam, from Qatada, from Anas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) made four 'umra pilgrimages, all in Dhu al-Qi'da, except for the one that was during his performance of his (major) pilgrimage." Yunus b. Bukayr related this from 'Umar b. Dharr, from Mujahid, from Abu Hurayra to the same.
lessons: Quality of devotion matters more than quantity; the Prophet performed one farewell pilgrimage, yet it became the definitive model for all Muslims for all time.
Consistency in the timing and manner of worship reflects a disciplined, intentional spiritual life.
Every act of worship done sincerely leaves a legacy far greater than the moment itself.
practical_advice: Rather than focusing on how many religious acts you perform, focus on the quality, presence, and sincerity you bring to each one.
Consider establishing regular rhythms in your own spiritual practice — consistency in timing and form builds depth over time.
When you have the opportunity to learn from those who have deep experience in worship or practice, observe not just what they do but when and how they do it.

title: The Prophet's Four Umra Pilgrimages
summary: A scholarly account of the four Umra pilgrimages performed by Prophet Muhammad, including the Hudaybiyya Umra, Umrat al-Qada, the Umra from al-Ji'rana, and the Umra performed alongside his major Hajj.
story: We will clarify these in the appropriate places. And in God help is to be sought. Chapter: Information on the fact that the Prophet (SAAS), made only one full pilgrimage from Medina, having previously departed for three <Umra, 'lesser', pilgrimages. This is as both al-Bukharl and Muslim related from Hudba, from Hammam, from Qittada, from Anas, who said, "The Messenger of God ($AAS) made four <umra pilgrimages, all in Dhu al-Qit<da, except for the one that was during his performance of his (major) pilgrimage." Yunus b. Bukayr related this from 'Vmar b. Dharr, from Mujahid, from Abu Hurayra to the same effect. Sa'd b. Man~ur stated, from al-Daraward!, from Hisham b. 'Vrwa, from his father, from 'Ji.'isha, who said, "The Messenger of God ($AAS) made three 'umra pilgrimages: one in shawwal and three in Dhu al-qa'da." Ibn Bukayr related similarly from Malik, from Hisham b. 'Vrwa. Imam Al,unad narrated from a badith of <Amr b. Shu'ayb, from his father, from his grandfather, that the Messenger of God ($AAS) made three 'umra pilgrimages, all in Dhu al-qa'da. Al).mad stated that Abu al-Nagr related to him, quoting Da'ud - meaning al-'Arrar - from 'Amr, from qkrima, from Ibn <Abbas, who said, "The Messenger of God ($AAS) made four 'umra pilgrimages: that of al-I:Iudaybiyya, the 'umrat al-qaqa', that from al-Ji<rana, and that coincident with his (major) pilgrimage." Abu Da'ud, al-Tirmidh! and ai-Nasa'! related this from a badith of Da'ud al-<Anar, and al-Tirmidh! characterized it as basan, "good". This issue is examined above relative to the 'umra from al-Ji'rana. It will also be examined in the section on those who stated that the Prophet ($AAS) made the qiran pilgrimage simultaneously. And help is to be sought from God. The first 'umra was that of al-I:Iudaybiyya from which he was blocked. Thereafter came the 'umrat al-qaqa', which others name the 'umrat al-qi~as, "the retaliation pilgrimage", and still others the <umrat al-qaqiyya, "the cause pilgrimage". Thereafter came the 'umra from al-Ji<rana, on his return from al-Ta'if, when he divided up the spoils from the batde of I:Iunayn. We have examined all this in the appropriate places above. The fourth was the 'umra he performed along with his major pilgrimage.
lessons: Persistence in worship is a mark of sincere devotion — the Prophet performed Umra multiple times, each journey reflecting renewed commitment to God.
Even when blocked or prevented from worship, as at Hudaybiyya, a believer finds another time and another way; obstacles do not end the journey.
Scholarly precision in preserving religious knowledge honors the traditions of faith and ensures future generations can learn from authentic sources.
practical_advice: When a spiritual goal is interrupted or blocked, note it, grieve it briefly, and then plan your return — the Prophet returned to complete what was prevented at Hudaybiyya.
Seek multiple reliable sources when learning about your faith; the richness of Islamic scholarship is a gift, not a burden.
Incorporate regular acts of worship — even small, repeated ones — into your routine as an anchor for emotional stability and closeness to God.

title: The Prophet Remains Nine Years in Medina Before Performing Hajj
summary: After nine years in Medina without performing the major pilgrimage, the Prophet announced his intention to make Hajj, drawing large numbers of people who gathered to accompany him.
story: In the 1;adith of Ja'far b. Mul;tammad b. 'Ali b. al-I:Iusayn, from his father, Jabir b. 'Abd Allah is quoted as having said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained in Medina for nine years without making a pilgrimage. He then made an announcement to the people that there would be a pilgrimage. A large number of people gathered in Medina, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth four or five days before the end of Dhu al-Q;t'da. When he was at Dhu al-I:Iulayfa he perfonned the prayers. He then mounted his camel and it stood up with him. When it moved off with him into the desert, he called out labbayka! 'I am at Your service!' At this we recited the al-talbiyya, thinking only of the pilgrimage."
lessons: Great acts of worship, long deferred, carry profound meaning when finally fulfilled — the Prophet's Hajj after nine years moved the entire community.
Announcing good intentions publicly can inspire collective participation and strengthen communal bonds.
The moment of full surrender — the Prophet calling out 'I am at Your service!' — reminds us that spiritual readiness comes from within, not merely from the passage of time.
practical_advice: If you have long deferred an important spiritual commitment, take one concrete step toward it today — announce it, plan it, or simply make sincere intention.
When you feel spiritually distant or that you have delayed too long, remember that God's door of return is always open and your sincere call will be answered.
Join with others in acts of worship and community; the Prophet's announcement drew multitudes, showing the power of shared spiritual purpose to ease anxiety and build resolve.

title: The Prophet's Departure from Medina: Day and Date of Setting Out
summary: Scholars debate whether the Prophet departed Medina on a Saturday, Thursday, or Friday for the Farewell Pilgrimage, with hadith evidence analyzed to determine the correct date.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that it was related to him by Ibrahim b. al-Mundhir, quoting Anas b. qyad, from 'Ubayd Allah - he being Ibn 'Umar - from Nafi', from 'Abd Allah b. COmar (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would leave down Shajara Street and enter by al-Mu'arras Street. And that whenever he left for Mecca, he would pray at the al-Shajara mosque and on his return he would pray at Dhu al-l::Iulayfa, in the centre of the valley, where he would spend the night until morning.
lessons: Scholarship and careful analysis help preserve the integrity of religious history, showing that truth matters even in small details.
The precision of early Muslim scholars in recording and verifying events reflects the deep reverence they had for the Prophet's example.
Even complex disagreements can be resolved through patient reasoning and return to authentic sources.
practical_advice: When faced with confusion or conflicting information, pause and gather evidence before drawing conclusions.
Practice intellectual humility by acknowledging what you do not know and seeking guidance from reliable sources.
Trust that dedicated effort to find truth, even in difficult matters, is itself a form of worship and sincerity.

title: The Prophet Sets Out for Hajj on a Worn Saddle
summary: The Prophet Muhammad departed for the Farewell Pilgrimage mounted on a tattered saddle, praying that his pilgrimage be free of eye service and reputation-seeking.
story: The /:taft? Abu Bakr al-Bazzar stated, 'I found in my book the statement from 'Amr b. Malik, from YazId b. Zuray', from Hisham, from 'Azra b. Thabit, from Thumama, from Anas, that the Prophet (SAAS) set off on the pilgrimage mounted on a tattered saddle beneath which was a piece of cloth. And he said, "(It is to be) a pilgrimage devoid of eye service or reputation-seeking."' AI-Bukharl commented upon this in his ,a/:ti/:t collection stating, 'And Mu1.lammad b. Abu Bakr al-MuqaddamI stated that it was related to him by YazId b. Zuray', from 'Azra b. Thabit, from Thumama, who said, "Anas rode away on the pilgrimage on a decrepit old saddle, and he was not stingy. He related that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had departed for the pilgrimage on a mount that had been his baggage transport."' The /:taft? Abu Yaqa al-Maw~ilI, in his musnad gave it from a different line - from Anas b. Malik. He stated, "'AlI b. al-Ja'd related to us, quoting al-RabI' b. SabI1.l, from YazId al-RaqashI, from Anas, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) went on the pilgrimage on a worn-out saddle and cloth worth' - or 'not worth' - 'four dirhams. And he said, "0 God, let it be a pilgrimage devoid of eye service."'"
lessons: True devotion is marked by sincerity and humility, not by outward show or the desire for others' admiration.
The Prophet's simple mount during the most important pilgrimage of his life teaches that spiritual acts are measured by intention, not appearance.
Seeking God's acceptance rather than people's praise is the foundation of a meaningful spiritual life.
practical_advice: Before any act of worship or good deed, pause and check your intention — are you doing it for God or for others' approval?
Deliberately choose simplicity over show in your acts of worship to protect the sincerity of your heart.
Regularly remind yourself that God sees what people cannot, and let that awareness motivate your private and public conduct.

title: Ibn Umar Compares a Yemeni Group to the Prophet's Companions on the Farewell Pilgrimage
summary: Ibn Umar, upon seeing a humble Yemeni caravan, remarked that they resembled the Prophet and his Companions as they set out for the Farewell Pilgrimage.
story: Imam A!.lmad stated that Hashim related to him, quoting Is1.laq b. Sa'Id, from his father, who said, 'I set out with Ibn Vmar and a Yemeni group passed by us. Their saddles were of leather and the muzzles of their camels were made of woven grass. 'Abd Allah commented, "Anyone wanting to see a group similar to that going with the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his Companions on their way to the /:tijjal al-wada' should look at that party."'
lessons: The simplicity and sincerity of the early Muslim community is a living standard that can be recognized and appreciated in every generation.
Longing for the example of the Prophet and his Companions is a sign of love and attachment to the roots of the faith.
Humility in outward appearance often reflects the richness of inner devotion.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually disconnected, reflect on the lives of the Prophet and his Companions to reconnect with the essence of your faith.
Seek out communities and people who embody sincere, unpretentious devotion as a source of inspiration.
Do not measure your spiritual worth by material displays; instead, focus on the quality of your heart and intentions.

title: Asma Bint Abi Bakr Witnesses Abu Bakr's Frustration Over a Lost Camel During Hajj
summary: During the journey to the Farewell Pilgrimage, Abu Bakr's slave lost their only baggage camel, and the Prophet smiled at Abu Bakr's flustered reaction despite being in the state of ihram.
story: Imam A!).mad stated, "'Abd Allah b. Idris related to us, quoting Ibn Is!).aq, from Ya!).ya b. 'Abbad b. 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr, from his father, (who said) that Asma', daughter of Abu Bakr said, 'We set out with the Prophet (SAAS) to make the pilgrimage. When we reached aI-'Arj, the Messenger of God (SAAS) dismounted. 'A'isha sat down beside him, and I sat next to my father. The baggage camel of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and that of Abu Bakr were one and the same, being conducted by a slave of Abu Bakr. Abu Bakr sat waiting for the latter to appear with it. Eventually, he did appear, but he had no baggage camel with him. Abu Bakr asked, "Where's your camel?" He replied, "I lost it yesterday." Abu Bakr commented, "Just one baggage camel and you lost it!" And he began striking him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) smiled and said, "Just look at that mu[lrim pilgrim and how he behaves!"'"
lessons: Even the most devoted and righteous people experience moments of frustration and human weakness, and acknowledging this with gentle humor is a mark of wisdom.
The Prophet's compassionate smile, rather than harsh criticism, teaches that responding to others' mistakes with mercy is more powerful than condemnation.
Stressful situations during worship reveal areas of personal growth and remind us to strive for patience even when circumstances are difficult.
practical_advice: When stress or frustration causes you to react sharply, pause and recall that even great companions of the Prophet struggled with patience in difficult moments.
Respond to the mistakes of others — especially those under your care — with measured correction rather than explosive anger.
Use moments of unexpected disruption or loss as opportunities to practice patience and trust that God is in control of outcomes.

title: The Prophet's Preparation for Ihram at Dhu al-Hulayfa
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) prepared for the farewell pilgrimage by visiting his wives, performing the zuhr prayer, applying perfume, braiding his hair, and adopting the state of ihram at Dhu al-Hulayfa before reciting the talbiyya.
story: What is implied here is that the Prophet (SAAS) was ordered to remain in the al-'Aqiq valley until the al-zuhr prayer; he obeyed this and did remain there. That morning he visited each of his wives, nine in number. They had all set out with him. He did remain there until he had performed the al-zuhr prayer. This will be shown in the hadith of Abu Hassan al-A'raj, from Ibn 'Abbas, (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the al-zuhr prayer at Dhu al-Hulayfa. He then dressed his sacrificial animal in ceremonial decorations, mounted up and recited the talbiyya. Imam Ahmad, similarly, stated, 'Rawh related to us, quoting Ash'ath - he being Ibn 'Abd al-Malik - from al-Hasan, from Anas b. Malik (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the al-zuhr prayer and then mounted his camel. When he was above al-Bayda', he recited the talbiyya.'
lessons: Following a structured, mindful routine before a major spiritual undertaking reflects the importance of preparation in worship.
Visiting and honoring one's family before a significant journey demonstrates the balance between devotion to God and care for loved ones.
Obeying divine instruction with patience and discipline, even in small details, is a mark of true faith.
practical_advice: Before embarking on any significant spiritual or life endeavor, take time to fulfill your obligations to family and loved ones first.
Establish a personal routine of preparation — spiritual, physical, and emotional — before major events to center yourself and reduce anxiety.
Practice patience by waiting for the right moment to act rather than rushing; trust that timing guided by faith leads to better outcomes.

title: Aisha Perfuming the Prophet Before and During Ihram
summary: Aisha (RA) described how she would personally perfume the Prophet (SAAS) both before he adopted the ihram state and before his circumambulation of the Ka'ba, and narrators recalled seeing the shine of musk in his hair parting even days into his ihram.
story: Muslim stated in his Sahih collection that Yahya b. Habib al-Harithi related to him, quoting Khalid - meaning Ibn al-Harith - quoting Shu'ba, from Ibrahim b. Muhammad b. al-Muntashir, who said that he heard his father quote 'A'isha as saying, 'I would perfume the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he would then make the rounds to his wives. Next morning, he would adopt the ihram state, sprinkling himself with perfume.' Al-Bukhari related this from a hadith of Shu'ba. Both al-Bukhari and Muslim gave it from a hadith of Abu 'Awana. In an account of Muslim, Ibrahim b. Muhammad b. al-Muntashir quotes his father as saying, 'I asked 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar about a man putting on perfume and adopting the ihram state. He replied, "I don't like to adopt the ihram state sprinkling myself with perfume; I would much rather anoint myself with aloes-wood perfume rather than do that!" 'A'isha commented, "But I put perfume on the Messenger of God (SAAS), when he adopted the ihram state. He would then make the rounds to his wives and after that would become muhrim."' This wording related by Muslim requires the Messenger of God (SAAS) to put on perfume before he went around to his wives, this being more pleasant for himself and something they liked. Then, after he had washed himself following janaba, 'sexual discharge', and preparatory to adopting the ihram state, he would put on another perfume for the ihram. This is how al-Tirmidhi and al-Bayhaqi related it, from a hadith of 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abu al-Zinnad, from his father, from Kharija b. Zayd b. Thabit, from his father, who said that he saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) disrobe to recite the talbiyya and wash himself. Al-Tirmidhi categorized this hadith as hasan gharib, 'good and unilateral'. Imam Ahmad stated that Zakariyya' b. 'Adi informed him, quoting 'Obayd Allah b. 'Amr, from 'Abd Allah b. Muhammad b. 'Aqil, from 'Orwa, from 'A'isha, who said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) wished to adopt the state of ihram, he would wash his head with marshmallow plant and potash and then lightly oil it.' Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad b. Idris al-Shafi'i, may God have mercy on him, stated that Sufyan b. 'Oyayna related to him, from 'Othman b. 'Orwa (who said that) he heard his father say, 'I heard 'A'isha say, "I perfumed the Messenger of God (SAAS), for both his ihram and ihlal states". I asked her, "What perfume did you use?" "The very best perfume," she replied.' Muslim related this, from a hadith of Sufyan b. 'Oyayna, and al-Bukhari quoted it from a hadith of Wahb, from Hisham b. 'Orwa, from his brother 'Othman, from his father 'Orwa, from 'A'isha. Al-Bukhari stated that 'Abd Allah b. Yusuf related to him, quoting Malik, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. al-Qasim, from his father, from 'A'isha, who said, 'I would perfume the Messenger of God (SAAS), for the ihram when he would adopt that state, as well as for the ihlal, prior to his circumambulating the ka'ba.' Muslim stated that 'Abd b. Humayd related to him, quoting Muhammad b. Abu Bakr, quoting Ibn Jurayj, who said that 'Umar b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Orwa related to him that he heard 'Orwa and al-Qasim, who both related that 'A'isha stated, 'I perfumed the Messenger of God (SAAS), with my own hands, using dharira on the hijjat al-wada' for both his states of ihlal and ihram.' Muslim also related, from a hadith of Sufyan b. 'Oyayna, from al-Zuhri, from 'Orwa, from 'A'isha, who said, 'I perfumed the Messenger of God (SAAS), with these two hands of mine both for his ihram when he adopted that and for his ihlal state before he circumambulated the ka'ba.' Muslim stated that Ahmad b. Mani' and Ya'qub al-Dawraqi both related to him, that Hushaym related to them, quoting Mansur, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. al-Qasim, from his father, from 'A'isha, who stated, 'I would put perfume on the Prophet (SAAS), before he would adopt the ihram or the ihlal state, as well as on the day of the slaughter of the sacrificial animals. I would use a perfume that had musk in it.' Muslim stated that both Abu Bakr b. Abu Shayba and Zuhayr b. Harb related to him, saying, 'Waki' related to us, quoting al-A'mash, from Abu al-Duha, from Masruq, from 'A'isha, who said, "It is as if I can still see the shine of the musk on the partings in the hair of the Messenger of God (SAAS), as he recited the talbiyya."' Then Muslim related it from a hadith of al-Thawri and others, from al-Hasan b. 'Ubayd Allah, from Ibrahim, from al-Aswad, from 'A'isha, who said, 'It is as if I can see now the shine of the musk in the hair parting of the Messenger of God (SAAS), while he was in the ihram state.' Al-Bukhari related it from a hadith of Sufyan al-Thawri and Muslim did so from a hadith of al-A'mash. Both of these gave it from Mansur, from Ibrahim, from al-Aswad, quoting 'A'isha. Both al-Bukhari and Muslim gave it in their Sahih collections from a hadith of Shu'ba, from al-Hakam b. Ibrahim, from al-Aswad, from 'A'isha. Abu Da'ud al-Tayalisi stated that Ash'ath informed him, from Mansur, from Ibrahim, from al-Aswad, from 'A'isha, who said, 'It's as though I can see now the shine of the perfume at the roots of the hair of the Messenger of God (SAAS), while he was in a state of ihram.' Imam Ahmad stated that 'Affan related to him, quoting Hammad b. Salama, from Ibrahim al-Nakha'i, from al-Aswad, from 'A'isha, who said, 'It's as though I can see now the shine in the parting of the hair of the Prophet (SAAS), after several days, he being in an ihram state.' 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr al-Humayydi stated that Sufyan b. 'Uyayna related to him, quoting 'Ata b. al-Sa'ib, from Ibrahim al-Nakha'i, from al-Aswad, from 'A'isha, who said, 'I saw the perfume in the parting of the hair of the Messenger of God (SAAS), after three days, he being in an ihram state.' These accounts show that the Prophet (SAAS) wore perfume after washing. For if the perfume had been applied before the washing, it would have been removed by it and not a trace of it would have remained, especially three days after his adoption of the ihram state.
lessons: Acts of love and care rendered by those close to us — even in small gestures — can be a form of worship and devotion when done with sincerity.
Attending to one's personal dignity and appearance before acts of worship reflects the honor due to God and the beauty of the Islamic tradition.
The vivid memories preserved by those who loved the Prophet (SAAS) remind us that love leaves lasting impressions on those who witness it.
practical_advice: Honor the people you care for through small, consistent acts of service; these build deep bonds and lasting memories.
Prepare yourself — physically and spiritually — before entering a space of worship or reflection, as this readiness helps transition the heart into a reverent state.
When recalling loved ones who have passed or are distant, allow those memories to inspire gratitude rather than grief, just as Aisha's vivid recollections conveyed warmth and love.

title: The Prophet Braids His Hair and Prepares the Sacrificial Animal at Dhu al-Hulayfa
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) braided his hair with honey to preserve the perfume, and marked his sacrificial camel with a sign and hung shoes around its neck at Dhu al-Hulayfa, indicating his commitment to completing the full hajj before releasing the ihram.
story: The Prophet (SAAS) then braided (the hair of) his head so that it would better retain the perfume and protect it from retaining the dirt and dust. Malik stated, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar (who said) that Hafsa, wife of the Prophet (SAAS), asked, 'Messenger of God, how is it that some people have freed themselves from the ihram of the 'umra, while you have not?' He replied, 'I have braided my hair and put necklaces on my sacrificial animal; I shall therefore not adopt the ihlal state until I have made the sacrifice.' Both scholars (al-Bukhari and Muslim) promulgated this in their Sahih collections from a hadith of Malik; it has many lines of transmission back to Nafi'. Al-Bayhaqi stated that al-Hakim related to him, quoting al-Assamm, quoting Yahya b. Muhammad b. Yahya, quoting 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Umar al-Qawariri, quoting 'Abd al-Aqa, quoting Muhammad b. Ishaq, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar (who said) that 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) braided his (hair on his) head using honey.' The chain of authorities for this is excellent. The Prophet (SAAS) then made a sign signifying that his hadi was for the sacrifice and hung that sign around its neck; the animal was with him at Dhu al-Hulayfa. Al-Layth stated, from 'Aqil, from al-Zuhri, from Salim, from his father, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the tamattu' at the hijjat al-wada'. He then selected an animal for sacrifice and led that hadi forth with him from Dhu al-Hulayfa. Muslim stated that Muhammad b. al-Muthanna related, quoting Mu'adh b. Hisham - he being al-Dastuwa'i - quoting his father, from Qitada, from Abu Hassan, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived at Dhu al-Hulayfa, he called for his (sacrificial) camel to be brought and that he made a sign on the right surface of its hump, pressed out some of its blood and hung a pair of shoes around its neck.
lessons: Commitment to a spiritual covenant, once made, should be honored fully regardless of what others around you choose to do.
Distinguishing your sacrificial offering publicly is a sign of sincerity and accountability in your devotion.
Small, deliberate acts of preparation — like braiding hair or marking an animal — reflect a disciplined and intentional approach to worship.
practical_advice: When you make a spiritual or personal commitment, make it visible and tangible so it anchors your resolve and keeps you accountable.
Do not compare your spiritual journey to others'; what is right for someone else may not align with the covenant you have made with God.
Approach important obligations with care and detail — thoroughness in preparation reflects the seriousness with which you take your responsibilities.

title: The Prophet's Marking and Decoration of the Sacrificial Camel at Dhu al-Hulayfa
summary: The Prophet Muhammad personally marked and decorated his sacrificial camel at Dhu al-Hulayfa before the pilgrimage, while others attended to the remaining animals. He sacrificed 63 animals by his own hand and allowed Ali to slaughter the remainder.
story: This lzadtth, given in both $alzilz collections, will be quoted hereafter in full, along with commentary upon it, if God so wills it. Muslim stated that Mu!;lammad b. al-Muthanna related, quoting Mu'adh b. Hisham - he being al-Dastuwa'i - quoting his father, from Qitada, from Abil I:!assan, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said that when the Messenger of God ($AAS) arrived at Dhil al-I:!ulayfa, he called for his (sacrificial) camel to be brought and that he made a sign on the right surface of its hump, pressed out some ofits blood and hung a pair of shoes around its neck. He then mounted his riding camel. The ahl al-sunan narrated this through various lines from Qitada. This shows that the Prophet ($AAS) performed this marking and neck decoration with his very own hands on this sacrificial camel, while others undertook the marking and decorating of the other sacrificial animals. For he had with him a large number of animals for sacrifice, 1O0, or close thereto. He himself sacrificed by his own hand 63 animals and allowed 'AIi to slaughter the remainder. In a lzadith ofJabir, it is said that 'Ali brought to the Prophet ($AAS) animals from Yemen for the sacrifice. In the text ofIbn Is/.1aq it is said that he shared his sacrificial animals with 'All. But God knows best. Others state that on the day of the slaughter, he and 'Ali sacrificed 100 animals. In this case, he would have brought them with him from Dhu al-Bulayfa; although he could have purchased them thereafter while he was in the ibram state.
lessons: Personal involvement in acts of worship, even when others could assist, demonstrates sincerity and devotion; leadership does not exempt one from hands-on participation in spiritual obligations.
Delegating responsibilities to trusted companions, as the Prophet did with Ali, reflects wisdom and the importance of building a community of capable, trustworthy individuals.
Generosity in acts of worship — offering a large number of sacrificial animals — reflects a heart that seeks closeness to God beyond the minimum required.
practical_advice: When performing religious or spiritual obligations, try to be personally engaged rather than entirely delegating, as your own participation deepens the spiritual experience.
Identify trustworthy people in your life to whom you can delegate important tasks, and invest in those relationships so that responsibilities are shared with integrity.
In times of abundance, consider giving generously rather than minimally; acts of gratitude expressed through giving can uplift your emotional and spiritual state.

title: The Vision in Wadi al-Aqiq and the Divine Command to Pray
summary: The Prophet Muhammad received a divine visitation in Wadi al-Aqiq, in which he was commanded to pray in the holy valley and to combine the umra within his hajj.
story: Hereabove there has been given the badith related by al-Bukh1lr1 from that of al-Awza'i, from Ya/.1ya b. Abu Kathir, from qkrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, from 'Umar, who stated, "I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say in Wadi al-'Aqiq, 'Tonight I received a visitation from a spirit from my Lord, who said, "Pray in this holy valley and say, '(Let it be) an 'umra within a bijja.'"'"
lessons: Divine guidance often comes in unexpected moments and forms; remaining spiritually attentive allows one to receive and act upon such guidance.
Combining obligations when possible — as in performing umra within hajj — reflects a wisdom of efficiency and wholeness in spiritual practice.
The Prophet's immediate compliance with the divine instruction models the virtue of responding to spiritual guidance without hesitation or delay.
practical_advice: Cultivate a habit of spiritual attentiveness through regular prayer and reflection, so that moments of clarity and divine guidance are not missed.
When you receive clear guidance — whether through prayer, reflection, or counsel — act upon it promptly rather than postponing it.
Look for ways to integrate your spiritual practices so they reinforce one another, creating a more holistic and less fragmented approach to your faith.

title: The Scholarly Dispute Over When the Prophet Recited the Talbiyya
summary: Ibn Abbas explained why the Companions differed in their accounts of when the Prophet first recited the talbiyya during the Farewell Pilgrimage, clarifying that he recited it at multiple points and different people witnessed different moments.
story: Imam A!).mad stated that Ya'qub related to him, quoting his father, from Ibn Is!).aq, quoting Khu~ayb b. 'Abd aI-RaQ.man aI-Jazarl, from Sa'ld b. Jubayr, who said, "I asked 'Abd Allah b. 'Abbas, 'Abu a1-'Abbas, 'It's strange that the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) should differ over his recitation of the talbiyya, over when he made it obligatory.' "He replied, 'I know better than anyone about that. The Messenger of God (SAAS) only performed just one ~ijja; that's why they differ. '''The Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth to make the pilgrimage. After performing the prayer, of two raka'at in his mosque at Dhu a1-Bulayfa, it was while in his seat that he made it obligatory. He recited the talbiyya for the pilgrimage when he had completed his two raka'at; people heard him do this and remembered it. He then mounted up, and when his camel stood, he recited the talbiyya. Various people knew he did so; that was because they were arriving in groups. These people heard him make the recitation when his mount stood upright; they said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) only recited the talbiyya once his mount had stood upright." '''The Messenger of God (SAAS) then proceeded on. And when he was at a point overlooking·a1-Baydal , he recited the talbiyya. Various people knew he did this and so they concluded, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) only recited the talbiyya once he reached a point overlooking ai-Bayda'." But, I swear by God, in fact it was while he was in his chapel that he made it obligatory; he recited the talbiyya when his camel stood up with him, and he also made the recitation when he reached a point overlooking ai-Bayda'.'''
lessons: Differences of opinion among scholars and witnesses often arise not from dishonesty but from the limitations of each person's vantage point; charitable interpretation of disagreement leads to greater understanding.
Seeking out those with the most direct knowledge — as Sa'id ibn Jubayr sought out Ibn Abbas — is the wisest approach when facing confusion or scholarly dispute.
The fact that the Prophet recited the talbiyya multiple times across different stages of the journey teaches that spiritual declarations can be repeated and renewed, deepening their impact.
practical_advice: When you encounter conflicting accounts or confusing information, resist the urge to pick a side hastily; seek out someone with deeper knowledge or broader perspective before forming a conclusion.
Understand that your personal experience of an event is valid but partial; others may have witnessed something equally true from a different angle, and both can be right.
In your own spiritual practice, do not limit sacred words or prayers to a single moment; repeat them at different points in your journey to keep your heart engaged and present.

title: Ibn Umar's Practice of Reciting the Talbiyya When His Camel Stood Upright
summary: Ibn Umar consistently followed the Prophet's practice of reciting the talbiyya only once his mount stood upright, facing the qibla, and maintained this practice as a deliberate preservation of the Prophetic example.
story: Al-Bukharl also related it, as did Muslim and the ahl al-sunan, along various lines of transmission, from Mui).ammad b. al-Munkadir and Ibrahim b. Maysara, from Anas. It is established in both Faltib collections from a /ladith of Malik, from Sa'ld al-Maqburl, from 'Ubayd b. Jurayj, from Ibn 'Umar, who said, "Regarding the recitation of the talbiyya, I never saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) recite it until his mount set off with him." Both scholars promulgated in their Fabib collections from an account of Ibn Wahb, from Yunus, from al-ZuhIi, from Salim, from his father, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would mount his camel at Dhu al-I;Iulayfa and he then recited the talbiyya when it stood upright. Al-Bukharl enticles a section: Chapter of those who recited the talbiyya when their camels stood upright. He went on, "Abu 'A~im related to us, quoting Ibn Jurayj, quoting Salii). b. Kaysan, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar, who said, 'The Prophet (SAAS) recited the talbiyya when his camel stood upright bearing him.'" Muslim and ai-Nasa'l related this from a badith ofIbn Jurayj. Muslim stated that Abu Bakr b. Abu Shayba related to him, quoting 'Ali b. Mushir, from 'Ubayd Allah, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) placed his foot in the stirrup and his mount stood up bearing him, he recited the talbiyya from Dhu al-I;Iulayfa." Al-Bukharl went on to head a section: Chapter on reciting the talbiyya pcing towards the qibla. He went on, "AbU Mamar stated that it was related to him by 'Abd al-Warith, quoting Ayyub, from Nafi', who said, 'When Ibn 'Umar prayed in the morning at Dhu al-I;Iulayfa, he ordered his mount to be brought. It was saddled and he mounted it. When it stood up bearing him, he would face the qibla. He would then ride on, reciting the talbiyya until he reached the baram, the sacred precincts of Mecca. He would then cease the recitation until he reached Dhu Tuwa, where he would spend the night until morning. After performing the morning prayer, he would take a bath; he claimed that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had done this.'" He then stated, "Ism.'II agreed with him, quoting Ayyilb, concerning the taking of a bath." Al-Bukhiirl also stated that Sulayman Abu al-RabI' related to him, quoting Fulay1)., from Nafi', who said, "When ('Abd Allah) Ibn 'Vmar was about to proceed to Mecca, he would put on a scentless pomade. He would then go to the mosque at Dhu al-J::Iulayfa, perform the prayer, then mount up. When his camel stood upright, bearing him, he declared himself to be in the ibram state. He then said, 'This is what I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) do.'"
lessons: The Companions' careful preservation of the Prophet's practices in their own lives reflects the deep love and reverence they had for him; emulating those we love is a natural expression of that love.
Attention to the precise details of sacred practice — the moment of mounting, the direction faced, the timing of the declaration — shows that mindfulness and intentionality are valued in worship.
Ibn Umar's words 'This is what I saw the Messenger of God do' reflect a humility in practice: grounding one's actions in transmitted knowledge rather than personal preference.
practical_advice: When establishing spiritual routines, pay attention to the details and sequences involved; deliberate, mindful practice deepens the quality of worship beyond mere completion of steps.
Consider adopting the practice of facing a meaningful direction — physically or metaphorically — when beginning an important undertaking, as an act of intentional orientation toward your purpose.
Learn from those who have directly witnessed or experienced what you wish to practice; firsthand transmission of knowledge carries a richness that secondary sources alone cannot fully convey.

title: Aisha's Account of the Prophet's Hajj and Umra Declarations at Hijjat al-Wada
summary: Aisha narrates how the Prophet declared talbiyya for both Hajj and Umra at the Farewell Pilgrimage, giving instructions to companions based on whether they had sacrificial animals, and herself being among those who declared ihram for Umra without a sacrificial animal.
story: The same applies to the hadith narrated by Imam Ahmad, who stated that Rawh related to him, quoting Salih b. Abu al-Akhdar, quoting Ibn Shihab (who said) that 'Urwa informed him that 'A'isha, wife of the Prophet (SAAS), said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) recited the talbiyya for the hajj and the 'umra on the hijjat al-wada', and he led his hady with him. (Some) people with him recited the talbiyya for the 'umra and led their sacrificial animals; other people recited the talbiyya for the 'umra and did not lead sacrificial animals." 'A'isha went on, "I was among those who recited the talbiyya for the 'umra and I did not lead a hady. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived (in Mecca), he said, 'Those of you who recited the talbiyya for the 'umra and led a hady should circumambulate the ka'ba and pass between al-Safa and al-Marwa; nothing for which you have declared the ihram will be permitted until you have completed the hajj and sacrificed your animal on the day for the slaughter. Those of you who recited the talbiyya for the 'umra and did not lead with you an animal for sacrifice should circumambulate the ka'ba, pass between al-Safa and al-Marwa; you may then shorten your hair and declare your ihram concluded. You may then recite the talbiyya for the hajj and lead an animal for sacrifice. Those unable to do this may fast for three days on the hajj and for seven days when you return to your family.'" 'A'isha went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave priority for the hajj that he feared he might miss, and postponed the 'umra."
lessons: Following clear, structured guidance during complex religious obligations reflects wisdom and care for the community.
Participating in worship according to one's capacity — with or without a sacrificial animal — demonstrates that Islam accommodates different circumstances.
The Prophet's detailed instruction ensured no one was left confused or excluded from the spiritual benefits of the pilgrimage.
