If you realize in hindsight that you would have chosen differently, acknowledge it honestly rather than defending a past choice out of pride.
Ask your scholars or trusted mentors the big clarifying questions — like Suraqa did — so you understand not just what to do but why.

title: Safiyya's Menstruation and the Prophet's Compassionate Resolution
summary: When Safiyya bint Huyayy began menstruating on the day of departure from Mina, the Prophet was concerned it would delay the group, but upon learning she had already performed the circumambulation, he permitted her to depart and arranged for her to perform umra from al-Tan'im.
story: When it was the night of aI-Nafr, Safiyya, daughter of I;Iuyayy began to menstruate. The Prophet (SAAS), exclaimed, 'Ifalqa 'aqra! "With a shaven head and menstruating!" I can only see her impeding you all.' He then asked (her), 'Did you make a circumambulation the day of the sacrifice?' 'Yes,' she replied. He then said, 'Then you may leave.' She said, 'Messenger of God, I did not recite the talbiyya (for the 'umra).' He said, 'So make an 'umra from al-Tancim.' The narrator continued, 'And so her brother accompanied her. She said, "And we met Mudlij, and he said, 'You're (to assume the state of i{lram) from so-and-so.""'
lessons: The Prophet's immediate concern for how Safiyya's situation would affect others, followed by a compassionate and practical solution, models the balance of community responsibility and individual care.
Religious obligations are never used as instruments of shame or punishment — the Prophet found a way forward that honored both the law and Safiyya's dignity.
When unexpected circumstances disrupt our plans, patient problem-solving guided by wisdom leads to resolution.
practical_advice: When something goes wrong at an inconvenient time, resist catastrophizing — focus instead on what practical solutions are available within your constraints.
If you are in a leadership or support role and someone faces an unexpected difficulty, ask clarifying questions before assuming the worst outcome.
Allow others to accompany and support you through difficult or embarrassing moments rather than facing them alone.

title: The Talbiyya of the Prophet During the Farewell Pilgrimage
summary: Multiple Companions narrated the precise words of the Prophet's talbiyya recited during the Farewell Pilgrimage, beginning when his camel rose at the mosque of Dhu al-Hulayta, with Ibn Umar adding his own extension to the supplication.
story: Al-Shafi'I stated that Malik informed him, from Nafi', from 'Abd Allah b. 'Vmar, that the talbiyya spoken by the Messenger of God (SAAS) was: 'Labbayka Allahumma, Labbayk! Labayka, La sharika laka! Labbayk! Inna al-bamda wa al-ni'mata laka, wa al-mulku lak. La sharika lak. "At Your service, o God, at Your service! At Your service; You have no associate! At Your service! Praise and grace are Yours! Power is Yours! You have no associate!"' 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar would make an addition to this: 'Labbayka laka wa sa'dayk! Wa al-khayru fi yadayka!, Labbayka! Wa al-raghba'u ilayka wa al-'amal. "At Your service, and at Your pleasure! And all good is in Your hands! At Your service! And all joy, and all good are in Your hands! At Your service! And all aspirations are to You, and (all) work!"' Muslim related that the Messenger of God (SAAS) recited the talbiyya when his camel stood up at the mosque at Dhu al-I;Iulayfa. He recited the talbiyya, saying, 'At Your service, 0 God, at Your service! At Your service; You have no associate; at Your service. Praise and grace are Yours. And power is Yours. You have no associate.' They said, 'Abd Allah used to say, "This is the talbiyya of the Messenger of God (SAAS)." Nafi' said, "Abd Allah would add along with this, 'At Your service! At Your service! At your service, and at Your pleasure! Good is in Your hands, and aspirations are to You, and so is work.'"' Ibn 'Umar stated, 'I learned the talbiyya from (the mouth of) the Messenger of God (SAAS).' 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar stated that 'Umar b. al-Khanab used to recite the talbiyya as had the Prophet (SAAS), using those words. He would say, 'At Your service, 0 God, at Your service! At Your service, and Your pleasure. Good is in Your hands. At Your service! Aspirations are to You, and so is work.' 'A'isha said, 'I well know how the Prophet ($AAS) would recite the talbiyya. He would say, "At Your service, 0 God! At Your service! At Your service; You have no associate. Praise and grace are Yours."'
lessons: The talbiyya encapsulates the essence of Islamic spirituality — total devotion, acknowledgment of God's oneness, and recognition that all praise, grace, and power belong to Him alone.
Preserving and transmitting the Prophet's exact words across generations reflects the deep love and care the Companions had for his legacy.
Declaring that all good is in God's hands and all aspirations are directed toward Him is a powerful antidote to anxiety, reminding us that we are not alone in our striving.
practical_advice: Memorize and regularly recite the talbiyya, allowing its meaning to settle in your heart as a daily reminder of your relationship with God.
When you feel overwhelmed, return to the simple declaration that all good is in God's hands — let this release the weight of trying to control outcomes.
Follow the Companions' example of learning sacred texts and supplications directly and carefully, preserving their exact wording as an act of love and reverence.

title: The Talbiyya of the Prophet During Hajj – Narrations from A'isha and Abu Hurayra
summary: Multiple narrations record the exact wording of the Prophet's talbiyya during Hajj, transmitted through A'isha, Abu Hurayra, and other companions, with slight variations in phrasing documented by hadith scholars.
story: He stated, "Mu!)arnmad b. Yusuf related to us, quoting Sufyan, from al-A'mash, from 'Omara, from Abu 'A(iyya, who quoted 'A'isha as saying, 'I well know how the Prophet ($AAS) would recite the talbiyya. He would say, "At Your service, 0 God! At Your service! At Your service; You have no associate. Praise and grace are Yours."'' Abu Mu'awiya gave it from al-A'mash. Shu'ba stated that Sulayman informed him that Khaytharna quoted Abu 'A(iyya from 'A'isha. Al-Bukhari is alone in giving this. Imam A!)mad narrated it from 'Abd al-Ra!)man b. Mahdi, from Sulayman al-Thawri, from Sulayman b. Mahran al-A'mash, from 'Omara b. 'Omayr, from Abu 'Atiyya ai-Wadi, quoting 'A'isha. His account is the same as that narrated by al-Bukhari. Ai)rnad narrated it from Abu Mu'awiya, and 'Abd Allah b. Nurnayr did so from al-A'mash, the sarne as was narrated by al-Bukhitri. He (A!)mad) also narrated it from Mu!)arnrnad b. Ja'far and Raw!) b. 'Obada, from Shu'ba, from Sulayman b. Mahran al-A'mash, giving it as had al-Bukhari. Abu Da'ud al-Tayalisi narrated it the sarne in his text from Shu'ba. Imam Ai)rnad stated that Mu!)arnmad b. Fugayl narrated to him, quoting al-A'mash, from 'Omara b. 'Omayr, from Abu 'A(iyya, who said, "'A'isha stated, 'I well know how the Messenger of God ($AAS) would recite the talbiyya.'" Abu 'Atiyya went on to state, "I then heard her recite the talbiyya. She said, 'At Your service, 0 God, at Your service! At Your service! You have no associate. At Your service. Praise and grace are Yours, and Power. You have no associate.'" He is alone in adding in this text the words, "and Power. You have no associate". AI-Bayhaqi stated that al-1::Iakim informed him, quoting al-A~arnm, quoting Mu!)ammad b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd al-1::Iakarn, quoting Ibn Wahb, quoting 'Abd al-'Aziz b. 'Abd Allah b. Abu Salama, quoting 'Abd Allah b. al-Fagl, from 'Abd al-Rai)rnan al-A'raj, from Abu Hurayra, who said, "Part of the talbiyya of the Messenger of God ($AAS) was the words, 'At Your service, 0 God of the truth.'" AI-Nasa'i narrated it from Qutayba, from 1::Iumayd b. 'Abd al-Ra!)man, from 'Abd al-'Aziz b. Abu Salama and Ibn Maja, from Abu Bakr b. Abu Shayba and 'Ali b. Mu!)arnmad, both of whom quoted Waki', from 'Abd al-'Aziz. AI-Nasa'i stated, "I know of no one except 'Abd al-'Aziz who gave its line of transmission from 'Abd Allah b. ai-Fag!." Isma'il b. Umayya narrated it as mursal. AI-Shafi'i stated that Sa'Id b. al-Qtddai) informed him, from Ibn Jurayj, quoting 1::Iumayd al-A'raj, from Mujahid, who said, "The Prophet ($AAS) would express in the talbiyya 'At Your service, 0 God!'" And he went on to state the talbiyya. He stated, "Eventually, one day when the people were standing away from him, it was as though he was delighted by his circumstances and he added to it, saying, 'At Your service! The (real) life is that of the hereafter!'" IbnJurayj went on, "I considered that to have been the day on Mt. 'Arafat." This l;adith is mursal from this line. The l;afi; Abu Bakr a1-Bayhaqi stated that 'Abd Allah al-I:Iaft? informed him, quoting Abu A!)mad Yusuf b. Mu!)ammad b. Mu!)ammad b. Yusuf, quoting Mu!)ammad b. Is!)aq b. Khuzayma, quoting N""r b. 'Ali al-Jahqami, quoting Ma!)bub b. al-I:Iasan, quoting Da'ud, from qkrima, from Ibn 'Abbas (who said) that the Messenger of God (~AAS) made an address at 'Arafat and that when he had said, "At Your service, 0 God, at Your service!" he went on to say, "The only good is that of the afterlife!"
lessons: The talbiyya is a declaration of complete submission and presence before God, reminding us that true fulfilment is found in drawing close to Him rather than in worldly distractions.

Preserving and transmitting sacred knowledge with precision and care is itself an act of devotion; the scholars' meticulous attention to chains of narration reflects how much they valued every word of the Prophet.

The Prophet's spontaneous addition of 'The real life is that of the hereafter!' on the Day of 'Arafat teaches that moments of spiritual joy naturally orient the heart away from the temporary toward the eternal.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by worldly pressures, pause and verbally affirm your reliance on God, even briefly, as the talbiyya does — this simple act of declaration can recentre your focus and reduce anxiety.
Practice learning and memorising short supplications or phrases of remembrance accurately; having them at hand during stressful moments gives you a proven spiritual anchor.

When you experience a moment of genuine gratitude or peace, let it remind you of the greater joy ahead rather than clinging to it anxiously — use that feeling as motivation to act rightly in the present.

title: Gabriel Commands the Prophet to Raise the Voice in Talbiyya
summary: The Angel Gabriel came to the Prophet and commanded him to instruct his companions to raise their voices when reciting the talbiyya, declaring it one of the rites of Hajj.
story: Imam A!)mad stated that Raw!) related to him, quoting Usama b. Zayd, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Abu Labid, from a1-Munalib b. 'Abd Allah b. I:Iantab, who quoted Abu Hurayra as having said, "The Messenger of God (~AAS) stated, 'Gabriel ordered me to raise my voice in the talbiyya; it is one of the rites of the l;ajj.'" A!)mad was alone in giving this. AI-Bayhaqi narrated it from al-I:Iakim, from al-A~amm, from Mu!)ammad b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd a1-I:Iakam, from Ibn Wahb, from Usama b. Zayd, from Mu!)ammad b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr b. 'Uthman and 'Abd Allah b. Abu Labid, from al-Muttalib, from Abu Hurayra, from the Messenger of God (~AAS), as above. 'Abd al-Razzaq stated that a1-Thawri informed him, from Ibn Abu Labid, from al-Muttalib b. I:Iantab, from Khallad b. al-Sli'ib, from Zayd b. Khalid, who said, "Gabriel came to the Prophet (~AAS) and said, 'Order your Companions to raise their voices in the talbiyya; it is a rite of the l;ajj.'" Ibn Maja narrated it thus, from 'Ali b. Mu!)ammad, from WakJ', from al-Thawri. Similarly Shu'ba narrated it, as did MUsa b. 'Uqba, from 'Abd Allah b. Abu Labid. Imam A!)mad stated that Waki' related to him, quoting Sufyan, from 'Abd Allah b. Abu Labid, from al-Munalib b. 'Abd Allah b. I:Iantab, from Khallad b. al-Sli'ib, from Zayd b. Khalid al-Juhani, who said, "The Messenger of God (~AAS) stated, 'Gabriel came to me and said, "Mu!)ammad, order your Companions to raise their voices in the talbiyya; it is a rite of the l;ajj."'" Our teacher Abu al-I:Iajjaj a1-Mizzi stated in his work al-Alra! that Mu'awiya also related the above from Hisham; and Q;tbi~a (gave it) from Sufyan al-Thawn, from 'Abd Allah b. Abu Labid, from a1-Munalib, from Khallad b. al-Sli'ib, from his father, from Zayd b. Khalid. Al:tmad stated that Sufyan b. 'Uyayna related it to him, from 'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr, from 'Abd ai-Malik b. Abu Bakr b. al-I:Iarith b. Hisham, from Khallad b. al-Sa'ib b. Khallad, from his father, who quoted the Prophet (SAAS), as saying, "Gabriel came to me and said, 'Order your Companions to raise their voices in reciting the talbiyya.'" Al:tmad stated, "I studied it under 'Abd al-Ral:tman b. Mahdi, from Malik. And Rawl:t related to us, quoting Malik - meaning Ibn Anas - from 'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr b. Mul:tammad b. 'Amr b. I:Iazam, from 'Abd al-Malik b. Abu Bakr b. 'Abd al-Ral:tman b. al-I:Iarith b. Hisham, from Khallad b. al-Sa'ib al-An~ari, from his father, who quoted the Messenger of God (SAAS), as saying, 'Gabriel came to me and ordered me to order my Companions - or those with me - to raise their voices in reciting the talbiyya or in the iblal'" - meaning of one of the two. Al-Shafi'i narrated it thus from Malik. And Abu Da'ud narrated it from al-Q;t'nabi, from Malik. Imam Al:tmad also narrated it from a badlth of Ibn Jurayj. AI-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i and Ibn Maja did so from a badlth of SufYan b. 'Uyayna, from 'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr. AI-Tirmidhi stated, "This badlth is basan ~abtlz." The bali.. al-Bayhaqi stated, "And Ibn Jurayj narrated that, 'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr wrote to me giving mention of it. And he did not refer to Abu Khallad in his line of transmission.'" He (al-Bayhaqi) went on, "What is authentic is the account of Malik and Sufyan b. 'Uyayna, from 'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr, from 'Abd ai-Malik, from Khallad b. al-Sa'ib, from his father, from the Prophet (sAAS), similarly."
lessons: Divine guidance reaches us through multiple channels and trusted messengers; when we are uncertain how to express our devotion, seeking authentic guidance — as the companions sought the Prophet's example — brings clarity and confidence.
Raising one's voice in worship is not mere volume but a declaration of conviction; expressing faith openly and without hesitation strengthens one's own resolve and can inspire those around us.

The repeated confirmation of this command through numerous narration chains illustrates that important spiritual practices are worth verifying and preserving carefully, teaching us the value of diligence in what matters most.
practical_advice: When engaging in acts of worship or remembrance, do so with full presence and audible intention rather than going through the motions quietly — letting your heart and voice align deepens the experience.

If you feel shy or self-conscious about expressing your faith or values openly, reflect on how the companions were explicitly encouraged to be unashamed and loud in their devotion; allow that example to give you courage.

Seek authentic sources when learning religious practices rather than guessing; the care scholars took in preserving this instruction shows how much the method of practice matters alongside the intention.

title: Jabir b. Abd Allah's Account of the Prophet's Farewell Hajj – Departure from Medina and Arrival at the Ka'ba
summary: Jabir ibn Abd Allah narrates in detail the Prophet's Farewell Hajj: the massive gathering of companions, the departure from Medina, the incident of Asma bint Umays going into labour, and the Prophet's arrival at the Ka'ba where he performed tawaf and sa'y.
story: Imam Al).mad stated that Yal).ya b. Sa'id related to· him, qnoting Ja'far b. Mul).ammad, quoting his father, who said, "We came to Jabir b. 'Abd Allah while he was with Banu Salama. We asked him about the /tijja of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Jabir related to us that the latter stayed in Medina for nine years without making the pilgrimage. He then had an announcement made to the people that he would be a pilgrim that year. Jabir went on, "A great mass of people came to Medina, all hoping to imitate the Messenger of God (SAAS), and to do as he did. He went forth five days prior to the end of Dhu al-Qt'da, and we left with him. When he reached Dhu al-I;:Iulayfa, Asma', daughter of'Vmays, went into labour with Mul).ammad, son of Abu Bakr. She sent a message to the Messenger of God (SAAS), asking what she should do. He replied, 'Wash, then put in place a folded cloth, then recite the talbiyya.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) proceeded on to where his camel came to a point overlooking aI-Bayda'; there he recited the talbiyya and the taw/tid, saying, 'At Your service, 0 God! You have no associate; at Your Service. Praise and grace are Yours, along with power; You have no associate.' "The people recited the talbiyya, adding words snch as dhii al-ma'iirij, 'He who possesses ladders.' The Prophet (SAAS) heard (this), but said nothing to them. "I looked as far as I could ahead of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and there were people riding and walking. And it was the same behind him, and to both of his sides. "He was right there among us; to him the Qur'an would be revealed, and he knew how to interpret it. Whatever he did, we knew. "We left, with our only intention being to make the l;ajj. When we reached the ka'ba, the Prophet (SAAS) saluted the 'black stone', jogged for three circumambulations and walked for four. When he had finished, he made his way to the maqiim Ibriihim. Behind it he performed two prayer raka'iit. He then recited, 'and use Abraham's station as a place for prayer' (surat al-Baqara; II, v.l2S)." Al).mad stated, "And Abu 'Abd Allah" - meaning Ja'far - "added, 'and he recited verses affirming the Oneness of God, and the verse, "Say, 0 yon unbelievers" (surat al-Kiifiriina; CIX, v.l). "'He then saluted the stone and went offto al-Safa. There he recited, 'al-Safa and al-Marwa are rites of God' (surat al-Baqara; II, v.ISS). "'He then stated, 'We will begin with that which God began.' And he scaled al-Safa and, gazing out at the kacba spoke the words, 'God is Most Great', and then 'There is no god but God alone. He has no associate. He has power, and to Him there is praise. He has control over everything. There is no god but God alone! He has fulfilled His promise, and proved His promise true. And he hazama, 'defeated' -"
lessons: The Prophet's nine years without Hajj and then the massive gathering for his Farewell Pilgrimage remind us that great acts of worship, when the time is right, draw communities together and create moments of profound collective meaning.

Even amid the sacred journey of Hajj, practical human realities — such as Asma' going into labour — arise, and the Prophet's compassionate, practical guidance shows that religion accommodates human need with dignity and ease.

Jabir's awe at seeing the Prophet surrounded in all directions by followers, receiving revelation and demonstrating practice, teaches us the blessing of righteous community and the importance of seeking out and learning from those with knowledge.
practical_advice: When embarking on something spiritually significant — a new practice, a pilgrimage, a major life commitment — prepare yourself thoroughly and gather a supportive community; the power of shared intention multiplies your resolve.

If you face unexpected difficulties at the start of an important undertaking, look to the Prophet's example of responding with calm, practical wisdom rather than anxiety; trust that God's path accommodates your circumstances.

Incorporate verbal declarations of God's greatness and oneness into moments when you feel moved or grateful, just as the Prophet did at al-Safa; anchoring strong emotions to remembrance of God transforms fleeting feelings into lasting spiritual growth.

title: The Prophet's Sa'i Between Al-Safa and Al-Marwa During the Farewell Pilgrimage
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) performed the sa'i between al-Safa and al-Marwa during the Farewell Pilgrimage, proclaiming the greatness of God and supplicating upon both hills, completing seven circuits.
story: 'He then stated, 'We will begin with that which God began.' And he scaled al-Safa and, gazing out at the ka'ba spoke the words, 'God is Most Great', and then 'There is no god but God alone. He has no associate. He has power, and to Him there is praise. He has control over everything. There is no god but God alone! He has fulfilled His promise, and proved His promise true. And he hazama, 'defeated' - or ghalaba, 'overcame' - the factions by Himself.' He then prayed and repeated these words. "'Thereafter he came down until his feet were in the wadi, when he proceeded at a jog until, having ascended, he walked and proceeded on until he reached al-Marwa. He scaled it until he stood facing out to the ka'ba and spoke the same words there as he had upon al-Safa. When it was the seventh circumambulation, at al-Marwa, he said, 'O people, if I had known beforehand what I came to learn, I would not have brought the hadi and would have made it an 'umra. Those who do not have hadis with them should declare their ihram concluded, and make it into an 'umra.' And all the people declared their ihram concluded. "'Suraqa b. Malik b. Ju'shum asked, while down in the lowest part of the valley, 'Messenger of God, is this for this year (alone) or for ever?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) intertwined his fingers and replied, 'For ever.' He repeated this five times. He then said, 'The 'umra has entered into the hajj until Judgement Day.'''
lessons: Ritual acts of worship carry profound meaning; beginning with what God has ordained reflects the virtue of following divine guidance over personal preference.
The Prophet's willingness to publicly acknowledge what he would have done differently models intellectual humility and a commitment to truth over ego.
Connecting worship to eternal principles — as the Prophet did by declaring the 'umra valid until Judgement Day — reminds us that our acts of devotion have lasting spiritual significance.
practical_advice: When performing any act of worship or righteous deed, begin it with a declaration of God's greatness to centre your heart and intention.
If you realize mid-way through something that a better approach existed, acknowledge it openly and guide others accordingly rather than concealing the better path.
Reflect on how your daily spiritual practices connect to eternal truths, using that awareness to find renewed motivation and gratitude in routine acts of worship.

title: 'Ali's Dispute with Fatima Over Her Dress During the Farewell Pilgrimage
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, 'Ali criticized Fatima for wearing coloured clothes and mascara, but the Prophet confirmed she had acted on his own instructions, resolving the misunderstanding.
story: Jabir went on, "'Ali arrived from Yemen with a hadi. And the Messenger of God (SAAS) had brought a hadi from those in Medina. Fatima had put on jewellery, colour-dyed clothing and had decorated her eyes with mascara. He ('Ali) criticized her for that and she replied, 'My father ordered me to do it.' He said, 'Bring me the head kerchief.' Ja'far commented: 'This word "he said" was not a reference to Jabir's speech' [but to 'Ali's] "So 'Ali left, upset, to enquire from the Messenger of God (SAAS), about what Fatima had said. 'Ali said, 'Fatima dressed in colour-dyed clothes and put on mascara, saying her father told her to do so.' He (the Messenger of God, SAAS), replied, 'She spoke the truth. She spoke the truth. I did tell her to do this.'"
lessons: Misunderstandings within families can arise even among the most righteous people; seeking clarification directly and respectfully is the path of wisdom.
Fatima's calm and confident response — citing her father's authority — models how to respond to criticism with composure and truth rather than defensiveness.
The Prophet's clear and immediate vindication of Fatima illustrates the importance of speaking the truth to resolve conflict and restore trust.
practical_advice: When you feel criticized unfairly by a loved one, take a breath before reacting and calmly state the truth of the matter as Fatima did.
If you are upset about something your spouse or family member has done, seek clarification by going to the source of the matter rather than escalating conflict.
Practice affirming the people you love when they are wrongly accused; a clear and direct defence, as the Prophet gave Fatima, can be deeply healing.

title: The Prophet Sacrifices Camels and Shares Meat with 'Ali During the Farewell Pilgrimage
summary: The Prophet sacrificed 63 camels by his own hand during the Farewell Pilgrimage, gave 'Ali permission to sacrifice the remainder, and ordered that meat from every animal be cooked so they could eat together.
story: Jabir went on, "The number of hadis brought by 'Ali from Yemen and by the Messenger of God (SAAS) totalled 100. The latter sacrificed by his own hand 63, and he then gave 'Ali (permission) to sacrifice the remainder. He allowed him to share in his own hadi. He then ordered that meat be removed from each sacrificed animal and it was placed in a cooking pot. They both then ate the meat and drank the gravy. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then stated, 'I have made sacrifice here; and all Mina is a place for sacrifice.' He then stood upon Mt. 'Arafat and said, 'I have stood here; and all 'Arafat is a station.' He then halted on al-Muzdalifa and said, 'I have stood here, and all Muzdalifa is a station.'"
lessons: Sharing in acts of worship and in the blessings they produce — as the Prophet did by eating with 'Ali — strengthens bonds of love and community.
The Prophet's declaration that all of Mina, 'Arafat, and Muzdalifa are valid stations reflects an expansive, inclusive spirit that removes hardship from worship.
Participating personally and fully in acts of devotion, rather than delegating everything, demonstrates sincerity and sets a powerful example for others.
practical_advice: Look for opportunities to involve loved ones meaningfully in your acts of worship or charity, as shared spiritual experiences deepen relationships.
When you receive a blessing — whether food, provision, or good fortune — make a point of sharing it with those around you rather than keeping it only for yourself.
In your spiritual practices, aim to be present and personal rather than merely going through the motions; the Prophet's hands-on participation models the spirit of true devotion.

title: The Prophet's Farewell Sermon at 'Arafat
summary: At the plain of 'Arafat during the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet delivered a landmark sermon abolishing blood feuds and interest, affirming the rights of women, and enjoining adherence to the Quran.
story: "When the sun had begun to decline, he called for his camel Qa'wa' and it was saddled for him. He then went on down into the centre of the valley where he made an address to the people. He said, 'Your blood and your possessions are sacrosanct for you, just as this day of yours is holy for you, in this month, in this your land. "'I declare that everything relating to the jahiliyya is cast down beneath my two feet. The blood (feuds) of the jahiliyya are abolished. The first blood feud I dismiss is that relating to the blood of Ibn Rabi'a b. al-Harith, brought up as a foster-child in Banu Sa'd and killed by Hudhayl. Also the interest charges of the jahiliyya are abolished. The first interest charge I abolish is that of al-'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Mutalib; it is all abolished. "'Fear God (in your treatment of) women. You have taken them in God's trust, and their persons are made permitted to you by God's word. You may require of them that they not allow anyone you dislike to enter your furnishings; if they should do this, then beat them, but not viciously. They have the right from you to have their sustenance and their clothing, with kindness. "'I have left amongst you something wherein, if you keep to it, you will not go astray thereafter: God's Book. You will be questioned about me. What is it you will say?' They responded, 'We will bear witness that you did convey (the message), that you gave good advice and did your duty.' He then spoke with his index finger, using it to convey meaning to the people, saying, 'O God, I bear witness! O God, I bear witness!' (He said this) three times."
lessons: The abolition of blood feuds and interest in the Farewell Sermon demonstrates that true spiritual transformation requires dismantling unjust social structures, not just personal piety.
The Prophet's emphasis on women's rights — at a time when such rights were rarely acknowledged — shows that justice and care for the vulnerable are central to Islamic teaching.
Holding fast to the Quran as a guiding anchor, as the Prophet instructed, is the surest protection against going astray in times of confusion or moral uncertainty.
practical_advice: When you find yourself carrying old grudges or resentments from the past, take inspiration from the Prophet's declaration and make a conscious decision to let go of 'jahiliyya' patterns in your own life.
Reflect regularly on how you treat the people closest to you — particularly those who depend on you — and ask whether you are fulfilling their rights with kindness.
In moments of confusion or when you feel spiritually lost, return to the Quran; let it be your compass, as the Prophet instructed it should be for all generations.

title: The Prophet Stands at 'Arafat and Departs at Sunset
summary: The Prophet stood at the plain of 'Arafat facing the Ka'ba until sunset, then departed for Muzdalifa with Usama b. Zayd riding behind him, gently urging the crowds to proceed calmly.
story: "After that the Messenger of God (SAAS) rode his camel to the mawqif ('Arafat), making the chest of his mount face towards the rocks. He positioned Mt. al-Mashar ahead of him and faced the ka'ba. He remained standing there until the sun set. The yellow of the sky had dissipated somewhat, and the sun-disk had set. Usama b. Zayd then mounted behind him and the Messenger of God (SAAS) moved away; he had so tightened al-Qa'wa's bridle that her head almost touched the front of his saddle. With his right hand he was making a gesture that said, 'People! Go easy! Go easy!' Whenever he passed over hilly terrain, he loosened his grip somewhat until she had climbed up. "When he came to Muzdalifa he prayed the al-maghrib and the al-'isha' with a single adhan and two iqamas; between these two prayer times he did not speak prayers."
lessons: The Prophet's calm instruction to the crowds — 'Go easy! Go easy!' — in the midst of a massive gathering models how composed leadership can prevent chaos and protect people.
Combining prayers when there is a legitimate need, as the Prophet did at Muzdalifa, illustrates that Islam accounts for human difficulty and does not demand hardship.
Standing in stillness and contemplation before God, as the Prophet did at 'Arafat, is a powerful spiritual practice that can anchor the soul even in the middle of great crowds and activity.
practical_advice: In stressful, crowded, or overwhelming situations, adopt the Prophet's spirit of 'go easy' — slow your pace, lower your voice, and help others around you to remain calm.
When life becomes demanding, remember that combining or adjusting your routines to accommodate genuine need is not weakness but wisdom; do not add unnecessary hardship to yourself.
Set aside time — even briefly — to stand quietly before God in contemplation, facing the direction of your spiritual centre, as the Prophet did at 'Arafat.

title: The Prophet Departs Muzdalifa Before Sunrise and Redirects Al-Fadl's Gaze
summary: The Prophet departed Muzdalifa before sunrise with al-Fadl b. al-Abbas riding behind him, and when al-Fadl began staring at passing women, the Prophet gently redirected his face away.
story: "He proceeded on before the sun rose, mounting al-Fadl b. al-Abbas behind himself; this was a man with fine hair, a pale complexion and dignified mien. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) moved forward, several women in howdahs passed by, at a gallop. When al-Fadl began looking towards them, the Messenger of God (SAAS) placed his hand over the former's face. Al-Fadl moved his hand over to the other side, but the Messenger of God (SAAS) moved it back over to the other side of al-Fadl's face. Al-Fadl then moved his face away from the other side, looking."
lessons: The Prophet's gentle, non-verbal redirection of al-Fadl's gaze illustrates that correcting a companion's behaviour can be done with kindness and without humiliation.
Guarding one's gaze is a spiritual discipline that requires active effort, as even a companion of dignified appearance needed the Prophet's reminder.
True mentorship involves being present and attentive to the moral welfare of those in your care, addressing subtle lapses with wisdom rather than harshness.
practical_advice: When you notice yourself — or someone you care for — drifting toward something that distracts from mindfulness and modesty, gently redirect without shaming or scolding.
Build habits of guarding your attention — whether from screens, content, or environments that pull your focus away from what is good — as this is a daily form of spiritual discipline.
If you are in a position of mentorship or leadership, be present enough to notice the small moments where gentle guidance is needed, and respond with care rather than criticism.

title: The Prophet Casts Pebbles at Al-Jamra and Returns to the Ka'ba
summary: The Prophet cast seven pebbles at al-Jamrat al-Kubra glorifying God with each throw, slaughtered sacrificial camels, then rode to Mecca to perform the Zuhr prayer and visit the Zamzam well.
story: "When he reached the wadi Mu'asir, he spurred on his mount a little, then followed the middle trail that emerges at al-Jamrat al-kubra and he went on to the jamra, the stone pile at the tree. He cast seven pebbles at the stone mound, glorifying God with each pebble; each pebble was small and he threw them from the centre of the wadi. "He then proceeded on to the sacrifice site, where he slaughtered 63 sacrificial camels by his own hand. He then allowed 'Ali to sacrifice those that remained and had him share in his own hadi. He ordered that a piece of the meat of each animal be cut out and placed in a pot, where it was all cooked. Both men ate from that meat and drank the gravy. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then rode off and proceeded to the ka'ba. He performed the al-zuhr prayer in Mecca, and went over to Banu 'Abd al-Munalib, who were distributing the water at zamzam. He said, 'Draw water, 'Abd al-Munalib! Were it not that people would usurp this right from you (by wanting to do the same in emulation of my sunna) I would certainly have joined you in drawing water.' They handed him a bucket and he drank from it."
lessons: Accompanying every symbolic ritual act — like casting pebbles — with the remembrance of God transforms outward gestures into genuine acts of worship.
The Prophet's concern that his drinking from Zamzam might cause people to make it an obligatory practice shows his deep care for the community's welfare, prioritizing ease over personal precedent.
Completing acts of worship with full participation, from sacrifice to prayer to sharing in communal water, models an integrated, wholehearted approach to spiritual life.
practical_advice: In any act of worship or meaningful ritual, accompany each step with the verbal remembrance of God; this keeps your heart present rather than letting the body go through motions alone.
Be mindful of how your personal practices might be emulated by others; lead by example, but be sensitive to what additional burdens your example might inadvertently create.

After completing significant spiritual milestones, take time to nourish yourself and share that nourishment with others, as the Prophet did in eating and drinking with his companions after the pilgrimage rites.

title: The Prophet Drinks from Zamzam with Banu Abd al-Muttalib
summary: During the Farewell Hajj, the Prophet approached Banu Abd al-Muttalib who were distributing Zamzam water and drank from their bucket, noting that were it not for people following his example he would have joined them in drawing the water.
story: over to Banu 'Abd al-Munalib, who were distributing the water at zamzam. He said, 'Draw water, 'Abd al-Munalib! Were it not that people would usurp this right from you (by wanting to do the same in emulation of my sunna) I would certainly have joined you in drawing water.' They handed him a bucket and he drank from it.
lessons: Humility is a mark of true greatness — the Prophet, despite his status, sought to join others in simple acts of service.
Consideration for unintended consequences is itself an act of wisdom; the Prophet refrained not out of reluctance but out of care for others.
Honoring the traditions of family and community strengthens bonds and preserves noble customs.
practical_advice: When you feel too proud to participate in humble tasks alongside others, ask yourself what the Prophet would have done.
Before acting, consider how your actions might unintentionally affect or burden those around you.
Find ways to honor and participate in your community's meaningful traditions, even in small symbolic gestures.

title: The Prophet's Guidance on the Places of Sacrifice and Standing at Arafat and Jam
summary: During the Farewell Hajj, the Prophet declared that all of Mina is a place of sacrifice, all of Arafat is a station, and all of Jam (al-Muzdalifa) is a station, generalizing the sacred rites for the entire Muslim community.
story: the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "I made sacrifice here, and all Mina is a place for sacrifice. And so make sacrifice on your journeys. I have stood here, and so all 'Arafat is a station. And I have stood over here, and all Jam is a station."
lessons: The Prophet's teachings were designed to ease the way for his community, removing unnecessary hardship from acts of worship.
Sacred acts are not confined to one precise spot when a Prophet's guidance expands them — generosity and accessibility are divine gifts.
Following the Prophet's example in worship connects us to a living spiritual legacy that spans generations.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by the details of religious practice, return to the spirit behind the rules — ease and sincerity.
Teach others about the breadth and accessibility of religious obligations rather than making them feel restricted or excluded.
Reflect on how the Prophet's guidance was always aimed at inclusion and clarity, and apply that spirit to how you guide others.

title: Ibn Umar Follows the Prophet's Prayer Stops on the Road from Medina to Mecca
summary: Abdullah ibn Umar diligently sought out every place the Prophet had prayed along the road between Medina and Mecca, praying in those exact spots in emulation of the Prophet's practice.
story: Muhammad b. Abu Bakr al-Muqaddimi related to us as follows, 'Fugayl b. Sulayman related to us, quoting Musa b. 'Uqba, who said, "I saw Salim b. 'Abd Allah searching out places on the route, and praying in them. He would relate that his father used to pray in them, and that the latter had seen the Prophet (SAAS), pray in those places." Nafi' related to me, from Ibn 'Umar, may God be pleased with both, that he used to pray in those places. I questioned Salim and I know only that he agreed with Nafi' about all these places. However, they did differ over a mosque at Sharaf al-Rawha'." He (Fugayl) went on, "Ibrahim b. al-Mundhir said, 'Musa b. 'Uqba narrated to us, from Nafi', that 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar told him that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would dismount at Dhu al-Bulayfa when he performed the 'umra and on his hajja, beneath an acacia tree at the place of the mosque that is there. Whenever he was returning from a military expedition, or a hajj or an 'umra, and his path would pass through there, he would descend to the centre of the wadi. When he appeared at the wadi, he would make his mount kneel at the plain that was on its eastern edge. He would dismount and rest there, and not at the mosque that was at a rock, nor at the palm trees near which the mosque stood. There was a deep wadi nearby in which 'Abd Allah would pray. At its bottom there were piles of sand near which the Messenger of God (SAAS) would pray. The flood from the plain ultimately buried that location where 'Abd Allah would pray.'" (The account went on to state that) 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar related to Nafi' that the Prophet (SAAS) would pray at the site of the small mosque, other than the one at the Sharaf al-Rawha'. 'Abd Allah would point out the place where the Prophet (SAAS) would pray, and say, "Over there at your right when you stand praying in the mosque." That mosque is at the right edge of the road when you are proceeding to Mecca. The distance between it and the bigger mosque is approximately a stone's throw. (The account goes on that) Ibn 'Umar would pray towards the sand hillock which was at the end of al-Rawha'. The side of that hillock would end at the edge of the road, below the mosque which lies between it and the beginning point of the open land, if on your way to Mecca. A mosque had been built there, but 'Abd Allah did not pray in that mosque. He would leave it on his left and his rear and pray in front of it, facing towards the sand hillock itself. 'Abd Allah would leave al-Rawha' and not perform the al-zuhr prayer until he reached that place, where he would pray. If he should be coming from Mecca and pass by it an hour before the al-subh prayer, or at the end of the period before dawn, he would dismount to rest until he could pray the al-subh prayer there. (The account went on that) 'Abd Allah (Ibn 'Umar) related to him that the Prophet (SAAS) would dismount beneath a large sarha tree below al-Ruwaytha, at the right of the road and opposite it, in a place where there was a broad, level gulley which comes out at a hillock a little below the mail-station of al-Ruwaytha, some two miles away. Its top had broken off and it has bent at its centre; it stands on a track in which there are many deposits of sand. (The account states that) 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar told Nafi' that the Prophet (SAAS) also prayed at the side of a hillock at the back of al-'Arj, on the way to Hadba; at that mosque there are two or three graves. Over them stand stone markers, to the right of the road, at some salam trees. It was between those trees that 'Abd Allah would pass from al-'Arj, after the sun had begun to decline after noon; he would perform the al-zuhr prayer in that mosque. (The account states that) 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar also related to him that the Messenger of God (SAAS) stopped at some trees to the left of the road at a rivulet below Harsha; that stream adjoins the extremity of Harsha, roughly an arrow's cast away from the road. 'Abd Allah would pray at the tree closest to the road; it was also the tallest tree. 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar also told him that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would go down into the stream that is very close to Marr al-Zahran, before Medina, when he would descend from al-Safruwat. He would go down to the bed of the stream, to the right side of the road if you are headed for Mecca, there being only a stone's throw between that place and the road.
lessons: Deep love for the Prophet is expressed not only in words but in following his footsteps as closely as possible.
Preserving and transmitting knowledge about the Prophet's practices is itself a form of worship and devotion.
The Companions' careful attention to detail in following the Prophet shows us that sincerity in worship includes effort and intentionality.
practical_advice: Identify one specific Sunnah you can revive in your daily life and practice it consistently as an act of love for the Prophet.
When learning about religious practices, seek out the 'why' behind them so your worship is grounded in meaning and not just routine.
Share what you know of the Prophet's practices with others — preserving and transmitting this knowledge is a communal responsibility.

title: The Prophet Spends the Night at Dhu Tuwa Before Entering Mecca
summary: The Prophet would stop and spend the night at Dhu Tuwa, a place near Mecca, performing the dawn prayer there before entering the sacred city in the morning, a practice emulated faithfully by Ibn Umar.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Musaddad related to him, quoting Yahya b. 'Abd Allah, quoting Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) spent the night at Dhu Tuwa. When morning came, he entered Mecca." Ibn 'Umar would do this. Muslim narrated it from a hadith of Yahya b. Sa'id al-Qattan and added, "until he performed the al-subh prayer". Or his words were "until morning came". Muslim stated that Abu al-Rabi' al-Zahrani related to him, quoting Hammad, from Ayyub, from Nafi' that Ibn 'Umar never came to Mecca without spending the night at Dhu Tuwa until morning came and he washed himself. He would then enter Mecca later that morning. He would relate that the Prophet (SAAS) would do this. Al-Bukhari narrated it from a hadith of Hammad b. Zayd, from Ayyub. Both (these scholars) give it from a different line, from Ayyub, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar, to the effect that when he entered close to the sanctified area, he would refrain from reciting the talbiyya until he had spent the night at Dhu Tuwa. And he narrated as above. We have given above the account given by both scholars through Musa b. 'Uqba, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar, who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would spend the night at Dhu Tuwa until morning came. When he reached Mecca he would perform the al-subh prayer. Moreover, it was at some rough hillocks that he would pray. And that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would face towards the two crevices of the mountain that stood between him and the Jabal al-Tawil, towards the ka'ba, and that he kept (the place) where the mosque was (later) built at the left of the mosque beside the hillocks. (The hadith states) that the prayer place of the Messenger of God (SAAS) was below it upon the black hillocks and that he would move some ten arms' lengths away from the hillocks, then pray facing the two gaps in the mountain that, "stood between you and the ka'ba".
lessons: Pausing before entering a sacred or significant moment — preparing oneself physically and spiritually — reflects profound reverence.
Great journeys deserve mindful arrivals; rushing to the destination without preparation robs us of the full blessing of the moment.
The Companions' faithful replication of the Prophet's habits teaches us that spiritual discipline is passed down through lived example.
practical_advice: Before entering any important or sacred space — a masjid, a new chapter in life, a difficult conversation — take a moment to pause, purify your intention, and be present.
Build transition rituals into your day: a brief pause, a prayer, or a moment of reflection before moving from one major activity to another.
When you find yourself rushing through meaningful moments, ask whether slowing down might allow you to experience them more fully and gratefully.

title: The Prophet's Entry into Mecca During Hajj
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) completed his journey to Dhu Tuwa, performed the Fajr prayer, bathed, and entered Mecca in daytime through the upper pass at al-Batha', following the same route he had taken on the day of the conquest.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had completed his journey to Dhu Tuwa, a place near Mecca adjacent to the sanctuary, he refrained from reciting the talbiyya because he had arrived at his objective. He stayed there overnight until morning, then performed the al-~ubh prayer at that place they described - between the two gaps of the Jabal al-Tawil over from there. Whoever has contemplated by sight these places referred to knows them very well and can visualize the spot where the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the prayer. He then bathed in order to enter Mecca. He then mounted and entered in full view, in daytime, from the high pass at al-Batha'. It is also called 'Kada' because people see it and it looks down upon them. He entered Mecca the same way the day of its conquest, as we related above. Malik stated, from Nafi', who quoted Ibn 'Umar as saying that the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca from the upper pass and left by the lower.
lessons: Spiritual milestones deserve deliberate, mindful preparation — the Prophet paused, rested, and purified himself before entering the sacred city, showing that great moments call for inner readiness.
Consistency in righteous practice is a mark of integrity; the Prophet entered Mecca the same honourable way on multiple occasions, reflecting that our conduct should be reliable and principled.
Arriving at a long-sought goal is a moment for humility and gratitude, not pride — the Prophet's restraint from the talbiyya upon arrival shows awareness of when one phase ends and another begins.
practical_advice: When approaching an important moment — a goal you have worked hard for, a significant meeting, or a milestone — take time to pause, reflect, and spiritually or mentally prepare yourself rather than rushing in.
Establish consistent, honourable habits in how you approach important matters in your life, so your conduct becomes a reflection of your values rather than your mood.
When you achieve something you have longed for, resist the urge to immediately move on; acknowledge the moment with gratitude and mindfulness.

title: The Prophet's Supplication Upon Seeing the Ka'ba
summary: Upon first seeing the Ka'ba upon entering Mecca, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) raised his hands and made a profound supplication asking God to honour the sacred house and bless all who pilgrim to it.
story: When his gaze fell upon the ka'ba, the Prophet (SAAS) spoke the words narrated by al-Shafi'i in his musnad collection, as follows. Al-Shafi'i stated that Sa'id b. Salim informed him, from Ibn Jurayj, that when he saw the ka'ba, the Messenger of God (SAAS) raised his hands and said, 'O God, grant this building ever more honour, glory, respect and awe. And favour those who venerate and revere it and all those who make the hajj or the 'umra pilgrimage to it, according them honour, respect, glory and reverence.' The hafiz al-Bayhaqi stated, 'This hadith is incomplete. It has one witness, with a (tradition) that is mursal, from Sufyan al-Thawri, from Abu Sa'id al-Shami, from Makhul, who said, "When the Prophet (SAAS) entered Mecca and saw the ka'ba, he would raise both hands and say, Allahu Akbar, "God is most Great!". Then he would state, "O God, You are peace, and peace comes from You. Greet us, therefore, our Lord in peace! O God, grant this building ever more honour, glory, awe and reverence. And favour those who make the hajj or the 'umra to it, augmenting their respect, honour, glory and reverence.""'
lessons: When we reach a place or moment of deep spiritual significance, turning immediately to God in supplication rather than self-congratulation reflects true devotion and awareness of God's majesty.
The Prophet's prayer was not only for himself but for all who would come to the sacred house — a reminder that a generous heart prays for the wellbeing of the entire community, not just oneself.
Recognising God's greatness — saying 'Allahu Akbar' — in moments of awe and arrival is a powerful way to anchor the heart in gratitude and perspective, preventing pride and fostering humility.
practical_advice: When you arrive at a long-awaited place, reunion, or achievement, begin with a prayer or moment of gratitude to God before anything else, grounding the experience in thankfulness rather than ego.
Practice praying not only for your own needs but for the benefit of others around you — this shifts the heart from self-centeredness to compassion and strengthens your sense of community.
In moments of awe or overwhelming beauty — whether in nature, worship, or life — pause and acknowledge God's greatness; this transforms ordinary experiences into spiritually nourishing ones.

title: The Prophet Enters the Mosque Through the Banu Shayba Gate
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) entered the mosque through the Banu Shayba gate, a practice connected to the blessed precedent he set when he placed the Black Stone during the rebuilding of the Ka'ba.
story: The Prophet (SAAS) entered the mosque through the Banu Shayba gate. The hafiz al-Bayhaqi stated that it was related to him from Ibn Jurayj, from 'Ata' b. Abu Rabah, who said, 'The person in a state of ihram may enter from wherever he wishes.' He went on to say, 'The Prophet (SAAS) entered through the Banu Shayba gate and left through the Banu Makhzum gate to al-Safa.' Al-Bayhaqi then stated, 'This hadith is very mursal.' Al-Bayhaqi gives evidence for the preferability of entering the ka'ba from the Banu Shayba gate by giving an account through Abu Da'ud al-Tayalisi, (who said), 'Hammad b. Salama related to me, as did Qays b. Sallam, from Simak b. Harb, from Khalid b. 'Ur'ura, from 'Ali, may God be pleased with him, who said, "When the ka'ba collapsed, following (the reign) of Jurhum, Quraysh rebuilt it. When they wished to position the (black) stone, they argued over who should place it there. They agreed that the first person to enter through that gate should do so. It was the Messenger of God (SAAS), who came in through the Banu Shayba gate. He asked for a cloth, placed the stone in its middle, then told (a representative of) each sub-tribe to take hold of one of its sides, and they raised it up. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then took it and positioned it."'
lessons: The Prophet's wisdom in resolving the tribal dispute over placing the Black Stone — by letting each tribe share in the honour — teaches us that true leadership seeks solutions that unite rather than divide.

Entering sacred spaces with intention and reverence, following the Prophet's example in how and where he entered the mosque, reflects that even our physical movements in worship can be acts of conscious devotion.

Small acts of precedent, like the Prophet entering through a particular gate, carry lasting significance — a reminder that our consistent habits and choices leave impressions on those who come after us.
practical_advice: When you face disputes or competition within a group, look for creative solutions that allow everyone to share in the honour or responsibility, rather than insisting on individual victory.
Be intentional about how you enter spaces of worship, work, or rest — bringing a conscious, purposeful attitude to transitions in your day can transform mundane moments into meaningful ones.

Reflect on the habits and precedents you are setting for those who look up to you, whether children, students, or colleagues, and ask whether your consistent choices reflect the values you wish to pass on.

title: The Prophet's Circumambulation of the Ka'ba and the Practice of the Companions
summary: Upon arriving in Mecca, the Prophet (SAAS) performed ablutions and began his Hajj with circumambulation of the Ka'ba, jogging for the first three circuits and walking the remaining four, a practice the Companions faithfully followed.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Ashbagh b. al-Faraj related to him, from Ibn Wahb, quoting 'Amr b. Muhammad, from Muhammad b. 'Abd al-Rahman, who said that he told the following to 'Urwa, "'A'isha told me, 'The first thing the Prophet (SAAS) did when he arrived (in Mecca) was to perform the ablutions. He then circumambulated; it was not an 'umra. Then Abu Bakr and 'Umar performed the hajj as he had done. Following that, I performed the hajj with Abu al-Zubayr. He began first with a circumambulation; then I saw the muhajirun and the ansar do as he had. My mother told me that she, her sister, al-Zubayr and various others had recited the talbiyya for an 'umra. After they had touched the corner (of the ka'ba with the "black stone") they declared their ihram concluded.'" This is the phraseology of al-Bukhari. Her statement, 'it was not an 'umra', shows that the Messenger of God (SAAS) did not declare his ihram concluded between both rites (of the hajj and the 'umra). Moreover, the first thing the Messenger of God (SAAS) did was to salute the 'black stone', before the circumambulation, as Jabir stated, 'until when he reached the ka'ba with him, he saluted the "corner", jogged for three circumambulations and walked four.'
lessons: The Companions' faithful replication of the Prophet's every act of worship demonstrates that true love is expressed through careful attention and emulation, not merely through words or feelings.

Beginning every sacred act with purification — as the Prophet performed ablutions before circumambulation — teaches that readiness of body and spirit is a prerequisite for meaningful worship and, by extension, for meaningful action in life.

The practice of jogging the first three circuits, a tradition rooted in displaying the Muslims' physical strength to onlookers in Mecca, reminds us that resilience and dignity in the face of those who once oppressed us is a form of strength, not arrogance.
practical_advice: Before beginning any important task or act of worship, take a moment to prepare — whether through prayer, deep breathing, or intention-setting — so that you enter the activity with full presence and readiness.
Identify someone whose character and practice you deeply respect and study how they approach significant responsibilities; conscious emulation of good role models accelerates personal growth.

When you have passed through a period of hardship and emerged stronger, carry that strength with quiet dignity rather than bitterness — let your recovery itself be a testimony to your faith and resilience.

title: 'Umar's Kiss of the Black Stone and His Declaration of Pure Monotheism
summary: 'Umar ibn al-Khattab kissed the Black Stone during Tawaf and openly declared that he knew it was merely a stone with no power to harm or benefit, doing so only because he saw the Prophet (SAAS) kiss it — a powerful statement against superstition.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Muhammad b. Kathir related to him, quoting Sufyan, from al-A'mash, from Ibrahim, from 'Abis b. Rabi'a, from 'Umar, (who said) that when he reached the (black) stone, he kissed it and said, 'I well know that you are a stone that can bring neither harm nor advantage. Had I not seen the Messenger of God (SAAS) kiss you, I would not have done so.' Muslim narrated this from Yahya b. Yahya, as did Abu Bakr b. Abu Shayba, Zuhayr b. Harb and Ibn Abu Numayr, all quoting from Abu Mu'awiya, from al-A'mash, from Ibrahim, from 'Abis b. Rabi'a, who said, 'I saw 'Umar kiss the stone and say, "I well know that you are a stone that can bring neither harm nor advantage. Had I not seen the Messenger of God (SAAS) kiss you, I would not have done so."' Imam Ahmad stated that Muhammad b. 'Ubayda and Abu Mu'awiya narrated to him, both saying, 'Al-A'mash related to me, from Ibrahim b. 'Abis b. Rabi'a, who said, "I saw 'Umar go to the stone and say, 'I swear by God, I well know that you are a stone and can bring neither harm nor advantage; had I not seen the Messenger of God (SAAS) kiss you, I would not have done so.' He then approached and kissed it."' Al-Bukhari also stated that Sa'id b. Abu Maryam related to him, quoting Muhammad b. Ja'far b. Abu Kathir, quoting Zayd b. Aslam, from his father, who said that 'Umar b. al-Khattab said to the corner (stone), 'I swear by God, I well know that you are a stone and can bring neither harm nor advantage. Had I not seen the Messenger of God (SAAS) salute you, I would not have done so.' And he did salute it. Then he ('Umar) stated, 'We had nothing to do with geomancy. We did, however, see the unbelievers using it, and God destroyed them.' He went on, 'It is something that the Messenger of God (SAAS) did and we do not want to give it up.'
lessons: 'Umar's statement is a masterclass in the distinction between worship and superstition — he performed a religious rite while simultaneously clarifying its nature, showing that faith does not require abandoning reason.

Following the Prophet's example out of love and trust, rather than out of blind habit or fear, is the hallmark of mature faith — 'Umar did not simply conform; he understood why he was doing what he was doing.

In a world full of practices with unclear origins, it is a mark of wisdom and integrity to ask 'why am I doing this?' and to ground our actions in clear, conscious intention rather than cultural momentum.
practical_advice: When you follow a tradition or practice — religious, cultural, or professional — take time to understand its purpose and origin. Conscious participation in any ritual or habit is far more meaningful and sustainable than blind conformity.
If you find yourself confused by a practice that others perform automatically, do not be embarrassed to ask questions and seek understanding; clarity of intention is a virtue, not a weakness.

In moments of doubt about whether an action is meaningful or merely superstitious, return to your foundational values and ask whether the action aligns with what you genuinely believe — and act accordingly.

title: Umar ibn al-Khattab Kissing the Black Stone
summary: Umar ibn al-Khattab kisses the Black Stone during Hajj while openly acknowledging that it is merely a stone that can bring neither harm nor benefit, but does so solely because he witnessed the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) do the same.
story: Khalaf stated, "Hammad b. Zayd related to us, from 'Asim al-Ahwal, from 'Abd Allah b. Sarjis, who said, 'I saw al-Asla" - meaning 'Umar - 'kiss the stone and say, "By God, I am kissing you even though I well know that you are a stone and that you can bring neither harm nor advantage. Had I not seen the Messenger of God (SAAS) kiss you, I would not have done so."'" In the account of al-Muqaddami and Abu Kamil, the words are, "I saw al-U~aylic". Imam Ahmad narrated it from Abu Mu'awiya, from 'Asim al-Ahwal, from 'Abd Allah b. Sarjis. Ahmad also narrated it from Ghundar, from Shu'ba, from 'Asim al-Ahwal. Imam Ahmad stated that 'Abd al-Rahman b. Mahdi related to him, from Sufyan, from Ibrahim b. 'Abd al-A'la, from Suwayd b. Ghafla, who said, "I saw 'Umar kiss the stone and say, 'I well know that you are a stone that cannot bring harm nor advantage, but I saw Abu al-Qasim" - may God's peace and blessings be upon him, saluting you." Ahmad then narrated it from Waki', from Sufyan al-Thawri. He added, "And he kissed it and embraced it." Muslim related it similarly from a hadith of 'Abd al-Rahman b. Mahdi, without additions. And from a hadith of Waki' with the following addition, "He kissed and embraced the stone and said, 'I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) saluting you.'" Imam Ahmad stated that 'Affan related to him, quoting Wahib, quoting 'Abd Allah b. 'Othman b. Khuthaym, from Sa'id b. Jubayr, from Ibn 'Abbas (who said) that 'Umar b. al-Khattab bent down over the corner and said, "I well know that you are a stone; had I not seen my beloved, the Messenger of God (SAAS), kiss you and salute you, I would not have saluted nor kissed you." He then recited, "In the Messenger of God (SAAS) you had a fine example" (surat al-Ahzab; XXXIII, v.21). Abu Da'ud al-Tayalisi stated that Ja'far b. 'Othman al-Qurashi related to him, from the people of Mecca. He said, "I saw Muhammad b. 'Abbas b. Ja'far kiss the stone and he prostrated over it. Then he said, 'I saw your maternal uncle Ibn 'Abbas kiss it and prostrate himself over it. And Ibn 'Abbas stated, "I saw 'Umar b. al-Khattab kiss it and prostrate over it. And 'Umar then said, 'Had I not seen the Prophet (SAAS) kiss you, I would not have done so.'"'" The hafiz al-Bayhaqi narrated it through Abu 'Asim al-Nabil, quoting Ja'far b. 'Abd Allah, who said, "I saw Muhammad b. 'Abbad b. Ja'far kiss the stone, prostrate over it and then say, 'I saw your maternal uncle, Khalid Ibn 'Abbas, kiss it and prostrate over it. And Ibn 'Abbas said, "I saw 'Umar kiss it and prostrate over it. He then said, 'I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) act like this, and so I did it."'" The hafiz al-Bayhaqi stated that Abu al-Hasan 'Ali b. Ahmad b. 'Abdan, quoting al-Tabrani, quoting Abu al-Zinba', quoting Yahya b. Sulayman al-Ju'fi, quoting Yahya b. Yaman, quoting Sufyan b. Abu Husayn, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) prostrate over the stone." Al-Tabrani stated, "Only Yahya b. Yaman related it from Sufyan."
lessons: Following the Prophet's example (Sunnah) is an act of love and devotion, even when the reason behind a practice is not immediately apparent to our own reasoning.
True faith involves submitting our intellect and personal judgment to divine guidance, acknowledging the limits of our understanding while still acting with full awareness and sincerity.
The companions' transparency — openly stating what they knew and did not know — demonstrates that intellectual honesty and spiritual obedience can coexist beautifully.
practical_advice: When you feel confused or uncertain about a religious practice, remind yourself of the prophetic example behind it and anchor your action in love for the Prophet (SAAS) rather than solely in your own comprehension.
Practice saying 'I follow this because of trust and love' rather than feeling you must always have a rational explanation for every act of worship — this builds sincere devotion.

Study the chain of transmission in Islamic scholarship as a model for tracing knowledge back to its source; in your own life, trace your beliefs and values back to their foundations to ensure they are sound.

title: Ibn Umar's Consistent Salutation of the Two Yamani Corners
summary: Ibn Umar reports that he never once neglected to salute the two Yamani corners of the Ka'ba in easy or difficult times, because he witnessed the Messenger of God (SAAS) salute them both.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Musaddad related to him, quoting Yahya, from 'Ubayd Allah, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar, who said, "I never omitted saluting these two corners, in hard times or easy, ever since I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) salute them both." ('Ubayd Allah continued) "I asked Nafi', 'Did Ibn 'Umar walk between the two corners?' He replied, 'He only walked so that it would be easier for his salutation.'" Abu Da'ud and al-Nasa'i narrated from a hadith of Yahya b. Sa'id al-Qattan, from 'Abd al-'Aziz b. Abu Rawwad, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar (who said) that the Prophet (SAAS) never failed to salute the Yamani (south-west) corner and the stone in each circumambulation. Al-Bukhari stated that Abu al-Walid related to him, quoting Layth, from Ibn Shihab, from Salim b. 'Abd Allah, from his father, who said, "The only corners of the ka'ba I saw the Prophet (SAAS) salute were the two Yamani corners." Muslim narrated it from Yahya b. Yahya and Qutayba, from al-Layth b. Sa'd. In one account from him, it is related that he said, "I don't think that the Prophet (SAAS) would have neglected to salute both Shami corners except for the fact that they were not completed on the foundations (set by) Abraham."
lessons: Consistency in worship — performing acts of devotion in both ease and difficulty — is a hallmark of sincere faith and builds a deeply rooted spiritual character.
Observing and internalizing the example of righteous role models can sustain our religious practice across a lifetime, as Ibn Umar's witnessing of the Prophet shaped his actions for decades.
Every small, repeated act of devotion carries meaning; consistency in small acts is more beloved and transformative than great acts performed only occasionally.
practical_advice: Identify one small act of worship or gratitude you can commit to performing consistently — in good times and bad — and let that consistency become the bedrock of your spiritual life.
When you feel unmotivated in worship, recall a moment when you witnessed someone you admired engaging in that same act sincerely; let their example reignite your own.
Do not skip your spiritual practices simply because circumstances are difficult; those are often the moments when consistent devotion has the greatest impact on your heart.

title: The Prophetic Supplication Between the Yamani Corner and the Black Stone
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) was heard reciting a specific supplication between the Yamani corner and the Black Stone, asking God for goodness in this world and the next and for protection from the punishment of hellfire.
story: Al-Nasa'i stated that Ya'qub b. Ibrahim al-Dawraqi narrated to him, quoting Yahya b. Sa'id al-Qattan, from Ibn Jurayj, from Yahya b. 'Ubayd, from his father, from 'Abd Allah b. al-Sa'ib, who said, "I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, between the Yamani corner and the stone, 'O our God, bring us good on earth, and also good in the hereafter; save us from the torture of hell-fire'" (surat al-Baqara; II, v.201). Abu Da'ud narrated it from Musaddad, from 'Isa b. Yunus, from Ibn Jurayj.
lessons: The Prophet's chosen supplication encompasses both worldly and spiritual wellbeing, teaching us that Islam does not separate material life from the life of the soul.
Asking God for protection from punishment reflects an awareness of accountability that motivates righteous conduct without inducing paralyzing fear.
Memorizing and using Quranic supplications connects our personal prayers to the living tradition of the Prophet and the wisdom of divine revelation.
practical_advice: Memorize and regularly recite the supplication 'Rabbana atina fi al-dunya hasanah wa fi al-akhirah hasanah wa qina 'adhab al-nar' — it is a comprehensive prayer for every dimension of your life.
When feeling anxious about the future, ground yourself in this supplication; its breadth reminds you that God's care covers every aspect of your existence.

Use moments of physical movement — such as walking, commuting, or exercise — as opportunities to recite supplications, following the prophetic model of combining physical acts with remembrance of God.

title: The Prophet's Jogging and Idtiba' During Circumambulation
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) performed the first three circuits of tawaf at a jogging pace and the remaining four at a walking pace, and he also practiced idtiba' (tucking the garment under the right arm) during circumambulation.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Asbagh b. al-Faraj related to him, quoting Ibn Wahb, from Yunus, from Ibn Shihab, from Salim, from his father, who said, "I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS), when he arrived in Mecca, act as follows. As he began the circumambulations, he saluted the 'black stone', and then he ran for three courses out of the seven." Muslim narrated it from Abu al-Tahir b. al-Sarh, as did Harmala, both of them quoting from Ibn Wahb. Al-Bukhari stated that Muhammad b. Sallam related to him, quoting Shurayk b. al-Nu'man, quoting Fulayh, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar, who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) ran for three courses, then walked for four during the hajj and the 'umra." Al-Layth agreed with this, quoting from Kathir b. Farqad, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar, from the Prophet (SAAS).
lessons: The Prophet's physical engagement in worship — jogging, walking, saluting — teaches us that devotion to God involves our whole body and being, not merely our thoughts or words.
The structured alternation between exertion and ease in the tawaf mirrors a broader wisdom: spiritual life benefits from periods of intensity balanced with steadiness.
Preserving and transmitting the precise details of prophetic practice honors the integrity of the faith and ensures that the community remains connected to its origins.
practical_advice: Approach your worship with full physical and mental presence — even small acts of mindful engagement, like a deliberate posture in prayer, can deepen your connection to God.
In your personal goals and spiritual practices, consider building in phases of greater exertion followed by steadier, sustained effort, rather than always trying to maintain maximum intensity.
Learn at least one detailed sunnah of the Prophet related to Hajj or 'Umra so that if you perform pilgrimage, you do so with awareness and love for the prophetic model behind each act.

title: The Prophet's Prayer at Maqam Ibrahim and Progression to Al-Safa
summary: During Hajj, the Prophet (SAAS) saluted the Black Stone, jogged three circuits and walked four, then prayed two rak'at at Maqam Ibrahim before proceeding to al-Safa, in fulfillment of Quranic instruction.
story: Al-Tirmidhi stated that Mahmud b. Ghaylan narrated to him, quoting Yahya b. Adam, quoting Sufyan, from Ja'far b. Muhammad, from his father, from Jabir, who said, "When the Prophet (SAAS) arrived in Mecca, he entered the mosque and saluted the stone. He then passed on to his right, jogged for three circumambulations and walked four. He thereafter went to the maqam Ibrahim, 'Abraham's station', and said, 'And adopt maqam Ibrahim as a place to pray.' He performed two raka'at with the maqam between himself and the ka'ba. He then, after two raka'at went to the stone and saluted it. After that he left for al-Safa. I think he said, 'Al-Safa and al-Marwa are rites of God.'" This is a hadith that is hasan and sahih. And scholars act on that basis. Ishaq b. Rahawayh narrated it as above, from Yahya b. Adam. And al-Tabrani narrated it from al-Nasa'i and others, from 'Abd al-A'la b. Wasil, from Yahya b. Adam.
lessons: The Prophet's pilgrimage was a living embodiment of Quranic verses, demonstrating that scripture and practice are inseparable in Islam.
Honoring the station of Ibrahim (AS) connects every Muslim's worship to the primordial covenant of monotheism, reminding us that we are part of a long and sacred lineage.
Each step of the prophetic pilgrimage was purposeful and ordered; this teaches us that disciplined, intentional structure in worship and in life leads to deeper spiritual experience.
practical_advice: When performing any act of worship, try to understand its connection to scripture and prophetic tradition — this knowledge transforms ritual into a meaningful conversation with God.
Reflect on the story of Ibrahim (AS) and his unwavering submission to God; when you face a demanding test, draw strength from the knowledge that God's greatest servants also faced immense trials.
Bring order and intentionality to your daily spiritual routine — a structured sequence of prayer, remembrance, and reflection mirrors the prophetic model and creates a sense of peace and purpose.

title: The Debate Over Saluting the Shami Corners of the Ka'ba
summary: A scholarly discussion arose among the companions about whether all four corners of the Ka'ba should be saluted, with Ibn Abbas correcting Mu'awiya by noting that the Prophet (SAAS) only saluted the two Yamani corners because the Shami corners were not built on the foundations of Ibrahim.
story: Al-Bukhari stated, "And Muhammad b. Bakr said that he was informed by Ibn Jurayj, quoting 'Ata b. Dinar, from Abu al-Sha'tha' who said, 'And who would avoid touching anything from the ka'ba!'" Mu'awiya used to salute the corners and so Ibn 'Abbas told him, "These two corners are not saluted." He replied, "No part of the ka'ba is to be left out. Ibn al-Zubayr used to salute them all." Al-Bukhari, may God Almighty have mercy upon him, was alone in giving this. The account related by Ibn 'Umar agrees with what Ibn 'Abbas stated — that the two Shami corners were not saluted because they were not completed on the foundations (set by) Abraham. This was because Quraysh could not meet the expense, and they put the stone outside of the ka'ba when they built it. This is explained above. The Prophet (SAAS) wished that it had been built and completed on the foundations of Abraham. But he was concerned over the fact that the people had so recently been in the jahiliyya period, and that they would have resented it (the omission). During the rule of 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr, he demolished the ka'ba and rebuilt it according to the directions given by the Messenger of God (SAAS), as related by his ('Abd Allah's) aunt, the Mother of the Believers, 'A'isha, daughter of Abu Bakr. If Ibn al-Zubayr used to salute all the corners, then it was after their rebuilding on the foundations of Abraham; then this would have been fine. And this is certainly the preponderant view.
lessons: The Prophet's concern for the spiritual readiness of his community — choosing not to rebuild the Ka'ba immediately because the people had just left ignorance — shows that great leadership is sensitive to the pace of people's hearts.
Scholarly correction, when done with knowledge and sincerity as Ibn Abbas did with Mu'awiya, is an act of service to the community and to truth.
Sometimes the ideal cannot be implemented immediately due to circumstances; this teaches us patience, wisdom, and the understanding that God accepts our sincere efforts within our limitations.
practical_advice: When you feel frustrated that an ideal situation cannot be achieved right away, take the prophetic example as comfort: even the Prophet (SAAS) sometimes delayed an ideal action out of wisdom and mercy for his community.
If someone gently corrects you on a matter of practice or knowledge, receive the correction with gratitude — it is a gift that helps you align more closely with truth.
Reflect on areas of your life where conditions are not yet ideal; instead of despairing, ask what the wisest and most merciful course of action is given where you and those around you actually are.

title: The Prophet's Tawaf: Running Three Courses and Walking Four
summary: During Hajj and Umra, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) established the practice of jogging for three circumambulations of the Ka'ba and walking for four, a practice confirmed by multiple companions including Ibn Umar and Jabir.
story: The Prophet ($AAS) ran for three courses, then walked for four during the hajj and the 'umra. Al-Bukhari stated that Ibrahim b. al-Mundhir related to him, quoting Abu Damra Anas b. Ayyaq, quoting Musa b. 'Uqba, from Nafi', from 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar, (who said) that the Messenger of God ($AAS), when he first arrived in Mecca and began the circumambulations for the hajj or the 'umra, would run for three courses and walk for four and then would make two raka'at before making the courses between al-Safa and al-Marwa. The Messenger of God (SAAS), 'would for the first tawaf (circumambulation) move at a fast pace for three circumambulations, and then walk for four. And he also ran in the middle of the course when he passed between al-Safa and al-Marwa.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) moved at a jog from the stone to the stone for three circumambulations, and then walked for four.
lessons: Consistent spiritual practice, even in its outward forms, carries deep meaning and should be preserved with care and reverence.
The Prophet's example of balancing exertion and rest in worship teaches us to pace ourselves in our spiritual journey without burning out.
Preserving the Sunnah even when the original reason has passed reflects deep love and loyalty to the Prophet's way.
practical_advice: When engaging in acts of worship or meaningful rituals, do them with full presence and intentionality rather than rushing through them.
Balance intensity and calm in your spiritual practice — periods of focused effort followed by more measured engagement can sustain long-term devotion.
