When you feel uncertain about a practice, research its origins and wisdom; understanding the 'why' deepens commitment and emotional connection to what you do.

title: Umar's Question About Jogging and Exposing the Shoulder
summary: Umar ibn al-Khattab questioned why Muslims continued to jog and expose the shoulder during Tawaf after Islam was firmly established, yet resolved to maintain the practice out of loyalty to the Prophet's example.
story: 'Umar b. al-Khattab asked, 'Why the movement at a jog and the "exposing of the shoulder", seeing that God had firmly established Islam and had ousted apostasy? However, we will not give up anything we used to do with the Messenger of God (SAAS).' All of this refutes Ibn 'Abbas and those who support his view that the movement at a jog is not an orthodox practice, since the Messenger of God (SAAS) did perform it when he and his Companions arrived on the morning of the Wednesday - during the 'umrat al-qada', that is - and the polytheists commented, 'A delegation is arriving who have been weakened by the Yathrib fever.' And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered them to move at a jog for three courses, then walk between the two corners. He only prevented them from jogging for all the circumambulations because of his concern for their health.
lessons: Even when the original context of a practice has changed, faithfully maintaining what the Prophet taught reflects deep devotion and love.
Asking sincere questions about religious practice — as Umar did — is a sign of intellectual integrity, not disrespect.
The Prophet's concern for his companions' health, even during sacred rites, shows that care for wellbeing is embedded within Islamic practice itself.
practical_advice: When you question why you do something meaningful, explore its origins honestly — you may find wisdom that deepens rather than diminishes the practice.
Maintain beneficial habits and commitments even when the urgent reason that started them has passed, as their value often transcends the original circumstance.
Let the example of those you love and respect guide your actions, especially during times of uncertainty or when motivation is low.

title: The Origin of Ramal: Showing Strength Before the Polytheists
summary: During the Umrat al-Qada, the Prophet ordered his companions to jog during Tawaf to counter the polytheists' claim that they had been weakened by the fever of Yathrib, demonstrating resilience in the face of mockery.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) did perform it when he and his Companions arrived on the morning of the Wednesday - during the 'umrat al-qada', that is - and the polytheists commented, 'A delegation is arriving who have been weakened by the Yathrib fever.' And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered them to move at a jog for three courses, then walk between the two corners. He only prevented them from jogging for all the circumambulations because of his concern for their health. This is firmly established in both Sahih collections; his statement giving the reasons for this are very clear in the Sahih collection of Muslim.
lessons: When others attempt to belittle or diminish you, responding with dignified strength — rather than anger or despair — is the most powerful answer.
The Prophet's balance between demonstrating strength and protecting his companions' health shows that wisdom lies in moderation, even in the face of provocation.
Community resilience is built when a leader inspires collective dignity rather than retreating under social pressure.
practical_advice: When you face mockery or are made to feel weak, focus on what you can control — your actions, your posture, and your response — rather than what others say.
Protect your energy while still showing up strongly; you do not need to exhaust yourself to prove your worth to those who doubt you.
Draw strength from your community and shared values when external voices try to diminish your confidence or identity.

title: The Prophet Circumambulates on a Camel During Hajjat al-Wada
summary: During the Farewell Hajj, the Prophet Muhammad circumambulated the Ka'ba mounted on a camel or mule, saluting the Black Stone with a staff, while the people gathered around him in great numbers.
story: The Prophet ($AAS) circumambulated while upon his camel at the hijjat al-wada', saluting the corner with a bent staff. The Messenger of God (SAAS) circumambulated upon his mount; and when he ended up at the corner, he gestured to it. The Prophet ($AAS) circumambulated the ka'ba upon a camel; and when he came to the corner, he gestured to it with something he had and invoked, 'Allahu Akbar! "God is most Great!"' On the hijjat al-wada' the Messenger of God (SAAS) circumambulated around the ka'ba on a mule saluting the corner, disliking (the possibility) that the people would turn away from him. However, during the hijjat al-wada' he made three tawaf. The first was that of al-qudum. The second that of the ifada, that being the tawaf al-fard, it being the day for the sacrifice. The third was the tawaf al-wada'.
lessons: Acts of worship can be adapted to one's physical circumstances without diminishing their spiritual sincerity or validity.
The Prophet's concern that people not be dispersed shows that maintaining communal connection during worship is itself a spiritual value.
Praising God — 'Allahu Akbar' — in moments of awe and gratitude transforms even a physical gesture into a profound act of devotion.
practical_advice: Do not let physical limitations or difficult circumstances prevent you from engaging in what is meaningful to you; find adaptive ways to show up.
During moments of spiritual significance, verbalize your gratitude and awe openly — words of praise can anchor you emotionally in the present.
When you are in a crowd or overwhelming situation, focus on the sacred act before you rather than being consumed by the chaos around you.

title: The Prophet Enters Mecca at Dawn and Weeps at the Black Stone
summary: At the Farewell Hajj, the Prophet entered Mecca at dawn, approached the Black Stone, and wept, before beginning his circumambulation of the Ka'ba.
story: 'We went into Mecca as dawn rose. The Prophet ($AAS) came to the door of the mosque and made his camel kneel. Then he entered the mosque, beginning at the stone, which he saluted. His eyes welled with tears. He then moved at a jog for three circumambulations, and walked for four. When he had finished, he kissed the stone, placed his hands upon it and them wiped them over his face.'
lessons: Tears in moments of spiritual encounter are not weakness — they are a sign of a heart that is alive, present, and deeply connected to the Divine.
Beginning a sacred act with reverence and emotional honesty, as the Prophet did at the Black Stone, sets the tone for meaningful worship.
Returning to a sacred place after hardship and trial can release deep emotions; allow yourself to feel the weight and beauty of such moments.
practical_advice: When you enter a space of worship or meaning, pause before beginning — let the significance of the moment settle in your heart before you act.
If emotions arise during prayer or reflection, do not suppress them; tears during sincere devotion are a form of release and spiritual cleansing.
After completing something meaningful, like the Prophet kissing the stone and wiping his face, create a closing gesture or ritual that seals the experience with gratitude.

title: The Prophet's Circumambulation Between Al-Safa and Al-Marwa During Hajjat Al-Wada'
summary: During Hajjat al-Wada', the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) performed the sa'y between al-Safa and al-Marwa seven times, jogging in the valley bed and walking elsewhere, reciting praises of God and fulfilling the rites of Hajj as commanded.
story: Muslim narrated in his ~a/:ti/:t collection quoting Jabir in his long /:tadith given above. Following his report of the Prophet (SAAS) having made seven circumambulations of the ka'ba and his having performed two prayer raka'at at the maqam Ibrahim, he went on to say that he returned to the corner (al-rukn), saluted it and then left through the door towards al-Safa. When he approached al-Safa, he recited, "al-Safa and al-Marwa are rites of God" (surat al-Baqara; II, v.IS8). He then said that he would begin with that with which God began. He commenced at al-Safa and scaled it until he saw the ka'ba. Facing towards it, he called out, "God is most Great!" Then he declaimed, "There is no god but God alone! He has no associate. He has power, and to Him there is praise. He has control over everything. There is no god but God! He has fulfilled His promise. He has aided his servant, and has alone defeated the factions." During this, he prayed and spoke similar words three times. He then came down until his feet were set in the wadi; he moved at a jog, until having ascended, he walked to al-Marwa. He scaled it until he looked over to the ka'ba and he spoke upon it what he had upon al-Safa.
lessons: Worship performed with full physical and spiritual engagement, as the Prophet jogged and recited praises simultaneously, reflects the holistic nature of devotion in Islam.
Beginning an act of worship with the name and remembrance of God, as the Prophet recited the Quranic verse before starting his sa'y, establishes the proper intention and spiritual foundation.
Proclaiming God's greatness and His fulfillment of promises, even during physically demanding acts of worship, reinforces gratitude and trust in divine support during one's own hardships.
practical_advice: When beginning any significant task or act of worship, start by consciously invoking God's name and recalling His blessings, as the Prophet began at al-Safa with recitation and praise.
Incorporate both physical effort and verbal remembrance of God into your daily routine — even simple acts like walking can become spiritually meaningful with dhikr (remembrance).

When feeling overwhelmed by life's challenges, recite 'There is no god but God alone, He has fulfilled His promise and aided His servant' to reconnect with trust in God's support and the certainty of His promises.

title: The Prophet's Jogging During Sa'y and His Command to His Companions to Run
summary: Eyewitnesses described the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) running vigorously between al-Safa and al-Marwa during sa'y, his garment swaying from the energy of his movement, while he called upon his Companions to run as God had made it incumbent.
story: Imam AJ:>mad stated that 'Umar b. Harun al-Balkhl, Abu I;Iaf~, that is, quoted IbnJurayj, from one of the Banu Ya'la b. Umayya, who quoted his father as saying, "I saw the Prophet (SAAS) proceed between al-Safa and al-Marwa wearing a najrani burda, mut/tabican, 'gown from Hajran tucked up beneath his armpit'." Imam AJ:>mad stated, quoting Yunus, quoting 'Abd Allah b. al-Mu'ammil, from 'Umar b. 'Abd al-Raħman, quoting 'Atiyya, from I;Iablba, daughter of Abu Nab-rat, who said, "I went into l:Iusayn's house, along with some women of Quraysh, while the Prophet (SAAS) was circumambulating between al-Safa and al-Marwa. As he ran, his izar, 'his waist-wrapper', was moving around because of the energy with which he was running. He was saying to his Companions, 'Run! God made running incumbent upon you.'" Aħmad also stated that Shurayḥ related to us, quoting 'Abd Allah b. al-Mu'ammil, quoting 'Aṭi' b. Abu Rabaḥ, from Safiyya, daughter of Shayba, from I;Iablba, daughter of Abu Tajrat. She said, "I saw the Prophet (SAAS) circumambulating between al-Safa and al-Marwa; the people were ahead of him, and he behind them. He was running so vigorously that I could see his knees. His izar was moving around and he was saying, 'Run, for God has made running incumbent upon you.'" Aħmad also narrated it from 'Abd al-Razzaq, from Ma'mar, from Waṣil, the freed-man of Abu 'Uyayna, from Musa b. 'Ubayda, from Safiyya, daughter of Shayba, (who stated) that a woman informed her that she had heard the Prophet (SAAS), while passing between al-Safa and al-Marwa, saying, "Running (between al-Safa and al-Marwa) has been made incumbent upon you, so run!" (It is also related) from the mother of the son of Shayba b. 'Uthman, to the effect that she saw the Prophet (SAAS), while he was running between al-Safa and al-Marwa, saying, "The valley is only to be traversed vigorously."
lessons: The Prophet's enthusiastic physical engagement in worship — running so vigorously that his garment swayed — teaches that wholehearted effort in obedience to God is itself an expression of faith and love.
Encouraging others to fulfill their religious obligations with energy and commitment, as the Prophet called out to his Companions to run, reflects the importance of communal spiritual support.
Even acts of worship that involve physical exertion carry spiritual meaning; the Prophet's example shows that the body and soul together participate in servitude to God.
practical_advice: Approach your acts of worship and religious duties with energy and full presence rather than going through the motions — wholehearted engagement transforms routine acts into meaningful devotion.

Encourage those around you in their spiritual journey; sometimes a gentle reminder or shared enthusiasm, as the Prophet called out to his Companions, is what helps others fulfill what is good for them.
When you feel spiritually low or disconnected, engage your body in an act of worship — prayer, walking, or movement paired with remembrance of God — to reawaken your sense of spiritual vitality.

title: The Scholarly Discussion on the Ruling of Jogging During Sa'y Between Al-Safa and Al-Marwa
summary: Islamic scholars, drawing on the hadith of Ibn Umar and Jabir, reached unanimous agreement that jogging in the valley bed during sa'y is preferred in every circumambulation, while walking suffices if one does not jog, and the elderly or weak are accommodated.
story: Al-Tirmidhl, may God have mercy upon him, transmitted this from the scholarly community. He stated that Yusuf b. 'Isa related to him, quoting Ibn Fuqayl, from 'Ata' b. al-Sa'ib, from Kathlr b. Jahman, who said, "I saw Ibn 'Umar walking on the course, and I asked, 'Are you going to walk on the passage between al-Safa and al-Marwa?' He replied, 'If I should run, I have seen the Messenger of God (SAAS) run; and if I should walk, I have also seen him walk. I am now a very old man (and am forced to walk).'" Al-Tirmidhl stated, "This ḥadith is ḥasan ṣaḥiḥ." Sa'ld b. Jubayr narrated from Ibn 'Abbas a similar account. Abu Da'ud, al-Nasa'l and Ibn Maja narrated it from a ḥadith of 'Ata b. al-Sa'ib, from Katblr b. Jahman al-Sulaml al-Kufl, from Ibn 'Vmar. Ibn 'Vmar's statement that he witnessed both activities by the Messenger of God (SAAS) offers two possibilities. One is that he saw him move on one occasion at a walking pace from which jogging was absolutely absent. The second is that he saw him run for part of the way and walk for a part. This (latter) is a strong possibility, since al-Bukhan and Muslim narrated from a ḥadith of 'Vbayd Allah b. 'Vmar al-'Vmari, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Vmar, to the effect that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would run along the bed of the river when he made the circumambulation between al-Safa and al-Marwa. It is stated above in the ḥadith of Jabir that the Messenger of God (SAAS) came down from al-Safa and when his feet were set in the wadi, he moved at a jog until he had climbed above, when he came to al-Marwa. This is what the scholars prefer in unanimity - that the person moving between al-Safa and al-Marwa (mentioned above in Jabir's account) should preferably jog on each circumambulation in the bed of the wadi, the stream bed that runs between them. They (the scholars) defined that to apply to the distance between the amyal al-khuḍr, the 'green markers', one standing alone towards al-Safa, adjacent to the mosque, and two more placed together towards al-Marwa, also adjacent to the mosque.
lessons: Ibn Umar's example of adapting his worship according to his age and physical capacity, while still following the Prophetic example as best he could, teaches that Islam accommodates human limitation with wisdom and mercy.
The unanimous agreement of scholars on preferred acts of worship, derived from careful study of the Prophet's practice, demonstrates how the tradition preserves guidance for every generation.

Observing and learning from the Prophet's actions, as the Companions did firsthand, underscores the importance of sitting with knowledgeable teachers and studying the Seerah to understand how to live one's faith.
practical_advice: When physical limitations prevent you from fulfilling a religious practice at its ideal level, do what you are able — God and His Prophet's example accommodate human weakness with grace, not condemnation.

Seek knowledge from established scholarly tradition when confused about religious practice; clarity and peace of mind come from understanding the evidence behind rulings rather than acting in uncertainty.

Reflect on the care with which the Companions preserved and transmitted every detail of the Prophet's worship — let this motivate you to be intentional and attentive in your own acts of worship rather than performing them mechanically.

title: The Prophet's Counsel to Umar: Do Not Jostle at the Black Stone, Lest You Harm the Weak
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) advised Umar ibn al-Khattab, known for his strength, not to push at the Black Stone during circumambulation for fear of harming weaker pilgrims, instructing him to salute it from a distance or say 'Allahu Akbar' if the way was not clear.
story: For the Messenger of God (SAAS) spoke as follows to Ibn 'Vmar's father, as quoted by Aḥmad in his musnad collection, "Waʼid' related to us, quoting Sufyan, from Abu Ya'fur al-'AbdJ, who said, 'I heard a sheikh in Mecca during the reign of al-Ḥajjaj quote from 'Vmar b. al-Khaṭṭab as having said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, "'Vmar, you are a strong man. Don't jostle at the stone, perhaps harming someone who is weak; if you should find the way to it clear, then salute it. Otherwise, face it and say Allahu Akbar 'God is most Great!'."'" This is an excellent line of transmission; however, the narrator who quotes 'Vmar is unknown and is not named. It is apparent, however, that this ḥadith is trustworthy and important. For al-Shafi'ī narrated it from Sufyan b. 'Vyayna, from Abu Ya'fur al-'AbdJ, his name being Waqdan, (who said), "I heard a man of Khuza'a say, following the killing of Ibn al-Zubayr who had been ruler of Mecca, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) said to 'Vmar, "Abu Ḥafṣ, you are a strong man, so don't jostle at the Corner in case you harm someone weak. However, if you find the way clear, then salute it; otherwise say, 'God is most Great', then pass on."'"
lessons: The Prophet's instruction to Umar — one of the strongest and most powerful Companions — to restrain his strength out of care for the weak reveals that true power in Islam is expressed through protection of the vulnerable, not dominance over them.
Acts of worship are not to be performed at the expense of others; the spirit of Hajj is mercy and brotherhood, and harming fellow worshippers undermines the very purpose of the pilgrimage.

When the ideal form of an act of worship is inaccessible due to circumstances, an alternative is always provided — saluting from a distance or saying Allahu Akbar — teaching that God's religion is built on ease and not hardship.
practical_advice: In any communal setting — whether at worship, work, or in public — use your strength, resources, or position to protect and make space for those who are weaker, rather than pushing past them to secure your own advantage.

When you cannot fulfill a goal or act of devotion in its ideal form, do not abandon it entirely — find the alternative that is within your reach, just as the Prophet taught Umar to say 'Allahu Akbar' when the stone was unreachable.

Regularly ask yourself whether your actions, even well-intentioned ones, are causing harm to those around you — the Prophet's concern for the weak pilgrim amid the sacred rites of Hajj shows that mindfulness of others is itself an act of worship.

title: The Prophet's Practice of Al-Ramal (Jogging) During Tawaf and Its Origins
summary: Ibn Abbas clarifies the historical context of the Prophet's jogging during Tawaf, explaining it was a strategic response to Quraysh's taunts at al-Hudaybiyya and not a permanent sunna. This account reconciles apparent contradictions in the hadith narrations about the Prophet's practice.
story: Abu Da'ud stated that Abu Salama MUsa related to him, quoting I:Iammad, quoting Abu 'li.~im aI-Ghanawl, from Abu al-Tufayl, who stated, "I said to Ibn 'Abbas, 'Your people claim that the Messenger of God (SAAS) moved at a jog at the ka'ba, and that that was his sunna, his "orthodox practice"." He replied, 'They both spoke the truth and lied.' I asked, 'In what did they speak the truth, and in what lie?' He replied, 'They spoke the truth by saying that the Messenger of God (SAAS) moved at a jog, but lied in that it was not a sunna. Quraysh said at aI-I:Iudaybiyya, 'Just leave Mu1)ammad and his Companions alone to die of worms!' And then they made peace with him on the basis of his making the pilgrimage the following year and agreeing to them staying in Mecca for three days. And then the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived while the unbelievers were over in front of Mt. Qu'ayqi'au. And the Messenger of God (SAAS) told his Companions, 'Move at a jog three times around the ka'ba.' This was not a sunna.'
lessons: Context matters deeply in understanding religious practice — what appears to be a rule may have been a response to a specific circumstance, teaching us not to adopt practices blindly without understanding their origins.

The Prophet responded to hostility and mockery not with retaliation but with dignified strength, demonstrating physical vitality to counter the enemy's wishful thinking about his community's weakness.
Scholars like Ibn Abbas modeled intellectual honesty by saying 'they spoke truth and lied' — teaching that nuanced understanding is more valuable than oversimplified answers.
practical_advice: When you feel mocked or dismissed by others, channel that energy into demonstrating your strength and resilience through action rather than argument.
Before adopting a practice or belief as absolute, research its context — many misunderstandings in life come from applying situational responses as permanent rules.
If someone challenges your understanding of something important to you, respond as Ibn Abbas did: with calm precision, distinguishing what is true from what is false rather than becoming defensive.

title: Aisha's Menstruation During Hajj and Her Request for Separate Umra
summary: Aisha began her pilgrimage intending umra but menstruated, causing her distress as others completed their rituals. The Prophet guided her to combine hajj and umra, then later allowed her to perform a separate umra from al-Tan'im to ease her concern.
story: The same happened to 'A'isha, 'the mother of the Believers'. She was one of those who recited the talbiyya for an 'umra since she had not brought a hadi with her. When she menstruated, the Messenger of God (SAAS) told her to wash and to recite the talbiyya for a hajj with her 'umra. And so she performed the hajj al-qiran. When they returned from Mina, she asked that he allow her to perform an 'umra after the hajj and the Messenger of God (SAAS) did so in order to please her, as is asserted in the hadith. Muslim narrated from a hadith of Ibn Jurayj, quoting Abu al-Zubayr, who said that he heard Jabir say, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) went in to see 'A'isha, she was weeping. He asked her, "Why are you crying?" "I am crying because the people have declared their ihram concluded, while I have not, and because they have circumambulated the ka'ba and I have not. And now is the time for this hajj." He stated, "This is a matter that God has decreed for Adam's daughters. Wash yourself and recite the talbiyya for a hajj." She stated, "And so I did that. When I had cleansed myself, he said, 'Circumambulate the ka'ba, and between al-Safa and al-Marwa, and then your state of ihram for the hajj will be concluded, as well as your 'umra.'" She asked, "Messenger of God, I am concerned about my 'umra and that I did not circumambulate until I had performed the hajj." He said, "Go with her, 'Abd al-Rahman, and let her make the 'umra from al-Tan'im."'
lessons: Hardship and physical vulnerability are part of the human condition; even those closest to the Prophet experienced unexpected difficulties during sacred worship.
A compassionate leader or companion does not dismiss grief but acknowledges it, offers a solution, and then goes further to address the emotional wound beneath the practical problem.
God's mercy accommodates human limitation; what appears to be a barrier to worship often has a divinely guided path around it.
practical_advice: When an unexpected obstacle prevents you from completing something meaningful, pause before despairing — seek counsel from a knowledgeable and caring person who may show you a way forward you had not considered.
If someone you love is grieving over something they feel they have missed or lost, resist the urge to minimize their feelings; first acknowledge the pain, then offer the practical remedy.

After a difficult experience in which your plans were disrupted, consider performing an act of devotion specifically to bring closure to that chapter, as Aisha's separate umra brought her peace of heart.

title: The Prophet's Announcement at al-Marwa: 'I Would Not Have Brought the Hadi'
summary: At the conclusion of his seventh circumambulation at al-Marwa during the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet openly admitted that had he known what he came to know, he would not have brought his sacrificial animal and would have made his pilgrimage a simple umra, then instructed those without hadi to do exactly that.
story: Jabir stated in his hadith, 'until, at the conclusion of his circumambulation, at al-Marwa, he (the Prophet (SAAS)) said, "If I had known beforehand what I came to learn, I would not have brought the hadi."' Muslim narrated this. Ahmad stated in his account in the hadith of Jabir, 'When it was the seventh circumambulation, at al-Marwa, he (the Messenger of God (SAAS)) said, "O people, if I had known beforehand what I came to learn, I would not have brought the hadi and I would have made it an 'umra. Those who have not brought a hadi with them may declare their ihram concluded and they should make it an 'umra." And so all the people did declare their ihram concluded.' Muslim stated, 'And so all the people did declare their ihram concluded and removed their hair, except for the Prophet (SAAS), and those who had brought hadis with them.'
lessons: True wisdom includes the humility to acknowledge, even publicly, that one would have chosen differently with the knowledge one now possesses — this is not weakness but integrity.
The Prophet's willingness to share his own reflection encouraged his Companions to embrace a mercy they might otherwise have felt unworthy of accepting.
Religious guidance is not rigid performance for its own sake; when a lighter path is permitted, taking it is itself an act of following the Prophet's example.
practical_advice: When you realize mid-journey that a different approach would have served you better, do not let pride prevent you from adjusting course or advising others to benefit from what you have learned.
Be honest with yourself and others about the limits of your foresight; admitting 'I would have done this differently' builds trust and models intellectual honesty.
If you are carrying an unnecessary burden out of habit or misunderstanding, give yourself permission to set it down once you learn that the path is easier than you thought.

title: Companions Ordered to Convert Hajj to Umra on the 4th of Dhu al-Hijja
summary: When the Prophet and his Companions arrived in Makkah on the morning of the 4th of Dhu al-Hijja having recited the talbiyya for hajj alone, the Prophet instructed those without a sacrificial animal to convert their intention to umra and conclude their ihram, including the permission to return to their wives — news that spread with some controversy among the people.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Abu al-Nu'man related to him, quoting Hammad b. Zayd, from 'Abd al-Malik b. Jurayj, from 'Ata', from Jabir. And from Tawus, from Ibn 'Abbas. They both stated, 'The Prophet (SAAS) and his Companions arrived on the morning of the 4th day of Dhu al-Hijja, reciting the talbiyya for the hajj, mixing in nothing with it. When we arrived, he gave us orders and we made it an 'umra. And he declared that our ihram was concluded (thus allowing us to go) to our wives. Gossip about this spread thereafter.'
lessons: Instructions that challenge established expectations can cause confusion and even controversy, but trusting in prophetic guidance over personal assumption is the mark of a sincere believer.
The Prophet's directives were always aimed at mercy and ease for his community, even when they seemed surprising or unconventional to those receiving them.
Community gossip often arises around acts of permitted ease because people mistake strictness for piety; true piety lies in following sound guidance, not in making things harder than they need to be.
practical_advice: When a trusted authority gives you guidance that contradicts your expectations, take time to understand the reasoning before dismissing or resisting it.
If you find yourself the subject of gossip for doing something that is genuinely permitted and right, hold firm — the discomfort of others' disapproval does not make a permitted action wrong.
Seek clarity about religious or ethical obligations from knowledgeable sources rather than relying on rumor or popular assumption, especially in unfamiliar situations.

title: The Prophet's Arrival at Mecca and Orders to Conclude Ihram
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) arrived in Mecca on the morning of the 4th of Dhul-Hijja and ordered Companions who had not brought sacrificial animals to convert their hajj into an Umra and conclude their ihram, causing some distress among those who wished to follow his example.
story: knows best. AI-Bukhari stated that Abu al-Nu'man related to him, quoting I;Iammad b. Zayd, from 'Abd aI-Malik b. Jurayj, from 'Atii', from Jabir. And from Tawus, from Ibn 'Abbas. They both stated, "The Prophet (SAAS) and his Companions arrived on the morning of the 4th day of Dhu aI-I;Iijja, reciting the talbiyya for the bajj, mixing in nothing with it. When we arrived, he gave us orders and we made it an 'umra. And he declared that ouribram was concluded (thus aIlowing us to go) to our wives. Gossip about this spread thereafter." 'Atil' went on to quote ]abir as saying, "So one of us might go off to Mina with his penis damp from sperm!" He went on, gesturing, "This reached the Prophet (SAAS), and he said, 'It has reached me that there are people saying such-and-such. I swear by God, I am more innocent, more pious towards God than they are! If I had known beforehand what I came to learn, I would not have made sacrifice. And if I had not had a hadi I would have declared my ibram concluded.' "Suraqa b. Ju'shum then arose and said, 'Messenger of God, is this just for us, or for ever?' 'For ever,' he replied." Muslim stated that Qutayba related to him, quoting aI-Layth - he being Ibn Sa'd - from Abu al-Zubayr, who quoted Jabir as saying, "We arrived aIong with the Messenger of God (SAAS), reciting the talbiyya for a bajj alone. 'A'isha arrived for an 'umra. When we were at Saraf she menstruated. Then when we reached (Mecca) we circumambulated the ka'ba, al-Safa and al-Marwa, and the Messenger of God (SAAS), told those of us who had not brought a bad; to declare our ibram concluded. 'To what extent?' we asked. 'Totally,' he replied. And so we had sexual relations with our wives, put on perfume and dressed in clothes. Only four nights remained from that time until (the ceremonies on) 'Arafat."
lessons: The Prophet (SAAS) demonstrated humility by acknowledging that had he known the impact of his decision on his Companions, he would have chosen differently — true leadership includes empathy for those you lead.
When facing confusion about religious rulings, returning to the source and asking directly — as Suraqa did — brings clarity and removes doubt.

Divine wisdom sometimes places us in circumstances different from what we would choose; accepting God's plan with trust, as the Prophet did in retaining his ihram due to his hadi, is itself an act of worship.
practical_advice: When you feel confused or anxious about a decision others around you seem to handle differently, remember that your unique circumstances may require a unique path — do not measure your journey by someone else's.
If gossip or social pressure causes you anxiety, reflect on the Prophet's response: he grounded himself in his own integrity and piety rather than being destabilized by what others said.
When a religious or life decision causes unintended distress to those around you, acknowledge their feelings openly and honestly as the Prophet did, rather than dismissing them.

title: The Debate Over Hajj al-Tamattu' vs Hajj al-Qiran
summary: Scholars debated the relative merits of hajj al-tamattu' and hajj al-qiran based on the Prophet's words of regret during the Farewell Pilgrimage, with Imam Ahmad concluding that hajj al-tamattu' is superior for those not bringing a sacrificial animal.
story: These two abad;th state clearly that the Prophet (SAAS) came to Mecca the year of the bijjat al-wada' on the morning of the fourth of Dhu al-I:Iijja. That was a Sunday, when daytime came, at dawn, because the first day of Dhu al-I:Iijja that year was indisputably a Thursday, because the day for (the congregation at) 'Araft was Friday, according to the text of the bad;th of'Umar b. aI-Khanab that is firmly established in both ~ab;b collections. As we will show. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived on Sunday, the fourth of that month, he began, as we have related, with circumambulation of the ka'ba then he made the al-sa'y between aI-Safa and aI-Marwa. When he completed his circumambulation between the two, at al-Marwa, he ordered those who had not brought had;s with them to declare their ibram decisively concluded. He required that of them definitely, and they did so. Some of them were regretful that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had not declared his ibram concluded because he had brought the had;. They had wanted to conform to his practice and would have found solace in this. When he perceived how they felt, he said to them, "If I had known beforehand what I came to learn, I would not have brought the had; and would have made it an 'umra." That is, "If I had known that this would upset you, I would have left bringing the had; until after I had declared my ibriim concluded, as you have done." From this the evidence might seem clear to you of the superiority of the bajj al-tamattu', as Imam AI.unad maintained on the basis of this. He stated, "I do not doubt that the Messenger of God (SAAS), performed the bajj al-qiriin; however, thebajj al-tamattu' is superior, since he (the Prophet (SAAS» expressed his regret at it." The response to this would be as follows: the Prophet (SAAS) did not express regret over the bajj al-tamattu' because of its superiority over the bajj al-qiriin, for those who had brought a had;. He expressed regret because then he would not have upset his Companions by retaining his ibram state and by telling them to declare their ibriim concluded. Therefore - but God knows best - when Imam AI.unad contemplated this "secret", he gave the text of another bad;th from the Prophet (SAAS), to the effect that the bajj al-tamattu' is best for those not bringing a had;, because of the latter's order to those of his Companions who had not brought had;s to perform the bajj al-tamattu', and that the bajj al-qiriin would be preferable for those having brought had;s, just as God, Almighty and Glorious is He, chose for His Prophet (SAAS), on the bijjat al-wada' and His order to him in that regard, as related above. But God knows best.
lessons: Scholarly disagreement conducted with sincerity and humility, as demonstrated by Imam Ahmad's careful reasoning, is a mercy — it shows that Islam accommodates genuine inquiry and nuanced understanding.
The Prophet's concern was not for religious superiority but for the emotional wellbeing of his Companions; compassion for others is itself a form of piety.
When facing difficult or complex decisions, seek understanding of the underlying wisdom rather than surface-level rules alone — context and intention matter deeply in Islamic guidance.
practical_advice: When you feel lost or confused by differing opinions, look for the underlying principle of compassion and wisdom rather than getting overwhelmed by competing views.
If you find yourself in a situation where your path differs from those around you, take comfort in the fact that different circumstances can call for different but equally valid responses.
Approach religious questions with the humility of the great scholars — hold your conclusions with confidence but remain open to the deeper wisdom that context reveals.

title: The Prophet Stays at al-Abtah Outside Mecca Without Returning to the Ka'ba
summary: After completing his initial circumambulation and sa'y, the Prophet (SAAS) stopped at al-Abtah outside Mecca and remained there from Sunday through Thursday morning, praying with his Companions without returning to the Ka'ba until departing for Arafat.
story: Thereafter, the Messenger of God (SAAS), having fmished his circumambulation between aI-Safa and al-Marwa and having ordered those who had not brought had,s to conclude their pilgrimage, left in the company of those with him and stopped at al-Abtal;1, to the east of Mecca. He stayed there for the remainder of that Sunday and the Monday, Tuesday and Wednesday, until he had performed the ~ubft prayer of Thursday. Throughout all those days he prayed there with his Companions without returning to the ka'ba. Al-Bukhiirl has a chapter entitled, Chapter of those who do not approach the ka'ba and do not circumambulate before going out to 'Arafat and then return after the first circumambulation. He went on to state therein that Mul;1ammad b. Abu Bakr narrated to him, quoting Fu<Jayl b. Sulayman, quoting Musa b. 'Uqba, as saying, "Kurayb quoted 'Abd Allah b. 'Abbas as having said to him, 'The Prophet (SAAS) came to Mecca, performed seven circumambulations and the sa'y between ai-sara and aI-Marwa. He did not approach the ka'ba after his circumambulation until he returned from 'Arafat.'" Al-Bukharl is alone in giving this.
lessons: The Prophet's deliberate pause at al-Abtah before the journey to Arafat teaches that spiritual preparation often requires stillness and patience before the great moments of worship.
Not every act of devotion requires constant motion — sometimes remaining present with your community in a place of waiting is itself a profound form of worship.
Following the Sunnah in its specific details, even those that seem minor like where to rest during Hajj, connects the believer to a sacred chain of practice across centuries.
practical_advice: Before major commitments or important events in your life, allow yourself deliberate periods of stillness and preparation rather than rushing forward anxiously.
When you feel the urge to constantly do more in worship or work, remember that patient waiting and being present with your community also has deep spiritual value.
Use periods of waiting — whether in life transitions or in worship — as opportunities for reflection, prayer, and grounding yourself in gratitude.

title: Ali's Arrival from Yemen and His Ihram Question
summary: Ali ibn Abi Talib arrived from Yemen during the Farewell Pilgrimage and found his wife Fatima had concluded her ihram on the Prophet's instruction; when he went to clarify with the Prophet, he was told to maintain his ihram because he had brought sacrificial animals.
story: During this tirue, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was staying at al-Batl;1a, outside Mecca, 'All arrived from Yemen. The Prophet (SAAS) had sent him - as we narrated above - to Yemen as its governor after Khalid b. al-Walld, may God be pleased with them both. When 'All arrived, he found that his wife Fa~ma, the daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS), had declared her iftram concluded, as had the wives of the latter, who had not brought had,s. Fatima had put on koftl and dressed in colour-dyed clothing. 'All asked her, "Who told you to do this?" "My father," she replied. Somewhat annoyed at her, he then went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and told him that she had declared her iftram concluded, had dressed in colour-dyed clothes and put on koft!. He told him, "And she claims that you told her to do that, Messenger of God." He replied, "She spoke the truth." He repeated this three times. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked him, "With what intent did you recite the talbiyya when you determined upon the ftajj?" He replied, "With the same intent as the Messenger of God," he replied. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then told him, "1 have brought the had, with me, so do not declare your iftram concluded." The number of had,s 'All had brought from Yemen and the Messenger of God (SAAS) had brought with him from Medina and had bought on the way totalled 100 camels. They shared all the hadjs between them. All this is related in the ,al,zjl,z of Muslim, may God have mercy upon him, and has been given above. This report refutes the account related by the l,zafi:;, Abu al-Qilsim al-Tabarani, may God have mercy upon him, from a l,zadjth ofqkrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, to the effect that 'Ali met with the Prophet (SAAS) at al-Ju1;tfa. But God knows best.
lessons: Ali's momentary annoyance followed by his immediate recourse to the Prophet (SAAS) for clarification demonstrates that when emotions cloud our judgment, turning to a trusted authority for truth is the mature response.
The Prophet's calm and thrice-repeated confirmation — 'She spoke the truth' — shows that a leader defends those who act rightly even when others come to him with complaints.
Linking your intentions to those of a righteous guide, as Ali did in his talbiyya, is a powerful spiritual act — aligning your heart's direction with those who walk closest to God.
practical_advice: When you feel confused or even irritated by what someone close to you has done, pause before reacting and seek clarification from a reliable source rather than assuming wrongdoing.
If you discover you have misdirected frustration toward someone who was actually acting correctly, let the truth settle your heart quickly and move forward without extended guilt.

When you are uncertain about the right course of action in an important matter, declare your intentions aligned with those who are closest to righteousness — this protects you from misstep and connects you to blessing.

title: Abu Musa's Hajj al-Tamattu' and His Later Legal Caution Under Umar
summary: Abu Musa al-Ash'ari, who arrived with Ali but without a sacrificial animal, was ordered by the Prophet to conclude his ihram and perform hajj al-tamattu'; he later followed this ruling in his legal decisions until he deferred to Umar ibn al-Khattab's differing opinion out of respect.
story: Abu Musa was among those who arrived with 'Ali; however, he himself did not bring a hadj and so the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered him to declare his il,zram concluded after he had circumambulated for the 'umra and had made the sa'y. He then revoked his l,zajj into an 'umra and performed the l,zajj al-tamattu'. He would use that experience in issuing his legal decision during the caliphate of 'Omar b. al-Khanab. When 'Omar b. al-Khanab gave his opinion that the l,zajj (alone) should be made quite separate from the 'umra, he (Abu Musa) abandoned his legal position out of respect for the Commander of the Believers, 'Omar, may God be pleased with him and grant him pleasure.
lessons: Abu Musa's deference to Umar despite holding a sound opinion based on direct prophetic instruction illustrates that wisdom sometimes chooses harmony and respect over insisting on being right.
Personal experience of prophetic guidance is a treasure — Abu Musa carried this lived knowledge and shared it faithfully throughout his life as a scholar and judge.
Respecting leadership and authority, even when you hold a valid alternative view, can itself be an expression of faith and communal responsibility.
practical_advice: When you are in disagreement with a leader or authority figure whose overall guidance you trust, consider whether maintaining harmony serves a greater good than winning the argument in that moment.
Draw on your own lived experiences of guidance and wisdom when helping others — your personal knowledge, honestly shared, is one of the most valuable things you can offer.
Hold your convictions with confidence but without rigidity; the willingness to defer gracefully when appropriate is a sign of strength, not weakness.

title: Bilal's Adhan at al-Abtah and the Prophet Praying with a Lance
summary: At al-Abtah during the Farewell Pilgrimage, Bilal gave the call to prayer turning his face in all directions, and the Prophet (SAAS) prayed facing a short lance planted in the ground while dressed in a red garment, with people, women, dogs and donkeys passing without disrupting the prayer.
story: Imam A1;tmad stated that 'Abd al-Razzaq related to him, quoting Sufyan, from 'Awn b. Abu Ju1;tayfa, from his father, who said, "I saw Bilal make the call to prayer and circulate all around, and turn his face everywhere, his fmgers in his ears. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was in a red pavilion he had, made of leather, I believe. Then Bilal went out ahead of the Messenger of God (SAAS), carrying a short lance. He set it up and the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the prayer." 'Abd al-Razzaq stated, "And I heard him in Mecca say that at al-Bat1;ta' dogs, women and donkeys would pass in front of him. He was dressed in a red l,zulla, 'outer garment', beneath which I thought I could see the sheen of his legs." He said, "Sufyan, we think it (what he wore) was a l,zibara, 'a shawl'." A1;tmad stated that Waki' related to him, quoting Sufyan, from 'Awn b. Abu Ju1;tayfa, from his father, who said, "I went to the Prophet (SAAS) at al-Abta\:t while he was in a red pavilion he had. Bilal came out with the residue of (the water from the ablution) sprinkling it (on those around him). "Bilal made the call to prayer and I watched as his mouth moved first here, then there" - meaning to the right and the left. "Then the 'anaza, 'a short lance', was set up, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) came out, dressed in a red jubba" - or a red l,zulla - "and I thought I could see the sheen of his legs. He performed the al-:;,uhr" - or the al-'av- "prayer, facing towards the 'anaza. Women, dogs and donkeys passed by without hindrance. He kept on performing prayers of two raka'at until he reached Medina." On one occasion, the account quotes the narrator as saying, "He performed the al-:;,uhr prayer of two raka'at and the al-'a,r of two raka'at." In both ,al,zjl,z collections this is given from an account of Sufyan al-Thawrl. A1;tmad also stated, that Mu1;tammad b. Ja'far related to him, quoting Shu'ba and I;Iajjaj, from al-I;Iakam, who quoted Abu Ju1;tayfa as saying, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) left and went to al-Bat1;ta'. He performed the ablution and the al-:;,uhr prayer with two raka'at with a short lance before him." 'Awn made an addition to this, quoting his father as quoting Abu Jui)ayfa as saying, "and there were donkeys and women passing behind us". I:Jajjaj stated in the !tadtth, "Then people began taking hold of his hand and wiping it over their faces. I took his hand and placed it on my face; I found it to be cooler than ice and more sweet-smelling than musk."
lessons: The Prophet's consistent, unhurried prayer amid the bustle of travel and open surroundings teaches that worship does not require perfect conditions — it requires a present heart.
Bilal's turning in all directions during the adhan to ensure everyone could hear reflects the Islamic principle of making the call to goodness as inclusive and far-reaching as possible.

The Companions' deep love for the Prophet — expressed in their eagerness to touch his blessed hand — reminds us that love for those who embody goodness is itself a spiritual act that draws us closer to God.
practical_advice: Do not wait for the perfect environment to pray, reflect, or practice your faith — establish your worship wherever you are, even in the midst of noise and movement.
Let the image of Bilal calling out in all directions inspire you to share goodness, encouragement, and truth widely, not just with those who are easy to reach.
When you are stressed or overwhelmed, recall moments of beauty and blessing — even something as simple as the memory of a cool, sweet-smelling hand — to reconnect with gratitude and calm.

title: The Prophet's Departure to Mina on the Day of Tarwiyya
summary: On the day of al-tarwiyya during the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) led the pilgrims from al-Abtah to Mina, performing prayers there and preparing for the journey to Arafat.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained, as we have stated above, at al-Abtah the Sunday, Monday, Tuesday and Wednesday. The people who had not brought hads had declared their ihram concluded. During that period, 'Ali b. Abu Talib arrived from Yemen with the Muslims accompanying him and the wealth he brought. The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not return to the ka'ba after he had circumambulated it. The morning of Thursday, the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the morning prayer, that day being that of al-tarwiyya. It is also called the 'Mina day' because (people) go there then. It has been narrated that the Prophet (SAAS) had made an address before that day. The previous day, as I have seen in various commentaries, is known as the yawm al-zina, 'decoration day', because on it the sacrificial animals are garlanded with ornaments and similar items. The Prophet (SAAS) then set off on his mount to Mina, before the noon - though some say thereafter. Those who had previously declared their ihram for the hajj concluded, now declared it in effect when they left al-Abtah for Mina. They did this as their mounts set off carrying them there. 'Abd al-Malik stated, quoting 'Ata', from Jabir b.'Abd Allah, who said, 'We arrived with the Messenger of God (SAAS), and declared our ihram in effect. When it was the day of the tarwiyya, and after we had turned our backs to Mecca, we recited the talbiyya for the hajj.' 'Ubayd b. Jurayj stated to Ibn 'Umar, 'I saw that when you were in Mecca and the people recited the talbiyya on seeing the crescent, you did not recite it until the day of al-tarwiyya.' He replied, 'I did not see the Prophet (SAAS) recite the talbiyya until his camel set off bearing him.' Imam Ahmad stated, 'Someone who saw the Prophet (SAAS) informed me that he went to Mina on al-tarwiyya day with Bilal at his side carrying a pole on which was a piece of cloth with which he was sheltering the Messenger of God (SAAS). (Sheltering him) from the heat, that is.' All the people declared their ihram at an end and clipped (their hair) except for the Prophet (SAAS), and those who had brought hadis with them. And when it was the tarwiyya day, they set off for Mina and recited the talbiyya for the hajj. The Messenger of God (SAAS) rode and performed the al-zuhr, the al-asr, the al-maghrib, the al-isha and the al-fajr there. He then stayed a little longer until the sun arose and he called for a pavilion of haircloth he had and it was set up for him at Namira.
lessons: Following the Sunnah precisely in acts of worship brings peace and unity to the community of believers.
Dedication to sacred rituals, even in the details of when and how to recite the talbiyya, reflects the depth of one's commitment to God.
Leadership by example — the Prophet's personal conduct during hajj guided generations of Muslims on the correct practice of worship.
practical_advice: When performing acts of worship, focus on following the established guidance carefully rather than rushing through the motions, as mindfulness deepens spiritual experience.
Use moments of physical journeying — whether in hajj or in daily commutes — as opportunities to engage in remembrance of God.
Learn the details of your religious obligations from reliable sources, just as the companions memorized the Prophet's actions precisely, so your worship is grounded and confident.

title: The Prophet's Address at Arafat: Sacred Month and Land
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) made a public address at Arafat through an announcer, declaring the sanctity of Muslim blood and property as sacred as the holy month and land.
story: Muḥammad b. Isḥāq stated that Yaḥyā b. 'Abbād b. 'Abd Allāh b. al-Zubayr quoted his father 'Abbād as saying, "The man who used to make announcements to the people relating to the pronouncements of the Messenger of God (SAAS), when he was on 'Arafat, was Rabī'a b. Umayya b. Khalaf. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said (to him), 'Say: "O people, the Messenger of God says, 'Do you know what month this is?'" They will say, "The holy months." Then say to them, "God has made your blood and your possessions sacrosanct for you, just as is this month of yours." Then say, "O people, the Messenger of God asks you, 'Do you know what land this is?'"'" And he went on to complete the ḥadīth as above.
lessons: The sanctity of human life and property is a divine principle, as important as the holy months and sacred land — a reminder to treat others with the utmost respect and care.
Public proclamation of truth and justice is a prophetic responsibility; speaking up for what is right honors the covenant with God.
Divine law transcends time and place — the principles declared at Arafat were meant for all generations, reminding us that moral boundaries are not arbitrary but God-given.
practical_advice: When you feel your rights or dignity have been violated, remind yourself that God has declared these sacred — seek justice calmly and through proper channels, knowing divine backing is with the oppressed.
Practice declaring your values openly in your community, even in small ways — speaking truth and upholding ethics mirrors the prophetic example.
Reflect on the sanctity of those around you: before speaking harshly or acting unjustly toward another person, pause and remember that their honor and wellbeing are as sacred as holy ground.

title: The Prophet's Address at Arafat: Inheritance, Lineage, and Divine Warnings
summary: Standing at Arafat on the day of the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet (SAAS) declared key rulings on inheritance, lineage, and stern warnings against false claims of parentage or patronage.
story: Muḥammad b. Isḥāq proceeded to state that Layth b. Abu Sulaym related to him, from Shahr b. Ḥawshab, from 'Amr b. Khārija, who said, "'Attāb b. Usayd sent me to the Messenger of God (SAAS), for a purpose while he was standing upon 'Arafat. I gave him the message, then stood below his camel. (I was so close that) its saliva was dripping upon my head. I heard him say, 'O people, God has assigned to each person his portion. It is not permissible to make a bequest to an heir. The child relates to the bed.' And stoning is for fornicators. Anyone claiming as his father someone who is not, or a freed-man claiming as his master someone who is not, the curse of God, the angels and all men is upon them. God will not accept for him any penance, nor any requital.'"
lessons: God's distribution of what each soul receives is perfect and just — coveting what belongs to others or manipulating inheritance corrupts the social fabric and invites divine displeasure.
Honesty about one's origins and loyalties is a matter of spiritual integrity; false claims of lineage or belonging are grave moral failings.
Even in proximity to sacred moments and holy places, accountability before God remains absolute — closeness to ritual does not erase ethical responsibility.
practical_advice: If you are dealing with a difficult family situation around inheritance or rights, seek clarity through lawful means and trust that God's portions are wisely assigned — resist the urge to grasp for more than what is rightfully yours.

Reflect on whether there are any false fronts you present to others — about your background, affiliations, or identity — and work toward greater authenticity, knowing that truthfulness before God matters more than social appearances.

When you feel that life has been unfair in what it has given you compared to others, return to the principle that God has assigned each person their portion — practice gratitude for your own and release envy of others'.

title: The Man Who Fell from His Camel at Arafat
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage at Arafat, a man in the state of ihram fell from his camel and died; the Prophet (SAAS) gave specific instructions for his burial and declared he would be resurrected in the state of talbiyya.
story: Al-Bukhārī stated that Sulaymān b. Ḥarb related to him, quoting Ḥammād b. Zayd, from Ayyūb, from Sa'īd b. Jubayr, from Ibn 'Abbās, who said, "A man standing with the Prophet (SAAS) at 'Arafat fell off his camel and broke his neck" -- or his words were, "and it broke his neck". "The Prophet (SAAS) said, 'Wash him with water boiled with wood of the lote tree, wrap him in a double shroud. Do not perfume him, cover his head or embalm him. God will resurrect him on Judgement Day, (and he will be) mulabbiyyan, "expressing his readiness to serve God".'" Muslim narrated it from Abu al-Rabī' al-Zahrānī, from Ḥammād b. Zayd.
lessons: Death can come at any moment, even during the most sacred acts of worship — the best preparation for death is to be found in a state of devotion and obedience to God.
The Prophet's compassionate and immediate guidance in the face of sudden death models how believers should respond to loss: with calm, care, and adherence to divine instruction rather than panic.
A soul that dies while engaged in worship is honored by God — the man's state of ihram was preserved even into his resurrection, a powerful reminder of the eternal weight of sincere devotion.
practical_advice: Use this story as a prompt to reflect on your own state of readiness — not with morbid anxiety, but with the healthy awareness that living each day in a state of worship and goodness is the most meaningful way to live.
When someone around you experiences sudden loss or tragedy, model the prophetic response: act with calm, follow the proper rites, and focus on dignifying the person rather than succumbing to panic.
If you are grieving someone who died during an act of worship or good deeds, find comfort in the Prophet's words — God honors those who depart while striving in His way.

title: Combining the Zuhr and Asr Prayers at Arafat
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet (SAAS) combined the Zuhr and Asr prayers at Arafat after delivering his address, establishing this as a Sunnah practice for pilgrims.
story: Jabir related this similarly in his ḥadīth after he gave the address quoted above. He went on, "Bilal then made the call to prayer followed by the iqama. Thereupon he (the Prophet (SAAS)) arose and performed the al-ẓuhr prayer, and after that arose again and performed the al-'aṣr, and he did not combine any other prayer between these two." Abu Dā'ūd stated that Aḥmad b. Ḥanbal related to him, quoting Ya'qūb, quoting Abu 'Awf, from Ibn Isḥāq, from Nāfi', from Ibn 'Umar, (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) left Mina early, having performed the al-ṣubḥ prayer, on the morning of the day for (the assembly on) Mt. 'Arafat. He stopped at Namira, at the station of the imam, where the latter would stay at 'Arafat, until, at the time of the al-ẓuhr prayer, he left as a muhājjir and he combined together the al-ẓuhr and the al-'aṣr prayers.
lessons: The Prophet's practice of combining prayers during pilgrimage reflects the spirit of ease and mercy that Islam brings to worship — God does not intend hardship for the believer.
Following the Sunnah precisely, even in procedural details like prayer timing, is a form of love and connection to the Prophet (SAAS) and the community of believers across time.
Sacred occasions call for focused presence — combining prayers at Arafat allowed the Companions to devote their full attention to worship and remembrance without fragmentation.
practical_advice: When life becomes demanding or you are traveling and find prayer difficult to maintain in its usual form, remember that Islam provides sanctioned accommodations — use them without guilt, as they are prophetic mercy.

Look for ways to structure your day so that essential spiritual practices are protected and prioritized, even when circumstances are unusual, just as the Prophet structured the entire Arafat day around worship.
Reflect on how combining two acts of worship into a focused, uninterrupted time can sometimes deepen your spiritual experience more than performing them separately under stress.

title: Whether the Prophet Fasted on the Day of Arafat
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, companions disagreed over whether the Prophet (SAAS) was fasting on the day of Arafat; Umm al-Fadl resolved the question by sending him milk, which he drank publicly.
story: Al-Bukhārī stated that 'Abd Allāh b. Yūsuf informed him, quoting Mālik, from Abu al-Naḍr, the freed-man of 'Umar b. 'Ubayd Allāh, from 'Umayr, freed-man of Ibn 'Abbās, from Umm al-Faḍl, daughter of al-Ḥārith, (who said), "People disputed in my presence on 'Arafat day concerning the fast of the Prophet (SAAS). Some people said, 'He is fasting.' Others said, 'He is not fasting.' So I sent a vessel of milk to him while he was standing up on his camel, and he drank it." Muslim narrated it from a ḥadīth of Mālik also. Imām Aḥmad stated that 'Abd al-Razzāq and Abu Bakr both said that Ibn Jurayj informed him that 'Aṭā' stated, "'Abd Allāh b. 'Abbās invited al-Faḍl b. 'Abbās to eat on 'Arafat day. He said, 'But I am fasting.' 'Abd Allāh told him, 'Don't fast. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was brought a vessel containing milk on 'Arafat day and he drank from it. So do not fast. The people are waiting for you.'"
lessons: When there is genuine uncertainty about religious practice, the example of the companions shows that seeking clarification through direct observation or reliable transmission is the right approach — not guesswork or assumption.
The Prophet's action of drinking publicly resolved the dispute without lengthy debate — sometimes actions speak more clearly than words in matters of practice and leadership.

Following the Sunnah means understanding the spirit behind rulings, not just their letter — the companions used this event to actively guide others away from unnecessary hardship on a day meant for worship.
practical_advice: When you are confused about the right thing to do in a religious or ethical situation, seek reliable guidance from authentic sources rather than letting doubt paralyze you or cause division.

On significant days of worship or commemoration, be mindful of whether extra practices you are adding are truly beneficial or whether they might be draining energy you need for the day's primary purpose.
When others around you are uncertain or disagreeing, consider whether you have knowledge or clarity that could calmly resolve the situation — sharing what you know with kindness is a form of service.

title: Ibn Umar Urges Al-Hajjaj to Follow the Sunnah at Arafat
summary: On the day of Arafat, Ibn Umar firmly and impatiently urged the governor al-Hajjaj to depart promptly in accordance with the Sunnah, demonstrating the companions' fierce commitment to preserving prophetic practice.
story: Al-Bukhārī stated that 'Abd Allāh b. Masalama related to him, quoting Mālik, from Ibn Shihāb, from Sālim b. 'Abd Allāh (who said) "'Abd al-Malik b. Marwān wrote to al-Ḥajjāj b. Yūsuf to the effect that he suggest to 'Abd Allāh b. 'Umar that he make the ḥajj. When it was the day for 'Arafat, Ibn 'Umar - along with myself - when the sun had begun to incline - or the narrator's words were 'when the sun had set' - 'and we were near al-Ḥajjāj's tent, Ibn 'Umar shouted out, "Well, where is he?" Al-Ḥajjāj emerged. Ibn 'Umar told him, "Time to leave!" "Right now?" al-Ḥajjāj asked. "Yes," Ibn 'Umar replied. Al-Ḥajjāj asked, "Wait while I pour water over myself." Ibn 'Umar dismounted and walked about (impatiently) between myself and my father. I said to al-Ḥajjāj, "If you want to act precisely in accord with the sunna today, then you should make the address short and proceed quickly to the stay (on 'Arafat)." Ibn 'Umar commented, "He spoke the truth."'"
lessons: Preserving the Sunnah sometimes requires urgency and even boldness — the companions did not defer to worldly authority when it conflicted with prophetic practice.
True adherence to the prophetic way is not about rigid rule-following but about a deep, internalized love for the way of the Prophet (SAAS) that makes deviation feel genuinely uncomfortable.
Younger believers can speak truth to those in authority with wisdom and tact — Sālim's suggestion to al-Ḥajjāj was effective precisely because it was framed constructively.
practical_advice: When you see a religious or ethical principle being delayed or compromised for the sake of comfort or convenience, find a respectful but clear way to uphold it — the companions modeled this courage.
Reflect on whether there are areas of your own religious practice where comfort or habit has led you to delay or modify what is right — recommit to alignment with authentic guidance.
Practice speaking truthfully and constructively to people in positions of authority over you when matters of principle are at stake, framing your words as guidance rather than criticism.

title: Instructions for Burying the Ihram Pilgrim Who Dies
summary: The Prophet Muhammad gave specific instructions for washing and shrouding a pilgrim who dies while in the state of ihram, stating that God will resurrect him on Judgement Day still expressing his readiness to serve God.
story: The Prophet ($AAS) said, 'Wash him with water boiled with wood of the lote tree, wrap him in a double shroud. Do not perfume him, cover his head or embalm him. God will resurrect him on Judgement Day, (and he will be) mulabbiyyan, 'expressing his readiness to serve God'.' Muslim narrated it from Abu al-Rabi' al-Zahrani, from I;Iammad b. Zayd.
lessons: Even in death, a believer's spiritual state is preserved and honored by God — no act of sincere devotion goes unnoticed.
The rituals surrounding death in Islam affirm dignity and continuity: our devotion in this life shapes how we appear before God in the next.
Grief and loss are eased by the certainty that a believer who dies in worship is met by God's mercy and recognition.
practical_advice: When grieving the loss of a loved one, reflect on the Islamic teaching that those who die in worship are resurrected in that state — let this bring comfort rather than despair.
Honor the memory of those who passed by continuing acts of worship they valued, connecting your remembrance of them to gratitude for God's mercy.
In moments of sadness around death, recite the shahada or a simple dhikr to ground yourself in the belief that this life is a passage, not an end.

title: The Prophet's Declaration: 'The Hajj Is Arafat'
summary: When people from Najd asked the Prophet about Hajj, he declared that the essence of Hajj is standing at Arafat, and that anyone present there before dawn on the night of gathering has completed their Hajj.
story: Al-Nasa'i stated that Isḥaq b. Ibrahim - he being Ibn Rahawayh - informed him, quoting Waki', quoting Sufyan al-Thawri, from Bukayr b. 'Ata', from 'Abd al-Raḥman b. Ya'mur al-Dili, who said, 'I witnessed the Messenger of God ($AAS), at 'Arafat. People from Najd came to him and asked him about the Ḥajj. The Messenger of God ($AAS) said, 'The Ḥajj is 'Arafat; those who are there in the night at 'Arafat, before dawn on the night of the assemblage, will have concluded their Ḥajj.'
lessons: The heart of a great act of worship can sometimes be a single, sincere moment of presence — quality of devotion matters more than elaborate performance.
Clarity in religious guidance reduces anxiety; the Prophet's concise answer gave pilgrims certainty and ease.
Being present — physically and spiritually — in a sacred moment can define an entire journey of faith.
practical_advice: When feeling overwhelmed by religious obligations, seek out the core intention behind each act of worship and focus on that essence rather than every surrounding detail.
If you feel anxious about whether you have done 'enough' in worship, remember that God's criteria are often simpler and more merciful than our fears suggest.
Practice being fully present during your moments of prayer or dhikr, treating each as the 'Arafat' of your day — the moment that defines your spiritual standing.

title: The Prophet Instructs Pilgrims to Return to Their Inherited Shrines at Arafat
summary: The Prophet sent Ibn Mirba' al-Ansari to instruct pilgrims who had positioned themselves far from the station at Arafat to return to their proper places, reminding them of the heritage passed down from Prophet Ibrahim.
story: Al-Nasa'i stated that Qutayba informed him, quoting Sufyan, from 'Amr b. Dinar, quoting 'Amr b. 'Abd Allah b. Ṣafwan, who quoted Yazid b. Shayban as saying, 'We were positioned at 'Arafat in a place far removed from the station. Ibn Mirba' al-Anṣari came over to us and said, 'I am sent to you by the Messenger of God ($AAS). He tells you, "(You should) be at your shrines; you have a heritage, a heritage from your father, Abraham."'
lessons: Our spiritual practices carry a lineage stretching back through the prophets — we are never starting from scratch but drawing on a deep, sacred inheritance.
Correction and guidance, when delivered with care and on behalf of a trusted authority, is an act of mercy, not judgment.
Sometimes we drift from where we are meant to be, spiritually or physically, without realizing it — being redirected is a gift.
practical_advice: When you feel lost or uncertain in your spiritual practice, trace it back to its roots — connect with the tradition and the community it comes from, and let that grounding restore your confidence.
If someone corrects you in a matter of faith or practice, receive it with openness rather than defensiveness, recognizing it may be guidance from someone who cares.
Reflect on the spiritual inheritance you have received — from family, teachers, or tradition — and consciously choose to honor and carry it forward.

title: The Prophet Breaks His Fast at Arafat to Strengthen Himself for Prayer
summary: The Prophet chose to break his fast on the day of Arafat to gain strength for the prayers and supplications that are the primary purpose of standing at Arafat, remaining mounted on his camel from noon to sunset.
story: It has been narrated above that the Messenger of God (SAAS) broke his fast on the day at 'Arafat; this shows that breaking fast there is preferable to fasting because of the added strength that provides for the prayers, since they are the more important objective there. This is why the Messenger of God (SAAS) remained mounted upon his camel (at 'Arafat) from noon to sunset. Abu Da'ud al-Tayalisi related in the musnad compendium, from Ḥawshab b. 'Aqil, from Mahdi al-Ḥajari, from 'Ikrima, from Abu Hurayra, from the Messenger of God (SAAS), to the effect that at 'Arafat, he forbad fasting on the day (of the assembly) at 'Arafat.
lessons: Wisdom in worship means understanding the purpose behind each act — sometimes refraining from one form of devotion enables a deeper one.
Taking care of the body is not opposed to spirituality; the Prophet's example shows that physical strength can be in service of spiritual endurance.
Religious rulings often reflect God's mercy — permission to ease one burden is frequently a door to greater focus on what matters most.
practical_advice: When preparing for an important spiritual or personal commitment, consider what physical and emotional preparations will help you show up fully — rest, nourishment, and pacing are acts of wisdom.
If you feel guilty for taking care of yourself during a period of worship or challenge, remember that the Prophet prioritized sustainability over performance.
Before a demanding task, identify the one most important goal and organize your energy around supporting it, letting secondary concerns take their proper place.

title: The Best Prayer: La Ilaha Illa Allah on the Day of Arafat
summary: The Prophet declared that the best prayer ever made — by him and all the prophets before him — was the declaration of God's oneness made on the day of Arafat.
story: Imam Malik stated, from Ziyad b. Abu Ziyad, the freed-man of Ibn 'Abbas, from Talḥa b. 'Ubayd Allah b. Kariz, that the Messenger of God ($AAS) said, 'The best prayer was that of the day of 'Arafat, and (on that day) was one made by me and the prophets before me: La Ilaha illa Allahu waḥdahu la sharika lah. "There is no god but God Alone; He has no associate".' Imam Aḥmad and al-Tirmidhi narrated, from a ḥadith of 'Amr b. Shu'ayb, from his father, from his grandfather, that the Messenger of God ($AAS) said, 'The best prayer was that on the day at 'Arafat. And the best statement I and the prophets before me made was, "There is no god but God alone; He has no associate. Power is His, and praise. He has control over everything."'
lessons: The simplest declarations of faith can be the most profound — the heart of all prophetic prayer was a single sentence of sincere acknowledgment of God.
Unity with the prophets who came before us is found in shared devotion — we are part of an unbroken chain of those who have turned their hearts toward God.
In moments of spiritual elevation, returning to foundational truths — God is One, all power belongs to Him — is not simplicity but depth.
practical_advice: In moments of anxiety, stress, or confusion, return to the simplest declaration of faith — 'There is no god but God' — and let its meaning settle your heart.
Make a habit of repeating this dhikr during your day, especially in transitional moments like waking up, before meals, or before sleep, to maintain a thread of conscious connection with God.
When prayer feels complicated or inadequate, remember that the prophets' greatest prayer was also their simplest — sincerity, not complexity, is what reaches God.

title: The Prophet's Prayers of Awe and Humility at Arafat
summary: Multiple narrations record the Prophet's deeply humble and heartfelt personal supplications at Arafat during the Farewell Pilgrimage, including prayers for light, ease, refuge from evil, and expressions of total dependence on God.
story: The ḥafiẓ Abu al-Qasim al-Tabrani stated in his work al-Manasik that al-Ḥasan b. Muthanna b. Mu'adh al-'Anbari stated, quoting 'Affan b. Muslim, quoting Qays b. al-Rabi', from al-Agharr b. al-Ṣabaḥ, from Khalifa, from 'Ali, who stated, 'The Messenger of God ($AAS) said, "The best statement I or the prophets before me ever made was on the evening (of the assembly) at 'Arafat: 'There is no god but God alone. He has no associate. Power is His, and praise. He has control over every thing.'"' Al-Tirmidhi stated in his work al-Da'wat that Muḥammad b. Ḥatim al-Mu'addib related to him, quoting 'Ali b. Thabit, quoting Qays b. al-Rabi', who was of Banu Asad, from al-Agharr b. al-Ṣabbaḥ, from Khalifa b. Busayn, from 'Ali, may God be pleased with him, who said, 'The most part of what the Messenger of God (SAAS) prayed on the day at 'Arafat, at the station, was "O God, praise is Yours, such as that we speak, and better than we speak. O God, my prayer goes to You, along with my devotion. And my living and my dying are yours, Lord of my heritage. I seek refuge with You from the agony of the grave, the whisperings of the breast and conflicting matters. O God, I take refuge with You from the evil of what the wind may bring."' The ḥafiẓ, al-Bayhaqi narrated it through Musa b. 'Ubayda, from his brother 'Abd Allah b. 'Ubayda, from 'Ali, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, "The greater part of the prayer of those before me and of my prayer at 'Arafat, is my saying, 'There is no god but God alone. He has no associate. Power is His, and praise. He has control over everything. O God, place a light in my seeing, a light in my hearing and a light in my heart. O God, give comfort to my breast and ease my affairs. O God, I seek refuge with you from the whisperings of the breast, and conflicting matters, the evil of the trials of the grave, the evil of what comes in by night, the evil of what comes by day, the evil of what the winds bring in and the evil of fate's calamities.'"' Among what the Messenger of God (SAAS) prayed for on the ḥijjat al-wada' was, 'O God, You hear my words, see my whereabouts and know of me what is secret and what is plain; nothing about me is hidden from You. I, the miserable, the poor, the seeker for help and protection, the concerned, the sympathetic, the consoler, the penitent, I petition You as do the poor, I plead with You in humility as do the humble, I pray to You as does one who is fearful and in distress, as one who submits his neck to You, and sheds his tears before You, bending low his body before You, humiliating himself before You. O God, do not render me, O God, wretched in my prayer to You; be compassionate and merciful with me, O Best of all who receive requests, and Best of all who give.'
lessons: The Prophet, the most beloved of God's creation, still approached God with profound humility and a sense of need — our vulnerability before God is not weakness but the truest form of connection.
Praying for light in our seeing, hearing, and heart is a recognition that understanding and clarity are gifts from God, not achievements we earn on our own.
Naming our fears and struggles honestly in prayer — as the Prophet did — is itself an act of faith and emotional courage.
practical_advice: When overwhelmed, try praying in the Prophet's words: 'O God, give comfort to my breast and ease my affairs' — letting the weight of your situation be carried in language shaped by prophetic wisdom.
If you struggle to know what to say in prayer, borrow from these narrations — identifying yourself as 'the poor, the seeker for help' can dissolve the barrier between you and honest supplication.
Practice the discipline of humility in prayer by periodically setting aside requests for outcomes and simply expressing your complete dependence on God, as the Prophet modeled at Arafat.

title: Usama b. Zayd Witnesses the Prophet Praying with One Hand at Arafat
summary: Usama b. Zayd, riding behind the Prophet at Arafat, witnessed him raise his hands in prayer while managing his camel's fallen halter with the other hand, continuing his supplication without interruption.
story: Imam Aḥmad stated that Hushaym related to him, quoting 'Abd al-Malik, quoting 'Ata', who said, "'Usama b. Zayd stated, 'I was mounted behind the Prophet (SAAS) at 'Arafat. He raised his hands praying. His camel bent forward and its halter fell. He picked up the halter with one of his hands while he raised up his other.'" Al-Nasa'i narrated it similarly from Ya'qub b. Ibrahim, from Hushaym.
