lessons: Devotion does not require perfect conditions — the Prophet maintained his prayer even while managing the practical demands of the moment.
Small moments of witness, like Usama's memory of this scene, carry great spiritual power and remind us that sacred moments happen in the midst of ordinary life.
Persisting in prayer or remembrance of God even when interrupted by life's small disruptions is itself an act of devotion.
practical_advice: When daily life interrupts your moments of reflection or prayer, do not abandon them — return to them as the Prophet returned to his raised hand, resuming where you left off.
Find small windows within busy or chaotic moments to maintain your connection with God, even if only through a brief internal prayer or word of remembrance.
Reflect on a personal memory of witnessing someone's quiet, consistent devotion and let it inspire your own practice.

title: The Prophet Prays at Arafat Like a Poor Man Pleading for Food
summary: Ibn Abbas narrated that he saw the Prophet praying at Arafat with his hands placed upon his chest in the posture of a poor person begging for food, embodying complete humility before God.
story: The ḥafiẓ al-Bayhaqi stated that Abu 'Abd Allah al-Ḥafiẓ related to him, quoting Abu 'Abd Allah Muḥammad b. Ya'qub, quoting 'Ali b. al-Ḥasan, quoting 'Abd al-Majid b. 'Abd al-'Aziz, quoting Ibn Jurayj, from Ḥusayn b. 'Abd Allah al-Ḥashimi, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS), praying at 'Arafat with his hands placed upon his chest like a poor man pleading for food.'
lessons: The outward posture of prayer reflects the inner state of the heart — the Prophet embodied in his body what he felt in his soul: total dependence on God.
Humility before God is not a feeling reserved for moments of crisis but a posture we can consciously choose and embody in every act of worship.
The greatest among humanity approached God as the most humble — this is an invitation for every person, regardless of their status, to come to God with open hands.
practical_advice: Try consciously adopting a posture of humility in your next prayer or moment of reflection — let your body language express what you want your heart to feel.
When you feel proud, self-sufficient, or distant from God, return to the image of the Prophet's hands upon his chest and let it soften your approach to supplication.
Make a practice of beginning each prayer or moment of du'a by acknowledging your need — 'I am poor before You, O God' — as a way of entering the right spiritual frame.

title: The Prophet's Prayer for His Nation at 'Arafat and God's Forgiveness
summary: At 'Arafat, the Prophet prayed at length for his nation's forgiveness. God responded by forgiving all sins between Himself and the people, and eventually also interceding for wrongs between people, causing the Prophet to smile at Satan's distress.
story: Abu Da'ud al-Tayalisi stated in his musnad collection, that 'Abd al-Qahir b. al-Sarri related to him, quoting a son of Kinana b. al-'Abbas b. Mirdas, from his father, from his grandfather 'Abbas b. Mirdas (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) prayed on the evening at 'Arafat for forgiveness and mercy for his nation, and that his prayer was lengthy. He ('Abbas) stated that God revealed to him, "I have forgiven (all), except the oppression of one another. As for their sins that are between Myself and them, those I have forgiven." ('Abbas went on,) "The Prophet (SAAS) said, 'O God, You have power to reward with good this unfortunate for his wrongs done to him, and to forgive this wrong-doer.' But He did not respond to him that evening. When morning for (the passage to) Muzdalifa came, he repeated his supplication. God Almighty responded to him, 'I have forgiven them.' And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) smiled and one of his Companions asked him, 'Messenger of God, why are you smiling at a time when you used not to smile?' He replied, 'I am smiling at that enemy of God, Satan. When he learned that God, the Almighty and Glorious, had responded to me about my nation, he fell down praying in distress and lamenting loudly, pouring dirt over his head.'"
lessons: Persistent supplication, even when an immediate response is not received, reflects deep trust in God's mercy and wisdom.
God's forgiveness is vast and encompasses sins between the servant and his Lord, and His mercy can extend even to wrongs between people through intercession.
The Prophet's unwavering concern and intercession for his nation is a source of hope and comfort for every believer.
practical_advice: When your du'a seems unanswered, do not despair — return to God the next morning, the next prayer, the next moment, with the same sincerity and trust.
Reflect on the breadth of divine forgiveness when you feel weighed down by guilt; God's mercy exceeds our transgressions when we turn to Him sincerely.
Let the Prophet's smile in that moment remind you that every hardship and delay has a purpose — victory and relief come in God's perfect timing.

title: God's Declaration: 'Today I Have Fulfilled Your Religion' — Revealed at 'Arafat on a Friday
summary: A Jewish scholar acknowledged to Umar ibn al-Khattab that the Quranic verse announcing the completion of Islam would have been celebrated as a festival by the Jews. Umar confirmed it was revealed at 'Arafat on a Friday evening during the Farewell Pilgrimage.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Ja'far b. 'Awn related to him, quoting Abu al-'Umayr, from Qays b. Muslim, from Tariq b. Shihab, who stated, "A Jew came to 'Umar b. al-Khattab and said, 'Commander of the Believers, you recite a certain verse in your Book; had it come down upon us Jews, we would have adopted that day as an 'id, "a festival day".' 'What verse is that?' he asked. He replied, 'The words of the Almighty, "Today I have fulfilled for you your religion. I have fulfilled of you my grace, and I have honoured you with Islam as a religion"' (surat al-Ma'ida; V, v.3). "'Umar commented, 'I swear by God, I well know the day when it was revealed to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and the hour too — in the evening at 'Arafat, on the Friday.'"
lessons: The completion of religion is among the greatest of God's blessings, one that even observers from other faiths recognized as monumental.
Bearing witness to the moments of divine favour and remembering them precisely, as Umar did, is a form of gratitude and spiritual alertness.
The religion of Islam was completed as a gift and mercy — not a burden — and this should inspire confidence and contentment in a believer's heart.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually incomplete or lost, return to the reminder that your religion has been perfected for you — you already have all you need to draw close to God.
Mark and remember the significant moments of your own faith journey; gratitude for past blessings strengthens resilience in present difficulties.
Read and reflect on Surah Al-Ma'ida verse 3 during moments of doubt or confusion about your identity as a Muslim, letting it anchor your sense of belonging.

title: The Prophet's Calm and Dignified Departure from 'Arafat to Muzdalifa
summary: As crowds jostled behind the Prophet on his departure from 'Arafat, he repeatedly called for calm and dignity, tightening his camel's reins and reminding the pilgrims that piety is not measured by speed.
story: Jabir stated in his long hadith, "He remained standing there until the sun set. The yellow of the sky dissipated somewhat, until the sun-disk had gone. Usama b. Zayd then mounted behind him and the Messenger of God (SAAS) moved away; he had so tightened al-Qaswa's bridle that her head almost touched the front of his saddle. With his right hand, he was making a gesture that said, 'People! Go easy! Go easy!' Whenever he came to a hill, he loosened his grip somewhat until she had climbed up. When he came to al-Muzdalifa he prayed the al-maghrib and the al-'isha', with an adhan and two iqamas; between these two prayer times he did not speak prayers." ... Usama went on, "When the people crowded around him, the Messenger of God (SAAS) would move off at the al-'anaq pace and if he found an opening ahead, he would increase to the al-nass speed. When he reached al-Muzdalifa, he combined both prayers, the al-maghrib and the al-'isha'." ... Imam Ahmad stated that Abu Kamil related to him, quoting Hammad, from Qays b. Sa'd, from 'Ata', from Ibn 'Abbas, from Usama b. Zayd, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) set forth from 'Arafat, I was mounted behind him. He began to rein in his camel to the point that the prominent bone behind its ear almost touched against the front of his saddle. He would say, 'People, go easy, and with dignity! Piety is not (measured) by a camel's speed.'"
lessons: Calmness and composure in a crowd or during a stressful journey is itself an act of worship and a sign of inner piety.
The Prophet modelled that spiritual excellence is not demonstrated through frantic urgency but through dignified, purposeful action.
Even in moments of great religious significance and large gatherings, maintaining tranquillity — al-sakina — is a prophetic value worth emulating.
practical_advice: When you feel rushed, overwhelmed, or swept along by the pace of those around you, consciously slow down and ask: is this haste serving my soul or harming it?
In crowded or stressful situations — at work, in traffic, during busy seasons — remind yourself of the prophetic principle: piety is not measured by speed.
Build intentional pauses into your day, the way the Prophet paused at the defile before reaching Muzdalifa, to collect yourself before the next act of worship or responsibility.

title: The Prophet Performs Ablution at the Defile and Combines Prayers at Muzdalifa
summary: On the journey from 'Arafat to Muzdalifa, the Prophet stopped briefly at a defile to relieve himself and perform a light ablution, telling Usama that prayer would be performed ahead at Muzdalifa, where he then combined the maghrib and isha prayers.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Sufyan related to him, from Ibrahim b. 'Uqba, from Kurayb, from Ibn 'Abbas, who quoted Usama b. Zayd as telling him that the Prophet (SAAS) mounted him behind himself from 'Arafat and that when the former reached the defile, he dismounted and urinated. He did not mention his pouring water, but stated, "I poured water on him and he performed a light ablution. I asked, '(For) the prayer?' He replied, 'Prayer is ahead of you.'" Usama went on, "He then reached al-Muzdalifa and performed the al-maghrib prayer. People untied their saddles and I helped him. After that he performed the al-'isha' prayer." ... Al-Bukhari stated that 'Abd Allah b. Yusuf related to him, quoting Malik, from Musa b. 'Uqba, from Kurayb, who quoted Usama b. Zayd as saying, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) set forth from 'Arafat and dismounted at the defile. He urinated and performed the ablution, but not fully. I asked him, 'Prayer?' He replied, 'Prayer is ahead of you.' He rode on to al-Muzdalifa and performed a complete ritual ablution. Prayer was held and he performed the al-maghrib. After that everyone made their camels kneel at his dwelling. Prayer was again held and he performed the al-'isha'. Between the two he did not say any prayers."
lessons: Flexibility within the framework of worship — such as combining prayers during travel — reflects the mercy and practicality built into Islamic practice.
The Prophet's instruction 'prayer is ahead of you' teaches us that not every moment requires immediate action; some things have their proper time and place.
Attending to the body's basic needs during a journey without guilt is consistent with prophetic practice and a healthy relationship with one's physical self.
practical_advice: When you feel anxious about fitting worship into a busy or travelling day, remember that Islam provides allowances like combining prayers — use them without guilt.
Trust that what is ahead of you in your schedule has its proper time; not everything needs to be rushed or completed right now.
Observe how the Prophet's companions eagerly helped him — look for small ways to serve others during shared journeys or group activities, as those acts build community and closeness.

title: Ibn 'Umar Following the Prophet's Exact Footsteps During Hajj
summary: Ibn 'Umar meticulously followed every detail of the Prophet's actions during Hajj, including stopping at the same spots to relieve himself, demonstrating profound love and devotion to the Sunnah.
story: Imam Al;tmad stated that Yazid b. Harun related to him, quoting 'Abd al-Malik, from Anas b. Sinn, who said, "I was with Ibn 'Umar at 'Arafat. When it was time for him to leave, I went along with him to the imam. He prayed with him the first (prayer) and the al-'a~r. He then (entered into the wuquf) rite, as did I and my companions, until the Imam set off and we did so with him. Eventually we arrived at the pass below al-Ma'zimin, where he made his camel kneel, as did we ours. We thought that he intended to pray, but his servant informed us that when the Prophet ($AAS) had reached that spot, he had urinated, and that he wished to do the same."
lessons: Deep love for a teacher or spiritual guide expresses itself in careful attention to their example, even in the smallest details of daily life.
Following a noble example with sincerity transforms ordinary, mundane acts into meaningful expressions of devotion and remembrance.
The companions' dedication to preserving and living the Prophet's Sunnah illustrates how memory and tradition are kept alive through embodied practice, not just words.
practical_advice: Identify a person of wisdom and good character whose life you admire, and consciously study not just their grand decisions but their everyday habits and manners.
When you engage in acts of worship, bring full attention and intention to each detail rather than going through the motions mechanically — presence transforms routine into meaning.
Consider how your own daily habits and choices reflect your deepest values; small consistent acts of faithfulness accumulate into a life of integrity.

title: The Prophet Combining the Maghrib and Isha Prayers at Muzdalifa
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage at Muzdalifa, the Prophet combined the Maghrib and Isha prayers at one time, demonstrating the flexibility and mercy built into Islamic worship during times of hardship and travel.
story: AI-Bukhari stated that Adam b. Abu Dhi'b related to him from a1-Zuhrl, from Salim b. 'Abd Allah, from Ibn 'Umar, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) combined the al-maghrib and the al-'ishii' prayers at aI-Jam', each one of them with an iqama. He did not speak any other (superogatory) prayers between them, nor following either of them." Muslim narrated it from Yal:tya b. Yal:tya, from Malik, from al-Zuhrl, from Salim, from Ibn 'Umar, who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the al-maghrib and al-'isha' prayers combined together at a1-Muzdalifa. Muslim went on to state that I:Iarmala related to him, quoting Ibn Wahb, quoting Yunus, from Ibn Shihab (who said) that 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar told him that his father said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) combined the al-maghrib and the al-'isha' prayers together at Jam' without (him making) any prayer prostrations between them both. He performed the al-maghrib with three raka'at and the al-'isha' with two raka'at. 'Abd Allah used to pray at Jam' the same way until he died." Jabir stated in his ltadith, "The Messenger of God ($AAS) went to bed until dawn arrived. He performed the al-ftjr prayer when the morning was visible, with an adhan and an iqama." Ibn 'Abbas stated, "These were two prayers that were transferred from their (usual) times - the al-maghrib after the people arrive at al-Muzdalifa, and the al-fajr, when the dawn rises."
lessons: Islamic worship is built with mercy and practicality — accommodations exist for genuine hardship, reminding us that God desires ease, not difficulty, for His servants.
The companions' careful preservation of the Prophet's precise prayer practices shows the importance of learning religious details from those who witnessed them firsthand.
Combining duties when circumstances demand it is not laziness but wisdom; recognizing when flexibility is appropriate is itself a form of understanding.
practical_advice: When life circumstances make your normal routines impossible to maintain perfectly, remember that your faith and values allow for flexibility — do what you can with sincerity rather than abandoning the practice entirely.

During stressful travel, major life events, or overwhelm, identify which of your regular practices can be simplified or combined without being abandoned, preserving the spirit even when the form must adapt.

Study the accommodations built into the things you value most — religion, relationships, professional commitments — so that when hardship comes, you know how to adapt gracefully rather than feeling like a failure.

title: The Pilgrim from Tai' Who Exhausted Himself Reaching Jam'
summary: A man from the tribe of Ta'i came to the Prophet exhausted, having traveled over every mountain to reach Jam', and anxiously asked whether his Hajj would count; the Prophet reassured him with a clear and merciful answer.
story: Imam A!).mad stated that Hushaym related to him, quoting Ibn Abu Khalid and Zakariyya', from al-Sha'!>i, quoting 'Urwa b. Muqarris, who said, "I went to the Prophet ($AAS), when he was at lam' and said, 'Messenger of God, I have come to you from the two mountains of Ta'i. I have worn myself out and exhausted my camel and, by God, I did not leave out any mountain without standing on it. Does this count for me as a ltajj?' He replied, 'Whoever has witnessed this prayer with us' - meaning the al-ftjr prayer - 'at Jam' and stood with us, then proceeded on from there, having previously proceeded from 'Arafat, whether by day or night, such a person will have completed his ftajj and accomplished the rites.'"
lessons: When we have given our utmost effort and still feel uncertain whether it was enough, turning to God and His guidance brings clarity and peace that our own anxious minds cannot provide.
God's mercy accommodates sincere effort made under difficult circumstances — the measure is not perfection of form but sincerity and presence.
The Prophet's direct, reassuring answer models how good spiritual leadership addresses anxiety with clarity rather than leaving people in prolonged uncertainty.
practical_advice: When you have given your genuine best effort to something important — a responsibility, a relationship, an act of worship — practice releasing the anxiety of 'was it enough?' and trust that sincere effort accompanied by right intention is honoured.
If you are overwhelmed by how much you feel you have missed or fallen short of, focus on what you can still do right now rather than spiraling over what is past.

Seek clarity from knowledgeable and trustworthy people when religious or moral uncertainty is causing you distress — uncertainty left unaddressed grows heavier, while a clear answer brings relief even when the news is mixed.

title: The Prophet Sending Ahead the Weak and Vulnerable at Night from Muzdalifa
summary: The Prophet showed compassion during Hajj by allowing weaker members of his family and companions to leave Muzdalifa ahead of the crowd at night, protecting them from the difficulty of the mass departure.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) used to send on ahead of himself a group of his family during the night before the departure of the people from al-Muzdalifa to Mina. AI-Bukhari gives a section entitled, Chapter on those who send on ahead their ftmily during the night; they stand at al-Muzdalift and pray and advance ahead after the moon has set. He went on to state that Ya1.Iya b. Bukayr related to him, quoting al-Layth, from Yunus, from Ibn Shihab, who said, "Salim stated, 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar used to send on ahead the weak members of his family. They would stand at the mash'ar al-ftaram at night, speaking the name of God for as long as they wished. Then they would leave before the Imam would stand there and before he proceeded ahead. Some of them would go to Mina for the al-fajr prayer, while some would proceed thereafter. When they arrived, they would cast stones at the al-jamra, "the mound". Ibn 'Umar used to say, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave them permission."'" Sulayrnan b. l;Iarb related to us, quoting l;Ianunad b. Zayd, from Ayyub, from qkrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched me from Jam' by night."
lessons: True leadership and mercy recognize that not everyone has the same capacity, and accommodating those with less strength is a mark of wisdom and compassion, not weakness.
Protecting the vulnerable from unnecessary hardship is itself an act of worship and reflects the core values of care and justice.
Spiritual communities thrive when those with strength use their position to shield those who are weaker, rather than expecting everyone to meet the same standard under all conditions.
practical_advice: Identify the people in your life — family, colleagues, community — who are carrying heavier burdens or have less capacity right now, and ask how you can arrange things to ease their load rather than assuming everyone can keep the same pace.
When you are the one who is overwhelmed or less able, give yourself permission to accept accommodations and help without shame — accepting mercy when it is offered is not weakness but wisdom.
Build flexibility into your plans and communities so that the most vulnerable are not left behind or crushed by systems designed only for those at full strength.

title: Dispatching the Weak and Women of the Family from Muzdalifa at Night
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) dispatched the weaker and slower members of his family from Muzdalifa before dawn, instructing them not to cast stones at the pillar until after sunrise.
story: Ibn 'Abbas said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent us on ahead, along with the suckling women of Banu 'Abd al-Munalib, in charge of our people who were tired. He set about slapping our thighs with his hand and saying, "Now, kinfolk, do not cast stones until the sun rises!"' Ibn 'Abbas went on, 'I don't imagine that anyone cast stones at the pillar until the sun rose.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) passed by us on the night of the sacrifice and it was already very dark. He set about slapping our thighs and saying, 'Now, kinsfolk, proceed forth, but don't cast stones at the pillar until the sun rises.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent on ahead the weak members of his family from al-Muzdalifa at night. He proceeded to advise them not to cast stones at the pillar at al-'Aqaba until the sun rose.
lessons: The Prophet's care for the vulnerable shows that true leadership means attending to those who are weaker and ensuring their wellbeing even amid large communal obligations.
Following divine instructions with precision, even when personal circumstances differ, reflects trust in God's wisdom and the Prophet's guidance.
Accommodating the needs of those who are slower or less capable without making them feel burdensome is a mark of compassion and mercy.
practical_advice: When you are feeling overwhelmed by large group responsibilities, look to see who around you may need extra care and attend to them — this act of service often brings personal calm and perspective.

If you are facing a task with specific timing requirements, prepare in advance by identifying what accommodations your situation may genuinely require, rather than assuming the standard approach applies to everyone equally.

When you feel stressed about keeping pace with others, remember that the Prophet (SAAS) himself created space for those who needed different arrangements — it is not weakness to acknowledge your limits and act within them.

title: Asma' bint Abi Bakr Casting Stones Before Sunrise at Muzdalifa
summary: Asma', daughter of Abu Bakr, waited at Muzdalifa watching for the moon to set before departing, then cast the stones before sunrise based on the permission granted by the Prophet (SAAS) to litter-borne women.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Abu Nu'aym related to him, quoting Aflah b. Humayd, from al-Qasim, from Muhammad, from 'A'isha, who said, 'We stopped at al-Muzdalifa and Sawda asked permission from the Prophet (SAAS), to move on prior to the departure of the rest of the people. She was a woman slow of movement. He did give her permission and she left before the rest. We remained until next morning and moved out along with him. For me to have asked his permission as Sawda had done would in fact have been far dearer to me than any other happiness.' Abd Allah, the freed-man of Asma', said that Asma' stayed on the night of the assembly at Muzdalifa and proceeded to pray and did so for an hour. She then asked, 'My son, has the moon disappeared?' He replied, 'No,' and so she prayed for another hour. Then she asked, 'My son, has the moon disappeared?' 'Yes,' he replied. 'Then set out,' she said. So they did and continued on until she had cast stones at the pillar. She then returned and performed the al-subh prayer in her dwelling. He asked her, 'Hantah, I believe we're still in dark of night.' She replied, 'Son, the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave permission for this prayer at this time to women borne in litters.' Asma' also said, 'We used to do that in the time of the Prophet (SAAS).'
lessons: Acting on genuine, established permission from the Prophet (SAAS) is not a compromise of faith but an expression of the mercy built into Islamic practice.
Patient vigilance — waiting and watching for the right sign before acting — reflects wisdom and mindfulness in carrying out worship.

Those who feel they must always keep pace with others in religious practice should know that Islam honors individual circumstances, and accommodations within prophetic guidance are acts of mercy, not weakness.
practical_advice: When you feel anxious about whether you are performing an act of worship correctly given your particular circumstances, seek knowledge from reliable sources rather than assuming you must do what everyone else does.
Practice mindful patience in worship: like Asma' waiting and watching for the moon, take moments to check in with your state before proceeding, rather than rushing through obligations.
If you have been given a legitimate concession in religious practice, accept it with gratitude rather than guilt — the Prophet's (SAAS) mercy in granting accommodations was itself an act of worship.

title: Sawda bint Zam'a Granted Permission to Depart Muzdalifa Early
summary: Sawda, a wife of the Prophet (SAAS) who was slow of movement, was granted special permission to depart from Muzdalifa before the crowds, while 'A'isha expressed that she wished she had asked for the same permission.
story: 'A'isha said, 'We stopped at al-Muzdalifa and Sawda asked permission from the Prophet (SAAS), to move on prior to the departure of the rest of the people. She was a woman slow of movement. He did give her permission and she left before the rest. We remained until next morning and moved out along with him. For me to have asked his permission as Sawda had done would in fact have been far dearer to me than any other happiness.'
lessons: The Prophet's (SAAS) sensitivity to the physical limitations of those around him shows that mercy in leadership is attentive and personal, not merely general.
Expressing a wish that we had made a different choice — as 'A'isha did — is a natural human feeling and does not diminish the value of the choice we made.
Those who feel left behind or slower than others should know that Islam does not punish slowness; it accommodates it with grace.
practical_advice: If you feel anxious about keeping up with others in a group religious or communal setting, speak up about your needs — the prophetic example shows that asking for accommodation is honored, not judged.
When you feel lonely or left behind because others around you seem more capable or faster, remember that your pace does not define your sincerity or the value of your worship.

Reflect on moments where you wished you had asked for help or made a different choice — rather than carrying regret, use those reflections as gentle encouragements to advocate for your needs in the future.

title: Umm Salama Dispatched to Cast Stones Before Dawn on the Night of Sacrifice
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) sent his wife Umm Salama ahead on the night of the sacrifice to cast the stones before dawn, after which she departed, and that day was designated as his day to spend with her.
story: Abu Da'ud stated that Harun b. 'Abd Allah related to him, quoting Ibn Abu Fudayk, from al-Dahhak — meaning 'Uthman's son — from Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, from 'A'isha, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched Umm Salama the night of the sacrifice and she cast the stones before dawn. She then left and proceeded onwards. And that was the day that would be for the Messenger of God (SAAS)' — to be with her, Abu Da'ud added.
lessons: The Prophet's (SAAS) thoughtful arrangement for each of his wives — ensuring Umm Salama could fulfill her rites and then return to him on his designated day with her — reflects how love and fairness can be maintained even amid the demands of large communal events.
Fulfilling religious obligations even in the dark and difficult hours of the night is a mark of devotion and trust in God's guidance.
Care for those we are responsible for does not pause during busy or demanding seasons; it finds creative and attentive expression even in those moments.
practical_advice: When you are managing multiple responsibilities and relationships, look for ways to honor each one meaningfully rather than letting the pressures of a busy time cause any person to feel overlooked.

If you feel stressed by the logistical demands of worship or religious gatherings, focus on what is within your control and trust that fulfilling your obligation sincerely, even in difficult conditions, is accepted.
Use moments of early morning worship — like Umm Salama's pre-dawn stoning — as a reminder that some of the most meaningful acts of devotion happen when no one else is watching.

title: The Prophet's Recitation of the Talbiyya at Jam' and Departure from Muzdalifa Before Sunrise
summary: At Jam' (Muzdalifa) during the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet (SAAS) performed the fajr prayer, stood at the mash'ar al-haram in dhikr and supplication until the light was strong, then departed before sunrise — distinguishing the Muslim practice from that of the pre-Islamic polytheists.
story: Jabir stated in his hadith, 'He performed the al-fajr prayer at dawn with an adhan and an iqama. He then mounted al-Qaswa' and proceeded to the al-mash'ar al-haram. He went to the qibla, spoke a prayer, praised and glorified God and asserted His oneness. He remained standing until the dawn light was strong. He proceeded on before the sun rose, mounting al-Fadl behind himself.' Al-Bukhari stated that Hajjaj b. Minhal related to him, quoting Shu'ba, from Ibn Ishaq, who said, 'I heard 'Amr b. Maymun say, "I witnessed 'Umar perform the al-subh prayer at Jam'. He then stood up and said: 'The unbelievers used not to proceed forth until the sun rose and they would call out, Ashriq Thabir! Look down upon Mt. Thabir! The Messenger of God (SAAS) proceeded forth before the sun rose."' The Messenger of God (SAAS) made an address to us at 'Arafat. He praised and lauded God and then he said, 'To proceed, those who believed in polytheism and in idols used to go on from here at sunset, so that the sun would be over the mountain tops like mens' turbans upon their heads. Our procedure is different from theirs. They would go on from al-mash'ar al-haram when the sun rose over the mountain tops like mens' turbans upon their heads. Our procedure is different from theirs.' 'Abd Allah said, while we were at Jam', 'I heard him to whom the surat al-Baqara was revealed say at this station, "Labbayka, Allahumma, Labbayk! At Your service, O God, at Your service."' He continued to recite the talbiyya until he cast the stones at the day of sacrifice.
lessons: The Prophet's deliberate distinction of Muslim practice from pre-Islamic customs teaches that conscious, intentional worship — knowing why we do what we do — deepens the meaning of every act.
Standing in remembrance of God, praising and glorifying Him while waiting for the light to grow, is itself a form of worship — patience in the presence of God is never wasted time.

The talbiyya — 'At Your service, O God' — recited continuously until the moment of the stoning is a reminder that the entirety of the pilgrimage, and of life itself, is an act of presenting oneself before God.
practical_advice: When you begin a day of important responsibilities, start it as the Prophet (SAAS) did at Muzdalifa: with prayer, praise, and a moment of stillness before moving forward into action.

Distinguish your intentions and actions from those driven by habit, culture, or the crowd — periodically ask yourself why you are doing what you are doing, and whether it aligns with your values and faith.

In moments of waiting or transition, fill the time with remembrance of God rather than anxiety about what comes next — the Prophet's standing at the mash'ar al-haram shows that these in-between moments are sacred.

title: The Prophet's Journey from Arafat to Muzdalifa with Usama and al-Fadl
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet carried Usama behind him from Arafat to Muzdalifa, and al-Fadl behind him from Muzdalifa to Mina, reciting the talbiyya throughout until casting stones at al-Aqaba.
story: Usama was mounted behind the Prophet (SAAS), from 'Arafat to al-Muzdalifa. He then mounted al-Fadl behind himself from al-Muzdalifa to Mina. Each of these men said, 'The Prophet (SAAS) went on reciting the talbiyya until he cast stones at al-'Aqaba.'
lessons: The Prophet honoured his companions by keeping them close even during the most sacred rituals, showing that true leadership includes personal warmth.
Continuous remembrance of God — symbolised by the unbroken talbiyya — keeps the heart anchored during life's most significant moments.
Completing a sacred act fully, from beginning to its proper conclusion, reflects integrity and devotion.
practical_advice: During important or stressful life transitions, maintain a consistent spiritual practice — prayer, dhikr, or reflection — until the task is truly complete.
Show those close to you that they are valued by including them meaningfully in significant moments rather than leaving them on the sidelines.
Identify the 'final stone' in your current challenge — the definitive act that marks completion — and commit to reaching it before you stop.

title: The Prophet's Instruction to Proceed with Tranquillity
summary: At Arafat and Muzdalifa during the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet repeatedly urged the crowds to move with calmness and tranquillity rather than pushing and jostling.
story: The latter said on the evening of the (assembly) at 'Arafat and on the morning (of the assembly) at Jam' when they proceeded on their way, 'Proceed with tranquillity.' He restrained his camel until he entered Mu/.1assir while on his way from Mina. He said, 'Gather the pebbles that will be used for casting at the jamara.'
lessons: In moments of collective rush and crowd pressure, the prophetic model is deliberate calm — tranquillity is itself an act of worship.
Restraining oneself when the environment is chaotic is a mark of spiritual strength, not weakness.
Sacred obligations are best fulfilled with presence of mind, not haste driven by anxiety.
practical_advice: When you feel swept up in urgency or group panic, consciously slow your pace and breathe — the Prophet's instruction to 'proceed with tranquillity' applies to everyday overwhelm too.
Before entering a stressful environment — a crowded space, a difficult meeting, a challenging conversation — set an intention to move and speak with calm.
Practice restraint as an active virtue: choose to hold back, to wait, to be measured, especially when everyone around you is rushing.

title: The Prophet's Warning Against Excess in Religion
summary: When choosing pebbles for the stoning ritual, the Prophet warned al-Fadl and the pilgrims against excess, saying it was excess in religion that destroyed those who came before.
story: Abu al-'Aliyya stated, from Ibn 'Abbas, that al-Fagl related to him, saying, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) said to me on the morning of the day for sacrifice, "Choose me some pebbles." So I chose him some like those used in slings. He put them in his hand and said, "With the like of these! With the like of these! Beware of excess; it was excess in religion that destroyed those before you."'
lessons: Moderation is not a compromise of faith but a pillar of it — the Prophet explicitly warned that extremism in religion leads to destruction.
Even in acts of worship, more is not always better; the quality and intention behind an act matters more than its magnitude.
History carries lessons about the consequences of going beyond proper boundaries, and wisdom is in heeding those lessons.
practical_advice: When you find yourself going to extremes — in self-criticism, in obligations you impose on yourself, or in judging others — pause and ask whether this intensity is truly required or whether it is excess.
Apply the principle of sufficiency: do what is needed well, then stop. Resist the anxiety that tells you more effort automatically means more virtue.
Reflect periodically on whether any area of your life has drifted into imbalance. The prophetic standard is the middle way.

title: The Farewell Hajj: The Prophet's Declaration at Arafat and Mina
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet stood at Arafat and Mina declaring sacred boundaries, performed the rites with care, and told the pilgrims to learn their rites from him as he might not see them after that year.
story: 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) stood at 'Arafat and said, "This is the station; and all 'Arafat is a station." He proceeded forth when the sun set and mounted Usama behind himself. He began forcefully urging on his camel, while the people were jostling at his right and left. He did not turn towards them. He was saying, "Go easy, people!" He then reached Jam'. There he performed both prayers with them, the al-maghrib and the al-'isha'. He spent the night there and next morning went to Mt. Quzah. He stood there and said, "This station and all Jam' is a station." He then proceeded on until he reached Mu/.1assir. He stood there, then urged his camel ahead until he had crossed the wadi, whereupon he restrained it. Thereafter he mounted al-Fagl behind himself and travelled on to the pillar, at which he cast stones. He then went on to the sacrifice site and said, "This is a sacrifice site; all Mina is a site for sacrifice."' He said, 'Adopt your rites from me; I may not see you after this year.'
lessons: The Prophet's farewell instruction — 'Adopt your rites from me; I may not see you after this year' — is a reminder that every gathering with loved ones may be the last, and presence should be total.
Declaring what is sacred — whether a place, a relationship, or a commitment — gives life structure and meaning.
Leadership in the most difficult moments is still marked by gentleness: the Prophet urged people to go easy even as thousands pressed around him.
practical_advice: Treat important gatherings and moments with loved ones as potentially irreplaceable. Be fully present, because you genuinely do not know which farewell will be the last.
Identify what is truly sacred in your own life — your values, your commitments, your relationships — and declare them clearly to yourself and others.
When you are in a position of responsibility and people around you are stressed or pushing, model the calm you want to see. Your demeanour becomes the permission others need to settle down.

title: The Khath'ami Woman Asking About Hajj on Her Father's Behalf
summary: A young woman from Banu Khath'am approached the Prophet during the Farewell Pilgrimage asking whether she could perform Hajj on behalf of her elderly, mentally incapacitated father, and the Prophet confirmed she could.
story: 'A young girl of (Banu) Khath'am then asked him to make a ruling, saying, "My father is an old man who is mentally senile. It is time for him to fulfil God's requirement of making the Hajj. Will he be rewarded if I accomplish it on his behalf?" "Yes; perform it for your father," he replied.'
lessons: Caring for an incapacitated parent is not only a duty but an act that God recognises and rewards — the daughter's love translated into spiritual merit for her father.
Islam provides compassionate accommodations for those who cannot fulfil obligations due to genuine incapacity, reflecting the mercy at the heart of the faith.
Seeking clarity on difficult obligations rather than assuming or avoiding is itself an act of courage and integrity.
practical_advice: If you are caring for an elderly or incapacitated parent or loved one, recognise that your effort on their behalf carries spiritual weight. You are not doing less — you are doing it for them.
When facing a religious or moral question that causes you anxiety, seek a direct and qualified answer rather than carrying the uncertainty alone.
Acts of love and proxy — doing things for those who cannot do them — are among the most meaningful expressions of compassion. Let that motivate you when the caregiving feels exhausting.

title: Al-Fadl's Head Turned Aside: The Prophet's Wisdom About Temptation
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet turned al-Fadl's gaze away from a young woman, explaining to al-Abbas that he could not trust Satan to leave a young man and young woman unchecked.
story: 'He (the Prophet (SAAS)) turned the head of al-Fagl aside. Al-'Abbas asked him, "Messenger of God, why did you turn aside the head of your cousin?" He replied, "I saw a young man and a young woman, and I could not trust Satan with them."'
lessons: Protecting people from temptation is an act of care, not suspicion — the Prophet acted preventatively out of concern for both individuals.
Acknowledging that humans are vulnerable to temptation is wisdom, not weakness. The Prophet did not blame but simply redirected.
Leadership sometimes means making small, discreet interventions that prevent larger harms, without embarrassing anyone involved.
practical_advice: Be honest with yourself about situations or environments that increase your vulnerability to choices you would regret. Redirecting yourself early is not failure — it is wisdom.
When you notice someone you care about drifting toward a situation that could harm them, consider a gentle, dignified redirection rather than a confrontation.
The Prophet's phrase 'I could not trust Satan with them' is a candid acknowledgment of human nature. Use it as permission to set boundaries for yourself without self-judgment.

title: Rulings on Errors in Hajj Rites: Shaving Before Sacrifice and Circumambulation Before Shaving
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, two men came to the Prophet having made mistakes in the sequence of Hajj rites, and the Prophet responded to both with ease and reassurance.
story: 'A man then came up to him and asked, "Messenger of God, I have shaved (my head) before making sacrifice." He replied, "Make sacrifice, despite the prohibition." Then another man came and asked, "Messenger of God, I have performed the circumambulation of the Hajj before I shaved (my head)." He replied, "Shave or cut it short; it's no problem."'
lessons: The Prophet's repeated response of 'it's no problem' to honest mistakes reflects a faith built on mercy and flexibility, not rigid punishment.
Errors made in good faith, without arrogance, are met with guidance and ease — this is a model for how to respond to our own mistakes and those of others.
Coming forward honestly to admit an error, even to a figure of great authority, takes courage and is rewarded with clarity and relief.
practical_advice: When you make a mistake — even in something that matters deeply to you — bring it into the open rather than hiding it. The prophetic response to honest error is always 'it's no problem; here is what to do now.'
Release the belief that a single misstep ruins everything. Correct what you can, continue forward, and trust in the mercy of God.
Extend to others the same ease the Prophet extended to these men. When someone comes to you having made an error, lead with reassurance before correction.

title: The Prophet at Zamzam: Declining to Draw Water to Protect Banu Abd al-Muttalib
summary: After completing the Farewell Hajj circumambulation, the Prophet visited Zamzam and told Banu Abd al-Muttalib he would have drawn water with them had he not feared the crowds would overwhelm them trying to emulate him.
story: 'After that he went to the ka'ba, made the circumambulation (of the Hajj) and proceeded on to zamzam. He then said, "Banu 'Abd al-Munalib; were it not that people would have overwhelmed you (in their wanting to emulate me) I would surely have drawn water with you."'
lessons: The Prophet's care extended to protecting his family from being overwhelmed by the consequences of his own example — true love anticipates unintended harm.
Sometimes the most loving act is restraint: not doing something beautiful because doing it would create a burden for those you love.
Honour for those who serve sacred roles — like the custodians of Zamzam — is expressed not only in praise but in protecting the dignity and manageability of their work.
practical_advice: Consider whether your actions, even well-intentioned ones, might create pressure or burden for those around you. Restraint can be its own form of generosity.
Reflect on the people in your life who serve quietly and faithfully. Express appreciation in ways that support their role rather than overwhelming it.
When you cannot do something you genuinely want to do because it would harm others, let that restraint itself be a form of worship and love.

title: The Continuous Talbiyya Until the First Stone at al-Aqaba
summary: Multiple companions witnessed and testified that the Prophet continued reciting the talbiyya without interruption throughout the Farewell Pilgrimage journey until the very moment he cast the first stone at the pillar of al-Aqaba.
story: 'Abd Allah who said, "I closely watched the Prophet (SAAS), and he went on reciting the talbiyya until he cast the first stone at the pillar of al-'Aqaba." He (al-BayhaqI) also stated that al-Fagl said, "I proceeded on with the Messenger of God (SAAS) from 'Arafat and he continued reciting the talbiyya until he cast stones at the pillar at al-'Aqaba, saying Allahu Akbar 'God is most Great' with each stone. He then broke off the recitation of the talbiyya with the final stone." 'Ikrma stated, "I proceeded on with al-Husayn b. 'Ali (from 'Arafat). I can still hear him reciting the talbiyya up until he cast the stone at al-'Aqaba. When he cast it, he ceased (the recitation). I asked, 'Why is this?' He replied, 'I saw my father 'Ali b. Abu Talib recite the talbiyya until he cast stones at the pillar at al-'Aqaba. And he told me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) used to do that."'
lessons: Sustained remembrance of God throughout a journey — not only at its beginning and end — transforms the entire experience into an act of worship.

The prophetic practice was transmitted across generations with precision and love: Ali learned from the Prophet, Hussein learned from Ali, and Ikrima witnessed Hussein. Knowledge passed person to person is alive.
Marking the transition between states — from supplication to action — with a clear, intentional act gives spiritual meaning to the moment of change.
practical_advice: Choose a prayer, phrase, or intention to carry with you through an important undertaking — and hold it until the task is truly complete rather than letting your focus drift once you can see the end.
Reflect on the spiritual practices and values you have received from those who came before you. Who taught you how to carry yourself through difficulty? Honour that chain.
Create personal rituals that mark meaningful transitions in your day or life — moments that signal 'this phase has ended and I am moving forward with intention.'

title: The Prophet's Method of Casting Stones at Jamrat al-Aqaba
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet Muhammad cast seven pebbles at the pillar of al-Aqaba while mounted on his camel, saying 'Allahu Akbar' with each stone, and warned pilgrims not to crowd or harm one another.
story: In one account he gave, she said, 'I saw him at the pillat at al-'Aqaba mounted. And between his fingers I saw stones, and he cast (them). The people also cast (stones). He did not stand at it.' According to Ibn Majo, she said, 'I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the day of the sacrifice at the pillat at al-'Aqaba. He was mounted on his mule.' He went on to complete the hadith. The reference here to the mule is very strange. Muslim narrated in his sahih collection from a hadith of Ibn Jurayj, quoting Abu al-Zubayr, quoting Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, who said, 'I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) cast at the mound while mounted on his camel on the day of the sacrifice. And he was saying, "You should take your rites (from me) for, I do not know, perhaps I will not make the pilgrimage after this hijja of mine."' Muslim also related (it) from a hadith of Zayd b. Abu Unaysa, from Yahya b. al-Husayn, from his grandmother Umm al-Husayn. Yahya related that he heard her say, 'I performed the hijjat al-wada' with the Messenger of God (SAAS). I saw him when he cast at the pillat at al-'Aqaba and (when) he left mounted on his camel on the day of sacrifice, as he said, "You should take your rites (from me), for I do not know, perhaps I will not make the pilgrimage after this hijja of mine."' In one account, she stated, 'I went on the hijjat al-wada' with the Messenger of God (SAAS). I saw Usama and Bilal; one of them was taking hold of the halter of the camel of the Prophet (SAAS), while the other man was raising his gown to shield him from the heat, until he cast at the pillat at al-'Aqaba.' Imam Ahmad stated that Abu Ahmad Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah al-Zubayri related to him, quoting Ayman b. Nabil, quoting Qudama b. 'Abd Allah al-Kilabi, (who said) that he saw the Messenger of God (SAAS), cast at the mound at al-'Aqaba from the middle of the wadi on the day of the sacrifice mounted upon a reddish-hued camel he owned. And there was no striking, no pushing away and no saying, 'Keep away! Keep away!'
lessons: The Prophet demonstrated religious rites with gentleness and ease, showing that worship should not be a source of harm to others.
His words 'perhaps I will not make the pilgrimage after this' reflect a consciousness of mortality that gives every act of worship deeper meaning and urgency.
True leadership means making space for others and protecting those around you, as exemplified by Usama and Bilal caring for the Prophet.
practical_advice: When performing religious obligations, focus on the quality and sincerity of your actions rather than rushing or competing with others.
Approach each act of worship as if it may be your last opportunity, allowing that awareness to deepen your presence and gratitude.
Look for ways to serve and ease the experience of others in communal worship, as doing so is itself an act of devotion.

title: The Prophet's Warning Against Crowding During the Pilgrimage
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage at Mina, the Prophet addressed the pilgrims, organized their positions by group, and warned them not to harm one another while casting stones at the pillar.
story: Imam Ahmad b. Hanbal stated that 'Abd al-Razzaq related to him, quoting Ma'mar, from Humayd al-A'raj, from Muhammad b. Ibrahim al-Taymi, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Mu'adh, from one of the men who was one of the Companions of the Prophet (SAAS). The man stated, 'The Prophet (SAAS) made an address at Mina and directed them to their places. He said, "Let the muhajirun stay there." And he gestured towards the right side of the qibla. "And the ansar over here." And he gestured towards the left side of the qibla. "And let the other people stay round about them."' He went on, 'He then instructed them in their rites. The hearing of those at Mina was accentuated so that they could hear him at their places. And I heard him say, "Cast at the pillar (small) pebbles like those shot from slings."' Ibn Maja gave it from the hadith of Ibn al-Mubarak, from 'Abd al-Warith, from Humayd b. Qays al-A'raj, from Muhammad b. Ibrahim al-Taymi, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Mu'adh al-Taymi, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) made an address to us while we were at Mina. Our hearing was so accentuated that we could hear what he was saying.'
lessons: Order and structure in communal gatherings reduce confusion and anxiety, reflecting the Prophet's wisdom in organizing large groups.
Clear and simple instructions — such as using small pebbles — prevent people from overcomplicating acts of worship and harming others.
Even in large crowds, a calm and authoritative voice of guidance can reach every heart that is truly attentive.
practical_advice: When feeling overwhelmed in a crowd or stressful situation, look for clear guidance and structure rather than reacting impulsively.
Simplify your approach to obligations — do what is required with ease, avoiding extremes that burden yourself or harm others.
In moments of communal stress, focus on your own actions and intentions rather than being swept along by the anxiety of those around you.

title: The Prophet Sacrifices Camels at the Farewell Pilgrimage
summary: At the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet sacrificed 63 camels by his own hand, shared the act with Ali, ordered the meat distributed among the people, and ate from a shared pot of the sacrificed animals.
story: Jabir stated, 'Then he left for the sacrifice site, where he sacrificed 63 camels by his own hand. He allowed 'Ali to sacrifice those that remained and had him share in his own hadl. He ordered that a piece of meat of each animal be cut out and placed in a pot, where it was all cooked. Both men ate from that meat and drank the gravy.' Jabir b. 'Abd Allah narrated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) shared the hadl with 'Ali b. Abu Talib, and that the sacrificial animals brought by 'Ali from Yemen and those brought with him by the Messenger of God (SAAS) totalled 100 camels, and that the latter sacrificed 63 animals by his own hand. Ibn Hibban and others stated, 'And that (figure) corresponded with his age, for he was (at the time) 63 years old!' Imam Ahmad stated that Yahya b. Adam related to him, quoting Zuhayr, quoting Muhammad b. 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abu Layla, from al-Hakam, from Miqsam, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) made sacrifice on the hajj of 100 animals of which he sacrificed 60 by his own hand. He ordered that the remainder be brought and they were sacrificed. A piece of each one was taken and placed in a pot. He ate from it and drank its gravy. On the day at al-Hudaybiyya, he sacrificed 70 animals, including the camel of Abu Jahl. When they were blocked from the ka'ba, they (the camels) expressed their anguish, as though yearning for their young.' It is established in both sahih collections from a hadith of Mujahid, from Ibn Abu Layla, from 'Ali, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered me to go over to his sacrificial camels and to give away their meat, skins and pack-saddles, and told me not to give any of them to the butcher. He said, "We will give him some of our own."'
lessons: The Prophet's personal participation in sacrifice — with his own hands — reflects that acts of worship and giving are most meaningful when done with full personal involvement.
Sharing a meal from the sacrifice with companions and ensuring equitable distribution models gratitude through generosity.
The Prophet's fairness to the butcher — compensating him from his own wealth rather than from the sacrificial animals — shows that justice extends even to those in service roles.
practical_advice: Practice gratitude not just through words but through tangible acts of giving and sharing with those around you.
When you have abundance, make it a point to include others — distribute what you have generously rather than hoarding or over-consuming.
Treat those who serve or assist you with fairness and respect, compensating them properly for their effort.

title: Ali and the Prophet Sacrifice Together with a Spear
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet called for Ali and the two of them together used a spear to slaughter the sacrificial camels, after which the Prophet seated Ali behind him on his mule.
story: Abu Da'ud stated that Muhammad b. Hatim related to him, quoting 'Abd al-Rahman b. Mahdi, quoting 'Abd Allah b. al-Mubarak, from Harmala b. 'Imran, from 'Abd Allah b. al-Harith al-Azdi, who quoted 'Arafa b. al-Harith as saying, 'I witnessed the Messenger of God (SAAS) being brought the sacrificial animals. He said, "Summon Abu Hasan to me." And 'Ali was summoned to him. He then said, "Take hold of the lower part of the spear." The Messenger of God (SAAS) took hold of its upper part. They then used it to stab the camels. When he had finished, he mounted his mule, seating 'Ali behind himself.' Abu Da'ud is alone in giving this. There is some strangeness in both its line of transmission and its content. But God knows best.
lessons: The Prophet's act of calling Ali to join him in the sacrifice reflects how meaningful acts of worship become even richer when shared with loved ones.
Seating Ali behind him after the act was a gesture of closeness and honour, showing that true leadership elevates and includes others.
Involving companions in significant moments builds bonds of trust and shared purpose that last beyond the event itself.
practical_advice: Invite the people you love or trust to participate alongside you in meaningful acts — shared experiences deepen relationships.
After completing a difficult or significant task with someone, take a moment to acknowledge and honour their contribution.
Look for ways to make others feel valued and included in moments that matter, rather than always acting alone.

title: The Prophet's Sacrifice and Head-Shaving at Hajj al-Wada'
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) cast stones at the pillar of al-Aqaba, sacrificed animals, and had his head shaved, distributing his hair among his Companions as a blessed gift.
story: After having cast stones at the pillar at al-'Aqaba, the Messenger of God (SAAS) dressed in robes, put on perfume and sacrificed his hadt. Muslim stated that Yahya b. Yahya related to him, quoting Hafs b. Ghiyath; from Hisham, from Ibn Sirin, from Anas b. Malik who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) went to Mt. Mina, proceeded to the pillar, cast at it, went to his dwelling at Mina and made sacrifice. He then said to the barber, "Take". He gestured towards his right side (of his head), then to his left side. He began giving the hair to the people. In one account he gives, he shaved the right side of his head and distributed it, a hair or two at a time, to the people. He gave the hair of the left side to Abu Talha. In another account of his, the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave the hair of the right side to Abu Talha, and also gave him the hair of the left side, telling him to distribute it among the people. Imam Ahmad stated that Sulayman b. Harb related to him, quoting Sulayman b. al-Mughira, from Thabit, from Anas, who said, "I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS), while the barber was shaving him. His Companions were encircling him, not wanting a hair to fall anywhere but into a man's hand." Ahmad is alone in giving this.
lessons: Sacred rituals performed with presence and sincerity become moments of immense barakah (blessing) that touch all those around us.
The Prophet's Companions treasured every connection to him, reminding us to cherish and protect what is spiritually precious in our lives.
Completing acts of worship step by step — casting stones, sacrificing, shaving, circumambulating — teaches us the value of patient, ordered fulfillment of our obligations.
practical_advice: When performing acts of worship or fulfilling religious duties, slow down and bring full presence and gratitude rather than rushing through the motions.
Look for ways to share the blessings of your spiritual experiences with others around you, just as the Prophet shared his hair as a gift of blessing.
Follow through on your commitments in the proper order, especially when overwhelmed — breaking large obligations into sequential steps makes them manageable and meaningful.

title: Aisha Perfumes the Prophet for Ihram and Ihlal at the Farewell Pilgrimage
summary: Aisha (RA) described how she personally perfumed the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) with her own hands both when he entered the state of ihram and when he concluded it before circumambulating the Ka'ba during the Farewell Pilgrimage.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that 'Ali b. 'Abd Allah b. al-Madini related to him, quoting Sufyan - he being Ibn 'Uyayna - quoting 'Abd al-Rahman b. al-Qasim b. Muhammad, the latter being the finest of men of his era, who said that he heard his father, the finest man of his era, say that he heard 'A'isha state, "I put perfume on the Messenger of God (SAAS), with these two hands of mine when he adopted the ihram state and again when he declared it at an end, before he circumambulated," and that she spread out her hands. Muslim stated that Ya'qub al-Dawraqi and Ahmad b. Mani' both related to him, quoting Hushaym, quoting Mansur, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. al-Qasim, from his father who quoted 'A'isha as saying, "I would put perfume on the Messenger of God (SAAS), before he adopted the ihram state, and on the day of the sacrifice, before he circumambulated the ka'ba; I used perfume mixed with musk." Al-Nasa'i narrated, from a hadith of Sufyan b. 'Uyayna, from al-Zuhri, from 'Urwa, from 'A'isha, who said, "I perfumed the Messenger of God (SAAS), for his state of ihram when he affirmed it and for his state of ihlal after he had cast stones at the pillar at al-'Aqaba, before he circumambulated the ka'ba." Al-Shafi'i stated that Sufyan b. 'Uyayna informed him, from 'Amr b. Dinar, from Salim, who quoted 'A'isha as saying, "I perfumed the Messenger of God (SAAS), for both his states, of ihram and ihlal." In both Sahih collections Ibn Jurayj is quoted as saying that 'Umar b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Urwa informed him that he heard 'Urwa and al-Qasim quoting 'A'isha as having said, "I perfumed the Messenger of God (SAAS), by my own hands, using perfume in powder form, on the hijjat al-wada', both for the ihram and the ihlal."
lessons: Acts of love and service performed for the sake of God carry deep spiritual meaning and become part of sacred history.
Aisha's role in this intimate act of preparation shows that those closest to us often play a quiet but essential role in our spiritual journey.
Transitions — entering a sacred state and leaving it — deserve mindful preparation and care, reminding us to mark our own life transitions with intention.
practical_advice: Mark the beginning and end of important commitments or spiritual seasons in your life with a meaningful personal ritual that signals your readiness and gratitude.
Cherish the people in your life who support your worship and spiritual growth, and express appreciation for their care.
When moving between significant life phases or responsibilities, take a moment to pause, prepare thoughtfully, and invite God's blessing before proceeding.

title: Ibn Abbas Explains the Rules of Ihlal — What Becomes Permissible After Stoning
summary: Ibn Abbas explained to a man the rules governing what becomes permissible after casting stones at the pillar during Hajj, citing the Prophet's example of rubbing musk on his own head.
story: Sufyan al-Thawri stated, on the authority of Salama b. Kuhayl, from al-Hasan al-'Awfi, who quoted Ibn 'Abbas as saying, "When you have cast stones at the pillar, your state of ihram will be concluded and all things previously haram will be halal for you, except for women (they will continue to be haram for you); until you circumambulate the ka'ba." A man asked, "What about perfume, Abu al-'Abbas?" He replied, "I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) rub musk on to his head. And is that perfume, or not?"
lessons: Seeking clarity about religious obligations from knowledgeable and trustworthy sources is a sign of sincerity and devotion.
The Prophet's personal example — his Sunnah — is the most reliable and living explanation of Islamic rulings.
When confused about what is permissible or forbidden, asking with humility and an open heart leads to illuminating answers.
practical_advice: When facing uncertainty about a religious or ethical matter, seek out someone knowledgeable and ask your question directly rather than guessing or assuming.
Use the example of those you admire and respect — as Ibn Abbas used the Prophet's example — to ground your own choices in something concrete and trustworthy.
Accept that transitions in life, like the transition out of ihram, come with new responsibilities and permissions; approach them with awareness rather than confusion.

title: Wahb b. Zam'a Is Corrected by the Prophet on the Day of Sacrifice
summary: On the night of the sacrifice, the Prophet corrected Wahb b. Zam'a and his companion for wearing their shirts before completing the tawaf of ifada, explaining the sequence of permissibility after stoning and sacrifice.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq stated that Abu 'Ubayda related to him, from 'Abd Allah b. Zam'a, from his father and his mother, Zaynab, daughter of Umm Salama, who quoted Umm Salama as saying, "It was the night when the Messenger of God (SAAS) would circulate, the night of the sacrifice. He was with me when Wahb b. Zam'a came in, accompanied by a man of the family of Abu Umayya. Both men were wearing long shirts. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked them, 'Have you performed the tawaf (of the ifada)?' 'No,' both men replied. 'Then take off your shirts,' he told them, and they did so. Wahb then asked him, 'Why, Messenger of God?' He replied, 'This is a day when you are given permission (to wear the shirts) if you have cast (stones) at the pillar and have sacrificed animals, if you had brought some. Then you would be in a state of ihlal for all that had previously been haram for you, except for women until you have circumambulated the ka'ba. If you have cast stones but not completed the tawaf, you will remain in a state of ihram, until after you circumambulate the ka'ba.'" Abu Da'ud narrated it similarly from Ahmad b. Hanbal and Yahya b. Ma'in, both of whom quoted from Ibn Abu 'Adi, from Ibn Ishaq. Al-Bayhaqi gave it from al-Hakim, from Abu Bakr b. Abu Ishaq, from Abu al-Muthanna al-'Anbari, from Yahya b. Ma'in. He adds the following at the end, "Abu 'Ubayda stated that Umm Qays, daughter of Mihsan related to him, as follows, ''Ukkasha b. Mihsan, along with a group of men of Banu Asad, all dressed in shirts, left my place on the evening of the day of the sacrifice. Then they returned to us that evening carrying their shirts in their hands.' She questioned them and they told her the same as the Messenger of God (SAAS) had said to Wahb b. Zam'a and his companion."
lessons: Gentle correction from a place of knowledge and care helps people return to the right path without shame or hostility.
Completing acts of worship in their proper sequence matters — skipping steps has consequences that require us to retrace our path.
The Prophet's role as a teacher was not to humiliate but to explain and guide, modeling how we should correct one another.
practical_advice: When you realize you have made a mistake in an important process, pause, accept the correction gracefully, and return to where you went wrong rather than pressing forward incorrectly.
If you are in a position to correct someone, do so clearly and with an explanation — tell them the why, not just the what.
Use moments of confusion or correction as reminders to slow down and confirm you have completed each step of an important commitment before moving to the next.

title: The Prophet Visits the Ka'ba, Drinks from Zamzam, and Prays at Mina on the Day of Sacrifice
summary: After completing the rites of stoning, sacrifice, and shaving at Mina, the Prophet rode to the Ka'ba, performed seven circumambulations while mounted, drank from Zamzam, and returned to Mina, fulfilling all the central rites of the Farewell Pilgrimage.
story: Jabir stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then rode on and proceeded in a throng to the ka'ba. He performed the al-zuhr prayer in Mecca and went over to Banu 'Abd al-Munalib who were distributing the water at zamzam. He said, 'Dismiss Banu 'Abd al-Munalib! Were it not for the people's preference for you having charge of the water distribution, I would have disputed with you!' They handed him a bucket and he drank from it." Muslim narrated this. In this text there is evidence that the Messenger of God (SAAS) rode to Mecca prior to the decline of the sun and circumambulated the ka'ba. Having finished this, he performed the al-zuhr prayer there. Muslim also stated that Muhammad b. Rafi' informed him, quoting 'Abd al-Razzaq, quoting 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Umar, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the tawaf (of the ifada) on the day of the sacrifice and then returned and performed the al-zuhr prayer at Mina. The outcome of this is that he did ride to the ka'ba and made seven circumambulations of it while mounted. He did not circumambulate between al-Safa' and al-Marwa, as is established in the Sahih collection of Muslim, from Jabir and 'A'isha, may God be pleased with them both. He then drank from the waters of zamzam and from a beverage of dates made from water from zamzam. He proceeded that day from al-Muzdalifa well after the light of dawn had come, but before actual sunrise. He then went to Mina and began casting pebbles at the pillar at al-'Aqaba. He proceeded on to sacrifice the 63 camels by his own hand, while 'Ali sacrificed the remainder of the 100. Thereafter, a piece of the meat of each camel was taken, placed in a pot and cooked until done. He ate some of the meat and drank some of the gravy. In the meantime, he shaved his head and put on perfume. And when he had completed all this, he rode to the ka'ba.
lessons: Completing great acts of devotion with humility — eating simple food, drinking water, returning to pray with one's community — keeps the heart grounded and grateful.
The Prophet's full participation in every rite, from the physical labor of sacrifice to the communal sharing of food, shows that worship is not only spiritual but embodied and communal.
Sharing blessings freely — as the Prophet acknowledged Banu 'Abd al-Munalib's service — honors the efforts of those who serve the community of believers.
practical_advice: After completing a major personal or spiritual milestone, resist the temptation to immediately rush to the next thing — take time to nourish yourself and be present with your community.
Acknowledge and affirm the people who serve others quietly and faithfully in your community; their work carries tremendous value even when unnoticed.
When you have accomplished something significant, mark the moment with gratitude — eat, rest, and reflect — before moving forward.

title: The Prophet's Circumambulation and Drinking from Zamzam on the Day of Sacrifice
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) circumambulated the Ka'ba while mounted on his camel, visited the zamzam well, drank from it, and honored the Banu Abd al-Muttalib who were serving water to the pilgrims.
story: What is well established is that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived in Mecca, he circumambulated the ka'ba seven times while he was mounted. He then went to zamzam where Banu 'Abd al-Muttalib were serving water to themselves and the people. From it he took a bucket from which he drank. It was then emptied over him. As Muslim stated, "Muhammad b. Minhal al-Darir informed us, quoting Humayd al-Tawil, from Bakr b. 'Abd Allah al-Muzani, who heard Ibn 'Abbas say, while he was sitting with him at the ka'ba, 'The Prophet (SAAS) arrived on his camel, with Usama mounted behind himself. They brought a vessel containing nabidh; he drank from it and gave the remainder to Usama. He said, 'You have acted correctly and done well. Continue in this way.''' Ibn 'Abbas went on, "And so we do not wish to change what the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered."
lessons: Humility is a hallmark of greatness — the Prophet drank the same drink served to all people, refusing special treatment.
Following righteous traditions passed down by those we love and respect is a form of devotion and gratitude.
Acts of service to others, such as providing water to pilgrims, are among the most honored forms of worship.
practical_advice: When you feel the urge to seek special treatment or exceptions for yourself, reflect on how the Prophet chose simplicity and equality, and try to embrace what is available to those around you.
If you have a meaningful tradition or practice passed down in your family or community, honor it as a connection to something greater than yourself.
Look for small acts of service you can offer to others today — even offering someone a drink of water carries spiritual weight.

title: Ibn Abbas Defends Serving Nabidh at Zamzam
summary: Ibn Abbas responds to a Bedouin who questioned why the family of the Prophet served nabidh at zamzam rather than milk or honey, explaining that the Prophet himself had approved this practice.
story: In an account from Bakr, a bedouin asked Ibn 'Abbas, "How come I see your uncle's folk pouring milk and honey, while you serve al-nabidh? Is this because of your poverty, or your miserliness?" Ibn 'Abbas then related to him this hadith. Ahmad stated that Rawh related to him, quoting Hammad, from Humayd, from Bakr, from 'Abd Allah (who said) that a bedouin asked Ibn 'Abbas, "How come Mu'awiya's kinsfolk serve water and honey, the family of so-and-so serves milk, while you serve al-nabidh? Is this due to your miserliness or your poverty?" "Ibn 'Abbas replied, 'We're neither miserly nor poor; however, the Messenger of God (SAAS) came to us, Usama b. Zayd mounted behind himself, and asked to drink. We served him this' - meaning the nabidh - 'and he drank from it. He said, You have done well. Continue in this way.'"
lessons: When criticized or judged unfairly, a calm and evidence-based response is more powerful than defensiveness or anger.
Holding firmly to a righteous practice even when others mock it is a form of spiritual integrity.
Not every tradition that appears simple or modest is inferior — its value may lie in its sacred origin.
practical_advice: The next time someone questions or mocks a value or practice you hold dear, take a breath before responding and ground your reply in what you know to be true rather than reacting emotionally.
Reflect on whether any of your habits or traditions have a meaningful origin story that you may have forgotten — revisiting that story can renew your commitment to it.
Do not measure the worth of your choices by comparing them to what others do; measure them by whether they align with your values and what you believe is right.

title: The Prophet Asks to Drink from the Common Water at Zamzam
summary: At the zamzam well, al-Abbas tried to fetch a special drink for the Prophet from inside the Ka'ba, but the Prophet insisted on drinking from the same vessel available to all the people.
story: Al-Bukhari narrated from Ishaq b. Sulayman, quoting Khalid, from Khalid al-Haddha', from Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, (who said) "The Messenger of God (SAAS) came to the watering place and asked to drink. Al-'Abbas said, 'Fadl, go to your mother and bring something from her for the Messenger of God to drink.' The latter asked, 'Give me to drink.' He (al-'Abbas) replied, 'Messenger of God, they are putting their hands into it.' He asked, 'Give me to drink!' and he drank from it. He then went to zamzam while they were serving its water (to the pilgrims) and working in it. He said, 'Work, for you are engaged in proper work.' He then said, 'Were it not that you would be overwhelmed (by people wanting to emulate me) I would also have drawn water by placing the rope on this' - meaning his own shoulder, to which he gestured."
lessons: True leadership means refusing to place oneself above those one leads, even when offered the opportunity.
Praising others for their honest, humble work is a form of encouragement that uplifts entire communities.
There is dignity in sharing in the common experience of those around you rather than seeking separation or privilege.
practical_advice: If you are in a position of authority or esteem, look for one concrete way today to participate alongside others rather than apart from them — it builds trust and genuine connection.
Acknowledge someone's hard work verbally today, especially if it is work that often goes unnoticed.
When you feel tempted to seek comfort or ease that others around you do not have, ask yourself whether accepting the common experience might bring you closer to those you care about.

title: The Prophet Spits into the Zamzam Bucket as a Blessing
summary: The Prophet drew water from zamzam, drank, and spat back into the bucket as a blessing, after which it was poured back into the well, and he remarked he would draw the water himself if not for the risk of people imitating him.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Rawh and 'Affan related to him, quoting Hammad, from Qays, and 'Affan stated in his hadith, that Qays informed him, from Mujahid, who quoted Ibn 'Abbas as saying, "The Prophet (SAAS) came to zamzam. We drew up a bucket for him, and he drank. Then he spat into it, and we poured it into zamzam. After that he said, 'Were it not that you would be overwhelmed (by people wanting to emulate me) I would draw it out with my own hand.'" Ahmad is alone in giving it; its line of transmission meets the criteria of Muslim.
lessons: The Prophet was always mindful of the unintended burdens his personal actions could place on his followers, reflecting his deep compassion for them.
Blessings can flow through the most ordinary of actions when they are rooted in sincerity and love.
Even the greatest among us must sometimes restrain themselves from virtuous acts for the sake of the wellbeing of those they lead.
practical_advice: Consider whether any of your habits or behaviors, even good ones, might be setting an unrealistic standard that causes stress or pressure for those around you — sometimes adjusting them is an act of kindness.
Reflect on a source of blessing in your life — a relationship, a practice, a memory — and spend a few minutes today simply being grateful for it without asking for anything more.
When you are in a position of influence, think carefully about which of your actions others might feel compelled to replicate, and whether that would truly serve them.

title: The Prophet's Sa'i Between Al-Safa and Al-Marwa: One Circumambulation Suffices
