summary: Scholarly accounts establish that the Prophet and his companions performed the sa'i between al-Safa and al-Marwa only once during the Farewell Pilgrimage, which became the basis for scholarly debate about whether one or two circumambulations are required for different types of hajj.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not repeat the circumambulation between al-Safa and al-Marwa a second time, being content with his first. As Muslim related in his Sahih collection through Ibn Jurayj, quoting Abu al-Zubayr, who heard Jabir b. 'Abd Allah say, "The Prophet (SAAS) and his Companions made only one circumambulation between al-Safa and al-Marwa." I note that the reference here to "his Companions" is to those who had brought hadis and who were performing the hajj al-qiran. It is similarly established in the Sahih collection of Muslim that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said to 'A'isha, who had joined the hajj to the 'umra and was therefore performing the hajj al-qiran, "Your circumambulation of the ka'ba and between al-Safa and al-Marwa (once) suffices for both your hajj and your 'umra."
lessons: Ease and sufficiency are principles the Prophet embedded into worship — God does not intend hardship for the believer.
Scholarly disagreement rooted in sincere pursuit of truth is a sign of intellectual vitality in a faith community, not a weakness.
When in doubt about a religious obligation, seeking knowledge from trustworthy sources brings clarity and peace of mind.
practical_advice: If you feel overwhelmed by religious obligations or worry that you are not doing enough, remind yourself that God values sincerity and ease — seek clarification from a knowledgeable source rather than assuming the hardest interpretation is always required.

When you encounter disagreement among people you respect, practice sitting with the uncertainty rather than rushing to take a side — often the answer becomes clearer with patience and more information.
Identify one area of your spiritual life where you have been unnecessarily hard on yourself, and explore whether a more compassionate interpretation is equally valid.

title: The Prophet's Farewell Sermon at Mina – Sanctity of Life, Property, and Honour
summary: On the Day of Sacrifice during the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) delivered a momentous address at Mina declaring the sanctity of Muslim blood, property, and honour, urging his followers never to revert to disbelief or mutual killing after him.
story: On that fine day the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave a wonderful address that numerous aḥādīth reiterate. We will relate those of them that God, the Almighty and Glorious, so enables us. Al-Bukhārī gave a section entitled, Chapter on the address made during the days at Minā. He went on to state that 'Alī b. 'Abd Allāh related to him, quoting Yaḥya b. Sa'īd, quoting Fuqayl b. Ghazwan, quoting 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbās, who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) made an address to the people on the day of the sacrifice. He said, 'O people, what day is this?' They replied, 'A day that is ḥarām, sacrosanct.' 'And what land is this?' he asked. 'A sacrosanct land,' they replied. 'And what month is this?' he asked. 'A sacrosanct month,' they replied. He then said, 'Your blood, your possessions and your honour are sacrosanct for you, like this day of yours in this your land in this your month.' Ibn 'Abbās went on, 'He repeated this several times. Then, raising his head, he went on, O God, have I delivered (the message well)? O God, I have delivered (the message).' Ibn 'Abbās continued, 'By Him in whose hand is my soul, it was his testament to his nation. (He then stated), Let those witnessing this inform those absent. Do not revert into being unbelievers after me, striking the necks of one another!' Al-Tirmidhī narrated it from al-Fallās, from Yaḥya al-Qaṭṭān. He characterized it as ḥasan ṣaḥīḥ. Al-Bukhārī also stated that 'Abd Allāh b. Muḥammad related to him, quoting Abu 'Āmir, quoting Qurra, from Muḥammad b. Sīrīn, quoting 'Abd al-Raḥmān b. Abu Bakra, from his father, and a man more worthy in my view than 'Abd al-Raḥmān – Ḥumayd b. 'Abd al-Raḥmān, from Abu Bakra, may God be pleased with him, who said, 'The Prophet (SAAS) made an address to us on the day of the sacrifice. He said, Do you know what day this is? We replied, God and His Messenger know best. He was silent, so that we thought he was going to give it some different name. He then asked, Is this not the day of the sacrifice? Why, yes, we replied. And what month is this? he asked. God and His Messenger know best, we replied. He was silent, so that we thought he was going to give it some different name. He then asked, Is this not Dhū al-Ḥijja? Yes indeed, we replied. And what land is this? he asked. God and His Messenger know best, we replied. He was silent so that we thought he was going to change its name. Is this not the sacrosanct land? he asked. Yes indeed, we replied. He went on, Your blood and your possessions are sacrosanct for you, just as this day of yours is holy for you, in this month of yours, in this land of yours, until the day you meet your Lord. And have I delivered (the message)? Yes, we replied. He went on, O God, bear witness! Let those present inform those absent; and many who will be informed (of this) will be more conscious than those who hear (it now). Do not revert into being unbelievers after me, striking the necks of one another.' Al-Bukhārī and Muslim narrated it through several lines, from Muḥammad b. Sīrīn. Muslim narrated it from a ḥadīth of 'Abd Allāh b. 'Awn, from Ibn Sīrīn, from 'Abd al-Raḥmān b. Abu Bakra, from his father. He added at its end, 'He then turned aside to two fine rams, both of which he sacrificed. He also (turned towards) a herd of goats and divided them up between us.' Imam Aḥmad stated that Ismā'īl related to him, quoting Ayyūb, from Muḥammad b. Sīrīn, from Abu Bakra, (who said) 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) made an address on his ḥijja, saying, Time has run a course, as was its way the day God created the heavens and the earth. The year has twelve months, of which four are sacrosanct. Three follow one another in succession – Dhū al-Qa'da, Dhū al-Ḥijja and al-Muḥarram. And there is Rajab of (Banu) Muḍar, which is between Jumādā and Sha'bān. He went on, And what day is this? We replied, God and His Messenger know best. He was silent, so that we thought he was giving it a different name. He then asked, Is this not the day of the sacrifice? We replied, Yes indeed. He then asked, What month is this? We said, God and His Messenger know best. He was silent so that we thought he was giving it a new name. He then asked, Is it not Dhū al-Ḥijja? Yes indeed, we replied. Then he asked, What country is this? God and His Messenger know best, we replied. He was silent, so that we thought he was giving it a new name. Then he asked, Is it not the sacrosanct land? We replied, Yes indeed. He then said, Your money and your possessions – I think he also said and your honour – are sacrosanct for you, as this day of yours is sacrosanct in this your month and this your country. You will meet your Lord and he will question you about your deeds. Do not revert after me into error, striking the necks of one another. Have I delivered (the message well)? Let those witnessing deliver the message to those absent. And perhaps those who are informed will be more conscious of it than some of those who heard it.' Al-Bukhārī (also) stated that Muḥammad b. al-Muthannā related to him, quoting Yazīd b. Hārūn, quoting 'Āṣim b. Muḥammad b. Zayd, from his father, from Ibn 'Umar. He said, 'The Prophet (SAAS) asked at Minā, Do you know what day this is? They replied, God and His Messenger know best. He went on, This day is sacrosanct; and do you know what land this is? They replied, God and His Messenger know best. He went on, It is a sacrosanct land. He asked, And do you know what month this is? They replied, God and His Messenger know best. It is a sacrosanct month, he said. He went on, And God made sacrosanct for you your blood, your possessions and your honour, like he made sacrosanct the nature of this your day in this your month in this your land.' Al-Bukhārī gave it in various places of his ṣaḥīḥ collection, as did the rest of the major scholars, except for al-Tirmidhī, through various routes from Muḥammad b. Zayd b. 'Abd Allāh b. 'Umar, from his grandfather, 'Abd Allāh b. 'Umar. Al-Bukhārī (also) stated, 'And Hishām b. al-Ghāz stated that Nāfi' informed him, from Ibn 'Umar, that the Prophet (SAAS) stood between the pillars on the ḥijja he made on his pilgrimage and said, This is the day of al-ḥajj al-akbar, the major pilgrimage. The Prophet (SAAS) then began by declaring, O God, bear witness! He then said farewell to the people and they said, This is the ḥijjat al-wadā', the farewell pilgrimage.'
lessons: The Prophet's final sermon reminds us that every human being's life, dignity, and wealth are sacred — a principle that should anchor how we treat ourselves and others even in our darkest moments.

Repeating core truths — as the Prophet did by asking the same questions multiple times — shows that important lessons need revisiting; we should not be ashamed to remind ourselves of what we already know when we are struggling.
The instruction 'let those witnessing inform those absent' teaches that knowledge, healing, and wisdom are meant to be shared, not hoarded; reaching out to others in difficulty is a prophetic act.
practical_advice: When you feel your sense of worth or dignity is under attack, recall that the Prophet declared your honour sacred before tens of thousands — write this down and place it somewhere visible as a daily reminder.

Use the technique of grounding through the familiar: just as the Prophet used known landmarks (the day, the land, the month) to anchor a profound message, anchor yourself during anxiety by naming five things you can see, hear, or touch around you.

Carry a lesson forward by telling someone else about something meaningful you have learned — teaching others reinforces your own understanding and creates a sense of purpose that combats hopelessness.

title: Umm al-Ḥuṣayn's Account: Bilal and Usama Sheltering the Prophet at the Farewell Pilgrimage
summary: The female companion Umm al-Ḥuṣayn witnessed the Farewell Pilgrimage and narrated how Bilal held the Prophet's camel and Usama b. Zayd shielded him from the heat as he cast pebbles at the pillar of al-'Aqaba, after which the Prophet addressed the people and commanded obedience to any just leader who governs by God's Book.
story: Al-Nasā'ī gave it from 'Amr b. Hishām al-Ḥarrānī related to him, quoting Muḥammad b. Salām, from Abu 'Abd al-Raḥmān, from Zayd b. Abu Unaysa, from Yaḥya b. Ḥuṣayn al-Aḥmasī, from his grandmother Umm Ḥuṣayn. She stated, 'I took part in the ḥijja of the Prophet (SAAS); I saw Bilāl holding the bridle of his camel, while Usāma b. Zayd raised a garment above him, sheltering him from the heat, while he was in the iḥrām state, until he cast at the pillar at al-'Aqaba. He then made an address to the people, praising and lauding God and saying many things.' Muslim narrated it from a ḥadīth of Zayd b. Abu Unaysa, from Yaḥya b. al-Ḥuṣayn, from his grandmother, Umm al-Ḥuṣayn. She stated, 'I performed the ḥijjat al-wadā' with the Messenger of God (SAAS). I saw Usāma and Bilāl; one of them was holding the bridle of the Messenger of God (SAAS), while the other raised his garment sheltering him from the heat until he cast (pebbles) at the mound at al-'Aqaba.' She went on, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) said many things. I then heard him say, (Even) if a castrated – I think he said black – slave should become your commander, who leads you with God's Book, then listen to him and obey.'
lessons: The image of the noble companions Bilal and Usama — one formerly enslaved, one of mixed heritage — caring for the Prophet dismantles any notion of racial or social hierarchy; true worth lies in service, devotion, and character.

The Prophet's command to obey any just leader who governs by God's Book — regardless of their background — teaches that we should not let bias, pride, or preconceptions prevent us from accepting guidance and help from unexpected sources.

Umm al-Ḥuṣayn's personal witnessing and narration of these events shows that every person, regardless of gender, has a role in preserving and transmitting what is good and true — your perspective and testimony matter.
practical_advice: When pride or ego makes it hard to accept help or direction, recall that the Prophet commanded obedience to a leader regardless of worldly status — practice accepting assistance graciously this week from someone you might normally overlook.

If you are caring for someone who is suffering, the simple act of shielding them from discomfort — as Usama shielded the Prophet from the heat — can be profoundly meaningful; consider one small, practical way you can reduce the burden of someone close to you today.

Write down a memory of witnessing something meaningful or being helped through a difficult moment, as Umm al-Ḥuṣayn did — revisiting such memories can restore a sense of gratitude and connection when you feel lost or alone.

title: The Farewell Sermon: Command to Obey Leaders
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet commanded Muslims to listen to and obey their leader even if he is a slave, as long as he leads by the Book of God.
story: I saw Usama and Bilal; one of them was holding the bridle of the Messenger of God (SAAS), while the other raised his garment sheltering him from the heat until he cast (pebbles) at the mound at al-'Aqaba. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said many things. I then heard him say, '(Even) if a castrated' - I think he said 'black' - 'slave should become your commander, who leads you with God's Book, then listen to him and obey.'
lessons: True leadership is defined by adherence to divine guidance, not by social status or worldly rank.
Islam dismantles systems of discrimination by elevating obedience to righteousness over ethnicity or class.
A community that submits to just and God-conscious leadership will remain united and protected.
practical_advice: When evaluating those in authority over you, assess their commitment to ethical and moral principles rather than their background or appearance.
Practice setting aside personal biases when following guidance that is clearly rooted in truth and goodness.
Reflect on whether your own judgments of others are influenced by social prejudice rather than by their character and conduct.

title: The Farewell Sermon: Sanctity of Life, Property, and the Day of Sacrifice
summary: On the day of sacrifice during the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet declared the absolute sanctity of Muslim blood, possessions, and honour, asking God to bear witness that he had delivered the message.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) made an address to us on the day of the sacrifice and he said, 'Which day is the most sacrosanct?' They replied, 'This day of ours.' He asked, 'Which month is the most sacrosanct?' They replied, 'This month of ours.' He asked, 'Which land is the most sacrosanct?' They replied, 'This land of ours.' He then said, 'Your blood and your possessions are sacrosanct for you as this day of yours in this country of yours in this month of yours are sacrosanct. Have I delivered the message successfully?' 'Yes,' they replied. He then said, 'O God, bear witness.'
lessons: Human dignity and the protection of life and property are among the highest values in Islam, as sacred as the holiest time and place.
The Prophet's act of calling God as witness shows the immense weight of moral accountability in delivering truth to others.
Sanctity of rights is not conditional on circumstances; it is absolute and timeless.
practical_advice: Treat the rights and wellbeing of others as inviolable, regardless of your personal feelings or grievances toward them.
When you have fulfilled a responsibility or conveyed an important truth, affirm it openly and call upon God as witness to your sincerity.
Remind yourself during moments of conflict that harming another person's dignity, property, or life carries the gravest moral weight.

title: The Farewell Sermon: Four Foundational Prohibitions
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet summarized the core moral prohibitions: associating partners with God, unlawful killing, adultery, and theft, which deeply impacted the narrator.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) said on the hijjat al-wada', 'They are only four in number; do not associate anything with God; do not kill, for God has forbidden this, unless by the right; do not commit adultery; and do not steal.' Salama b. Qays al-Ashja'i went on, 'And no one became more careful regarding these four than myself when I heard them from the Messenger of God (SAAS).'
lessons: The most transformative teachings are often the simplest and most direct; clarity of principle produces clarity of conscience.
Hearing a command from a trusted and beloved source can permanently reshape one's commitment to righteousness.
Personal moral discipline begins with a sincere internal resolve, not merely external compliance.
practical_advice: Identify the core ethical principles that guide your life and commit to them with the same intensity as Salama did after hearing the Prophet.
When you encounter a moral reminder that strikes your heart, do not let the moment pass — let it anchor a lasting change in your behavior.
Reflect regularly on foundational values such as honesty, harm avoidance, and faithfulness to reinforce your moral compass.

title: The Farewell Sermon: Addressing Disputes, Forgotten Rituals, and Ease in Religion
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet addressed various questions about forgotten ritual acts with reassurance, and warned about the danger of debt while affirming that God provides a cure for every disease.
story: 'I witnessed the Messenger of God (SAAS), on the hijjat al-wada' while he was delivering an address. He said, 'Your mother, your father, your sister, your brother and then the closest to you, and the closest to you.' Some people then came and said, 'Messenger of God, Banu Yarbu' attacked us!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'No person should harm any other.' A man who had forgotten to cast stones at the pillars then asked him what he should do. He replied, 'So cast the pebbles (now); no matter.' Another man then came to him and said, 'Messenger of God, I forgot to circumambulate.' He replied, 'So circumambulate (now); no matter.' Another man came to him who had shaved before he had made sacrifice. He told him, 'Make sacrifice; no matter.' And that day whenever anyone asked him anything, he always replied, 'No matter; no matter!' He then said, 'God may make all problems go away - except for a man who borrows money from a man who is a Muslim; for him there is a problem, and he is ruined.' He went on, 'God brought down no disease for which He did not provide a cure - except for senility, that is.'
lessons: Islam is a religion of ease; mistakes made in ignorance or forgetfulness are met with compassion and correction, not condemnation.
Financial responsibility and debt are treated with great moral seriousness in Islam, as debt can enslave and ruin a person.
Hope is built into the fabric of creation — for every hardship, God has provided a remedy; trusting this is an act of faith.
practical_advice: When you make a mistake or forget an obligation, do not despair — seek to correct it as soon as possible with a spirit of renewal, not guilt.
Be extremely cautious about taking on debt, and if you must borrow, make repayment a top priority to preserve your integrity and peace of mind.
When facing illness, difficulty, or suffering, remind yourself that relief and cure exist; maintain hope while taking the means available to you.

title: The Farewell Sermon: Warning Against Reverting to Disbelief and Mutual Killing
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet warned his companions in the gravest terms not to revert to disbelief and tribal violence after his death, striking the necks of one another.
story: The Prophet (SAAS) said on the hijjat al-wada', 'Jarir, listen to the people.' He then said in his address, 'Do not revert into unbelievers after me, striking the necks of one another.' Ahmad then narrated it from Ghundar, and from Ibn Mahdi, both of whom quoted it from Shu'ba. 'We were informed that Jarir stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Listen to the people. I really do not know, after what I see, (whether) you will revert to being unbelievers, striking the necks of one another.'''
lessons: The Prophet's deepest fear for his community was not external enemies but internal division and moral regression.
Unity and brotherhood are not passive states — they require active, conscious commitment against pride, tribalism, and anger.
A community that fragments and turns on itself has effectively abandoned the spiritual foundation it was built upon.
practical_advice: Guard against allowing disputes, whether personal, ideological, or communal, to escalate into enmity and hostility.
When you feel the pull of tribalism, nationalism, or sectarianism, remind yourself of the Prophet's final warning against such division.
Actively build bridges within your community rather than waiting for conflict to resolve itself; unity is a daily practice.

title: The Farewell Sermon: Honour, Usury, Satan's Despair, and Parental Rights
summary: In his address at Mina during the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet declared the abolition of usury from the pre-Islamic era, the inviolability of honour, the rights of parents and children, and warned that Satan had despaired of being worshipped but would work through small sins.
story: 'People!' He said this three times. 'What day is this?' They replied, 'It is the day of the "greater pilgrimage".' He went on, 'Your blood, your possessions and your honour are sacrosanct among yourselves as this day of yours in this land of yours is sacrosanct. Let no man do harm to his child. Satan has despaired that he will be worshipped in this your land! However, you will be obedient to him in your performance of some acts you think insignificant, and he will be pleased. All usury of the jahiliyya period is abolished. You have (the right to) your capital only. Do no wrong, and you will not be wronged.'
lessons: Satan does not need grand acts of disobedience; he is content with accumulating small, seemingly insignificant sins over time.
The abolition of usury reflects Islam's commitment to economic justice and the protection of the vulnerable from exploitation.
Honour is as sacred as life itself; protecting the dignity of others is a religious obligation, not merely a social courtesy.
practical_advice: Pay careful attention to the small habits and seemingly minor sins in your life, as these can quietly erode your moral and spiritual foundation.
Review your financial dealings and ensure they are free from interest or exploitation, reflecting the Prophet's emphasis on economic justice.
Commit to protecting the honour and reputation of those around you, refusing to engage in backbiting, mockery, or humiliation.

title: The Farewell Sermon: Inheritance, Lineage, and the Rights of Spouses
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet declared that God has assigned every worthy person their due share of inheritance, affirmed lineage through the family bed, and instructed that women may not spend household funds without their husband's permission.
story: 'God has given to each worthy man his due, and so there is no testamentary inheritance for an heir. The child applies to the bed (to the parents). For the adulteress there is the stone, and their reckoning is with God. Anyone making a claim to anyone but his father or claims an association with any patron not his own, upon him shall be God's curse that shall follow him till Judgement Day. A woman may not expend from her household (funds) except with the permission of her husband.' Someone asked him, 'Messenger of God, not even for food?' He replied, 'That is the best of our possessions.' He then went on to say, 'Things borrowed must be returned.'
lessons: Divine legislation on inheritance ensures justice across generations by removing human manipulation of wealth distribution after death.
Honesty about one's lineage and social ties is a matter of profound moral seriousness, with lasting spiritual consequences.
Mutual consent and transparency between spouses in financial matters is a foundation of a healthy and trusting marriage.
practical_advice: Ensure your estate planning adheres to Islamic inheritance principles so that rights are protected and no one is unjustly deprived after your passing.
Be scrupulously honest about your origins, affiliations, and identity; do not misrepresent yourself for social gain.
Foster open communication with your spouse about household finances, building trust and cooperation in your shared responsibilities.

title: The Farewell Sermon: Five Pillars and Entry into Paradise
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet called out to the gathered multitude and summarized the path to paradise in five essential obligations: worship of God, the five prayers, fasting, charity, and obedience.
story: I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS), one day while mounted upon al-Jad'a', his feet placed in the stirrups, stretching out so that people would hear. He said at the top of his voice, 'Do you not hear?' One man in one of the groups of people asked, 'Messenger of God, with what do you charge us?' He replied, 'Worship your Lord. Perform your five (prayers). Fast for your month. And obey when you are commanded. (Then) you will enter your Lord's garden.' 'I (Sulaym) asked him, 'Abu Umama, (how old were you) at that time?' He replied, 'At that time I was a man of 30, controlling the camel and urging it forwards towards the Messenger of God (SAAS).'
lessons: The path to paradise, as summarized by the Prophet, is accessible, clear, and built on daily acts of worship and surrender to God.
The eagerness of the companions to draw near to the Prophet and hear his words models the attitude of a sincere seeker of guidance.
Simple, consistent practice of the fundamentals is the surest route to spiritual success and divine acceptance.
practical_advice: Do not overcomplicate your spiritual life; return to the foundations — prayer, fasting, worship, and righteous obedience — and commit to them consistently.
Cultivate the eagerness of the companion Abu Umama, who urged his camel forward just to be closer to divine guidance; actively seek knowledge and nearness to God.
When you feel spiritually lost or overwhelmed, recite the Prophet's simple summary to yourself and renew your commitment to these core practices.

title: The Prophet's Address at Mina and Instruction on Hajj Rites
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) delivered a series of addresses to the pilgrims at Mina during the Farewell Pilgrimage, instructing them on the proper performance of hajj rites, positioning the Muhajirin and Ansar, and responding with patience and ease to all questions about the sequence of rituals.
story: Chapter. The Prophet (SAAS) then stopped at Mina where the mosque is today, so it is said. He positioned the muhajirin to his right and the ansar to his left, with the rest of the people thereafter around them. The hafiz al-Bayhaqi stated that 'Abd Allah al-Hafiz informed him that 'Ali b. Muhammad b. 'Iqba al-Shaybani of al-Kufa (related to him) quoting Ibrahim b. Ishaq al-Zuhri, quoting 'Ubayd Allah b. Musa, quoting Isra'il, from Ibrahim b. Muhajir, from Yusuf b. Mahik, from Umm Musayka, from 'A'isha, who said, "Someone asked the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'Should we not construct for you at Mina a building to shade you!' He replied, 'No; Mina was a residence of former people.'" It is established in both sahih collections, from a hadith of Ibn Jurayj, from al-Zuhri, from 'Isa b. Talha, from 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr b. al-'As (who said). "While the Messenger of God (SAAS) was making an address to us on the day of the sacrifice, a man got up and said to him, 'I used to think that this-and-that was before so-and-such.' Another man then arose and said, 'I used to think that this-and-that was before this.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, 'Do it (thus). No matter.'" In the wording of both sahih collections, he stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was not that day asked about anything relating (to the sequence of the hajj rites) without him responding, 'Do it; no matter.'"
lessons: True leadership is characterized by humility and accessibility — the Prophet refused a shaded shelter at Mina, choosing to remain among the people in simplicity.

Ease and flexibility are core principles of Islamic practice; when people made mistakes in the order of hajj rites, the Prophet responded with 'Do it; no matter,' teaching that God's religion is not meant to burden.
Honoring the legacy of those who came before us, as the Prophet's words 'Mina was a residence of former people' remind us to remain connected to sacred history.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by getting the details of a task 'right,' remember that sincere effort matters more than perfect sequence — extend yourself the same grace the Prophet extended to the pilgrims.
Practice simplicity in your circumstances rather than always seeking comfort and convenience; this cultivates gratitude and keeps the heart present.
When others around you make mistakes or feel confused, respond with patience and encouragement rather than criticism, following the prophetic example of saying 'Do it; no matter.'

title: The Prophet Shortens Prayers at Mina and Performs the Stoning of the Pillars
summary: During the days of Mina in the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet (SAAS) shortened his prayers to two rak'at and performed the stoning of the three pillars daily after the sun's decline, pausing in long supplication at the first and second pillars but not at the third.
story: At Mina the Messenger of God (SAAS) would shorten (the daily prayers of four raka'at) to two raka'at, as is established in both sahih collections from a hadith of Ibn Mas'ud and Haritha b. Wahb, may God be pleased with them both. The Messenger of God (SAAS) used to cast stones at the three pillars every day of those he spent at Mina, after the sun had declined, as Jabir related above. He would do so on foot, as Ibn 'Umar stated heretofore, casting seven stones saying, "God is most Great!" with each one. He would, moreover, stop and pray at the first and the second and pray to God, Almighty and Glorious is He, but he would not stop at the third. Abu Da'ud stated that 'Ali b. Bahr related to him, as did 'Abd Allah b. Sa'id al-Ma'ni, who both stated that Abu Khalid al-Ahmar related to them, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. al-Qasim, from his father, who quoted 'A'isha as saying, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the tawaf al-ifada at the end of his day when he performed the al-zuhr prayer. He then returned to Mina and stayed there for the nights of the ayyam al-tashriq, casting (stones) at the pillar when the sun began to decline, seven pebbles at each pillar, and saying 'God is most Great!' with each one. He stood and performed prayers at the first and the second, standing at both at length and making humble entreaties to God. At the third he cast (stones) without standing at it." Al-Bukhari narrated from more than one line, from Yunus b. Yazid, from al-Zuhri, from Salim, from Ibn 'Umar, that the latter would cast seven pebbles at the lower pillar, saying "God is most Great!" after each one. He then would go forwards and stand on level ground at length facing the qibla, praying and raising his hands. After that he would cast stones at the middle pillar, move to the north and then stand at length, on level ground, facing the qibla, praying with his hands raised. Thereafter, he would cast (stones) at the big pillar, from the middle of the wadi, but he would not stand at it. Then he would move away, saying, 'It was thus that I saw the Messenger of God, God's peace and blessings be upon him, do it.'"
lessons: The Prophet's practice of pausing after the first and second pillars for extended supplication teaches that moments of spiritual exertion are also moments of deep connection with God — action and contemplation go together.
The allowance to shorten prayers during travel and pilgrimage reflects Islam's built-in mercy for human limitation; God does not demand more than we can bear.
Consistency in worship — performing the same acts day after day during the ayyam al-tashriq — builds spiritual discipline and reinforces devotion through repetition.
practical_advice: When life is demanding and your routine feels strained, look for the 'shortened prayers' in your own life — legitimate simplifications that preserve your spiritual core without breaking you.
Build small pauses of gratitude and supplication into physically demanding tasks, just as the Prophet paused for prayer between the pillars during the physically taxing rite of stoning.
Use repetitive spiritual phrases like 'God is most Great' (Allahu Akbar) during moments of stress or exertion as an anchor that keeps your heart oriented toward God amid difficulty.

title: Permission Granted to Shepherds and Camel Herders for Flexible Stone-Casting
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) granted special dispensation to shepherds and camel herders to combine their stone-casting obligations across days during the hajj, accommodating the necessities of their work and responsibilities.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Sufyan b. 'Uyayna related to him, from 'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr, from his father, from Abu al-Qudah, from his father, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave permission to the shepherds that they cast (stones) on one day and graze their flocks for a day. Ahmad stated that Muhammad b. Abu Bakr related to him, and that Rawh informed him, quoting Ibn Jurayj, quoting Muhammad b. Abu Bakr b. Muhammad b. 'Amr, from his father, from Abu al-Qaddah b. 'Asim b. 'Adi, from his father, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave permission to the shepherds to act consecutively and to cast stones on the day of the sacrifice. They would then pray for a day and a night and cast stones on the following day. Imam Ahmad stated that 'Abd al-Rahman related to him, quoting Malik, from 'Abd Allah b. Bakr, from his father, from Abu al-Quddah b. 'Asim b. 'Adi, from his father (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave permission to the camel herdsmen to overnight at Mina until they (could) cast stones on the day of the sacrifice. After having done this, they would also cast stones on the next day, or on the day thereafter, for two days. And they would then cast stones on the yawm al-nafr, 'the day of the return from Mina'.
lessons: The Prophet's accommodation of shepherds and herders reveals that Islam honors the dignity of work and does not penalize people for fulfilling their professional and familial responsibilities.
True religious leadership accounts for the real circumstances of all people, not only those with leisure and privilege — the Prophet actively sought to remove hardship from those on the margins.
Flexibility within divine law is itself a form of mercy; recognizing legitimate needs and creating space for them is a prophetic value that we can apply in how we treat others.
practical_advice: If you feel guilty for modifying your worship or good habits during exceptionally busy or demanding seasons of life, remember that the Prophet himself made lawful accommodations for people with responsibilities — seek the valid options available to you.

When designing systems, rules, or expectations for others — at work, at home, in community — build in flexibility for those with caregiving or demanding occupational duties, as the Prophet did for the herders.

When you are overwhelmed by obligations, identify which of your commitments have legitimate 'combined' or 'deferred' versions and use them without guilt, returning to your full practice when circumstances allow.

title: The Prophet's Address on the Middle Day of the Ayyam al-Tashriq
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) delivered a notable public address to the pilgrims on the middle day of the ayyam al-tashriq at Mina, which became known as 'the day of the heads,' as attested by multiple companions including Sarra' daughter of Nabhan.
story: Chapter: Ahadith showing that the Prophet (SAAS) made an address to the people on the second, the middle that is, day of the ayyam al-tashriq. Abu Da'ud gave a section entitled, Chapter regarding which day it was that he made an address to the people. He went on to state that Muhammad b. al-'Ala' related to him, quoting Ibn al-Mubarak, from Ibrahim b. Nafi', from Ibn Abu Najih, from his father, from two men of Banu Bakr, both of whom stated, "We saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) make an address around the middle of the days of the ayyam al-tashriq; we were close by his camel. This was the address he made at Mina." Abu Da'ud then stated that Muhammad b. Bashshar related to him, quoting Abu 'Asim, quoting Rabi'a b. 'Abd al-Rahman b. Husayn, quoting his grandmother Sarra', daughter of Nabhan, who had headed a household before the coming of Islam. She said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) made an address to us on the yawm al-ru'us, 'the day of the heads'." He (Rabi'a) asked, 'And what day is that?' We stated, 'God and His Messenger know best!' He asked, 'Is that not the middle day of the ayyam al-tashriq?'" Abu Da'ud went on, "The uncle of Abu Hurrat al-Raqashi stated that he made the address on the middle day of the ayyam al-tashriq."
lessons: The preservation of prophetic guidance through the testimony of ordinary people — including women like Sarra' bint Nabhan who headed a household — shows that sacred knowledge belongs to the entire community, not only the learned elite.
The response 'God and His Messenger know best' models an admirable humility: acknowledging the limits of one's knowledge rather than guessing or fabricating, especially in religious matters.
The Prophet's repeated public addresses during hajj ensured that guidance reached people clearly and directly; repetition and accessibility in teaching is a prophetic value worth emulating.
practical_advice: When you don't know the answer to something important, practice saying 'I don't know' with confidence — it is a sign of integrity and the beginning of genuine learning, just as the companions deferred to the Prophet's superior knowledge.
Seek out teachings and reminders during the busy and celebratory moments of life, not only during hardship — the Prophet addressed the people even during the joyous days of hajj.
Value the spiritual knowledge carried by ordinary members of your community, including elders and those without formal credentials; their witnessed memories may carry wisdom you cannot find in books.

title: The Farewell Sermon at Mina – Sanctity of Life, Property, and Honour
summary: During the middle days of the ayyam al-tashriq, the Prophet delivered a comprehensive sermon at Mina affirming the sanctity of Muslim blood, property, and honour, abolishing debts and usury of the pre-Islamic era, and warning against reverting to disbelief.
story: I was holding the reins of the camel of the Messenger of God (SAAS), in the middle of the days of the ayyam al-tashriq, keeping the people away from him. He said, 'O people, do you know on which day of which month and in which land you are?' They replied, 'On a sacrosanct day, of a sacrosanct month, in a sacrosanct land.' He went on, 'Your blood, your possessions and your honour are sacrosanct for you, as is this your day, in this your month, in this your land, on until you meet Him.' "He went on, 'Listen to me and live. Do not do wrong! Do not do wrong! Do not do wrong! The property of a Muslim is not permitted to you, except by his wish. All blood, money and deeds done in the jahiliyya are (crushed) beneath these feet of mine until Judgement Day. The first blood debt abolished is that of Ibn Rabi'a b. al-Harith b. 'Abd al-Muttalib - he was fostered among Banu Sa'd, and Hudhayl killed him. All the usury charges of the jahiliyya are abolished. God has decreed that the first usury charges to be abolished are those of al-'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Muttalib. Your capital is your own; do not do wrong and you will not be wronged.' "'Time has revolved as was its fashion the day God created the heavens and the earth.' He then recited, 'The number of the months with God is twelve months in God's ordinance since the day He created the heavens and the earth. Of these four are sacrosanct. That is the correct reckoning. Therefore do not harm yourselves regarding them' (surat al-Tawba (al-Bara'a); IX, v.36). 'Do not revert after me into unbelievers, striking the necks of one another. Satan has despaired that those who pray will worship him. However, he is present in the dissention between yourselves.' "'Fear God in (your relationships) with women. They are (as) captives with you. They own nothing but themselves. They have rights over you, and you have rights over them - that they not allow anyone other than yourselves to set foot in your furnishings, and that they not give permission (to anyone you dislike) to enter your homes. If you fear those of them who are fractious, then admonish them and leave them in the beds and strike them, but not viciously. They have rights to their provisions and their clothing, in kindness. It was only by God's covenant that you took them; and it is by God's word that you are permitted access to their sexual organs. And whoever has a covenant should discharge it to whomever entrusted it to him.' "He then spread out his hands and said, 'Have I delivered the message? Have I delivered the message?' He went on, 'Let those witnessing inform those absent. Many of those who are informed are happier than those who hear.'"
lessons: The Prophet used the sanctity of the sacred day, month, and land to reinforce the sanctity of human life, dignity, and wealth — reminding us that God's boundaries protect us as much as they bind us.

Grievances and injustices accumulated over a lifetime can be formally released; the Prophet modelled institutional forgiveness by abolishing blood debts and usury, showing that communal healing requires deliberate closure.
Satan does not give up on humanity through open worship but through sowing discord between people — vigilance against division is itself an act of worship.
practical_advice: When you feel your rights have been violated, reflect on this sermon: your blood, property, and honour are sacred in God's sight even when others forget that. Let that divine recognition be a source of dignity rather than waiting for human validation.

If you are carrying resentment from old debts — financial, emotional, or relational — ask yourself what it would look like to formally close that chapter. The Prophet's act of abolishing old debts teaches that healing sometimes requires a deliberate declaration of release.

When conflict arises in your community or family, remember the Prophet's warning that Satan thrives in dissension. Before reacting, pause and ask: am I being manipulated into a division that serves no one?

title: The Farewell Sermon – Satan, Women's Rights, and the Book of God
summary: At al-'Aqaba during the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet delivered a sermon abolishing pre-Islamic injustices, warning against Satan's subtle influence through sin, affirming the rights of women, and leaving the Quran as the enduring guide for all Muslims.
story: The hafiz Abu Bakr al-Bazzar stated that al-Walid b. 'Amr b. MisKin related to him, quoting Abu Hammam Muhammad b. al-Zabraqan, quoting Musa b. 'Ubayda, from 'Abd Allah b. Dinar and Sadaqa b. Yasar from 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar, who said, 'This surat was revealed to the Messenger of God (SAAS), at Mina during the awsat of the ayyam al-tashriq, on the hijjat al-wada': "When God's help and the victory came" (surat al-Nasr; CX, v.1). He knew that it was the farewell, and he ordered that his camel al-Qaswa' be brought. It was saddled for him and he mounted it and stood for the people at al-'Aqaba. There assembled around him as many of the Muslims as God wished. He gave appropriate praise and thanks to God and then he said, "To proceed: O people! All blood debts of the jahiliyya are made null and void; the first blood debt so rendered is that of Ibn Rabi'a b. al-Harith - who was suckled among the Banu Layth and killed by Hudhayl. All usury of the jahiliyya is abolished. The first such usury abolished is that of al-'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Muttalib." "'O people! Time has revolved as is its fashion (since) the day God created the skies and the earth. The number of the months with God is twelve, of which four are sacrosanct. (These are) Rajab of (Banu) Mudar that is between Jumada and Sha'ban, Dhu al-Qa'da, Dhu al-Hijja and al-Muharram. "That is the correct faith; do not wrong yourselves in them" (surat al-Tawba; IX, v.36). And "al-nasi', 'intercalation', is an excess of disbelief; by it those who disbelieve are led astray. One year they make it permissible and another year they make it prohibited, in order to conform (outwardly) with the number that God made sacred" (surat al-Tawba; IX, v.37). One year they would make Safar halal and another year they would make al-Muharram haram. One year they would make Safar haram and another they would make al-Muharram halal. That is al-nasi', 'intercalation'.' "'O people, those having deposits should award them to those who entrusted them with them. O people, Satan has despaired that he will be worshipped in your land till the end of time. Yet he may be pleased with you, by (your) despicable acts. Beware of him (harming) your religion by despicable acts. O people! Women are (as) captives with you. You took them with God's covenant and you are permitted access to their sexual organs by God's word. You have rights over them and they have rights over you. One of your rights you have over them is that they not allow others than yourselves to set foot upon your furnishings. And they should not disobey you in (your) permissible requests. If they so obey, then you have no right to (chastise them). They are due their food and their clothing in kindness. If you strike (them), then do so without viciousness. No man should have access to his brother's funds, unless with his goodwill. O people! I have left among you that with which, if you adopt it, you will not go astray, the Book of God; behave by it.' "'O People! What day is this?' They replied, 'A sacrosanct day.' He said, 'What land is this?' They replied, 'A sacrosanct land.' He asked, 'What month is this?' They replied, 'A sacrosanct month.' He said, 'God has made sacrosanct your blood, your possessions and your honour, just as this day in this land and this month are sacrosanct. Let those of you who witness inform those of you who are absent. There will be no prophet after me, and no nation after you.' He then raised his hands and said, 'O God, bear witness!'"
lessons: Even when outright sin seems impossible, Satan finds entry through 'despicable acts' — small compromises, moral laziness, and communal carelessness. Guarding one's character at the level of habits is a form of spiritual defence.

The Prophet's final public instructions included the rights of women and the honouring of trusts, reminding us that relationships and responsibilities are at the centre of faith, not peripheral to it.
The Quran was explicitly designated as the enduring guide after the Prophet's passing — returning to it in moments of confusion or loss is not merely tradition but a prophetic directive.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually distant or find yourself slipping into small bad habits, take it seriously. The Prophet warned that Satan's real victories come through gradual moral decline, not dramatic falls. Audit your daily small choices.

If you are in a position of responsibility over others — as a spouse, parent, employer, or leader — revisit the Prophet's framework: those in your care have rights over you first, before you claim rights over them.

When you feel lost or overwhelmed and don't know where to turn, open the Quran with intention. The Prophet's last promise was that holding to it prevents going astray — treat it as a living guide, not a ceremonial text.

title: The Prophet's Visits to the Ka'ba During the Days at Mina
summary: Narrations from Ibn Abbas report that the Prophet would visit and circumambulate the Ka'ba every night during his stay at Mina in the Farewell Pilgrimage, though scholars noted the hadith's transmission was considered weak or mursal.
story: Al-Bukhari stated, 'It is recounted from Abu Hassan from Ibn 'Abbas, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would visit the ka'ba during the days at Mina.' He related it thus, with a comment that it was a weak tradition. The hafiz al-Bayhaqi stated that Abu al-Hasan b. 'Abdan informed him, quoting Ahmad b. 'Ubayd al-Saffar, quoting al-'Umari, quoting Ibn 'Ar'ara, who said, 'Mu'adh b. Hisham presented us a document, saying, "I heard it from my father; he did not read it." He went on, "In it it states, 'from Qatada, from Abu Hassan, from Ibn 'Abbas (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would visit the ka'ba every night for so long as he was at Mina.'" He commented, "I never saw anyone agree with him over this."' Al-Bayhaqi stated that al-Thawri narrated in the book al-Jami', from Ibn Tawus, from Tawus, from Ibn 'Abbas (who said) 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) would perform the circumambulation (of the ka'ba) every night' - those at Mina, that is. This hadith is mursal.
lessons: The Prophet's reported night visits to the Ka'ba during Mina reflect a heart that was never fully away from the house of God, teaching that the sacred can be revisited again and again without exhaustion.

Scholars' honest evaluation of weak or mursal narrations models intellectual humility — not every story that seems spiritually rich must be accepted uncritically, and honesty about uncertainty is itself an act of integrity.
Devotion does not require grand gestures done once; the image of the Prophet quietly returning to the Ka'ba each night speaks to the power of consistent, private worship.
practical_advice: If you have a place, practice, or moment of connection with God that restores you, return to it regularly — not just in crisis but as a steady rhythm. Consistency in devotion is more sustaining than intensity.

When you encounter religious information, especially on social media or in informal conversations, practice the scholar's habit: ask about the source, and hold uncertain claims with appropriate humility rather than certainty.
Build a small nightly ritual of return — to prayer, to Quran, to dhikr — that anchors your day regardless of how full or empty it felt. The night visit is a metaphor for re-centering before rest.

title: The Names and Meanings of the Hajj Days
summary: Ibn Kathir explains the traditional names given to each day of the Hajj pilgrimage season, from the day of decoration to the days of departure, illuminating the historical meaning behind each ritual landmark.
story: Chapter. The sixth day of Dhu al-Hijja, according to some, is said to have been known as the yawm al-zina, 'the day for decoration', because on it the sacrificial camels would be adorned with garlands and other things. The seventh day is known as the yawm al-tarwiyya, 'the day for the provisioning with water', because on it they refresh themselves with the water and carry away what they need of it for the al-wuquf, 'the stationing' (at 'Arafat), and thereafter. The eighth day is known as yawm Mina, 'the day at Mina', because pilgrims proceed then from al-Abtah to Mina. The ninth day is known as yawm 'Arafat, 'the day at Mt. 'Arafat', because they stand upon it that day. The tenth day is known as yawm al-nahr, 'the day of the sacrifice', and yawm al-adha', 'the day of the immolation', and as yawm al-hajj al-akbaru, 'day of the greater hajj'. The day following that is known as yawm al-qarr, 'the day of rest', since on that day pilgrims rest. It is also known as yawm al-ru'us, 'the day of the heads', because on that day they eat the heads of the animals sacrificed. It is the first of the ayyam al-tashriq. The second day of the ayyam al-tashriq is known as the yawm al-nafr al-awwal, 'the first day of the departure', because it is permissible to leave on it. It is also said to refer to the day known as the yawm al-ru'us. The third day of the ayyam al-tashriq is known as the yawm al-nafr al-akhir, 'the other day of the departure'. God Almighty stated, 'Those who hasten away in two days shall not be considered sinful; and those who remain behind are not considered sinful' (surat al-Baqara; II, v.203).
lessons: Every ritual of Hajj has a name rooted in lived human need — water, rest, food, departure — reminding us that Islam sanctifies the ordinary and elevates the mundane into worship.
The Quran explicitly releases pilgrims from guilt whether they leave early or stay longer, modelling God's mercy in accommodating different human circumstances and capacities.
Understanding the meaning behind ritual names deepens engagement with worship; knowledge transforms participation from mere compliance into conscious connection.
practical_advice: When religious practices feel mechanical or empty, research their origins and meanings. Understanding why a ritual exists — as with these Hajj day names — can rekindle the feeling of connection and intentionality.

Notice how God's allowance for both early departure and extended stay in Hajj reflects a broader principle: He does not impose a single path for everyone. Apply this generosity to how you judge your own pace of spiritual growth.

Use the concept of yawm al-qarr, the day of rest, as a reminder that rest is built into divine design. If you are overwhelmed or exhausted, scheduled stillness is not laziness — it is part of the sacred rhythm.

title: The Prophet's Departure from Mina and Prayer at al-Abtah
summary: On the third day of the ayyam al-tashriq, the Prophet and his companions mounted their animals and departed from Mina, stopping at al-Muhasab — the valley between Mecca and Mina — where he performed the Asr prayer.
story: On the yawm al-nafr, the third day of the ayyam al-tashriq, that being a Tuesday, the Messenger of God (SAAS) and the Muslims with him got upon their mounts. He departed with them from Mina and stopped at al-Muhasab, a wadi between Mecca and Mina. There he performed the al-'asr prayer. Al-Bukhari stated that Muhammad b. al-Muthanna related to him, quoting Ishaq b. Yusuf, quoting Sufyan al-Thawri, from 'Abd al-'Aziz b. Rufay', who said, 'I asked Anas b. Malik, "Tell me about something you heard related about the Messenger of God (SAAS). Where did he perform the al-zuhr prayer on the yawm al-tarwiyya?" He replied, "At Mina". I asked, "Where did he perform the al-'asr prayer on the yawm al-nafr?" "At al-Abtah; do as your [leaders do]".'
lessons: The Prophet maintained his prayers on time even in the midst of travel and transition, demonstrating that worship is not suspended by life's movements but woven through them.
The instruction 'do as your leaders do' points to the importance of learning worship through living example, not only through text — embodied transmission has always been central to Islamic practice.

Every departure has a place and a prayer — the Prophet's deliberate stop at al-Muhasab before leaving the sacred precincts models the value of pausing to mark transitions rather than rushing past them.
practical_advice: During busy, transitional periods in your life — moving, travelling, changing jobs — make a conscious effort to maintain your prayer times. The Prophet's example shows that routine worship is precisely what anchors you when everything else is shifting.

When you are leaving something meaningful behind — a place, a phase of life, a relationship — find a way to mark the transition intentionally. A moment of prayer, reflection, or gratitude honours what was, even as you move forward.
Seek out people who embody the practice you want to develop, not only books that describe it. The Companions learned by watching; find a mentor or community whose lived example you can follow.

title: The Prophet's Stay at Al-Muhassab (Al-Abtah) After the Farewell Pilgrimage
summary: After completing the Hajj rituals at Mina, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) and his Companions departed and stopped at Al-Muhassab, a valley between Mecca and Mina, where he performed four prayers, rested, and then proceeded to the Ka'ba for the farewell circumambulation. Scholars differed on whether his stay there was intentional—as an act of symbolic defiance against the Quraysh pact of boycott—or merely for the convenience of departure.
story: the Muslims with him got upon their mounts. He departed with them from Mina and stopped at al~Mu1).a~~ab, a wadi between Mecca and Mina. There he performed the al~'a,r prayer. Al~Bukhari stated that Mu1).ammad b. al-Muthanna related to him, quoting Is1).aq b. Yilsuf, quoting Suf'yan al-ThawrI, from 'Abd al~'AziZ b. Rufay', who said, "I asked Anas b. Malik, 'Tell me about something you heard related about the Messenger ofGod (~AAS). Where did he perform the al~:;.uhrprayer on theyawm al~tarwiyya?' He replied, 'At Mina'. I asked, 'Where did he perform the al-'a,r prayer on theyawm al-nafr?' 'At al~Abta1).; do as your leaders do,' he replied." It is also narrated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the al-:;,uhr prayer on the yawm al-nafr atal-AbtaJ:1; that is aI-Mul)",,~ab. But God knows best. Al-BukhilIi (also) stated that 'Abd al-Mutta'all b. T"alib related to him, quoting Ibn Wahb, quoting 'Amr b. al-J::Iarith, (who said) that Qttada related to him that Anas b. Malik related to him that, "The Prophet (SAAS) performed the al-:;,uhr, the al-'a$r, the al-maghrib and the al-'ishia' prayers and laid down to rest at aI-Mul)",,~ab, rode to the ka'ba and circumambulated it." I note that he is referring to the tawil!al-wadil', the farewell circumambulation (after completion of the bijja). Al-Bukbarl stated that 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd al-Wahhab related to him, quoting Khalid b. aI-J::Iarith, who said, "'Abd Allah was asked about al-Mul)a~~ab and so 'Obayd Allah related to us, from Nafi', who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed there, as did 'Omar and Ibn 'Omar.'" And from Nafi' (it is narrated that), "Ibn 'Omar would pray there - meaning aI-Mul)a~~ab - for the al-:;,uhr and the al-'a$r prayers. I think he said, 'and the al-maghrib prayer'." Khalid (b. al-J::Iarith) stated, "I have no doubt about the al-'ishia' prayer. He would then lie down and sleep; and he would narrate that about the Prophet (SAAS)." Imam Ai)mad stated that Nul) b. Maymun related to him, quoting 'Abd Allah, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Vmar, who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS), Abu Bakr, 'Omar and 'Othman stayed at al-Mul)a~~ab. I saw it thus in the musnad of Imam Al)mad, from a badith of 'Abd Allah aI-'Omarl, from Nafi'. A1-Tirmidhl narrated this badith from Isi)aq b. Man~ur. Ibn Milja promulgated it from Mul)anunad b. YaJ:1ya, both quoting from 'Abd al-Razzaq, from 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Omar, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Omar, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS), Abu Bakr, 'Omar and 'Othman would stay at al-AbtaJ:1." Al-Tirmidhl stated, "And on this subject (there are abildith) from 'A'isha, Abu Rafi' and Ibn 'Abbas. The badith ofIbn 'Omar is basan gharib. And we only know it from a badith of'Abd aI-Razzaq, from 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Omar." Muslim narrated it from Mul)anunad b. Mahran al-Razl, from 'Abd al-Razzaq, from Ma'mar, from ;'\yynb, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Vmar (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS), Abu Bakr and 'Omar would stay at al-Abtal). Muslim also narrated it from a badith of Sakhr b. Juwayriyya, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Omar, (who said) that he would stay at aI-Mul)a~~ab, and that he would perform the al-:;,uhr prayer on the yawm al-nafr at aI-J::Iasaba. Nafi' stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed at al-Mul)assab, and that the caliphs did so after him. Imam Al)mad stated that YUnus related to him, quoting J::Iammad - meaning Ibn SaI;m;a - from Ayyub and J::Iumayd, from Bakr b. 'Abd Allah, from Ibn 'Omar, (who said) "The Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the al-:;,uhr, the al-'a$r, the al-maghrib and the al-'ishia' prayers at aI-Ba\i.ta' and he then slept. Thereafter he entered" -meaning Mecca - "and circ\lmambulated the ka'ba." AJ:unad also narrated it from 'Affan, from l;Iarnmad, from l;Iumayd, from Bakr, from Ibn 'Vmar. At the end of this badith he added, "Ibn 'Vmar would do this." Abu Da'ud narrated it like this from AJ:unad b. l;Ianbal. AI-BukhiirI stated that al-l;IurnaydI related to him, quoting al-Walld, quoting al-Awza'I, quoting aI-Zuhn, from Abu Salama, from Abu l;Iurayra, who said, "The Messenger of God (~AAS) said that as from tomorrow, the day for the sacrifice at Mina, (they would stay in the khayj); 'Tomorrow we will stay at the khayF 27 of Banu Kinana, where they pledged disbelief to one another.'" By this he was referring to aI-Mu\.!a~~ab. Muslim narrated it from Zuhayr b. l;Iarb, from aI-WalId b. Muslim, from aI-Aw?a'I. He related it the same. Imam A\.!mad stated that 'Abd al-Razzaq related to him, quoting Ma'mar, from al-ZuhrI, from 'All b. aI-l;Iusayn, from 'Amr b. 'Vthman, from Usama b. Zayd, who said, "I asked the Messenger of God (~AAS), 'Where will you stay tomorrow?' This was on his bijja. He replied, 'Has 'AqII left us anywhere to stay?' He then said, 'Tomorrow, if God wills it, we will stay at the khayfofBanu Kinana' - meaning aI-Mu\.!a~~ab - 'where they made a pact of unbelief with Quraysh.'" This referred to how Banu Kinana made a pact with Quraysh regarding Banu Hashim - that they would not intermarry, nor ally with them, nor accommodate them - until the latter had delivered over to them the Messenger of God (~AAS), that is. He (the Prophet (~AAS)) went on to say, "Muslims shall not iuherit from unbelievers, nor unbelievers from Muslims." AI-ZuhrI stated that the word al-khayfmeant aI-wadi, "the dry river bed". They (the authors of the two $abib collections) both gave it from a badith of 'Abd al-Razzaq. These two abiidith provide evidence that the Prophet (~AAS) planned to stay at aI-Mu\.!a~~ab to spite them for the agreement the Quraysh unbelievers had made when they signed the pact to boycott Banu Hashim and Banu aI-Munalib until they delivered over to them the Messenger of God (~AAS), as we have explained above in the appropriate place. He similarly stayed there in the year of the conquest (of Mecca). His having stayed there therefore constitutes a sunna to be emulated. This is one of the two comments made by the scholars. AI-Bukhart stated that Abu Nu'aym related to him, quoting Sufyan, from Hisham b. 'Vrwa, from his father, from 'A'isha, who said, "The Prophet (~AAS) only stayed there since it would be more convenient for his departure." This reference was to aI-Abta\.. Muslim gave it from a J;adlth of Hashim. Abu Da'ud narrated it from Al:tmad b. I:Ianbal, from Yal;tya b. Sa'id, from Hisham, from his father, from 'A'isha who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) only stayed at al-Mul:ta~~ab so that it would be more convenient for his departure. It is not a sunna. Staying there is a matter of personal preference." Al-Bukhari stated that 'Ali b. 'Abd Allah related to him, quoting SufYan, who said, "'Amr stated, quoting 'Ata' from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'Al-Tal:t~ib is nothing (of importance); it is just somewhere the Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed." Muslim narrated it from Abu Bakr b. Abu Shayba, and others from Sufyan, the latter being Ibn 'Uyayna. Abu Da'ud stated that Al:tmad b. I:Ianbal, 'Othman b. Abu Shayba al-Ma'ni and Musaddad stated that Sufyan related to them, quoting Salil:t b. Kaysan, from Sulayrnan b. Yasar, who said, "Abu Rafi' stated, 'He' - meaning the Messenger of God (SAAS) - 'did not order me to stay there. However, his pavilion was pitched and so he did stay there.'" Abu Da'ud quoted Musaddad as stating, "He was in charge of the baggage of the Prophet (SAAS)." Abu Da'ud also quoted 'Othman as stating that this (place) reference was to a1-Abtal:t. Muslim narrated it from Qutayba, Abu Bakr and Zuhayr b. I:Iarb from Sufyan b. 'Uyayna. The outcome of the above is that all of these are agreed that the Prophet (SAAS) did stay at a1-Mul:ta~~ab when he left from Mina. However, they do differ somewhat. Some (scholars) maintain that he had not actually planned to stay there but had done so by chance and for the conveneince of his departure. Others interpret his words to imply his having stayed there intentionally. This is more likely. That is because the Prophet (SAAS) ordered people that they spend their last time there at the ka'ba; before that they would leave from all parts, as Ibn 'Abbas stated. And so he ordered people that their last time there should be at the ka'ba - this reference being to the lawaI al-wada' "the farewell circumambulation". The Messenger of God (SAAS) wanted himself and those Muslims there with him to perform the lawaIal-wada'. He had departed from Mina at around noon and had not been able to corne to the ka'ba for the rest of that day, to circumambulate it and travel on into the outskirts of Mecca on the Medina side, since that would have been impossible for so numerous a throng. And so he needed to spend the night near Mecca. There was no place more appropriate for him to stay than a1-Mul:ta~~ab, where Quraysh had made a pact against Banu Hashim and Banu a1-Munalib. But God did not fulfil anything for Quraysh, but crushed and defeated them instead, making His religion dominant, aiding His Prophet and enhancing his influence. God fulfilled for him the true religion and through him made clear the straight path. And so he went on pilgrimage with the people and made clear to them God's rites and regulations. He had made his departure after having completed the rituals and then stayed in that place where Quraysh had sworn a pact for evil, aggression and boycott. There he performed the prayers for the al-:;.uhr, the al-'a$r, the al-maghrib and the al-'ishiil and then he went to sleep. He had dispatched 'j\'isha, "mother of the Believers", along with her brother 'Abd al-Ra1)man, to perform the al-'umra from al-Tan'lm and to come to him after finishing. When she had completed her 'umra and returned, he gave permission to the Muslims to proceed to the ancient lea'ba. As Abu Da'ud stated, "Wahb b. Baqiyya related to me, quoting Khalid, from Afla\., from a1-Qi'sim, from 'j\'isha, who said, 'I declared myself in a state of ibriim for the 'umra from a1-Tan'im, entered Mecca and performed it. The Messenger of God (~AAS) waited for me at a1-Abta\. until I had finished and then ordered the people to set off. He went to the ka'ba, circumambulated it and left.'" Both (authors of the $abib collections) included it, from a badith of Afla\. b. I;!umayd. Abu Da'ud went on to state that Mu\:lammad b. Bashshar related to him, quoting Abu Bakr - meaning al-I;!anafi - quoting Aflal;1, from a1-Q!isim, from. 'j\'isha, who said, "I went forth with him" - meaning the Messenger of God (~AAS) - "on the occasion of the final departure, and he stayed at al-Mu\:la~~ab." Abu Da'ud stated, "Ibn Bashshar mentioned her having been sent to a1-Tan'im. She said, 'I then came (back shortly) before daybreak. He gave his Companions permission to depart and did so himself. He passed by the ka'ba before the al-$ubb prayer. When he left, he circumambulated it and proceeded offto Medina.'" AI-Bukhari narrated it from Mu\:lammad b. Bashshar. I observe that it is clearthat the Prophet (~AAS) performed the al-$ubb prayer with his COl)1panions that day at the ka'ba. In that prayer of his, he recited, "By Mt. ai-Till and a book inscribed on a stretched leather! By the inhabited house and the raised roofl And by the swelling sea" (surat al-Tur; LII, v.I~). This (conclusion) stems from what al-Bukhan narrated. He stated that Isma'll related to him, quoting Malik, from Mu\:lanunad b. 'Abd a1-Ra\:lman b. Nawfal, from 'Urwa b. a1-Zubayr, from Zaynab, daughter of Abu Salama, from Umm Salama, the wife of the Messenger of God (~AAS), who said, "I complained to the Messenger of God (~AAS), telling him I was in pain. He replied, 'Circumambulate at the rear of the people,
lessons: God's promise always prevails over human schemes of oppression—the very place where enemies plotted against the Prophet became a place of his triumphant worship and rest.
Following the Sunnah (prophetic practice) with understanding of its context deepens both faith and resilience; the Prophet's actions carried layers of spiritual and historical meaning.
Patience and steadfastness in the face of boycott and persecution, as demonstrated by the Prophet and Banu Hashim, are ultimately vindicated by divine support.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in a place or situation that once caused you pain, try to reframe it as a site of your growth and God's vindication—your presence there as a person of faith is itself a form of victory.

When facing confusion about religious practice (as scholars differed about Al-Muhassab), approach scholarly disagreement with humility, recognizing that sincere, informed Muslims can differ on secondary matters without division.

Use moments of rest and transition—like the Prophet's overnight stay before the farewell circumambulation—as opportunities for reflection, prayer, and gratitude before moving to the next chapter of your journey.

title: Aisha's Umra from Al-Tan'im and the Prophet's Patient Wait at Al-Abtah
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) sent his wife Aisha with her brother Abd al-Rahman to perform Umra from Al-Tan'im after she had been unable to perform it earlier due to illness. He waited patiently for her at Al-Abtah until she completed her Umra before sunrise, then led the Muslims to the Ka'ba for the farewell circumambulation and departed for Medina.
story: He had dispatched 'j\'isha, "mother of the Believers", along with her brother 'Abd al-Ra1)man, to perform the al-'umra from al-Tan'lm and to come to him after finishing. When she had completed her 'umra and returned, he gave permission to the Muslims to proceed to the ancient lea'ba. As Abu Da'ud stated, "Wahb b. Baqiyya related to me, quoting Khalid, from Afla\., from a1-Qi'sim, from 'j\'isha, who said, 'I declared myself in a state of ibriim for the 'umra from a1-Tan'im, entered Mecca and performed it. The Messenger of God (~AAS) waited for me at a1-Abta\. until I had finished and then ordered the people to set off. He went to the ka'ba, circumambulated it and left.'" Both (authors of the $abib collections) included it, from a badith of Afla\. b. I;!umayd. Abu Da'ud went on to state that Mu\:lammad b. Bashshar related to him, quoting Abu Bakr - meaning al-I;!anafi - quoting Aflal;1, from a1-Q!isim, from. 'j\'isha, who said, "I went forth with him" - meaning the Messenger of God (~AAS) - "on the occasion of the final departure, and he stayed at al-Mu\:la~~ab." Abu Da'ud stated, "Ibn Bashshar mentioned her having been sent to a1-Tan'im. She said, 'I then came (back shortly) before daybreak. He gave his Companions permission to depart and did so himself. He passed by the ka'ba before the al-$ubb prayer. When he left, he circumambulated it and proceeded offto Medina.'" AI-Bukhari narrated it from Mu\:lammad b. Bashshar. I observe that it is clearthat the Prophet (~AAS) performed the al-$ubb prayer with his COl)1panions that day at the ka'ba. In that prayer of his, he recited, "By Mt. ai-Till and a book inscribed on a stretched leather! By the inhabited house and the raised roofl And by the swelling sea" (surat al-Tur; LII, v.I~).
lessons: The Prophet's willingness to wait patiently through the night for Aisha to complete her Umra demonstrates that caring for the spiritual needs of those in our charge is a form of worship itself.
No one should be left behind in their spiritual journey if it is within our power to help them complete it; the Prophet made sure Aisha had the opportunity to fulfill her religious obligation.
Acts of devotion completed under difficulty—Aisha performing Umra at night after a challenging pilgrimage—carry a special weight of sincerity and sacrifice.
practical_advice: When someone you care for is struggling to fulfill their goals or obligations, offer practical support and wait for them without complaint, as the Prophet modeled by waiting at Al-Abtah.
If you miss an important spiritual or personal goal due to circumstances beyond your control, seek another opportunity to fulfill it rather than giving up; God honors sincere effort.
Begin important transitions—like the Prophet beginning his farewell journey with the Fajr prayer at the Ka'ba—with worship and remembrance of God to anchor your heart before the road ahead.

title: Umm Salama's Circumambulation During Farewell Pilgrimage
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, Umm Salama was ill and unable to circumambulate on foot. The Prophet instructed her to circumambulate on her camel at the rear of the people while he led the morning prayer beside the Ka'ba.
story: I complained to the Messenger of God (ṣAAS), telling him I was in pain. He replied, 'Circumambulate at the rear of the people, while mounted.' I did so while he was praying that day beside the ka'ba and reciting, 'By Mt. al-Tur and a book inscribed ...' Al-Bukhari narrated it from a ḥadith of Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, from Zaynab, from Umm Salama, to the effect that the Messenger of God (ṣAAS) was at Mecca and wished to leave; however, Umm Salama had not yet circumambulated but wanted to leave. He then told her, 'When the al-Ṣubḥ prayer is being performed, circumambulate on your camel while the people are praying.' Imām Aḥmad narrated that Abu Mu'awiya related to him, quoting Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, from Zaynab, daughter of Abu Salama, from Umm Salama, who said that the Messenger of God (ṣAAS), 'ordered her to circumambulate at Mecca with him at the time of the al-Ṣubḥ prayer on the day of the sacrifice.'
lessons: Islam makes sincere accommodation for those who are ill or physically limited, showing that religious observance is built on mercy and ease.
The Prophet's personal attention to an individual's hardship, even during one of the most significant religious events, reflects the depth of his pastoral care.
Fulfilling one's religious duties, even in a modified form when facing genuine difficulty, reflects both devotion and trust in God's mercy.
practical_advice: When you are unwell but wish to fulfill a spiritual or religious obligation, seek legitimate accommodations rather than abandoning the practice altogether.
If you are supporting someone who is ill or struggling, look for creative, compassionate ways to help them participate rather than excluding them.
Remember that God and the Prophet's example emphasize ease over hardship — when overwhelmed by circumstances, seek the permitted relief without guilt.

title: The Prophet's Farewell to the Ka'ba at al-Multazam
summary: After completing his farewell circumambulation, the Prophet pressed his face and chest to the al-Multazam — the sacred space between the Black Stone and the door of the Ka'ba — and offered a heartfelt supplication to God before departing Mecca.
story: When the Messenger of God (ṣAAS) had completed the al-Ṣubḥ prayer, he performed seven circumambulations of the ka'ba and stood at the al-multazam that is between the corner bearing the 'black stone' and the door of the ka'ba. He said a prayer to God, the Almighty and Glorious, and pressed his body close to the ka'ba. Al-Thawri stated, from al-Muthanna b. al-Ṣabbāḥ, from 'Atā b. Shu'ayb, from his father, who quoted his grandfather as saying, 'I saw the Messenger of God (ṣAAS) press his face and his chest to the al-multazam.'
lessons: Expressing heartfelt emotion before God — including in moments of farewell — is a sign of spiritual depth, not weakness.
Physical acts of devotion, such as pressing oneself to a sacred place in supplication, can carry profound meaning when done with sincere intention.
Even the Prophet, the greatest of God's creation, turned to God in earnest supplication and closeness, reminding us that all strength comes from Him alone.
practical_advice: During moments of transition or farewell, take time to pause and offer a sincere prayer of gratitude and trust before moving forward.
Incorporate physical postures of humility and closeness to God — such as prostration or raising your hands — to deepen your emotional connection during supplication.
When leaving a beloved place, person, or chapter of life, mark the departure with a moment of prayer rather than rushing past the significance of the moment.

title: The Prophet's Departure from Mecca by the Lower Pass
summary: The Prophet departed Mecca from its lower pass after having entered from its upper pass, following a distinct route that his companions carefully preserved as a sunnah.
story: The Messenger of God (ṣAAS) then made his departure from the lower part of Mecca. As 'Ā'isha stated, 'The Messenger of God (ṣAAS) entered Mecca from its upper part and left from its lower part.' Both Ṣaḥīḥ collections include this. Ibn 'Umar stated, 'The Messenger of God (ṣAAS) entered from the upper pass at al-Baṭḥā', and left by the lower pass.' Al-Bukhārī and Muslim narrated this. In one version the wording is, 'He entered from Kadā' and left from Kudā.'
lessons: The Prophet's deliberate choices, even in matters that may seem minor such as which road to travel, carried spiritual intention and meaning.
Those who loved the Prophet observed and preserved even small details of his conduct, reflecting how love for a teacher manifests in careful attention.
Every departure and every arrival in our lives can be approached with mindfulness and intention, as the Prophet demonstrated.
practical_advice: Bring intentionality to your comings and goings — how you leave a situation or relationship can be just as meaningful as how you enter it.
Cultivate the habit of observing and learning from those you respect and love, preserving their wisdom in your memory and practice.
When closing a significant chapter of life, do so with grace and purposefulness rather than simply drifting away.

title: The Prophet Carrying Zamzam Water Upon Departure
summary: The Prophet Muhammad and 'Ā'isha were reported to have carried water from the sacred Zamzam well when departing from Mecca, demonstrating the special reverence held for this blessed water.
story: The Ḥāfiẓ Abū 'Īsā al-Tirmidhī stated that Abu Kurayb related to him, quoting Khallād b. Yazīd al-Ju'fī, quoting Zuhayr b. Mu'āwiya, from Hishām b. 'Urwa, from his father, from 'Ā'isha, (who said) that she would carry water from zamzam and she reported that the Messenger of God (ṣAAS) would carry it. He (al-Tirmidhī) then stated, 'This ḥadīth is ḥasan gharīb; we know of it only from this line.'
lessons: Carrying a physical reminder of a sacred experience helps sustain spiritual connection long after the moment has passed.
The Prophet's act of taking Zamzam water reflects a deep appreciation for God's blessings and a desire to remain close to them.
Small, personal acts of devotion — like carrying blessed water — can nurture spiritual awareness in daily life.
