practical_advice: When returning from a spiritually significant experience such as a pilgrimage, retreat, or worship gathering, bring something tangible back with you as a reminder of what you felt and learned.
Cherish and use sacred or meaningful objects intentionally, allowing them to reconnect you to God when daily life feels distant from the spiritual.
Find small, consistent practices that anchor your spiritual memory and keep the heart connected to times of closeness with God.

title: The Prophet's Supplication Upon Returning from Hajj or Battle
summary: Whenever the Prophet returned from a military expedition, Hajj, or 'Umra, he would proclaim God's greatness three times and recite a specific supplication acknowledging God's power, fulfillment of His promise, and support for His servant.
story: Al-Bukhārī stated that Muḥammad b. Muqātil related to him, quoting 'Abd Allāh — he being Ibn al-Mubārak — quoting Mūsā b. 'Uqba, from Sālim and Nāfi', from 'Abd Allāh b. 'Umar (who said) 'When the Messenger of God (ṣAAS) returned from a military expedition or from the ḥajj or the 'umra, he would thrice proclaim, "God is most Great!". Then he would say, "There is no god but God alone! He has no associate! Power is His, and praise. He has control over everything. We return, repenting, worshipping, prostrating to our Lord, praising. God has fulfilled His promise, given aid to His servant, and defeated the factions (by Himself) alone."' The accounts stating this are numerous.
lessons: Returning from any significant trial, journey, or achievement is best marked by turning to God in gratitude rather than pride or self-congratulation.
The Prophet's supplication models how to acknowledge both human effort and divine support — recognizing that all outcomes come from God.
Establishing routines of gratitude at the conclusion of significant events trains the heart to remain humble and connected to its source of strength.
practical_advice: After completing a major goal, project, or difficult period, take a moment to give thanks to God before celebrating or moving on.
Memorize and use a short supplication of gratitude that you return to at the end of significant experiences — this creates spiritual continuity across life's chapters.
When you feel proud of an accomplishment, consciously redirect that energy toward gratitude to God, acknowledging that the ability and opportunity came from Him.

title: The Address at Ghadir Khumm: Defending 'Ali's Honor
summary: On his return from the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet stopped at Ghadir Khumm and delivered a major address to defend the honor of 'Ali ibn Abi Talib, who had been criticized by some companions over decisions he made during an expedition to Yemen.
story: When 'Ali arrived from Yemen to meet the Messenger of God (ṣAAS) in Mecca, he hurried ahead leaving behind one of his men in command of his troops. The latter proceeded to dress each of his men in a special robe made from the cloth that 'Ali had. When the army approached, he ('Ali) went out to meet them and was surprised to see them dressed in robes. 'What is all this?' he asked. He (his deputy commander) replied, 'I dressed up the men so that they would look well when they arrive among the people.' 'Confound you!' 'Ali exclaimed. 'Take them off before you get to the Messenger of God.' His deputy proceeded to remove their robes and replaced them in the store of cloth. The army expressed complaints at how he ('Ali) had treated them. Ibn Isḥāq went on to state that Abu Sa'īd al-Khudrī said, 'People complained about 'Ali and the Messenger of God (ṣAAS) arose among us to make an address. I heard him say, "People! Don't complain about 'Ali! I swear by God, he is too rigorous regarding God and His cause than for him to be the object of complaint."' Imām Aḥmad also stated from Burayda, who said, 'I went on the expedition to Yemen with 'Ali and considered his behaviour harsh. When I reached the Messenger of God (ṣAAS), I referred to 'Ali with criticism. I saw the expression on the face of the Messenger of God (ṣAAS) change and he said, "Burayda, do I not have a higher claim on the Muslims than they have on themselves?" "Yes indeed, Messenger of God," I replied. He said, "Whoever has myself as his lord, so is 'Ali his lord."'
lessons: A leader who acts with principled strictness in the cause of God may be misunderstood or criticized by those who do not see the full picture — patience in such moments is essential.
When someone we trust and respect is unjustly criticized, standing up to clarify the truth is a moral responsibility, as the Prophet demonstrated.
Misunderstandings between people often arise from incomplete information; seeking full context before judging someone's actions reflects wisdom and fairness.
practical_advice: When you are criticized or misunderstood despite acting with integrity, trust that those who truly know your intentions will eventually recognize the truth.
If you witness someone being unfairly treated or spoken about in your community, find a respectful and clear way to speak in their defense.
Before forming a negative judgment about someone's actions — especially a leader's — pause and ask whether you have the full picture of the circumstances they were navigating.

title: The Declaration at Ghadir Khumm: 'Ali as Guardian of the Believers
summary: During the farewell pilgrimage, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) stopped at Ghadir Khumm and publicly declared 'Ali ibn Abi Talib as the guardian of all believers, with witnesses later testifying to this event. Multiple chains of narration from companions including Zayd ibn Arqam and al-Bara' ibn 'Azib confirm this declaration.
story: so is 'AlI his lord.' Al-Nasa'i narrated it thus from Abu Da'ud al-Harrani, from Abu Nu'aym al-Fadl b. Dukayn, from 'Abd al-Malik b. Abu Ghaniyya; his line of transmission is similar. This line is excellent and firm; all the men quoted are considered trustworthy. Al-Nasa'i narrated in his al-Sunan from Muhammad b. al-Muthanna, from Yahya b. Hammad, from Abu Mu'awiya, from al-A'mash, from Habib b. Abu Thabit, from Abu al-Tufayl, who quoted Zayd b. Arqam as having said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) returned from the Hijjat al-wada' and stayed at Ghadir Khumm, he ordered that the area under the large trees be swept. He then said, "It is as if I have received an invitation and have accepted. I have left among you the two treasures: the Book of God and my family, my household. Watch how you succeed me in both these. They will never split apart until they come to me at al-hawdh." He then said, "God is my Lord, and I am the guardian of every believer." He then took 'Ali by the hand and said, "Whoever has myself as his lord, so is this (man) his guardian. O God, protect all who protect him, and oppose all who oppose him."' 'I (Abu al-Tufayl) asked Zayd, "You actually heard this from the Messenger of God (SAAS)?" He replied, "There was no one in the tents who did not see him with his own eyes and hear him with his own ears."' Al-Nasa'i is alone in giving this from this line. Our teacher, Sheikh Abu 'Abd Allah b. Dhahabi said that this was an authentic hadith. Ibn Maja stated that 'Ali b. Muhammad related to him, quoting Abu al-Husayn, quoting Hammad b. Salama, from 'Ali b. Zayd b. Jud'an, from 'Adi b. Thabit, from al-Bara' b. 'Azib, who said, 'We came (to Mecca) with the Messenger of God (SAAS), on the Hijjat al-wada' that he attended. He made a halt on the way and gave orders that there be a general prayer. He took 'Ali by the hand and said, "Do I not have a higher claim on the Muslims than they have on themselves?" They replied, "Yes, indeed!" He then asked, "Do I not have a higher claim on each Muslim than he has on himself?" "Yes, indeed," they replied. He then said, "This (man) is the lord of all those whose lord I am. O God, protect those who protect him and oppose all who oppose him."' 'Abd al-Razzaq narrated it thus, from Ma'mar, from Ali b. Zayd b. Jud'an, from 'Adi, from al-Bara'. The hafiz Abu Ya'la al-Mawsili and al-Hasan b. Sufyan stated that Hudba related to them, quoting Hammad b. Salama, from 'Ali b. Zayd and Abu Harun, from 'Adi b. Thabit, from al-Bara', who said, 'We were with the Messenger of God (SAAS), on the Hijjat al-wada'. When we came to Ghadir Khumm, the ground beneath two trees was swept for the Messenger of God (SAAS). A call was made for a general prayer. The Messenger of God (SAAS) summoned 'Ali, took him by the hand and placed him to his right. He then asked, "Do I not have a higher claim on each man than he has on himself?" They replied, "Yes, indeed." He then said, "This man is lord of all those whose lord I am. O God, protect all who protect him and oppose those who oppose him." "'Umar b. al-Khattab met him and he said, "Good for you! Morning, noon and night you're to be lord of every believing man and woman!""' Ibn Jarir narrated it from Abu Zur'a, from Musa b. Isma'il, from Hammad b. Salama, from 'Ali b. Zayd and Abu Harun al-'Abdi - both of whom are weak sources - from 'Adi b. Thabit, from al-Bara' b. 'Azib. Ibn Jarir narrated this hadith from a hadith of Musa b. 'Othman al-Hadrami - a very weak source - from Abu Ishaq al-Sabi'i, from al-Bara' and Zayd b. Arqam. But God knows best. Imam Ahmad stated that Ibn Numayr related to him, quoting 'Abd al-Malik, from Abu 'Abd al-Rahim al-Kindi, from Zadhan Abu 'Umar, who said, 'I heard 'Ali at al-Rahba while he was addressing the people saying, "Who witnessed the Messenger of God (SAAS), on the day at Ghadir Khumm when he spoke as he did?"' Zadhan went on, 'Twelve men arose and bore witness that they had heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, "Those whose lord I have been have 'Ali as their lord."' Ahmad is alone in giving this; the 'Abu 'Abd al-Rahim' quoted is not known. 'Abd Allah, son of al-Imam Ahmad, stated in his father's musnad hadith collection, 'A hadith of 'Ali b. Hakim al-'Azdi quoted Sharik, from Abu Ishaq, from Sa'id b. Wahb, and from Zayd b. Yuthay', who said, "'Ali addressed those at al-Rahba, asking, "Anyone who heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) speak on the day at Ghadir Khumm should rise." "Six men arose on behalf of Sa'id, while six men arose on behalf of Zayd. They testified that they had heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say to 'Ali on the day at Ghadir Khumm, "Is not God more solicitous of the Muslims than (they are) of themselves?" "Yes indeed," they replied. He then asked, "O God, those whose lord I have been have 'Ali as their lord. O God, protect those who protect him and oppose those who oppose him.""' 'Abd Allah went on to state that 'Ali b. Hakim related to him, quoting Sharik, from Abu Ishaq, from 'Amr Dhu Amr, a hadith similar to that of Abu Ishaq, from Sa'id and Zayd, that is. He added to it the words, 'Aid those who aid him and forsake those whom he forsakes.' 'Abd Allah stated that 'Ali related to him, quoting Sharik, from al-A'mash, from Habib b. Abu Thabit, from Abu al-Tufayl, from Zayd b. Arqam, who quoted the Prophet (SAAS), as above. Al-Nasa'i stated in the book Khasa'is 'Ali that al-Husayn b. Harb related to him, quoting al-Fadl b. Musa, from al-A'mash, from Abu Ishaq, from Sa'id b. Wahb, who said, "'Ali said at al-Rahba, 'I adjure by God any man who heard the Messenger of God (SAAS), that day at Ghadir Khumm say, "God is the lord of the Believers and those whose lord I have been have this (man) as their lord. O God, protect those who protect him, oppose those who oppose him and aid those who aid him" (to rise and so testify)'." Shu'ba narrated it thus from Abu Ishaq. And this is an excellent line of transmission. Al-Nasa'i also narrated it from a hadith of Isra'il, from Abu Ishaq, from 'Amr Dhu Amr, (who said) "'Ali adjured the people at al-Rahba and some men got up and bore witness that they had heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say on the day at Ghadir Khumm, 'Those whose lord I have been (now have) 'Ali as their lord. O God, protect those who protect him, oppose those who oppose him, and favour those who favour him; hate those who hate him and aid those who aid him.'" Ibn Jarir narrated it from Ahmad b. Mansur, from 'Abd al-Razzaq, from Isra'il, from Abu Ishaq, from Zayd b. Wahb and 'Abd Khayr, from 'Ali. Ibn Jarir also narrated it from Ahmad b. Mansur, from 'Ubayd Allah b. Musa, who is a Shi'i considered a trustworthy source, from Matar b. Khalifa, from Abu Ishaq, from Zayd b. Wahb, Zayd b. Yuthay', and 'Amr Dhu Amr (who said) that 'Ali adjured the people at al-Kufa. He then gave the hadith as above. 'Abd Allah b. Ahmad stated that 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar al-Qawariri related to him, quoting Yunus b. Arqam, quoting Yazid b. Abu Ziyad, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abu Layla (who said) 'I witnessed 'Ali at al-Rahba adjuring the people. He said, "I adjure by God anyone who heard the Messenger of God (SAAS), on the day at Ghadir Khumm, say, "Anyone whose guardian I have been, has 'Ali as his guardian" to get up and so testify."' 'Abd al-Rahman went on, 'So twelve men arose; it is as though I am at this moment looking at one of them. They said, "We testify that we heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say on the day at Ghadir Khumm, 'Do I not have a higher claim on the Muslims than they have on themselves? And are not my wives like their own mothers?' 'Yes indeed, Messenger of God,' we replied. He went on, 'Those whose lord I have been have 'Ali as their lord. O God, protect those who protect him and oppose those who oppose him.'"' This line of transmission is da'if gharib, 'weak and anomalous'. 'Abd Allah b. Ahmad stated that Ahmad b. Numayr al-Waki'i stated, quoting Zayd b. al-Hubab, quoting al-Walid b. 'Uqba b. Dirar al-Qaysi, quoting Simak, from 'Ubayd b. al-Walid al-Qaysi, who said, 'I went in to 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abu Layla and he related to me that he witnessed 'Ali at al-Rahba. He said, "He adjured any man who had heard and witnessed the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the day at Ghadir Khumm to rise; only those who had actually seen him should arise. Twelve men got up and said, 'We saw and heard him when he took him, ('Ali) by the hand, saying, "O God, protect those who protect him and oppose those who oppose him. Aid whoever aids him and forsake any who forsake him." (All) except three (who had heard him) stood up; these three did not. He then cursed them (the three) and his curse was effective.'"' It was also narrated from 'Abd al-A'la b. 'Amir al-Tha'labi and others, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abu Layla. Ibn Jarir stated that Ahmad b. Mansur related to him, quoting Abu 'Amir al-'Aqdi and Ibn Abu 'Asim narrated, from Sulayman al-Ghallabi, from Abu 'Amir al-'Aqdi, quoting Kathir b. Zayd, quoting Muhammad b. 'Umar b. 'Ali, from his father, from 'Ali (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was present near a tree at Khumm. He went on to relate the hadith which contained the words, 'Whose lord I have been, has 'Ali as his lord.' Some authorities narrated it from Abu 'Amir, from Kathir, from Muhammad b. 'Umar b. 'Ali, from 'Ali, with a line of transmission that is munqati'. Isma'il b. 'Amr al-Bajali - he being a weak source - stated, from Mis'ar, from Talha b. Musrif, from 'Umayra b. Sa'd (who said) that he witnessed 'Ali on the minbar adjuring the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS), who had heard the latter on the day at Ghadir Khumm (to stand up). Twelve men did stand; these included Abu Hurayra, Abu Sa'id and Anas b. Malik. They testified that they had heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, 'Anyone whose lord I have been has 'Ali as his lord. O God, protect those who protect him and oppose those who oppose him.' 'Ubayd Allah b. Musa narrated it from Hani' b. Ayyub - he being considered a trustworthy source - from Talha b. Musarrif. 'Abd Allah b. Ahmad stated that Hajjaj b. al-Sha'ir related to him, quoting Shababa, quoting Nu'aym b. Hakim, quoting Abu Maryam and a man who was a close companion of 'Ali, who quoted 'Ali as saying that, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) said on the day at Ghadir Khumm, "Anyone whose lord I have been, has 'Ali as his lord." He went on to say that, "Some people add to that the words, 'protect those who protect him and oppose those who oppose him.'"'
lessons: The Prophet's declaration at Ghadir Khumm demonstrates that leadership and spiritual authority must be clearly designated and publicly witnessed, so that communities are not left without guidance after a leader departs.

The preservation of testimony across multiple chains of narration — with companions like 'Ali later calling on eyewitnesses to stand and testify — teaches us that truth must be actively maintained, recalled, and upheld by those who witnessed it, even long after the event.

The concept of wilayah (guardianship and loyalty) as proclaimed at Ghadir Khumm reminds believers that loyalty to those who carry divine trust is not merely a political matter but a spiritual obligation rooted in love and sincere devotion to God.
practical_advice: When you feel confused about who to trust or follow in matters of faith or leadership, return to the primary sources — the Quran and authenticated Sunnah — and seek scholars who trace their knowledge through reliable chains, just as the companions verified the Ghadir Khumm event through multiple witnesses.

If you have witnessed something true and important, do not let fear or social pressure cause you to remain silent; the companions who stood at al-Rahba to testify modeled the courage of speaking truth even years after the event.

Cultivate loyalty and love for those who carry the trust of guiding the community in righteousness; express that loyalty not merely in words but in defending their honor and supporting their work, as the Prophet instructed believers to do for 'Ali.

title: The Declaration at Ghadir Khumm: 'Anyone Whose Lord I Have Been Has Ali as His Lord'
summary: At Ghadir Khumm, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) stopped the caravan, addressed the gathered believers, and declared that anyone whose lord he had been would also have Ali as their lord, calling on God to protect those who protect Ali and oppose those who oppose him. Multiple chains of transmission from companions including Zayd b. Arqam, Jabir b. Abd Allah, and Abu Hurayra confirm the event.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Husayn b. Muhammad and Abu Nu'aym al-Ma'ni both stated that Qitn quoted Abu al-Tufayl as having said, "'Ali assembled the people at al-Rahba - meaning the courtyard of the mosque at al-Kufa - and said, 'God calls upon all who heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) speak at Ghadir Khumm to stand (and say) what they heard.' Many people did stand and testified to his having taken 'Ali by the hand and having asked the people, 'Do you know that I have a higher claim on the people than they have on themselves?' They replied, 'Yes, Messenger of God.' He continued, 'Anyone whose lord I have been has 'Ali as his lord. O God, protect those who protect him and oppose those who oppose him.' "I then left, feeling somewhat disturbed, and met Zayd b. Arqam. I told him what I had heard 'Ali say. He asked, 'What is it you are disputing? I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say that to him.'" Imam Ahmad related it thus in the musnad ascribed to Zayd b. Arqam, may God be pleased with him. Al-Nasa'i narrated it from a hadith of al-A'mash, from Habib b. Abu Thabit, from Abu al-Tufayl, from Zayd b. Arqam. Imam Ahmad stated that Yahya b. Adam related to him, quoting Hanash b. al-Harith b. Laqit al-Ashja'i, from Rabbah b. al-Harith, who said, "A group of men came to 'Ali at al-Rahba and said, 'Peace be upon you, lord of ours!' He replied, 'How can I be your "lord" when you are Arabs?' They responded, 'We heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say on the day at Ghadir Khumm, "Anyone whose lord I have been has this man as his lord."'" Rabbah went on, "When they left, I went in after them and asked, 'Who were those?' They replied, 'A group of ansar; Abu Ayyub al-Ansari was one of them.'" Ibn Jarir stated that Ahmad b. Uthman Abu al-Jawsa' related to him, quoting Muhammad b. Khalid b. Athama, quoting Musa b. Ya'qub al-Zam'i - he being entirely trustworthy - quoting Muhajir b. Mismar, from 'A'isha, daughter of Sa'd, who quoted her father as saying, "I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) speak on the day at al-Juhfa. He took 'Ali by the hand and made an address. Then he said, 'People, I am your guardian.' 'You are right,' the people responded. He then raised 'Ali's hand and said, 'This is my wali, "my deputy", and mu'addi, "the one deputized by me". God is the protector of those who protect him and the opponent of those who oppose him.'" Our teacher al-Dhahabi stated, "This hadith is hasan gharib, 'good yet anomalous'." Imam Ahmad stated that al-Zubayri related to him, quoting Shurayk, from Abu Ishaq, from Habashi b. Janada in similar terms, "I asked Abu Ishaq, 'Where did you hear him say this?' He replied, 'He stood over us on a mare at our majlis, "assembly", at the al-Sabrc cemetery.'" The hafiz Abu Yaqa al-Mawsili stated that Abu Bakr b. Abu Shayba related to him, quoting Shurayk, from Abu Yazid al-Azdi, from his father, who said, "Abu Hurayra came in to the mosque and the people gathered about him. One young man went up to him and said, 'I adjure you by God to tell whether you heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, "Anyone whose lord I have been has 'Ali as his lord. O God, protect those who protect him and oppose those who oppose him." "Yes," he replied.'" Also there is the hadith narrated by Damra from Ibn Shawdhab, from Mafar al-Warraq, from Shahr b. Hawshab, from Abu Hurayra, which states, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) took 'Ali by the hand, he said, 'Anyone whose lord I have been has 'Ali as his lord.' And so God the Almighty and Glorious revealed, 'Today I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favours upon you'" (surat al-Ma'ida; V, v.3).
lessons: The Prophet's public declaration at Ghadir Khumm shows that important guidance is meant to be witnessed, remembered, and transmitted by a community, not kept secret — truth gains strength through collective testimony.

When something feels disturbing or confusing, the example of Abu al-Tufayl teaches us to seek clarification from those who have direct knowledge rather than remaining unsettled; going to a trustworthy source resolves doubt.

Loyalty and love are values that must be acted upon publicly; the companions who stood to testify demonstrate that witnessing truth, even when it is inconvenient or contested, is an act of integrity and devotion.
practical_advice: When you feel confused or unsettled by something you have heard, write down your questions and actively seek out a knowledgeable, trustworthy person to discuss them with rather than letting doubt fester.
If you are navigating a disagreement within your community, look to the example of those who sought witnesses and testimony — bring evidence and calm clarity to disputes rather than emotion alone.

Reflect on who in your life you have pledged loyalty and support to, and ask yourself whether your actions consistently reflect that commitment, especially in moments when it is not easy or convenient to do so.

title: Ali's Assembly at al-Rahba: Calling Witnesses to the Ghadir Khumm Declaration
summary: Years after the Prophet's death, Ali gathered the people at the courtyard of the mosque in Kufa and called upon those who had personally heard the Prophet's declaration at Ghadir Khumm to stand and testify. Many companions rose and bore witness, affirming what they had heard directly from the Prophet.
story: 'Ali assembled the people at al-Rahba - meaning the courtyard of the mosque at al-Kufa - and said, 'God calls upon all who heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) speak at Ghadir Khumm to stand (and say) what they heard.' Many people did stand and testified to his having taken 'Ali by the hand and having asked the people, 'Do you know that I have a higher claim on the people than they have on themselves?' They replied, 'Yes, Messenger of God.' He continued, 'Anyone whose lord I have been has 'Ali as his lord. O God, protect those who protect him and oppose those who oppose him.' "I then left, feeling somewhat disturbed, and met Zayd b. Arqam. I told him what I had heard 'Ali say. He asked, 'What is it you are disputing? I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say that to him.'"
lessons: Truth does not expire with the passing of time; the companions' willingness to stand and testify publicly decades after the event shows that honest witness has no statute of limitations.
When facing community conflict or contested claims, Ali's example shows the wisdom of appealing to shared experience and collective memory rather than relying solely on personal authority.

The discomfort felt by Abu al-Tufayl upon hearing Ali's call, and his subsequent search for confirmation, illustrates that spiritual and intellectual honesty requires us to investigate before we judge.
practical_advice: If you are in a dispute where your credibility or memory is being questioned, consider calmly identifying others who shared the same experience and can speak to what actually occurred.
When you feel disturbed or unsettled by a public claim, resist the urge to react immediately; instead, seek out a wise, informed person and have a quiet conversation before forming your conclusion.
Practice being a person whose word is reliable — show up, speak truthfully, and support others when your testimony can bring clarity and justice to a situation.

title: The Hadith of Habashi b. Janada: 'Ali Is of Me and I Am of Him'
summary: The companion Habashi b. Janada, present at the Farewell Pilgrimage, narrated that the Prophet declared Ali to be of him and he of Ali, and that no one settles debts on his behalf except himself or Ali. This hadith was transmitted through multiple chains and authenticated by al-Tirmidhi.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Yahya b. Adam and Ibn Abu Bukayr related to him, that Isra'il related to them both, from Abu Ishaq, from Habashi b. Janada - who had, Yahya b. Adam stated, been present on the hijjat al-wada' - and stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Ali is of me, and I am of him; and only I or 'Ali direct from me.'" Ibn Abu Bukayr said (that the words spoken were), "No one settles debts on my behalf except myself or 'Ali." Ahmad also narrated it thus from Abu Ahmad al-Zubayri, from Isra'il. Imam Ahmad stated that al-Zubayri related to him, quoting Shurayk, from Abu Ishaq, from Habashi b. Janada in similar terms, "I asked Abu Ishaq, 'Where did you hear him say this?' He replied, 'He stood over us on a mare at our majlis, "assembly", at the al-Sabrc cemetery.'" Al-Tirmidhi characterized it as hasan sahih gharib.
lessons: The Prophet's declaration that Ali is 'of him' reflects a principle of deep spiritual and personal bonds forged through shared values and trust, reminding us that our closest relationships reflect our own character.
Authentic transmission of knowledge requires diligence, verification, and care; the scholars who traced each narrator in this chain model how seriously we should treat the sources of our beliefs.

Being entrusted with a weighty responsibility, as Ali was entrusted to settle the Prophet's debts and obligations, is a mark of deep trust — and fulfilling such trust faithfully is among the highest forms of loyalty.
practical_advice: Reflect on the relationships in your life where someone has said, in effect, 'I trust only you with this.' Honor that trust with consistency, follow-through, and discretion.

When evaluating information you receive — whether religious, personal, or professional — ask yourself about the reliability of the source and the chain through which it reached you before acting on it.
Consider whether there are any unresolved obligations or 'debts' in your relationships — emotional, practical, or otherwise — and take steps this week to address at least one of them.

title: The Fabricated Hadith About Fasting the 18th of Dhu al-Hijja and Its Scholarly Refutation
summary: A hadith attributed to Abu Hurayra claimed that fasting the 18th of Dhu al-Hijja earns the reward of 60 months of fasting, and linked the Ghadir Khumm event to the revelation of the verse on the perfection of religion. Scholars including al-Dhahabi categorized this hadith as fabricated and objectionable, contradicting established authentic reports.
story: Also there is the hadith narrated by Damra from Ibn Shawdhab, from Mafar al-Warraq, from Shahr b. Hawshab, from Abu Hurayra, which states, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) took 'Ali by the hand, he said, 'Anyone whose lord I have been has 'Ali as his lord.' And so God the Almighty and Glorious revealed, 'Today I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favours upon you'" (surat al-Ma'ida; V, v.3). Abu Hurayra went on, "This was the day at Ghadir Khumm; whoever fasts the 18th day of Dhu al-Hijja has credited for him a fast of 60 months." This is a highly objectionable hadith; indeed it is mendacious! Because contradictory material is established in both Sahih collections from the Commander of the Believers, Umar b. al-Khattab, to the effect that this verse was revealed on the Friday, the day at 'Arafat, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was standing there. We have given this above. Similarly, his statement, "whoever fasts the 18th day of Dhu al-Hijja has credited for him a fast of 60 months" is untrue. For what is established in the Sahih collections is that fasting the month of Ramadan brings credit of 10 months. And so how could it be that fasting one day would be counted as 60 months? This is nonsense. After relating this hadith, our teacher, the hafiz Abu 'Abd Allah al-Dhahabi commented, "This hadith is extremely objectionable." Habshun al-Khallal and Ahmad b. 'Abd Allah b. Ahmad al-Nili, both highly trustworthy sources, narrated it from 'Ali b. Sa'id al-Ramali, from Damra, who said, "This hadith is narrated from those (traced to) Umar b. al-Khattab, Malik b. al-Huwayrith, Anas b. Malik, Abu Sa'id and others, with weak lines of transmission." Damra went on, "The beginning part of the hadith is successively given, with high certainty that the Messenger of God (SAAS) spoke it. Regarding the words, 'O God, protect those who protect him', it is an addition."
lessons: Islamic scholarship has always contained robust mechanisms for identifying and rejecting fabricated religious claims; the tradition of hadith criticism is a gift that protects believers from being misled.
Claims that promise outsized spiritual rewards for minimal effort should be examined carefully — authentic Islamic teachings maintain internal consistency and proportion between effort and reward.

The willingness of scholars like al-Dhahabi to plainly call a hadith 'mendacious' or 'nonsense' when the evidence demands it models intellectual honesty and courage as religious virtues, not forms of disrespect.
practical_advice: When you encounter a religious claim — especially one circulating on social media or in informal settings — that promises extraordinary rewards for a simple action, pause and verify it with a reliable scholar or authenticated source before sharing or acting on it.
Practice intellectual honesty in your own life: when you realize you have held or repeated something that turns out to be inaccurate, correct it openly rather than quietly moving on.

Build a habit of cross-referencing important beliefs against multiple reliable sources; just as scholars compared hadith against established Sahih collections, we benefit from checking our assumptions against more than one trustworthy point of reference.

title: The Prophet's Final Address After Hajj al-Wada'
summary: After returning from the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet mounted the minbar in Medina and gave a final public address praising Abu Bakr and expressing his pleasure with the senior Companions, urging the Muslims to honor them and speak well of the deceased.
story: Sahl b. Hanif b. Sahl b. Malik, the brother of Ka'b b. Malik, from his father who quoted his grandfather as saying, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived in Medina from the hijjat al-wada', he mounted the minbar, gave praise and thanks to God and said, 'People, Abu Bakr never did me harm; give him credit for that. People, I am well pleased with Abu Bakr, 'Umar, 'Uthman, 'Ali, Talha, al-Zubayr, 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Awf and the initial muhajirin. Give them all credit for that. People, preserve me in my Companions, my relatives and my friends. God does not seek for you to harm anyone of them. People, keep your tongues off the Muslims, and if one of them should die, then say good of him.'"
lessons: The Prophet publicly honored his closest Companions, teaching us to acknowledge and appreciate those who stand by us.
Speaking well of others, especially the deceased, is a mark of faith and gratitude.
A leader who is near the end of his mission ensures those who served faithfully are remembered with dignity.
practical_advice: Take time today to express genuine appreciation to someone who has supported you without harming you.
Make a habit of speaking positively about others, especially those who are absent or have passed away.
Reflect on the people in your life who deserve acknowledgment and find a way to honor them openly.

title: The Year 11 AH and the Passing of the Prophet
summary: The text describes the momentous year 11 AH, marked above all by the death of the Prophet Muhammad, framed as his transition from the transient world to an elevated and eternal place with God.
story: This year commenced after the return of the noble Prophet (SAAS), to that city of prophecy, Medina, "the purified", following his completion of the hijjat al-wada'. Momentous events took place that year, among the most shocking being the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS). However, he was in fact transported by Almighty God from this transient abode away into eternal ease in an elevated place on high, the most exalted and most splendid level of paradise. As the Almighty stated, "The afterlife is better for you than the former; your Lord will give to you, and you will be pleased" (surat al-Duha; XCIII, v.4). That was after he had completed carrying out the mission that God Almighty had ordered him to announce. He had given advice to his nation and had directed them to the very best that he knew for them. He had warned them and restrained them from what would have harmed them here on earth and in their afterlife.
lessons: Death is not an ending but a transition to something greater, a perspective that can transform how we face loss.
A life fully devoted to a purpose — as the Prophet's was — leaves behind a legacy that transcends the individual.
God's promise that the afterlife is better than this world offers genuine consolation in times of grief.
practical_advice: When grieving a loss, try to hold alongside the sorrow a belief that the one you mourn is in a better place.
Reflect on what mission or purpose gives your own life meaning, especially during moments of despair.
Read or recite Surah al-Duha (Chapter 93) when feeling hopeless, as it carries a direct divine promise of comfort and sufficiency.

title: Umar Weeps at the Verse of Perfection and Senses the Prophet's Death
summary: When the Quranic verse announcing the perfection of religion was revealed on the day of Arafat, Umar ibn al-Khattab wept, understanding that after perfection can only come loss — sensing the approaching death of the Prophet.
story: We have given above the hadith of 'Umar b. al-Khattab narrated by both authors of the Sahih collections, to the effect that the words of the Almighty: "Today I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favours upon you, and willed that Islam be your religion" (surat al-Ma'ida; V, v.3) were revealed on Friday, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was standing on 'Arafat. We have narrated, from a fine line of transmission, that when this surat was revealed, 'Umar b. al-Khattab wept. He was asked, "What makes you weep?" He replied, "After perfection there can only be loss." It was as though he had sensed the death of the Prophet (SAAS).
lessons: Deep understanding of spiritual signs can grant us foresight that prepares the heart for what is coming.
Weeping out of love for a teacher or prophet is not weakness but a sign of the depth of that bond.
Every completion carries within it the seed of an ending; wisdom lies in recognizing this and cherishing what we have.
practical_advice: When you sense that a beautiful chapter of life is closing, allow yourself to feel it honestly rather than suppressing the grief.
Practice gratitude for what is whole and complete in your life right now, before it changes.
Take the example of Umar: let your emotional intelligence guide you toward spiritual awareness rather than denial.

title: The Prophet's Words at al-Aqaba: 'Take Your Rituals From Me'
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet stood at the pillar of al-Aqaba and instructed the people to learn the pilgrimage rites from him, hinting that he may not live to make another pilgrimage.
story: The latter pointed to this in what was narrated by Muslim from a hadith of Ibn Jurayj, from Abu al-Zubayr, from Jabir, that, "the Messenger of God (SAAS) stood at the pillar at al-'Aqaba and said to us, 'Take from me your rituals, for I may perhaps not make the pilgrimage after this year.'"
lessons: The Prophet used his final pilgrimage to ensure that the rituals of worship were preserved and transmitted accurately to all generations.
Urgent and honest communication about the possibility of loss is an act of love, not fear.
Moments of farewell can be the most instructive moments of a relationship if we pay attention.
practical_advice: Use times of transition or farewell to pass on what matters most — knowledge, values, and love — to those around you.
When you sense a chapter ending, be intentional about what you want to leave behind for others.
Approach each act of worship as though it may be your last, bringing full presence and sincerity to it.

title: Surah al-Nasr Revealed as a Sign of the Prophet's Approaching Death
summary: The revelation of Surah al-Nasr during the middle days of al-Tashriq was understood by the Prophet and senior Companions as a divine notification of his impending death, causing him to prepare for departure.
story: What the two huffaz Abu Bakr al-Bazzar and al-Bayhaqi narrated from the hadith of Musa b. 'Ubayda al-Rabadhi, from Sadaqa b. Yasar, from Ibn 'Umar, who said, "This surat: '(Remember) when God's help and the victory came' (surat al-Nasr; CX, v.1) was revealed in the middle days of al-tashriq, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) knew that it meant the farewell. He therefore ordered that his camel al-Qaswa' be brought, and it was saddled." He went on to refer to the address the Prophet (SAAS) made that day, as is told above. 'Abd Allah b. 'Abbas, may God be pleased with him, spoke similarly to 'Umar b. al-Khattab when he asked him about the interpretation of this surat in the presence of many of the Companions, to show them the virtue of Ibn 'Abbas, his superiority and knowledge. This related to his having been brought forwards and seated (in a prominent position) with the elders who had participated in the battle of Badr. He ('Umar) said, "You know from where he came." He proceeded, in the presence of Ibn 'Abbas, to question them on the interpretation of this surat: "(Remember) when God's help and the victory came and you saw the people entering God's religion in droves. Give joyful praise to your lord, and seek His forgiveness. He is Forgiving" (surat al-Nasr; CX v.1-3). They replied, "We were ordered, after having been given (God's) help, to speak His name, to praise Him and to seek His forgiveness." "He ('Umar) asked, 'What do you say, Ibn 'Umar?' He replied, 'It (referred to) the impending death of the Messenger of God (SAAS); he was being notified of it.' 'Umar commented, 'I know of it only what you know.'"
lessons: Divine signs of transition can come wrapped in apparent success and victory, reminding us to remain humble even at the height of achievement.
Ibn Abbas's profound Quranic insight, recognized and honored by Umar, shows that wisdom is not always tied to age.
The instruction to give praise and seek forgiveness at the moment of completion teaches us to end every chapter with gratitude and humility.
practical_advice: Recite Surah al-Nasr and reflect on its layers: victory, gratitude, and the reminder of mortality.
When you experience a peak moment of achievement, pair it with gratitude and self-reflection rather than pride.
Seek forgiveness regularly, especially at the close of major life milestones, as the Prophet was instructed to do.

title: The Prophet's Warning About His Wives Remaining in Confinement After This Hajj
summary: During the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet told his wives that after this pilgrimage they would remain in confinement, understood as another indication that he knew his death was near.
story: What is narrated by Imam Ahmad is similar. (He stated) that Waki' related to him, from Ibn Abu Dhi'b, from Salih, the freed-man of al-Taw'ama, from Abu Hurayra, (who said) that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) went on the pilgrimage accompanied by his wives, he said, "There will only be this hijja, and then they will remain in confinement." Ahmad is alone in giving it from this line. Abu Da'ud narrated it in his work al-sunan, from another excellent line.
lessons: The Prophet's awareness of his approaching death extended to making provisions for those most dependent on him, reflecting profound responsibility.
Even in moments of personal farewell, a leader's thoughts turn to the welfare and protection of those in his care.
Preparing those we love for life without us is an act of profound love, not morbidity.
practical_advice: Consider what practical arrangements you would want in place for those who depend on you, and begin addressing them while you are well.
Have honest conversations with loved ones about the future, even when those conversations are difficult.
Reflect on what it means to be a source of care and protection for others, and take that responsibility seriously each day.

title: A Summary of the Prophet's Military Expeditions and Pilgrimages
summary: Scholars compile varying accounts of the total number of military expeditions the Prophet led or dispatched and the pilgrimages he performed, providing a comprehensive overview of a life of profound mission and sacrifice.
story: In both Sahih collections, it is stated in a hadith of Abu Ishaq al-Sabi'i, from Zayd b. Arqam, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) went on nineteen military expeditions and, following his hijra, performed the hijjat al-wada' but made no pilgrimage thereafter. Abu Ishaq stated that there was one other at Mecca. Abu Ishaq al-Sabi'i stated the same. Zayd b. al-Hubab stated, from Sufyan al-Thawri, from Ja'far b. Muhammad, from his father, from Jabir, (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) made three pilgrimages - two before his Hijra and one thereafter, along with which he also performed an 'umra. To the latter he brought thirty-six camels for sacrifice and 'Ali brought the complement thereto from Yemen. We have presented information above given in both Sahih collections from more than one of the Companions, including Anas b. Malik, to the effect that the Prophet (SAAS) performed the 'umra four times, that at al-Hudaybiyya, the 'umrat al-qada', the 'umra from al-Ji'rana, and the 'umra that accompanied his Hijjat al-wada'. Regarding the military expedition, al-Bukhari narrated, from Abu 'Asim al-Nabil, from Yazid b. Abu 'Ubayd, from Salama b. al-Akwa', who said, "I participated in seven military expeditions with the Messenger of God (SAAS), and in nine with Zayd b. Haritha, whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) had appointed in command of us." In both Sahih collections a hadith is given from Qutayba, from Hatim b. Isma'il, from Zayd, from Salama who said, "I went on seven military expeditions with the Messenger of God (SAAS), and on nine others he dispatched (without himself participating). On one Abu Bakr was in command of us, and on another it was Usama b. Zayd." In the Sahih collection of al-Bukhari, there is a hadith from Isma'il, from Abu Ishaq, from al-Bara', who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) went out on fifteen military expeditions." In both Sahih collections it is stated in a hadith from Shu'ba, from Abu Ishaq, from al-Bara', that the Messenger of God (SAAS) made nineteen expeditions, in seventeen of which he participated. The first of these was that to "al-'Ushayr" or "al-'Usayr". Muslim narrated from Ahmad b. Hanbal, from Mu'tamir, from Kahmas b. al-Hasan, from Ibn Burayda, who quoted his father as saying that he participated along with the Messenger of God (SAAS) in sixteen military expeditions. There is also an account of Muslim, through al-Husayn b. Waqid, from 'Abd Allah b. Burayda, who quoted his father as saying that he participated in nineteen military expeditions with the Messenger of God (SAAS), in eight of which he engaged in combat. In another account from him with this line of transmission, it is said that he dispatched twenty-four military expeditions, and that he engaged in the battles of Badr, Uhud, al-Ahzab, al-Muraysi', Khaybar, Mecca and Hunayn. In the Sahih collection of Muslim, from a hadith of Abu al-Zubayr, Jabir is quoted as saying, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) engaged in twenty-one military expeditions. I took part with him in nineteen, and did not fight at Badr or Uhud because my father prevented me. When the latter was killed at Uhud, I never missed any military expedition he sent out." 'Abd al-Razzaq stated that Ma'mar related to him, from al-Zuhri, who said, "I heard Sa'id b. al-Musayyab say, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) organized eighteen military expeditions.' And once I heard him say that they totalled twenty-four. But I don't know whether I imagined that or heard it thereafter." Qatada stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) organized nineteen military expeditions in eight of which he himself engaged in combat. He also sent out twenty-four missions. His expeditions and raids together totalled forty-three." 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr, al-Zuhri, Musa b. 'Uqba, Muhammad b. Ishaq b. Yasar and other scholars expert in this subject state that the Prophet (SAAS) fought at Badr in Ramadan in 2 AH, then at Uhud in Shawwal of 3 AH, at al-khandaq and against Banu Qurayza, also in Shawwal, of 4 AH - for which date 5 AH is also given - against Banu al-Mustaliq at al-Muraysi' in Sha'ban of 5 AH, and at Khaybar in Safar of 7 AH, dated by some as 6 AH. What is correct is that it occurred at the beginning of 7 AH and the end of 6 AH. Then he fought against the Meccans in Ramadan of 8 AH, and against (Banu) Hawazin, then besieged the inhabitants of al-Ta'if in Shawwal and part of Dhu al-Hijja in 8 AH, as is told above in detail. In 8 AH 'Attab b. Usayd, the Governor of Mecca, made the pilgrimage with the people and then in 9 AH, Abu Bakr, "the trusting", did so. Thereafter, in 10 AH the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the pilgrimage with the Muslims.
lessons: The Prophet's life was one of extraordinary, sustained effort over decades — a reminder that meaningful change requires consistent, long-term commitment.
The careful preservation of these historical accounts by Companions and scholars reflects how deeply they valued the Prophet's example.
Even scholarly differences in exact numbers do not diminish the overwhelming testimony that the Prophet's mission was one of relentless dedication to God and humanity.
practical_advice: When feeling that your efforts are small or insufficient, reflect on how each individual act of striving contributes to a larger legacy.
Study the life of the Prophet not as a distant history but as a lived model of perseverance, courage, and faith under pressure.
Keep a personal record of meaningful actions and commitments you have made; it can be a source of motivation and accountability over time.

title: Summary of the Prophet's Military Expeditions
summary: Ibn Ishaq catalogues all twenty-seven military expeditions led personally by the Prophet and thirty-eight missions and raids he dispatched, noting the nine in which he personally engaged in combat.
story: Mu\u1ammad b. Is\u1aq stated, "The number of military expeditions which the Messenger of God (SAAS) organized totalled twenty-seven. These were, in order, Waddan, also known as al-Abwa'; Buwat, over towards Ragwa; al-'Ushayra, in the Yanbu' valley; the first Badr battle, in pursuit of Kurz b. Jabir; 'the great battle ofBadr', in which God slew the Quraysh chiefs; Banu Sulaym, proceeding as far as al-Kudr; al-Sawiq, in pursuit of Abu Sufyan b. Harb; Ghatafan, also known as Dhu Amarr; Bahran, a mine in Hijaz; Uhud; Hamra' al-Asad; Banu al-Nadir; Dhat al-Riqa' of Nakhl; the 'other' battle of Badr; Dumat al-Jandal; al-khandaq; Banu Qurana; Banu Lihyan of Hudhayl; Dhu Qarad; Banu al-Mustaliq ofKhuza'a; al-Hudaybiyya, where he did not intend to do battle and was blocked by the polytheists; Khaybar; the 'umrat al-qada pilgrimage; al-Fath; Hunayn; al-Ta'if; and Tabuk." Ibn Ishaq went on, "He actually engaged in combat in nine expeditions - Badr; Uhud; al-khandaq; Qurana; al-Mustaliq; Khaybar; al-Fath; Hunayn; and al-Ta'if." I note that all this is narrated in detail above in the appropriate places and with reference to various witnesses and evidence. And to God praise is due. Ibn Ishaq went on to state that the missions and raids he dispatched totalled thirty-eight.
lessons: The Prophet's life was one of immense responsibility and sacrifice; true leadership often demands personal engagement in the most difficult of circumstances.
Keeping a record of trials and efforts is itself an act of gratitude, acknowledging that every step was taken in service of a higher purpose.
The scope of the Prophet's striving reminds us that meaningful change requires sustained, organised effort over many years, not a single moment of heroism.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by the scale of your responsibilities, list what you have already accomplished to regain perspective and gratitude.
Recognise that sustained effort over time, even when difficult, is more transformative than waiting for a single perfect opportunity to act.
Reflect on the lives of those who bore greater burdens gracefully; let their example reframe your own challenges as manageable and purposeful.

title: Dihya's Gifts Ambushed and the Expedition of Zayd to Judham
summary: When Dihya b. Khalifa returned from the Byzantine emperor with gifts for the Prophet, he was ambushed by men of Banu Judham who stole everything; the Prophet dispatched Zayd b. Haritha who avenged the attack, and then the Prophet ordered full restitution to be given back to the tribe.
story: Regarding the expedition of Zayd b. al-Haritha to Judham, territory of Banu Khushayn. Ibn Hisham commented that this was part of the territory of Hasbmi. The reason for this, as told by Ibn Ishaq and others, was that when Dihya b. Khalifa returned from his trip to the Byzantine emperor, having taken to him the document from the Messenger of God (SAAS), inviting him to God and having received treasures and gifts, he arrived in a valley in Banu Judham territory called Shanar. At that time al-Hunayd b. 'Aws and his son 'Aws b. al-Hunayd, men of (Banu) al-Dulay', a clan of Judham, attacked him. They took all he had with him. One group of them, who had accepted Islam, fled from them, retrieving for Dihya what had been seized from him. When Dihya returned to the Messenger of God (SAAS), he told him what had happened and asked his permission to take the blood of al-Hunayd and his son 'Aws. Thereupon the Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched Zayd b. Haritha with a force of men against them. They approached from near al-Awlaj, attacking Maqis from the direction of the lava field. They captured the men and property they could find and killed al-Hunayd, his son, two men of Banu al-Ahnaf and one of Banu Khasib. After Zayd had captured their livestock and families, a group of them met with Rifa'a b. Zayd, who had received a letter from the Messenger of God (SAAS), inviting them to Islam. Rifa'a read it out to them and a group responded. Zayd b. Haritha did not, however, know this. After three days riding they reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) in Medina. They gave him the letter and he ordered that it be read out in public. He then asked aloud, three times, 'What should I do about those slain?' One of their men, named Abu Zayd b. 'Amr, replied, 'Release those who are alive, Messenger of God. Those dead are already beneath these feet of mine.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered 'Ali b. Abu Talib to accompany them. 'Ali responded, 'Zayd will not obey me.' And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave him his own sword as a sign. 'Ali then left with them, riding one of their camels. At Fayfa' al-Fadlatayn they met up with Zayd, and 'Ali gave back to them all that had been taken from them; they lost nothing at all.
lessons: Justice does not require the perpetuation of harm; when people embrace truth, past wrongs can be met with forgiveness and full restitution.
A leader's personal symbol of authority, given as a sign of trust, can resolve conflicts that force alone cannot.
Acting on incomplete information can lead to injustice; seeking to correct mistakes once the truth is known is a mark of moral courage.
practical_advice: When you have acted on incomplete information and caused unintended harm, take concrete steps to make full restitution rather than merely apologising.
If you find yourself in conflict with someone, look for a trusted mediator whose authority both sides respect to help resolve the matter peacefully.
Before acting in anger over a perceived wrong, pause to verify whether all parties have been heard; information gathered in haste is often incomplete.

title: Zayd b. Haritha's Vow of Revenge Against Banu Fazara and the Capture of Umm Qirfa
summary: After Zayd b. Haritha and his men were wounded and several killed in a first encounter with Banu Fazara at Wadi al-Qura, Zayd vowed not to perform a ritual bath until he had returned and fought them again; he recovered, led a second expedition, and defeated them.
story: He also dispatched Zayd b. Haritha to Banu Fazara at Wadi al-Qura, where a number of his men were killed. He was wounded, along with those killed. When he returned home, he swore that no water would touch his head after a janaba until he had attacked them again. When he had recovered from his wound, the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent him off once more in command of a force. He fought them at Wadi al-Qura, taking prisoner Umm Qirfa, Fatima, daughter of Rabi'a b. Badr, the wife of Malik b. Hudhayfa b. Badr, who was accompanied by a daughter of hers. Zayd b. Haritha gave orders to Qays b. al-Musahhar al-Ya'muri and he killed Umm Qirfa, allowing her daughter to live. Umm Qirfa had been held in very high honour, maxims being in common use referring to her nobility. Salama b. al-Akwa took charge of her daughter and he petitioned the Messenger of God (SAAS), to award her to him, which he did. Thereafter the Messenger of God (SAAS) presented her to his (Salama's) uncle, Hazn b. Abu Wahb, to whom she bore his son 'Abd al-Rahman.
lessons: Grief and injury, when channelled with discipline and purpose, can become the fuel for perseverance rather than destructive despair.
A vow made in a moment of pain can be a powerful tool for maintaining resolve through a long period of recovery.
Resilience is not the absence of being wounded; it is the determination to rise, heal, and continue despite the wounds.
practical_advice: When setbacks leave you feeling defeated, make a clear and specific commitment to yourself about the next concrete step you will take once you have recovered.
Give yourself permission to fully heal before re-engaging with a challenge; rushing back too soon often leads to greater failure.
Use the memory of past hardship not to fuel bitterness but to remind yourself of your own proven capacity to endure and overcome.

title: 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha Kills al-Yusayr b. Rizam Who Was Assembling Forces Against the Muslims
summary: When al-Yusayr b. Rizam began assembling the Ghatafan tribe to attack the Muslims, the Prophet dispatched 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha with a force; al-Yusayr agreed to be escorted to the Prophet but then regretted it, drew his sword, and was killed in the ensuing struggle.
story: He twice sent 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha to Khaybar. On one expedition he killed al-Yusayr b. Rizam who had been assembling Ghatafan to attack the Messenger of God (SAAS). The latter therefore dispatched 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha with a force of men that included 'Abd Allah b. Unays. When they approached al-Yusayr b. Rizam, they wished to conduct him back to the Messenger of God (SAAS). He did proceed with them, but when they were at al-Qarqara, some six miles from Khaybar, al-Yusayr regretted having come. His son, 'Abd Allah, attacked him while he was drawing his sword and used his own to strike him, cutting off his father's leg. Al-Yusayr struck him back on the head with a staff of shawha wood. Each Muslim then attacked and killed his Jewish companion. One of them, however, escaped on foot. When 'Abd Allah b. Unays arrived, the Messenger of God (SAAS) spat upon his head and his wound did not suppurate or cause him pain.
lessons: Threats to a community's safety must be addressed with wisdom and measured action before they escalate into full conflict.
A person who starts down the path of good faith but then reverses course out of fear or regret often brings greater harm upon themselves.
The Prophet's blessing upon the wounded companion illustrates that those who sacrifice in service of others are held in special care and protection.
practical_advice: When you are aware of a growing threat in your life, address it early and decisively rather than waiting for it to become a crisis.
If you have agreed to a course of action but feel regret midway, seek counsel before acting on that regret impulsively, as reversals made in panic rarely end well.
After enduring a difficult or dangerous experience, lean on the support of those you trust; their care and presence can be genuinely healing.

title: Usama b. Zayd Kills a Man Who Declared the Shahada and the Prophet's Rebuke
summary: Usama b. Zayd killed a man named Mirdas who declared 'There is no god but God' as the sword was drawn upon him; the Prophet rebuked Usama severely, repeatedly asking how he would answer for this on the Day of Judgement, leaving Usama wishing he had not yet been a Muslim before that day.
story: He also sent Ghalib b. 'Abd Allah to Syria where Mirdas b. Nahik, their ally from al-Hurqa of (Banu) Juhayna was struck down. He was killed by Usama b. Zayd and one of the ansar who overtook him. When they drew their swords upon him, he uttered the words, "There is no god but God" (but they still killed him). When the two men returned, the Messenger of God (SAAS) criticized them severely. They excused themselves by maintaining that he had only spoken these words to avoid being killed. He asked Usama, "And so did you tear up his heart (to learn whether he was feigning belief)?" He began saying to Usama, "And what will happen to you on Judgement Day, when confronted by the statement 'There is no god but God'?" Usama said, "And he went on repeating this until I wished I had myself not become a Muslim before that (incident)."
lessons: Outward declarations of faith must be accepted at face value; only God knows what is in a person's heart, and presuming to judge inward sincerity is a grave overreach.
The weight of guilt over a serious moral failure can be so acute that it becomes a turning point for deep spiritual growth and lifelong vigilance.
The Prophet's persistent questioning was not cruelty but mercy; making someone truly feel the gravity of a wrong is sometimes the most compassionate thing one can do.
practical_advice: When you have harmed someone by acting on assumptions rather than evidence, sit with the full weight of that mistake rather than quickly rationalising it away.
If a trusted mentor or guide confronts you repeatedly about a wrongdoing, resist the urge to defend yourself and instead allow the correction to reshape your character.
Before making a judgement about another person's motives or sincerity, ask yourself honestly: do I have any real evidence for this, or am I acting on suspicion alone?

title: 'Amr b. al-'As Commands the Expedition to Dhat al-Salasil and Questions the Prophet About His Favourites
summary: 'Amr b. al-'As was sent to Dhat al-Salasil to enlist Arab tribes near Syria; feeling outnumbered and afraid, he requested reinforcements including Abu Bakr and 'Umar under Abu 'Ubayda, then assumed overall command and led prayers; upon returning he asked the Prophet who his favourite people were.
story: He sent 'Amr b. al-'As to Dhat al-Salasil in Banu 'Udhra territory to encourage the Arabs to attack Syria. This was because the mother of al-'As b. Wa'il was from Bali; the Messenger of God (SAAS) therefore sent 'Amr to enlist them, believing he would be received most favourably by them. When he reached a well of theirs called al-Salsal, he grew fearful of them. He therefore sent to the Messenger of God (SAAS) for help. The latter dispatched to him a force that included Abu Bakr and 'Umar and was commanded by Abu 'Ubayda b. al-Jarrah. When they reached him, 'Amr took command over them all, saying, "You have only been dispatched as reinforcements for myself." Abu 'Ubayda, an easy-going man who cared little for prestige, accepted submissively. 'Amr would lead the prayer with all of them. Upon his return, therefore, he asked, "Messenger of God, who is your favourite person?" "'A'isha," he replied. "And of the men?" 'Amr asked. "Her father," he responded.
lessons: Acknowledging fear and asking for help is not weakness; it is the wise and courageous act of a responsible leader who prioritises the mission over personal pride.
True nobility of character, like that of Abu 'Ubayda who set aside prestige for the sake of unity, is among the most valuable qualities a person can cultivate.
Knowing who you are trusted by and who you are close to gives you confidence, but that closeness must be earned through consistent loyalty and sincere service.
practical_advice: When you reach the limits of your capacity, ask for help without shame; doing so protects not only yourself but everyone who depends on you.
If you find yourself in a situation where yielding authority would serve the greater good, practice letting go of prestige gracefully as Abu 'Ubayda did.
After completing a difficult responsibility, take time to reconnect with the people and relationships that ground and nourish you.

title: The Dispatch of Abd al-Rahman b. Awf to Dumat al-Jandal
summary: The Prophet sent Abd al-Rahman b. Awf on a military expedition to Dumat al-Jandal, first delivering a sermon warning the Companions of five societal sins, then personally adjusting Abd al-Rahman's turban and presenting him with a banner along with ethical instructions for conduct in battle.
story: Mn/:1ammad b. Is/:1aq stated that someone above suspicion quoted 'A(a' b. Abu Raba/:1 as having said, "I heard a man from Ba~ra ask 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar b. aI-Khatrab about a man's allowing his turban, if wearing one, to fly freely behind him." 'Abd Allah stated, "I will tell you about that, if God so wills it. You should know that I was the tenth in a group of Companions of the Prophet (SAAS), in his mosque. The others were Abu Bakr, 'Umar, 'Uthmm, 'Ali, 'Abd al-Ra/:1mm b. 'Awf, Ibn Mas'ud, Mu'i1dh b. Jabal, l::Iudhayfa b. al-Yamm, and Abu Sa'ld aI-Khudrl. I was there with the Messenger of God (SAAS), when a young an,ari came in, greeted him and sat down. He then asked, 'Messenger of God, which of the Believers is the best?' 'The most virtuous,' he replied. He then asked, 'And which of the Believers is the most intelligent?' 'The one who is the most aware of death and best prepared for it before it comes; those like that are the most intelligent.' The young man became silent. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then came over to us and said, 'Mukaj;rs, there are five faults that I pray to God will never befall you. If immorality ever settles upon and overcomes a people, plague and disease will appear among them such as never did among their forefathers. Ifthey give short measure and weight, they will be overwhelmed by famine and the ruler's tyranny. If they ever refuse to give the zakat from their wealth, they will be deprived of moisture from the sky; for were it not for the animals, no rain would fall on them. If ever they break the pact with God or His Messenger, an enemy will overpower them and take some of what they had. If their imams should ever rule without God's book and what God revealed, then He will bring calamity among them.' "He then ordered 'Abd al-Ra/:1mm b. 'Awf to make preparations for an expedition on which he was sending him. Next morning he wore a turban made of black cotton. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked him to approach, and untied and then rearranged the turban so that four. or so fingers' length were lying behind him. He said, 'That's the way, Ibn 'Awf. Put your turban on thus - that is better and more usual.' "He then told Bilal to present him with the banner, and he did so. The Messenger of God (SAAS) praised God, spoke a prayer for himself and said, 'Take it, Ibn 'Awf. Combat all in God's cause and do battle against those who disbelieve in Him. Do not defraud or use deceit, mutilate, or kill children. This is God's covenant and the way of your Prophet among you.' "And so 'l d al-Ra/:1mm took the banner." Ibn Hishilm added, "He proceeded out to Dumat al-Jandal."
lessons: True intelligence, as defined by the Prophet, lies in awareness of death and sincere preparation for the afterlife — a reminder to live with purpose rather than distraction.
Leadership carries ethical responsibility: even in conflict, the Prophet's instructions forbade deception, mutilation, and harming children, showing that moral integrity must never be abandoned.
Societal collapse begins with individual moral failures; the five warnings remind us that personal accountability is inseparable from communal wellbeing.
practical_advice: When facing a challenging responsibility, take time to reflect on your values and the ethical boundaries you will not cross, just as Abd al-Rahman received clear guidance before departing.
When you feel anxious about a task ahead, seek counsel from people of wisdom and ground yourself in your principles before acting.
Regularly examine whether your daily actions — in honesty, charity, and keeping your commitments — align with the values you claim to hold.

title: The Expedition of Abu Ubayda b. al-Jarrah and the Great Whale
summary: Abu Ubayda led approximately 300 riders on a coastal expedition provisioned only with dates; when supplies ran out, God provided for them miraculously through a massive whale cast ashore, which fed and restored the entire expedition for nearly a month.
story: He also despatched Abu 'Ubayda b. al-Jarri1/:1. They were some 300 riders who travelled to the coast. The Messenger of God (SAAS) provisioned him with a stock of dates. The story of the 'anbar, "the great whale", cast ashore from the sea, relates to this expedition. It provided food for them all for close to a month. It restored them to health and they cut it up into steaks to provision themselves for their return to the Messenger of God (SAAS). They gave him some of it and he ate it.
lessons: When human resources and planning reach their limits, trust in God opens doors of provision that reason could not anticipate.
Hardship endured together builds bonds of solidarity; the shared trial of the expedition and the shared blessing of the whale united the Companions in gratitude.
Gratitude is made complete when blessings are shared — the Companions brought a portion back for the Prophet, demonstrating that thankfulness is expressed through generosity.
practical_advice: In moments of scarcity or stress, remind yourself of times when provision came from unexpected sources, and let those memories strengthen your trust in God's care.
When you are overwhelmed by a lack of resources, focus on what you do have — like the dates — and take each step forward with what is available rather than being paralyzed by what is missing.
Practice sharing your blessings with others, especially those who supported you; this transforms personal relief into communal gratitude.

title: The Killing of Abu Afak the Hypocrite
summary: Abu Afak, an elderly hypocrite of Banu Amr b. Awf, publicly mocked the Prophet and the Muslim community in verse, and was subsequently killed by Salim b. Umayr after the Prophet expressed his displeasure at the man's open hostility.
story: He sent Salim b. 'Umayr, one of the bukii?ina, "the weepers", to Abu 'Afak, of Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf. He had made his hypocrisy apparent when the Messenger of God (SAAS) killed al-Barith b. Suwayd b. al-Samit, as was told above. He mourned him with the following verses, criticizing - may God damn him - his acceptance of the faith: "I have lived for an age and I have never seen a house nor a group More true, when called upon, to promises; nor more trustworthy to those who have compacted with them Than the sons of QtylaI35 in their assembly, making mountains shake and never submitting. A rider who came to them split them apart (by saying) 'Pennissible!' 'ForbiddenI' to various things. If you had believed in glory or kingship, you would have followed Tubbac." The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, "Who will take care of this foul fellow for me?" And so this man Salim b. 'Umayr volunteered and killed him. Umama al-Mundiyya spoke the following verses on this subject: "You deny the veracity of God's religion and the man A1)mad! By the life of him who bore you, evil is he who did so! A banif presented you with a thrust in the night, (saying)-'Take that, Abu <Afak, despite your age!'"
lessons: Hypocrisy and open hostility toward a community of faith can erode social cohesion from within; the early Muslim community recognized the threat posed by those who undermine unity through mockery and incitement.
Actions have consequences: public opposition carried out through inflammatory speech was met with decisive response, reminding us that words carry real social weight.
Community loyalty and defense of shared values can be expressed in many forms, including through poetry and verse as illustrated by the responses recorded here.
practical_advice: When someone in your circle consistently undermines your values or community with mockery, it is appropriate to set firm boundaries rather than tolerating corrosive influence indefinitely.
If you feel angry about attacks on something you hold sacred, channel that energy into constructive responses — as Umama did through verse — rather than letting it fester internally.
Reflect on whether your own words build up or tear down the communities you belong to; even casual criticism, when habitual, can become a form of quiet hostility.

title: Thumama b. Uthal Enters Mecca for Umra Despite Opposition
summary: After accepting Islam, Thumama b. Uthal al-Hanafi entered Mecca to perform Umra while reciting the talbiyya, defying the Meccans who tried to stop him, and later threatened to cut off their grain supply from Yamama until the Prophet intervened.
story: Ibn Is1,J.aq went on to refer to those who captured Thumama b. UthaI al-I;fanafi and the circumstances relating to his acceptance of Islam. We have referred to this above in relating authentic abiidith. Ibn Hisham stated that it was in relation to him that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "The Believer eats with one Stomach, the unbeliever with seven!" This was because of the little food Thumama ate after his acceptance of Islam. He related how, having left Medina, he entered Mecca to make the 'umra, reciting the talbiyya. The Meccans forbad him from doing this, but he disobeyed them, threatening to cut off their supply of grain from Yamama. When he returned home there, he did deny them grain until the Messenger of God (SAAS) wrote to him and he restored it to them. A man of Banil I;fanifa said, "Among us is he who recited the talbiyya in Mecca, though forbidden, in spite of Abil Sufyan, in the sacrosanct months."
lessons: True faith transforms not just the heart but one's entire way of being — Thumama's reduced appetite after accepting Islam symbolizes how sincere belief reshapes one's relationship with the world.
Standing firm in one's religious practice, even when externally opposed and mocked, is a form of spiritual courage that commands respect even from adversaries.

Mercy and moderation are hallmarks of prophetic leadership: the Prophet's intervention to restore grain to Mecca, even those who opposed him, demonstrated that power should be used with compassion not spite.
practical_advice: When your values or spiritual practices are challenged by others, find the quiet courage to continue them with dignity rather than abandoning them under social pressure.
After a major life change such as a shift in faith, identity, or purpose, pay attention to how your desires and habits shift; these changes can be signs of genuine inner transformation.
If you find yourself in a position of power or leverage over those who have wronged you, consider how mercy and restraint — rather than retaliation — can build more lasting respect.

title: The Test of Obedience: Abdullah b. Hudhafa and the Fire
summary: During an expedition led by Alqama b. Mujazziz, a subordinate commander Abdullah b. Hudhafa — known for his humor — ordered his men to enter a fire as a test; when some nearly obeyed, he revealed it was a joke, prompting the Prophet to declare that obedience to God's commands takes precedence over obedience to any human commander.
story: He sent CAlqama b. Mujazziz aI-Mudliji to take vengeance for his brother Waqqiilj b. Mujazziz the day he was killed at Dhil Qirad. He asked permission from the Messenger of God (SAAS) to follow their tracks; he gave it and appointed him commander of a force of men. When they were on their way, he ordered a group of them to come to him, and he appointed 'Abd Allah b. I;Iudhafa to be their leader. He liked to joke. He lit a fire and ordered the men to enter it. When some were about to do so, he said, "I was only joking!" When this reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), he commented, "Do not obey someone who orders you to disobey God."
lessons: Obedience has a clear limit: no human authority — regardless of rank or relationship — can override the commands of God, and recognizing this boundary is a mark of moral maturity.

The willingness of soldiers to enter a fire out of loyalty illustrates how powerful the human need for belonging and obedience can be; this makes it all the more important to internalize principled boundaries before facing such pressures.
Even those with good qualities — like loyalty — can be misused; wisdom lies in knowing which authority deserves our ultimate allegiance.
practical_advice: Whenever you feel pressured by a person in authority to do something that conflicts with your core values or conscience, remember that no human loyalty supersedes your obligation to what is right.
Reflect regularly on your own patterns of compliance: do you say yes to things out of fear, habit, or genuine conviction? Developing that awareness protects you from being led astray.
If you are in a leadership role, take seriously the weight of the trust placed in you; using authority to test, manipulate, or joke at others' expense — even in jest — can cause real harm.

title: The Men of Ukl or Urayna: Apostasy, Betrayal, and Punishment
summary: A group of men from Ukl or Urayna came to Medina, fell ill, were sent to recover among the Prophet's camels and drink their milk and urine, then brutally killed the herdsman Yasar and stole the camels; the Prophet sent Kurz b. Jabir after them and upon their capture ordered a severe punishment mirroring their crime.
story: He sent Kurz b. Jabir to fight those men who had come to Medina. They were of Q;tys of (Banu) Bajila. They found Medina unhealthy and became sick and so the Messenger of God (SAAS) told them to go out to his camels and to drink their urine and their mille When they recovered, they butchered Yasar, the freed-man of the Messenger of God (SAAS), who was the camels' herdsman and stuck thorns into his eyes. They then took away all the milch-camels. The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent after them Kurz b. Jabir with a force of his men and they brought them back from Bajila following the return of the Messenger of God (SAAS) from the expedition to Dhu Qarad. He gave his order and their hands and feet were cut off and their eyes were gouged out. It may well be that these are the men referred to in the well-accepted /.tadith of Anas - a group of eight men of COkl or COrayna who came to Medina with the same consequences.
lessons: Betraying the trust and generosity of those who helped you in your time of need is one of the gravest moral failures; the men were nursed back to health through the Prophet's compassion and repaid it with murder.
Every act of betrayal and cruelty has consequences; the severity of the punishment in this account reflects the gravity with which the early Muslim community regarded the violation of sacred trust.
The story of Yasar, the freed herdsman who was murdered, is a reminder that those in humble, unseen positions of service deserve our protection and remembrance.
practical_advice: When someone trusts you with care, resources, or vulnerability, honor that trust — ingratitude and betrayal corrode both the individual soul and the social fabric.
If you have experienced betrayal, acknowledge the grief and anger it brings rather than suppressing it, but also seek justice through legitimate means rather than letting bitterness consume you.
Reflect on those in your life who serve quietly and without recognition; express appreciation for them before it is too late.

title: The Dispatch of Usama b. Zayd to Syria: The Final Expedition
summary: The Prophet's last dispatched military expedition was under the command of the young Usama b. Zayd b. Haritha, sent toward the al-Balqa and al-Darum regions of Syria; the initial Muhajirun accompanied him in what would be the Prophet's final act of military leadership.
story: Ibn Isl.iaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched Usatna b. Zayd b. I;Iaritha to Syria. He ordered him to have the cavalry patrol the al-Balqa> and al-Darum regions of Syria. The men equipped themselves and it was the initial muhiijirnn who accompanied Usama." Ibn Hisham added, "This was the last expedition dispatched by the Messenger of God (SAAS)." AI-Bukhari stated that Isma'Il related to him, quoting Malik, from 'Abd Allah b. Dinar, from 'Abd Allah b. COmar, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent out a force under the command ofUsama b. Zayd.
lessons: The Prophet's appointment of the young Usama as commander of the final expedition — over senior Companions — teaches that competence and trust are more important than age or seniority in leadership.
Endings carry their own weight and meaning; the final expedition represents the completion of a prophetic mission and reminds us that every role and season of life has its conclusion.
Continuity of purpose beyond the life of a leader is itself a testament to the enduring strength of the institutions and values that leader built.
