practical_advice: When transitioning out of a role or responsibility, invest in those who will carry it forward — as the Prophet did by equipping and dispatching Usama — rather than holding on out of habit or reluctance.
If you are young and have been given unexpected responsibility, take it seriously; being trusted beyond your years is both an honor and a call to grow into your potential.
In moments of endings — whether personal, professional, or communal — take time to reflect on what was accomplished and what legacy is being passed on to those who come after you.

title: The Dispatch of Usama b. Zayd's Expedition to Syria
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) dispatched Usama b. Zayd to lead an expedition to Syria, but faced criticism from companions about his young age and appointment. The Prophet defended Usama's leadership, comparing it to criticism once directed at his father.
story: Ibn Isl.iaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched Usatna b. Zayd b. I;Iaritha to Syria. He ordered him to have the cavalry patrol the al-Balqa> and al-Darum regions of Syria. The men equipped themselves and it was the initial muhiijirnn who accompanied Usama." Ibn Hisham added, "This was the last expedition dispatched by the Messenger of God (SAAS)." AI-Bukhari stated that Isma'Il related to him, quoting Malik, from 'Abd Allah b. Dinar, from 'Abd Allah b. COmar, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent out a force under the command ofUsama b. Zayd, but that the people criticized his appointment. The Prophet (SAAS) therefore arose and said, "Your criticism of his leadership is similar to your earlier criticism of his father's appointment to command. The former was, by God, qualified for command and one of those most beloved by me. And this man is also of those most loved by me." AI-Tirmidhi narrated it from a {zadith of Malik. He categorized it as a {zadith that was ~a{zi{z {zasan. A large number of the senior initial muhajirin and an~ar took part in this army. The most important of these was 'Vmar b. al-Khanab. Those who say that Abu Bakr was among them are in error. The illness of the Messenger of God (SAAS) was increasing in severity while the force was encamped at al-Jurf. The Prophet (SAAS) at that time apointed Abu Bakr to lead the prayer, as will be recounted. How, then, could he have been in the army while being the imam for the Muslim community, by permission of the Messenger of God (SAAS), from the lord of the worlds? Even if it be supposed that he had proceeded out with them, the law-giver (the Prophet) selected him out from among them by appointing him to leadership of the prayer, one of the most important ofthe pillars ofIslam. And when the Messenger of God (SAAS) died, Abu Bakr sought 'Vmar b. al-Khanab's exclusion (from the expedition) from Vsama, so the latter allowed 'Vmar to remain at the side ofAbu Bakr. Abu Bakr, "the trusting", then led Vsama's force (for a short distance to the outskirts of Medina).
lessons: True leadership is judged by character and capability, not by age or the opinion of the crowd. The Prophet's defense of Usama reminds us that those who are criticized unfairly often prove their worth through their actions.
Standing firmly by those we love and trust, even when others doubt them, is an act of great loyalty and moral courage.

Even in times of personal illness and difficulty, fulfilling our responsibilities and supporting those under our care remains a priority, as the Prophet continued to make critical decisions even as his health declined.
practical_advice: When you face criticism or doubt from others about a decision you have made with sincerity, reflect on your intentions and trust in your judgment rather than being swayed by popular opinion.
If someone you trust is being unfairly criticized, speak up for them with evidence and clarity, as the Prophet did for Usama.
In times of stress or declining health, identify the most essential responsibilities you must still fulfill and focus your limited energy on those, delegating or adjusting where possible.

title: Quranic Verses Forewarning of the Prophet's Death
summary: God Almighty revealed multiple Quranic verses that forewarned of the inevitable death of the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS), reminding believers that he was a mortal messenger and that every soul must taste death.
story: God Almighty stated, "¥ou (will be) dead and they shall be dead. Then, on resurrection day, you will be contending with one another before your Lord" (surat ai-Zumar; XXXIX, v.30--31). And, "We ordained no immortality for any person before you; and if you die, shall they be immortal?" (surat ai-Anbiyyit'; XXI, v.34). And, "Every soul will taste death, and you shall only be fully paid your wages on the day of resurrection. Those drawn away from the fire and brought into paradise will be those who succeed. Life on earth is nothing but the baggage of vanity" (surat Ai 'lmran; III, v.285). The Ahnighty also stated, "Mul,Jammad is nothing but a messenger; messengers have passed away before him. Ifhe dies or is killed, you might tum away on your heels. Whoever runs away on his heels will cause no harm to God. And God will reward the grateful" (surat Ai 'lmran; III, v.l44). It was this verse that Abu Bakr, "the trusting", recited on the day of the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS). When the people heard it, it was as though they had not done so previously. The Ahnighty also stated, "(Remember) when God's help and the victory came and yon saw the people entering God's religion in droves. Give joyful praise to your Lord, and seek His forgiveness. He is forgiving" (surat ai-Na~r; ex, v.l3). 'Umar b. al-Khanab and Ibn 'Abbas stated, "This was (referring to) the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS), of which he was being informed." Ibn 'Umar stated, "This was revealed in the middle part of Tishrin, during the !tijjat al-wada', 'the farewell pilgrimage'. The Messenger of God (SAAS) knew it meant farewell and he delivered to the people an address in which he gave them instruction and prohibition."
lessons: The Quran repeatedly reminds us that every soul, including the greatest of prophets, must taste death. Accepting mortality is not a cause for despair but an invitation to live purposefully and prepare for what lies ahead.

The verse recited by Abu Bakr upon the Prophet's death — that Muhammad was a messenger and messengers have passed before him — is a profound lesson that our faith must be in God, not only in any individual, no matter how beloved.

The Surah al-Nasr, which speaks of victory and masses entering Islam, was understood by the companions as a signal of the Prophet's impending departure. Even moments of great triumph can carry within them the seeds of transition and ending.
practical_advice: When facing grief over the loss of someone beloved, return to the words of the Quran about the nature of death and the eternal life to come. These words have the power to ground and stabilize the heart.
Reflect regularly on your own mortality not as something morbid, but as a tool to clarify your priorities, deepen your gratitude, and motivate you to act with sincerity today.
In moments of collective shock or grief, be like Abu Bakr — grounded in scripture and truth — and gently help those around you anchor themselves in faith rather than be swept away by emotion.

title: The Prophet Informs Fatima of His Approaching Death
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) privately informed his daughter Fatima that Gabriel had reviewed the Quran with him twice that year instead of once, and that he understood this to signal the approach of his death.
story: Jabir stated, "I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS), casting at the pillars. He stopped and said, 'You should take your rites and ceremonies from me. I might perhaps not perform the pilgrimage after this year.'" The Messenger of God (SAAS) said the following to his daughter Fatima, "Gabriel would review the Qur'an with me once each year; he did so twice this year. I can only think that this means the approach of my death." In the ,a!ti!t collection of al-Bukhilri, it is narrated from a !tadith of Abu Bakr b. 'Ayyash, from Abu I;Iusayn, from Abu Salih, that Abu Hurayra stated, "Each month of Ramaqan the Messenger of God (SAAS) would practise i'tiraj, withdraw into seclusion (in the mosque) for ten days. The year he died he so withdrew for twenty days. The Qur'an was reviewed to him each Ramaqan; the year he died, it was reviewed to him twice."
lessons: The Prophet's quiet, tender way of informing Fatima of his approaching death teaches us that preparing loved ones for loss — with gentleness and truth — is an act of deep compassion.

The doubling of the Quranic review in the Prophet's final year was understood as a divine sign of completion. Our lives too have natural signals of transitions and endings that invite reflection and gratitude rather than denial.
Increased worship and spiritual practice in one's final period of life, as exemplified by the Prophet's extended i'tikaf, reflects a beautiful orientation toward God even as the world recedes.
practical_advice: When you sense a major life transition approaching — whether your own or a loved one's — find gentle, honest ways to acknowledge it with those closest to you rather than avoiding the conversation.
Use significant milestones or transitions in your life as occasions to deepen your spiritual practice, as the Prophet increased his i'tikaf and Quranic review in his final year.
If you are facing grief or anticipatory loss, try journaling or speaking with a trusted person about what this person or chapter of your life has meant to you, so you can honor it fully.

title: Aisha's Headache and the Prophet's Illness Begins
summary: As the Prophet's final illness began, he returned from Baqi' to find Aisha complaining of a headache. He tenderly joked with her about dying before him, then as his pain worsened he sought her permission to be nursed in her home, entering it supported by two companions.
story: Ibn Isi).aq stated, "Ya'qub b. 'Utba related to me, from al-Zuhri, from 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Utba, from Ibn Mas'ud, who quoted 'A'isha as stating, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) returned from the cemetery and found me suffering from a pain in my head. I was complaining, "O my head!" He commented, "I swear by God, 'A'isha, I'm the one with the headache!" He then asked, "Would you mind if you died before me, and I were to stand over you, put you in your shroud, pray over you and bury you?" I replied, "I imagine that if you did that you would return to my house and celebrate a wedding night there with one of your wives!" The Messenger of God (SAAS) smiled. The pain persisted in him while he visited in turn his wives. He was overcome by it in the home of Maymuna. He summoned his wives and asked their permission to be nursed in my home; this was agreed. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then left, being helped along by two men of his household, al-Fa<jl b. 'Abbas and another man. He had his head bound and he was dragging his feet when he entered my house.' 'Ubayd Allah stated, 'I related this to Ibn 'Abbas and he said, "Do you know who the other man was? That was 'All b. Abu TaIib."'" Al-Bayhaqi stated that al-I:Iakim informed him, quoting al-A~amm, quoting AQmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar, from Yunus b. Bukayr, from Mui:tammad b. Isl:taq, quoting Ya'qub b. 'Utba, from al-Zuhri, from 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Abd Allah, who quoted 'A'isha as having said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) came in to me while suffering from a headache while I was complaining about my own head. I said, 'O my head!' He said, 'I swear by God, 'A'isha, I'm the one with headache!' He then said, 'Would you mind, if you were to die before myself, if I were to take care of you, prayed over you and buried you?' I replied, 'I swear by God, I believe that if that happened, you would closet yourself with one of your wives in my house as soon as the day was over!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) laughed. His pain persisted and became severe while, visiting his wives in turn, he was at the house of Maymuna. His family assembled, and al-'Abbas said, 'We think the Messenger of God has pleurisy. Let's give him medicine through the corner of his mouth.' They did so and the Messenger of God (SAAS) came to. 'Who did this?' he asked."
lessons: Even in the beginning of his final illness, the Prophet maintained his warmth, humor, and love for Aisha. This teaches us that tenderness and lightness with our loved ones can and should persist even in difficult times.
The Prophet's care in asking the permission of his wives before being moved to Aisha's home reflects a deep respect for the feelings and consent of those around him, even as he was suffering.
Being supported physically and emotionally by those we love in our most vulnerable moments is not weakness — it is part of the human experience that even the greatest of people shared.
practical_advice: When you or someone you love is unwell, small moments of warmth, humor, and gentle conversation can be profoundly comforting. Do not feel that illness demands only solemnity.

If you are a caregiver for someone who is ill, take cues from how the Prophet's companions supported him — with physical presence, attentiveness, and love — rather than feeling helpless in the face of illness.
When illness or hardship forces you to ask for help, do so with the same dignity the Prophet showed — acknowledging the need, seeking permission, and allowing others the honor of serving you.

title: The Prophet's Illness Worsens and He Seeks Nursing in Aisha's Home
summary: As the Prophet's illness intensified, he sought and received permission from his wives to be nursed in Aisha's home, and was helped there by al-Abbas and Ali, his feet dragging on the ground.
story: you?' I replied, 'I swear by God, I believe that if that happened, you would closet yourself with one of your wives in my house as soon as the day was over!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) laughed. "His pain persisted and became severe while, visiting his wives in turn, he was at the house of Maymuna. His family assembled, and al-'Abbas said, 'We think the Messenger of God has pleurisy. Let's give him medicine through the corner of his mouth.' They did so and the Messenger of God (SAAS) came to. 'Who did this?' he asked. 'It was your uncle al-'Abbas; he is afraid you have pleurisy.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'It is from Satan! God would never have inflicted me with it! You should not omit giving medicine to anyone in my house except for my uncle al-'Abbas!' And all the household were given medicine even Maymuna, who was fasting. That was done in the sight of the Messenger of God (SAAS). "Thereafter he asked permission of his wives to be nursed in my home; he received permission. So he left, between al-'Abbas and another man" - she did not name him - "his feet dragging on the ground." 'Ubayd Allah said that Ibn 'Abbas stated, "The other man was 'Ali b. Abu Talib."
lessons: Illness is a universal experience; even the greatest of people face physical suffering with patience and grace.
Seeking care and support during weakness is a sign of wisdom, not failure — the Prophet himself leaned on his loved ones.
The community's love and concern for those who are ill reflects the depth of their bonds and faith.
practical_advice: When you are ill or struggling, allow yourself to accept help from those around you without shame or pride.
Care for the sick in your life with attentiveness and compassion, as the companions cared for the Prophet.
In moments of communal concern, consult with trusted people around you and act with care and deliberateness.

title: The Prophet is Bathed with Water from Seven Skins to Address the People
summary: During his severe illness, the Prophet requested to be poured water from seven untied water-skins so he could recover enough strength to go out and speak to and advise the people.
story: 'A'isha, wife of the Messenger of God (SAAS), used to relate, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered my house and his pain was severe, he said, 'Pour over me from seven water-skins the tops of which have not been untied so that I will (be well enough to) give advice to people.' "We seated him in a tub belonging to Hafsa, the wife of the Prophet (SAAS). Then we set about pouring over him water from those skins until he gestured to us with his hand saying, 'You have done it now.' "He then went outside to the people, spoke a prayer for them and made them an address."
lessons: Even in tremendous personal suffering, the Prophet's first thought was for his community — a model of selfless leadership.
Concern for others can give us the strength to push through our own pain and limitations.
Small acts of care and preparation by those around us can enable us to fulfil our responsibilities.
practical_advice: When you are going through difficulty, ask yourself if there is someone who needs you — that sense of purpose can be a source of strength.
Allow trusted people to support you physically and emotionally so that you can continue to contribute to those who depend on you.
Prepare and rest adequately before important responsibilities, even when circumstances are hard.

title: The Prophet Dies in Aisha's Arms
summary: The Prophet Muhammad passed away on the day of his turn to visit Aisha, dying with his head resting against her chest, and she softened a tooth-pick for him in his final moments.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Isma'il related to him, quoting Sulayman b. Bilal, quoting Hisham b. 'Urwa, quoting his father, who quoted 'A'isha as having said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would ask during the illness in which he died, "Where will I be tomorrow? Where will I be tomorrow?' He was referring to 'A'isha's day (for his visit to her). His wives permitted him to be wherever he wished, so he was in 'A'isha's house until he died there. (The account narrates that) "'A'isha, may God be pleased with her, stated, 'He died on the day he would come to visit me in my house. God took him while his head lay between my breasts and my throat. His saliva mixed with mine.'" She went on, "'Abd al-Rahman b. Abu Bakr came in carrying a tooth-pick with which he was cleaning his teeth. The Messenger of God (SAAS) looked over at him and I asked 'Abd al-Rahman, 'Give me the tooth-pick, 'Abd al-Rahman.' He gave it to me, and I chewed and softened it and gave it to the Messenger of God (SAAS). He cleansed his teeth with it while he rested against my chest." "The Prophet (SAAS) died while between my breast and my chin. And I no longer abhor the death throes of anyone after (those of) the Prophet (SAAS)."
lessons: The Prophet's death in the arms of someone he loved reminds us that love and companionship are among the greatest gifts in life.
Witnessing the death of a beloved can transform our relationship with mortality, teaching acceptance and reducing our fear of it.
Acts of tender care in someone's final moments — however small — are among the most meaningful things we can do for another person.
practical_advice: Be present with those who are suffering or dying; your company and care are irreplaceable gifts.
Reflect on the impermanence of life not as a source of despair but as a reminder to cherish those you love now.
When grief feels overwhelming, remember that even the closest companions of the Prophet endured profound loss and found ways to carry on with faith.

title: Aisha Recites the Mu'awwidhat Over the Prophet During His Illness
summary: During his fatal illness, Aisha recited the last two surahs of the Quran over the Prophet and wiped him with his own hand, just as he used to do for himself when in pain.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Hibban related to him, quoting 'Abd Allah, quoting Yunus, from Ibn Shihab, who said, "'Urwa informed me that 'A'isha narrated to him that whenever the Messenger of God (SAAS) was in pain, he would breathe over himself, recite the al-mu'awwidhat and wipe his hands over his body. And so when he was in pain during the illness from which he died, I set about reciting the al-mu'awwidhat over him, breathing over him as he had, and using the hand of the Prophet (SAAS) to wipe over him."
lessons: Returning to the spiritual practices of those we love is a way of honouring them and finding comfort in difficulty.
The Quran is a source of healing and peace; turning to it in times of pain is a timeless act of trust in God.
Love expressed through familiar rituals and gestures carries deep meaning, especially in moments of loss.
practical_advice: In times of illness or distress, return to the spiritual practices that have comforted you or those you love — recitation, prayer, and remembrance.
Care for the sick by doing for them what they can no longer do for themselves, adapting to their needs with gentleness.
When you cannot take away someone's suffering, your presence and spiritual support are still profoundly valuable.

title: Fatima Weeps Then Laughs at the Prophet's Confidential Words
summary: The Prophet whispered twice to his daughter Fatima during his illness — first telling her of his impending death, which made her weep, then telling her she would be mistress of the believing women, which made her laugh.
story: It is established in both sahih collections from a hadith of Abu 'Awana, from Firas, from al-Sha'bi, from Masruq, who quoted 'A'isha as having stated, "The wives of the Messenger of God (SAAS) all assembled around him; none of them was left out. Fatima came in, her footsteps not missing the style of his own walk. He said, 'Welcome indeed to my daughter!' He sat her down at his right, or his left. He then spoke something confidentially to her, and she wept. He then spoke to her quietly again, and she laughed. I commented to her, 'The Messenger of God speaks secrets to you alone, and yet you cry!' "When she arose, I asked her, 'Tell me what he spoke to you.' She replied, 'I could not disclose a secret of the Messenger of God.' After he had died, I asked her, 'I ask you, by my rights over you, to tell me.' She replied, 'Now I can do so. The first comment he made in confidence to me was, "Gabriel would review the Qur'an with me once each year. This year he has done so twice. I can see this only as meaning the approach of my death. Fear God and be patient; I am a worthy predecessor to you." And so I wept. He then whispered to me again, saying, "Will it not please you to be mistress of the women of the Believers, or mistress of (all) the women of this nation?" I laughed.'"
lessons: The Prophet comforted his daughter with both honesty about his death and reassurance of her honour — a model of compassionate communication.
Grief and joy can coexist in the same moment; acknowledging both is part of the full human experience.
Keeping a confidence, even in grief, is a mark of integrity and trustworthiness — Fatima did not reveal the secret until after his passing.
practical_advice: When delivering difficult news to someone you love, balance honesty with compassion and reassurance wherever it is truthful.
Allow yourself to feel both sadness and gratitude simultaneously — these emotions are not contradictory but are part of processing loss.
Honour the confidences people place in you; trust is one of the most sacred aspects of any relationship.

title: The Prophet Asks for Writing Materials on 'The Terrible Thursday'
summary: As his illness worsened on a Thursday, the Prophet asked for writing materials to record guidance that would prevent his community from going astray, but disagreement among those present prevented this from happening.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Qutayba related to him, quoting Sufyan, from Sulayman al-Ahwal, from Sa'id b. Jubayr, who said, "Ibn 'Abbas stated, 'What a terrible Thursday that was! The illness of the Messenger of God (SAAS) worsened. Then he asked, "Bring me writing materials with which I can write you a document after which you will never go astray." People disputed over this - and there should never be disputation in the presence of a prophet. Some said, "What is wrong with him; is he delirious? Question him." So they went to him to ask him. He told them, "Leave me alone; my present condition is better than that to which you invite me." He then advised them to do three things. He said, "Remove the unbelievers from the Arabian peninsula. Treat with dignity delegations as you saw me treat them." And he either did not speak the third request, or he spoke it but I have forgotten it.'"
lessons: Moments of confusion or disagreement at critical times can lead to missed opportunities — the importance of unity and deference to wisdom cannot be overstated.
The Prophet's final instructions focused on the welfare of the community, reminding us that leadership is defined by concern for others.
Even when guidance is not perfectly transmitted, the spirit of that guidance — justice, dignity, and protection of the community — remains.
practical_advice: In moments of collective stress or grief, resist the urge to argue and instead listen carefully to those with wisdom and authority.
Reflect on the responsibilities entrusted to you and ask whether you are honouring them with the seriousness they deserve.
When you are given the opportunity to receive guidance or wisdom from those you trust, receive it with openness and humility.

title: Al-Abbas Warns Ali About the Prophet's Impending Death
summary: Al-Abbas, recognising signs of impending death in the Prophet, urged Ali to ask the Prophet about succession, but Ali refused, saying that if the Prophet refused them, the people would never grant it to them either.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Ishaq related to him, quoting Bishr b. Shu'ayb b. Abu Hamza, quoting his father, from al-Zuhri, who said, "'Abd Allah b. Ka'b b. Malik al-Ansari - Ka'b b. Malik having been one of the three men who had received forgiveness - informed me that 'Abd Allah b. 'Abbas told him when 'Ali b. Abu Talib came out after having been with the Messenger of God (SAAS), during the illness in which he died, people asked him, 'Abu al-Hasan! How is the Messenger of God doing this morning?' He replied, 'Thanks be to God, he is recovered.' "'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Muttalib took 'Ali by the hand and said, 'Three days from now, I swear by God, you will be slave of the staff. I swear, I can see that the Messenger of God (SAAS) will die from this illness. I well know how members of the 'Abd al-Muttalib family look when near death. Let us go to the Messenger of God, and ask him to whom rule will go. If to us, then we will know it; if to someone other than us, we can tell him and he (the Prophet (SAAS)) can tell him to care for us.' "'Ali stated, 'I swear by God, if we asked the Messenger of God (SAAS) for it (the rule) and he refused it, then the people would never give it to us after him. So I swear, I'll not ask the Messenger of God for it.'"
lessons: Facing the reality of an imminent loss, even when painful, allows us to prepare and act wisely rather than be paralysed by denial.
Integrity means not using a moment of someone's vulnerability to seek personal advantage, even if others might consider it pragmatic.
Disagreement between well-intentioned people about what to do in a crisis is natural; what matters is that each acts according to their conscience.
practical_advice: When facing the impending loss of a loved one, have necessary conversations while there is still time — but let them be guided by love, not self-interest.
Reflect on your motivations when making important decisions during moments of crisis: are you acting for others or for yourself?
Trust that principled restraint, as Ali demonstrated, is a form of strength — not every opportunity needs to be seized.

title: The Prophet Attributes His Lingering Pain to the Poison at Khaybar
summary: During his fatal illness, the Prophet told Aisha that he still felt the effects of the poisoned food he had eaten at Khaybar, and sensed his aorta being severed by it.
story: Al-Bukhari also stated that Yunus stated that al-Zuhri quoted 'Urwa as saying, "'A'isha stated, 'The Prophet (SAAS) would say during the illness in which he died, "'A'isha, I still suffer pain from the food I ate at Khaybar. At this time I sense my aorta being severed by that poison."'"  Al-Bayhaqi stated that al-Hakim informed him, quoting al-Assamm, quoting Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar, from Abu Mu'awiya, from al-A'mash, from 'Abd Allah b. Murra, from Abu al-Ahwas, from 'Abd Allah b. Mas'ud, who said, "I would prefer to swear nine times over that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was murdered than to do so once that he was not killed! That is because God assigned him to be a prophet and a martyr as well."
lessons: The Prophet's death as a martyr — even through the delayed effects of poison — demonstrates that suffering endured for a righteous cause has the highest honour in the sight of God.
Physical suffering that is borne with patience and without bitterness is itself a form of worship and draws one closer to God.
The companions' grief and their insistence on the honour of the Prophet's death reflects the depth of their love and understanding of divine wisdom.
practical_advice: When chronic pain or the lingering effects of past harm affect you, seek both medical care and spiritual grounding to carry that burden with patience.
Reflect on how trials endured for the sake of what is right carry their own dignity — they are not meaningless suffering.
Allow yourself and others to name grief and loss clearly; the companions did not shy away from acknowledging the weight of the Prophet's passing.

title: The Disputed Document at the Prophet's Deathbed
summary: As the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) lay gravely ill, he expressed a desire to write a document to guide his followers, but disagreement among those present prevented him from doing so, an event described by Ibn Abbas as truly disastrous.
story: from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "When death was close for the Messenger of God (SAAS), and there were men present in his home, he said, 'Come close; I will write a document for you after which you will never go astray.' Some of them then said, '(But) the Messenger of God is overcome by illness, and you have the Qur'an. God's Book is enough for us.' "The household differed among themselves and disputed. Some said, 'Go over near him and let him write something after which you will never stray.' Others said the contrary. When their disputation increased, the Messenger of God (SAAS) told them, 'Go away!' "'Ubayd Allah said, 'Ibn 'Abbas stated, "It was truly disastrous that, due to their noise and disputation, the Messenger of God (SAAS) was prevented from writing that document for them."'"
lessons: Disputes and disagreements at critical moments can lead to lasting regret; maintaining calm and unity in times of crisis is essential.
Even those closest to the Prophet (SAAS) were not free from human error; humility and self-reflection are necessary for every believer.
When a moment of guidance or clarity presents itself, one should receive it with an open heart rather than resistance or debate.
practical_advice: When facing an important or emotionally charged situation with others, pause before reacting and consciously choose calm over argument.
Reflect on moments in your life where conflict or stubbornness led to missed opportunities, and use that reflection to commit to better responses in the future.
Practice active listening in group settings, especially during times of stress, so that important guidance is not lost amid noise and disagreement.

title: The Prophet Appoints Abu Bakr Through His Final Wishes
summary: During his terminal illness, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) repeatedly indicated his wish for Abu Bakr to lead the community after him, expressing through both words and actions that Abu Bakr was his most trusted companion.
story: "When the illness of the Messenger of God (SAAS) worsened, he told 'Abd al-Ral).man, Abu Bakr's son, 'Bring me a shoulder bone or a tablet so that I can write for Abu Bakr a document about which no one can dispute.' When 'Abd al-Ral).man went to do this, he said, 'God and the Believers reject there being any disagreement over you, Abu Bakr!'" Al-Bukhari narrated from Yal).ya b. Yal).ya, from Sulayman b. Bilal, from Yal).ya b. Sa'id, from al-Qasim b. Mul).ammad, who quoted 'A'isha as stating that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "I plan to send for Abu Bakr and his son to make a covenant, lest people argue or have ambitions.' He went on, 'Either God rejects or the Believers impel, or God impels and the Believers reject.'" In the ~al).il). collection of al-Bukhari and in that of Muslim, there is a l).adith of Ibrahim b. Sa'd, from his father, from Mul).ammad b. Jubayr b. Mut'im, who quoted his father as saying, "A woman came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he told her to return to him again. She asked, 'But supposing I came and did not find you?' - she seemed to be implying his death. He replied, 'If you do not find me, then go to Abu Bakr.'"
lessons: True leadership is not seized by ambition but is recognized and entrusted by those of wisdom and integrity.
The Prophet's care for the community's unity even during his own suffering reflects that love for others can transcend personal pain.
Preparing for transitions and ensuring continuity is an act of wisdom and responsibility, not pessimism.
practical_advice: When you are facing a major life transition or illness, take practical steps to provide clarity and direction for those who depend on you, rather than leaving things unresolved.
Reflect on the people in your life who have shown consistent trustworthiness, and consider how you might better honor and rely on them.
When experiencing personal hardship, ask yourself how you can still serve and protect those around you, drawing strength from a sense of purpose.

title: The Prophet's Final Address from the Minbar After Ritual Washing
summary: Five days before his death, the Prophet (SAAS) had water poured over him from seven water-skins, then went out and delivered a profound farewell address from the minbar, praying for the martyrs of Uhud, praising Abu Bakr, and alluding to his own imminent death through the parable of a servant given a divine choice.
story: Al-Bayhaqi stated that al-Hakim informed him, quoting al-A~amm, from Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar, from Yunus b. Bukayr, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, from al-Zuhri, from Ayyub b. Bashir, who stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said during his illness, 'Pour water over me from seven water-skins from seven different wells so that I can go out and make a covenant with the people.' They did so and he went out and sat upon the minbar. The first thing he did, after praising and thanking God, was to refer to his Companions who had attended the battle of Uhud; he asked God's forgiveness for them and said a prayer for them. He then said, 'Muhajiriin, you have become very numerous while the ansar have remained as they were and have not increased in number. They are my saviours, those with whom I sought refuge. Honour them for their exploits and disregard their shortcomings.' He went on, 'People, one servant of God has been given by God to choose between what is upon earth and what is with God. And he has chosen what is with God.' Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, was one among the people there who understood this and he wept, saying, 'We would redeem you with ourselves, our children and our possessions!' But the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Take it easy, Abu Bakr! Look at these doors leading in to the mosque and close them all off, except for the one from Abu Bakr's house. I know of no one whom I consider a finer Companion than him.'"
lessons: Even in the face of death, the Prophet (SAAS) prioritized the spiritual and communal welfare of his people, modeling selfless leadership until the very end.
Gratitude toward those who supported us in difficult times should be expressed openly and repeatedly, as the Prophet honored the Ansar even in his final address.
The willingness to choose what is with God over worldly comfort is the highest expression of faith and trust in the Divine.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed or near the end of your strength, consider what legacy of kindness and gratitude you can still offer to those around you.
Make it a habit to verbally honor and acknowledge those who have supported you through hardship, following the Prophet's example of publicly praising the Ansar.
In moments of deep grief or loss, allow yourself to feel that emotion as Abu Bakr did, while also trusting that God's plan is greater than your current understanding.

title: Abu Bakr Weeps as the Prophet Speaks of a Servant Given a Divine Choice
summary: During his final illness, the Prophet (SAAS) gave a veiled address about a servant of God who chose to meet his Lord over remaining in the world; only Abu Bakr understood the meaning and wept, while others were puzzled by his tears.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Abu al-Walid related to him, quoting Hisham, quoting Abu 'Awana, from 'Abd al-Malik, from Ibn Abu al-Mu'alla, from his father (who said) "The Messenger of God (SAAS) one day made an address and said, 'A man has been given by his Lord to choose between living on earth so long as he wishes and eating on earth whatever he wishes, and between meeting his Lord. And he has chosen to meet with his Lord.' Abu Bakr wept. The Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked one another, 'Aren't you surprised at this sheikh and the Messenger of God, referring to a fine man to whom God has given the choice of remaining on earth or meeting his Lord and him choosing to meet his Lord?' For it was Abu Bakr who was most aware of what the Messenger of God (SAAS) had meant. And Abu Bakr responded, 'No, we will redeem you with our possessions and our children!' And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'No person is more faithful to us in his friendship and generosity than (Abu Bakr) Ibn Abu Quhafa. And were I to take someone as my Companion, that person would be Ibn Abu Quhafa. However, there (is between us) love, brotherly feelings and faith. Your friend is the companion of God, Almighty and Glorious is He.'"
lessons: Deep spiritual insight, like Abu Bakr's, comes from years of devoted closeness to truth; proximity to righteousness sharpens the heart's understanding.
Grief for the loss of a beloved and righteous person is not weakness but a sign of the depth of genuine love and connection.
The bond of faith and brotherhood can be deeper and more enduring than any worldly relationship or title.
practical_advice: Cultivate relationships with people of wisdom and faith who help you see beyond the surface of events to their deeper spiritual meaning.
When you experience anticipatory grief or the fear of losing someone dear, allow yourself to express it honestly as Abu Bakr did, without shame.
Reflect on the relationships in your life that are built on shared faith and values, and invest in nurturing them as among your most precious assets.

title: The Prophet's Final Address: Seeking Forgiveness and Settling Debts
summary: During his fatal illness, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) mounted the minbar and delivered a farewell address, inviting anyone he had wronged to seek retribution or repayment before his death.
story: Al-Bayhaql stated that 'AlJ b. Al:Imad b. 'Abdan informed him, qnoting Al:Imad b. 'Ubayd al-Saffar, quoting Ibn Abu Qtmmash - he being Mul:Iammad b. 'lsaquoting Musa b. Isma'il Abu qmran al-Jubbuli, quoting Ma'an b. 'lsa al-Qazzaz, from al-I:Iarith b. 'Abd ai-Malik b. 'Abd Allah b. Unays al-Laythi, from al-Qisirn b. Yazld b. 'Abd Allah b. Qtslt, from his father, from 'Ata', from Ibn 'Abbas, from al-Faql b. 'Abbas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) came to me while severely ill and having bandaged his head. He said, 'Faql, take my hand.' I did so until he had mounted the minbar. He then said, 'Summon the people, Faql.' I cried ont, 'General assembly for prayer!' "People assembled and the Messenger of God {SAAS) arose to address them. He said, 'To proceed, my absence from among you is near at hand. You will not longer see me among you in this place. I have been thinking that there was no one who could dispense with my affairs unless I do it among you (personally). If there is anyone whose back I have whipped, then here is my own back; let that person retaliate. Anyone whose money I took: here is mine from which he may take. Anyone against whose honour I have cursed should retaliate against mine.' "Let no man say, 'I fear rancour from the Messenger of God.' Indeed not; rancour is not my nature or my character. Those of you I like best are those who will either take their due ifI am in their debt or release me therefrom, that I meet Almighty God, without any injustice on anyone because of me.' "One of the men arose and said, 'Messenger of God, you have three dirhams ofmine.' He replied, 'I will not give the lie to anyone, nor will I ask him to swear an oath. Why do I have your money?' The man answered, 'Don't you remember how once a beggar passed you and you gave me orders, so I awarded him three dirhams?' He said, 'Give it to him, Fag!!' And he told the man to sit. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then returned to his original address and said, 'Anyone of you who still has anything resulting from deception should return it.' A man got up and said, 'Messenger of God, I have three dirhams I took by deceit.' 'Why did you do so?' he asked. 'I needed it,' he replied. 'Take it from him, Fagl,' the Messenger of God (SAAS) said. "He again reverted to his original address, then said, 'People, if any of you are troubled, stand and I will say a prayer for you.' "One man arose and said, 'Messenger of God, I am a hypocrite, a liar and I sleep to excess.' 'Umar b. al-Khanab interjected, 'Confound you, fellow! God would have covered for you (your shortcomings) if only you had done so for yourself]' The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Quiet, Ibn al-Khanab! Scandal on earth is easier to bear than scandal in the afterlife! 0 God, favour him with veracity and faith and take away his sleep, if he so wishes that.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, ''Umar is with me, and I with 'Umar. And the truth after me is with 'Umar.'"
lessons: The Prophet's concern for justice and accountability even on his deathbed shows that settling one's affairs with others is a spiritual priority, not merely a social one.
True moral character means being the first to invite accountability upon oneself, not waiting to be accused — a mark of profound integrity and trust in God's judgment.
Concealing one's own sins is a mercy to oneself, as public scandal carries its own burden; but when one voluntarily confesses seeking spiritual healing, compassion and prayer are the proper response.
practical_advice: Before major life transitions — illness, travel, or any significant change — make an effort to resolve outstanding debts, grudges, or wrongs with those around you, rather than leaving them unaddressed.
When you feel guilt over past wrongs, consider making quiet, private restitution rather than public confession, unless the wrong directly affects another person who is owed acknowledgment.
If someone comes to you confessing a fault or weakness, respond with compassion and prayer rather than judgment, following the Prophet's example with the man who admitted his flaws openly.

title: The Prophet's Farewell Words on Abu Bakr's Companionship and the Warning Against Grave Worship
summary: Five days before his death, the Prophet (SAAS) addressed his companions, affirmed Abu Bakr's unique place as his closest friend, and issued a solemn warning against turning graves into places of worship.
story: The I;afi.. al-Bayhaqj narrated through Is/.1aq b. Ibrahjm - he being Ibn Rahaway/.1 - who quoted Zakariyya' b. 'Adl, quoting 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Amr al-Raqqj, from Zayd b. Abu Anlsa, from 'Amr b. Murra, from 'Abd Allah b. al-I:Iarith, who quoted ]undab as having said that he heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) saying, five days before his death, "From (all of) you I have had brothers and friends. I release each companion from his companionship. If I were to take from my nation one companion, then that would be Abu Bakr. My Lord has taken me as His companion, as He did Abraham. A people who came prior to yourselves treated the graves oftheir prophets and holy men as mosques. Do not treat graves as mosques; I prohibit you from doing that." Muslim narrated this in his ~al;il; collection, from Is/.1aq b. Rahawayh in similar terms. This day that preceded his death by five days was that Thursday referred to above by Ibn 'Abbas.
lessons: The Prophet's final days were marked not by personal fear but by concern for his community's spiritual integrity long after his departure, showing that true leadership looks beyond the present moment.
Friendship with God — being taken as His companion as Abraham was — is the highest station, one that transcends all human bonds no matter how beloved.
Warnings against innovation in worship, especially those that blur the boundary between veneration and worship, are a form of mercy and protection for the community across generations.
practical_advice: In your final moments of any chapter of life — a job, a relationship, a phase of growth — focus not on what you are leaving behind but on what safeguards and wisdom you can pass forward to those who remain.
Reflect on the spiritual friendships and bonds in your life and express gratitude for them openly, as the Prophet did with Abu Bakr, before circumstances make it impossible to do so.
Be conscious of the practices and habits you adopt in grief or devotion, ensuring they remain grounded in sound principles rather than emotional impulse that could lead to excess.

title: The Prophet's Last Public Address: Praising Abu Bakr and Closing the Mosque Doors
summary: In one of his final appearances, the Prophet (SAAS) mounted the minbar during his fatal illness, praised Abu Bakr as his closest companion, and ordered all doors into the mosque closed except Abu Bakr's — a sign of the coming caliphate.
story: The I;afi.. aI-Bayhaqj stated that Abu aI-I:Iasan b. Mul:Iammad al-Muqri' informed him, quoting aI-I:Iasan b. Mul:Iamad b. Isl:Iaq, quoting YUsufb. Ya'qub - he being Ibn 'Awana al-Isfaraylnl - who said, "Mul:Iammad b. Abu Bakr related to us, quoting Wahb b. Jarlr, quoting his father, who said, 'I heard Ya'1a b. I:Iaklm relate from qkrima, who quoted Ibn 'Abbas as saying, "The Prophet (SAAS) went outside during the illness from which he died with his head bandaged by a piece of cloth. He mounted the minbar, gave praise and thanks to God, then said, 'Not one of the people has been more faithful to me with both himself and his possessions than Abu Bakr. If I were taking anyone of the people as a companion, that person would be Abu Bakr. However, (what I have with him) is the companionship of Islam, which is best. Close off from me every entry-way into the mosque except that of Abu Bakr.'"" Al-Bukharl narrated it from 'Ubayd Allah b. Mul:Iammad al-Ju'fl, from Wahb b. Jarlr b. I:Iazim, from his father. In the words of the Prophet (SAAS), "Close· off from me every khawja, 'entry-way'" - by which he meant the small doors - 'into the mosque except that of Abu Bakr", he was making a reference to the caliphate. That is, so that he (Abu Bakr) could pass through it to lead the prayer with the Muslims. Al-BukhaIi also narrated it from the ftadith of 'Abd al-Ral.iman b. Sulayman b. I:Ian?ala b. al-Ghasll, from qkrima, from Ibn 'Abbas. He stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) came out in the illness from which he died with his head wrapped in a blackish cloth, with a mantle over his shoulders and sat upon the minbar. Ibn 'Abbas went on to quote the address, inclnding his counsel to the anFiir, and concluded with the words, "It was the last meeting the Messenger of God (SAAS) attended until he died." By this he meant that this was the last address made by the Messenger of God (SAAS).
lessons: Loyalty that is tested over a lifetime of sacrifice is a rare and sacred thing; the Prophet's repeated public affirmation of Abu Bakr teaches us to recognize and honour those who have stood by us faithfully.
Leadership succession, when handled with clarity and wisdom even under personal suffering, is itself an act of mercy and service to the community.
The companionship of Islam — a bond forged in faith rather than blood or affection alone — is described as the highest form of human relationship, a reminder of what truly endures.
practical_advice: Take time, especially during difficult personal seasons, to openly acknowledge those whose loyalty and faithfulness have supported you — do not assume they know how much they mean to you.
When you are in a position of responsibility, think carefully about who is best equipped to carry that responsibility forward, and make your intention clear while you still can.
Reflect on the relationships in your life that are sustained by shared values and faith rather than convenience alone — invest in and protect those bonds deliberately.

title: 'Umar Leads Prayer in Error; The Prophet Insists on Abu Bakr
summary: When Abu Bakr was absent and 'Umar was asked to lead the prayer during the Prophet's illness, the Prophet (SAAS) heard 'Umar's voice and firmly rejected it, insisting that only Abu Bakr should lead the people.
story: Imam Al.Jmad stated that Ya'qub related to him, quoting his father, from Ibn Isl.Jaq, quoting Ibn Shihab al-Zuhri, quoting 'Abd ai-Malik b. Abu Bakr b. 'Abd al-Ral.Jman b. al-I;Iarith b. Hisham, from his father, from 'Abd Allah b. Hisham, from his father, from 'Abd Allah b. Zam'a b. al-Aswad b. al-Muttalib b. Asad, who said, "When the illness of the Messenger of God (SAAS) grew more intense, I was there with him, along with a group of his Companions. Bila! made the call to prayer and he (the Prophet (SAAS)) said, 'Tell someone to lead the people in prayer.' "So I went off and found 'Omar among the people, Abu Bakr being absent. I said, ''Omar, get up and lead people in ptayer.' When 'Omar made the declaration that 'God is most Great!' the Messenger of God ($AAS) heard his voice- he was a man with a loud voice - and asked, 'Where is Abu Bakr? God refuses that, as do the Muslims! God refuses that, as do the Muslims!' "He then sent for Abu Bakr and he came after 'Umar had led that prayer. And then Abu Bakr prayed with the people." 'Abd Allah b. Zam'a went on, "'Umar said to me, 'Confound you! What have you done, Ibn Zam'a! By God, I only thought that it was the Messenger of God who told me to do it. Otherwise, I would not have led the ptayer.' I replied, 'I swear that was not what the Messenger of God ordered. However, when I did not see Abu Bakr, I thought you the most deserving man present at the prayer.'" That is also how Abu Da'ud narrated it, from the 1;adith of Ibn Isl)aq quoting a1-Zuhri. Yunus b. Bukayr narrated it from Ibn Isl)aq, quoting Ya'qub b. 'Otba, from Abu Bakr b. 'Abd a1-Ral)man, from 'Abd Allah b. Zam'a, as above. Abu Da'ud stated that AJ:tmad b. $a!il:t related to him, quoting Ibn Abu Fudayk, quoting Musa b. Ya'qub, from 'Abd a1-Ral)man b. Isl)aq, from Ibn Shihab, from 'Obayd Allah b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Otba, (who said) that 'Abd Allah b. Zam'a related to him the following account, "When the Prophet ($AAS) heard 'Omar's voice, he came out, so that his head could be seen looking out from his room. Then he exclaimed, 'No! No! Only Ibn Abu Qul)afa shall lead the people in prayer!' He spoke this in anger."
lessons: Acting on one's own judgment in matters of authority, even with good intentions, can lead to outcomes that were never sanctioned — humility requires checking one's assumptions before acting.
'Umar's honest acknowledgment of his mistake and his clear statement that he would not have acted differently had he known shows that integrity means owning one's errors without defensiveness.
The Prophet's repeated and firm insistence on Abu Bakr reflects that true leadership is not about volume or presence alone, but about spiritual readiness and the trust of those in authority over us.
practical_advice: When you are uncertain whether you have been given a responsibility or have simply assumed it, pause and verify before acting — especially in matters that affect a community or group.
If you make a decision based on your best judgment and it turns out to be wrong, own it clearly and without excuses, as 'Umar did — this preserves trust and models integrity for those around you.
Resist the temptation to fill a vacuum of leadership simply because you are present and capable; sometimes the most faithful act is to wait for the right person, even if it means a brief delay.

title: The Prophet Prays Behind Abu Bakr in His Final Days
summary: Despite his severe illness, the Prophet (SAAS) came out to join the congregation, was seated beside Abu Bakr, and prayed behind him — one of the most moving scenes of his final days.
story: AI-Bukhari stated that 'Omar b. I:Iaf~ related to him, quoting his father, quoting al-A'mash, from Ibrahim (who stated that) al-Aswad said, "We were with 'A'isha at her home and made reference to the assiduity (of the Messenger of God ($AAS)), to prayer and to her. She said, 'When the Messenger of God ($AAS) was suffering from the illness from which he died, and the time for the prayer arrived and Bilal made the call to it, he said, "Tell AbU Bakr to lead the people in prayer." He was told, "Abu Bakr is a grievously sad man; if he were to stand up in your place, he would not be able to proceed with the prayer." The Messenger of God ($AAS) repeated what he had said and the same response was given him. He said it a third time, then went on, "You women are like Joseph's companions. Tell Abu Bakr to lead the ptayer with the people!" "'Abu Bakr went out (to perform the prayer). The Messenger of God ($AAS) felt somewhat better and went out, being helped forward between two men; I seem to see now his two feet dragging in pain. Abu Bakr wanted to stay back, but the Prophet ($AAS) gestured to him to remain in his place. He was then brought and seated at his side.'" (AI-Bukhari's account continues) "Someone asked al-A'mash, 'Was the Messenger of God ($AAS) praying and Abu Bakr ptaying as he did, with the people then following the prayer of Abu Bakr?' Using his head, al-A'mash answered in the affinnative." AI-Bukhali weut on, "Abu Da'ud narrated this in part from Shu'ba. Abu Mu'awiya added the following to the account given by al-A'mash, 'He sat at the right side of Abu Bakr; Abu Bakr was standiug as he prayed.'"
lessons: The Prophet dragging his feet to join the congregation in prayer, even in his final days, is a timeless testament to the centrality of communal worship and not allowing personal suffering to become an excuse for abandoning what matters most.
Abu Bakr's grief was so deep that those around him doubted he could lead — yet he did so faithfully, reminding us that grief and duty are not mutually exclusive; we can carry both.
The image of the Prophet seated at Abu Bakr's side, following his lead, is a profound act of trust and honour — true leaders are not diminished by acknowledging others, but elevated.
practical_advice: When illness, grief, or exhaustion makes it feel impossible to maintain your obligations, ask yourself which ones are truly non-negotiable and find creative, supported ways to fulfil them rather than abandoning them entirely.

If you are in a position of leadership and feel the weight of grief or sadness, do not let it paralyse you — those who depend on you need your presence and steadiness even in your most vulnerable moments.
When someone you love and respect steps aside to honour another, receive that gesture with the humility Abu Bakr showed — stay in your place and carry the trust that has been placed in you.

title: Abu Bakr Appointed to Lead Prayers During the Prophet's Illness
summary: During his final illness, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) repeatedly instructed Abu Bakr to lead the people in prayer, despite Aisha's concerns that the people would view this as an ill omen. The Prophet insisted, comparing the women's objections to those of Joseph's companions.
story: people then following the prayer of Abu Bakr?' Using his head, al-A'mash answered in the affinnative." Al-Bukhari weut on, "Abu Da'ud narrated this in part from Shu'ba. Abu Mu'awiya added the following to the account given by al-A'mash, 'He sat at the right side of Abu Bakr; Abu Bakr was standiug as he prayed.'" AI-Bukhali narrated this in several places in his work. Muslim, al-Nasa'i and Ibn Maja narrated it along various lines of transmission from al-A'mash. In one of these al-Bukhari gave it from Qutayba; Muslim gave it from Abu Bakr b. Abu Shayba and Yaḥya b. Yai)ya, from Abu Mu'awiya. AI-Bukhan also stated that 'Abd Allah b. Yusufrelated to him, quoting Malik, from Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, from 'A'isha, to the effect that she stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said during his illness, 'Tell Abu Bakr to lead the prayer for the people.'" Ibn Shihab stated, "'Ubayd Allah b. 'Abd Allah quoted 'A>isha as having said, 'I opposed the Messenger of God (SAAS) in this; the only thing making me oppose him was my concern that the people would perceive Abu Bakr's praying as an evil portent, though I knew that would be the case no matter who took his place. What I wanted was to have the Messenger of God (SAAS) deflect this away from Abu Bakr to someone else.'" In the 5alḥiḥ collection of Muslim, it is stated in a ḥadith of 'Abd al-Razzaq, from Ma'mar, from al-Zuhli, who said, "Ḥarnza b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar informed me that 'A'isha stated, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) carne into my house, he said, "Tell Abu Bakr to lead the people in the prayer." I said, "Messenger of God, Abu Bakr is a sensitive man; ifhe recites the QJIr'an, he will not be able to restrain his tears. What if you were to tell someone else?"' "'A'isha went on, 'I swear by God, I only did this out of my dislike that the people would blame the fIrst man who prayed in place of the Messenger of God (SAAS) as an evil portent. I reiterated this two or three times, but he said, "Let Abu Bakr lead the prayer for the people. You women are like Joseph's companions."'" In both 5alḥiḥ collections there is a ḥadith of 'Abd ai-Malik b. 'Umayr, from Abu Burda, from Abu Musa, who quoted his father as statiug, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was ill and said, 'Tell Abu Bakr to lead the prayer for the people.' "'A'isha said, 'Messeuger of God, Abu Bakr is a sensitive man; when he stands up in your place, he won't be able to pray with the people.' He said, 'Tell Abu Bakr to lead the prayer for the people. You women are like Joseph's companions.' And so Abu Bakr did lead the prayer throughout the (remainder of) the life of the Messenger of God (SAAS)."
lessons: Divine guidance often comes with clarity and firmness, even when those around us are hesitant or fearful — trusting God's direction is essential in moments of uncertainty.
Leadership is not merely about capability but about readiness to step into responsibility even when it feels daunting or burdensome.
Those who act out of sincere concern for others, as Aisha did, may still need to yield to a higher wisdom; good intentions do not always override divine instruction.
practical_advice: When you feel anxious about a decision that has been clearly guided, remind yourself that your worry, while valid, may be rooted in fear rather than wisdom — pause, reflect, and trust the process.
If you are asked to take on a role that feels too large or symbolic, remember that you are chosen for your character, not your comfort — step forward with humility.
When your counsel is not accepted, examine whether your advice came from genuine concern or from your own emotional projections, and practice releasing outcomes you cannot control.

title: The Prophet Faints Repeatedly While Trying to Lead Prayer
summary: During his final illness, the Prophet (SAAS) attempted multiple times to go out and lead the people in prayer, fainting each time before he could rise. The community waited anxiously in the mosque as he struggled.
story: Imam Ai)mad stated that 'Abd al-Rai)man b. Mabdi related to him, quoting Za'ida, from Musa b. Abu 'A'isha, from 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Abd Allah, who said, "I went in to 'A'isha and asked, 'Would you tell me about the illness of the Messenger of God (SAAS)?' She replied, 'Certainly. When he became very ill, he asked, "Have the people prayed?" We replied, "No; they are waiting for you, Messenger of God." He then said, "Pour some water into the wash basin for me." We did so. He-then washed and made as if to get up to leave, but fainted. When he recovered, he asked, "Have the people prayed?" We replied, 'No; they are waiting for you, Messenger of God.' "Pour some water into the wash basin for me," he asked. We did so and he washed and made as if to get up to leave, but fainted. When he recovered, he asked, "Have the people prayed?" We replied, "No; they are waiting for you, Messenger of God." He said, "Pour some water into the wash basin for me," he asked. We did so and he washed and made as if to get up to leave, but fainted. When he recovered, he asked, "Have the people prayed?" We replied, "No; they are waiting for you."' "'A>isha went on, "The people were still in the mosque, waiting for the Messenger of God ($AAS), for the al-'isha' prayer. The Messenger of God ($AAS) sent a message to Abu Bakr saying that he should lead the people in prayer. Abu Bakr was a sensitive man and he asked, 'CUmar, you lead the people in prayer.' He replied, 'No; you are more worthy of that.' But he did lead them in prayer for those days."
lessons: Even in profound physical weakness, the Prophet's (SAAS) first concern was for his community's worship — this teaches us that devotion to one's purpose transcends personal suffering.
Persistence in the face of repeated setbacks, even when the body fails, is a mark of sincere commitment; we should not abandon our responsibilities at the first sign of difficulty.
The community's patient waiting in the mosque reflects the profound love and loyalty that binds a believing community — genuine bonds are not broken by hardship.
practical_advice: When illness or exhaustion prevents you from fulfilling your duties, do not be consumed by guilt — delegate with intention, as the Prophet (SAAS) did, and trust that your community or support network can carry forward what you began.
If you are waiting on someone you love who is suffering, channel your anxiety into prayer and presence rather than restlessness.

In moments where you feel you are failing repeatedly at something important, ask yourself whether you are pushing forward out of love and duty or out of ego, and adjust your approach with gentleness toward yourself.

title: The Prophet Joins Abu Bakr in Prayer While Seated, Led Between Two Men
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) recovered slightly during his illness and was led between two men, one of them al-Abbas, to perform the Zuhr prayer. He prayed seated beside Abu Bakr, completing the prayer in a deeply moving scene witnessed by the congregation.
story: "Then the Messenger of God ($AAS) recovered somewhat and went out, being led between two men, one ofthem al-'Abbas, to perform the al-ẓuhr prayer. When Abu Bakr saw him, he went to move to the back, but the Messenger of God ($AAS) gestured to him not to do so and ordered the two men to seat him beside Abu Bakr. Abu Bakr began praying, standing while the Messenger of God ($AAS) prayed while seated." 'Ubayd Allah went on, "I then went in to Ibn 'Abbas and said, 'Should I tell you what 'A'isha told me about the illness of the Messenger of God ($AAS)?' 'Let's hear it,' he replied. So I did tell him, and he contradicted nothing. He did ask, 'Did she name to you the man who was with al-'Abbas?' I replied, 'No.' 'That was cAlI,' he said." Both al-Bukharl and Muslim narrated it from Alḥmad b. Yunus, from Za'ida.. In one account, the text states, "Abu Bakr began praying with the prayer of the Messenger of God ($AAS) while standing; the people repeated the prayer delivered by Abu Bakr, while the Messenger of God ($AAS) remained seated."
lessons: Humility in leadership is demonstrated when the Prophet (SAAS) did not displace Abu Bakr even when he appeared, but instead took his place beside him — great leaders strengthen others rather than overshadow them.
Continuing to worship God even when one's body is failing is one of the most powerful expressions of faith; our spiritual practice need not wait for perfect circumstances.
The act of Ali and al-Abbas supporting the Prophet (SAAS) physically illustrates that accepting help from loved ones is not weakness but a form of human dignity and mutual care.
practical_advice: When you are going through something physically or emotionally exhausting, allow yourself to be 'led between two men' — let trusted people support you without shame or resistance.
If you are in a position of authority and someone more senior appears, resist the impulse to immediately step aside out of anxiety; wait for guidance rather than acting from insecurity.
Practice whatever form of worship or spiritual connection is available to you even in diminished capacity — a seated prayer is still a prayer, and a small good deed still counts.

title: The Prophet's Final Appearance at the Mosque Curtain on the Last Monday
summary: On the Monday of his death, the Prophet (SAAS) drew aside the curtain of his chamber and looked out at the congregation mid-prayer. His luminous face caused the Muslims to be overcome with joy, but he gestured for them to complete the prayer and drew the curtain down — it was his final farewell to his community.
story: "Abu Bakr would pray before them during the illness from which the Messenger of God (SAAS) died. This was until the Monday. They (the Muslims) were in lines for the prayer when the Prophet (SAAS) withdrew the curtain across his chamber and looked out at us. He was standing, and his face was like that of a sheet from a musḥaf." He smiled and laughed. We were becoming overwhelmed with delight at sight of the Prophet (SAAS), when Abu Bakr turned around to straighten the line. He thought that the Prophet (SAAS) was coming out for the prayer. But the latter gestured to us to complete the prayer. He then drew down the curtain. That was the same day he died." Muslim narrated it from a ḥadith of Sufyan b. 'Uyayna, Sablh b. Kaysan and Ma'mar, from al-Zuhri, from Anas.
lessons: The Prophet's (SAAS) final smile at his community was a gesture of love and reassurance — even at the threshold of death, he thought of others' peace before his own.
The companions' overwhelming joy at merely seeing the Prophet's (SAAS) face reminds us to cherish the presence of those we love while we still have access to them.
His gesture to complete the prayer, rather than interrupt it, teaches us that even in final moments, the obligations of worship and community transcend personal sentiment.
practical_advice: When someone you love is near the end of their life, do not only grieve — also allow yourself to receive the gift of their presence, their smile, and even their final gestures of love.
If you find yourself in a state of joy or grief that threatens to overwhelm a communal moment, take a breath and re-center on the collective responsibility at hand, as Abu Bakr did.
Reflect regularly on what final message you would want to communicate to those who love you — and begin living that message now, through your daily interactions and expressions of care.

title: The Prophet's Last Prayer with the Community, Supported by Usama ibn Zayd
summary: In what would be his final communal prayer, the Prophet (SAAS) prayed behind Abu Bakr wearing a single garment, and afterward called for Usama ibn Zayd to support him. He leaned his back against Usama's chest — a final act of trust and closeness before his death.
story: Al-Bayhaql narrated through Sulayman b. Bilal and Yaḥya b. Ayyfib, from Ḥurnayd, from Anas, that the Prophet (SAAS) prayed while behind Abu Bakr, wearing one garment the ends of which were crossed over. When he wished to get up, he said, "Call Usama b. Zayd for me." He came aud the Messenger of God (SAAS), rested his back against Usama's chest. This was the last prayer he performed. AI-Bayhaql stated, "In this there is evidence that this prayer was that of the morning of Monday, the day on which his death occurred, because that was the last prayer he performed and because it is established that he died in the forenoon of Monday."
lessons: The Prophet's (SAAS) choice to lean on Usama — a young man he loved deeply — in his final moments teaches us that love expressed through physical closeness and trust is among the most profound human gifts.
Performing one's worship to the very end of one's capacity, wrapped in a single garment and leaning on another, is a testament to the priority of spiritual duty over worldly comfort.
Death, when it comes to the righteous, does not find them unprepared or alone — it finds them in the company of those they have loved and nurtured throughout their lives.
practical_advice: Identify the people in your life whose presence brings you deep comfort, and invest in those relationships now — do not wait for a crisis to lean on them.
When your physical or emotional resources are at their lowest, allow yourself to rest against the support of others without guilt; interdependence is a human virtue, not a weakness.

Consider how you are living your final days of any chapter — a project, a relationship, a season of life — and ask whether you are showing up with the same devotion as if it were truly your last opportunity.

title: The Prophet's Final Appearance at the Mosque
summary: On the last Monday of his life, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) lifted the curtain of his room and appeared before the congregation mid-prayer, his face radiant, and gestured for them to complete the prayer before drawing the curtain for the last time.
story: He was standing, and his face was like that of a sheet from a mus/:taj1" He smiled and laughed. We were becoming overwhelmed with delight at sight of the Prophet (SAAS), when Abu Bakr turned around to straighten the line. He thought that the Prophet (SAAS) was coming out for the prayer. But the latter gestured to us to complete the prayer. He then drew down the curtain. That was the same day he died." Muslim narrated it from a /:tadith of Sufyan b. 'Uyayna, Sablh b. Kaysan and Ma'mar, from al-Zului, from Anas. AI-Bukharl then stated that Abu Ma'mar related, quoting 'Abd al-Warith, quoting 'Abd ai-'Azlz, from Anas b. Malik, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not come out for three days. Then the prayer was held. Abu Bakr went to advance and the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Take the screen.' He raised it. And when the face of the Prophet (SAAS) became visible, we felt as though we had never seen any more wonderful sight than it. The Prophet (SAAS) gestured to Abu Bakr to go forward. The Prophet (SAAS) then released the screen. He remained incapacitated (thereafter) until he died." Muslim narrated it from a /:tadith of 'Abd al-Samad b. 'Abd al-Warith, from his father. This provides very clear evidence that the Messenger of God (SAAS) did not perform the al-~ub{t prayer with the people on the Monday, and that he had withdrawn from them, not having gone out to them for three days. We note that therefore the last prayer he performed with them was that of al-;,uhr, as is clearly stated in the {tadith given above from 'A'isha. That would have been on the Thursday, not the Saturday, nor the Sunday, as al-Bayhaqi told it from the work on the military campaigns by Musil b. 'Uqba. That {tadith is weak, especially in light of the address he made following it, and of his having been absent from them on the Friday, Saturday and Sunday, these being those full days. AI-Zuhri stated, quoting Abu Bakr b. Abu Sabra, that Abu Bakr led them in prayer 17 times. Others give tJiat number as 20. God knows best. Then his noble visage appeared before them early in the morning of the Monday, and he bad them farewell with a gaze that almost overwhelmed them with delight. That was the last occasion on which he gathered with them. One who might have spoken for them would have agreed with the poet, who said: "I looked on as though death were only an hour away; how would it be if the time remaining were to end with Judgement Day!"
lessons: The final parting moments of the Prophet remind us that beauty and grace can exist even at the threshold of death, teaching us that our last acts and expressions carry great weight and meaning.
The Prophet's gesture to continue the prayer despite his own incapacity shows that community worship and spiritual responsibility are maintained even in moments of personal weakness.
The overwhelming joy the Companions felt at merely seeing the Prophet's face teaches us to cherish and truly value the presence of those we love while they are still with us.
practical_advice: When facing the impending loss of someone dear, allow yourself to fully absorb and treasure each remaining moment with them rather than looking away from the reality of goodbye.
Even in your most difficult or painful moments, try to offer what small gestures of reassurance and love you can to those around you who are worried about you.
Reflect on the people in your life whose presence brings you deep joy and express that appreciation to them today, rather than waiting until farewell becomes unavoidable.

title: Abu Bakr Appointed as Imam: The Prophet's Last Selection
summary: During his final illness, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) consistently chose Abu Bakr to lead the congregational prayers, a selection understood by scholars as a testament to Abu Bakr's preeminence in knowledge, recitation, and faith.
story: The outcome is that the Messenger of God (SAAS) did select Abu Bakr, "the trusting", as imam for all the Companions in the prayer assembly, what is in actuality the most important of the pillars of Islam. Sheikh Abu aI-Basan aI-Ash'ari stated, "This selection (of Abu Bakr) is a fact about the religion oflslam necessarily to be recognized." He went on, "The selection of him by the Prophet (SAAS) is proof that Abu Bakr was the Companion who was the most learned and the best reciter (of the Qur'an). This is established in that statement of the Prophet (SAAS), accepted as authentic by the scholars, in which he said, 'The person to act as the people's imam is to be the one who best recites the Book of God. Should several be equal in this, then the imam should be the one most knowledgeable in the sunna. If equal in this too, then the oldest of them. If equal in this, then the one of them who first accepted Islam.'" I note that these words of al-Ash'art, may God have mercy upon him, ought to be inscribed in gold! All these qualities, moreover, were combined in Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him.
lessons: Leadership rooted in knowledge, piety, and devotion to God's word is the most enduring and trustworthy form of leadership.
The Prophet's deliberate choice of Abu Bakr as imam even in his final days shows us that preparing others for responsibility is itself an act of care and wisdom.
True merit in the eyes of God is measured by knowledge, practice of the sunnah, seniority in faith, and sincerity — qualities that outweigh worldly status or influence.
practical_advice: When you are in a position of leadership or mentorship, use your remaining influence to intentionally elevate and prepare those who are most qualified and trustworthy to carry on after you.
Invest in deepening your knowledge of your faith and its practice, knowing that such qualities are recognized by God and by the righteous community around you.
When choosing whom to trust or follow in matters of spiritual or communal importance, look for those who combine knowledge, consistent practice, and seniority in faith.

title: Ibn Mas'ud Visits the Gravely Ill Prophet
summary: Ibn Mas'ud visited the Prophet during his severe illness, and the Prophet consoled him by explaining that prophets suffer double the affliction of others but also receive double the reward, and that illness erases a Muslim's sins.
story: 'Imam AJ.unad stated that Abu Mu'awiya related to him, quoting ai-A'mash, from Ibrahim aI-Taymi, from al-I;Iarith b. Suwayd, from 'Abd Allah, he being Ibn Mas'ud, who said, "I went in to the Prophet (SAAS), when he was ill and I touched him. I told him, 'Messenger of God, you are seriously ill.' 'Yes,' he replied. 'I am ill enough for two!' I commented, 'You shall be doubly rewarded.' 'Yes,' he agreed, 'by Him in whose hand is my soul, every single Muslim who suffers illness shall have God cast away his sins, just as a tree drops its leaves.'" AI-Bukhari and Muslim gave it through various lines from Sulayrnan b. Mahran al-A'mash.
lessons: Illness and suffering are not signs of God's abandonment but can be channels of spiritual purification and elevated reward for the believer.
The Prophet's willingness to acknowledge his intense suffering honestly — 'I am ill enough for two' — shows us that admitting pain is not weakness but honesty before God and those who love us.
The image of sins falling away like leaves from a tree offers a profoundly hopeful perspective on hardship: every difficulty endured with faith is a form of mercy and cleansing.
practical_advice: When you are suffering through illness or hardship, remind yourself that God does not waste any pain you endure with patience — each moment of difficulty can be a source of spiritual elevation.
Allow yourself to honestly acknowledge your pain to trusted people in your life rather than minimizing it; truthfulness about suffering opens the door to genuine comfort and support.
On days when illness or hardship feels unbearable, hold on to the hope that your struggles may be removing burdens from your soul that you cannot even perceive.

title: Abu Sa'id al-Khudri Feels the Intensity of the Prophet's Fever
summary: Abu Sa'id al-Khudri placed his hand on the Prophet during his illness and was struck by the intense heat of his fever, to which the Prophet responded that prophets face doubled afflictions just as they receive doubled rewards.
story: The /;aft" Abu Yaqa al-Maw~ili stated in his musnad collection that Isl:Iaq b. Abu Isra'il related to him, quoting 'Abd aI-Razzaq, quoting Ma'mar, from Zayd b. Aslam, from "a man", from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri (who said) that he placed his hand upon the Prophet (SAAS), and told him, "By God, I can't bear to place my hand upon you, your fever is so severe!" The Prophet (SAAS) replied, "For us prophets affliction is doubled, just as our rewards are doubled. If one is a true prophet one might be afflicted unto death by lice. A man might be so afflicted by nakedness as to take his 'aba'a and make ajubba out of it. People should rejoice in deprivation as they do in prosperity."
lessons: The greatest of people in God's sight are often those who endure the greatest suffering, reminding us that hardship is not a measure of one's distance from God but sometimes of one's closeness to Him.
The Prophet's teaching that people should 'rejoice in deprivation as they do in prosperity' challenges us to cultivate a heart that finds contentment and gratitude in all conditions of life.
Witnessing the suffering of those we deeply love is itself a form of trial, and responding with compassion and presence — as Abu Sa'id did — is an act of profound love.
practical_advice: Practice reframing moments of scarcity, loss, or hardship as opportunities for inner growth, knowing that ease and difficulty are both gifts when received with the right heart.
