When you sit with someone who is suffering and feel helpless, remember that your presence and your touch of concern — like Abu Sa'id's — is itself a meaningful act of love and not nothing.
On days when life strips away comfort, status, or ease, ask yourself what this moment might be teaching you or purifying in you, rather than only asking when it will end.

title: Aisha Witnesses the Prophet's Intense Final Suffering
summary: Aisha (RA) bore witness to the most intense suffering she had ever seen in the Prophet during his final illness, holding him as he passed away, and declared that after witnessing his death throes she could no longer resent death's difficulty for anyone.
story: AI-Bukhari and Muslim narrated it from a /;adilk of SufYan aI-Thawri and Shu'ba b. aI-I;Iajjaj, to whom Muslim added Jarir. All three quote from ai-A'mash, from Abu Wa'il, the brother oflbn Salama, from Masruq, from 'j\'isha, who said, "I never saw the pain of any person more intense than that of the Messenger of God (SAAS)." In the Fa/;,/; collection of al-Bukhari, from a /;aditk ofYazid b. aI-Hadd, from 'Abd aI-Ra\.tman b. al-~sim, from his father, 'j\'isha is quoted as saying, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) died (while resting) his head between my breast and my chin. And I no longer abhor the death throes of anyone after (those of) the Prophet (SAAS)."
lessons: Witnessing another's profound suffering with love and presence — as Aisha held the Prophet through his final moments — is one of the highest acts of devotion and care a human being can offer.
Aisha's transformed perspective on death — no longer resenting its difficulty after witnessing the Prophet's passing — shows us that profound grief can lead to profound acceptance and compassion.
The fact that even the Messenger of God suffered intensely at death reminds us that death's difficulty is universal and does not reflect failure or lack of faith; it is part of the human journey.
practical_advice: If you are accompanying a loved one through serious illness or dying, know that being physically present and holding them — as Aisha held the Prophet — is an act of immeasurable love even when you cannot fix anything.
Allow grief and the witnessing of loss to soften rather than harden your heart, letting it deepen your compassion for every other person who must also face suffering and death.
When you feel overwhelmed by the suffering of someone you love, remember that your willingness to stay present in their pain rather than turn away is itself a form of healing for both of you.

title: The Prophet's Final Silent Prayer for Usama ibn Zayd
summary: As the Prophet's illness grew severe and he could no longer speak, Usama ibn Zayd visited him and witnessed the Prophet silently raising his hands to the sky in prayer before lowering them onto Usama — a wordless final blessing.
story: Imam Al:unad stated that Ya'qub related to him, quoting his father, quoting Mu\.lanunad b. Is\.laq, quoting Sa'id b. 'Ubayd b. al-Sabbaq from Mu\.lammad b. Usama b. Zayd, from his father Usama b. Zayd, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) became severely ill, I came down, along with others, to Medina. I went in to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and found him totally silent, not speaking at all. He began raising his hands towards the sky, then dropped them down on to me. I know that he was praying for me." Al-Tirmidhi narrated this from Abu Kurayb, from YUnus b. Bukayr, from Ibn Is\.laq. He categorized it as I;asan gharib.
lessons: Love and supplication for those we care about need no words — the Prophet's silent raising of hands toward heaven and lowering them onto Usama is a profound reminder that prayer and care transcend speech.
Even at the very threshold of death, the Prophet's concern was for others, teaching us that a life truly lived in service to others naturally extends that service to its final breath.

Being the recipient of someone's silent, wordless prayers and blessings is a profound gift — Usama's recognition of what was happening reminds us to stay spiritually attentive to the love given to us.
practical_advice: When words fail you in expressing love or supplication for someone you care about, know that lifting your hands in silent prayer for them is a complete and powerful act before God.
Make it a practice to pray specifically for those you love, not only in times of crisis but regularly, so that intercession for others becomes woven into the fabric of your daily life.
If you have been entrusted with someone's love and prayers — as Usama was — honour that gift by living in a way that reflects the care they invested in you.

title: The Prophet's Final Words and Last Moments in A'isha's Arms
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) spent his final moments in the arms of his wife A'isha, repeatedly calling out 'With the Highest Companion!' and wiping his face with water while experiencing the pangs of death. A'isha considered it among God's greatest blessings that the Prophet died on her day, in her house, and that their saliva mixed at the moment of his passing.
story:
severely ill, I came down, along with others, to Medina. I went in to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and found him totally silent, not speaking at all. He began raising his hands towards the sky, then dropped them down on to me. I know that he was praying for me."

Ḥammad b. Zayd stated, from Ayyūb, from Ibn Abu Mulayka, who said, "ʿĀ'isha stated, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) died in my house. And he died between my chest and my throat. Gabriel would protect him by saying a prayer when he was sick; I started to ask God to protect him, and he raised his sight to the sky. And he said, "With the Highest Companion! With the Highest Companion!" ʿAbd al-Raḥman b. Abu Bakr came in carrying a fresh date-palm leaf. He looked at it, and I thought he needed it. So I took it and chewed on it and gave it to him. He cleaned his teeth with it with more care than he ever did. He then went to give it to me, but it fell from his hand. And so God mixed my saliva with his on his last day on earth, (which was) also his first day in the Hereafter.'"

Al-Bukhārī narrated it from Sulayman b. Ḥarb, from Ḥammad b. Zayd. Al-Bayhaqi stated that Abu ʿAbd Allah al-Ḥāfiẓ informed him, quoting Abu Nasr Aḥmad b. Sahl, the jurist at Bukhara, quoting Ṣāliḥ b. Muḥammad al-Ḥāfiẓ of Baghdad, quoting Dāʾūd, from ʿAmr b. Zuhayr al-Ḍabbi, quoting ʿĪsa b. Yunus, from ʿUmar b. Saʿid b. Abu Ḥusayn, quoting Ibn Abu Mulayka (who said) that Abu ʿAmr Dhakwan, the freed-man of ʿĀ'isha informed him that ʿĀ'isha used to say, "It is one of God's blessings to me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) died on the day of my turn, in my house, between my chest and my neck, and that he mixed my saliva with his at his death." She went on, "My brother came in carrying a palm-wood toothpick. I was resting the Messenger of God (SAAS), on my chest and saw him looking at the toothpick. I knew that he was accustomed to and enjoyed using them. I asked him, 'Shall I get it for you!' He nodded in assent. I softened it for him and he used it in his mouth. Between his hands he was holding a pot or a tin containing water. He began putting his hand in the water and wiping his face with it. After that he raised his hands and said, 'There is no god but God! Death has its pangs.' Then he lifted his left (fore-finger) and began saying, 'With the Highest Companion! With the Highest Companion!' until he died and his hand slipped into the water."

Abu Dāʾūd al-Tayālisī stated that Shuʿba related to him, from Saʿd b. Ibrahim, who said that he heard ʿUrwa quote ʿĀ'isha as having said, "We used to tell one another that a prophet would not die until given to choose between this world and the hereafter. And during the illness of the Messenger of God (SAAS), from which he died, I offered him a seed and heard him say, 'With those prophets, holy men, martyrs and men of purity - they would be fine Companions.' And we thought that he had been given to choose."

Al-Zuhrī stated that Saʿid b. al-Musayyab and ʿUrwa b. al-Zubayr were among various scholars who quoted ʿĀ'isha as having said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) used to say - while healthy - that no prophet would be taken by death until he had seen his resting place in heaven and had been asked to make a choice. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) put his head down upon my thigh, he lost consciousness for a period. Then he came to and fixed his gaze upon the ceiling. He said, 'O God, the Highest Companion!' I know it related to what we had been talking about when he was healthy, that a prophet would not be taken in death before he had seen his resting place in heaven and had been offered a choice. So I commented, 'Then he is not choosing (to remain with) us.' And those words, 'the Highest Companion' were the final words uttered by the Messenger of God (SAAS)."

Sufyan - Sufyan al-Thawrī, that is - quoted from Ismāʿīl b. Abu Khālid, from Abu Burda, from ʿĀ'isha, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) lost consciousness while he was in my lap. I set about wiping his face and praying for a cure for him. He said, 'No; I ask God, the Most Gracious, the Highest Companion, with Gabriel, Mikāʾīl and Isrāfīl.'"

Imam Aḥmad stated that Yaʿqub related to him, quoting his father, from Ibn Isḥāq, quoting Ibn ʿAbbād b. ʿAbd Allah b. al-Zubayr, from his father ʿAbbād, who quoted ʿĀ'isha as saying, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) died between my chest and my neck and in my domain. And I did not do harm to anyone therein. And it is because of my comfort and my youth that the Messenger of God (SAAS) died."
lessons: The Prophet's final words — 'With the Highest Companion!' — reveal that a believer's last moments can be oriented entirely toward God and the eternal realm, showing that how we die reflects how we lived.
True love is expressed in small acts of care: A'isha softening a toothpick, wiping his face with water, holding him in her arms — these humble gestures carried immense tenderness and dignity.

The Prophet was given a choice between this world and the next, and he chose the Hereafter without hesitation, reminding us that our ultimate home is not this transient life but the eternal one to come.

Gratitude transforms even grief: A'isha regarded the Prophet dying in her arms as one of God's greatest blessings, teaching us that the framing of our trials can become a source of deep spiritual consolation.
practical_advice: When facing loss or grief, reflect on what blessings were woven into that painful moment — gratitude and sorrow can coexist and together become a source of healing.
Practice remembering God (dhikr) consistently in good times so that when difficulty or death approaches, the tongue and heart turn naturally toward Him, as the Prophet's did.
Show love through present, attentive care for those who are suffering — sitting with them, meeting their small needs, and staying close can be the most profound act of compassion.

When overwhelmed by the fear of death or endings, recall that the Prophet saw his resting place in the Hereafter as a reunion with the best of companions — reframe endings as transitions toward something greater.

title: The Prophet's Final Testamentary Warnings: Prayer, Care for the Vulnerable, and Thinking Well of God
summary: As the Prophet (SAAS) lay dying, his repeated and final instructions to his community were to uphold prayer and care for those under their protection, continuing to urge these until his chest gurgled and his tongue could no longer speak clearly. He also instructed believers to always think well of God.
story:
The ḥāfiẓ al-Bayhaqī stated that Abu Bakr b. Abu Rājaʾ al-Adīb informed him, quoting Abu al-ʿAbbās al-Aṣamm, quoting Aḥmad b. ʿAbd al-Jabbār, quoting Abu Bakr b. ʿAyyāsh, from al-Aʿmash, from Abu Sufyān, from Jābir b. ʿAbd Allāh, who said, "I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say three times before his death, 'Think well of God!'"

In one ḥadīth it is narrated by Muslim from al-Aʿmash, from Abu Sufyān Ṭalḥa b. Nāfiʿ, who quoted Jābir as stating, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Let none of you die without thinking well of God, Almighty is He.'"

In another ḥadīth narrated by Muslim, God Almighty is quoted as having stated, "I am as My worshippers regard Me; let them therefore think well of Me."

Al-Bayhaqi stated that al-Ḥākim informed him, quoting al-Aṣamm, quoting Muḥammad b. Isḥāq al-Ṣaghānī, quoting Abu Khaythama Zuhayr b. Ḥarb, quoting Jarīr, from Sulayman al-Taymī, from Qatāda, from Anas, who said, "The general testamentary statement spoken by the Messenger of God (SAAS) when his death approached was, '(Uphold) prayer; and (care for) what your right hands possess'. (He was saying this) until he began to gurgle over it and his tongue could not pronounce it clearly."

Imam Aḥmad stated that Asbaṭ b. Muḥammad related to him, quoting al-Taymī, from Qatāda, from Anas b. Mālik, who said, "The general testamentary statement made by the Messenger of God (SAAS), when his death approached was, '(Uphold) prayer; and (care for) what your right hands possess', until his chest began to gurgle as he spoke, and his tongue could scarcely express it."

Aḥmad stated that Bakr b. ʿĪsā al-Rāsibī related to him, quoting ʿUmar b. al-Faḍl, from Nuʿaym b. Yazīd, from ʿAlī b. Abu Ṭālib, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered me to take him a thin bone on which he could write what would prevent his nation from going astray after him. I was afraid that his spirit would slip away before (my return). I told him, 'I will memorize and be alert.' He said, 'I charge you (to attend to) the prayer, the zakāt and what your right hands possess.'"

Yaʿqub b. Sufyān stated that Abu al-Nuʿmān Muḥammad b. al-Fuḍayl related to him, quoting Abu ʿAwāna, from Qatāda, from Safīna, from Umm Salama, who said, "The general testamentary statement made by the Messenger of God (SAAS), at his death was, 'prayer and what your right hands possess' until he was gurgling with it in his chest and his tongue was not producing it."
lessons: The Prophet's insistence on repeating his final instructions even as speech became physically impossible shows that the values we truly hold will be the ones we cling to until our very last breath.

Thinking well of God (husn al-dhann billah) is not naive optimism but a spiritual discipline the Prophet commanded even at the threshold of death — our inner orientation toward God shapes our entire experience of life and death.

The two pillars of the Prophet's final testament — prayer and care for the vulnerable — reveal the essence of Islam: a vertical relationship with God expressed through worship, and a horizontal responsibility toward others expressed through justice and compassion.
True priorities become clear at the end of life; the Prophet's deathbed instructions invite us to ask ourselves now what we would most want to have upheld if these were our final moments.
practical_advice: Establish a daily prayer practice not as a ritual obligation alone but as a lifeline — the Prophet's last breath was spent urging it because consistent prayer sustains the soul through every difficulty.

Actively cultivate husn al-dhann billah — the habit of thinking well of God — especially during hardship. When things go wrong, practice asking 'What good might God intend through this?' rather than assuming abandonment.
Identify who in your life or community is most vulnerable and ask yourself honestly whether you are fulfilling your duty of care toward them, as the Prophet made this his dying instruction.
When overwhelmed or approaching the limits of your strength, focus on the most essential things — as the Prophet distilled his entire guidance into two principles — and release what is secondary.

title: The Prophet's Warning Against Making Graves into Places of Worship
summary: Among the Prophet's final statements before his death was a repeated warning against making the graves of prophets into mosques or places of worship, condemning this practice as he himself lay dying. He issued this warning as a caution to his community against falling into the same error as the Jews and Christians.
story:
In his work al-Muwatta' Imam Malik quoted Ismāʿīl b. Abu Bakr as saying that he heard ʿUmar b. ʿAbd al-ʿAzīz say, "The last of the statements made by the Messenger of God (SAAS) was, 'May God destroy the Jews and the Christians! They have adopted the graves of their prophets as mosques. Two religions shall not remain on the land of the Arabs!'" He narrated it thus in the mursal mode from the Commander of the Believers ʿUmar b. ʿAbd al-ʿAzīz, may God have mercy upon him.

Al-Bukhārī and Muslim narrated from a ḥadīth of al-Zuhrī, from ʿUbayd Allāh b. ʿAbd Allāh b. ʿUtba, from ʿĀ'isha and Ibn ʿAbbās, both of whom stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) received revelation, he would immediately throw a khamīṣa over his face. If he felt depressed, he would take it off his face. While thus, he once said, 'May God damn the Jews and the Christians! They have adopted the graves of their prophets as mosques!' He was cautioning against what they did."
lessons: The Prophet's concern in his final moments was not for himself but for the protection of his community's faith — a leader's greatest legacy is the clarity of guidance they leave behind.
Sincere monotheism requires ongoing vigilance; even practices that seem to honor the righteous can gradually drift into forms of veneration that compromise the purity of worship.
The Prophet issued this warning while himself approaching death and burial, showing the depth of his commitment to protecting his community from errors he could foresee even then.
practical_advice: Regularly examine your own spiritual practices to ensure they remain rooted in sincerity and clear guidance rather than cultural accretion or uncritical imitation.
When you are in a position of responsibility — as a parent, teacher, or leader — think about what final guidance you would want to leave those in your care, and begin living and teaching that now.
Study the boundaries and principles of worship in your tradition carefully so that devotion and love for righteous figures never inadvertently displaces direct connection with God.

title: The Prophet's Love for A'isha: A Vision of Her Hand in Paradise
summary: As the Prophet (SAAS) lay dying, he expressed the depth of his love for A'isha by saying that he found comfort in seeing the whiteness of her palm in paradise, a statement which scholars noted surpassed all other expressions of love ever recorded.
story:
Imam Aḥmad stated that Wakīʿ related to him, from Ismāʿīl, from Muṣʿab b. Isḥāq b. Ṭalḥa, from ʿĀ'isha, who quoted the Prophet (SAAS) as saying, "It eases me that I have seen the whiteness of ʿĀ'isha's palm in paradise."

Aḥmad is alone in giving this; its line of transmission is not bad. This gives evidence of the intensity of the love of the Messenger of God (SAAS) for ʿĀ'isha, may God be pleased with her. People have made many comments relating to the magnitude of love, but none of them has reached this extent. They, moreover, exaggerate in their words that have no truth to them. These words are truth, definitely and unquestionably.
lessons: Genuine love, rooted in virtue and shared devotion to God, transcends even the boundary between this world and the next — the Prophet's vision of A'isha in paradise speaks to a love that is spiritually elevated, not merely worldly.
Finding comfort in a beloved during the hardest moments of life — including death — is not weakness but a profound human grace that the Prophet himself exemplified.
Authentic expressions of love are distinguished from exaggeration by their truthfulness; the Prophet's words carry weight precisely because they came at his most sincere and vulnerable moment.
practical_advice: Reflect on those you love most and consider how your relationship with them reflects your relationship with God — love that is grounded in shared faith and goodness is among the greatest gifts of this life.
In moments of acute distress or pain, allow yourself to draw genuine comfort from the people and blessings God has placed in your life — this is not escapism but a mercy.
Practice expressing love and appreciation for those close to you sincerely and specifically, not in exaggerated flattery but in honest, heartfelt words that reflect true feeling.

title: The Prophet's Final Moments and Last Words in Aisha's Arms
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) breathed his last while resting against Aisha's chest, uttering prayers to join the Highest Companion, and Aisha later described the remarkable fragrance that accompanied his passing.
story: resting on her chest, saying, "0 God, forgive and have mercy on me; and let me join the Highest Companion!" Both compilers of the ~aftift collections gave it from a ftadith of Hisham b. 'lJrwa. Imam Al).mad stated that Ya'qub related to him, quoting his father, from Ibn Isl:Iaq, quoting Ibn 'Abbad b. 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr, from his father 'Abbad, who quoted 'A'isha as saying, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) died between my chest and my neck and in my domain. And I did not do harm to anyone therein. And it is because of my comfort and my youth that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was taken while in my lap. Then I placed his head upon a pillow and arose to slap myself and strike my face along with the women." Imam Al)mad stated that Mul)ammad b. 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr, quoted Kathir b. Zayd, from al-Munalib b. 'Abd Allah, who quoted 'I\.'isha as having said, "The Messenger of God ($AAS) used to say, 'Every single prophet has his spirit taken, and he sees the reward and thereafter it is returned to him; and he is made to choose between having it returned to him or joining (God).' I had learned that from him. As I rested him against my chest, I looked at him as he turned his neck and I said, 'He has decided.' I knew what he had said. I was looking right at him when he raised himself up and stared. I said, 'Then, by God, he is not choosing us!' He said, 'With the Highest Companion, in paradise with those prophets, men of trust, martyrs and men of purity ... fme, those men, as companions.'" Al)mad is alone in giving this. They (the other major compilers ofthe traditions) did not include it. Imam Al)mad stated that 'Affan related to him, quoting Hammam, quoting Hisham b. 'lJrwa, from his father, who quoted 'I\.'isha as having said, "The Messenger of God ($AAS) died while his head was between my breast and my neck. And when his soul came forth, I had never experienced a finer scent than it." This line of transmission is ,a[l;[I, according to the criteria of both ,a[l;[I collections; not one of the compilers of the six (canonical) books of traditions gave it. AI-Bayhaqi narrated it from a [ladith of l::Ianbal b. Isl).aq, from 'Affan. AI-Bayhaqi stated that Abii 'Abd Allah al-l::Iafi~ informed him, quoting Abii al-'Abbas al-Asamm, quoting Al)mad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar, quoting Yiinus, from Abii Ma'shar, from Mul).ammad b. Q;tys, from Abii 'lJrwa, from Umm Salama, who said, "I placed my hand upon the chest of the Messenger of God ($AAS), the day he died, and a mouthful passed on to me. And (even though) I perform the ablutions the scent of musk never leaves my hand." Al)mad stated that both 'Affan and Bahz stated to him, "Sulayman b. al-Mughlra related to us, quoting l::Iumayd b. Hilal, from Abii Burda, who said, 'I went in to visit 'I\.'isha and she brought out to us a coarse wrap like those made in Yemen and one of those garments they call al-mulabbada, "closely woven". She said, "The Messenger of God ($AAS) died while wearing these."''
lessons: Even in the most profound moments of loss, there is beauty and divine grace — the miraculous fragrance at the Prophet's passing reminds us that God's mercy surrounds those who are devoted to Him.
The Prophet consciously chose to join God rather than return to this world, teaching us that true contentment lies in closeness to the Divine, not attachment to worldly comfort.
Preserving small physical reminders of loved ones — as Aisha kept the Prophet's garments — is a natural and honoured part of human grief and memory.
practical_advice: When facing the loss of a loved one, allow yourself to grieve openly and naturally, as even those closest to the Prophet wept and expressed their sorrow without shame.
In moments of deep sadness, turn your supplication toward God as the Prophet did, asking for mercy and closeness to Him — this reframes loss as a return rather than an ending.
Hold onto meaningful mementos of those you have lost; they can serve as tangible bridges to cherished memories and sources of spiritual comfort.

title: Aisha Nurses the Prophet Through His Final Illness
summary: Aisha describes in detail the Prophet's final illness, how he came to stay in her quarters, and her experience nursing him until his death, including the moment she first sensed he had passed.
story: Imam Al).mad stated that Bahz related to him, quoting l::Iammad b. Salama, quoting Abii 'Irnran al-Jawni, from Yazid b. Babaniis, who said, "land a companion went to 'I\.'isha and asked if we could visit with her. She set down a cushion for us and threw a veil over herself. My companion asked, 'Mother of the Believers, what do you have to say about the al-'iriik, "the death agony"? She replied, 'What do you imply with that word, al-'iriik?' I knocked against my companion's shoulder and she commented, 'Careful! You hurt your brother!' She went on, 'By al-'iriik do you mean al-ma[l;tf., "menstruation"? Speak what God, Almighty and Glorious is He, said about menstruation! Well, the Messenger of God (SAAS) would put a cloak over me and touch my head. There would be a blanket between myself and him when I was menstruating. 'Whenever the Messenger of God (SAAS) passed by my door, he would speak words with which God would help me. One day he went by without saying anything. Then he passed by again twice or thrice without speaking. I told my servant girl, "Put out a cushion for me at the door." I then bandaged up my head. He passed by and asked, "What is wrong with you, 'A'isha?" "My head hurts," I replied. He commented, "And mine too!" 'Soon after that he was brought back, being borne in a cloth. He entered my quarters and sent a message to the (other) wives saying, "I am ill and unable to make my round to you. Permit me to remain with 'A'isha." I would nurse him, though I had never nursed anyone before. One day, while he was resting on my shoulder, he turned his head towards mine. I thought he wanted something from my head, but a cold discharge came from inside his mouth and fell at the opening of my neck. My skin shivered at it, and I thought he had fainted. So I covered him with a blanket. 'Umar and al-Mughlra arrived and asked to come in; I invited them in, drawing the veil over myself. 'Vmar then looked at him and exclaimed, "What a faint! The Messenger of God is totally unconscious!" They both arose, and when they drew near the door, al-Mughlra said, "'Vmar, the Messenger of God has died." I exclaimed, "You're lying! You must be planning some mischief] The Messenger of God would not die before God had wiped out the hypocrites!"
lessons: Caring for a loved one through illness is one of the most profound acts of love; Aisha's dedication to nursing the Prophet despite never having done so before shows that love gives us strength we did not know we had.
Denial is a natural first response to unbearable news — Aisha's initial refusal to accept the Prophet's death reflects the human heart's instinct to protect itself from grief.
Even the greatest of human beings experienced physical suffering and vulnerability; this reminds us that illness and hardship are not signs of divine abandonment but part of the human journey.
practical_advice: If you are caring for an ill loved one and feel overwhelmed, remember that you are fulfilling one of the most honoured roles a person can have — take it one moment at a time and ask for help when needed.
When faced with sudden devastating news, give yourself permission to feel shock and disbelief; these are normal protective responses and do not mean you lack faith or strength.

During times of caregiving or crisis, maintain small routines and human connections — as Aisha spoke with visitors even during the Prophet's illness — because these anchors help sustain you through prolonged stress.

title: Abu Bakr Arrives and Confirms the Prophet's Death
summary: Abu Bakr rode from al-Sunh, entered Aisha's quarters, uncovered the Prophet's face, kissed his forehead, and confirmed his death with words of deep grief and devotion before addressing the stunned community.
story: Abu Bakr came. I drew aside the veil and he looked at him. Then he said, "To God we belong, and to Him we return! The Messenger of God has died!" He then moved towards his head, lowered his own mouth and kissed his forehead. He then said, "Alas for a prophet!" He raised his head, lowered his mouth and kissed his forehead, saying, "Alas for a man of purity!" Again he raised his head, lowered his mouth and kissed his forehead, saying, "Alas for a bosom friend. The Messenger of God (SAAS) is dead." He left for the mosque while 'Vmar was making an address to the people in which he was saying, "The Messenger of God would not die before God wiped out the hypocrites." Abu Bakr then spoke, praising and thanking God and went on to say, "God states, 'You (will be) dead and they (will be) dead'" (surat al-Zumar; XXXIX, v.30), reciting the verse to its conclusion. He went on to recite, "Mul)ammad is no more than a messenger. And the messengers have passed away before him. If he were to die or be killed, would you turn on your heels? And those who turn on their heels" ... (surat Al 'lmriin; III, v.143). He continued, "To those of you who have been worshipping God, (I say) God is alive and will not die. To those of you who have been worshipping Mul)ammad, (I say) Mul)ammad is dead." 'Umar asked, "And is that in God's Book? I never was aware that that was in God's Book!" He went on, "People, this is Abu Bakr. He is the most prominent of all the Muslims. Pledge allegiance to him!" And they did pledge allegiance to him. Abu Da'ud and al-Tirmidhi narrated it in al-Shamil.'il, from a {tadith of Marl;1um b. 'Abd al-'Aziz al-'At\iiT, from Abu qrnran al-]awni, who gave it in part. The {taft:;, al-Bayhaqi stated that Abu 'Abd Allah al-Bafi?, quoting Abu Bakr b. Isl;1aq, quoting Al;1mad b. Ibrarum b. Mall;1an. quoting YaI;1ya b. Bukayr, quoting al-Layth, from 'Aqil, from Ibn Shihab, quoting Abu Salama, from 'Abd al-Ral;1man, who stated that 'A'isha told him that Abu Bakr rode in on his horse from his house in al-Sunl;1. He dismounted and entered the mosque, but did not speak to the people before he went in to see 'A'isha. He went over to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who was shrouded in a striped cloak. He uncovered his face, bent over him, kissed him and wept. He then said, "How I wish I could ransom you with my father and mother! I swear by God, God will not ever lay upon you two deaths, and you have now suffered the death decreed for you."
lessons: Abu Bakr's composed clarity in the midst of his own grief — kissing the Prophet's forehead while weeping, then immediately addressing the community — shows that true leadership means channelling personal pain into service of others.
His reminder that 'God is alive and will not die' teaches us that while we may lose those we love most, our relationship with God is eternal and cannot be taken from us.
Returning to scripture in moments of crisis, as Abu Bakr did by reciting the Quranic verse, is a profound source of grounding and stability when emotions threaten to overwhelm reason.
practical_advice: When you receive devastating news, allow yourself a private moment of grief — as Abu Bakr wept at the Prophet's side — before you must face the world; this is not weakness but necessary humanity.
In times of collective panic or confusion, a calm voice grounded in truth and scripture can restore peace to many — be that steadying presence if you are able.

Distinguish between what is eternal and what is temporal in your attachments; loving people is good, but ensuring that love is ultimately anchored in love of God protects you from despair when those people are gone.

title: Umar's Denial and Abu Bakr's Recitation That Brought the Community to Certainty
summary: Umar ibn al-Khattab initially refused to believe the Prophet had died and threatened those who said so, until Abu Bakr recited a Quranic verse that struck the entire community into silence and acceptance.
story: AI-Zuhri stated that Abu Salama related to him, from Ibn 'Abbas, that Abu Bakr came out while 'Umar was speaking with the people. He said, "Sit down, 'Umar." But 'Umar declined to sit. Again he said, "Sit down, 'Umar!" but again 'Urnar declined to sit. Abu Bakr made the invocation, "I testify that there is no god but God and that Mul;1ammad is the Messenger of God" and the people assembled around him. He then said, "To proceed, to those of you who have been worshipping Mul;1ammad, Mul;1ammad has died. To those of you who have been worshipping God, God will not die. God, Almighty is he, recited, 'Mul;1ammad is no more than a messenger. And the messengers have passed away before him. If he were to die or be killed, would you turn on your heels?'" (surat.iiI'1mran; III, v.143). Ibn 'Abbas went on, "And I swear by God, it was as though the people did not know that God had revealed this verse until Abu Bakr recited it. The people all learned it from him and that was alI you could hear, people reciting it." AI-Zuhri stated, "Sa'id b. al-Musayyab informed me that 'Umar said, 'I swear by God, no sooner did I hear Abu Bakr reciting it than I knew that it was the truth and I was so awe-struck that my legs would not bear me, and I fell to the ground. And I knew, when I heard him recite it, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had died.'" AI-Bukhari narrated it from Yal;1ya b. Bukayr. The {taft:;, al-Bayhaqi narrated through Ibn Labi'a, quoting Abu al-Aswad, from 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr, concerning the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS), as follows, "'Umar b. al-Khanab then got up to address the people, threatening anyone who might say that he died by violence. He was saying, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) would be under a covering if there had been any violence.' 'Amr b. Q!.ys b. Za'ida b. al-A~amm b. Umm Maktum was at the back of the mosque, reciting, 'Mul;1ammad is no more than a messenger. And the messengers have passed away before him' while the people there in the mosque were weeping io confusion, not listening. "'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Munalib then went out to the people and said, 'People, does anyone of you have any agreement from the Messenger of God (SAAS), relating to his death about which he should tell us?' 'No,' they replied. He then asked, ''Vmar, do you have (any such) knowledge?' 'No,' he replied. "AI-'Abbas went on, 'Bear witness, people, that no one is testifying that they have any claim against the Messenger of God (SAAS), due upon his death. I swear by the God than whom there is no other, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) has tasted death.' "Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, then arrived on his camel from al-Sunl;1 and dismounted at the door of the mosque. He came in looking grief-stricken and depressed. He asked permission to enter the apartment of his daughter 'A>isha and she invited him in. He entered. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had died on the mattress, and the women were all around him. They veiled their faces, concealing themselves from Abu Bakr, except in"
lessons: Even the mightiest in faith — like Umar — can be temporarily overwhelmed by grief into denial; this is not a failure of faith but a testament to the depth of human love and the enormity of loss.
Sacred knowledge preserved in scripture can pierce through the deepest confusion and restore clarity in an instant, as one Quranic verse brought an entire community from chaos to calm.
Al-Abbas's call for honest testimony — asking whether anyone had any contrary knowledge — models the importance of grounding collective grief in truth rather than wishful thinking.
practical_advice: When grief or shock causes you to resist an unbearable truth, allow someone you trust to gently guide you back to reality; this is not weakness but the necessary work of communal support.
Keep close to you the verses and words of wisdom that have brought you peace before — in your most disorienting moments, returning to familiar sacred language can act as an anchor.

If you find yourself in a role of leadership during a communal tragedy, prioritise clarity and truth delivered with compassion over emotional speeches, as Abu Bakr's simple recitation did more than Umar's passionate address.

title: Abu Bakr Confirms the Death of the Prophet and Addresses the People
summary: Abu Bakr arrives at the mosque after the Prophet's death, confirms the news, kisses the Prophet farewell, then delivers a powerful address from the minbar reminding the community that Muhammad was a messenger who has passed, and that God alone is eternal.
story: Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, then arrived on his camel from al-Sunl;1 and dismounted at the door of the mosque. He came in looking grief-stricken and depressed. He asked permission to enter the apartment of his daughter 'A>isha and she invited him in. He entered. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had died on the mattress, and the women were all around him. They veiled their faces, concealing themselves from Abu Bakr, except in the case of 'A'isha. He uncovered the Messenger of God (SAAS), and bent over him, kissing him and weeping, saying, 'Whatever Ibn al-Khanab says is nothing; the Messenger of God (SAAS) is dead, by Him who holds my life in His hands! May God have mercy upon you, Messenger of God! How fine you are, alive or dead!' He then covered him with the cloth. Thereafter he hurried into the mosque, passing through the throng of people, and reached the minbar. 'Umar sat down when he saw Abu Bakr approaching. Abu Bakr stood beside the minbar and called out to the people, who sat down and listened. He reiterated the shahada several times, then said, 'God, Almighty and Glorious is He, has announced to His Prophet his own death. Yet He is alive among you and is making announcement to you too, of death. Eventually none of you will remain - (all will die) except God, Almighty and Glorious is He. The Almighty stated, "Muhammad is no more than a messenger. And the messengers have passed away before him."' 'Umar asked, 'And this verse is in the Qur'an? By God, I never knew before today that this verse had been revealed! God Almighty did say to Muhammad, "You (will be) dead and they (will be) dead" (surat al-Zumar; XXXIX, v.30). And He did say, "Every thing perishes except for His face. Judgement is His, and to Him you shall be returned" (surat al-Qasas; XXVIII, v.88). And He said, "Everyone upon it will pass away; and the face of Your Lord, Possessor of glory and honour, shall endure" (surat al-Rahman; LV, v.26, 7). And he also said, "Every soul shall taste death and you will only receive your rewards in full on Judgement Day"' (surat Al-'Imran; III, v.185). He (Abu Bakr) went on, 'God gave life to Muhammad (SAAS), and kept him alive until he had established God's religion, made God's orders plain, delivered His message and fought in His cause. Thereafter God took him to Himself and left you upon the path. And none shall perish except after clear signs and pain. Those whose Lord is God (shall know) that God is alive and will not die. For those who worshipped Muhammad and treated him as a god (should know) that their god has expired. Fear God, people! Hold fast to your religion and put your trust in your Lord. God's religion is established. God's word is complete. God will help those who support Him and who revere His religion. God's Book is among us. It is both the light and the cure. By it God led Muhammad (SAAS). In it it states what God considers halal, "permissible", and what haram, "prohibited". And, by God, we will not care who of God's creation descend upon us. God's swords are still unsheathed; we have not yet put them down. We will fight vigorously against those who oppose us, just as we fought with the Messenger of God (SAAS). Let no one depend upon other than himself.'
lessons: Even the greatest of leaders are mortal; only God endures, and anchoring one's faith in God rather than in any individual protects the heart from despair.
True leadership in crisis means bringing clarity to confusion — Abu Bakr's composed address transformed a community in panic into one of renewed resolve.
The Quran is a living source of comfort and guidance that does not pass away, even when beloved people do; returning to it in times of loss is a profound act of faith.
practical_advice: When you lose someone central to your life, remind yourself that your relationship with God is the one constant that cannot be taken from you.
In moments of collective shock or confusion, seek out or be the voice of calm clarity — grounding others in truth is one of the greatest acts of service.
Revisit verses of the Quran that speak to the reality of mortality and the permanence of God; sitting with them slowly can bring unexpected peace during grief.

title: The Last Sight of the Prophet: His Final Glimpse at the Praying Congregation
summary: During his final illness, the Prophet briefly drew aside the curtain of his apartment and looked out at the Muslims praying behind Abu Bakr, smiling at them in what would be their last sight of him.
story: What happened was that on the day of the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS), Abu Bakr had performed the al-subh prayer with the people. At that time the Messenger of God (SAAS) had briefly recovered from the throes of the illness from which he was suffering; he had drawn aside the curtain to his apartment and had looked out at the Muslims lined up in prayer behind Abu Bakr. This sight delighted him and he smiled at the Muslims, who, in their pleasure at seeing him, were almost distracted from the prayer in which they were engaged. And Abu Bakr had been about to return to the line of worshippers, but the Messenger of God (SAAS) signalled to them to remain where they were and let down the curtain. This was their last experience of him. When Abu Bakr had completed the prayer, he came in to him and said to 'A'isha, 'I see that the illness of the Messenger of God (SAAS) is somewhat alleviated. This is the day assigned to Bint Kharija.' He was referring to one of his two wives, who resided in al-Sunl;1, over towards the east side of Medina. He then mounted a horse he owned and went to his house there. The Messenger of God (SAAS) died in the late morning of that day; some say after the sun had passed the zenith. But God knows best.
lessons: The Prophet's last public act was a smile of love toward his community at prayer — a reminder that connection to God and to one's people sustains the spirit even in the most difficult moments.
Small, final moments of beauty are gifts; they remind us that love and devotion endure right to the very end of a life well-lived.
Continuing in one's duties and worship, even in hardship, is itself an act of profound faith and grace.
practical_advice: When facing illness or exhaustion, look for the small moments of joy available to you — a face, a scene, a memory — and let them remind you of what you love.
Maintain your spiritual practices, like prayer, even in reduced capacity during hard times; they are anchors that keep you grounded.
Cherish ordinary shared moments with loved ones, as they may become the memories that carry the most meaning later.

title: The Companions' Confusion and Abu Bakr's Resolution of the Question of Succession
summary: After the Prophet's death, the Companions fell into confusion and disagreement about leadership, with some among the Ansar proposing their own candidates, until Abu Bakr clarified that the caliphate must come from Quraysh and the community united around him.
story: Upon his death, the Companions fell into disagreement. Some said he had died, some that he had not. Salim b. 'Ubayd went after Abu Bakr in al-Sunl;1 and told him of the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS). The latter left his house when he received the news and went in to the apartment of the Messenger of God (SAAS). He drew back the covering from over his face, kissed him and established that he had died. Then he went out to the people and, from the side of the minbar addressed them. He told them about the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS), as we have related above, and put an end to their confusion and doubt. All the people came back around him, and a number of the Companions pledged their allegiance to him in the mosque. Some of the ansar had doubts and it occurred to certain of them that it would be acceptable to appoint one of the ansar as khalifa, "caliph", "successor". Others of them compromised and suggested that one leader be appointed for the muhajirin and one for the ansar. Eventually Abu Bakr made clear to them that the caliphate could only be from Quraysh. They therefore came back to him and resolved unanimously on him, as we will narrate and elucidate.
lessons: Communities in grief are vulnerable to fragmentation; clear, principled leadership grounded in tradition is essential to preserving unity.
Disagreement in times of crisis is natural and human; what matters is a willingness to return to shared foundations and resolve differences through wisdom rather than conflict.
Every generation faces moments of transition that feel impossible to navigate; trust in the frameworks of guidance that have been established, and move forward together.
practical_advice: When a community or family faces a loss, prioritize unity and communication before making major decisions — grief clouds judgment, and premature divisions can cause lasting harm.
If you find yourself in disagreement with others during a time of crisis, seek counsel from those with knowledge and wisdom rather than acting on emotion alone.
After a significant loss or transition, give yourself and those around you time to grieve before rushing into questions of 'what comes next.'

title: The Hall of Banu Sa'ida: 'Umar's Warning About Disputed Pledges of Allegiance
summary: During the last Hajj of 'Umar ibn al-Khattab, 'Abd al-Rahman ibn 'Awf recounts how 'Umar intended to warn the people publicly about disputed claims to leadership, but was counselled to wait until he returned to Medina to speak among scholars who could properly understand his words.
story:
Imam Ahmad stated that Ishaq b. 'Isa al-Tabba' related to him, quoting Malik b. Anas, quoting Ibn Shihab, from 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Utba b. Mas'ud, who said that Ibn 'Abbas reported to him that 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Awf returned to his baggage. Ibn 'Abbas stated, 'I had been teaching 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Awf to recite and (on his return) he found me waiting for him; that was at Mina, during the last hajja performed by 'Umar b. al-Khattab. 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Awf said, "A man came to 'Umar b. al-Khattab and said, 'So-and-so is saying that if 'Umar were to have died, I would have pledged allegiance to so-and-so.' At this 'Umar said, 'I intend to stand up among the people this evening, if God so wills it, and I will warn them against that band who wish to seize power over them!'"""
'Abd al-Rahman went on, "I told him, 'Commander of the Believers, don't do that; the pilgrimage brings together all kinds of low-class riff-raff and they will be in the majority in your assembly should you rise to address the people. I am afraid you may say something they will report all over the place without understanding or placing in the right context. You should first arrive in Medina, the seat of the Hijra and the home of the sunna, and which only consists of scholars and the leading men. Say (in Medina) whatever you want in security. They will understand and properly interpret your words.'"
"'Umar replied, 'If I reach Medina safely, I certainly will tell the people of it at the first assembly I hold.'"
"After reaching Medina, at the end of Dhu al-Hijja, when it was Friday, I hurried over in (a state of) sakkat al-a'ma. [I, (Ishaq b. 'Isa al-Taba') asked Malik, 'What did the narrator mean by sakkat al-a'ma?' He replied, 'It means approximately when one doesn't care what the time is or how hot or cold it may be.'] 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Awf went on, 'I found Sa'id b. Zayd at the right corner of the minbar, he having preceded me there. I sat down facing him, my knee touching his. 'Umar was there right away. When I saw him, I said, "This evening he is going to speak on this minbar what no one has ever expressed before."""
"Sa'id b. 'Umar disputed this, saying, 'What might he say that no one has said before?'"
"'Umar sat on the minbar and when the muezzin had finished making the call to prayer, he got up and gave appropriate praise to God. Then he said, 'To proceed, I am about to speak what it was decreed for me to say. I do not know whether it will prove to be immediately before my death. Those who listen and understand it should talk about it wherever they may travel. Those who do not understand it I do not permit to tell lies about me.'"
"God sent Muhammad with the truth and revealed the Book to him. One part of what He revealed to him was the verse relating to stoning. We recited it and fully appreciated and understood it. The Messenger of God (SAAS) cast stones, and we did so after him. I fear that eventually people may say, 'We don't find any verse referring to the stoning to be present in God's Book.' And so they will go astray by abandoning an ordinance revealed by God the Almighty and Glorious. The stoning in God's Book is a punishment upon married men and women who commit [adultery]."
lessons: Wise counsel is knowing when and where to speak — the right message delivered in the wrong context can cause more harm than good.
Leaders bear a responsibility to preserve authentic understanding and warn communities against deviation, even when it is difficult or unpopular.
Choosing the right audience and setting for important truths is itself an act of wisdom and care for those you serve.
practical_advice: Before speaking on a sensitive or important matter, consider carefully whether your audience is ready to receive and correctly interpret your words — timing and context matter greatly.
When you feel an urgent need to address a wrong or warn others, pause and consult a trusted adviser before acting; their perspective may help you act more effectively.
Preserve and pass on knowledge carefully, especially when you sense that future generations may lose access to important guidance.

title: Umar's Speech on the Stoning Verse and Warnings Against Deviation
summary: Umar ibn al-Khattab warns the Muslim community about the danger of abandoning the Quranic injunction on stoning for adultery and cautions against excessive praise of the Prophet, emphasizing his role as a servant and messenger of God.
story: Mui)anunad with the truth and revealed the Book to him. One part of what He revealed to him was the verse relating to stoning. We recited it and fully appreciated and understood it. The Messenger of God (SAAS) cast stones, and we did so after him. I fear that eventually people may say, 'We don't find any verse referring to the stoning to be present in God's Book.' And so they will go astray by abandoning an ordinance revealed by God the Almighty and Glorious. The stoning in God's Book is a punishment upon married men and women who commit adultery when proof is present, or there is a pregnancy or a confession.' "Moreover, we used to recite, 'Do not deny your own forebears, for it is unbelief for you to deny your forebears.' And the Messenger of God (SAAS) also said, 'Do not praise me to excess as people would do about Jesus, son of Mary. I am only a servant; say (he is), "The servant and Messenger of God."'
lessons: Preserving the integrity of religious guidance requires courageous voices willing to speak difficult truths, even in times of grief and transition.
Moderation in reverence — even for the most beloved — is itself a form of spiritual discipline and fidelity to God's guidance.
Communities that fail to transmit their foundational principles risk losing them entirely; safeguarding knowledge is an act of devotion.
practical_advice: When you notice a community or group drifting from its core values, speak up respectfully and clearly rather than staying silent out of social discomfort.
Balance your admiration for role models and leaders with a grounded understanding of their human nature, to avoid placing expectations on them that belong only to God.
Regularly revisit foundational texts and teachings in your life so that they remain living guides rather than distant memories.

title: Abu Bakr Arrives, Kisses the Prophet, and Joins the Leadership Council
summary: Abu Bakr arrives from his residence outside Medina upon hearing of the Prophet's death, kisses him farewell, then rushes to the gathering at Banu Sa'ida where he makes a decisive speech that leads to the community's pledge of allegiance.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) died while Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, was in his summer residence outside Medina. He arrived, uncovered his face and kissed him, saying, 'Would that my mother and my father could ransom you! There was none finer than you, alive or dead! Muhammad has died, by the lord of the ka'ba.' He went on to narrate the hadith as above and stated, 'Abu Bakr and 'Umar hurried off and joined them. Abu Bakr spoke, omitting nothing that had been revealed about the ansar or that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had said about them. He stated, "You well know that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'If the people were to take a path along a valley while the ansar followed a different one, I would take that of the ansar.' You well know, Sa'd, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said — while you were seated — 'Quraysh are those entrusted with this authority. Those of the people who are righteous are but followers of the righteous (of Quraysh), while the sinners would do the same.'" Sa'd said to him, "You are right; we are the viziers, you the princes!"'
lessons: Even in the depths of grief, the responsibilities of leadership and community care must be attended to — love and duty are not opposites.
Quoting the words and example of a beloved guide during moments of community uncertainty is a powerful way to anchor people in shared values.
Acknowledging another's merit while also affirming a broader principle — as Abu Bakr did with the Ansar — models the kind of dignified statesmanship that preserves unity.
practical_advice: When you lose someone deeply beloved, allow yourself a moment to grieve fully and personally before taking on the responsibilities that await — both are necessary.
In moments of community disagreement, ground your argument in shared values and respected precedent rather than personal opinion or emotional appeals alone.
Practice acknowledging the contributions and worth of those you may disagree with — doing so disarms defensiveness and opens the door to resolution.

title: Abu Bakr Accepts the Caliphate Out of Fear of Community Unrest
summary: Abu Bakr explains that he accepted the pledge of allegiance not out of personal desire for power but out of sincere concern that refusing it would lead to dangerous unrest and division within the Muslim community.
story: I asked him what had been said when allegiance was pledged to him. He replied, (while telling him what the ansar had been discussing, what he had said to them and what 'Umar b. al-Khattab had said to the ansar) 'and he reminded them of my being appointed their imam by order of the Messenger of God (SAAS), during his illness. And they therefore pledged allegiance to me, and I accepted this from them. I was worried that (otherwise) there would be unrest after which would come secession.' The line of transmission for this is excellent and strong. The implication of this is that Abu Bakr only accepted the appointment as imam due to his concern that there would be unrest worse than if he had refused to accept it. I note that this activity took place during the remainder of that Monday. On the morning of the following day, Tuesday, the people gathered in the mosque where the pledge of allegiance was made by the entire body of the muhajirin and the ansar. This preceded the burial of the Messenger of God (SAAS).
lessons: True leadership is often accepted as a burden and a responsibility rather than sought as a privilege or reward — the most trustworthy leaders are frequently those who do not desire power for its own sake.
Prioritizing community stability and preventing harm can sometimes require accepting a role or responsibility one would not otherwise choose.
The measure of a leader's sincerity lies in the reasoning behind their decisions, not merely the decisions themselves.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether your motivations for seeking roles of influence or responsibility are centered on service to others or personal gain — sincerity in this distinction matters greatly.
When placed in a position of responsibility you did not seek, approach it as a trust and an obligation rather than a right or privilege.
Consider how your decisions — including whether to accept or decline a role — affect the broader community around you, not just your own comfort or preference.

title: Umar's Address from the Minbar the Day After the Prophet's Death
summary: The day after the Prophet's death, Umar delivers an address from the mosque minbar, expressing his wish that the Prophet had outlived them all, while affirming that God has left the community with guidance and that Abu Bakr is most worthy of authority.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Ibrahim b. Musa related to him, quoting Hisham, from Ma'mar, from al-Zuhri, who quoted Anas b. Malik as having said that he heard the final address when 'Umar sat upon the minbar. That was the day following that of the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Abu Bakr was there, not speaking. 'Umar said, 'What I wanted was for the Messenger of God (SAAS), to live on until he dabbarana' — by this word he meant until he was the last one of them alive — 'however, even if he has died, God has placed among you a light by which you may be guided. God led Muhammad (SAAS) aright; Abu Bakr, his Companion and the second of two is the Muslim most fitted to have authority over you. Come forward.'
lessons: Grief does not erase the responsibilities of those who remain; continuing to serve one's community is itself a form of honoring those who have passed.
Even in the most shattering moments of loss, affirming hope and continuity for those who grieve is a profound act of leadership and compassion.
The light of guidance — whether in the form of scripture, sound leadership, or enduring values — remains even after the one who brought it is gone.
practical_advice: When you experience a profound loss, look for the sources of continuity and guidance that remain in your life rather than focusing solely on what has been taken away.
If you are in a position to speak to or lead others in a moment of collective grief, ground your words in hope and shared purpose rather than despair.
Honor those you have lost by continuing the work and values they exemplified, understanding that legacy is kept alive through action.

title: Abu Bakr's Inaugural Address as Caliph
summary: Upon ascending the minbar as the newly pledged Caliph, Abu Bakr delivered a humble and principled inaugural address, outlining his philosophy of just and accountable leadership.
story: Abu Bakr then spoke. He gave appropriate praise and thanks to God, then said, 'To proceed: people, I have assumed authority over you, even though I am not the best of you. If I do well, then help me. If I err, then set me straight. Honesty is loyalty; dishonesty is perfidy. It is the weak among you who are powerful in my view until I can do away with their infirmity. It is the strong among you who are weak until I can draw forth from them the right, if God so wills it. No people abandons the fight for God's cause without God afflicting them with degradation. Depravity never spreads over a people without God making them experience calamity. Obey me for as long as I obey God and His Messenger. If! disobey God and His Messenger, then I should expect no obedience from you. Proceed with your prayer, and may God have mercy upon you!' This has a line of transmission that is $ab/b. His statement, "I have assumed authority over you, even though I am not the best of you" stems from (a sense) of forbearance and modesty. They were united in belief that he was the most virtuous and best of them, may God be pleased with them all.
lessons: True leadership is grounded in humility and accountability, not in pride or the pursuit of power.
A just leader protects the weak and holds the powerful to account, ensuring that strength is never used to oppress.
Obedience to any authority is conditional on that authority's adherence to divine guidance — blind obedience without moral grounding is not a virtue.
practical_advice: When you are placed in any position of responsibility, begin by honestly acknowledging your limitations and inviting constructive feedback from those you serve.
In your own life, examine whether you are protecting those who are vulnerable around you, whether in your family, workplace, or community, rather than only looking out for the powerful.
Regularly evaluate whether the leaders and influencers in your life are themselves adhering to principled and ethical conduct, and feel empowered to respectfully speak up when they are not.

title: The Ansar's Noble Concession and Umar's Response at the Gathering of Banu Sa'ida
summary: At the gathering following the Prophet's death, the Ansar spokesman nobly declared their continued support for whoever succeeded the Prophet from the Muhajirun, and Umar affirmed this and led the pledge of allegiance to Abu Bakr.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) died, the people gathered in the home of Sa'd b. 'Vbada; they included Abu Bakr and 'Vmar. 'The spokesman for the an$ar arose and said, "You should know that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was one of the muhajirin and that his successor will be from the muhajirin. We have been the an$ar, "the Helpers", of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and we will be the an$arofhis caliph, just as we were his an$ar.' "'Vmar b. al-Khanab then arose and said, "Your speaker is correct. Ifyou had said anything other than this, we would not have agreed with you." He then took the hand of Abu Bakr and said, "This is your leader; pledge allegiance to him." 'Vmar pledged allegiance to him, as did the muhajirun and the an$ar."
lessons: Prioritizing the unity of the community over personal or tribal interests is among the highest expressions of faith and selflessness.
Those who have a history of service and sacrifice, as the Ansar did, demonstrate their true character not only in times of ease but in moments of potential loss or transition.
Decisive and clear action from respected leaders at moments of uncertainty can prevent confusion and preserve communal harmony.
practical_advice: When you are in a group situation where there is competition for influence or recognition, consider what response would best serve the collective good rather than your personal standing.
Practice the discipline of supporting principled decisions even when a different outcome might have benefited you personally, trusting that integrity yields better long-term results.
In moments of organizational or communal transition, look for opportunities to affirm and strengthen the bonds between different groups rather than allowing divisions to deepen.

title: Abu Bakr Summons Al-Zubayr and Ali to Pledge Allegiance
summary: After mounting the minbar, Abu Bakr personally summoned both al-Zubayr and Ali ibn Abi Talib, who had been absent from the initial pledge, and both ultimately gave their allegiance, affirming the unity of the Muslims.
story: "Abu Bakr mounted the minbar and, looking out into their faces, he did not see al-Zubayr. And so he summoned al-Zubayr and said, 'I aSk, would you, the nephew of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and his disciple, wish to break asunder the staff of the Muslims?' He replied, 'Let there be no reproof, Caliph of the Messenger of God (SAAS).' And he arose and made his pledge of allegiance to him. "Abu Bakr then looked out into the faces of the people and did not see 'Ali. He then summoned 'Ali b. Abu Talib, and he came. Abu Bakr said, 'I ask, would the nephew of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and his son-in-law, wish to break asunder the staff of the Muslims?' He replied, 'Let the.re be no reproof, Caliph of the Messenger of God (SAAS).' And he pledged to him his allegiance."
lessons: Community unity requires active effort; leaders must be willing to reach out to those who feel marginalized or overlooked rather than ignoring their absence.
Even those with legitimate grievances or personal reservations can choose the path of communal harmony over division.
The framing of a question can open doors that confrontation would close — Abu Bakr appealed to shared values rather than demanding compliance.
practical_advice: When you notice someone withdrawing from a group or situation, consider reaching out to them personally and appealing to your shared values and the greater good rather than waiting for them to come around on their own.

If you are feeling personally hurt or overlooked during a transition, find a way to express your concerns constructively without letting resentment push you into actions that harm the wider community.
When addressing conflict or tension, try reframing the issue around shared goals and collective wellbeing rather than who was right or wrong.

title: Ali and Al-Zubayr Explain Their Delay in Pledging Allegiance
summary: Ali ibn Abi Talib and al-Zubayr clarified that their delay in pledging allegiance to Abu Bakr was due to feeling excluded from the initial consultation, not opposition, and both affirmed their recognition of Abu Bakr's fitness for leadership.
story: 'Ali and al-Zubayr both stated, "The only thing that angered us is that we were too late for the mashura, 'the consultation activity'. We consider that Abu Bakr is the most deserving of the people for power. He is the 'companion of the cave' and we well know his nobility and goodness. And the Messenger of God (SAAS) did, while alive, appoint him to lead the people in prayer."' The line of transmission for this is excellent. And to God be all praise and credit.
lessons: Feeling excluded from important decisions is a natural and valid human emotion, but it need not lead to permanent rupture if one is committed to truth and justice.
Acknowledging the virtues of another, especially when one might have harbored a personal sense of grievance, is a sign of spiritual nobility and honesty.
Transparency about the source of one's hurt, rather than letting it fester silently, opens the door to reconciliation and healing.
practical_advice: If you feel sidelined or excluded from an important decision that affects you, communicate your feelings clearly and honestly rather than expressing them through withdrawal or passive resistance.
Practice the difficult but valuable discipline of separately evaluating whether someone is qualified for a role from whether you were treated fairly in the process that led to their appointment.
When you have resolved an internal conflict about someone or a situation, articulate your resolution clearly to those involved so that misunderstandings do not continue to simmer.

title: Fatima's Grievance Over Inheritance and Her Estrangement from Abu Bakr
summary: Fatima, the Prophet's daughter, felt entitled to inherit from her father's estate, but Abu Bakr informed her of the Prophet's statement that his legacy would be a charitable trust, leading to her resentment and estrangement from Abu Bakr until her death.
story: When it so happened that Fatima, may God be pleased with her, criticized Abu Bakr because she fancied that she had rights to inherit from the Messenger of God (~AAS), this was due to her not having known what Abu Bakr told her of the latter's statement, "What we leave shall not be inherited but will become a ~adaqa, 'a charitable trust'". He therefore blocked her and others of the wives of the Messenger of God (~AAS), as well as his uncle from the inheritance, due to this clear statement as we will explain in its proper place. She then asked him that 'Ali look into the land at Khaybar and Fadak made into a ~adaqa. Abu Bakr did not, however, respond favourably in this because he - that man who was truthful, pious, upright and virtuous - thought it his duty to administer all those matters entrusted to him by the Messenger of God (~AAS). She, a very human woman with no aspirations for infallibility, felt resentful and angry and never again spoke to Abu Bakr before she died. 'Ali was obliged to respect her feelings somewhat. When she died, some six months following the death of her father (SAAS), 'Ali thought it best to renew his pledge of allegiance to Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, in addition to the pledge he had made earlier, prior to the burial of the Messenger of God (SAAS).
lessons: Even the most righteous and beloved individuals can experience genuine grief, misunderstanding, and feelings of injustice — being human means being vulnerable to these emotions.
A leader who acts on principle, even when it causes pain to those he loves and respects, demonstrates a rare and difficult form of integrity.
Grief and loss can distort our perception of fairness; what feels like injustice may sometimes be rooted in incomplete information rather than malice.
practical_advice: When you feel you have been treated unjustly, take time to fully understand the reasoning and evidence on the other side before allowing resentment to become permanent estrangement.
If you are in a position of authority and must make an unpopular decision based on principle, be compassionate in how you communicate it while remaining firm in your adherence to what is right.
Recognize that prolonged estrangement from people you care about carries its own spiritual and emotional cost; seek reconciliation wherever possible without compromising your integrity.

title: Umar's Reflection on Succession When He Was Stabbed
summary: When asked about appointing a successor after being fatally stabbed, Umar reflected on the precedents set by Abu Bakr and the Prophet, and left the matter open, demonstrating deep wisdom about the nature of leadership succession.
story: It is similarly established in both yadith collections from a yadith of Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, from Ibn 'Umar, that when 'Umar was stabbed, he was asked, "Will you not appoint a successor, Commander of the Believers?" He replied, "If I were to appoint a successor, someone better than myself did so" - meaning Abu Bakr - "and if I do not, someone else better than myself did not" - meaning the Messenger of God (SAAS). Ibn 'Umar went on, "I knew, when he mentioned the Messenger of God (SAAS), that he was not going to appoint a successor."
lessons: Wisdom in leadership includes knowing the limits of one's authority and trusting the community to find its way forward through principle rather than personal decree.
Humility before God means acknowledging that even our best decisions are made in the shadow of those wiser and more guided than ourselves.
Facing death with composure and clarity of mind, as Umar did, reflects a life spent in preparation for accountability before God.
practical_advice: In your own decisions, practice the humility of learning from those who came before you, studying how they handled similar challenges rather than assuming you must invent every answer yourself.
When you face a difficult decision with no clear right answer, reflect on the principles held by the people you most admire and ask what they would have done in your position.
Prepare for significant life transitions — whether in your career, family, or community — with enough forethought that those who come after you are not left in confusion or conflict.

title: Umar's Decision Not to Appoint a Successor
summary: When Umar was stabbed and asked whether he would appoint a successor, he declined, noting that both the Prophet and Abu Bakr had set precedents on either side of the question.
story: similarly established in both yadith collections from a yadith of Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, from Ibn 'Umar, that when 'Umar was stabbed, he was asked, "Will you not appoint a successor, Commander of the Believers?" He replied, "If I were to appoint a successor, someone better than myself did so" - meaning Abu Bakr - "and if I do not, someone else better than myself did not" - meaning the Messenger of God (SAAS). Ibn 'Umar went on, "I knew, when he mentioned the Messenger of God (SAAS), that he was not going to appoint a successor."
lessons: True leadership sometimes means accepting uncertainty rather than forcing premature decisions.
Wise leaders look to the example of those who came before them when facing difficult choices.
Humility in acknowledging the limits of one's own authority is a mark of sincere faith.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult decision, reflect on the precedents set by those you respect before acting impulsively.
Accept that some outcomes must be left in God's hands rather than controlled by human planning.
In moments of personal crisis, focus on what you know to be right rather than what is merely expected of you.

title: Ali's Account of the Succession After the Prophet
summary: Ali ibn Abi Talib addressed the people at the Battle of Basra, recounting how the early Muslim community navigated leadership after the Prophet's death through Abu Bakr and Umar, followed by trials and disorder.
story: Sufyan al-Thawrj stated that 'Amr b. Q:1ys quoted 'Amr b. SufYan as having said, "When 'Ali took command over the people, he said, 'People, the Messenger of God (~AAS) left us no instructions regarding power. We considered it appropriate to appoint Abu Bakr as his successor. And he took various action, and so it went until he passed away. Then Abu Bakr decided to appoint 'Vmar as his successor. And he took various actions and so it went till he passed away' - or he said, 'until his authority was formly established'. And so on." Imam Al;imad stated that Abu Nu'aym related to him, quoting Shurayk, from al-Aswad b. Q:1ys, from 'Amr b. Sufyan, who said, "A man made an address at the battIe ofBasra at which 'Ali was victorious and 'Ali spoke, saying, '(Regarding) this uneven speaker, the Messenger of God (~AAS) came first, Abu Bakr came second and 'Vmar came third. After them we were rocked by disorders in which God performed His will.'"
lessons: Community leadership after a great loss requires humility, consultation, and willingness to act for the greater good.
Even the most devoted communities face internal trials and disorder; this is part of God's plan.
Acknowledging difficulty and disorder openly is more honest and spiritually sound than pretending things are perfect.
practical_advice: When your community or family faces a leadership transition, prioritize collective well-being over personal ambition.
During periods of confusion and disorder, hold firm to what you know is true and right, even if the path forward is unclear.
Trust that God's will works even through human imperfection and communal conflict.

title: Ali's Statement on Appointing a Successor and Trusting God
summary: When asked whether he would appoint a successor, Ali replied that the Prophet did not appoint one, and expressed trust that if God wills good for the people, He will unite them behind the best person after him.
story: 'All b. Abu Talib was asked, 'Will you not appoint a successor over us?' He replied, 'The Messenger of God (~AAS) did not appoint a successor, so should I appoint one? If God wishes the people well, He will unite them, after me, behind the best man among them, as He united them behind the best man among them after their prophet.'
lessons: Trusting God with outcomes beyond our control is a form of profound spiritual maturity.
A leader who truly loves their community can release the need to control what comes after them.
The belief that God will guide a community to what is best for them is a source of deep peace amid uncertainty.
practical_advice: Practice releasing control over outcomes you cannot determine, trusting that God guides those who are sincere.
When you are anxious about what comes after a major transition in your life, remind yourself of God's promise to care for those who are righteous.
Focus on doing your best in the present moment rather than trying to engineer every detail of the future.

title: Abbas Recognizes the Signs of the Prophet's Approaching Death
summary: Abbas, recognizing the signs of approaching death in the Prophet's face, urged Ali to ask the Prophet about who would inherit leadership, but Ali refused, fearing such a question would close off all possibilities.
story: We have quoted hereabove al-Bukharl's account of the {tadith of al-Zuhrj, from 'Abd Allah b. Ka'b b. Malik, from Ibn 'Abbas, to the effect that when 'Abbas and 'All came away from the Messenger of God (~AAS), a man asked them, "How is the Messenger of God (~AAS) this morning?" 'All responded, "Thanks be to God, he is recovered." AI-'Abbas then said, "Three days from now, I swear by God, you will be 'slave of the staff. I well know how members of Banu Hashim look when near death. And I see death in the face ofthe Messenger of God (~AAS). Let us go to him and ask him to whom rule will go. If to us, then we will know it; if to someone other than us, we can tell him and he (the Prophet (~AAS)) can tell him to care for us." 'All said, "I'll not ask him that, I swear by God. If he refused it to us, the people would never give it to us after him."
lessons: Different people respond to grief and impending loss in different ways; both Abbas's pragmatism and Ali's restraint were expressions of love and concern.
The fear of difficult answers can sometimes paralyze us even when those answers might bring clarity.
In the face of great loss, holding one's tongue can be an act of honor and deep trust.
practical_advice: When facing an imminent and painful reality, do not suppress your grief, but channel it into prayer and preparation rather than panic.
Recognize that the people around you may grieve and cope differently; offer them patience rather than judgment.
Sometimes the most courageous thing is to not ask a question whose answer might break you, and to trust instead in God's arrangement.

title: Aisha's Testimony: The Prophet Made No Testamentary Appointment
summary: Aisha, who was with the Prophet at the moment of his death, testified that he made no testamentary statement appointing Ali or anyone else as his successor, as he died gently while resting against her.
story: In both ,a{li{l collections it is stated from a {ladith of'Abd Allah b. 'Awn, from IbrahIm aI-Taymi, that aI-Aswad is quoted as having said, "Someone told 'A'isha that people were saying that the Messenger of God (SAAS) made a testamentary statement appointing 'Ali as his successor. She asked, 'Regarding what? He did ask for a bowl in which to urinate while I was resting him against my chest, and he then turned aside and died, without my sensing it. How is it they say he made a testamentary statement appointing 'Ali as his successor?'"
