lessons: Bearing witness to truth, even painful truth, is a sacred responsibility.
The closest witnesses to a moment carry the most reliable testimony; we should be cautious about accepting accounts that contradict direct experience.
The death of a beloved person, even when peaceful, can leave a profound sense of disbelief and sorrow in those present.
practical_advice: When you hear claims that conflict with what you know to be true, speak with clarity and calm rather than anger.
Honor the memory of those you have lost by preserving the truth of what you witnessed, even when others tell different stories.
In moments of grief, hold tightly to your direct experience of the loved one's last moments; these memories are a trust.

title: Ibn Abi Awfa's Answer: The Prophet Left the Quran as His Testament
summary: When asked whether the Prophet left a testamentary statement, Ibn Abi Awfa confirmed he did not leave a specific instruction about succession but rather commended the community to the Book of God.
story: In both ,a{li{l collections there is a {ladith of Malik b. MighwaI which quotes TaIl;1a b. Mu~rif as saying, "I asked 'Abd Allah b. Abu Awfa whether the Messenger of God (SAAS) had made a testamentary statement. He replied, 'No.' I asked, 'So he made us no testamentary statement?' He replied, 'He commended to us the Book of God, Almighty and Glorious is He.'"
lessons: The Quran itself is the ultimate guidance for the Muslim community, sufficient and complete.
Sometimes the most profound testament a person leaves is not instructions about power, but a pointing toward enduring principles.
Simplicity and clarity in one's final message to those left behind is a form of mercy.
practical_advice: Return to the Quran whenever you feel lost or in need of direction; it was the Prophet's final gift to his community.
When you face uncertainty after a loss, seek guidance in the timeless principles left behind rather than in speculation about what was never said.
Let the sacred texts and principles you believe in serve as your compass, especially when human guidance is absent.

title: Ali's Address Refuting Claims of a Written Testament Appointing Him
summary: Ali ibn Abi Talib publicly addressed the Muslim community, asserting that the only written legacy the Prophet left was the Quran and a sheet dealing with specific legal matters, implicitly rejecting claims of a separate political testament.
story: It is also stated in both ,abi{l collections, from a {ladith of ai-A'mash, from Ibrahim al-Taymi, who quotes his father as having said, "'Ali b. Abu Talib, may God be pleased with him, made an address to us in which he said, 'Whoever claims that we have anything to read that is not in God's Book and on this sheet' - referring to a sheet suspended on his sword that contained some camel's teeth and items taken from wounds - 'is telling lies!'" Concerning it (the sheet) he said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated (in it), 'Medina is sacrosanct, from Mt. 'Ayr to Mt. Thawr. Whoever commits a sin inside it or provides sanctuary for someone who has sinned shaIl have upon him the curses of God, the angels and the people combined. God will not accept from him on Judgement Day any repentance or compensation. Anyone claiming descent from someone not his father or who claims connection to those not his protectors shall have upon him the curses of God, the angels and the people combined. God will not accept from him on Judgement Day any repentance or compensation. Inviolability ofMuslims, even those ofthe lowest ranks, is all one; any person who violates his pact with a Muslim shall be cursed by God, the angels and the people combined. God will not accept from him on Judgement Day any repentance or compensation.'"
lessons: A person of integrity is willing to publicly correct false narratives even when those narratives might personally benefit them.
The sanctity of truth in Islam extends to correcting fabrications about the Prophet and his Companions, no matter how convenient those fabrications might seem.
The Prophet's legacy was one of clear, documented principles rather than secret or hidden instructions.
practical_advice: Speak truth even when a lie might seem to serve you; your integrity is more valuable than any short-term advantage.
When false stories circulate about you or those you love, address them with calm evidence rather than emotion.
Anchor your decisions in clear, established principles rather than in rumor or speculation.

title: The Prophet's Farewell Words and Instructions for His Burial
summary: As the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) lay gravely ill, he gathered his companions and delivered his final counsel, addressing questions about his washing, shrouding, funeral prayer, and burial, while sending greetings to all Muslims until the Day of Judgement.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) became seriously ill, we gathered in 'A'isha's home. As the Messenger of God (SAAS) looked over at us, his eyes welled with tears. He then told us, 'The time for the parting is near.' And he went on to tell us of his impending death. He then said, 'Welcome to you all; may God give you long life. May He give you guidance, help, benefit, success, instruction, protection, support and acceptance. I commend you to fear of God; I commend you to God and ask Him to take charge of you. I am to you all a clear harbinger, warning you not to go against God by being haughty towards His worshippers and His land. God said to me and to you, "That abode, the Hereafter, We assign to those who do not seek self-aggrandisement on earth or iniquity; reward shall come to the pious" (surat al-Qasas; XXVIII, v.83). He also stated, "Is there not in hell an abode for the conceited?" (surat al-Zumar; XXXIX, v.60). We asked him, 'When will your death come, Messenger of God?' He replied, 'It is near; as is the return to God, to the al-sidrat al-muntaha, to the most bountiful cup and the highest furnishings.' We asked, 'Who is it will wash you (after death), Messenger of God?' He replied, 'The very closest menfolk of my household, along with many angels who see you from where you do not see them.' We asked, 'In what should we shroud you, Messenger of God?' He replied, 'In these clothes of mine, if you wish, or in a Yemeni cloth or a white one from Egypt.' We asked, 'Who will pray over you, Messenger of God?' He wept and we did so too. He said, 'Easy! May God forgive you and reward you well on behalf of your Prophet! When you have washed me, embalmed me, wound me in a shroud and placed me at the edge of my grave, then depart from me for a while. The first person to pray for me shall be my two Companions and friends, Gabriel and Mika'il, then Israfil, then the angel of death, along with troops of angels, upon whom be peace. Let the men of my own household begin prayers for me; then the women, then let others come in groups and as individuals. Do not harm me by weeping, lamenting or screaming. Give my greetings to those of my Companions who are absent. I ask you to bear witness that I send greetings to all who have entered into Islam and to those who have followed me in this faith of mine, from this day and on to the Day of Judgement.' We said, 'Who will place you in your grave, Messenger of God?' He replied, 'The very closest of the men of my household, along with many angels who see you from where you do not see them.'
lessons: The Prophet's final concern was for the spiritual wellbeing of his community, reminding us that true leadership is rooted in selfless love and guidance even in one's most vulnerable moments.
Humility before God and His creation is a defining quality of the believer; the Prophet explicitly warned against arrogance and self-aggrandisement as among the most serious of spiritual dangers.

Death is a reality that should be faced with preparation, dignity, and trust in God — the Prophet modelled this by answering questions about his own burial calmly and with full acceptance of God's decree.
practical_advice: When facing the end of a relationship, a role, or a chapter of life, take time to offer your loved ones your sincerest counsel and blessings, as the Prophet did — this transforms grief into a legacy of love.

Reflect regularly on the warning against arrogance: examine whether pride in status, wealth, or ability is creating distance between you and others or between you and God, and actively work to soften it.
Prepare for your own death in practical and spiritual terms — not as a morbid act, but as the Prophet demonstrated, as an act of care for those who will remain and as a sign of trust in God's mercy.

title: The Day of the Prophet's Death: A Monday of Historic Significance
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) died on a Monday, a day that held remarkable significance throughout his life — his birth, prophethood, migration, arrival in Madinah, and death all occurred on Mondays — and his companions recorded this with profound grief and reverence.
story: There is no disagreement over the Messenger of God (SAAS) having died on a Monday. Ibn 'Abbas stated, 'Your Prophet (SAAS) was born, became a prophet, left Mecca as a muhajir, entered Medina and died, all on Mondays.' Imam Ahmad and al-Bayhaqi both narrated this. Sufyan al-Thawri stated, from Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, who quoted 'A'isha as having said, 'Abu Bakr asked me, "On what day did the Messenger of God (SAAS) die?" I answered, "On Monday." "I hope I die on that day," he commented. And he did so.' Al-Bayhaqi narrated this from a hadith of al-Thawri. Imam Ahmad stated that Aswad b. 'Amir narrated to him, quoting Huraym, quoting Ibn Ishaq, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. al-Qasim, from his father, who quoted 'A'isha as having said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) died on Monday; he was buried on the night of the Wednesday.' Ahmad is alone in giving this. 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr stated in his work on the military campaigns, as did Musa b. 'Uqba from Ibn Shihab (who said), 'When the pain of the Messenger of God (SAAS) became severe, 'A'isha sent a message to Abu Bakr, and she dispatched Hafsa to 'Umar and Fatima to 'Ali. However, they did not gather together until the Messenger of God (SAAS) had died, while resting on 'A'isha's chest and on the day scheduled for his visit to her. This was on Monday, as the sun passed its zenith, early in the month of Rabi' al-Awwal.' Abu Ya'la stated that Abu Khaythama related to him, quoting Ibn 'Uyayna, from al-Zuhri, from Anas, who said, 'The last look I had at the Messenger of God (SAAS) was on the Monday. He withdrew the screen; there were people behind the screen and others behind Abu Bakr. His face appeared to me like a sheet of a document. The people wanted to move away, but he gestured to them to stay. He then drew the screen across. He died late in that day.'
lessons: The remarkable pattern of the Prophet's life milestones all falling on Mondays is a reminder that God's hand is present in the unfolding of history, weaving meaning and symmetry into the lives of those devoted to Him.

Abu Bakr's wish to die on the same day as the Prophet — and his being granted that wish — illustrates how deeply the love of a righteous companion can align one's life and death with those they admire.
Grief at the loss of someone beloved is natural and human; even the greatest companions wept and were shaken, reminding us that mourning is not weakness but the honest expression of love.
practical_advice: When you lose someone dear to you, try to find and hold onto meaningful details or patterns in their life that remind you of God's care and intentionality — these can be sources of comfort in grief.
As Abu Bakr did, identify qualities in those you admire and let those qualities inspire how you wish to live — and even how you wish to face your own end.
Allow yourself to grieve fully and without shame; suppressing sorrow does not honour those we have lost — expressing it honestly, as the companions did, is a healthy and human response to loss.

title: Scholarly Debate on the Exact Date of the Prophet's Death
summary: Islamic scholars debate the precise date of the Prophet Muhammad's death, with various narrations pointing to different days in Rabi' al-Awwal, and Al-Suhayli presenting a calendrical argument against the 12th being a Monday.
story: Sa'd b. Ibrahim al-Zuhri stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) died on Monday, two days after the beginning of Rabi' al-Awwal, at the end of ten years after his arrival in Medina." Ibn 'Asakir narrated this. Al-Waqidi narrated it from Abu Ma'shar, from Muhammad b. Qays, exactly as above. Khalifa b. Khayyat also stated this. Abu Nu'aym al-Fadl b. Dukayn stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) died on Monday at the beginning of Rabi' al-Awwal in 11 AH, the year of his arrival in Medina. Ibn 'Asakir narrated it also. It was narrated above from 'Urwa and Musa b. 'Uqba and al-Zuhri also, as we quoted from the works of the military expeditions. But God knows best. What is most widely known is the statements made by Ibn Ishaq and al-Waqidi. Al-Waqidi narrated it from Ibn 'Abbas and 'A'isha, may God be pleased with them both. He stated that Ibrahim b. Yazid related to him, from Ibn Tawus, from his father, from Ibn 'Abbas, and Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah related to him from al-Zuhri, from 'Urwa, from 'A'isha. Both accounts stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) died on Monday, twelve days following the beginning of Rabi' al-Awwal. Ibn Ishaq narrated it from 'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr b. Hazm, from his father in similar words; he added, "and he was buried on the night of Wednesday". Sayf b. 'Umar narrated, from Muhammad b. 'Ubayd Allah al-'Arzami, from al-Hakam, from Miqsam, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had completed his 'farewell pilgrimage', he travelled back to Medina, where he remained for the rest of Dhu al-Hijja, al-Muharram and Safar. He died on a Monday, ten days after the beginning of Rabi' al-Awwal." It is also narrated from Muhammad b. Ishaq, from al-Zuhri, from 'Urwa. In a hadith of Fatima from 'Urwa, from 'A'isha, the statement is similar, except that Ibn 'Abbas initially stated, "some days having passed since its beginning". And 'A'isha's wording given was, "after some days of it had passed". Abu al-Qasim al-Suhayli stated in his work al-Rawda, words to the effect that it is impossible to conceive of him (the Prophet (SAAS)) having died on Monday, the 12th of Rabi' al-Awwal of 11 AH. That is because the Messenger of God (SAAS) was present at one of the stations on the hijjat al-wada' of 10 AH on a Friday. The first day of Dhu al-Hijja was a Thursday. On the basis of calculating the months thereafter either as full in length or shortened, or any combination of these, it is unimaginable that the 12th of Rabi' al-Awwal could have been a Monday. This conclusion, made in these words, has become widely known. A number of scholars have attempted to refute this. It can only, however, be refuted in one way, by there having been a difference in the moon's rising. This view required the people of Mecca to have seen the new moon of Dhu al-Hijja on the night of the Thursday, while the people of Medina did not see it until the night of the Friday. This is supported by the statement of 'A'isha, and others as well, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) left Medina on the hijjat al-wada' five days prior to the end of Dhu al-Qa'da. It is clearly established, as we noted above, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) set forth (on the pilgrimage) on Saturday, not on Thursday as Ibn Hazm asserted, for he remained (away) for more than five days, without doubt. It is not possible that he left on the Friday, because Anas stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the al-zuhr prayer of four raka'at in Medina and the al-'asr prayer of two raka'at at Dhu al-Hulayfа. It is well established that he left (there) on Saturday, five days before the end of that month." In light of this, the people of Medina saw the new moon of Dhu al-Hijja on the eve of Friday. If that was on the first day of Dhu al-Hijja, a Friday, with the people of Medina and the months thereafter are calculated in full, then the first day of Rabi' al-Awwal would be a Thursday, and the twelfth day would be a Monday. But God knows best.
lessons: Scholarly disagreement does not undermine truth; it reflects the rigor of Islamic scholarship in preserving authentic knowledge.
Uncertainty about minor historical details does not diminish the profound significance of the events themselves.
Seeking precision and truth, even in matters of grief, is a form of devotion and respect for the Prophet (SAAS).
practical_advice: When faced with conflicting information, remain calm and trust that scholars dedicating their lives to truth-seeking are a mercy for the community.
Accept that not every question has a definitive human answer, and find peace in saying 'God knows best' when uncertainty remains.
Channel confusion into curiosity by studying Islamic history methodically, which can deepen your faith and bring clarity over time.

title: The Physical Description and Age of the Prophet at His Death
summary: Multiple companions and narrators describe the Prophet Muhammad's age at death as sixty-three, noting he had very few white hairs and was the strongest, finest, and most handsome of men.
story: It is established in both Sahih collections from a hadith of Malik, from Rabi'a b. Abu 'Abd al-Rahman, from Anas b. Malik, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was neither very tall, nor short. He was neither pure white nor deep brown. His hair was neither curly nor straight. God, the Almighty and Glorious, gave him His mission when he was forty years old. He resided in Mecca ten (more) years and then for ten years in Medina. God took him to Himself early in his sixtieth year. At that time he did not have twenty white hairs in his head and beard." Ibn Wahb narrated it thus, from 'Urwa, from al-Zuhri, from Anas, and from Qurra b. Rabi'a, from Anas, similarly to the above. The hafiz Ibn 'Asakir stated, "The hadith of Qurra from al-Zuhri is gharib. However, the account of Rabi'a from Anas is narrated thus by a group (of scholars)." He then gave it with a line of transmission through Sulayman b. Bilal, from Yahya b. Sa'id and Rabi'a from Anas, (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS), was sixty-three years old when he died. He then gave it through Malik; al-Awza'i, Mis'ar, Ibrahim b. Tahman, 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar, Sulayman b. Bilal, Anas b. Bilal, Anas b. 'Ayad, al-Darawardi and Muhammad b. Qays al-Madani all gave it from Rabi'a, from Anas, who stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) died when he was sixty years of age." Al-Bayhaqi stated that Abu al-Husayn b. Bishran reported to him, quoting Abu 'Amr b. al-Sammak, quoting Hanbal b. Ishaq, quoting Abu Ma'mar 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr, quoting 'Abd al-Warith, quoting Abu Ghalib al-Bahili, who said, "I asked Anas b. Malik, 'How old was the Messenger of God (SAAS), when he received his mission?' He replied, 'He was forty years of age.' 'What happened then?' I asked. He replied, 'He was in Mecca for ten years and then ten in Medina. He had reached sixty by the time he was taken by God, the Almighty and Glorious. He was the strongest, finest, most handsome and well-built of men.'"
lessons: The Prophet's life was a complete and purposeful journey — every decade marked by dedication to God's mission — reminding us that our own lives have divine purpose.
True beauty is not diminished by age; the Prophet's vigor and grace at sixty-three teach us to honor the elderly.
The preservation of even the smallest physical details about the Prophet reflects the deep love his companions had for him, showing us how love motivates careful remembrance.
practical_advice: Reflect on the different seasons of your own life and identify the divine gifts and purpose God has placed in each phase.
When grieving a loss, remember the beauty and completeness of the life lived rather than focusing solely on its ending.
Cultivate love for the Prophet (SAAS) by learning about his character and appearance, as this connection can be a source of comfort and spiritual grounding during difficult times.

title: The Prophet's Age at Death: Sixty-Three Years — Consensus of the Companions
summary: Numerous companions including 'A'isha, Ibn 'Abbas, Anas, Mu'awiya, and Jarir b. 'Abd Allah all confirm that the Prophet Muhammad died at the age of sixty-three, a view affirmed by Imam Ahmad as the most clearly established.
story: Muslim narrated from Abu Ghassan Muhammad b. 'Amr al-Razi, known as Rubayh, from Hakkam b. Salm, from 'Uthman b. Za'ida, from al-Zubayr b. 'Adl, from Anas b. Malik, who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) died when he was sixty-three; Abu Bakr died when he was sixty-three, and 'Umar similarly, when he too was sixty-three." It is established in both Sahih collections from a hadith of al-Layth b. Sa'd, from 'Aqil, from al-Zuhri, who quoted 'A'isha as having said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) died at age sixty-three." Al-Zuhri stated that it was reported to him similarly by Sa'id b. al-Musayyab. Musa b. 'Uqba, 'Uqayl, Yunus b. Yazid and Ibn Jurayj narrated it from al-Zuhri, from 'Urwa, from 'A'isha. She said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) died when he was sixty-three." Al-Zuhri stated, "And Sa'id b. al-Musayyab told it to me similarly." Al-Bukhari stated that Abu Nu'aym narrated to him, quoting Shayban, from Yahya b. Abu Kathir, from Abu Salama, from 'A'isha, and Ibn 'Abbas, who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) remained in Mecca for ten years receiving revelation of the Qur'an, and also ten years in Medina. Muslim did not include it. Abu Da'ud al-Tayalisi stated in his musnad collection that Shu'ba related to him, from Abu Ishaq, from 'Amir b. Sa'd, from Jarir b. 'Abd Allah, from Mu'awiya b. Abu Sufyan, who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) died when he was sixty-three years old. Abu Bakr also died at age sixty-three, as did 'Umar." Muslim narrated it thus from a hadith of Ghundar, from Shu'ba. It is one of the ahadith that he alone gives, one not included by al-Bukhari. Some give its line of transmission from 'Amir b. Sa'd, from Mu'awiya. The correct line is as we have given it, from 'Amir b. Sa'd, from Jarir, from Mu'awiya. We also narrated it through 'Amir b. Sharahil, from al-Sha'bi, from Jarir b. 'Abd Allah al-Bajali, from Mu'awiya. The hafiz Ibn 'Asakir gave it through al-Qadi Abu Yusuf, from Yahya b. Sa'id al-Ansari, from Anas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) died at age sixty-three. Abu Bakr died when he was sixty-three, as did 'Umar." Ibn Labi'a stated, from Abu al-Aswad, from 'Urwa, from 'A'isha, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) and Abu Bakr made mention of their ages at my home. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was older than Abu Bakr. The Messenger of God (SAAS) died at the age of sixty-three; Abu Bakr died after him, also at the age of sixty-three." Al-Thawri stated that al-A'mash quoted al-Qasim b. 'Abd al-Rahman as having said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS), along with Abu Bakr and 'Umar, died at the age of sixty-three." Hanbal stated that Imam Ahmad related to him quoting Yahya b. Sa'id, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyab, who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) (first) received revelation at the age of forty-three. He stayed ten more years in Mecca and lived on in Medina for ten." Ahmad stated that Hushaym related to him, quoting Da'ud b. Abu Hind, from al-Sha'bi, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) became a prophet at age forty; he stayed there for three years, then Gabriel was sent to him with his mission. Thereafter he remained there for ten years, then went into exile to Medina. He died at age sixty-three." Imam Abu 'Abd Allah Ahmad b. Hanbal stated, "What is clearly established in our view is (that he died) at age sixty-three." In both Sahih collections it is stated from a hadith of Rawh b. 'Ubada, from Zakariyya' b. Ishaq, from 'Amr b. Dinar, from Ibn 'Abbas (who stated) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) remained in Mecca thirteen years and died when he was sixty-three years of age. In the Sahih collection of al-Bukhari it is stated, from a hadith of Rawh b. 'Ubada also, from Hisham, from 'Ikrama, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) received his mission at the age of forty. He remained in Mecca for thirteen years and then was ordered to go into exile; he did so for ten years. Then he died at the age of sixty-three." Muslim narrated it from a hadith of Hammad b. Salama, from Abu Jamra, from Ibn 'Abbas (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) remained in Mecca receiving revelation for thirteen years. He lived on for ten years in Medina and died at age sixty-three.
lessons: The Prophet's life of sixty-three years encompassed a complete mission — from receiving revelation to transforming an entire civilization — reminding us that quality of purpose matters more than length of years.
The remarkable fact that Abu Bakr and 'Umar also died at sixty-three reflects divine wisdom and the unity of the righteous, teaching us to find meaning in life's apparent coincidences.
The careful preservation of this historical knowledge by so many companions demonstrates the value of community in safeguarding what is precious and true.
practical_advice: When confronting your own mortality or the loss of loved ones, reflect on the completeness and purpose of the lives lived rather than dwelling on what was left undone.
Take stock of your own life's mission periodically — are you spending your years in ways aligned with your deepest values and beliefs?
Draw strength from knowing that the greatest human beings faced death at ordinary ages, reminding us that a meaningful life is not measured by its length.

title: Preparing the Prophet's Body for Washing and the Miraculous Voice
summary: After the Prophet's death, his Companions hesitated over how to wash his body, and a mysterious voice instructed them to wash him with his shirt on, which they did with great reverence.
story: We have stated above that the Companions, may God be pleased with them, were engaged in making their pledge of allegiance' to Abu Bakr for the remainder of the Monday and part of the Tuesday. Having gone through all the preliminary stages of this and brought it to completion, they began to prepare for the burial of the Messenger of God (SAAS), relying in all this upon Abu Bakr in what he directed them. Ibn Isl;1aq stated, "When Abu Bakr had received the pledge of allegiance, the people began preparing, on the Tuesday, for the burial of the Messenger of God (SAAS)." It has been narrated above, from the badtth ofIbn Isl:taq, from 'Abd a1-Ral:tman b. a1-Qisim, from his father, from 'A'isha, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) died on Monday and was buried on Wednesday night. AbU Bakr b. Abu Shayba stated that Abu Mu'awiya related to him, quoting Abu Burda, from 'Alqama b. Yazjd, from Sulayman b. Burayda, from his father, who said, "When they set about washing the Messenger of God (SAAS), a voice cried out to them from inside, saying, 'Do not remove the shirt of the Messenger of God!'" Ibn Maja narrated it from the badtth of Abu Mu'awiya, from Abu Burda - his full name being 'Amr b. Yazjd a1-Tamjmj Kuft. Mul;tammad b. Isl:taq stated that Yal:tya b. 'Abbad b. 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr related to him, from his father, who stated that he heard 'A'isha say, "When they wished to wash the Prophet (SAAS), they said, 'We don't know whether we should remove the clothes from the Messenger of God (SAAS), as we do from our dead, or should we wash him with his clothes on?' "When they differed over this, God cast sleep over them, so that everyone of them soon had his chin on his chest! Then a voice that they could not recognize addressed them from the direction of the house, saying, 'Wash the Messenger of God (SAAS), with his clothes on.' "They got up and went over to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and washed him while he was still in his shirt. They poured water on the shirt and rubbed with the shirt, not their hands. 'A'isha used to say, 'Had I known fIrst what I later learned, only the wives would have washed the Messenger of God (SAAS).'"
lessons: Even in the most overwhelming grief, divine guidance arrives for those who are sincere and uncertain — God does not abandon those who seek to do right.
The reverence shown by the Companions in their uncertainty about washing the Prophet teaches that slowing down and seeking guidance is better than rushing through sacred matters.
Grief does not excuse us from our duties; the Companions, despite their devastating loss, pressed forward with love and care in preparing their beloved Prophet for burial.
practical_advice: When you face a difficult situation and feel paralyzed by uncertainty, pause, seek guidance from trusted sources or through prayer, and trust that clarity will come.
In moments of loss, channel your grief into purposeful action — caring for others, fulfilling obligations, and honoring those you love are powerful antidotes to feeling helpless.
Approach sacred responsibilities with humility; admit what you do not know, consult others, and remain open to guidance from unexpected directions.

title: The Washing of the Prophet by His Family
summary: Ali ibn Abi Talib and other family members washed the Prophet's body with profound tenderness, with Ali noting that the Prophet emitted no unpleasant odor as the dead normally do, saying he was sweet-smelling alive and dead.
story: Imam Al;tmad stated that Ya'qub related to him, quoting his ,futher, from Ibn Isl:taq, quoting I:Iusayn b. 'Abd Allah, from 'Ikrama, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "The people gathered to wash the Messenger of God (SAAS); there were only his own family in the house - his uncle al-'Abbas b. 'Abd a1-Munalib, 'All b. AbU Talib, a1-FaQI b. 'Abbas, Qutham b. al-'Abbas, Usama b. Zayd b. I:Iaritha and Salil:t, freed-man of the Messenger of God (SAAS). "When they gathered to wash him, Aws b. Khawla a1-An~arj, of Banu 'Awf b. al-Khazraj, who had fought at Badr, called out from the back of the crowd to 'All b. Abu Talib, 'All, we plead with you, by God and our fIne experience of the Messenger of God (SAAS)!' 'All replied to him, 'Come inside.' He did so and was present at the washing of the Messenger of God (SAAS); he did not participate at all in washing him. "'Ab rested him (the Prophet (SAAS), who was still dressed in his shirt, upon his own chest, while a1-'Abbas, FaQI and Qutham, along with 'All were turning him over, while Usama b. Zayd and Salil:t, his freed-man, both poured water upon him. As 'All set about washing him, not seeing from the Messenger of God (SAAS), any of what usually is emitted from the dead, he called out, 'By my father and my mother! How sweet-smelling you are, alive or dead!' "When they had finished washing the Messenger of God (SAAS), having done so using water and lote-tree balm, they dried him. Then there was done to him what is normally done to the dead. After that he was wrapped io three cloths, two white and one striped cloak. "AI-'Abbas then summone_d two men. He told them, 'One of you should go to Abu 'Ubayda b. al-Jarra!}..' Abu 'Ubayda would make graves for the Meccans. AI-'Abbas went on, 'The other of you should go to AbU Tali)a b. SaW al-An~ilIi.' Abu Tali)a would make the burial niche!5! for the people of Medina. "Ai-'Abbas then said, as he dispatched the two men, '0 God, choose for your messenger!' "The two men left. However, the one looking for Abu 'Ubayda could not find him; the man seekiog Abu Tali)a did locate him and he came and prepared the niche for the Messenger of God (SAAS)." Al;1mad is alone in giving this. The bafi:;:. Abu Bakr al-Bayhaqi stated that Abu 'Abd Allah al-Bafi~ informed him, quoting Mui)ammad b. Ya'qub, quoting Yai)ya b. Mui)ammad b. Ya1)yi1, quoting !;>amra, quoting 'Abd al-Wa!}.id b. Ziyad, quoting Ma'mar, from al-Zuhri, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyab, who said, "'Ali stated, 'As I washed the Messenger of God (SAAS), I watched for any discharge such as comes from a dead person, but saw nothing. He was sweet-smelling alive and dead (SAAS).'" Abu Da'ud narrated it in his work al-Maras'l. In the account from which he quotes the above, al-Bayhaqi adds the following words from Sa'id b. al-Musayyab: "Four persons took charge ofthe burial of the Messenger of God (SAAS). These were: 'Ali, al-'Abbas, al-Fagl and Salii), the freed-man."
lessons: Love expressed through service in the hardest moments is among the most profound forms of devotion; Ali's tender care of the Prophet's body in grief models how to honor those we love.
Entrusting the most sacred tasks to those closest and most devoted ensures they are done with the care, sincerity, and love they deserve.
Even in death, the Prophet's character shone through in miraculous ways, reminding us that a life of righteousness leaves a legacy that transcends physical death.
practical_advice: When someone you love passes away, find a constructive and meaningful way to serve and honor them rather than being consumed entirely by grief — action can be a form of healing.
Reflect on how you treat your most sacred relationships and responsibilities today; the love Ali showed in this moment grew from years of devoted companionship during life.
Let the Prophet's example remind you that how you live each day — your character, your kindness, your integrity — is the legacy you will leave behind.

title: Ali Washes the Prophet's Body
summary: Ali ibn Abi Talib describes washing the body of the Prophet Muhammad, noting his sweet fragrance and the miraculous ease of the task despite its weight.
story: b. Mui)ammad b. Ya1)yi1, quoting !;>amra, quoting 'Abd al-Wa!}.id b. Ziyad, quoting Ma'mar, from al-Zuhri, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyab, who said, "'Ali stated, 'As I washed the Messenger of God (SAAS), I watched for any discharge such as comes from a dead person, but saw nothing. He was sweet-smelling alive and dead (SAAS).'" Abu Da'ud narrated it in his work al-Maras'l. In the account from which he quotes the above, al-Bayhaqi adds the following words from Sa'id b. al-Musayyab: "Four persons took charge of the burial of the Messenger of God (SAAS). These were: 'Ali, al-'Abbas, al-Fagl and Salii), the freed-man of the Messenger of God (SAAS). They placed him in his niche and erected bricks over it as a marker."
lessons: The Prophet's purity extended beyond his life, reminding us that those who live righteously carry a spiritual nobility even in death.
Even the most painful duties, when performed with love and devotion, become acts of honor rather than grief alone.
The trust placed in Ali to perform this sacred task reflects the importance of being someone others rely on in their most vulnerable moments.
practical_advice: When facing a painful responsibility you cannot avoid, focus on the honor of being entrusted with it rather than only the grief it brings.
In moments of overwhelming sorrow, channel your energy into purposeful action as Ali did, allowing service to carry you through.
Reflect on the legacy of those you have lost — what fragrance, metaphorically speaking, do they leave behind in your life?

title: The Washing of the Prophet with Water from the Well of al-Ghars
summary: The Prophet was washed three times with lote-tree balm using water from the blessed well of al-Ghars at Quba, which the Prophet himself used to drink from, while al-Fadl cried out from the emotional burden of the task.
story: Al-Bayhaqi stated that Mul).ammad b. Musa b. aI-Faql informed us, quoting Abu al-'Abbas aI-A~amm, quoting COsayd b. 'A~im, quoting aI-I:Iusayn b. I:Iaf~, from Sufyan, from 'Abd ai-Malik b. Jurayj, who said he heard Mul).ammad b. 'Ali Abu Ja'far say, "The Prophet (SAAS) was washed three times with lote-tree balm. He was washed while he still wore his shirt. The water came from a well called al-Ghars at Quba' that was the property of Sa'd b. Khaythama. The Messenger of God (SAAS) used to drink from it. 'Ali and al-Faql took charge of washing him; al-Faql cradled him, while aI-'Abbas poured the water. Al-Faql began crying, 'Relieve me! You're cutting my heart artery! Something is making me feel faint!'" Al-Waqidi stated that 'A~im b. 'Abd Allah al-I:Iakami related to him, from COmar b. 'Abd al-I:Iakam, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Yes; that well, the well called Gharas is one of the water-sources of heaven. Its water is the very sweetest.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) would have its sweet water drawn for him, and he was washed with water from the Gharas well."
lessons: Grief can physically overwhelm us, as it did al-Fadl, and there is no shame in being broken by love for someone you have lost.
Even in death, the Prophet remained connected to blessings he had known in life — a reminder that a life lived in goodness leaves beautiful traces.
Sacred duties performed with love, even when emotionally shattering, are among the highest acts of devotion.
practical_advice: Allow yourself to grieve fully when you lose someone dear — suppressing grief does not honor the love you had; expressing it does.
Find comfort in the meaningful connections that remain after a loss: the places they loved, the things they cherished, the memories tied to ordinary moments.
When grief makes you feel faint, do not abandon your responsibilities — pause, breathe, ask for support, and continue with what needs to be done.

title: The Mysterious Voice During the Washing of the Prophet
summary: During the washing of the Prophet's body, a mysterious voice called out twice with guidance, but al-Abbas insisted on following established practice rather than an unidentifiable voice.
story: Sayf (b. 'limar) went on to quote from aI-I;)al).l).ak b. Yarbu' aI-I:Ianan, from Mahan al-I:Ianan, from Ibn 'Abbas, who related the construction of the cover of netting, how al-'Abbas brought 'Ali, al-Faql, Abu Sufyan and Usama inside it, and men of Banu Hashim; all stood inside the house behind the netting. He narrated, "A drowsiness overcame them and they heard a voice saying, 'Do not wash the Messenger of God; he was unsullied.' Al-'Abbas responded, 'No; we must.' The members of the household said, 'The voice spoke the truth, so do not wash him.' But al-'Abbas said, 'We can't give up a hallowed sunna practice because of some voice we can't identify.' "Then drowsiness overcame them a second time and a voice called out, 'Wash him with his clothes on!' The members of the household responded, 'No; no!' Al-'Abbas said, 'I certainly will.' They then set about washing him, while he was still dressed in his shirt and an open mijwal. They washed him in the clear water, perfumed him with camphor on the parts of his body which he would prostrate in prayer and on his joints. His shirt and his mijwal were squeezed dry. He was then wrapped in his winding-sheet and they burned incense and perfumes over him. They picked him up, placed him on his bed and shrouded him."
lessons: Steadfast adherence to established religious practice over emotionally-charged moments reflects wisdom and discipline.
In times of confusion and grief, those with knowledge and leadership must hold steady so others are not led astray.
The extraordinary nature of the Prophet's death was reflected even in the supernatural experiences of those who buried him, reminding us of his singular station.
practical_advice: When you are emotionally overwhelmed and unsure of what to do, return to what is established and proven rather than being swept away by every unexpected impulse.
In a crisis, someone must take the lead calmly — if that person is you, draw on your knowledge and hold firm for the sake of those around you.
Trust the wisdom encoded in established spiritual practice; it exists precisely to guide you when emotions alone cannot.

title: The Shrouding of the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS)
summary: Accounts from multiple companions describe the shrouding of the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) in two or three white garments, with differing narrations about whether a striped cloak or Yemeni cloth was included.
story: al-Ra!:lman - quoting '1sa - meaning Ibn al-Mnkhtar - from Mul:lammad b. 'Abd al~Ra!:lman - he being Ibn Abu Layla - from 'A\ii', from Ibn 'Abbas, from al-Fagl b. 'Abbas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was shrouded in two white garments and a red cloak." Abu Yaqa stated that Sulayman al-Shadhkfinj related to him, quoting Ya!:lya b. Abu al~Haytham, quoting 'Othman b. 'Ata', from his father, from Ibn 'Abbas, from al-Fagl, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was shrouded in two white garments from Sa!:lul." Mn!:lammad b. 'Abd al-Ra!:lman b. Abu Layla added, "and a red cloak". Several authorities narrated it from Isma'!1 al-Mu'adib, from Ya'qub b. 'Ata', from his father, from Ibn 'Abbas, from al-Fagl, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was shrouded in two white garments." And in one account the garments were stated to be from Sa!:lul. But God knows best. The /.taft:;. Ibn 'Asakir narrated through Abu 'f""a1tir al-Mukhalli~, quoting A!:lmad b. Is!:laq, from al-Bahlul, qnoting 'Abbad b. Ya'qUb, quoting SharIk, from Abu Is!:laq, who said, "I happened to attend a meeting of Banu 'Abd al-Muttalib, many of them being present. I asked them, 'In how many pieces of cloth was the Messenger of God (SAAS) shrouded?' They stated, 'In three garments, without a shirt, a qiba', "a long-sleeved outer garment", or a turban.' I asked them, 'How many of you were taken prisoner at the battle of Badr?' 'Al-'Abbas, Nawfal and 'AqIl,' they replied." AI-Bayhaql narrated through al-Zuhrl, from 'AlI b. al-I;IusaynZayn al-'AbidIn, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was shrouded in three garments, one being a striped cloak." The {taft" Ibn 'Asakir gave it along a line of doubtful authenticity, from 'Ali b. Abu Tiilib, who said, "I shrouded the Messenger of God (SAAS) in two garments from Sa1;lul and a striped cloak." Abu Sa'id b. al-A'rabi stated that Ibrahim b. al-Waild related to him, quoting Mul;1ammad b. Katliir, quoting Hisham, from Q;ttada, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyab, from Abu Hurayra, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was shrouded in two fine-mesh cloths and a striped cloak from Najran." Abu Da'ild al- Tayali~i narrated it from Hisham and 'Irman al-Q;tnan, from Q;ttada, from Sa'id, from Abu Hurayra. AI-Rabi' b. Sulayman narrated it from Asad b. Musa, quoting N~r b. Tarif, from Q;ttada, quoting Ibn al-Musayyab, from Umm Salama (who said) that, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was shrouded in three garments, one of which was a cloak from Najran." AI-Bayhaqi stated, "From what we have narrated from 'A'isha, it is clear why there is confusion for people, and that the striped cloth was removed from him. But God knows best."
lessons: Scholarly disagreement over minor details does not undermine the core truth of an event; uncertainty should be met with humility and deference to God's knowledge.
The care taken in preparing the Prophet's body for burial reflects the deep love his companions had for him, teaching us that honoring those we love even in death is an act of devotion.
The diversity of narrations preserved by the companions demonstrates the importance of careful scholarship and preserving religious history with integrity.
practical_advice: When facing uncertainty or conflicting information in your own life, practice saying 'God knows best' as a reminder that not all things need a definitive answer to move forward.
Honor the memories of loved ones who have passed by preserving their legacy carefully and speaking of them with love and accuracy.
In moments of grief and loss, focus on the small acts of care and service you can offer to those around you, as the companions did in attending to the Prophet's burial.

title: The Use of Musk from the Prophet's Embalming
summary: Ali ibn Abi Talib preserved musk that had been used in embalming the Prophet (SAAS) and later instructed that it be used again, honoring the blessed nature of what had touched the Prophet's body.
story: The {taft" al-Bayhaqi narrated through Mui).ammad b. Isi).aq b. Khuzayma, quoting Ya'qub b. Ibrahim al-Dawraqi, from I:Iumayd b. 'Abd al-Ra1).man al-Ru'asi, from I:Iasan b. Salii)., from Hariln b. Sa'id, who said, "'Ali had some musk, and he gave instruction that it be used for embalming. He said, 'It is some of the musk left over from embalming the Messenger of God (SAAS).'" He narrated this through Ibrahim b. Musa, from I:Iamld, from I:Iasan, from Hariln, from Abu Wa'il, from 'Ali.
lessons: Treasuring what connects us to those we love is a natural and beautiful expression of grief and devotion.
Ali's act shows that the companions found comfort in preserving physical connections to the Prophet, reminding us that grief can be expressed through acts of remembrance.
Even the smallest items associated with a beloved person can carry profound spiritual and emotional significance.
practical_advice: Consider keeping a meaningful memento of a loved one who has passed as a way to stay connected to their memory and find comfort in grief.
When grieving, find small rituals of remembrance that keep the spirit of your loved one present in your daily life.
Reflect on the blessings that came through the people you have loved and lost, and let gratitude for their presence soften the pain of their absence.

title: The Funeral Prayer Over the Prophet Without an Imam
summary: After the Prophet's death, Muslims prayed over him in groups without a single imam leading them — men, women, children, and slaves each taking their turn in a unique act of collective farewell.
story: THE MANNER IN WHICH PRAYERS WERE OFFERED OVER THE MESSENGER OF GOD ($AAS). The {tadith narrated above by al-Bayhaqi, quoting from al-Ash'ath Q. Taliq and al-Bazzar from a {tadith of al-I~bahani, both quoting from Murra, from Ibn Mas'ud, tells of the instruction made by the Prophet (SAAS), that he should be washed by the men of his household. In it he is also quoted as having said, "Shroud me in these clothes of mine, or in a Yemeni cloth, or a white one from Egypt." After placing him in his shroud, they were to place him on the edge of his grave, then leave him until the angels had prayed over him, after which the men of his household were to pray over him, with the other people to follow thereafter individually. The {tadith is given above in full. There is some dispute over its authenticity, as we have stated. God knows best. Mui).ammad b. Isi).aq stated that al-I:Iusayn b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Abbas related to him, quoting qkrama, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) died, the men were brought in and they prayed over him in groups and without an imam. When the men had finished, the women were brought in and they prayed over him. Then the children came and prayed, then the slaves. All these prayed over him in groups, without anyone acting as imam." Al-Waqidi stated that Ubayy b. 'Ayyiish b. Sahl b. Sa'd related to him, from his father, from his grandfather, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had been wrapped in his shroud, he was placed on his bed. After that he was placed on the edge of his grave and the people came in (to pray) over him in groups, without anyone acting as their imam." Al-Waqidi also stated that Musa b. Mu!)ammad b. Ibrahim related to him, that, "I have discovered a document in the handwriting of my father. In it it states that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) was wrapped in his shroud and placed on his bed, Abu Baler and 'Umar, may God be pleased with them, arrived, accompanied by a group of muhajirun and an~ar as large as the house could contain. The two men said, 'Peace be upon you, Prophet, along with God's mercy and blessings.' The muhiijiriin and the an~ar made the same salutation as had Abu Baler and 'Umar, and then they arranged in lines, though without anyone acting as their imam. "Abu Baler and 'Umar - they being in the first line - then spoke, facing towards the Messenger of God (SAAS), saying, '0 God, we bear witness that he has delivered the message that was revealed to him and that he has properly advised his nation. He strove hard in God's cause until God made His religion firm and completed His word; He has become worshipped alone and without associate. Make us, 0 God, of those who follow the words revealed with him. Unite us with him, letting him know us, and making us know him. He was always forgiving and merciful towards the believers. We seek no alternative to having faith in him, and we would never exchange it at any price.' "At this, those present called out Amen! Amen! They then.. left and others came in. This went on until all the men had prayed, then the women and then the children.'" It has been said that they prayed over him following the sun's passing its zenith on Monday until the same time on the Tuesday, and also that they continued praying over him for three days, as will be explained shortly. But God knows best. This practice, their having prayed over him individually without anyone acting as their imam is accepted unanimously, without any disagreement. However, there is dispute over interpreting this. If the badlth we have given from Ibn Mas'ud is authentic, then it constitutes documentary evidence. This (behaviour) would constitute al-ta'abbud "an act of religious devotion", the purpose of which is difficultto explain. It is not up to anyone to say, "This was because they had no imam", because we have explained above that they only began preparing him for burial after completion of the pledge of allegaince made to Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him. Some scholars have stated that no one acted as their imam only in order that each person might engage in direct, personal prayer over him. This was also because of the constant repetition of the prayers of the Muslims over him, one following the other, each of the Companions, the men, their women, their children and their slaves, both male and female, acting as indivuals. AI-Suhayll stated, in essence, that God had informed him (the Prophet (SAAS)) that He and His angels would pray over him, and ordered that each individual Muslim should pray directly for him after his death in this way. He stated, "And also. the angels act for Us as imams in that." But God knows best.
lessons: The Prophet's instruction that all Muslims pray over him individually — men, women, children, and slaves alike — teaches that every soul has equal worth before God and equal right to express love and devotion.

Abu Bakr and Umar's prayer at the Prophet's side is a model of how to give witness to a beloved person's life and legacy: acknowledge their faithfulness, pray for continued connection, and affirm your commitment to their values.
The communal act of shared grief, where an entire community came together over days to honor one person, demonstrates that grief is not meant to be carried alone.
practical_advice: When you lose someone significant, allow yourself and others the time to grieve properly — do not rush the process of farewell, just as the companions prayed for days.
Consider writing or speaking a personal testimony of a beloved person's qualities and contributions as a way to process grief and honor their memory.
In times of community loss, create space for everyone — regardless of rank or status — to participate in mourning and remembrance, as this shared experience strengthens bonds and aids healing.

title: The Decision on Where to Bury the Prophet
summary: After the Prophet's death, the companions disagreed about where he should be buried until Abu Bakr recalled the Prophet's own words that every prophet is buried in the place where he dies.
story: DESCRIPTIONS OF THE BURIAL OF THE MESSENGER OF GOD (SAAS), AND WHERE HE WAS BURIED. REFERENCE TO THE DIFFERENCES OVER WHETHER HE WAS BURIED BY NIGHT OR BY DAY. Imam Al:unad stated that 'Abd. al-Razzaq reported that Ibn ]nrayj, quoting his father, 'Abd al-'Azlz b. ]urayj, as having said, "The Companions of the Prophet (SAAS) did not know where to bnry him until Abu Bakr stated, 'I heard the Prophet (SAAS), say, "Every prophet has been buried wherever he died." And so they took away his bed and dug a grave beneath it." This f:tadith has a link missing between 'Abd al-'AzlZ b. ]nrayj and Abu Bakr, the former not having met the latter. However, the f:taft:;, Abu Yaqa narrated it from a f:tadith of Ibn 'Abbas and 'A'isha, from Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with them all. He stated that Abu Musa al-Harawl related to him, quoting Abu Mu'awiya, qnoting 'Abd al-Ra1)man b. Abu Bakr, from Ibn Abu Mulayka, who quoted 'A'isha as having said, "There was disagreement over the buri~ of the Prophet (SAAS), after his death. Abu Bakr stated, 'I heard the Prophet (SAAS) say, "A prophet only dies in the places most loved by him." He went on, 'Bury him where he died.'" Al-Tirmidhl related this similarly, from Abu Kurayb, from Abu Mu'awiya, from 'Abd al-Ra1)man b. Abu Bakr, from Ibn Abu Mulayka, who quoted 'A'isha as having said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) died, there was disagreement over his burial. Abu Bakr stated, 'I heard from the Messenger of God (SAAS) something I have not forgotten. He said, "A prophet always dies in that place where he wishes to be buried." Bury him where his bed is positioned.'" AI-Tirmishl classified al-Mulayka as a weak sonrce and then stated, "This . f:tadith is also narrated from a different line. Ibn 'Abbas related that Abu Bakr quoted this statement from the Messenger of God (SAAS)." AI-UmawI quoted his father, from Ibn 1sgaq, from a man who quoted from 'lJrwa, from 'A'isha, who said that Abu Bakr stated, "I heard the Messenger of God ($AAS) say, 'No prophet has ever been buried in any place other than that where he died.'" Abu Bakr b. Abu alcDunya stated that Mugammad b. SaW al-Tamlmi related to him, quoting Hisham b. 'Abd al-Malik al-TayalisI, from I;Iammad b. Salama, from Hisham b. 'lJrwa, from his father, who quoted 'A'isha as having said, "There were two grave diggers in Medina, and when the Prophet ($AAS) died, people asked, 'Where should we bury him?' Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, said, 'In the place where he died.' One of the grave-diggers would make a niche, the other would dig a pit. The one who made niches did arrive, and he did so for the Prophet ($AAS)."
lessons: Abu Bakr's calm recall of the Prophet's own guidance in a moment of communal confusion demonstrates the value of preserving and returning to the wisdom of those we love when facing difficult decisions after their loss.
The Prophet's teaching that a prophet dies where he most loves to be reminds us that where we dwell, worship, and serve is sacred — our places of devotion carry deep spiritual significance.
Confusion and disagreement among good people after a loss is natural; what matters is that guidance is sought with sincerity and the decision is made with care.
practical_advice: When facing confusion after a loss, return to the words and values of the person you lost — their own guidance may have already answered the questions you are struggling with.
Designate a place of personal significance in your life as a space for reflection, prayer, and remembrance, understanding that sacred places anchor us emotionally and spiritually.

When a community is divided on a difficult decision during grief, look for the most knowledgeable and trusted voice to guide the group, just as Abu Bakr's remembered wisdom resolved the companions' uncertainty.

title: Disagreement Over Where to Bury the Prophet
summary: After the Prophet's death, the companions disagreed about where he should be buried — in the mosque, with companions, or elsewhere — until Abu Bakr settled the matter by recalling the Prophet's words that every prophet is buried where he dies.
story: When the Messenger of God ($AAS) had been prepared for burial on Tuesday, he was placed on his bed in his house. The Muslims were in disagreement about his burial. One said, 'We should bury him in his mosque.' Another suggested, 'We should bury him with his Companions.' Abu Bakr said, 'I heard the Messenger of God ($AAS) say, "Every prophet who died was buried where he died."' The bed of the Messenger of God ($AAS), on which he had died, was taken away and they dug a grave beneath it. The people were then brought in to pray over him in groups. When the men had finished, the women were brought in. When they had finished the children were brought. No one acted as imam for the people over the Messenger of God ($AAS). He was buried in the middle of Wednesday night.
lessons: In moments of grief and confusion, knowledge of prophetic guidance brings clarity and unity to a community.
True leadership means stepping forward with wisdom and precedent when others are lost in uncertainty.
Even the greatest of losses can be navigated with patience, order, and reverence.
practical_advice: When facing an overwhelming decision during a time of grief, seek guidance from established wisdom or trusted counsel rather than acting on emotion alone.
Allow yourself to grieve in community — the companions prayed in organized groups, finding strength in shared mourning.
If you are in a position of leadership during a crisis, recall what you know from reliable sources and share it calmly to restore order.

title: Abu Bakr's Knowledge of the Prophet's Burial Place
summary: Abu Bakr resolved multiple disputes about the Prophet's burial location by recounting the hadith that every prophet is buried where he dies, demonstrating the profound value of preserving and transmitting prophetic knowledge.
story: Abu Bakr came and said, 'I have knowledge and a comment about that. I heard the Messenger of God ($AAS) say, "Every prophet who died was buried where he died."' And so he was buried where his bed had been; the bed was lifted away and a (grave) was dug beneath it. Abu Bakr went in to the Messenger of God ($AAS), when he died, and then he left again. He was asked 'Has the Messenger of God died?' 'Yes,' he replied. And people knew it to be as he said. He was asked, 'Should we pray over him? And how should we pray?' He replied, 'You should come in in groups and then pray.' And they knew it should be as he said. They asked, 'Will he be buried, and where?' He replied, 'In the place where his soul was taken. His soul would only have been taken in a good place.' And they knew it should be as he said.
lessons: Preserving and learning sacred knowledge is an act of service that benefits the entire community in its most difficult hours.
A calm, knowledgeable voice in a time of crisis is one of the greatest gifts a community can possess.
Trust that God chooses the best circumstances for those He loves, including the place and moment of their passing.
practical_advice: Invest time in learning your faith deeply so that when crises arise, you have something stable to hold onto and share with others.
In moments of panic or communal grief, be the person who speaks slowly, calmly, and from a place of knowledge rather than emotion.
When you are unsure about what to do during hardship, reflect on the guidance left by those wiser than you — there is often an answer already given.

title: The Two Grave Diggers and God's Choice
summary: The companions could not agree on whether the Prophet's grave should be dug as a niche or a trench, so they sent for both grave-diggers and trusted God to choose by whichever arrived first — and the niche-maker arrived first.
story: When the Messenger of God ($AAS) died, there was in Medina one man who specialized in making wall niches in graves and another who dug trench graves. People said, 'We will let God choose; we will send for both. And we'll let the one do it who arrives first.' They were sent for and the one who made the niches arrived first. And so they dug for him a grave with a niche. Al-'Abbas called to two men. To one he said, 'Go and fetch Abu 'Ubayda.' To the other he said, 'Go and fetch Abu Talha.' (He then said) 'O God, choose for Your messenger!' The man sent to Abu Talha did locate him. He came and made a grave with a niche for the Messenger of God ($AAS).
lessons: When humans are divided and no clear answer is available, entrusting the outcome to God is itself an act of profound faith.
The companions' approach shows that surrendering control to God is not passivity — it is an active and dignified form of tawakkul (reliance on God).
Even in the smallest details of honoring the beloved, the companions sought divine guidance, showing that no moment is too small for God's involvement.
practical_advice: When you face a decision where reasonable people disagree and no option is clearly better, make du'a and then act — trust that God can guide outcomes even through seemingly random circumstances.
Practice releasing the need to control every outcome; sometimes the most faithful thing you can do is set up the conditions and let God determine the result.
Honor those you love with the same care and intentionality the companions showed — even in grief, they gave their best to the Prophet.

title: Ali Washes the Prophet and Finds Him Sweet-Smelling
summary: Ali ibn Abi Talib washed the Prophet's body after his death and reported finding no unpleasant discharge, noting that the Prophet was sweet-smelling both in life and in death.
story: 'Ali stated, 'I washed the Prophet ($AAS), and I set about looking for any such discharge as comes from the dead, but I saw nothing; he was sweet-smelling both alive and dead.' There were four persons who, more than the other people, took charge of the burial and refilling the grave of the Prophet ($AAS). These men were 'Ali, al-'Abbas, al-Fadl and Salih, the freed-man of the Prophet ($AAS). A niche was dug in his grave and unburnt bricks were placed over it as a marker.
lessons: The Prophet's distinction extended even to the manner of his death, reminding believers that those who are spiritually pure carry that purity in a profound way.
Serving a beloved person through the most intimate and difficult acts — such as washing the deceased — is among the highest expressions of love and loyalty.
Grief shared among those who loved the same person creates bonds that endure beyond loss.
practical_advice: When someone you love passes away, find meaning in the practical acts of care that follow — preparing, honoring, and burying them is a form of healing and tribute.
Reflect on how you are cultivating inner purity in your own life; the companions' testimony about the Prophet inspires us to strive for a life that leaves a lasting good impression.
Do not grieve alone; gather those who shared your love and face the loss together, as Ali, Abbas, and others did for the Prophet.

title: Shuqran Places the Prophet's Cloak in His Grave
summary: Shuqran, the freed slave of the Prophet, placed the cloak the Prophet used to wear into his grave, swearing that no one else would ever wear it after him.
story: Shuqran, the freed-man of the Prophet ($AAS), took a cloak the Messenger of God ($AAS) would wear and put it in the grave. He said, 'I swear by God, no one will ever wear it after you!' And it was buried with him. 'Ali b. Abu Talib, and al-Fadl and Qutham, the two sons of 'Abbas, along with Shuqran, the freed-man of the Messenger of God ($AAS) went down into his tomb. And Aws b. Khawla — Abu Layla, that is — asked 'Ali b. Abu Talib, 'I charge you by God and our fine experience of the Messenger of God ($AAS).' 'Ali told him, 'Come down then.'
lessons: True love for a person means that their memory becomes sacred — Shuqran's gesture shows how deeply the Prophet was cherished even by those society had once marginalized.
The freed slave standing beside the sons of the Prophet's family in burying him speaks to the Prophet's legacy of equality and dignity for all people.
Small personal acts of devotion, like placing a beloved garment in a grave, carry enormous emotional and spiritual weight.
practical_advice: Find your own meaningful way to honor those you have lost — a personal, heartfelt gesture of remembrance can be part of your healing process.
Reflect on the relationships in your life that cross social boundaries; the Prophet's freed slave mourning him as an equal reminds us to cherish every sincere relationship.
When grief feels overwhelming, channel it into an act of love rather than letting it become paralysis.

title: A'isha's Vision of Three Moons and Its Interpretation
summary: A'isha had a vision of three moons falling into her lap, which her father Abu Bakr interpreted as meaning three of the finest men on earth would be buried in her house — the first being the Prophet himself.
story: Al-Bayhaqi narrated from a hadith of Sufyan b. 'Uyayna, from Yahya b. Sa'id al-Ansari, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyab, who said, "'A'isha described to her father a vision she had seen; he was one of the most highly skilled of men in interpretation. She said, 'I saw three moons fall into my lap.' He responded, 'If your vision is accurate, then three of the finest men on earth will be buried in your house.' When the Messenger of God ($AAS) died, he told her, 'A'isha, this is the best of your moons!'"
lessons: God sometimes prepares those He loves for what is to come through dreams and visions, a mercy that softens the blow of what lies ahead.
The Prophet's death in A'isha's house and arms was not a tragedy without meaning — it was the fulfillment of a divine promise of honor.
Grief can coexist with gratitude; A'isha's room became the most honored burial ground on earth, a blessing even amid immeasurable loss.
practical_advice: When you experience loss, try to hold space for both the pain of what was taken and the gratitude for the privilege of having been close to someone so beloved.
Reflect on the meaningful coincidences or blessings that have accompanied difficult moments in your life — sometimes God's mercy is woven into the very fabric of our pain.
Talk to trusted, wise people about your dreams, fears, and visions; sometimes an outside perspective can reveal the grace hidden in what troubles you.

title: The Prophet Dies in A'isha's Arms
summary: A'isha narrated that the Prophet died in her house on her day of visitation, resting between her chest and neck, and that God joined their saliva in his final and first moments.
story: She is quoted in both Sahih collections as having said, 'The Prophet ($AAS) died in my house, on the day of his visit to me, and resting between my chest and neck. And God joined my saliva with his in his last moments on earth and his first in the Hereafter.'
lessons: The intimate mercy shown to A'isha — that the Prophet's last breath was in her arms — reflects how God honors devoted love and companionship.
Even the ending of the greatest life ever lived was tender and human, reminding us that death is not something to fear but a passage cradled in divine care.
The Prophet's passing was attended with love on all sides — a reminder that how we treat those around us in life shapes the quality of our final moments.
practical_advice: Allow yourself to feel the full weight of love and loss together — A'isha's account is both heartbreaking and deeply beautiful, and holding both is part of being human.
Cherish the ordinary moments of closeness with those you love; A'isha's most precious memory was simply being the person whose arms held the Prophet at his end.
If you are caring for someone who is dying or ill, know that your presence — just being there — is one of the most profound gifts you can give.

title: The Prophet's Warning Against Worshipping at Graves
summary: During his final illness, the Prophet warned against adopting the graves of prophets as places of worship, which influenced the decision not to make his tomb prominent.
story: In the Sahih collection of al-Bukhari, it is related from ahadith of Abu 'Awana, from Hilal al-Warraq, from 'Urwa, who quoted 'A'isha as having said, 'I heard the Messenger of God ($AAS) say during the illness from which he died, "May God curse the Jews and the Christians who adopt the graves of their prophets as places of worship."' 'A'isha went on, 'Had it not been for that, he would have made a prominent tomb; he feared, however, that it would be adopted as a mosque.'
lessons: The Prophet's concern even in his final illness was for the preservation of pure monotheism and the spiritual well-being of his community after him.
True love for a leader means honoring the principles they stood for, not merely venerating the places associated with them.
Prophetic foresight protects communities across generations — guidance given centuries ago continues to guard against spiritual deviation today.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether your expressions of love and honor for sacred figures remain within the boundaries of what those figures themselves taught.
When someone you admire or love passes away, ask yourself: what would they want me to hold onto — their memory, or their message?
Study the boundaries your tradition draws around honoring the deceased, and let that knowledge guide your grief into healthy and spiritually sound expression.

title: Red Velvet Placed Beneath the Prophet in His Grave
summary: A cloth of red velvet — which the Prophet had worn and won at the battle of Hunayn — was placed in his grave beneath him, both out of love and because the ground at Medina was damp.
story: Ibn Sa'd stated that Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah al-Ansari informed him, quoting Ash'ath b. 'Abd al-Malik al-Humrani, from al-Hasan, that the Messenger of God ($AAS) was laid out with a red velvet garment beneath him that he had used to wear. Al-Hasan said, 'The earth was damp.' Hushaym b. Mansur quoted al-Hasan as having said, 'A cloth of red velvet was placed in the grave of the Prophet; he had won it at the battle of Hunayn.' Al-Hasan went on, 'This was done because the ground at Medina is marshlike.' Muhammad b. Sa'd stated that Hammad b. Khalid al-Khayyat related to him, from 'Uqba b. Abu al-Sahba' who said that he heard al-Hasan say, 'The Messenger of God ($AAS) said, "Layout for me a piece of velvet in my niche, for the earth should not be given power over the bodies of prophets."'
lessons: Even in burial, the Prophet was honored with something that had been part of his life — a reminder that our worldly objects can carry spiritual and emotional significance.
The companions attended to every detail of the Prophet's burial with care and intentionality, modeling how to honor the dead with reverence.
God distinguishes those He loves even in death, as expressed in the Prophet's own words about prophets being protected from the earth.
practical_advice: When preparing to honor someone who has died, think carefully and personally about what objects or gestures would be most meaningful — small acts of care carry great love.
Reflect on the items or memories from your own life that hold spiritual or personal significance; cultivating meaning in the everyday prepares you for graceful endings.
Take comfort in the belief that God honors and protects those who devoted their lives to His path, even beyond the moment of death.

title: Al-Mughira ibn Shu'ba and the Ring in the Prophet's Grave
summary: Al-Mughira ibn Shu'ba deliberately threw his ring into the Prophet's grave claiming it fell by accident, hoping to be the last person to touch the Prophet (SAAS), but Ali (RA) recognized the ruse and sent someone else to retrieve it.
story: Yilnus b. Bukayr narrated it from MUQammad b. ISQaq exactly the same, except that he added to the beginning (of the chain) MUQammad b. ISQaq, who said, "AI-Mughira b. Shu'ba used to say, 'I took my ring and threw it iuto the grave of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and said, when the people left, "My ring has fallen into the grave." I dropped it there deliberately so that I would touch the Messenger of God (SAAS) with it and so be the last person to be with him.'" Ibn ISQaq went on, "My father ISQaq b. Yasar related to me, from Miqsam who quoted his freed-man 'Abd Allah b. al-I;Iarith as having said, 'I went on the 'umra pilgrimage with 'Ali ...'" and he proceeded to narrate the badith as above. This material narrated from al-Mughira b. Shu'ba does not necessarily mean that it occurred as he had hoped. It could be that 'Ali, may God be pleased with him, did not permit him to go down into the grave and that he directed someone else to do so, and the latter gave it to him (al-Mughira). According to the information given above, it would have been Qutham b. 'Abbas whom he asked to take it. AI-Waqidi stated that 'Abd al-Rai)mw b. Abu al-Zinnad related to him, from his father, who quoted 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Abd Allah b. COtba as having said, "AI-Mughira b. Shu'ba threw his ring down into the grave of the Messenger of God (SAAS). And 'Ali commented, 'You only did that to say, "I went down into the grave of the Prophet (SAAS)."' And he then went down himself and gave it back to him - or he told another man to do so, who returned it."
lessons: True love for the Prophet (SAAS) is expressed through sincere devotion and following his teachings, not through outward gestures meant to gain prestige among others.
Sincerity of intention is foundational in Islam — actions done for show rather than for God's pleasure ultimately ring hollow and may even be exposed.
Ali's calm and clear-sighted response to al-Mughira's ruse shows the importance of maintaining integrity and accountability within a community, especially in sacred moments.
practical_advice: Reflect honestly on whether your acts of worship or remembrance are driven by sincere love or by a desire for recognition — sincerity is a daily practice that requires self-examination.
When you catch yourself seeking validation from others in spiritual matters, redirect that energy toward private devotion and gratitude to God.
If someone close to you acts with mixed motives during a sacred or solemn moment, address it gently and constructively, as Ali did, rather than shaming them publicly.

title: Ali Asks Who Was the Last to Be with the Prophet (SAAS) — The Account of Qutham ibn Abbas
summary: When men from Iraq came to Ali asking whether al-Mughira ibn Shu'ba was the last person with the Prophet (SAAS), Ali clarified that it was Qutham ibn Abbas, shedding light on a question that had persisted among the companions.
story: Imam Ai)mad stated that Ya'qub related to him, quoting his father, from Ibn Is1;taq, quoting his father, Is1;taq b. Yasar, from Miqsam Abu al-QIsim, the freedman of 'Abd Allah b. al-I;Iarith b. Nawfal, from his freed-man, 'Abd Allah b. al-I;Iarith, who said, "I went on the 'umra pilgrimage with 'AlI in the reign of 'Umar - or 'Uthman - and 'AlI stopped to visit his sister Umrn Ham', daughter of Abu Talib. When he had finished performing the 'umra, he returned (to her home) and she poured out water for him with which he washed. "When he had finished washing, a group of men from Iraq came in to see him and asked, 'Abu I;Iasan, we have come to ask you to tell us about something.' He responded, 'I imagine al-Mughira b. Shn'ba is telling you how he was the last person to be with the Messenger of God (SAAS).' 'Yes, that's right', they answered. 'That's what we have come to ask you about.' He replied, 'The last person to be with the Messenger of God (SAAS), was Qutham b. 'Abbas.'" AQmad is alone in giving this from this line.
lessons: Seeking accurate knowledge about the Prophet (SAAS) is a noble act of love, and the companions took great care to preserve the truth even in matters of personal detail.
Ali's patient and knowledgeable response illustrates that true authority comes from those who were present and witnessed events firsthand, reminding us to seek knowledge from reliable sources.
The grief of the companions over the Prophet's passing was so profound that even years later they sought to understand every detail of his final moments, showing the depth of their love.
practical_advice: When you are grieving the loss of someone beloved, it is natural to want to understand every detail of their final moments — allow yourself that process without guilt.
Seek out those with genuine knowledge and experience when you have important questions, rather than relying on rumor or the loudest voice in the room.
Channel deep love for role models and mentors into learning about their lives and character, letting that knowledge shape your own values and actions.

title: Al-Mughira Touches the Prophet's Feet in the Grave
summary: During the burial, al-Mughira ibn Shu'ba entered the Prophet's grave under the pretext of arranging the shroud over his feet, and later claimed to be the last person to have been with the Messenger of God (SAAS).
story: Imam Ai)mad stated that Bahz and Abu Kamil related to him that I;Iammad b. Salama related to them both, from Abu COmran al-Jawni, from Abu 'Asib - or Abu 'Asim - who quoted Bahz as having said that he witnessed the prayers being spoken over the Prophet (SAAS), and that people asked, "How should we pray?" He (presumably Abu Bakr) replied, "Go in in groups." And they entered from one door, prayed over him and then went out through the other door. Bahz went on, "When he had been placed in his niche, al-Mughira said, 'You did not properly arrange (the shroud over) part of his feet.' They told him, 'Well, you go down and do it.' So he did enter the grave and placed his hand inside (the shroud) and touched both his feet. He then said, 'Now pass down some earth to me.' They did so until it was covering half his legs. He then came out. He used to say, 'I was later than any of you to be with the Messenger of God (SAAS)!'"
lessons: The companions prayed over the Prophet (SAAS) in organized groups, demonstrating that even in the most profound grief, structure and order in worship bring communal comfort.
The intensity of the companions' desire to be close to the Prophet (SAAS) — even in his final moments — is a reflection of the transformative love his presence inspired.
Every moment of closeness to those we love is precious; the companions understood this deeply and sought to preserve even a final physical connection.
practical_advice: When you lose someone dear, find meaningful ways to be present and contribute to honoring them — even small acts of care in their final rites bring healing to the heart.
Participate actively in communal rituals of mourning and burial; being part of the community during grief is one of the most powerful sources of solace.
Reflect on the legacy of those you have loved and lost, and allow gratitude for having known them to soften the pain of their absence.

title: Aisha and Umm Salama Hear the Spades of the Prophet's Burial
summary: The wives of the Prophet (SAAS) did not know when he was buried until they heard the sound of spades and pickaxes in the night, and Umm Salama describes the collective wailing that spread throughout Madinah.
story: Yilnus quoted Ibn Isl.taq as having stated, "Fatima told me, she being the daughter of Mul.tammad, the wife of 'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr, who let me in to visit her so that I heard it from her. She cited from 'Amra, who quoted 'A.'isha as having said, 'We did not know about the burial of the Prophet (SAAS), until we heard the sound of the spades in the middle of the Wednesday night.'" AI-WaqidI stated that Ibn Abu Sabra related to him, from al-l;Ialyas b. Hisham, from 'Abd Allah b. Wahb, who quoted Umm Salama to say, "While we were gathered together we wept; we did nOt sleep while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was there in our apartments. We took comfort in seeing him on the bed. Then we heard the sound of the pickaxes in the pre-dawn night. We screamed in lament and those in the mosque did so too. The whole town was screaming with one voice. Then Bilal made the dawn call to prayer. When he mentioned the Prophet (SAAS), he wept and lamented. His pain increased our own. Then people began going in to his grave, but they were shut out.
