What a disaster that was! Any later tragedy we came to suffer was alleviated when we recalled our disastrous loss of the Messenger of God (SAAS)."
lessons: Grief does not follow a neat timeline; the companions continued to find comfort in the physical presence of the Prophet (SAAS) on his bed, showing that it is natural to hold on before fully letting go.
Communal grief, where an entire community mourns together, is a deeply human and divinely sanctioned response to loss — we are not meant to grieve entirely alone.

Umm Salama's reflection that all later tragedies were lightened in comparison to the loss of the Prophet (SAAS) teaches us that some losses reframe all others, and that perspective on grief can itself be a source of endurance.
practical_advice: When grieving, allow yourself to be physically present with others who share your loss — shared weeping and remembrance is not weakness but a source of communal healing.
If you find a later difficulty easier to bear, take a moment to acknowledge the resilience you have built through surviving previous hardships.
Even in the depths of grief, try to maintain your worship and daily obligations as Bilal did, making the call to prayer even through tears — structure can be an anchor when emotion is overwhelming.

title: Bilal Weeps During the Dawn Call to Prayer After the Prophet's Burial
summary: When Bilal made the dawn call to prayer after the Prophet's burial, he wept upon mentioning the Prophet's name, and his grief intensified the sorrow of the Prophet's wives and the entire community of Madinah.
story: AI-WaqidI stated that Ibn Abu Sabra related to him, from al-l;Ialyas b. Hisham, from 'Abd Allah b. Wahb, who quoted Umm Salama to say, "While we were gathered together we wept; we did nOt sleep while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was there in our apartments. We took comfort in seeing him on the bed. Then we heard the sound of the pickaxes in the pre-dawn night. We screamed in lament and those in the mosque did so too. The whole town was screaming with one voice. Then Bilal made the dawn call to prayer. When he mentioned the Prophet (SAAS), he wept and lamented. His pain increased our own. Then people began going in to his grave, but they were shut out. What a disaster that was! Any later tragedy we came to suffer was alleviated when we recalled our disastrous loss of the Messenger of God (SAAS)."
lessons: Bilal's tears during the adhan show that even the most disciplined acts of worship can and should carry deep personal feeling — devotion and emotion are not opposites.
Fulfilling our duties to God and the community even in our most broken moments, as Bilal did, is itself a form of spiritual strength and service.
The grief of one sincere person can ripple outward and deepen the shared experience of a community, reminding us that our emotional honesty affects those around us.
practical_advice: When grief makes even routine acts difficult, do them anyway — the discipline of continuing to serve and worship through pain is itself a path to healing.
Allow yourself to cry in worship and remembrance; tears shed out of love for God and His Prophet are honored in Islamic tradition and should not be suppressed.
If you are a leader or person others look to, your authentic expression of grief can give others permission to grieve openly and healthily, creating bonds of shared humanity.

title: Bilal Sprays Water Over the Prophet's Grave
summary: After the Prophet's burial, Bilal ibn Rabba sprayed the grave with water from a water-skin, beginning from the head on the right side and finishing at the feet, in accordance with Islamic burial practice.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that 'Abd Allah b. Ja'far related to him, from Ibn Abu 'Awn, from Abu 'Atiq, from Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, who said, "The grave of the Messenger of God (SAAS) was sprayed with water. The person who did this was Bilal b. Rabba1)., using a water-skin. He began at his head, from his right side, and finished with his legs. He then struck the water on the wall, but was not able to turn around from the wall."
lessons: Even the smallest acts of care in honoring the deceased carry spiritual weight, as seen in Bilal's careful and methodical watering of the Prophet's grave.

Bilal, who had suffered so much — from slavery to torture for his faith — now stood as the one entrusted to honor the Prophet in his final resting place, showing how God elevates the patient and sincere.
The right side is honored first in Islamic practice, reflecting an overall ethic of beauty, order, and intentionality even in grief.
practical_advice: Bring the same care and intentionality to acts of remembrance and mourning that you bring to acts of celebration — both deserve your full presence.
Reflect on how your own journey of hardship may be preparing you for roles of honor and trust you cannot yet see.
In times of loss, small, practical acts of care — for the deceased or for the bereaved — are profound expressions of love that should not be underestimated.

title: Fatima's Grief at the Prophet's Death and Burial
summary: Fatima, the Prophet's daughter, expressed profound grief at his suffering during his final illness and lamented deeply upon his death and burial, asking the companions if they were truly content to bury the Messenger of God.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Sulayman b. Harb related to him, quoting Hammad b. Zayd, quoting Thabit, from Anas, who said, "When the illness of the Messenger of God (SAAS) grew intense, he was overwhelmed by agony. Fatima exclaimed, 'What agony, father!' He responded, 'Your father will suffer no more agony after today.' "When he died, she said, '0 for father! He has responded to a Lord Who has summoned him! 0 father, whose abode is (now) the garden of paradise! 0 father, we make Gabriel aware of your death.' "When he was buried, Fatima asked, 'Anas, are you men pleased to be piling earth over the Messenger of God (SAAS)?'" Al-Bukhari, may God have mercy upon him, was alone in giving this. Imam Ahmad stated that Yazid related to him, quoting Hammad b. Zayd, quoting Thabit al-Banani, who quoted Anas as having said, "When the Prophet (SAAS) was buried, Fatima said, 'Anas, are you men now pleased that you have buried the Messenger of God (SAAS), in the earth and have come back?'" Ibn Maja narrated it thus, in abbreviated form from a hadith of Hammad b. Zayd. According to him, Hammad stated, "When Thabit related this hadith he would weep so hard his chest would heave." This should not be considered tantamount to lamentation. It is, rather, the expression of just admiration for him, may he receive the very best of prayers and salutations. We have made this comment only because the Messenger of God (SAAS) forbad lamentation.
lessons: Grief over the loss of a beloved is natural and does not contradict faith; Fatima's tears reflect the depth of her love, not a lack of trust in God's decree.
Expressing sorrow through words of remembrance and praise — as Fatima did — is a spiritually healthy way to process loss while affirming one's belief in the afterlife.
The companions' concern about whether they were right to proceed with burial reflects how overwhelming grief can make even necessary actions feel unbearable; this is part of being human.
practical_advice: When grieving a loss, allow yourself to express sorrow through words of remembrance — speaking about the person's qualities and your love for them is healing, not weakness.
If grief makes you question whether you are doing the right thing, lean on trusted community members who can gently reassure you and help you through necessary steps.
Channel intense grief into remembrance of God and the promise of paradise, as Fatima did when she spoke of her father's blessed abode — this reframing brings comfort without denying the pain.

title: Medina Lit Up at the Prophet's Arrival and Darkened at His Death
summary: Companions described how Medina was spiritually brightened when the Prophet arrived and became dark and disorienting upon his death, with some feeling unable to see one another and overcome by doubt.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that 'Affan related to him, quoting Ja'far b. Sulayman, quoting Thabit al-Banani, who quoted Anas as having said, "On the occasion of the return of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to Medina, everything was brightly lit up. The day he died everything there became dark. And no sooner had we finished with the Messenger of God (SAAS), than we were overcome by doubts." Both al-Tirmidhi and Ibn Maja narrated it thus, from Bishr b. Hilal al-Sawwaf, from Ja'far b. Sulayman al-Dab'i. Al-Tirmidhi stated, "This hadith is sahih gharib." Al-Kudaymi, he being Muhammad b. Yunus, may God have mercy upon him, narrated a hadith that could be considered gharib, in this regard. He stated that Abu al-Walid Hisham b. 'Abd al-Malik al-Tayalisi, quoted Ja'far b. Sulayman al-Dab'i, from Thabit, from Anas, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) died, Medina became so dark that we could not see one another. One would stretch out one's hand but not see it" — or "not catch sight of it". "No sooner had we completed his burial than we were overcome by doubts." Al-Bayhaqi narrated it similarly through that line. The great hafiz Abu al-Qasim b. 'Asakir narrated through Abu Hafs b. Shahin, quoting Husayn b. Ahmad b. Bistam at al-Abilla, quoting Muhammad b. Yazid al-Ru'asi, quoting Maslama b. 'Alqama, from Da'ud b. Abu Hind, from Abu Naqra, from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Medina everything there became bright. The day he died everything there became dark."
lessons: The loss of a great spiritual guide can leave an entire community feeling disoriented and lost — this is a sign of how deeply the guide's presence nurtured those around them.
Doubt and uncertainty arising after a profound loss are natural human responses; recognizing them honestly is the first step to finding one's footing again.
The light that great people bring to a community reminds us to carry forward their teachings so that light does not die with them.
practical_advice: When you feel lost after the death of a mentor, teacher, or loved one, recognize that this disorientation is normal — give yourself permission to grieve before expecting yourself to move forward.
In times of communal grief and confusion, hold tightly to the principles and teachings the departed person left behind — these become the light in the darkness.
If you feel overcome by doubt after a major loss, seek out others who share your faith and values; the community that grieves together can help one another find direction again.

title: The Companions' Unity Dissolved After the Prophet's Death
summary: Ubayy b. Ka'b described how the companions, who had been united in one direction under the Prophet, scattered and looked disunited in every direction upon his death.
story: Ibn Maja stated that Ishaq b. Mansur related to us, quoting 'Abd al-Wahhab b. 'Ata al-'Ijli, from Ibn 'Awn, from al-Hasan, from Ubayy b. Ka'b, who said, "While we were with the Messenger of God (SAAS), we were (united) in one direction, and when he died, we looked (disunited) here and there."
lessons: True leadership provides a community with direction and unity; when that leadership is lost, the community must consciously work to preserve what it was taught rather than drifting apart.
Feeling spiritually and communally disoriented after a great loss is not a failure of faith — it is an honest reflection of how much the beloved guide meant to those around them.
The unity the companions felt under the Prophet is itself a lesson: cultivating shared values and direction during times of ease makes a community resilient when hardship comes.
practical_advice: After losing a guiding figure in your life — a parent, mentor, or spiritual leader — deliberately revisit the core values and direction they instilled in you, so that you do not drift without anchor.
When a community or family feels scattered after a loss, one healing step is to gather and recall together what the departed person stood for and what they would want for those left behind.
Use times of stability and guidance to build strong inner conviction, so that when guidance is no longer externally present, you have an internal compass to rely on.

title: Umm Ayman Weeps for the Loss of Revelation
summary: When Umm Ayman wept after the Prophet's death, she explained that her tears were not merely for the Prophet's passing but for the cessation of divine revelation that his death meant for the community.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that 'Abd al-Samad related to him, quoting Hammad, from Thabit, from Anas, that Umm Ayman wept when the Messenger of God (SAAS) died. She was asked, "Why are you weeping over the Prophet (SAAS)?" She replied, "I knew the Messenger of God (SAAS) would die; what I'm lamenting for is the revelation that was taken from us." He narrated it thus, in a condensed form. Al-Bayhaqi stated that Abu 'Abd Allah al-Hafiz informed us, quoting Abu 'Abd Allah Muhammad b. Ya'qub, quoting Muhammad b. Nu'aym and Muhammad b. al-Nadr al-Jarudi, both of whom said, "Al-Hasan b. 'Ali al-Khulani related to us, quoting 'Amr b. 'Asim al-Kilabi, quoting Sulayman b. al-Mughira, from Thabit, from Anas, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) went to visit Umm Ayman and I accompanied him. She brought him a cordial. He was either fasting or did not want it, and he refused it. She then went over to him to make him laugh. After the death of the Prophet (SAAS), Abu Bakr said to 'Umar, "Let's go and visit Umm Ayman." When we arrived to see her, she wept. The two men asked'"
lessons: Umm Ayman's grief reveals a profound spiritual maturity: she could accept God's decree regarding death, but she mourned the immense blessing of divine revelation that the community would no longer directly receive.
Recognizing what a blessing we had only becomes fully clear when it is gone — this is a reminder to value and engage deeply with the gifts we have been given while we still have them.
Grief can be an expression of deep gratitude — weeping for what was lost is also an acknowledgment of how precious and irreplaceable it was.
practical_advice: When you grieve, try to identify specifically what it is you are mourning — as Umm Ayman did — because naming the loss precisely helps you process it and honor what was truly valuable.
Make it a practice to consciously appreciate sources of spiritual nourishment in your life while they are present, rather than taking them for granted until they are gone.

If you find that grief keeps returning even after accepting a loss, ask yourself whether there is a deeper gift or blessing connected to that person or relationship that you are also mourning — acknowledging this can bring clarity and peace.

title: The Grave of the Prophet and His Two Companions
summary: Historical accounts describe the nature and appearance of the Prophet's grave in the apartment of Aisha, alongside the graves of Abu Bakr and Umar, and the circumstances under which the graves were viewed and the mosque was later expanded.
story: It has been reiterated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was buried in the apartment of 'A'isha which had been set aside for her to the east of the mosque, in the south-west corner of the room. Later on, after him, Abu Bakr and then 'Umar, may God be pleased with them both, were also buried there. Al-Bukhari stated that Muhammad b. Muqatil related to him, quoting Abu Bakr b. 'Ayyash, who stated to him that Sufyan al-Tammar narrated to him that he had seen the grave of the Prophet (SAAS), as a convex pile. Abu Da'ud stated that Ahmad b. Salih narrated to him, quoting Ibn Abu Fudayk, quoting 'Amr b. 'Uthman b. Hani, from al-Qasim, who said, "I went in to 'A'isha and asked her, 'Expose for me the grave of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and those of his two Companions.' She then exposed three graves that were neither elevated nor recessed and inscribed on the red surface of the floor were (the words) 'The Prophet', (SAAS), 'Abu Bakr', may God be pleased with him, and ''Umar', may God be pleased with him." Al-Hakim and al-Bayhaqi narrated this from a hadith of Ibn Abu Fudayk, from 'Amr b. 'Uthman, from al-Qasim, who said, "I saw the Prophet (SAAS) in front; the head of Abu Bakr was resting between the shoulders of the Prophet (SAAS). The head of 'Umar was at the leg of the Prophet (SAAS)." Al-Waqidi narrated from al-Darawardi, from Ja'far b. Muhammad, from his father, who said, "The grave of the Messenger of God (SAAS) was flat." Al-Bukhari stated that Farwa b. Abu al-Maghra' related to him, quoting 'Ali b. Mushir, from Hisham, from 'Urwa, who quoted his father as having said, "When the wall collapsed in the era of al-Walid b. 'Abd al-Malik, they set about rebuilding it. A foot became visible to them and they were extremely shocked, thinking it to be the foot of the Prophet (SAAS). No one could be found who knew about it, but eventually 'Urwa told them, 'No, I swear by God, that's not the foot of the Prophet (SAAS); that's 'Umar's foot.'" It is also given from Hisham, from his father, who said that 'A'isha instructed 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr, saying, "Don't bury me with them. Bury me with my companions in (the cemetery of) al-Baqi'. There I'll never receive inappropriate praise." I note that when al-Walid b. 'Abd al-Malik came to power, in the year 86 AH, he began building the Damascus mosque. He wrote to his governor in Medina, his cousin 'Umar b. 'Abd al-'Aziz, telling him to expand the Medina mosque. He did so, including its eastern side, and so the apartment of the Prophet (SAAS) became encompassed within it.
lessons: The simplicity of the Prophet's grave — flat, unmarked by grandeur — reflects the Islamic principle that true greatness lies in one's deeds and character, not in monuments built to one's memory.
Aisha's request to be buried separately so as not to receive inappropriate praise demonstrates the importance of sincerity and humility even in how one wishes to be remembered after death.
The preservation of knowledge about sacred sites across generations — as 'Urwa provided when others were confused — shows how vital it is to transmit religious and historical knowledge carefully.
practical_advice: Reflect on how you wish to be remembered: not through grand monuments but through the positive impact of your actions on those around you.
Practice humility by resisting the temptation to seek status or elevated recognition, even for genuine good deeds — sincerity means your intention is for God alone, not for human praise.
Take seriously your role in passing on knowledge, history, and values to the next generation; there will come a time when your knowledge fills a gap that no one else can fill.

title: The Changing Quality of Prayer After the Prophet's Death
summary: Umm Salama observed how the quality and focus of the companions in prayer gradually declined through the eras of the early caliphs, with dissension arising under Uthman causing people to look in all directions during prayer.
story: Ibn Maja stated that Ibrahim b. al-Mundhir al-Hizami related to us, quoting his uncle Muhammad b. Ibrahim b. al-Muttalib b. al-Sa'ib b. Abu Wada'a al-Sahmi, quoting Musa b. 'Abd Allah b. Abu Umayya al-Makhzumi, quoting Mus'ab b. 'Abd Allah, who quoted Umm Salama, daughter of Abu Umayya, the wife of the Prophet (SAAS), as having said, "At the time of the Messenger of God (SAAS), if someone arose to pray he would do so without allowing his sight to rise from the position of his feet. Then he died and there was Abu Bakr (in command) and if someone arose to pray, he would not allow his sight to move from the spot where his head rested (during prostration). Then Abu Bakr died and there was 'Umar (in command) and if someone arose to pray he would not allow his sight to move away from the qibla. Then 'Umar died and there was 'Uthman (in command) and dissension arose, and at that time people would look everywhere, to right and to left."
lessons: The quality of one's inner state is often reflected in the quality of one's worship; when hearts are troubled and distracted by worldly conflict, even prayer is affected.

The Prophet's era was one of unparalleled focus and presence in worship — his example sets the highest standard, and striving to return to that focus is always worthwhile regardless of the era we live in.
Community harmony and spiritual focus are deeply interconnected; when a community is at peace, individuals worship with greater presence of heart.
practical_advice: If you find your mind wandering during prayer or other spiritual practices, do not be discouraged — instead, use simple techniques like fixing your gaze on a point before you and consciously setting an intention before each act of worship.

When community conflicts or social tensions are affecting your spiritual life, make a conscious effort to create a personal sanctuary of calm — a time and space where you set aside external noise and focus solely on your relationship with God.
Use the example of the Prophet's companions in prayer as an aspirational standard: strive for presence, humility, and focus in worship, returning to it again and again whenever you drift.

title: The Date of the Prophet's Death and Burial — Scholarly Accounts
summary: Various chains of narration from companions and scholars confirm that the Prophet died on a Monday in Rabi al-Awwal and was buried between Tuesday and Wednesday, with slight variation among narrators.
story: Sa'id b. Mansur stated, from al-Darawardi, from Yazid b. 'Abd Allah b. Abu Yaman, from Umm Salama, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) died on Monday and was buried on Tuesday." Ibn Khuzayma stated that Muslim b. Hammad related to him, from his father, from 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar, from Kurayb, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) died on Monday and was buried on Tuesday." Al-Waqidi stated that Ubayd b. 'Ayyash b. Sahl b. Sa'd related to him, quoting his father as having said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) died on Monday and was buried on Tuesday night." Abu Bakr b. Abu al-Dunya quoted Muhammad b. Sa'd as having said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) died on Monday, 12 days after the beginning of Rabi' al-Awwal, and he was buried on Tuesday." 'Abd Allah b. Muhammad b. Abu al-Dunya stated that al-Hasan b. Isra'il Abu Muhammad al-Nahriti related to him, quoting 'Isa b. Yunus, from Isma'il b. Abu Khalid, who stated that he heard 'Abd Allah b. Abu Awfa say, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) died on Monday; and he was not buried until Wednesday." Sa'id b. al-Musayyab said the same, as did Abu Salama b. 'Abd al-Rahman and Abu Ja'far al-Baqir.
lessons: The care with which Islamic scholars preserved and transmitted even the precise details of the Prophet's death reflects the depth of love the community had for him and the seriousness with which they treated their trust.
Scholarly differences in narrated details do not undermine the core truth of events; they are evidence of honest transmission rather than fabrication.
Remembering the date and manner of the Prophet's passing is an act of love and connection that keeps his memory alive across generations.
practical_advice: When honoring the memory of a loved one, pay attention to the details of their life and passing — it is a form of respect and love to remember not just the broad strokes but the particulars.
When you encounter differing accounts of the same event among trustworthy sources, practice holding uncertainty with peace rather than anxiety — truth can often be approached from multiple angles.
Let the memory of those who have passed inspire you to live more intentionally, as the companions were inspired by the Prophet's life and death to hold fast to what he taught them.

title: Umm Ayman Weeps Over the Cessation of Divine Revelation
summary: After the Prophet's death, Abu Bakr and Umar visit Umm Ayman and find her weeping — not over the loss of the Prophet himself, but over the ending of divine revelation from heaven, which she described as fresh fruit that came daily and nightly.
story: b" al-Nagr al-Jarudi, both ofwhom said, "Al-I;!asan b. 'Ali al-KhuIani related to us, quoting 'Amr b. 'A~im al-Kilabi, quoting Sulayman b. al-Mughira, from Thabit, from Anas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) went to visit Umm Ayman and I accompanied him. She brought him a cordial. He was either fasting or did not want it, and he refused it. She then went over to him to make him laugh. After the death of the Prophet (SAAS), Abu Bakr said to 'Vmar, 'Let's go and visit Umm Ayman.' When we arrived to see her, she wept. The two men asked her, 'What is making you weep? Being with God is good for His Messenger.' She replied, 'I swear by God, I'm not weeping because I don't realize that to be with God is good for His Messenger; I'm weeping that revelation from heaven has ceased.' "She aroused them to tears, and they began weeping." Muslim alone (of the major scholars) narrated it, from Zuhayr b. I;!arb, from 'Amr b. 'A~im. MUsa b. 'Uqba stated, in his account of the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and of Abu Bakr's address on that occasion, as follows, "And the people came back after Abu Bakr had finished the address and there was Umm Ayman sitting weeping. She was asked, 'What is making you cry? God has honoured His Prophet (SAAS), taken him into His garden and given him rest from worldly cares.' She responded, 'I am only weeping for that news of heaven that used to come to us like a fresh fruit every day and night. Now it has been cut off and ended, and I weep for it.' "People were much impressed by her words."
lessons: Grief can be spiritually profound — Umm Ayman's tears were not a failure of faith but a recognition of a sacred loss, showing that mourning what is holy is itself a form of devotion.
True understanding of loss goes beyond the surface; Umm Ayman distinguished between accepting God's decree and grieving the ending of a blessed era, modeling nuanced emotional maturity.
Even the greatest companions wept together in times of loss, reminding us that communal grief and mutual support are not signs of weakness but of shared love and faith.
practical_advice: When grieving, take time to identify what specifically you are mourning — sometimes the deepest sadness is about the ending of something sacred, not just the loss itself, and naming that can bring clarity.
Visit those who are grieving, as Abu Bakr and Umar did; your presence matters even when you have no words, and shared tears can be a form of healing.

Allow yourself to grieve things that are genuinely worthy of grief — the ending of a blessed chapter in your life, a relationship, or a source of spiritual nourishment — without feeling that grief contradicts your faith.

title: The Prophet's Hadith: His Life and Death Are Both Good for His Nation
summary: The Prophet Muhammad taught that both his life and his death were a source of goodness for his nation — in life, their deeds were fulfilled; in death, their deeds would be presented to him and he would seek forgiveness for any evil he saw.
story: The I;afi" Abu Bakr al-Bazzar stated that Yusuf b. Musa related to him, quoting 'Abd ai-Majid b. 'Abd aI-'Aziz b. Abu Rawwad, from Suryan, from 'Abd Allah b. al-Sa'ib, from Zadhan, from 'Abd Allah - he being Ibn Mas'ud - who quoted the Prophet (SAAS), as having said, "God has angels who will travel all around and pass on to me greetings from my nation." The went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'My life is a good thing for you; you take action and it comes to pass for you. And my death is a good thing for you; your actions will be shown to me. For that which I have seen to be good I have praised God. For that which I have seen to be evil, I have asked God's forgiveness for you.'" AI-Bazzar then stated, "We know of the latter part of this I;adith being narrated from 'Abd Allah only from this line."
lessons: The Prophet's ongoing intercession and care for his nation even after death illustrates that divine mercy operates beyond the boundaries of time and life, encouraging believers to remain hopeful in God's forgiveness.
Knowing that one's deeds are witnessed by those who love us and will pray for us instills a sense of spiritual accountability grounded in love rather than fear alone.
The framing of both life and death as 'good' teaches believers to find meaning and blessing in all phases of existence, including endings.
practical_advice: When facing anxiety about your past mistakes, remind yourself that the Prophet actively sought forgiveness on behalf of his nation — channel that reminder into sincere repentance and renewed effort rather than spiraling guilt.
Send blessings upon the Prophet regularly, knowing that angels carry your salutations to him; this simple act can serve as a daily anchor of spiritual connection.
Reflect on how the people you love continue to carry goodness for you even after they are gone — let that motivate you to live in ways that honor their legacy.

title: The Prophet's Teaching on Praying for Him on Fridays and the Preservation of Prophets' Bodies
summary: The Prophet Muhammad taught that Friday is the best of days and instructed his companions to pray for him frequently on that day, assuring them that their prayers would reach him — and that God has forbidden the earth from consuming the bodies of prophets.
story: Imam Alpnad stated that I:Iusayo b. 'Ali al-Ju'fi related to him, from 'Abd al-Ralpnan b. Yazid b. Jabir, from Abu aI-Ash'ath al-San'ani, from Aws b. Aws, who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, "Friday is one of the best of your days. On it Adam was created and on it he died. On it (life began with) a breath, and on it (life will end with) a thunderbolt. Pray for me often on that day, for your prayer will be made known to me." People asked, "Messenger of God, how will our prayers be made known to you when you will have arimta, 'disintegrated'?" By this word he meant balita, "decayed". He replied, "God has forbidden the earth to eat the bodies of prophets, may peace be upon them." Abu Da'ud narrated it thus from Harlin b. 'Abd Allah, and from aI-I:Iasan b. 'Ali. AI-Nasa'i gave it from Is1;Iaq b. Man~ur, all three of them quoting it from I:Iusayn b. 'Ali. Ibn Maja narrated it from Abu Bakr b. Abu Shayba, from I:Iusayn b. 'Ali, from Ibn Jabir, from Abu aI-Ash'ath, from Shaddad b. Aws.
lessons: The institution of Friday as a day of heightened spiritual connection reminds believers that sacred time is a gift — certain moments carry more weight and should be used with intention.
The Prophet's assurance that prayers reach him even after death speaks to the continuity of the spiritual bond between the believer and the Prophet, offering comfort amid grief and separation.
The companions' honest question about how prayers could reach the Prophet shows that sincere curiosity about faith is welcomed and answered — doubt expressed openly leads to deeper knowledge.
practical_advice: Establish a Friday practice of sending abundant blessings upon the Prophet; use it as a weekly spiritual reset to realign your intentions and feel connected to your faith community.
When grief or loss makes you feel cut off from those you love, remember that spiritual bonds are not severed by death — prayer and remembrance keep those connections alive.
Use the structure of sacred time — weekly, daily, or seasonal — as an emotional anchor; having dedicated moments of reflection can reduce feelings of anxiety and being overwhelmed.

title: The Prophet's Final Illness: Gabriel Visits and the Angel of Death Seeks Permission
summary: During the Prophet's final illness, Gabriel visited him three times asking how he was, then brought the Angel of Death who — uniquely for the Prophet — asked permission before taking his soul, and the Prophet chose to proceed upon Gabriel's reminder that God longed to meet him.
story: The baji" al-Bayhaqi stated that Abu Isi).aq Ibrahim b. Mui).ammad al-Faqih informed him, quoting Shafi' b. Mui).ammad, quoting Abu Ja'far b. Salama al-Tai).awi, quoting al-Mizzi, quoting al-Shafi'i, from al-Qasim b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar b. Bafs, from Ja'far b. Mui).ammad, from his father, that some men of Quraysh went in to·visit his father, 'Ali b. al-Busayn. The latter said, "Should I talk to you about the Messenger of God (SAAS)?" Please do, they asked. And so he related to us about Abu al-Qisim - the Prophet (SAAS) - saying, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) became ill, Gabriel came to him and said, 'Mui).ammad, God has dispatched me to you to express special honour and respect for you, to ask you about what He knows better than yourself: How are you?' 'Gabriel, I am much afflicted. Gabriel, I am in agony.' "Gabriel returned to him the second day, asked him the same and the Prophet (SAAS) responded to him as he had on the first day. Gabriel then came to him a third day, asking as he had on the first, and the response was the same. Then he came to him accompanied by an angel named Isma'!l, who had charge of 100,000 angels, each angel in charge of another 100,000 angels. He asked to introduce him, and he (the Prophet (SAAS)) asked about him. Gabriel told him, 'This is the angel of death who asks permission to visit you, having never asked such permission ofany human being before yourself, and never will of any person after you.' The Prophet (SAAS) responded, 'Allow him in', and Gabriel did so. "He (Isma'!l) entered and said, 'Mui).ammad, God has sent me to you. If you order me to take your soul, I will do so. If you order me to leave you alone, I will do so.' 'Would you really do that, angel of death?' he asked. 'Yes,' he replied. 'That was what I was ordered, and I was ordered to obey you.' "The Prophet (SAAS) looked over at Gabriel and Gabriel said to him, 'Mui).ammad, God is longing to meet you.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said to the angel of death, 'Proceed as you were ordered.' And he took away his soul."
lessons: The Prophet's physical suffering during his final illness reminds believers that pain and hardship are not signs of God's displeasure — even the most beloved of creation experienced agony, and this dignifies the experience of all who suffer.

The Angel of Death asking the Prophet's permission illustrates that God honored His Prophet with agency even in the moment of death — a reminder that believers approach God not as coerced subjects but as willing servants.
The Prophet's choice to proceed upon hearing that 'God is longing to meet you' models the highest form of trust and love in God: choosing the divine meeting over continued earthly life.
practical_advice: When experiencing physical or emotional pain, remind yourself that suffering is not abandonment — Gabriel's repeated visits to check on the Prophet show that even in agony, one is seen, honored, and cared for.

Reflect on what it means to be 'ready to meet God' — cultivate a life of deeds and intentions you would not be ashamed to present, so that the idea of returning to God brings comfort rather than dread.
In moments where you feel you have no choice or agency, remember that even the greatest transitions in life can be approached with dignity and willing surrender to what is best.

title: Condolences at the Death of the Prophet – Speeches and Consolation of the Companions
summary: After the Prophet Muhammad's death, his Companions gathered weeping around him. Various narrations describe consoling words offered to them, including a visit attributed to al-Khidr, urging the bereaved to trust in God and seek His reward.
story:
you, people of the house, and mercy, and His blessings. In God there is consolation from all disaster and permanence from all transience. Put your trust in God and in Him hope. Those truly deprived are those deprived of reward. Peace be upon you all, and God's mercy and blessings.' " AI-Bayhaqi then stated, "Even though one of these two lines of transmission is weak, the one does substantiate the other, and shows that it originates from the I;adith ofJa'far. But God knows best."

Abu 'Abd Allah al-I;!afi? informed us, quoting Abu Bakr A!)mad b. Balawayh, quoting Mu!)ammad b. Bishr b. Matar, quoting Kiimil b. Tal!)a, quoting 'Abbad b. 'Abd aI-Samad, from Anas b. Malik, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) died, his Companions stared at him and gathered around him weeping. Then a large, handsome man with a white beard arrived who watched them and wept. He then turned to the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and said, 'There is in God consolation for every disaster, compensation for everything that passes away, and substitute for all that perishes. Return to God and express to Him your wishes. His gaze is towards you in your distress, so look. The affiicted are those not restored.' He then left. "They asked one another, 'Do you know this man?' Abu Bakr and 'Ali replied, 'Yes; this is aI-Khiqr, the brother of the Messenger of God (SAAS).'"

AI-Bayhaqi then stated, "'Ayyad b. 'Abd aI-Samad is a weak source. And this (tradition) is extremely objectionable."

AI-I;!arith b. Abu Usama narrated from Mu!)ammad b. Sa'd, quoting Hashim b. aI-Q!i.sim, quoting Sali!) aI-Mizzi, from Abu I;!azim aI-Madani, that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) was taken away by God, the Almighty and Glorious, the muhiijiriin went in in groups, praying over him and then leaving. The an~iir would go in next and do the same, then the people of Medina followed. After the men had finished, the women went in. From among them there came cries of the kind that occurred in the past. Then they heard a thud inside the house and knew and kept silent. Suddenly a voice spoke, "In God there is consolation from all that passes away and compensation from all disaster, substitute for all that perishes. The person restored is one whom the reward (of God) has restored; the one (truly) affiicted is he whom the reward does not restore."
lessons: Grief is a natural human response to loss, but true consolation comes from turning to God rather than remaining lost in sorrow.
The measure of one's loss is not the absence of the beloved but whether one loses the eternal reward that comes from patience and faith.
Even in the depths of communal grief, the community of believers supports one another and reminds each other of God's enduring presence.
practical_advice: When struck by grief or loss, consciously remind yourself that God is aware of your distress and that turning to Him in prayer and supplication is the most healing action you can take.
Seek out or offer words of sincere consolation to those who are grieving, as the community's collective support lightens individual burdens.
Reflect on whether your response to hardship is drawing you closer to God and His reward, or pulling you away — let that reflection guide how you grieve.

title: The People of the Book's Knowledge of the Prophet's Death
summary: Jarir b. Abdullah al-Bajali recounts meeting two Yemeni leaders who foreknew of the Prophet's death, and a high priest who confirmed the Prophet died on Monday, demonstrating that even non-Muslims had knowledge of his prophethood.
story:
Abu Bakr b. Abu Shayba stated that 'Abd Allah b. Idris related to him, from Ismii'il b. Abu KhaIid, from Qlys b. AbU I;!azim, from Jarir b. 'Abd Allah al-Bajali, who said, "I was in Yemen and met two men from there, (one named) Dhu Kala', the other Dhu 'Amr. I began talking with them about the Messenger of God (SAAS). They told me, 'If what you say is true, your master passed away three days ago.' "I continued on, as did they with me. On the road a party travelling from Medina appeared before us. We questioned them and they told us, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) has passed away and Abu Bakr was appointed his successor; the people are fme.' "

He went on, "The two Yemenis then said to me, 'Tell your master that we came; perhaps we will return, if God the Almighty and Glorious so wills it.' "The two men did return to Yemen: When I arrived, I informed Abu Bakr what they had said. He asked, 'Why did you not bring them?' "Later, Dhu 'Amr said to me, 'Janr, lowe you a favour; I'm going to inform you of something. You Arab people will continue to be fine for so long as whenever one of your leaders dies you appoint another to power. If, however, it happens by the sword, you'll end up a kingdom, enduring the rage of the kings and being pleased when they so please.'"

Imam AQrnad narrated it thus, as did aI-BukharI, from Abu Bakr b. Abu Shayba. Al-Bayhaqi also narrated it thus, from al-I::Iakim, from 'Abd Allah b. Ja'far, from Ya'qub b. Sufyan.

AI-BayhaqI stated that aI-I::Iakim informed him, quoting 'AlI b. aI-Mutawakkil, quoting Mul:>ammad b. Yusuf, quoting Ya'qub b. Isl:>aq al-I::Iaqrami, quoting Za'ida, from Ziyad b. 'Alaqa, from Jarir, who said, "A high priest met me in Yemen and told me, 'If your master was a prophet, he died on Monday.'" That is how aI-Bayhaqi narrated it.

Imam AQrnad stated that Abu Sa'Id related to him, quoting Za'ida, quoting Ziyad b. 'Alaqa, from JarIr, who said, "A high priest in Yemen told me, 'If your master was a prophet, he died today.'" Jarir added, "And he died on Monday."
lessons: The truth of prophethood transcends religious boundaries — even those outside the faith possessed knowledge that confirmed the Prophet's status.
Leadership built on principled succession and consultation is more durable than leadership seized by force.
God's signs are often visible to those who seek truth sincerely, regardless of their background.
practical_advice: When you feel confused about your faith or doubt creeps in, look for the convergence of signs across multiple sources — truth tends to confirm itself from unexpected directions.
Reflect on the wisdom of peaceful, principled transitions of authority in any organization or community you are part of, and advocate for such processes.
Pay attention to wise counsel even when it comes from unexpected or unfamiliar sources, as truth is not the exclusive property of any single group.

title: Ka'b b. Adi's Faith Strengthened Through a Monk's Ancient Text
summary: Ka'b b. Adi, who had accepted Islam after meeting the Prophet, wavered when news of the Prophet's death caused his companions to doubt. His conviction was restored when a monk's ancient scripture confirmed the Prophet's description and predicted time of death.
story:
AI-BayhaqI stated that Abu al-I::Iusayn b. Bishran al-Mu'addil of Baghdad informed him, quoting Abu Ja'far Mul:>ammad b. 'Amr, quoting Mul:>ammad b. al-Haytham, quoting Sa'Id b. Kathir b. 'Ufayr, quoting 'Abd al-I::Iamid b. Ka'b b. 'Alqama b. Ka'b b. 'Adl al-Tanukhi, from 'Amr b. al-I::Iarith, from Na'Im b. Ajbal, from Ka'b b. 'AdI, who said, "I arrived in a delegation from the people of aI-I::Iira to the Prophet (SAAS), and he explained Islam to us. We accepted Islam and then left for al-I::IIra. "Soon news reached us of the death of the Prophet (SAAS). My friends were confused by this and said, 'Well, if he were a prophet, he would not have died!' I replied, 'The prophets before him died.' So I remained firm in my Islam. Later I left for Medina and passed by a monk without whose advice we never decided anything. I asked him, 'Would you advise me about something that is bothering me?' He replied, 'Bring me some name.' I brought him a bone dice. He said, 'Cast it into this text', referring to a text he had brought out. So I cast the bone into it. He then leafed through the text and came upon the description of the Prophet (SAAS), just as I had seen him. And it was foretold that he would die at the time his death actually occurred.

"And so my conviction in my faith was strengthened. I went to Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, and told him of this, then I remained there with him. After that he dispatched me to Chosroe, and I later returned. 'Vmar b. al-Khanab also dispatched me to him and I delivered to him his ('Vmar's) letter. Then the battle of ¥ armuk took place, without my having knowledge of it. He (Chosroe) asked me, 'Do you know that the Byzantines have killed and routed the Arabs?' I replied, 'Not possible!' 'Why not?' he asked. I replied, 'God promised His Prophet that he would give him victory over all religions, and He is not one to break His promise.' He told me, '¥our Prophet spoke the truth to you. The Byzantines were killed. And God did kill 'Ad.' He then asked me about the most important Companions of the Messenger of God ($AAS), and I told him of them. He gave gifts to 'Vmar and to them also. Those to whom he made gifts were 'Ali, 'Abd al-Ral:unan and al-Zubayr."
lessons: Holding firm to one's faith in a moment of communal doubt requires both individual courage and the ability to reason from established principles.
God's promises do not expire with the death of His messenger — the fruits of the Prophet's mission continued to unfold long after his passing.
External confirmation can sometimes strengthen internal conviction, but the seeds of that conviction must already exist within the believer.
practical_advice: When those around you express doubt about your beliefs or values, ground yourself in the broader context — ask what principles and precedents speak to the question before allowing doubt to take hold.
Seek knowledge and counsel from reliable sources when you feel confused, but ultimately measure what you find against your established understanding of truth.
When you feel your faith or confidence being tested by unexpected news or events, remind yourself of the promises that have already been fulfilled as evidence for those yet to come.

title: The Apostasy of Arab Tribes After the Prophet's Death and Abu Bakr's Response
summary: Following the Prophet's death, many Arab tribes apostatized, Judaism and Christianity became emboldened, hypocrisy spread, and the Muslims felt like lost sheep — until God united them under Abu Bakr, who restored them to Islam.
story:
Mu/:tanunad b. Is/:taq stated, "When the Messenger of God ($AAS) died, the Arab tribes seceded. Judaism and Christianity became arrogant and hypocrisy spread. The Muslims became like sheep in the rain on a winter's night at loss of their Prophet ($AAS), until God united them around Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him."

Ibn Hisham stated, "Abu 'Ubayda and other scholars related to me that when the Messenger of God ($AAS) died, most of the Meccans considered leaving Islam - and indeed were intending to do so. 'Attab b. Usayd, may God be pleased with him, was afraid of them and went into hiding. But then Suhayl b. 'Amr, may God be pleased with him, arOse, gave praise and thanks to God, then made reference to the death of the Messenger of God ($AAS). He said, 'That event has only increased the strength of Islam; and we will behead anyone doubting it!' And so the Meccans reconsidered and gave up what they had been considering. 'Attab b. Usayd reappeared. This is the position the Messenger of God ($AAS) prophesied that Suhayl would take, as shown by his words to 'Vmar b. al-Khanab. When, that is, 'Vmar made reference to pulling out Suhayl's two front teeth when he was among the prisoners at the batde ofBadr, the Messenger of God (SAAS), had said, 'He may well take a position for which you certainly won't blame him!'"

We have recounted elsewhere the apostacy that occurred in many Arab tribes after the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and events involving Musaylima b. I:Iabib, the self-professed prophet in 31-Yamama, and the uprising of al-Aswad al-'Ansi in Yemen. We have there shown what became of these people, until they retracted and came back to God, expressing repentance and regret for the great stupidity and ignorance they had displayed in seceding, attitudes to which they had been seduced by Satan. God then gave them aid and settled them down, restoring them to His true religion at the hands of his caliph, Abu Bakr, "the trusting", may God be pleased with him.
lessons: Even the most widespread communal collapse can be reversed when strong, principled leadership steps forward with clarity and courage.
The Prophet's prophetic insight into individuals — such as his prediction about Suhayl — demonstrates that a true leader sees potential in people that others overlook.
Moments of collective crisis reveal who possesses genuine conviction versus who was attached to a person or worldly advantage rather than the truth itself.
practical_advice: When your community or organization faces a moment of collective panic or loss of direction, look for those with steady resolve and rally around them rather than following the crowd toward retreat.
Do not judge someone's ultimate character solely by their worst moment — people who were once opponents or doubters can become the firmest supporters when it matters most.
In times of widespread confusion or moral drift, recommit yourself to core principles rather than letting the behavior of the crowd define your own choices.

title: Hassan b. Thabit's Elegy Mourning the Prophet at His Grave in Madinah
summary: Hassan b. Thabit, the Prophet's companion and poet, composed one of the most eloquent elegies mourning the Messenger of God, describing his grief at the Prophet's grave in Madinah and his inability to fully enumerate the Prophet's blessings and wonders.
story:
Ibn Isl:taq and others quote poems by I:Iassan b. Thabit, may God be pleased with him, on the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS). One of the finest and most eloquent of these is that narrated by 'Abd ai-Malik b. Hisham, may God be pleased with him, from Abu Zayd al-An~ari, in which I:Iassan b. Thabit, may God be pleased with him, mourns the Messenger of God (SAAS):

"At Tayba traces remain of the Messenger, and a light~emitting locality, though traces may often fade and disappear,
The signs of a sacred building with the minbar the guide would mount will never disappear.
It signs are clear, its features lasting, and that home with a chapel and a mosque.
There are chambers amidst which light from God would descend, giving illumination and fire,
Knowledge that will never be effaced, signs which, if removed become renewed.
There I knew the Messenger's design and commission and a grave in whose dust he was hidden and buried.
I remained there weeping for the Messenger, though the eyes of the spirits were delighted,
Recalling those blessings of the Messenger. I see my soul unable to enumerate and so is confused.
My spirit is tonnented, drained by loss of Al;tmad, as it goes on enumerating the wonders of the Messenger.
It cannot manage to account for one-tenth of all his deeds, and my soul has still more to suffer.
I stood long there at the mound of that grave containing Al).mad, my eyes weeping all they could."
lessons: Poetry, art, and creative expression are legitimate and honoured ways of processing grief and preserving the memory of those we love.
The legacy of a great person outlasts their physical presence — their knowledge, their light, and the signs of their mission endure and are renewed.
Acknowledging that we cannot fully comprehend or express the greatness of those we have lost is itself a form of humble gratitude.
practical_advice: When words fail you in grief, allow yourself to express your feelings through writing, poetry, or another creative form — this is a time-honoured human practice that can bring genuine relief.
Visit or revisit the places and memories connected to those you have lost as a way of maintaining connection and finding comfort in the traces they have left behind.
Rather than feeling overwhelmed by trying to fully honour someone's legacy, begin with one blessing or one lesson from their life and let gratitude for that one thing anchor your remembrance.

title: Elegy and Mourning Poems for the Prophet Muhammad
summary: Companions and poets composed heartfelt elegies mourning the death of Prophet Muhammad, expressing grief at the loss of revelation, divine guidance, and the most noble of men.
story: and buried. I remained there weeping for the Messenger, though the eyes of the spirits were delighted, Recalling those blessings of the Messenger. I see my soul unable to enumerate and so is confused. My spirit is tonnented, drained by loss of Al;tmad, as it goes on enumerating the wonders of the Messenger. It cannot manage to account for one-tent4 of all his deeds, and my soul has still more to suffer. I stood long there at the mound of that grave containing Al).mad, my eyes weeping all they could. You are blessed indeed, grave of the Messenger, and blessed you, that land where the wise and rightly-guided one resided. Blessed be that grave that contains a good man, over which stands a structure of broad stones in tiers. Hands poured earth over him, eyes their tears, and at that the stars set. They sent away wisdom, knowledge and mercy, that night they covered him, pillowless, with dirt. Then they left sadly, their Prophet not among them, their backs and arms feeling weak, Weeping for one whose death-day the heavens and the earth mourn, and the people were more sad. Could ever mourning for a dead man equal that of the day when Mul;tammad died? A day when the site of revelation was taken from them, a source oflight that gave benefit and help. Leading to the Merciful One those who imitated him, saving them from the horror of shame, guiding aright, An imam for them energetically leading them to the truth, a teacher of truth; those obeying him found happiness, Forgiving their errors, accepting their excuse; if they behaved well, God was very generous with reward. If disaster struck them too hard to bear, from Him came ease for what pained them While they enjoyed God's favour, a guide amidst them to show the way to be followed. It hurt him if they strayed from the true path, and he was eager that they go straight in right guidance. He was sympathetic towards them, not excluding any from his protection, leading them towards shelter. While there in that light an arrow sent by death struck into their light, And so Mal:unud was sent back to God while the angels mourned him while giving praise. The sites in the land of the sacred places became desolate at the absence of the revelation it had known, Deserted, except for that spot with the grave where our long-lost friend rests, between Bahl.t and Gharqad And his mosque and those places deserted at his loss are an empty area where once he stood and prostrated. And the great stoning mound there, the houses, vacant land, encampments and birth-sites miss him. Weep tears, eye, for the Messenger of God, and may I never know you with your tears dried up! What is wrong with you, eye, that you do not weep for that generous man whose ample robe encompassed the people? Shed tears copiously and mourn aloud at loss of him the like of whom will never again exist. The ancients never lost the like of Mul;t.ammad, and his like will never be mourned again till Judgement Day, Nor anyone more kind or trustworthy in obligation after obligation, nor anyone more generous, one never ill-tempered. Nor anyone more munificent with wealth both newly acquired and long-held; while others are generous, some are niggardly with their inheritance. Nor anyone more noble in descent in the clans when ancestry is checked, not more noble in ancestry to leaders of the (Mecca) valley, Nor anyone more fortressed by eminence, nor more secure in a prominence that is supported by towering supports. Nor anyone more firm in his roots and his branches and his trunk, watered by rain clouds, his trunk strong and supple. It was an illustrious Lord who reared him, bringing him to perfection in the most fine of deeds. Instruction for the Muslims rested with him, and knowledge was not kept back, nor was opinion disaproved. I say, and no one could complain at my words, unless devoid of sense and out of touch, That it is not my intent to refrain from praising him; it may be that I will reside in paradise forever, With the Chosen One, whose aid 1 hope thereto; and to attain that day I will constantly strive.
lessons: Grief expressed through poetry and remembrance is a healthy and honored tradition in Islam; mourning the righteous is itself an act of love.
The Prophet's greatness was not in worldly power but in his guidance, mercy, and embodiment of divine instruction — qualities that outlast any individual life.
True loss calls us to reflect on what we valued most in someone, deepening our own commitment to the values they represented.
practical_advice: When grieving someone beloved, try writing or reading poetry and reflective prose to give shape and expression to your emotions rather than suppressing them.
Reflect on the qualities you most admired in those you have lost and ask yourself how you can carry those qualities forward in your own life.
Allow yourself to weep and mourn openly; Islam validates grief as a natural human response, and expressing it honestly is part of healing.

title: Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith's Elegy for the Prophet
summary: Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith, a companion and relative of the Prophet, composed a moving elegy lamenting the death of the Prophet and the enormity of the Muslims' loss.
story: The hajiz, Abu al-~sim aI-SuhaylJ stated at the end of his book al_Rawaq. that Abu Sufyan b. aI-I;Iarith Ibn 'Abd a1-Munalib said, in mourning over the Messenger of God ($AAS), "I lay awake, my night endless, and the night of the friend of one struck down is ever long. My weeping cheered me, and my mourning was little indeed compated to what the Muslims had lost. Our disaster was enormous, momentous, that evening when it was said that the Messenger had died. Our very earth, because of what had afflicted it, seemed ready to quake. We had lost the divine inspiration and its revelation among us, that had been brought by Gabriel as he came to and fro. That man was fully worthy to have the souls of the people flow out to him, or almost so. A Prophet who would lift doubt away from us, by what was revealed to him, and what he said. He guided us, and we never feared going astray, the Messenger being our guide. Fa:tun(a), you have excuse indeed to mourn, but if you do not, that is the (right) path. For the grave of your father is the most important grave of all, and in it lies the most important of men, the Messenger."
lessons: Even the most devoted companions experienced the dark night of grief and sleeplessness; such feelings are a testament to love, not a sign of weak faith.
The Prophet's greatest gift to humanity was guidance — lifting doubt and showing the straight path — a legacy that continues beyond his physical life.
Acknowledging the enormity of a loss honestly, as Abu Sufyan did, is more spiritually mature than pretending grief does not exist.
practical_advice: If you find yourself awake at night overwhelmed by grief or loss, know this is a shared human experience; use those quiet hours for reflection, supplication, and remembrance of God.
When facing a loss that feels catastrophic, try to articulate what specifically has been lost — the guidance, the companionship, the wisdom — as naming the loss helps begin to process it.
Find comfort in the legacy and teachings of those you have lost; their influence and guidance can continue to light your path even in their absence.

title: The Prophet Left No Material Inheritance
summary: Multiple narrations confirm that the Prophet Muhammad died leaving no dinar, dirham, slave, livestock, or material wealth as inheritance, having dedicated everything to God's cause.
story: AI-Bukhari stated that Qutayba related to him, quoting Abu aI-AI)Wat;, from Abu Isl:taq, from 'Amr b. al-Barith, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not leave a dinar, a dirham, a slave, a slave-girl- nothing except his white mule that he used to ride, his weapons and land that he left as a charitable bequest for wayfarers." AI-Bukhari is alone in giving this; Muslim did not. AI-Bukhari narrated it in various places in his $altilt collection, from various lines of transmission from Abu al-Ahwa~, Sufyan al-Thawrl and Zuhayr b. Mu'awiya. AI-Tirmidhi narrated it from a ltadith of Isra'il. AI-Nasa'i gave it also, from a ltadith ofYunus b. Abu Isl:taq, all of them quoting from Abu Isl:taq. 'Amr b. 'Abd Allah aI-Sabi'!, from 'Amr b. al-Barith b. al-Mu~taliq b. Abu Dinar, the brother ofJuwayriyya, daughter of aI-Barith, "the mother ofthe Believers", may God be pleased with them both. Imam Al:tmad stated that Abu Mu'awiya related to him, quoting al-A'mash and Ibn Numayr, from al-A'mash, from Shaqiq, from Masruq, from 'A'isha, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not leave a dinar, a dirham, a sheep, a camel, nor did he leave testamentary statements over anything." Muslim is alone in narrating it, aI-Bukh3.rl not having given it. Abu Da'ud, aI-Nasa'i and Ibn Maja gave it from several lines from Sulayman b. Mahran ai-A'mash, from Shaqiq b. Salama Abu Wa'il, from Masruq b. aI-Ajda', from the mother of the Believers, 'A'isha, "the trusting lady", the daughter of (Abu Bakr) "the trusting", the beloved of him beloved by God, the lady declared innocent from above the seven heavens, may God be pleased with her and give her pleasure. Imam Al.unad stated that Is!.laq b. Yusuf related to him, from Sufyan, from 'A~im, from Zirr b. I:Iubaysh, from 'A'isha, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not leave a d,nar, a dirham, a slave-girl, a slave, a sheep or a cameL" It is established in both ~a~,~ collections, from a ~adith of al-A'mash, from Ibrahim, from al-Aswad, from 'A'isha, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) bought food from a Jew for a given length of time and gave as security an iron chain-mail. In the version given by al-Bukhari, he narrated it from Qibi~a, from al-ThaWri, from al-A'mash, from Ibrahim, from al-Aswad, from 'A'isha, may God be pleased with her, who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) died while his armour was mortgaged to a Jew fot 30." Al-Bayhaqi narrated it from a ~adith ofYazid b. Harun, from al-Thawri, from al-A'mash, from Ibrahlm, from al-Aswad, who quoted her as having said, "The Prophet (SAAS), died while his armour was mortgaged for 30 sa' of barley."
lessons: The Prophet's complete detachment from material wealth demonstrates that true greatness lies in character, guidance, and service — not in worldly accumulation.
Leaving the world with clean hands and a clear conscience, having given everything in service to others, is among the highest spiritual achievements.
The Prophet's example teaches that wealth and possessions are means, not ends, and that contentment with simplicity is a form of spiritual freedom.
practical_advice: When anxious about financial security or material provision, reflect on the Prophet's life as a reminder that the most blessed life is not necessarily the most materially wealthy one.
Practice regular detachment from material possessions by giving in charity, even small amounts, to cultivate the inner freedom that comes from not being enslaved to wealth.
Ask yourself periodically: what legacy am I building that is not material? What guidance, kindness, or knowledge will I leave behind?

title: The Prophet's Armour Mortgaged for Food and His Family's Simplicity
summary: At the time of his death, the Prophet's armour was mortgaged to a Jewish man for barley to feed his family, and his household had no stored provisions, illustrating his extraordinary simplicity and trust in God.
story: Al-Bayhaqi went on to state that 'Ali b. A!.lmad b. 'Abdan informed him, quoting Abu Bakr Mu!.lammad b. I:Iammuwayh al-'Askari, quoting Ja'far b. Mu!.lammad al-Qalanisi, quoting Adam, quoting Shayban, from Qitada, from Anas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was invited to a meal of barley bread and rancid fat." Anas went on, "And I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, 'By Him who holds the life of MuJ:1ammad in his hands, MuJ:1ammad's family has no longer a sac's weight of wheat or of dates.' At that time he had nine wives. He had pawned a coat of armour he had to a Jew in Medina in exchange for food; and he had not found money to redeem it up to his death."
lessons: The Prophet accepted material hardship without complaint or shame, demonstrating that dignity comes from one's relationship with God, not one's financial circumstances.
Even at the height of his leadership over a vast Muslim community, the Prophet maintained a lifestyle of profound simplicity, showing that power and luxury need not go hand in hand.
Contentment and gratitude in difficult material circumstances, as modeled by the Prophet, is itself a form of worship and a source of inner peace.
practical_advice: When facing financial stress or scarcity, remind yourself that the Prophet himself experienced lack without losing his dignity, faith, or sense of purpose.
Practice gratitude for whatever food and shelter you have, however simple, recognizing that sufficiency — not abundance — is the standard the Prophet lived by.
If you feel embarrassed by financial hardship, remember that the leader of all humanity pawned his armour for a meal of barley bread; there is no shame in honest need.

title: The Prophet Gazes at Mount Uhud and Speaks of Wealth
summary: The Prophet looked toward Mount Uhud and expressed that he would not wish for a mountain of gold to remain after his death, preferring instead to spend everything in God's cause.
story: Imam AJ:1mad stated that 'Abd a1-Samad related to him, quoting Thabit, quoting Hilal, from qkrama, from Ibn 'Abbas who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) gazed towards Mt. UJ:1ud and said, 'By Him who holds my soul in His hand, how it would please me if Mt. UJ:1ud belonged to MuJ:1ammad's people and I could
lessons: The Prophet's longing to spend rather than hoard any wealth reflects an active, generous orientation toward life rather than an accumulative one.
Wealth is most meaningful when it is channeled toward others; the Prophet's instinct was always to give, not to hold.
Reflecting on what we would do with unlimited resources reveals our true values and priorities in life.
practical_advice: When you receive a financial blessing, ask yourself how a portion of it can benefit others before focusing solely on personal use.
Use moments of abundance as opportunities to increase in generosity, following the Prophet's instinct that wealth is best when it flows outward.
Periodically reflect on your relationship with money and possessions: are they a source of anxiety and attachment, or do you hold them lightly in service of larger purposes?

title: The Prophet's Pledged Armour and Ascetic Death
summary: At the time of his death, the Prophet Muhammad left behind no wealth, having pledged his armour to a Jew for food, and expressed his indifference to worldly possessions by likening himself to a traveller resting briefly under a tree.
story: had pawned a coat of armour he had to a Jew in Medina in exchange for food; and he had not found money to redeem it up to his death. Ibn Maja narrated some of this from a hadith of Sufyan b. 'Abd al-Rahman al-Nahwi, from Qutada. Imam Ahmad stated that 'Abd al-Samad related to him, quoting Thabit, quoting Hilal, from 'Ikrama, from Ibn 'Abbas who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) gazed towards Mt. Uhud and said, 'By Him who holds my soul in His hand, how it would please me if Mt. Uhud belonged to Muhammad's people and I could spend it in God's cause! The day I die I would have two dinars worth of it; these, however, I would set aside for debt.' "He did die, but did not leave a dinar, a dirham, a slave, nor a slave-born female. He left his armour still pledged to a Jew for thirty sa's weight of barley." Ibn Maja narrated the latter part of this, from 'Abd Allah b. Mu'awiya al-Jumahi, from Thabit b. Yazid, from Hilal b. Khabbab al-'Abdi al-Kufi. For the first part there is testimony in the sahih collections from a hadith of Abu Dharr, may God be pleased with him. Imam Ahmad stated that 'Abd al-Samad, Abu Sa'id and 'Affan related to him, quoting Thabit - Ibn Yazid, that is - quoting Hilal - Ibn Khabbab, that is - from 'Ikrama, from Ibn 'Abbas, that 'Umar came in to see the Prophet (SAAS), while he was lying on a straw mat that had left a mark on his side. 'Umar asked, 'Prophet of God, should you not have a more comfortable bed than this?' He replied, 'I'm not concerned with worldly things; I am merely like a rider travelling on a summer's day, resting for a while during the day time beneath a tree, then moving away and leaving it behind.' Ahmad is alone in giving this. Its line of transmission is excellent.
lessons: True wealth is measured in one's relationship with God and contribution to others, not in material accumulation.
Contentment with little and detachment from worldly possessions is a source of inner peace and spiritual freedom.
A leader's integrity is demonstrated not through power or wealth but through personal sacrifice and simplicity.
practical_advice: Reflect on one item you are overly attached to this week and consider how your life would feel if you let go of that attachment.
When feeling financial stress, remember that the most honoured person in Islamic history died without a dirham, yet left a legacy that transformed the world — your worth is not your net worth.
Practice the 'traveller's mindset': treat today's comforts as temporary rest stops, not permanent possessions, to reduce anxiety about losing them.

title: The Prophet's Only Bequest: The Book of God
summary: When asked whether the Prophet left a will, his companion Ibn Abi Awfa confirmed he left no material bequest, for his only testament to his people was the Book of God.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Abu Nu'aym related to him, quoting Malik b. Mighwal, from Talha, who said, "I asked 'Abd Allah b. Abi Awfa, 'Did the Prophet (SAAS) leave a will?' 'No,' he replied. I asked, 'Why was it, then, recorded that the people should make wills or that they were ordered to do so?' He replied, 'He bequeathed the Book of God, Almighty and Glorious is He.'" Al-Bukhari, Muslim, and all the orthodox scholars, except Abu Da'ud, narrated this, from several lines of transmission, from Malik b. Mi'wal. Al-Tirmidhi stated, "It is hasan sahih gharib; we know of it only from the hadith of Malik b. Mighwal."
lessons: The greatest inheritance a person can leave is guidance, values, and wisdom — not material wealth.
The Quran, as the Prophet's bequest, reminds us that divine guidance is the most enduring gift across generations.
Prioritising spiritual legacy over material legacy reflects a profound understanding of what truly lasts.
practical_advice: Ask yourself: if you were to leave the world today, what non-material legacy — values, knowledge, kindness — would you want to pass on to those you love?
Spend time this week engaging with the Quran not just as recitation but as a living bequest and personal letter from God to you.
Write down three principles or values you want to embody and pass on to your family or community, regardless of your material wealth.

title: The Prophet's Estate Declared Charity: The Principle of No Prophetic Inheritance
summary: The Prophet declared that his heirs would inherit no money or property, and that whatever he left beyond basic provisions for his wives and employees would be treated as charity for the Muslims.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Sufyan related to him, from Abu al-Zinad, from al-A'raj, from Abu Hurayra to whom this evidence came. He once said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'My heirs will not inherit a dinar nor a dirham; whatever I leave, after the expenses for my wives and provision for my employees, is to be treated as a charity.' Al-Bukhari, Muslim and Abu Da'ud narrated it from various sources from Malik b. Anas, from Abu al-Zinad 'Abd Allah b. Dhakwan, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Hurmuz al-A'raj, from Abu Hurayra, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "My heirs will not inherit a dinar nor a dirham; whatever I leave, after the expenses for my wives and provision for my employees, is to be treated as a charity." This is the text of al-Bukhari. Al-Bukhari went on to state that 'Abd Allah b. Maslama related to him, from Malik, from Ibn Shihab, from 'Urwa, from 'A'isha, who stated that, following the death of the Prophet (SAAS), his wives wished to send 'Uthman to Abu Bakr for him to ask about their inheritances. 'A'isha said, "But did the Messenger of God (SAAS) not say, 'We will not leave (property for) inheritance; what we leave will be for charity'?" Muslim narrated it thus, from Yahya b. Yahya. Abu Da'ud gave it from al-Qa'nabi, al-Nasa'i from Qutayba. All of them quoted it from Malik. This comment was from one of the inheriting wives — if inheritance had been decreed — who acknowledged that the Messenger of God (SAAS) established his estate for charity, not inheritance. It is clear that the remainder of "the mothers of the Believers" agreed with what she narrated and remembered what had been said to them in that regard. Her expression shows that this was a matter well established among them. But God knows best.
lessons: Generosity that extends beyond one's lifetime — through charity and endowments — is among the most honourable acts a person can undertake.
The Prophet's example teaches us that communal welfare takes precedence over personal or familial gain.
True trust in God means releasing attachment to possessions and entrusting them to a cause greater than oneself.
practical_advice: Consider setting aside a portion of your wealth, however small, as ongoing charity (sadaqah jariyah) that will benefit others after your death.

If you are experiencing conflict over inheritance or shared resources within your family, reflect on the Prophet's example of prioritising communal good and ask: what outcome would bring the most benefit to all?
Practice releasing the need to accumulate by giving something away today that you have been holding onto 'just in case'.

title: Fatima's Claim to Fadak and Her Grief Over Abu Bakr's Refusal
summary: Fatima, the Prophet's daughter, approached Abu Bakr seeking her inheritance from the Prophet's land at Fadak and Khaybar, but Abu Bakr upheld the Prophet's declaration that prophets leave no inheritable wealth, causing Fatima to depart in anger and never speak to him again until her death.
story: Al-Bukhari also stated, under the section entitled, The statements made by the Messenger of God (SAAS) as follows, "'Abd Allah b. Muhammad related to us, quoting Hisham, quoting Ma'mar, from al-Zuhri, from 'Urwa, from 'A'isha, that Fatima and al-'Abbas went to Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, to receive their inheritance from the Messenger of God (SAAS). They were, at that time, seeking his land at Fadak and his share of Khaybar. Abu Bakr told them, 'I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, "Our property is not to be inherited; what we leave will be for charity. However, Muhammad's family may feed themselves from this property."' "Abu Bakr went on, 'I swear by God, I will never omit doing anything I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) doing.' "Fatima then left him and did not speak to him again from then to her death." Imam Ahmad narrated it thus from 'Abd al-Razzaq, from Ma'mar. Ahmad then narrated it from Ya'qub b. Ibrahim, from his father, from Salih b. Kaysan, from al-Zuhri, from 'Urwa, from 'A'isha, that following the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS), Fatima asked Abu Bakr for her inheritance from what he had left that God had provided him. Abu Bakr told her, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Our property is not to be inherited; what we leave will be for charity.'" Fatima grew angry and left Abu Bakr; she continued avoiding him up to her death. Ahmad stated, "Fatima lived on for six months following the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS)."
lessons: Grief and anger after loss are natural human responses, even among the most righteous; feeling these emotions does not diminish one's faith.
Leaders sometimes must uphold difficult principles even when those closest to them are hurt, and this requires immense moral courage.
Unresolved conflicts and emotional distance can persist even among good people; this is a reminder to seek reconciliation whenever possible before it is too late.
practical_advice: If you are holding onto anger or a sense of injustice after a loss, allow yourself to grieve fully while also seeking a trusted person — a counsellor, imam, or friend — to help you process the emotions without letting them calcify into permanent estrangement.
When you feel a decision has been made against you unfairly, try to separate your emotional pain from the factual question: is there a principle being upheld that, even if it hurts, may be correct?
If there is someone in your life from whom you are estranged, consider whether reconciliation is possible before circumstances make it permanent.

title: Ali's Delayed Pledge of Allegiance to Abu Bakr and Their Reconciliation
summary: After six months of tension following Fatima's death, Ali ibn Abi Talib sought reconciliation with Abu Bakr, acknowledged his right to leadership, and publicly pledged his allegiance, ending a painful period of political and personal distance.
story: Al-Bukhari narrated this hadith in his work Kitab al-Maghazi within his Sahih collection, from Ibn Bukayr, from al-Layth, from 'Aqil, from al-Zuhri, from 'Urwa, from 'A'isha, as is given above. And he added, "When she died, 'Ali buried her at night. He did not inform Abu Bakr; he did pray over her." ('A'isha stated,) "During the life of Fatima, 'Ali had great prestige among the people. When Fatima died, he lost this special position and set about making peace with Abu Bakr and pledging allegiance to him. He had not pledged allegiance for those months. So he sent a message to Abu Bakr, saying, 'Come to us, but don't bring anyone else.' He did not want 'Umar to come because of what he knew of 'Umar's severity. 'Umar said, (to Abu Bakr), 'Don't go in to them alone, by God!' Abu Bakr replied, 'What could they do to me? I shall go to them, by God!' "Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, then set off. 'Ali spoke the shahada and went on, 'We well know your virtue and what God has awarded you. We'll not envy you for the good to which God has led you. But you monopolized power, to which we thought, because of our close relationship to the Messenger of God (SAAS), we had a right to share.' 'Ali went on talking until Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, wept. The latter then said, 'By Him in whose hand is my soul, I would prefer to do good to relatives of the Messenger of God (SAAS), than to my own! Regarding the question of the properties that inspired antagonism between us, I am not inclining in this away from what is right; I would never omit doing anything that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had himself done.' "When Abu Bakr had finished performing the al-zuhr prayer, he mounted the minbar and spoke the shahada. He then made reference to 'Ali's having been late in pledging allegiance to him and the excuses he had made that had been accepted. 'Ali, may God be pleased with him, then spoke the shahada and emphasized Abu Bakr's right and went on to refer to his virtue and precedence. He stated that his actions had not been influenced by any jealousy towards Abu Bakr. He then went up to Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, and expressed his allegiance to him. The people gathered around 'Ali saying, 'You have done well!' 'Ali became very popular when he rejoined the common cause." Al-Bukhari also narrated this, as did Muslim, Abu Da'ud, and al-Nasa'i, from several lines of transmission, from al-Zuhri, from 'Urwa, from 'A'isha in similar form. This pledge of allegiance made by 'Ali, may God be pleased with him, to Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, came after the death of Fatima, may God be pleased with her.
