It affirmed the peace between them. It was subsequent to that pledge to which we referred first on that day in the hall, as was narrated by Ibn Khuzayma and declared authentic by Muslim b. al-Hajjaj. 'Ali had not been avoiding Abu Bakr for these six months, but had been performing the prayer behind him and attending al-mashwara, "the consultative assemblies", with him. He had also accompanied him to Dhu al-Qassa.
lessons: Reconciliation after conflict, even when delayed, is a profound act of courage and spiritual maturity.
Expressing grievances honestly and openly, as Ali did, is often the necessary first step toward healing a broken relationship.
A leader who approaches reconciliation with humility and tears, as Abu Bakr did, demonstrates that strength lies in compassion, not dominance.
practical_advice: If you have been distancing yourself from someone due to hurt or a sense of injustice, consider taking the first step toward reconciliation — even a small message saying you want to talk can begin the healing process.
When reconciling with someone, try to name your feelings and perceived wrongs clearly and calmly, as Ali did, rather than suppressing them — honest dialogue is more healing than forced forgiveness.
Reflect on whether any current tension in your relationships stems from grief rather than true wrongdoing; sometimes what looks like conflict is really unprocessed loss.

title: Ali's Relationship with Abu Bakr After Fatima's Death
summary: An account clarifying that Ali did not boycott Abu Bakr for six months after the Prophet's death, but continued praying behind him and attending consultative assemblies. It also recounts Abu Bakr's affectionate encounter with Hasan, Ali's son.
story: as was narrated by Ibn Khuzayrna and declared authentic by Muslim b. al-I:Iajjaj. 'Ali had not been avoiding Abu Bakr for these six months, but had been performing the prayer behind him and attending al-mashwara, "the consultative assemblies". with him. He had also accompanied him to Dhu al-Q!~~a. In the ~abib collection of al-BukhiiIi it is stated that Abu £lakr. lIla) God be pleased with him, performed the al-'a~r prayer some nights following the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and, leaving the mosque, found al-I:Iasan, 'Ali's son, playing with the other boys. He carried him on his back and began chanting, "Oh, by my father, the image of the Prophet, but not the image of 'Ali!" 'Ali was laughing. However, when this second pledge of allegiance took place, some narrators believed that 'Ali had not spoken a pledge before that, and denied that he had done so. But what is positively affIrmed takes precedence over what is negative, as we have shown and stated above. But God knows best.
lessons: Maintaining community bonds and fulfilling religious duties during personal grief reflects profound spiritual maturity.
Historical accounts must be weighed carefully, prioritizing what is positively affirmed over unverified negations.
Moments of warmth and humor between companions, even in times of grief, reveal the depth of human connection among the early Muslim community.
practical_advice: During personal conflicts or grief, continue fulfilling your communal and religious responsibilities rather than withdrawing entirely.
When navigating disagreements, seek verified information before drawing conclusions about others' behavior.
Look for small moments of warmth and connection with those around you, as they can ease tension and remind you of shared bonds.

title: Fatima's Estrangement from Abu Bakr Over Inheritance
summary: An examination of Fatima's estrangement from Abu Bakr following his refusal to grant her inheritance from the Prophet's property, based on the hadith that prophets leave no inheritable wealth, only charity.
story: As for the anger being expressed by Fatima, may God be pleased with her and give her pleasure, against Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him and give him pleasure, I cannot account for it. If it was due to his refusal to award her the inheritance for which she asked him, he had given her a reason that had to be accepted. This was his reference to the statement by her father, the Messenger of God ($AAS), who said, "Our property is not to be inherited; what we leave will be for charity." She was one of those who submitted to the text of the law-giver that had been unknown to her before she asked about inheritance, just as it was unknown to the wives of the Prophet ($AAS), until 'A'isha informed them all of it; and they accepted it. It should not be thought of Fa!ima, may God be pleased with her, that she was making some accusation to (Abu Bakr) "the trusting", may God be pleased with him, for what he had told her - heaven forbid that, both for her and for him! How could this be, since 'Umar b. al-Khanab, 'Uthman b. 'AtTan, 'AlI b. Abu Tiilib, ai-'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Munalib, 'Abd al-RaQmiln b. 'Awf, Tall;1a b. 'Ubayd Allah, a1-Zubayr b. a1-'Awwam, Sa'd b. Abu Waqq~, Abu Hurayra and 'A'isha had all agreed with him in his narration of this badith, as we will shortly demonstrate. And even if it had been an account narrated solely by Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, it would still have been incumbent upon the whole world to accept it, and to yield to him in the matter. If her anger related to what she had asked Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, these lands were a public charity, not an inheritance that her husband should supervise. His reason amounted to his assertion that, because he was the successor of the Messenger of God ($AAS), he considered it his duty to do as the former had done, and to rule as he had. The badith quotes him as saying, "I will never omit doing anything I saw the Messenger of God ($AAS) doing." And it goes on, "Fatima then avoided him and did not speak to him again from then to her death." This circumstance and her estrangement from him opened a broad field for the "rejectionist faction" and extensive foolishness, because of which they intruded themselves where they had no business. Had they properly understood, they would have credited Abu Bakr with his virtue and accepted the reason he gave as it should be by everyone. However, they are a forsaken faction, a contemptible group, people who stubbornly maintain the dubious, while ignoring what is accepted as definite and incontrovertible by the great imams of Islam, the Companions and their immediate successors, and the well-respected scholars who followed them throughout the ages and everywhere - may God be pleased with them all and give them pleasure!
lessons: Grief and a sense of injustice can cause estrangement even between righteous people, and this is a deeply human experience.
Accepting religious rulings that contradict our personal expectations is one of the most difficult but necessary aspects of faith.
Misunderstandings between beloved figures can be exploited by those with divisive agendas; seeking truth with sincerity protects the community.
practical_advice: When you feel you have been treated unjustly, seek clarification based on authentic knowledge before allowing resentment to grow.
If a religious ruling disappoints you personally, take time to understand its basis fully before reacting, as knowledge can bring peace.
Be wary of those who exploit grief or disagreement to sow division; anchor yourself to verified scholarship and the consensus of trusted scholars.

title: Umar's Ruling on the Prophet's Property Before the Companions
summary: Umar ibn al-Khattab adjudicates a dispute between Ali and Abbas over the Prophet's property, gathering senior Companions and affirming the hadith that prophets leave no inheritable wealth, only charity to be managed by the Muslim leader.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Ya!).ya b. Bukayr narrated to him, quoting al-Layth, from 'AqIl, from Ibn Shihab, who said, "Malik b. Aws b. al-I;Iadathan informed me" - and Mu!).anunad b. Jubayr b. Mut'im also narrated part of this badith, saying that he went in to him, (Malik) and asked him and that he replied to him as follows - '''I had gone in to see 'Vmar, when his doorkeeper, Yarfa' carne and asked him, 'Would you like to see 'VthmiIn, 'Abd al-Ra\:J.rniIn b. 'Awf, al-Zubayr and Sa'd?' He replied, 'Yes, let them corne in.' Yarfa' then asked, 'And 'AlI and 'Abbas too?' 'Yes,' he replied.' "'Abbas asked, 'Commander of the Believers, give judgement between myself and this man ('AlI).' 'Vmar replied, 'I charge you by God through whose permission the heavens and the earth exist, do you know that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "Our property is not to be inherited; what we leave will be for charity", and that by this he (the Prophet) was referring to himself?' The group agreed that he had indeed said that. 'V mar then approached 'AlI and 'Abbas and said, 'Do you both know that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said that?' They replied, 'Yes, he did say that.'" 'Vmar b. al-Khauab went on, "I will talk to you about this matter. God chose His Messenger for this flu>, 'booty', as something he had not given to anyone else. As God stated, 'What God had awarded as fa;> for His messenger ... has power over all things' (surat al-ljashr; LIX, v.6). "That property was therefore specifically for the Messenger of God (SAAS). He did not, however, I swear by God, keep it for himself or withhold it from you, but gave out its income which he distributed to you so that the present property remained. And from it he would provide the yearly expenses for his family, treating whatever remained as God's property. He acted in this way throughout his life. I adjure you by God, do you know that?' 'Yes,' they replied. 'Vmar then asked 'AlI and 'Abbas, 'I adjure you by God, do you two men know that?' 'Yes,' they replied. "'Vmar went on, 'Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) died and Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, said, 'I am the successor of the Messenger of God (SAAS).' And he took that property and managed it as the Messenger of God (SAAS) had done. Then Abu Bakr died and I said, 'Now I am the successor of the successor to the Messenger of God (SAAS).' And I took that property for two years, managing it as the Messenger of God (SAAS) had done. Then you both came to me, each saying the same thing and making the same case. Eventually you ('Abbas) came to me asking me for your share from the property of your nephew (the Prophet (SAAS)). After that, this man ('AlI) carne requesting the share of his wife from her father. I told you both, 'If you wish, I will give it to you to be managed in the same way.' Are you now seeking from me a different judgement? By God by whose permission the heavens and earth exist, I'll never give you any decision that is different, not even till Judgement Day! If you are incapable of managing it, then return it to me, and I'll take care of it for you!'" Al-Bukhari narrated this in various places in his $al;il; collection. Muslim and the other orthodox scholars gave it from various lines, from al-Zuhri. In one narrative in both $al;il; collections, 'Umar is quoted as saying, "Then Abu Bakr became in charge of it, and managed it as the Messenger of God (SAAS) had done. And God knows that he was truthful, pious, rightly guided, and always in pursuit of the truth. Then I took control of it and managed it as had both the Messenger of God (SAAS) and Abu Bakr. And God knows that I too am truthful, pious, rightly guided and always in pursuit of the truth. Then you both came to me and I gave it over to your control, to manage it as had the Messenger of God (SAAS), Abu Bakr and I. I adjure you by God, did I not pass it to you to do likewise?' 'Yes,' they said. 'Umar then said to them, 'I adjure you two men by God, did I not pass it to you both to do likewise?' 'Yes,' they said. 'Umar went on, 'Do you (now) seek from me some different decision? No, by Him whose permission the heavens and the earth exist!'" Imam Aḥmad stated that Sufyan narrated to him, from 'Amr, from al-Zuhri, from Malik b. Aws, who said, "I heard 'Umar say to 'Abd al-Raḥman, Talḥa, al-Zubayr and Sa'd, 'I implore you by God by whose order the heavens and the earth exist, do you know that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "Our property will not be inherited; we have left it as charity"?' 'Yes,' they said.'" This ḥadith meets the criteria of both $al;il; collections. I note that what the two men asked him - though God knows best - was that, following the award of authority to them, the income would be divided between them so that they would receive what would have been their right if it had been decreed that each be an actual heir to the land. They apparently sent ahead of themselves a group of the Companions, including 'Uthman, Ibn 'Awf, Talḥa, al-Zubayr and Sa'd. They had developed a serious rivalry because the management authority had been divided between them. The Companions who had gone on ahead asked 'Umar, "Commander of the Believers, decide between them both. Or relieve one of the other." It seems that 'Umar, may God be pleased with him, was reluctant to divide authority between them as if dividing a heritage, even if doing so only in appearance, since he wished to act in accord with the words of the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'Our property is not to be inherited; what we ieave will be for charity.' He therefore resisted them all and gave them an outright refusal, may God be pleased with him. 'Ali and al-'Abbas continued thereafter to manage the property jointly up to the time of 'Uthman b. 'Affan. Then 'Ali gained the advantage to it and al-'Abbas gave it up to him, with the assent of his son 'Abd Allah, may God be pleased with them both, in the presence of 'Uthman.
lessons: True leadership requires the courage to uphold principle even when pressured by beloved and respected individuals.
The Prophet's example of generosity with communal wealth — spending it on others rather than hoarding it — is a model for those in positions of trust.
Disputes over resources, even among the most righteous people, are a natural human challenge; what matters is resolving them with reference to clear religious guidance.
practical_advice: When facing a conflict over resources or rights, return to clear, verified principles rather than letting personal feelings dictate your decisions.
If you hold a position of trust, manage shared resources exactly as your predecessors did when their way was right and just.
When tension arises between people you care about, seek a fair resolution grounded in principle, and be firm in that resolution even if it disappoints both sides.

title: Fatima Presents Her Case to Abu Bakr Using Quranic Analogy
summary: Fatima initially argued her inheritance claim using Quranic analogy and general references, to which Abu Bakr responded with specific prophetic texts restricting the inheritance of prophets, and she ultimately accepted his reasoning.
story: I have closely examined the lines of transmission of this ḥadith and its phraseology in both musnad collections of aḥadith of the shaikhan, Abu Bakr and 'Umar, may God be pleased with them both. For each one I have assembled a large volume of narrations they made relating to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and their views on what constitutes proper and useful law. I have arranged this material under legal headings currently used today. I have narrated that Fatima, may God be pleased with her, initially argued using analogy and general reference to verses in the Holy Qur'an. Abu Bakr responded to her by quoting the texts restricting the rights of the Prophet (SAAS), and she accepted his words. This is what is attributed to her, may God be pleased with her. Imam Aḥmad stated that 'Affan narrated to him, quoting Ḥammad b. Salama, from Muḥammad b. 'Amr, from Abu Salama, that Fatima asked Abu Bakr, "Who
lessons: Accepting a well-reasoned ruling that contradicts one's initial position is a mark of intellectual and spiritual integrity.
Even the most knowledgeable people may be unaware of specific rulings; humility in seeking and accepting knowledge is a virtue.
Scholarly examination of the chain of transmission in religious matters protects the community from misinformation and ensures sound decision-making.
practical_advice: When you feel strongly about a position, be willing to present your reasoning openly and also to genuinely listen to the response before concluding.
If new information contradicts your previous understanding, take time to verify it properly before either accepting or rejecting it.
Study the principles behind religious rulings, not just the rulings themselves, so you can understand the reasoning when facing difficult personal situations.

title: Fatima's Claim to Inheritance and Her Dialogue with Abu Bakr
summary: After the Prophet's death, Fatima bint Muhammad approached Abu Bakr claiming inheritance from the Prophet's estate. Abu Bakr responded by citing a hadith that prophets do not leave property for inheritance, and Fatima ultimately accepted his words.
story: what constitutes proper and useful law. I have arranged this material under legal headings currently used today. I have narrated that Fatima, may God be pleased with her, initially argued using analogy and general reference to verses in the Holy Qur'an. Abu Bakr responded to her by quoting the texts restricting the rights of the Prophet (SAAS), and she accepted his words. This is what is attributed to her, may God be pleased with her. Imam A1)mad stated that 'Affan narrated to him, quoting I;Iammad b. Salama, from Mu1)ammad b. 'Amr, from Abu Salama, that Fatima asked Abu Bakr, "Who would inherit for you if you died?" "My son and my family," he replied. She asked, "Then why can we not inherit from the Messenger of God (SAAS)?" He replied, "I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, 'Prophets do not leave (property for) inheritance.' However, I myself provide for those whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) provided and give money to those to whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave."
lessons: Seeking clarity through dialogue, even in grief, reflects wisdom and courage rather than weakness.
Accepting the truth when it is presented, even when it is difficult or disappointing, is a mark of sincere faith.
Grief can cloud our judgment, and it is important to be patient with ourselves and others during times of loss.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult decision involving inheritance or family matters, seek guidance from trusted, knowledgeable sources before acting on emotion.
If you feel you have been wronged, articulate your concern calmly and be open to hearing evidence that may change your perspective.
When someone you love passes away, allow yourself to grieve while also trusting that those in authority are doing their best to uphold what is right.

title: Abu Bakr's Reconciliation with Fatima During Her Final Illness
summary: During Fatima's illness before her death, Abu Bakr sought permission to visit her. He reassured her of his sincere devotion to God, the Prophet, and the Prophet's family, and they were reconciled before she passed.
story: The /:taft:; Abu Bakr al-Bayhaqi stated that Abu 'Abd Allah Mul;1ammad b. Ya'qub informed him, quoting Mul;1ammad b. 'Abd al-Wahhab, quoting 'Abdan b. 'Uthman al-'Aw, of Nishapur, quoting Abu Jamra, from Isma'il b. Abu Khalid, from al-Sha'bi, who said, "When Fatima fell ill, Abu Bakr went and asked permission to visit with her. 'Ali said, 'Falima, this is Abu Bakr. May he visit with you?' She asked, 'Do you wish me to give him permission?' 'Yes,' he replied. "She permitted him to enter and he did so, in order to cheer her. He said, 'I swear by God, I have never left home, property, family and tribe unless to please God, His Messenger and all of you, his family!' He then reconciled with her so that she was content."
lessons: Reconciliation before death or parting is among the most noble acts a person can undertake.
Sincerity and transparency in explaining one's intentions can heal even the deepest wounds between loved ones.
Holding onto resentment only adds to one's suffering; choosing to forgive brings peace to both parties.
practical_advice: If there is a rift between you and someone you care about, do not wait for a perfect moment — seek reconciliation while you still have the opportunity.
When reconciling with someone, focus on expressing your sincere intentions and love rather than defending your past decisions.
If you are ill or grieving, allow those who care for you to come close; isolation in pain can deepen suffering unnecessarily.

title: Zayd b. Ali Affirms Abu Bakr's Decision Regarding Fadak
summary: Zayd b. Ali b. al-Husayn, a descendant of the Prophet's family, stated that had he been in Abu Bakr's position, he would have reached the same decision regarding the property at Fadak, affirming the legitimacy of Abu Bakr's ruling.
story: The /:taft:; al-Bayhaqi stated that Mul;1ammad b. 'Abd Allah al-I:Iafi~ informed him, quoting Abu 'Abd Allah al-Saffar, quoting Isma'il b. Isl;1aq al~qi, quoting Na~r b. 'Ali, quoting Ibn Da'ud, from Fuqayl b. Marzuq, who said, "Zayd b. 'Ali b. al-I:Iusayn b. 'Ali b. Abu T""alib stated, 'For my part, if I had been in Abu Bakr's place, I would have reached the same decision he reached over (the property at) Fadak.'"
lessons: True fairness means applying the same standard regardless of who is involved, including one's own family.
Acknowledging the correctness of a difficult decision, even when it disadvantages your own loved ones, is a sign of deep integrity.
Wisdom and justice can sometimes lead to conclusions that are personally painful but communally necessary.
practical_advice: When evaluating a decision that affected you or your family negatively, try to assess it on its merits rather than solely through the lens of personal loss.
Practice intellectual honesty by asking yourself: if the roles were reversed, would I reach the same conclusion?
Seek out respected and knowledgeable voices when navigating disputes over resources, inheritance, or fairness within families.

title: The Prophet's Declaration That Prophets Do Not Leave Property for Inheritance
summary: The hadith in which the Prophet Muhammad stated that prophets do not leave property for inheritance was cited by Abu Bakr as the basis for his decision regarding the Prophet's estate, and this ruling was upheld by the four rightly-guided caliphs and recognized scholars.
story: It is given hereabove in the account of Abu Salama, from Abu Hurayra, from Abu Bakr, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "Prophets do not leave (property) for inheritance." This is a comprehensive, all-inclusive reference encompassing all prophets. Al-Tirmidhl categorized the !}adith as !}asan. And in another !}adith he is quoted as saying, "We prophets do not leave (property for) inheritance." Secondly, the Messenger of God (SAAS) was singled out from all the prophets with ordinances in which others do not participate with him; we will give a separate chapter on this at the end of this biography, if God So wills it. If it is supposed that prophets other than himself might leave property in inheritance and that is not the case - then that would have been narrated in the information we have given from the Companions, including the "four imams", Abu Bakr, 'Umar, 'Uthman and 'Ab, and this would have made clear his having been singled out from the others by this ordinance. He did, indeed, state, "We will not leave (property for) inheritance; what we leave will be for charity." It is possible, therefore, from the phrase used, that the words of the Prophet (SAAS), "What we leave will be for charity", represent information about his decision or that of the rest of the prophets, along with himself, in accord with the above; this seems apparent. It is also possible that the words constitute a testamentary statement, suggesting that he meant, "We will not leave (property for) inheritance, since all that we have left is for charity", his specific purpose being to affirm the validity of all his property being considered to be for charity. The first of these possibilities is preferable and is that accepted by the majority. The second might be considered strengthened by what is given above in the !}adith of Malik and others, from Abu aI-Zinad, from ai-A'raj, from Abu Hurayra, to the effect that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "My heirs will not inherit a dinar nor a dirham; whatever I leave, after the expenses for my wives and provision for my employees is to be treated as a charity." This wording is given in both Fa!}i!} collections.
lessons: The Prophet's foresight in clarifying the disposition of his estate before his death demonstrates the importance of leaving clear instructions to avoid conflict among loved ones.
Material wealth, when given as charity, continues to benefit others long after the giver has passed — a form of living legacy.
When facing disputes over inheritance, returning to clear textual evidence and the consensus of trustworthy scholars is the wisest course.
practical_advice: Consider making a clear and documented will to protect your family from disputes after your passing.
Reflect on how your resources can serve others beyond your immediate family, such as through charitable giving or endowments.
When a religious or legal ruling feels personally unfair, seek deeper understanding of its basis before forming a final judgment.

title: The Prophet's Wives and Children: An Overview
summary: A comprehensive account of the Prophet Muhammad's wives, children, and family lineage, detailing the nine widows he left behind at his death and the lineage of his daughters and sons.
story: Chapter: An Account of the wives and children, may God be pleased with them all, of the Prophet (SAAS). God Almighty stated, "Wives of the Prophet, you are not like any other women, provided you remain conscious of God. Hence be not alluring in your speech, lest those men with sickness in their hearts be attracted. Speak with politeness. Remain in your homes and do not display yourselves as women did in the days of ignorance. Perform the prayers, pay the alms and obey God and His Messenger. God wishes only to preseve you from what is vile, you family members of the household, and to keep you thoroughly pure. And remember those verses and the wisdom from God that are revealed in your homes. God is kind and knowledgeable" (surat al-Aitzab; XXXIII, v.32-4). There is no dispute over the fact that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) died, he left nine widows. These were 'A'isha al-Tayrniyya, daughter of Abu Bakr, "the trusting"; I;l.af~a al-'Adawiyya, daughter of'Umar b. al-Khanab; Umm I;l.abiba Ramla al-Umawiyya, daughter ofAbu Sufyan Sakhr b.l;l.arb b. Umayya; Zaynab al-Asadiyya, daughter of Ja1;Ish; Umm Salama I;l.ind al-Makhzumiyya, daughter of Abu Umayya; Mayrnuna al-Hilaliyya, daughter of al-I;l.arith; Sawda al-'Amiriyya, daughter of Zam'a; Juwayriyya al-Mu~raliqiyya, daughter of al-I;l.arith b. Abu l)irar; and Safiyya al-NaQariyya al-Isra'iliyya al-HarUniyya, daughter of I;l.uyayy b. Akhtab. May God be pleased with them and give them pleasure. He also had two wives donated to him. The first of these were Mariyya al-Qubtiyya al-Mi~riyya, from Kawrat An~ina', who was the mother of his son IbrahJm, upon whom be peace. The second was Rayl;1ana al-Qur~iyya, daughter of Sham'un; she accepted Islam and he manumitted her. She then rejoined her family. Some claim that she remained in seclusion among them. But God knows best.
lessons: The Prophet's household was held to the highest standard of conduct, reminding us that those closest to sources of guidance carry greater responsibility in their actions and speech.
The Prophet maintained loyalty and exclusivity to Khadija throughout her life, demonstrating that faithfulness and devotion are foundational to a blessed marriage.
The diversity of the Prophet's wives in background and lineage reflects the universal nature of Islam and the dignity afforded to women of all origins within the faith.
practical_advice: When navigating grief or loss of a loved one, reflect on the lives of those who surrounded the Prophet — their resilience in loss is a model for processing bereavement with faith.
Honor the people in your life who have stood by you; the Prophet's loyalty to Khadija teaches us not to take long-term companionship for granted.
When feeling overwhelmed by family complexity or relational challenges, seek guidance in Islamic principles of fairness, patience, and clear communication as modeled by the Prophet's household.

title: Al-Shanba's Rejection of the Prophet After Ibrahim's Death
summary: One of the women brought to the Prophet as a potential wife was divorced after she reacted to the death of his son Ibrahim by denying his prophethood, saying a prophet's son would not have died.
story: Al-Shanba', when brought in to him, was not receptive and he left her, waiting for her to become so. However, when his son Ibrahim died shortly thereafter, she commented, 'If he were a prophet, his son would not have died!' And so he divorced her, provided a dowry for her and she was prohibited from other men.
lessons: Grief and loss can be misused as an occasion for doubt and cruelty; the death of Ibrahim tested both the Prophet's patience and the faith of those around him.
True belief is not contingent on being shielded from loss — prophets and righteous people experience the same human sorrows, and this does not diminish divine favor.
How we respond to others in their moments of loss reveals the depth of our character and compassion.
practical_advice: When you suffer loss and those around you react with insensitivity or blame, remember that the Prophet himself experienced this and responded with dignity rather than bitterness.
Do not measure divine love or favor by the presence or absence of hardship; loss is a universal human experience that does not signal abandonment by God.
If you find yourself doubting God during a painful moment, pause before speaking — grief can distort our thinking, and words spoken in pain can cause lasting harm to relationships.

title: Khadija: The Prophet's First and Beloved Wife
summary: An account of the Prophet's marriage to Khadija, his first and most beloved wife, who bore him all his children except Ibrahim, and with whom he remained exclusively devoted throughout her lifetime.
story: The great bali:;: Abu Bakr al-Bayhaql narrated, through Sa'ld b. Abu 'Aruba, from Q;ttada, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) married fifteen women. He consummated marriage with thirteen of these and eleven lived with him. He died leaving nine widows." ... The !taji" b. 'Asakir gave this in part, also attributing it to him, to the effect that the first wife the Messenger of God (SAAS), married was KhadJja, daughter of Khuwaylid b. Asad b. 'Abd a1-'lJzza b. Qusayy, and that she was married to him by her father prior to his having received his prophetic mission. In one account, al-Zuhri stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was 21 years of age when he married Khadlja; it is also said that he was 25 and that the marriage took place around the time that the ka'ba was being reconstructed." AI-Waqidl added to this, "And she was 45 years old." ... Ibn Jarlr stated, "(At that time) the Prophet (SAAS) was 37 years old. To him were born al-QIsim, by whose name he was commonly known, a1-Tayyib, ai-Tahir, Zaynab, Ruqayya, Umm Kulthum and Fatima." I note that she was the mother of all his children, except for IbrahIm, who was born to Mariyya, as will be explained hereafter. ... I note that the Messenger of God (SAAS) did not marry another woman while she was still alive. 'Abd al-Razzaq narrated it thus from Ma'mar, from aI-Zuhri, from 'Urwa, who quoted 'A'isha, as above.
lessons: Khadija's story teaches that a strong, faithful partnership built on mutual trust and support is one of the greatest blessings a person can receive in this life.
The Prophet's complete loyalty to Khadija during her lifetime — refusing to take another wife — demonstrates that true love is expressed through consistent devotion and not merely through words.
The loss of children, as experienced by both the Prophet and Khadija, is one of the most profound human sorrows, and enduring it together with patience strengthens rather than breaks bonds of faith.
practical_advice: When you are fortunate enough to have a supportive and loyal partner, invest in that relationship with the same care and exclusivity the Prophet showed Khadija.
If you are grieving the loss of a child or a loved one, find comfort in knowing that the Prophet himself experienced this grief — you are not alone, and this pain does not mean God has abandoned you.
Reflect on the people in your life who believed in you before the world did, as Khadija believed in the Prophet; express gratitude to those who have been your earliest and most faithful supporters.

title: The Daughters of the Prophet and Their Marriages
summary: A detailed account of the Prophet's daughters — Zaynab, Ruqayya, Umm Kulthum, and Fatima — their marriages, children, and deaths, many of which occurred during the Prophet's own lifetime.
story: Ibn Jarlr went on to speak about each of the daughters of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and those who married them. His conclusion was that Zaynab was married by aPAs b. al-Rabl' b. 'Abd al-q]zza b. 'Abd Shams b. 'Abd Manaf, who was Khadlja's nephew. His mother was Hala, daughter of Khuwaylid. To him she bore a son named 'All and a daughter named Umama. 'All b. Abu Talib married her following the death of Fatima. He died while still married to her. After his death, she married al-Mughlra b. Nawfal b. al-I:Iarith b. 'Abd al-Munalib. (Ibn Jarlr went on) "Ruqayya was married to q]thmao b. 'AfTao. She bore him a son, 'Abd Allah; q]thmao was first known by this son's name and later by his son 'Amr. Ruqayya died while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was at the battle ofBadr. When Zayd b. I:Iaritha returned with the news (of the victory) he found them levelling the earth over her grave. q]thmao had remained with her, nursing her, but the Messenger of God (SAAS) awarded him his payment and share of the booty. He then married q]thmao to his other daughter Umm Kulthum. This is why q]thman is known as 'he who possessed the two lights'. She died, also while living with q]thman, during the life ofthe Messenger of God (SAAS). "Fatima was married by her cousin 'Ail b. Abu Talib b. 'Abd a1-Munalib. He consummated his marriage to her following the battle of Badr, as we recounted above. She bore him I:Iasan, by whom he was known, and I:Iusayn who was martyred in qraq." I note that he was also named "Mui).sin". (Ibn Jarlr went on) "Then there were Zaynab and Umm Kulthum. This Zaynab was married by her cousin, 'Abd Allah b. Ja'far. She bore him 'Ail and 'Awn and died while living with 'Abd Allah. Umm Kulthum was married by the Commander of the Believers, 'Umar b. al-Khanab. She bore him Zayd; 'Umar died while still married to her. After him she married the sons ofher uncle Ja'far, one after the other. First she married 'Awn b. Ja'far, and he died. His brother MulJammad then married her and he too died. Then the brother of both these men, 'Abd Allah b. Ja'far, married her, and she died while still married to him."
lessons: The Prophet buried multiple daughters during his own lifetime, a grief that few parents can imagine, yet he continued his mission with patience and trust in God — a profound lesson in enduring sorrow with faith.
Ruqayya's death at the time of Badr, and Uthman's devotion in nursing her, reminds us that love and care for a spouse in illness is one of the noblest forms of human devotion.
The repeated losses experienced by Umm Kulthum — multiple husbands dying — teach us that some lives are marked by extraordinary hardship, and that endurance itself is a form of worship.
practical_advice: When you are facing the loss of someone you love, remember that even the Prophet experienced the death of his children; your grief is not a sign of weakness but a reflection of the depth of your love.
If you are supporting someone through bereavement, take a lesson from Uthman, who stayed by Ruqayya's side during her final illness — presence and care matter more than words.

When life brings repeated losses, resist the temptation to withdraw entirely; the women of the Prophet's family continued to live, remarry, and contribute to their communities despite repeated heartbreak.

title: Khadija's Prior Marriages and Her Marriage to the Prophet
summary: An account of Khadija's marriages before the Prophet, her children from those marriages, and the circumstances of her marriage to Muhammad, with scholarly differences on their respective ages.
story: AI-Zuhri stated, "Khadija, daughter of Khuwaylid, had been married prior to her marriage to the Messenger of God (SAAS) to two men. The first of these was 'Atiq b. 'A'idh b. Makhzum. To him she bore Jariyya, the mother of MulJammad b. Sayfi. The second was Abu Hala aI-Taymi; to him she bore Hind b. Hind." Ibn IslJaq gave his name and went on, "After the death of 'A'idh Abu Hala, she married aI-Nabbash b. Zurara, a man ofBanu 'Amr b. Tamim, an ally ofBanu 'Abd ai-Dar. To him she bore one boy and one girl, and he died. Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) married her, and to him she bore his four daughters and after them his sons aI-Qisim, al-Tayyib and ai-Tahir. All the boys died while still suckling." I note that the Messenger of God (SAAS) did not marry another woman while she was still alive.
lessons: Khadija's life before her marriage to the Prophet — marked by widowhood, children from previous unions, and resilience — shows that a person's past does not diminish their capacity for their greatest contributions.
The Prophet's choice of Khadija, a widow older than himself, reflects that character, wisdom, and integrity matter far more than social conventions about age or marital history.
The death of all the Prophet's infant sons is a reminder that worldly legacy is not measured in biological continuity but in the spiritual and moral inheritance one leaves behind.
practical_advice: If you carry the weight of a difficult past — loss, multiple hardships, or societal judgment — take heart from Khadija's story; your history does not define the ceiling of your future.
Resist judging yourself or others by conventional social metrics; the Prophet chose a partner based on virtue and mutual respect, not on age or prior circumstance.

When all of one's children die in infancy, as happened to the Prophet and Khadija, the grief is unimaginable — if you are walking through infant loss, know this grief has been shared by the most honored of God's creation, and that you are not alone.

title: Overview of the Prophet's Marriages: 'A'isha and Hafsa
summary: A scholarly account detailing the Prophet's marriages to 'A'isha bint Abi Bakr and Hafsa bint 'Umar, including lineage and circumstances of each union.
story: b. Ka'b b. Lu'ayy b. Ghalib b. Fihrb. Malik b. al-Nagr b. Kinana; she was the only virgin he ever married." I note that she bore him no children. It is said, however, that she miscarried a child of his whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) had named 'Abd Allah. And she was therefore named Umm 'Abd Allah. Others say, however, that she bore this name after the son of her sister Asmii', his father being al-Zubayr b. aI-'Awwam, may God be pleased with them all. I also note that it is also said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) married Sawda prior to 'A'isha; Ibn IslJaq and others stated this. We have mentioned above the dispute over this. God knows best. I have described the marriage of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to both these women prior to the Hijra, and how he postponed the consummation of his marriage to 'A'isha until after the Hijra. (AI-Zuhri went on) "And he married I:Iafsa, daughter of 'Umar b. aI-Khanab. She had previously been the wife of Khunays b. I:Iudhafa b. Q;tys b. 'Adi b. I:Iudhafa b. Sahm b. 'Amr b. Husays b. Ka'b b. Lu'ayy. He died, a Muslim, while still married to her.
lessons: The Prophet honored widows and divorcees by marrying them, showing that a person's worth is not diminished by past loss.
Scholarly precision in preserving historical details reflects the Islamic tradition of careful knowledge transmission.
Marriages in Islam carry deep social and spiritual significance, not merely personal convenience.
practical_advice: When you have experienced loss in a relationship, remember that your story is not over and that new beginnings are possible.
Approach historical and religious knowledge with humility, recognizing that scholars may differ and that certainty belongs to God.
Honor the memory of those you have lost while remaining open to new chapters in your life.

title: The Marriage to Umm Salama After the Death of Abu Salama
summary: The Prophet married Umm Salama Hind bint Abi Umayya after the death of her husband Abu Salama, providing her with companionship and dignity after her loss.
story: "Then he married Umm Salama Hind, daughter ofAbu Umayya b. al-Mughlra b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Vmar b. Makhzum. She had previously been the wife of her cousin AbU Salama 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd al-Asad b. Hila! b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Vmar b. Makhzum.
lessons: Widowhood does not define a person's future; God can open doors of honor and care after devastating loss.
The Prophet's marriage to widows demonstrated compassion and social responsibility toward vulnerable members of the community.
Grief and new beginnings can coexist; accepting care after loss is a sign of strength, not disloyalty.
practical_advice: If you are grieving the loss of a spouse or loved one, allow yourself to receive support from your community rather than isolating yourself.
Trust that God's plan for your life continues even after your greatest losses.
Remember Umm Salama's patience in grief — she made a du'a for something better and received it; use supplication as a tool during bereavement.

title: Sawda bint Zam'a: Returning from Abyssinia and Marriage to the Prophet
summary: Sawda bint Zam'a, whose husband al-Sakran died as a Muslim after their return from Abyssinia, was later married by the Prophet, giving her protection and dignity.
story: "After that he married Sawda, daughter ofZam'a b. 'Abd Shams b. 'Abd Wudd b. Na~r b. Malik b. J::Iisl b. 'Atnir b. Lu'ayy. She had previously been married to al-Sakran b. 'Arnr, the brother of Suhayl b. 'Arnr b. 'Abd Shams. He died as a Muslim while married to her after his and her return to Mecca from Abyssinia; may God be pleased with them both.
lessons: Those who make sacrifices for their faith — like emigrating to Abyssinia — may face further hardships, but God does not abandon them.
The Prophet's care for early Muslim widows shows that community responsibility for the vulnerable is a prophetic value.
Loss of a spouse while far from home is one of the loneliest experiences; seeking closeness to God during such moments is a powerful source of strength.
practical_advice: If you feel lonely after a major life transition or loss, reach out to your community and allow others to provide support.
Acknowledge the sacrifices of those who came before you in your faith tradition — their perseverance can inspire your own.
When facing compounded hardships, focus on one day at a time and trust that God sees your struggle.

title: Umm Habiba: Her Husband's Apostasy and the Prophet's Marriage Proposal from Afar
summary: Umm Habiba Ramla bint Abi Sufyan endured her husband 'Ubayd Allah's conversion to Christianity and his death in Abyssinia; the Prophet then sent a proposal to her through 'Amr b. Umayya, with the Negus facilitating the marriage.
story: "He next married Umm J::Iabiba Ramla, daughter of Abu Sufyan b. J::Iarb b. Umayya b. 'Abd Shams b. 'Abd Manaf b. Qu~ay. She had previously been married to 'Vbayd Allah b. ]aI;lsh b. Ri'ab of Banu Asad b. Khuzayma. He died a Christian in Abyssinia. The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent to her 'Amr b. Umayya al-l)amIi in Abyssinia to arrange his betrothal to her. 'Vthmlln b. 'AfIan conducted his marriage to her." I note that this is what he stated; however, the correct name here should be 'Vthman b. Abu al-'A~. The Negus awarded her a dowry on his behalf of 400 dinars and dispatched her with ShurahbIl b. J::Iasana. We have narrated all this at length above - and to God be praise.
lessons: Faith can be tested through the betrayal of those closest to us, yet God provides a way forward for those who remain steadfast.
Umm Habiba's story shows that remaining firm in one's beliefs, even when a spouse abandons the faith, is a form of profound courage.
God can orchestrate dignified solutions to seemingly impossible situations, as demonstrated by the Negus generously facilitating this marriage.
practical_advice: If your faith is tested by the choices of someone you love, hold firmly to your own convictions while entrusting the outcome to God.
When you feel abandoned or betrayed, remember that God sees your faithfulness and will not leave you without support.
Do not allow another person's spiritual decline to pull you away from your own path; your relationship with God is your own.

title: Zaynab bint Jahsh: First Wife to Reunite with the Prophet After Death and First Grave Monument
summary: Zaynab bint Jahsh, previously married to the Prophet's freed-man Zayd b. Haritha, was the first of the Prophet's wives to die and rejoin him, and the first to have a grave monument constructed over her in Madinah.
story: Al-ZuhIi went on, "Then he married Zaynab, daughter of ]aI;lsh b. Ri'ab b. Arad b. Khuzayma. Her mother was Umayma, daughter of 'Abd al-Munalib, the aunt of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Zaynab had previously been the wife of Zayd b. J::Iaritha, the freed-man of the Messenger of God (SAAS). She was the first of his wives to have joined him (after her death) and the first to have a grave monument constructed over her. Asmii', daughter of 'Vmays, made this over her, having seen this done in Abyssinia.
lessons: The honor of being first to rejoin the Prophet in the hereafter belongs to someone who had endured significant trials, showing that difficulty and divine proximity are linked.
The practice of honoring the deceased with care and dignity — as seen in the grave monument — reflects Islam's respect for human beings even after death.
Life transitions, including marriage after divorce, can be the will of God rather than a mark of failure.
practical_advice: When processing a painful ending — a divorce, a loss — consider that God may be redirecting you toward something greater.
Honor and remember the deceased in your life through appropriate, dignified acts of remembrance.
Do not judge a person's worth by the difficulties they have experienced; often those who suffer most are closest to God.

title: Zaynab bint Khuzayma: The Mother of the Unfortunate Who Died Shortly After Marriage
summary: Zaynab bint Khuzayma, known as 'the mother of the unfortunate' for her generosity to the poor, was widowed at Uhud and married the Prophet but died only a short time later.
story: "He next married Zaynab, daughter of Khuzayma. She was of Banu 'Abd Manaf b. Hila! b. 'Amir b. Sa'l;a'a. She was known as umm al-masakin, 'the mother of the unfortunate', and had previously been the wife of 'Abd Allah b. ]aI;lsh b. Ri'ab, who was killed at the battle of U1)ud. She lived only a short time with the Messenger of God (SAAS), before she died; may God be pleased with her." Yunus quoted Mu1)ammad b. Is1)aq as having stated, "Prior to this she had also been married to al-J::Iusayn b. al-J::Iarith b. 'Abd al-MunaJib b. 'Abd Manaf, or to his brother al-Tufayl b. al-J::Iarith."
lessons: A life of generosity and compassion toward the poor — as embodied by Zaynab — is itself a form of worship regardless of how long that life lasts.
Not all chapters in life are long; what matters is the quality of love and service offered within them.
The Prophet's willingness to marry a widow who had suffered multiple losses shows that care for the grieving is a prophetic virtue.
practical_advice: If your time with someone was cut short, honor the good that existed rather than mourning only what was lost.
Find meaning in serving others during your own periods of pain — generosity toward those less fortunate is a powerful antidote to grief.
Remember that the length of a life or a relationship does not determine its significance before God.

title: Maymuna bint al-Harith: The Last of the Prophet's Marriages
summary: Maymuna bint al-Harith offered herself in marriage to the Prophet, and their union — facilitated by Abu Rafi' — was contracted during the 'Umrat al-Qada.
story: AI-Zuhrl went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) also married Maymuna, daughter ofal-J::Iarith b.J::Iazn, son ofBujayr b. al-Huzam b. Ru'aba b. 'Abd Allah b. Hilal b. 'Amir b. Sa'~a'a. And it was she who offered herself in marriage." I note that in fact he did ask for her in marriage. The matchmaker between them was Abu Rafi', his freed-man, as we have narrated above in the section of the 'umrat aI-qat/a'. In his account, Sayf b. 'Vmar stated that she had been the wife of'Vmayr b. 'Amr, a son of'Aqda b. Thaqlfb. 'Amr al-Thaqafi. He died while still married to her. She then married Abu Ruhm b. 'Abd al-'Vzza b. Abu Q;tys b. 'Abd Wudd b. N."r b. Malik b. I:Iisl b. 'Amir b. Lu'ayy.
lessons: Taking initiative in seeking what is good — as Maymuna did — is a sign of courage and clarity of purpose, not immodesty.
A freed-man like Abu Rafi' acting as matchmaker reflects the Prophet's household's values of inclusion and dignity for all people regardless of social status.
Every marriage in the Prophet's life carried spiritual, social, and communal significance beyond personal desire.
practical_advice: Do not be afraid to take appropriate steps toward good things you desire; initiative combined with proper conduct is praiseworthy.
Value the people in your life who act as bridges and facilitators of goodness, as Abu Rafi' did.
When seeking a life partner, approach the matter with clarity of intention and reliance on God.

title: Juwayriyya bint al-Harith: From Captive to Wife of the Prophet
summary: Juwayriyya bint al-Harith of Banu al-Mustaliq was taken captive at the battle of al-Muraysi', after which the Prophet freed and married her, resulting in the release of many captives by the Companions.
story: Sayf went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) took Juwayriyya, daughter of al-I:Iarith b. Abu Oirar b. al-I:Iarith b. 'Amir b. Malik b. al-Mu~taliq ofKhuza'a at the battle of al-MuraysI'. He freed her, then married her. It is also said that her father al-I:Iarith, the leader of Khuza'a, came forward and accepted Islam and then married her to him. She had previously been married to her cousin Safwan b. Abu Shufr." Qitada quoted from Sa'Id b. al-Musayyab, al-Sha'bl, Mu/;1ammad b. Is/;1aq and others as having stated, "This clan of Khuza'a had been allies ofAbu Sufyan against the Messenger of God (SAAS). This is why I:Iassan (b. Thabit) spoke the verse, 'The alliance of al-I;Iarith b. Abu Oirar and that of Quray~a among you are the same.'" In his account from Sa'ld b. 'Abd Allah, Sayf b. 'Vmar stated that Ibn Abu Mulayka quoted 'A'isha as having said, "Juwayriyya had been married to her cousin Malik b. Safwan b. Tawlab Dhu al-Shufr b. Abu al-Sar/;1 b. Malik b. al-Mu~taliq."
lessons: The Prophet's freeing and marriage of Juwayriyya led to the freeing of all her people's captives, showing that a single noble act can have rippling effects of mercy.
Dignity can be restored even after the most humiliating circumstances; God can transform a person's condition completely.
Reconciliation between former enemies — as seen with Juwayriyya's tribe — is possible through magnanimity and justice.
practical_advice: When you are in a position of power or advantage over others, choose the path of generosity and mercy — its effects extend far beyond what you can see.
If you have experienced a loss of dignity or status, remember that your worth before God is not defined by your circumstances.
Look for opportunities where a single act of kindness might ripple outward to benefit many; such opportunities are rarely as rare as we think.

title: Safiyya bint Huyayy: From Khaybar to the Prophet's Household
summary: Safiyya bint Huyayy of Banu al-Nadir was taken captive at the battle of Khaybar, after which the Prophet chose her, freed her, and married her.
story: Sayf went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) also took captive Safiyya, daughter ofI:Iuyayy b. Akhtab, ofBanu al-Na<Jlr, at Khaybar. She was then the bride of Kinana b. Abu al-I:Iuqayq." In his account, Sayfb. 'Vmar maintained that prior to her marriage to Kinana she had been the wife of Sallam b. Mishkam. But God knows best. Sayf concluded, "These, then, are the II women with whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) consummated marriage." During his caliphate, 'Vmar b. al-Khanab awarded 12,000 (dirhams) to each of the wives ofthe Messenger of God (SAAS). However, he gave 6,000 to Juwayriyya and Safiyya since they had been taken captive. AI-Zuhrl stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) had kept both these women in seclusion and had provided for them."
lessons: The Prophet's treatment of Safiyya — freeing her, honoring her with marriage, and providing for her — set a standard of dignity for those who come from opposing communities.
Material provision and seclusion for protection were forms of care in the Prophet's household, reminding us that care takes many practical forms.
Identity shaped by trauma and conflict can be transcended through faith and belonging to a new community.
practical_advice: If you carry wounds from conflict or a difficult past, know that a new chapter of dignity and belonging is possible.
Provide practical, material support to those who are vulnerable in your community — care is often expressed through action more than words.
When integrating into a new community or environment, allow yourself time to adjust and accept the protection and provision that is offered to you.

title: Mariyya the Copt and Her Son Ibrahim
summary: Mariyya the Copt was a bond-maid in the Prophet's household who bore him a son named Ibrahim, who died in infancy.
story: He went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) also had in his household a bond-maid called Mariyya. A son was born to her named Ibrahim. He died when several months old.
lessons: The loss of a child is one of the most profound forms of grief; even the Prophet experienced this loss, and it does not indicate abandonment by God.
Every life, no matter how brief, has meaning and is recorded with God.
Grief over a child's death is natural and human; the Prophet's own tears at Ibrahim's funeral show that expressing grief is not contrary to faith.
practical_advice: If you have experienced the loss of a child or a loved one who died young, know that your grief is valid and shared even by the Prophet himself.
Allow yourself to mourn, and seek comfort in the belief that those who die in innocence are in God's mercy.
Reach out to others who have experienced similar losses; shared grief can become a source of unexpected community and healing.

title: The Woman with Leprosy: A Marriage Annulled Out of Compassion
summary: The Prophet contracted marriage with a woman of Banu 'Amr b. Kilab but divorced her without consummation upon learning she had leprosy, handling the matter with care and without humiliation.
story: AI-Zuhri stated on this subject, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) contracted marriage to a woman of Banu 'Amr b. Kilab. Then he was told that she had leprosy and he divorced her without having consummated the marriage." I comment that it is evident that this refers to the woman previously mentioned. But God knows best.
lessons: The Prophet's handling of this situation shows that health considerations are legitimate in marital decisions without requiring cruelty or humiliation.
Dissolving a commitment out of genuine necessity, handled with grace, is better than proceeding under false pretenses.
Those who carry illness or physical difference deserve dignity, not shame; the manner of parting matters as much as the decision itself.
practical_advice: When difficult decisions must be made about relationships, seek to handle them with the maximum possible kindness and the minimum possible harm to the other person.
If you carry a health condition that affects your relationships, know that you are worthy of honest and compassionate treatment.
Do not prolong situations that are not right out of fear of confrontation; address them gently and directly.

title: The Woman of Banu al-Jawn Seeks Refuge from the Prophet
summary: A woman of Banu al-Jawn was brought to the Prophet (SAAS) for marriage, but when he approached her she said 'I seek refuge with God from you,' and he honored her request by releasing her and gifting her two garments before sending her back to her family.
story: to two walls. We sat between them, the Messenger of God (~AAS), having told us to do so. He then went on inside; the woman of Banu ai-lawn, accompanied by her maid, had been brought to him (there) and left alone in a house belonging to Umayma, daughter of al-Nu'man b. SharalJ.il. When he went in to the woman of Banu ai-lawn, he said to her, 'Give yourself to me!' But she answered, 'Should a queen give herself to a common person! I seek refuge with God from You!' He replied, 'You have gained refuge with Him who awards it!' He then came out to us and said, 'Abu Usayd, present two coats of armour to her and take her back to her family.' Al-Bukhari also stated that al-I;Iusayn b. al-Walid said, quoting 'Abd al-Ral)man b. al-Ghasil, from 'Abbas b. Sahl b. Sa'd, from his father and from Abu Usayd, both of whom stated, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) contracted marriage with Umayma, daughter of SharaQll. When he went in to her, he stretched out his hand towards her, and she seems to have disliked that. He then told Abu Usayd to give her a trousseau and two white linen dresses.' Al-Bukhari also stated that al-I;Iumaydi related to him, quoting al-Walld, quoting al-Awza'i, who said he asked al-Zuhri, 'Which one of the wives of the Prophet (SAAS) sought refuge (with God) from him?' He replied, ''Urwa quoted to me 'A'isha as having said that when the daughter of al-Jawn went in to the Messenger of God (SAAS), she said, "I seek refuge with God from you!" And he replied, "You have gained refuge with the Great One. Go and join your family."'
lessons: Dignity and personal boundaries are respected in Islam, even in the most intimate of circumstances.
The Prophet's (SAAS) response to rejection was gracious and generous, demonstrating that true nobility does not force itself upon others.
A person of character responds to rejection not with anger or punishment, but with kindness and restraint.
practical_advice: When someone declines or withdraws from a relationship, honor their decision with grace rather than pressure or resentment.
Channel feelings of rejection into acts of generosity, as the Prophet did by gifting garments to the woman before sending her home.
Remind yourself that not every connection is meant to be, and that accepting this with dignity is a sign of spiritual maturity.

title: Mulayka Seeks Refuge After 'A'isha's Words and Is Divorced
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) married Mulayka, daughter of Ka'b, a reportedly beautiful woman, but 'A'isha visited her and asked if she was not ashamed to marry the one who killed her father, after which Mulayka sought refuge with God from the Prophet and was divorced.
story: Muhammad b. Sa'd quoted al-Waqidi as having stated that Abu Ma'shar related to him, saying, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) married Mulayka, daughter of Ka'b. She was reputed to be exceedingly beautiful. 'A'isha went in to visit her and asked, "Are you not ashamed to be marrying the man who killed your father?" Mulayka then sought refuge with God from him and he divorced her. Her people then came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and told him, "She is just young and of unsound judgement. And she has been deceived. Take her back." But he refused. They then asked his permission for her to marry a close relative of hers of Banu 'Udhra, and he agreed. Her father had been killed by Khalid b. al-Walid at the conquest of Mecca.'
lessons: Words spoken carelessly or with ill intent can cause irreparable harm to others, even when those words come from someone close to us.
Grief over a parent's death is a profound wound, and sensitivity toward those who have suffered loss is a mark of true compassion.
Even in situations of personal loss or confusion, it is important to seek clarity and not make irreversible decisions based on the words of others.
practical_advice: Before speaking to someone in a vulnerable situation, pause and consider the emotional weight your words may carry for them.
If you have been hurt or confused by what someone said, seek to verify the truth independently before making a life-altering decision.
When a relationship ends due to misunderstanding or outside interference, focus on healing rather than placing blame, and trust that God's plan for you remains intact.

title: Qittila, Sister of al-Ash'ath, and the Disagreement of Abu Bakr and 'Umar
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) contracted marriage with Qittila, sister of al-Ash'ath b. Qays, but died before consummating it or giving her a choice; a dispute then arose between Abu Bakr and 'Umar over her status and her subsequent marriage to 'Ikrama b. Abu Jahl.
story: Al-Hakim Abu 'Abd Allah al-Naysaburi stated, 'Abu 'Ubayda Ma'mar b. al-Muthanna said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) contracted marriage with 18 women." Among these he mentioned Qittila, daughter of Qays, sister of al-Ash'ath b. Qays. Some claim that he married her two months prior to his death. Others maintain that he married her during his illness. But she had not come to him, nor had he seen her, nor had he consummated marriage with her. Others claim that the Prophet (SAAS) left a testamentary statement that Qittila be given a choice. If she wished, she could go into seclusion and be forbidden access to the Muslims, and if she wished, she could marry anyone she liked. She did choose to marry, and 'Ikrama b. Abu Jahl married her in Hadramawt. News of this reached Abu Bakr and he commented, "I'm almost grinding my teeth in anger at them both!" 'Umar b. al-Khattab said, "But she is not one of 'the mothers of the Believers'. He did not consummate marriage with her, nor was she placed in seclusion." Abu 'Ubayda stated that some people maintain that the Messenger of God (SAAS) left no testamentary statement about her, that she apostatized after his death, and 'Umar used her apostacy as argument against Abu Bakr by stating that she was not one of 'the mothers of the Believers'. Ibn Manda stated that the woman who apostatized was al-Bar'sa', of Banu 'Awf b. Sa'd b. Dhubyan. Hammad b. Salama narrated, from Da'ud b. Abu Hind, from al-Sha'bi, that Abu Bakr wanted to behead 'Ikrama b. Abu Jahl after his marriage to Qittila. 'Umar b. al-Khattab, however, persuaded him against this, saying that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had not consummated marriage with her, that she had apostatized with her brother, and had become disassociated from both God and His Prophet. He kept insisting on this until Abu Bakr gave up the issue.'
lessons: Matters of religious law require careful deliberation and consultation among knowledgeable people rather than reactions driven by emotion or anger.
Even the closest companions of the Prophet (SAAS) could hold differing views and were willing to reason through disagreements with patience.
Ambiguous situations call for humility, restraint, and a willingness to be corrected rather than rigid insistence on one's first reading.
practical_advice: When facing a complex or unclear situation, seek out wise counsel and be willing to revise your position in light of sound reasoning.
Allow yourself to feel strong emotions in difficult moments, but do not let those feelings drive irreversible actions; pause and consult before acting.
Trust that questions which feel unresolvable in the moment will become clearer with time, patience, and sincere seeking of truth.

title: The Prophet's Wives as Listed by al-Zuhri: A Record of His Marriages
summary: Al-Zuhri provides a comprehensive account of all the women the Prophet (SAAS) married, tracing their names, prior marriages, and how each came into his life, serving as a historical record of his household.
story: Yunus quoted Ibn Ishaq as having said that among the women with whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) contracted but did not consummate marriage were Asma' al-Jawniyya, daughter of Ka'b, and 'Amra, daughter of Yazid al-Kilabiyya. Ibn 'Abbas and Qatada gave the name as Asma', daughter of al-Nu'man b. Abu al-Jawn. The hafiz Ibn 'Asakir stated that Abu al-Fath Yusuf b. 'Abd al-Wahid al-Mahani informed him, quoting Shuja' b. 'Ali b. Shuja', quoting Abu 'Abd Allah b. Manda, quoting al-Hasan b. Muhammad b. Hakim al-Marwazi, quoting Abu al-Muwajjih Muhammad b. 'Amr b. al-Muwajjih al-Fazzari, quoting 'Abd Allah b. 'Othman, quoting 'Abd Allah b. al-Mubarak, quoting Yunus b. Yazid, from Ibn Shihab al-Zuhri, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) married Khadija, daughter of Khuwaylid b. Asad in Mecca. She had previously been married to 'Atiq b. 'A'idh al-Makhzumi. He next arranged marriage, in Mecca, to 'A'isha, daughter of Abu Bakr. Then, in Medina, he married Hafsa, daughter of 'Umar. She had previously been married to Khunays b. Hudhafa al-Sahmi. Next he married Sawda, daughter of Zam'a, who had previously been married to al-Sakran b. 'Amr, a brother of Banu 'Amir b. Lu'ayy. Then he married Umm Habiba, daughter of Abu Sufyan, who had previously been married to 'Ubayd Allah b. Jahsh al-Asadi, of Banu Khuzayma. He next married Umm Salama, daughter of Abu Umayya, her given name being Hind. She had previously been married to Abu Salama 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd al-Asad b. 'Abd al-'Uzza. He then married Zaynab, daughter of Khuzayma al-Hilaliyya, also al-'Aliyya, daughter of Zabyan, of Banu Bakr b. 'Amr b. Kilab. He married a woman of Banu al-Jawn of Kinda, and received as a captive Juwayriyya, in the expedition to al-Muraysi', in which Manat were defeated. The latter was the daughter of al-Harith b. Abu Dirar of Banu al-Mustaliq of Khuz'a. He also received as a captive Safiyya, daughter of Huyayy b. Akhtab, of Banu al-Nadir. These two women were part of the booty God had granted him, having awarded both to him. He also received Mariyya, the Copt, as a tribute, and she bore him Ibrahim. He similarly received Rayhana of Banu Qurana as a tribute; he set her free, and she rejoined her family with whom she remained in seclusion.'
lessons: The Prophet's (SAAS) marriages each carried a distinct social, political, or humanitarian purpose, reflecting a life lived in service to community and justice.
The women who entered the Prophet's household came from vastly different backgrounds, reminding us that human dignity transcends status, ethnicity, and prior circumstance.
Recording history with care and precision is itself an act of worship, preserving the truth for generations who will seek to understand and learn from it.
practical_advice: When reflecting on your own relationships and life history, take time to recognize the unique purpose and lesson embedded in each significant connection.
Study the lives of those who came before you not merely as historical data but as lived examples of patience, faith, and resilience.
Appreciate the diversity of people God places in your life, understanding that each encounter carries the potential to deepen your character and broaden your compassion.

title: The Prophet's Marriages: An Overview of His Wives
summary: A comprehensive historical account listing all the women the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) married, their sequence, and their origins, as compiled by Ibn Kathir from multiple chains of narration.
story: the expedition to al-Muraysi', in which Manat were defeated. The latter was the daughter of al-J.:Iarith b. Abu Oirar of Banu al-Mu~taliq of Khuza'a. He also received as a captiye'Safiyya, daughter ofJ.:Iuyayy b. Akhtab, of Banu al-Na<jir. These two women:were partof the booty God had granted him, having awarded both to him. He also received Mariyya, the Copt, as a tribute, and she bore him Ibrahim. He similarly received Rayl:tana of Banu Qurana as a tribute; he set her free, and she rejqined her family with whom. she remained in seclusion. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) divorced al-'Aliyya, daughter of Zabyan. He separated from a woman of Banu 'Amr b. Kilab, and also from a woman of Banu al-Jawn, of Kinda, because she had leprosy. Zaynab al-Hilaliyya, daughter ofKhuzayma, died during the lifetime ofthe Messenger of God (SAAS). We have also been told that al-'Aliyya, daughter of Zubyan, whom he divorced, married again before the wives of the Messenger of God (SAAS), had been declared (by God) to be prohibited. She married a cousin, from her own people, and had children among th~m." We have given this text in full because of the strangeness of his reference to the marriage of Sawda as having been in Medina. The truth is that this took place in Mecca, before the Hijra, as we have related above. Though God knows best. Yunus b, Bukayr quoted Mul:tammad b. Isl:taq as having stated, "Khadija, daughter of KhuwayIid, died three years before the Messenger of God (SAAS) left on the Hijra. Be did not marry any other woman during her lifetime; she and Abu Talib died the same year. After Khadija, the Messenger of God (SAAS) married Sawda, daughter of Zam'a. After Sawda, he married 'A'isha, daughter of Abu Bakr. 'A'isha was the only virgin he married; he did not receive a child from her before he died. After 'A'isha, he married J.:Iaf~a, daughter of'Vmar. Then he married, Zaynab al-Hilaliyya, daughter of Khuzayma, (known as) umm al-masaktn, 'the mother of the poor'. After her he married Umm J.:Iabiba, daughter of Abu Sufyan, and after her Umm Salama Hind, daughter of Abu Umayya. After her ~e married Zaynab, daughter ofJal;1sh, and after herJuwayriyya, daughter ofal-J.:Iaiith b. Abu Dirar. Then, after Juwayriyya, he married Safiyya, daughter ofJ.:Iuyayy b. Akhtab. After Safiyya he married Maymiina al-HilaIiyya, daughter of al-J.:Iarith." This sequence is preferable and more accurate than that listed by al-Zuhri. But God knows best.
lessons: Scholarly rigor and careful attribution of sources reflects the Islamic tradition of preserving truth with precision and honesty.
The Prophet's marriages each had distinct circumstances — political, social, and humanitarian — reminding us that major life decisions often carry deep wisdom not visible on the surface.
Accepting uncertainty gracefully, as Ibn Kathir repeatedly says 'God knows best,' is a sign of intellectual humility and spiritual maturity.
practical_advice: When you feel confused about complex matters, acknowledge the limits of your knowledge rather than forcing certainty where none exists.
Study the context behind major life events — your own or others' — before passing judgment; there is often more wisdom beneath the surface.
Practice saying 'I don't know' or 'God knows best' when faced with ambiguous situations; this reduces anxiety and fosters trust in a higher wisdom.

title: The Prophet's Marriage to a Woman of Banu Ghifar Who Had Leprosy
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) married a woman of Banu Ghifar but upon discovering she had leprosy he separated from her with kindness, paying her dowry in full.
story: Yunus b. Bukayr stated, from Abu YaQya, from I;Iumayd b. Zayd al-Ta'i, from Sahl b. Zayd al-An~ari, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) arranged his marriage to a woman ofBanu Ghifar. He went in to her and he told her to undress, and she did so. He then saw a white spot ofleprosy at her breasts and so kept away from her. He then told her, 'Take your clothes and rejoin your family.' He paid her her dowry in full." Abu Nu'aym narrated it from a ftadith of I;Iumayyil b. Zayd, from Sahl b. Zayd aI-An~ari, someone who had seen the Prophet (SAAS). He stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) married a woman ofBanu Ghifar", and he proceeded to narrate as above.
lessons: True dignity in ending a relationship means treating the other person with full fairness, meeting every obligation even when you are not required to go beyond the minimum.
Compassion does not require staying in every situation; sometimes the most merciful act is a kind and honest separation.
Illness and hardship can befall anyone; those who are left or separated from others due to illness deserve dignity, not shame.
practical_advice: If you must end or step back from a relationship or commitment, do so with full honesty and without taking what is not rightfully yours.
When someone in your life faces illness or difficulty, respond with compassion rather than withdrawal — offer support even as you maintain appropriate boundaries.
If you have been separated from someone due to circumstances beyond your control, remind yourself that your worth is not diminished by the situation.

title: Umm Sharik Gives Herself to the Prophet and the Question of Her Marriage
summary: Various accounts detail how Umm Sharik al-Azdiyya presented herself to the Prophet (SAAS) for marriage, with differing narrations on whether the marriage was consummated or not.
story: I note that Umm Sharik aI-Azdiyya was among those women with whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) contracted marriage but did not consummate. AI-Waqidi stated, "It is well established that she was of Banu Daws, though some say of the an~ar. Others name her as ofBanu 'Amir, and that her name was Khawla, daughter of I;Iakim al-Sulami." AI-Waqidi stated that her name was Ghaziyya, daughter ofJabir b. I;Iakim. MUQammad b. ISQaq quoted I;Iakim b. I;Iakim, from MUQammad b. 'Ali b. aI-I;Iusayn, as having stated that his father said, "The total number of women the Messenger of God (SAAS) married was 15. These included Umm Sharik al-An~ariyya; she gave herself to the Prophet (SAAS)." Sa'id b. Abu 'Aruba quoted Qltada as having stated, "He married Umm Shank aI-Am;ariyya ofBanu ai-Najjar. And he said, 'I love to marry an~ari women, though I hate their ghayra, "jealousy"." And he did not consummate marriage with her."
lessons: Sincere offering of oneself in service or devotion, even if not accepted in the way hoped, carries its own spiritual merit.
Differing scholarly opinions remind us that not every question has a single clear answer — remaining humble in the face of ambiguity is itself a form of wisdom.
Jealousy (ghayra) in relationships, while natural, can become a burden if left unchecked; the Prophet's gentle acknowledgment of this trait shows his awareness of human nature.
practical_advice: If you have offered something sincerely — your time, effort, or heart — and it was not received as expected, trust that sincerity itself has value before God.
When you notice jealousy arising in yourself, acknowledge it without shame but examine whether it is serving or harming your relationships.
Seek clarity in ambiguous situations by consulting multiple reliable sources, just as scholars gathered different narrations to piece together the truth.

title: Layla Daughter of al-Khatim Presents Herself and Then Seeks Release
summary: Layla daughter of al-Khatim boldly proposed herself to the Prophet (SAAS), was accepted, but then sought release after her people warned her that her jealous nature would lead to her ruin.
story:
Ibn ISQaq stated, from I;Iaklm, from MUQammad b. 'Ali, from his father, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) married Layla al-An~ariyya, daughter of al-Khapm; she was very jealous. She feared for herself from him and asked his permission to be released. And so he did release her."

Mul}ammad b. Sa'd narrated, from Hisham b. al-Kalbi, from his father, from Abu $alil}, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "Layla, daughter of al-Khapm, came up to the Messenger of God ($AAS) while he had his back turned towards the sun and tapped him on the shoulder. He exclaimed, 'Who is that? May the Black One devour you!' She replied, 'I am the daughter of him who feeds the birds and outpaces the wind. I am Layla, daughter of al-Khapm. I have come to you to present myself to you. Will you marry me?' 'Consider it done,' he replied. She then returned to her people and said, 'I have arranged my marriage to the Messenger of God!' People told her, 'What you did was a mistake; you area jealous woman and the Messenger of God is already master of women who are jealous about him. He will curse you to God. Seek your release.' "So she went back and asked, 'Messenger of God, please release me.' He did so. Mas'ud b. Aws b. Sawad b.Zufr then married her and she bore him a child. One day, while she was washing herself in one of the gardens of Medina, a black wolfleapt at her and ate part of her, and so she died."
lessons: Self-awareness is a gift — Layla knew her own nature well enough to step back from a situation that would have caused her suffering, and this kind of honest self-knowledge is a form of courage.
Acting impulsively out of excitement or admiration can lead to decisions we later regret; taking time to reflect before major commitments is wisdom, not weakness.
The Prophet's gracious release of Layla shows that true nobility does not hold people against their will; allowing others to exit with dignity is a sign of greatness.
practical_advice: Before making a major life decision out of excitement or impulse, pause and honestly assess whether you are truly prepared for its demands.
If you recognize that a particular situation or relationship will bring out the worst in you rather than the best, it is a sign of maturity — not failure — to step back.
When someone asks to be released from an obligation or commitment, respond with grace rather than resentment; freeing others generously is its own reward.

title: The Prophet's Proposal to Umm Hani' Daughter of Abu Talib
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) proposed to his cousin Umm Hani' daughter of Abu Talib, but she declined citing her young children, and he accepted her excuse graciously.
story:
Isma'il b. Abu Khalid stated, from al-Sha'bi, that Umm Hani', Fakhita, daughter of Abu Talib, stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) proposed marriage to her. She told him that she had young children and he then left her, saying, "The best women are those who ride camels and righteous women of Quraysh. They are those most kind to their children when young, and those most careful with their husband's property."

'Abd aI-Razzaq stated, from Ma'mar, from aI-Zuhri, from Sa'id b. aI-Musayyab, who quoted Abu Hurayra as having said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) proposed marriage to Umm Hani', daughter of Abu Talib, and that she replied, "Messenger of God, I am too old and I have young children."

AI-Tirmidhi stated that 'Abd b. I;Iumayd related to him, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Musa, quoting Isra'il, from aI-Suddi, from Abu SaliQ, from Umm Hani', daughter of Abu Talib, who said, "The Messenger of God ($AAS) proposed marriage to me, but I asked to be excused and he did excuse me. God then revealed, 'We have made lawful to you your wives to whom you have given dowries, as well as those women in your power whom God has awarded you as spoils of war, along with the daughters ofyour paternal uncle and the daughters of your paternal aunts and the daughters of your maternal uncle and the daughters of your maternal aunts who went into exile with you' (silrat al-Abzab; XXXIII, v.SO). I was not made lawful for him, because I had not gone into exile; I was one of those women divorced."
lessons: Prioritizing the welfare of your children is a noble and Islamically honored form of devotion; motherhood itself carries immense spiritual weight.
