Saying no graciously, and having that 'no' received with grace, models the kind of mutual respect that should characterize all human dealings.
Divine wisdom sometimes confirms human decisions retroactively, as the Quranic verse revealed after Umm Hani's refusal showed that her choice aligned with what God had ordained.
practical_advice: When you must decline an opportunity or invitation out of duty to your family, do so with confidence — caring for dependents is itself a form of worship.
If someone declines your offer, request, or invitation, practice receiving their 'no' with the same grace the Prophet showed; not all rejection is personal.
Trust that when you make a principled decision, even a difficult one, divine wisdom may be working through your circumstances in ways you cannot yet see.

title: The Prophet's Proposal to Dhaba'a Daughter of Amir Through Her Son Salama
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) sought engagement to Dhaba'a daughter of Amir through her son Salama, but Salama's silence upon returning suggested he considered her too old, and the matter was dropped.
story: He also stated, from Ibn 'Abbas, that I)aba'a, daughter of 'Amir b. Qirt was the wife of'Abd Allah b. Jud'an who divorced her. Hisham b. aI-Mughira then married her and she bore him Salama. She (I)aba'a) was a well-built and beautiful woman with abundant hair covering her body. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked her son Salama to accept his engagement to her. Salama asked, "(Will you wait) until I ask her permission?" He did ask her and she replied, "Do you have to ask permission when it's the Messenger of God!" When her son came back, he remained silent, not giving any answer; it seems that he felt her to be too old. The Prophet (SAAS) said nothing (more) about her.
lessons: Not every proposal or initiative needs to be forced to a conclusion; sometimes graceful silence is its own answer, and wisdom lies in knowing when to let something go.
The woman's enthusiastic willingness — 'Do you have to ask permission when it's the Messenger of God!' — is a reminder that readiness and openness to goodness should be embraced without hesitation.
The Prophet's dignified silence upon receiving no clear answer demonstrates emotional intelligence: he did not pursue where pursuit was unwelcome.
practical_advice: When you sense that someone is hesitant or unwilling, resist the urge to push or seek explicit rejection; let silence communicate what words cannot.
Practice enthusiastic willingness when good opportunities come your way rather than overthinking or holding back out of false modesty.
If an opportunity or relationship does not develop despite your hopes, accept it with dignity rather than demanding explanation or closure.

title: Safiyya Daughter of Bashama Is Given a Choice Between the Prophet and Her Husband
summary: Safiyya daughter of Bashama, received as war spoils, was given the choice by the Prophet (SAAS) between staying with him or returning to her husband; she chose her husband, was sent away, and her own tribe cursed her for it.
story: He (Mul;iammad b. Sa'd) also stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) also became engaged to Safiyya, daughter of Bashama b. Na<jla al-'AnbarI. He had received her as war spoils. The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave her a choice, saying, 'If you wish, then myself; if you wish, then your husband.' She replied, 'My husband.' He then sent her away. Banu Tamlm cursed her."
lessons: True freedom of choice, even in the most unequal power situations, is a Prophetic value — the Prophet gave Safiyya a genuine choice rather than imposing his will.
Choosing loyalty to one's existing commitments, even at personal cost, reflects deep integrity — Safiyya chose her husband and held to that despite the social consequences.
Being cursed or criticized by your own community for a principled choice is a painful reality; what matters before God is the sincerity of your decision, not the approval of those around you.
practical_advice: When you face a life-altering choice, weigh your existing commitments and loyalties carefully before deciding — loyalty to those you have already pledged yourself to carries its own honor.
If your community or social circle criticizes or rejects you for a choice you made with integrity, find stability in the conviction that you acted according to your conscience.
Extend genuine choice and agency to those in your care or authority; forcing decisions on vulnerable people is contrary to the Prophetic example.

title: Hamra Daughter of al-Harith and the False Accusation of Her Father
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) sought engagement to Hamra daughter of al-Harith, but her father falsely claimed something was wrong with her; upon his return she had actually contracted leprosy.
story: Those to whom he became engaged but did not marry included I;Iamra, daughter of al-I;Iarith b. 'Awn b. Abu I;Iaritha al-MUrrI, whose father said there was something wrong with her - though there was not. When her father returned to her, she had contracted leprosy. She was the mother of Shabib b. al-Barsa', the poet.
lessons: Deception, even when motivated by a desire to protect or redirect, can invite consequences we did not foresee — truth remains the safest path.
Life's trials can arrive in unexpected ways; illness following a moment of dishonesty in this story is a reminder that we are not in full control of outcomes.
Every person — even one marked by illness or misfortune — carries their own worth and legacy; Hamra became the mother of a poet, a contribution that outlasted her hardship.
practical_advice: Resist the temptation to make excuses or small deceptions to avoid uncomfortable situations; the consequences of dishonesty often outweigh the discomfort of truth.
If you or someone you love is facing illness, remember that illness does not erase identity, legacy, or dignity.
When life brings unexpected hardship after a difficult decision or mistake, resist the urge to spiral into guilt; focus instead on what you can still contribute and build.

title: The Prophet's Proposal to Habiba Daughter of Abbas Prevented by Fosterage
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) sought engagement to Habiba daughter of al-Abbas but discovered that her father was his foster-brother through Thuwayba, making the marriage prohibited.
story: He went on, "He also sought to be engaged to I;Iabiba, daughter of al-'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Munalib. But he discovered that her father was his foster-brother, both having been suckled by Thuwayba, Abu Lahab's freed-woman."
lessons: Islamic law's intricate family ethics — including fosterage ties — demonstrate how deeply the tradition values the sanctity of familial bonds, even those formed outside blood relations.
Discovering a hidden or forgotten connection that changes everything is a reminder to investigate thoroughly before making major commitments.
Accepting a prohibition gracefully, without resentment, when a higher principle is revealed is itself a form of submission and trust in God's design.
practical_advice: Before making significant commitments, take time to investigate relevant background, history, and any existing ties that could affect the relationship.
When you discover that something you desired is not available or appropriate for you, practice accepting that reality as part of a larger wisdom rather than as personal loss.
Honor the bonds and connections — even informal or non-biological ones — that shaped your family and community; they carry real weight and deserve respect.

title: The Three Categories of the Prophet's Wives After His Death
summary: Ibn Kathir categorizes the Prophet's wives into those with whom he consummated marriage and who survived him, those he divorced during his lifetime, and those he did not fully marry, with distinct rulings applying to each group.
story:
These, then are his wives. They were of three categories. One group consisted of women with whom he consummated marriage, and he died before their deaths. These were the nine who were mentioned initially. They were prohibited to others following his death. This was universally recognized to be required by the faith, and that their 'idda 174 would last until the end of their lives. God Almighty had stated, "It is not for you to harm the Messenger of God, nor ever to marry his wives after him; that would be very serious to God" (surat al-Aftzab; XXXIII, v.53).

174. The period prescribed for a woman to wait from the end of one marriage to the beginning of another.

Another category consists of those women with whom he consummated marriage but divorced during his lifetime. There is a question as to whether it would be permissible for them to rematry after the expiration
lessons: Divine law establishes clear and dignified frameworks for the most sensitive human situations, including the question of widowhood and remarriage after the loss of a great man.
The Mothers of the Believers were honored with a unique and enduring status — a reminder that nearness to righteousness carries its own sacred obligations.
The Quran's direct address on protecting the dignity of the Prophet's family models how communities should protect the honor of those who gave much in service of a greater cause.
practical_advice: When navigating complex legal, social, or family situations after loss, seek clear and well-established guidance rather than relying on assumptions or informal opinions.
Honor those who have dedicated their lives to a cause greater than themselves — their sacrifices create obligations of respect that outlast their lifetimes.
If you find yourself in a period of waiting or transition after a significant ending, understand that such periods exist for wisdom and healing, not as punishment.

title: Mariya the Copt: The Gift from Alexandria and the Birth of Ibrahim
summary: The Governor of Alexandria gifted the Prophet with Mariya the Copt, a beautiful woman who later bore him his son Ibrahim. Her presence stirred jealousy among the Prophet's wives, and her son's birth brought both joy and controversy.
story: Two women were awarded to the Messenger of God (~AAS) as tribute. One of these was Mariya, the Copt, daughter of Sham'un. She was awarded to him by the Governor of Alexandria, whose name was Jurayj b. Mlna. Along with her he also presented her sister ShIrIn. Abu Nu'aym stated that he presented her as one of four girls. But God knows best. Included was a eunuch slave named Mabur, and a female mule called al-DulduI. The Prophet (~AAS) accepted his gifts and chose Mariya for himself. She was from an Egyptian village called I;:Iafn, from the Kura Ansina region. During the period of his governorate (over Egypt) Mu'awiya b. Abu Sufyan exempted this village from payment of the kharaj, "the land tax", in honour of her because she bore a male child, IbrahIm, upon whom be peace, to the Messenger ofGod (~AAS). They say that Mariya was beautiful and fair-skinned, that the Messenger of God (~AAS) admired and fell in love with her and that she enjoyed much favour with him. This was especially true after she bore him his son IbrahIm. The Messenger of God (SAAS) presented her sister Shirin to I;Iassan b. Thabit, to whom she bore his son 'Abd al-Rahman. The eunuch slave Mabiir was allowed to enter the quarters of Mariya and Shirin without special permission, as had been his practice in Egypt. Some people, however, began making comments about Miiriya because of that; they did not know that he was an eunuch until the fact was manifest, as we will explain shortly, if God so wills it. The mule mentioned above was one the Messenger of God (SAAS) would ride; it appears - though God knows best - that it was this donkey he rode at the battle ofI;lunayn. This donkey lived on to great age and was kept by 'Ali b. Abu Talib during his rule. When 'Ali died it became owned by 'Abd Allah b. Ja'far b. Abu Talib. When it grew very old, barley was crushed for it to eat.
lessons: Kindness and honour can echo across generations — the Prophet's treatment of Mariya was remembered and honoured long after her passing, as shown by the tax exemption of her village.
Jealousy is a natural human emotion, but it must be managed with patience and faith; even those closest to the Prophet experienced it.
Every soul, regardless of their origin or circumstances, has the capacity to find faith and live with dignity, as Mariya embraced Islam and was honoured.
practical_advice: When you feel jealousy arising in your heart, acknowledge it honestly rather than suppressing it, then redirect your focus to gratitude for your own blessings.
If you find yourself the subject of rumours or suspicion, stay grounded in your integrity and trust that truth will ultimately become clear.
Honour and respect the people in your life not only for their status but for their character — the Prophet honoured Mariya for her devotion and the child she bore.

title: The Birth of Ibrahim and the Celebration of New Life
summary: Mariya the Copt gave birth to the Prophet's son Ibrahim, whose arrival was celebrated with charity and sacrifice on the seventh day. The joyful news was brought to the Prophet by Abu Rafi', who was rewarded with a necklace.
story: "Mariya bore to the Messenger of God (SAAS) a son whom he named IbrahIm. He sacrificed a sheep for him on the seventh day after the child's birth. He shaved his head and made a donation in silver to the poor to the weight of his hair that he thereafter had buried in the ground. He named the child Ibriihim. The midwife was Sahna, the freed-woman of the Messenger of God (SAAS). She came out to see her husband Abu Riifi' and told him that Mariya had given birth to a son. Abu Rafi' went with the good news to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who gave him a necklace as a gift. The wives of the Messenger of God (SAAS) were already jealous (ofMiiriya) and this envy grew more intense after she bore him the son."
lessons: Celebrating the arrival of new life with acts of charity connects personal joy to communal blessing, turning private happiness into a gift for others.
Gratitude for good news should be expressed generously — the Prophet rewarded the bearer of glad tidings, teaching us to honour those who share joy with us.
Even in moments of great personal happiness, we should remain aware that others around us may be struggling with their own emotions, and respond with compassion.
practical_advice: Mark moments of personal joy and gratitude with an act of giving to others — even something small connects your happiness to a larger sense of purpose.
When you receive good news, take a moment to genuinely thank those who shared it with you, as this strengthens bonds of community.
If you notice tension or jealousy in those around you during your happy moments, respond with extra kindness and inclusivity rather than pulling away.

title: Ali Sent to Investigate the Rumours About Mariya
summary: Rumours spread about Mariya and a Coptic relative who visited her. The Prophet sent Ali with a sword to investigate, and Ali discovered that the man was a eunuch, completely clearing Mariya of all suspicion.
story: Yunus b. Bukayr quoted Mul.>ammad b. Isl.>aq, from Ibrahim b. Mul.>ammad b. 'All b. Abu Talib, from his father, who quoted his grandfather 'Ab b. Abu Talib as having said, "People kept spreading rumours about Mariya, Ibrahim's mother, and a Coptic cousin of hers who would visit her frequently. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Take this sword and go. If you find him with her, then kill him!' I replied, 'Messenger of God, I would do your bidding (directly) as on a fully secured road; nothing would divert me until I accomplished whatever you had ordered me to do. Or (should I be as) an eye-witness, seeing what the absent does not?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'Indeed yes; as an eye-witness who sees what the absent does not.' "So off! went, wearing the sword, and I found him with her. I unsheathed the sword and when he saw me, he knew that I was after him. He went over to a palm tree and climbed it, then turned over on his back and drew up his legs. He had been made emasculated and completely smooth. There was absolutely no trace of a male member. So I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and told him. He said, 'Praise be to God, who has diverted evil from the household members.'"
lessons: False rumours can cause tremendous harm to the innocent — this account shows how quickly suspicion can spread and the serious consequences it can have on real people's lives.

A person of integrity carries out their responsibilities with careful discernment, not blind action — Ali's question to the Prophet about how to proceed shows the importance of wisdom alongside obedience.
When the truth is revealed and an innocent person is vindicated, the proper response is gratitude to God, not gloating or further discussion of the rumour.
practical_advice: Before repeating or reacting to rumours about someone, pause and ask yourself whether you have verified the information — unverified gossip can destroy reputations and relationships.
If you are the target of false rumours, place your trust in God that the truth will be revealed, and focus on maintaining your own integrity rather than anxiously defending yourself to everyone.
When a situation you feared turns out to be harmless, respond with gratitude — saying 'alhamdulillah' and acknowledging God's mercy helps reframe relief as a spiritual moment.

title: Gabriel Reassures the Prophet About Ibrahim
summary: When Mariya gave birth to Ibrahim, the Prophet reportedly began to harbour some suspicion. Gabriel descended and greeted him as 'father of Ibrahim,' offering divine reassurance at a moment of personal vulnerability.
story: Al-Tabraru stated that Mul.>anunad b. 'Amr b. Khalidal-I;Iarranl related to him, quoting his father, quoting Ibn Lahj'a, from Yazjd b. Abu I;Iabjb and 'Aqjl, from al-Zuhrl, from Anas, who said, "When Mariya gave birth to Ibrahjm, the Messenger of God (SAAS) seemed almost to begin having snspicions about him. Then Gabriel came down to him and said, 'Peace be upon you, father ofIbrahtm!'"
lessons: Even in moments of doubt or uncertainty, divine reassurance can come in unexpected ways — this account reminds us that God is aware of our inner struggles and responds to them.
A greeting that affirms someone's identity and dignity — as Gabriel did by calling the Prophet 'father of Ibrahim' — can be a profound act of comfort and validation.
Doubt is a human experience, and even the greatest of people pass through moments of uncertainty; what matters is how we respond to those moments.
practical_advice: When you are going through a moment of doubt or internal conflict, open yourself to signs of reassurance from God — these may come through prayer, the words of someone you trust, or a quiet sense of peace.
Affirm the identities and roles of the people around you, especially when they are vulnerable — a simple acknowledgement of who someone is can have a powerful healing effect.
Do not let a passing moment of doubt define you; acknowledge it, seek clarity, and trust that God sees your sincerity.

title: Aisha's Account of Mariya and the Question of Ibrahim's Resemblance
summary: Aisha recounts her feelings of annoyance when Mariya became pregnant. She also describes an exchange with Mariya about baby Ibrahim's appearance, highlighting the emotional complexity within the Prophet's household.
story: Abu Nu'ayrn stated that 'Abd Allah b. Mu!:tammad related to him, quoting Abu Bakr b. AbU 'A~im, quoting Mul:tammad b. Ya!:tya aI-Bahili, quoting Ya'qub b. Mu!:tammad, from a man he named who quoted al-Layth b. Sa'd, from al-ZuhrI, from 'Urwa, who quoted 'A'isha as having said, "A king of the Byzantine patriarchs (sic) called al-Muqawqis presented a Coptic girl of royal descent named Mariya, along with a young male cousin of hers. The Messenger of God (SAAS) went in to see her one day when he would go to his private room and consummated with her. She became pregnant with IbrahIm. "When her pregnancy became visible, I was much annoyed. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said nothing. She did not produce milk, so he bought for her a ewe giving milk from which to feed the boy. His body grew strong and his colour was excellent and his complexion clear. One day she arrived carrying him on her shoulder. She asked, "'A'isha, how do you think the lad looks?' 'Neither I nor others see a resemblance,' I replied. She then asked, 'No comment about his flesh?' I replied, 'By my life, anyone fed on sheep's milk is going to have nice flesh!'"
lessons: Difficult emotions such as jealousy and annoyance are part of the human experience — even someone as noble as Aisha experienced them, and this normalises these feelings for all of us.
Honest acknowledgement of our emotions, even when they are unflattering, is a form of integrity — Aisha's candid narration shows the value of truthfulness about one's inner life.
Children are innocent and deserve to be seen with fresh eyes, regardless of the complicated circumstances of the adults around them.
practical_advice: When you feel jealousy or annoyance in a relationship, give yourself permission to feel it without acting on it destructively — feelings are not sins, but how we respond to them matters.
If you find yourself making cutting or indirect remarks when someone reaches out to you, reflect on whether grief or jealousy is driving your words, and try to respond with more generosity.
Care for children and vulnerable people around you regardless of personal tensions with adults in their lives — their needs are separate from the conflicts of grown-ups.

title: The Death of Mariya the Copt
summary: Mariya the Copt passed away in either 15 or 16 AH. Umar led her funeral prayer and she was buried in the al-Baqi' cemetery in Madinah, where the Prophet's companions and family members rest.
story: Al-Waqidl stated, "Mariya died in Mu!:tarram of 15 AH. 'Umar prayed over her and buried her in the al-Baqj' cemetery." Al-Mufa<;l<jal b. Ghassan aI-GhallabI said the same. Khaljfa, Abu 'Ubayda and Ya'qub b. Sufyan all stated that she died in 16 AH. May God have mercy on her.
lessons: Every life, no matter how extraordinary or how contested, comes to its appointed end — remembering death keeps us focused on what truly matters.
The honouring of Mariya in death, with a funeral led by the Caliph Umar and burial in the most honoured cemetery of Madinah, reflects the Islamic principle of dignifying the deceased.
The phrase 'May God have mercy on her,' recorded by the historian, teaches us to extend compassion and supplication to those who have passed, especially those who lived through hardship.
practical_advice: Cultivate the habit of making du'a for those who have passed away, especially those who lived difficult lives — this is a simple but profound act of spiritual solidarity.
When someone in your community passes away, honour their memory by focusing on their faith and their contributions rather than the controversies of their life.
Reflect regularly on your own mortality as a way to clarify your priorities and deepen your gratitude for the time you have.

title: Rayhana bint Zayd: Refusing Islam and Living in Seclusion
summary: Rayhana bint Zayd, taken as a captive after the Banu Qurayza affair, refused the Prophet's invitation to accept Islam and insisted on remaining Jewish. The Prophet was considerably annoyed and placed her in seclusion.
story: Included in this category of woman was Ray!:tana, daughter of Zayd, of Banu aI-Na<;lIr, also said to have been ofBanu Quray?a. Al-Waqidj stated, "Ray!:tana, daughter ofZayd, was of Banu al-Na<;lIr or, it is said, ofBanu Qurana." Al_WaqidI (also) stated, "Ray!:tana, daughter of Zayd, was ofBanu al-Na<;lIr and had married within the tribe. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had taken her for himself as booty. She was beautiful. The Messenger of God (SAAS) proposed to her that she accept Islam, but she refused to be anything but Jewish. He then placed her in seclusion, being considerably annoyed. He sent a message to Ibn Sa'ya telling him of this and Ibn Sa'ya responded, 'May my father and mother be your ransom, I swear she
lessons: Faith cannot be forced — the Prophet invited Rayhana to Islam but did not compel her, reflecting the Quranic principle that there is no compulsion in religion.
Holding onto one's identity in circumstances of captivity and loss is an expression of the human spirit's resilience, even when that choice brings further isolation.
Being in a state of seclusion or social distance from others can be an opportunity for deep inner reflection, even if it begins in grief or conflict.
practical_advice: If you feel pressure to change who you are or what you believe in order to be accepted, take time to distinguish between genuine growth and compromising your core identity.
When someone in your life refuses what you offer them — whether advice, help, or connection — try to respond with patience rather than prolonged annoyance, trusting their journey.
If you are currently feeling isolated or in a kind of 'seclusion' in your life, use this time to reconnect with your own values and ask what truly grounds you.

title: Rayhana bint Zayd: Captive of Banu Qurayza and Her Relationship with the Prophet
summary: After the defeat of Banu Qurayza, Rayhana bint Zayd was taken captive and brought before the Prophet, who proposed Islam and marriage to her. Multiple narrations describe her eventual acceptance of Islam, her status, and her life until her death.
story: stated, "Ray!:tana, daughter ofZayd, was of Banu al-Na<;lIr or, it is said, ofBanu Qurana." Al_WaqidI (also) stated, "Ray!:tana, daughter of Zayd, was ofBanu al-Na<;lIr and had married within the tribe. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had taken her for himself as booty. She was beautiful. The Messenger of God (SAAS) proposed to her that she accept Islam, but she refused to be anything but Jewish. He then placed her in seclusion, being considerably annoyed. He sent a message to Ibn Sa'ya telling him of this and Ibn Sa'ya responded, 'May my father and mother be your ransom, I swear she will accept Islam!' He then went and told her, 'Don't follow your own people! You have seen what I;Iuyayy b. Akhrab brought them to! Accept Islam; and in this Case the Messenger of God is to choose you for himself.' "While the Messenger of God (SAAS) was with his Companions, he heard the sound of slippers. He said, 'Those are the slippers of Ibn Sa'ya who is coming to tell me the good news ofRayl:tana accepting Islam.' He did come and said, 'Messenger of God, Ray!:tana has accepted Islam!' He was delighted at this." Mu!:tammad b. Is!:taq stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) defeated Qurana, he chose for himself Ray!:tana, daughter of 'Amr b. Khanafa. She was with him until his death, at which time she was still within his domain. He had proposed Islam to her and to marry her, but she refused, accepting only Judaism." Ibn Isl)aq went on to narrate her acceptance ofIslam, as above. AI-Waqidl stated that 'Abd aI-Malik b. Sulayman related to him,. from Ayyub b. 'Abd aI-Ral:>man b. Abu Sa'lla'a, from AyyUb b. Bashlr al"Ma'awl, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent her to the house of Salma Umm al-Mundhir, daughter of Qiys. She remained there with her until she had fully menstruated and was cleansed ofher menstruation. Umm al-Mundhir then went and informed the Messenger of God (SAAS). He went to Rayl)ana in Umm Mundhir's house and said, 'If you wish me to free you and to marry you, I will do so. If you wish to remain in my domain and for me to have intercourse with you by that right, then that I will do.' She replied, 'Messenger of God, it would be easier on you and on me for me to remain in your domain.' And she remained in his domain, with him having intercourse with her, until she died." AI-Waqidl stated that Ibn Abu Dhi'b related to him, "I asked aI-Zuhrt about Rayl)ana, and he said, 'She was a bond-maid whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) had manumitted and then married. She remained in seclusion in her family and would say, "No one shall see me after the Messenger of God."'" AI-Waqidl went on, "This is, in our view, the most secure of the two alzadith. Her husband, prior to the Messenger of God (SAAS), was al-I;Iakam." AI-Waqidl stated that 'A~im b. 'Abd Allah b. aI-I;Iakam related to him, from 'Umar b. aI-I;Iakam, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) manumitted Rayl)ana, daughter of Zayd b. 'Amr b. Khanafa; she had been living with her husband who loved her and treated her honourably. She said, 'I will never go to anyone after him.' She was a beautiful woman. When Banu Qurana were taken captive, the prisoners were paraded before the Messenger of God (SAAS). Rayl)ana said, 'I was among those paraded before him; he gave orders, and I was placed aside. He had the practice of taking (booty for himself) on each occasion when there were war spoils. When I was placed aside, God selected me. The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent me to the home of Umm al-Mundhir, daughter of Qiys for some days until after he had executed the prisoners and divided up the slaves. He then came to see me, and I turned aside from him in modesty. He called me to him and sat me down before him, saying, "Ifyou choose God and His Messenger, the Messenger of God will choose you for himself." I replied, "I do choose God and His Messenger." When I accepted Islam, he manumitted me and married me, presenting as my dowry the weight (in silver) of 12 awqiyya and one nash,''' the sum he customarily gave to his wives. The wedding was conducted in the home ofUmm aI-Mundhir. He provided me the same expenses as to his other wives and required me to wear the veil.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) admired her greatly and wonld give her anything she asked of him. People would tell her, 'If you had asked the Messenger of God (SAAS), for Banu Qurana, he would have set them free.' She would reply, 'He was not alone with me until after he had divided up the slaves.' He would spend time aIone with her and desired her much. She remained with him until her death following his return from the I;ijjat al-wada'. He buried her at the al-Baqi' cemetery." His marriage to her was in Mu!).arram of 6 AH. Ibn Wahb stated that Yunus b. Yazid quoted al-Zuhri as having said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) received Ray!).ana as a captive from Banu Qurana. He manumitted her and she rejoined her family." Abu 'Ubayda Ma'mar b. al-Muthanna stated, "Ray!).ana was the daughter of Zayd b. Sham'un, ofBanu Qurana." Some say that she was from Banu Q!1rana and that she would be in one of the palm-groves reserved for charity, and that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would sometimes take a siesta with her there. He received her as a captive in Shawwal of4AH. Abu Bakr b. Abu Khaythama stated that A!).mad b. al-Miqdam related to him, quoting Zuhayr, from Sa'id, from Q;ltada, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) had two female slaves: Mariya, the Copt, and Ray!).a or Ray!).ana, daughter of Sham'un b. Zayd b. Khanafa, of Banu 'Amr b. Quray?'l. She had been married to a cousin of hers called 'Abd al-I;Iakam, so I have been told, and that she died before the death of the Prophet (SAAS)." Abu 'Ubayda Ma'mar b. al-Muthanna stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) had in his domain four bond-maids. These were Mariya, the Copt, and Ray!).ana of Banu Quray?a. He also had another beautiful bond-maid whom his wives kept from him for fear that she would gain favour with him over themselves. He also had a valuable bond-maid presented to him by Zaynab. He had abandoned her, because of Safiyya, daughter of I;Iuyayy, for the months of Dhu al-I;Iijja, al-Mu!).arram and Safar. When the Rabi' al-Awwal came in which he died, he was pleased to go in to Zaynab and she told him, 'I don't know with what to reward you!' And she presented the girl to him." Sayf b. 'Vmar narrated, from Sa'id b. 'Abd Allah, from Ibn Abu Mulayka, who quoted 'A'isha as having said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would sometimes share funds with Mariya and Ray!).ana, and at others he would leave them out. Abu Nu'aym quoted Abu Mu!).ammad b. 'Vmar al-Waqidi as having stated, "Ray!).ana died in 10 iIH; 'Vmar prayed over her and buried her in al-Baqi'. And to God be praise."
lessons: Hardship and loss of one's familiar world can be a gateway to spiritual transformation, as Rayhana's journey from captivity ultimately led her to choose faith freely.
Even in circumstances of profound grief and displacement, a person retains an inner dignity and the capacity to make meaningful choices about their identity and values.
Community members who intervene with wisdom and compassion, as Ibn Sa'ya did, can be instrumental in guiding others through their darkest moments toward healing.
practical_advice: When you feel your world has been turned upside down and your identity is uncertain, take time in seclusion to reflect rather than making hasty decisions driven purely by grief or defiance.
If you are supporting someone who has experienced trauma and loss, approach them with patience and gentle persuasion rather than pressure, giving them space to arrive at their own decisions.
In times of displacement or loss, look for trustworthy community figures who can offer guidance and a safe space, as Umm al-Mundhir provided for Rayhana during her transition.

title: The Children of the Prophet: His Sons and Daughters Born to Khadija and Mariya
summary: A detailed account of the Prophet's children, all born to Khadija except Ibrahim who was born to Mariya the Copt, including the names, birth order, and early deaths of his sons, and the taunt of being 'abtar' that prompted the revelation of Surat al-Kawthar.
story: INFORMATION CONCERNING HIS CHILDREN, MAY PRA YERS AND PEACE BE FOR THEM ALL. There is no disagreement over all his children having been born to Khadija, daughter of Khuwaylid, with the exception ofIbrahIm, who was born to Mariya, the Copt, daughter of Sham'un. Mu!).ammad b. Sa'd stated that Hisham b. al-Kalbi informed him, quoting his father, from Abu Sali!)., from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "The first son of the Messenger of God ($AAS) was al-Qlsim. Then came, in succession, Zaynab, 'Abd Allah, Umm Kulthum, Fatima and Ruqayya. Al-Qlsim died in Mecca; he was the first of his children to die. Then 'Abd Allah died. A1-'A~ b. Wa'il al-Sahmi stated, 'His line had been severed, and so he was abtar, "cut off". And so God, the Ahnighty and Glorious, sent down, "We have given abundance to you. And so pray to your Lord, and make sacrifice! It is your antagonist who is abtar, "cut oft""' (sUrat al-Kawthar; eVIII, v.1-3). He went on, "Thereafter Mariya bore him Ibrahim in Medina, in Dhu al-I:Iijja, 8 AB. He died at the age of eighteen months." Abu al-Faraj al-Mu'afa b. Zakariyya' al-Juwayrl stated that 'Abd al-Baqi b. Nafi' related to him, quoting Mul}ammad b. Zakariyya', quoting al-'Abbas b. Bakkar, quoting Mul}ammad b. Ziyad and al-Furat b. al-Sa'ib, from Maymun b. Mihran, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "Khadija bore the Prophet ($AAS) 'Abd Allah and after him there was a period without a child being born to him. When the Messenger of God ($AAS) was one day talking to a man, while al-'A~ b. Wa'illooked on, a man asked him (al-A~), 'Who is that?' He (a1-'A~) replied, 'That is al-abtar.' Among Quraysh, if a man were to have a child and then there was a delay in the next birth, they would say, 'This man is abtar'. And so God sent down, 'It is your antagonist who is the abtar.' This implied, 'It is the one angering you who is "cut off" from all good.'" He (Ibn 'Abbas) went on, "Then she bore him Zaynab, Ruqayya, al-Qlsim, al-Tahir, al-Munahar, al-Tayyib, al-Mutayyab, Umm Kulthum and then Fatima, the youngest of them. When Khadija had a child, she would give it to someone to nurse. Only she, however, nursed Fatima." Al-Haytham b. 'Adi stated that Hisham b. 'lJrwa related to him, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyab, from his father, who said, "The Prophet ($AAS) had two sons: ai-Tahir and al-Tayyib. One of them he also named 'Abd Shams, the other 'Abd al-'lJzza." This is somewhat objectionable. I>7 But God knows best. Mul}ammad b. 'A'idh stated, "AI-Walid b. Muslim informed me, from Sa'id b. 'Abd al-'AziZ, that Khadija gave birth to al-Qlsim, al-Tayyib, al-Tahir, Munahir, Zaynab, Ruqayya, Fatima and Umm Kulthum." AI-Zubayr b. Bakkar quoted his uncle Mu~'ab b. 'Abd Allah as having said, "Khadija bore al-Qlsim and ai-Tahir, who was also called al-Tayyib. AI--r-ahir was born after the beginning of the prophethood. He died when young; 'Abd Allah was another name given him. Then were born Fatima, Zaynab, Ruqayya and Umm Kulthum." AI-Zubayr also stated that Ibrahim b. al-Mundhir related to him, from Ibn Wahb, from Ibn Lahi'a, from Abu al-Aswad, that Khadija gave birth to al-Qlsim, al-Tahir, al-Tayyib, 'Abd Allah, Zaynab, Ruqayya, Fatima, and Umm Kulthum. (He went on) "Mul.>ammad b. Fagala quoted to me a sheikh who had been a contempotary ofthe Prophet (SAAS), as having said, 'Khadlja bore al-Q;i.sim and 'Abd Allah. AI-Qasim lived until he could walk; 'Abd Allah died while still small." A1-Zubayr b. Bakkar also stated, "In the jahiliyya Khadlja was known as 'a1-Tahira, daughter of Khuwaylid'. She bore to the Messenger of God (SAAS) al-Q;i.sim, his elder son, by whose name he was himself known. After al-Q;i.sim came Zaynab and 'Abd Allah, who was known as 'a1-Tayyib', 'the good', and as ai-Tahir, 'the pure'. The latter was born after the commencement of the· prophethood and died while small. Then she bore him, in order, his daughters Umm Kulthum, Fatima and Ruqayya. AI-Qasim died in Mecca; he was the first of his children to die. And then 'Abd Allah died. "Later Mariya, daughter of Sham'un, bore him IbrahIm. She was a Copt; a1-Muqawqis, Governor of Alexandria, had presented her, along with her sister ShInn and a eunuch named Mabur. The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave Shlrln to .l;Iassan b. Thabit, and she bore him his son 'Abd a1-Ral.>man. The line of .l;Iassan b. Thabit"
lessons: The loss of children is among the most profound griefs a person can endure, yet the Prophet's steadfast faith and continued mission remind us that divine purpose endures beyond personal tragedy.

When enemies mock us in our pain, using our grief as a weapon, God's response — as in the revelation of Surat al-Kawthar — reminds us that true legacy and abundance come from God alone, not from worldly continuity.
The care Khadija showed by personally nursing Fatima, even while delegating the nursing of other children, illustrates how grief and loss can deepen the bonds of love between those who remain.
practical_advice: When you experience the loss of someone dear and others use that loss to diminish you, resist the urge to prove your worth through worldly measures and instead reconnect with the spiritual abundance God has given you.

In moments of grief over lost loved ones, reflect on the legacy of love and values they left behind rather than focusing solely on their absence, as the Prophet's children who survived carried his mission forward.
If someone you know is being mocked or belittled during a time of personal loss, stand by them with words of affirmation and remind them that true worth is not measured by what critics say.

title: The Children of the Prophet: Overview of His Sons and Daughters
summary: An overview of the Prophet Muhammad's children, their births, marriages, and deaths, including his sons al-Qasim, Abd Allah, and Ibrahim, and his daughters Zaynab, Ruqayya, Umm Kulthum, and Fatima.
story: pure'. The latter was born after the commencement of the· prophethood and died while small. Then she bore him, in order, his daughters Umm Kulthum, Fatima and Ruqayya. AI-Qasim died in Mecca; he was the first of his children to die. And then 'Abd Allah died. "Later Mariya, daughter of Sham'un, bore him IbrahIm. She was a Copt; a1-Muqawqis, Governor of Alexandria, had presented her, along with her sister ShInn and a eunuch named Mabur. The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave Shlrln to .l;Iassan b. Thabit, and she bore him his son 'Abd a1-Ral.>man. The line of .l;Iassan b. Thabit died out." Abu Bakr b. al-BarqI.stated, "It is said that 'ai-Tahir' was 'a1-Tayyib' and also "Abd Allah'. It is also said that al-Tayyib and al-Mutayyab were twins, as also were ai-Tahir and al-Mutahhar." AI-Mufaggal b. Ghassan stated, from Al.>mad b. .l;Ianbal, quoting 'Abd al-Razzaq, quoting Ibn ]urayj, from Mujahid, who said, "AI-Qasim, son of the Prophet (SAAS), remained alive for seven days, then died." AI-Mufaggal went on, "This is an error; in fact, he lived for seventeen months." The baji:;: Abu Nu'aym quoted Mujahid as having said, "AI-Q;i.sim died when he was seven days old." A1-Zuhn said that he was two years old. Q;J.tada stated that he lived until he could walk. Hisham b. CUrwa stated, "It was the Iraqis who invented reference to al-Tayyib and ai-Tahir. Our scholars say, (that his children were) 'Abd al-CUzza, 'Abd Manaf, and a1-Q;i.sim; the females were Ruqayya, Umm Kulthum and Fa!ima." Thus also Ibn 'Asakir gave it and it is objectionable; what he denied is what is commonly accepted. He also left out any reference - which is obligatory - to Zaynab. But God knows best. Regarding Zaynab, 'Abd al-Razzaq quoted Ibn]urayj as having said, "Several people told me that Zaynab was the oldest of the daughters of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Fa!ima was the youngest of them and the one he most favoured. Abu a1-'A~ b. al-RabI' married Zaynab and she bore him 'All and Uniama. It was Umama whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) would carry during prayer. When he prostrated, he would put her down; when he stood up, he carried her."
lessons: The Prophet experienced the profound grief of losing multiple children, reminding us that loss and hardship are part of every human life, regardless of one's closeness to God.
Family is a source of both joy and sorrow; cherishing loved ones while they are present is a deeply prophetic value.
Scholarly differences in narrating history teach us humility — certainty is not always possible, and intellectual honesty requires acknowledging what we do not know.
practical_advice: When grieving the loss of a loved one, allow yourself to feel the sadness while trusting that God's plan encompasses both life and death.
Reflect on your family relationships today and express gratitude for those who are still present in your life.
When you encounter conflicting information or uncertainty, practice intellectual humility by acknowledging the limits of your knowledge rather than forcing false certainty.

title: Zaynab bint Muhammad: Her Marriage, Migration, and Martyrdom
summary: The Prophet's eldest daughter Zaynab suffered a serious injury during her migration to Madinah, leading to her eventual death, which was regarded as a martyrdom.
story: Perhaps this was following the death of her mother, in 8 AH. This was what a1-WaqidI, Q;J.tada, 'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr b. .l;Iazm and others stated. She seems to have been a small child at the time. But God knows best. 'All b. Abu TaIib, may God be pleased with him, married her after the death of Fatima. The death ofZaynab, may God be pleased with her, occurred in 8 AH. Q;uada stated this from 'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr b. I;lazm, from Khalifa b. Khayyat, from Abu Bakr b. Abu Khaythama and from others also. Q;itada also quoted Ibn I;lazm as having stated that she died at the beginning of 8 AH. I;lammad b. Salama reported from Hisham b. 'Orwa, from his father, that when Zaynab left on the Hijra, a man pushed her and she fell on a rock and had a miscarriage, remaining thereafter in pain until she died. People considered that she died a martyr.
lessons: Zaynab's patient endurance of physical suffering sustained over years is a powerful model of perseverance through prolonged hardship.
A person can face injustice and violence while emigrating for their faith and still be elevated in spiritual rank — hardship does not signal abandonment by God.
The recognition of Zaynab as a martyr shows that suffering borne with faith and sincerity carries eternal significance, even when the world does not witness or acknowledge it.
practical_advice: When you are enduring prolonged pain or chronic difficulty, remind yourself that consistent patience through hardship is itself a form of spiritual elevation.
If you have experienced trauma or injury caused by the cruelty of others, know that your suffering is seen by God and is not wasted.
Seek medical and emotional support when dealing with long-term pain, and do not feel that needing help contradicts trust in God.

title: Ruqayya bint Muhammad: Her Marriage to Uthman and Her Death at Badr
summary: Ruqayya, the Prophet's daughter, was divorced by Abu Lahab's son before marriage was consummated, then married Uthman, migrated with him, and died while the Prophet was winning victory at Badr.
story: Ruqayya was initially married by her cousin, 'Otba b. Abu Lahab, whose brother 'Utayba b. Abu Lahab married her own sister, Umm Kulthum. Both men divorced the sisters, before consummating marriage with them, out ofanger at the Messenger of God (SAAS), when God sent down, "May both hands of Abu Lahab perish and may he perish! His wealth and earnings will not avail him! He will bnrn in fire of flames, along with his wife, that bearer of fuel, around her neck a twisted rope!" (surat al-Lahab; eXI, v.l~5). 'Othman b. 'Affan, may God be pleased with him, married Ruqayya, and she went into exile with him to Abyssinia; he is said to have been the first person to leave for there. Thereafter they both returned to Mecca, as we narrated above. They then went into exile to Medina. She bore him his son, 'Abd Allah, who lived on to age six. A cock pecked him in the eyes and he died. 'Othman was initially named for him, and then later for his son 'Amr. She died after the Messenger of God (SAAS) had won victory at Badr, on the yawm al-furqiin, when the two forces clashed. When the messenger bearing the good news of the victory, Zayd b. I;laritha, that is, reached Medina, he found them levelling the soil over her grave. 'Othman had remained with her nursing her, by orders of the Messenger of God (SAAS). The latter gave him his salary and his share of the booty. Following his return, the Messenger of God (SAAS) married 'Othman to Ruqayya's sister Umm Kulthum. 'Othman was therefore known as dhu al-nurayn, "he who had the two lights".
lessons: Being rejected or humiliated by others — as Ruqayya was when divorced out of spite — does not define one's worth; God's plan always holds something better.
The Prophet's compassion in allowing Uthman to stay with his dying wife, and then compensating him fairly, shows that caring for family is honoured even in moments of communal triumph.
The simultaneity of victory at Badr and the loss of Ruqayya reminds us that joy and grief often arrive together in life, and both must be held with faith.
practical_advice: If you have been rejected or treated unfairly by someone, resist the urge to define yourself by that rejection — trust that God's redirection holds a better outcome.
When someone you love is ill, prioritise being present with them; the Prophet himself validated this by keeping Uthman at Ruqayya's side during a critical moment.
In moments when good news and loss arrive simultaneously, allow yourself to grieve and celebrate without guilt — both emotions are valid and human.

title: Umm Kulthum bint Muhammad: Her Marriage to Uthman and Her Death
summary: Umm Kulthum, the Prophet's daughter, married Uthman after her sister Ruqayya's death and died without bearing him children, prompting the Prophet's remarkable praise of Uthman.
story: Following his return, the Messenger of God (SAAS) married 'Othman to Ruqayya's sister Umm Kulthum. 'Othman was therefore known as dhu al-nurayn, "he who had the two lights". Umm KUlthum died in his quarters in Sha'ban of9 AH; she bore 'Othman no children. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had said, "If I had had three daughters, I would have married them all to 'Othman!" In one account he is quoted as having said, "If! had had ten daughters, I would have married them all to 'Othman!"
lessons: The Prophet's extraordinary praise of Uthman teaches us to recognise and affirm righteous character in those around us — sincere praise is a prophetic act.
A marriage without children is not a failed or lesser marriage; its worth is measured in character, love, and faithfulness, not in outcomes alone.
The Prophet experienced the grief of losing daughter after daughter, yet continued to lead, to praise, and to guide — grief and leadership can coexist.
practical_advice: Make a habit of sincerely affirming the good character of those around you; the Prophet's words about Uthman were a gift of honour that outlasted all of them.
If you or someone you love is grieving the absence of children in a marriage, resist measuring the relationship's worth by that absence alone.
When you face repeated losses, look for the continuity of purpose and meaning that can carry you through — the Prophet's life demonstrates this beautifully.

title: The Birth of Ibrahim ibn Muhammad from Mariya al-Qibtiyya
summary: Ibrahim, the Prophet's son from Mariya the Copt, was born in Dhul Hijja of 8 AH, and the angel Jibreel came to congratulate the Prophet and confirm the child's name.
story: IbrahIm was born to Mariya al-Q!btlya, as stated above. His birth was in Dhu al-I;lijja of 8 AH. It has been narrated from Ibn LahI'a and others, quoting 'Abd al-Ra!:lman b. Ziyad as having said, "When she became pregnant with IbrahIm, Gabriel came to him and said, 'Peace be upon you, father of IbrahIm! God has given you a son from Mariya, his mother, and He commands you to name him IbrahIm. He has blessed you in him, and has made him a source of delight for you in this world and the hereafter.'" The bafi,!, Abu Bakr al-Bazzilr narrated, from Mu!:lammad b. Miskln, from 'Uthman b. Sali!:l, from Ibn LahI'a, from 'AqII and Yazld b. Abu I;lablb, from al-ZuhrI, from Anas, who said, "When his son IbrahIm was born to the Prophet (SAAS), he had doubts about him. And so Gabriel came to him and said, 'Peace be upon you, father of IbrahIm!'" And Asbilt quoted al-Suddl - Isma'il b. 'Abd al-Ra!:lman, that is - as having said, "I asked Anas b. Malik as follows, 'What age did IbrahIm, the son of the Prophet (SAAS) attain?' He replied, 'He filled up his cradle. Ifhe had remained alive, he would have been a prophet. However, he was not to be kept alive, for your Prophet (SAAS), was the last of the prophets.'"
lessons: The angel Jibreel's greeting of the Prophet as 'father of Ibrahim' at the child's birth shows that God celebrates the gift of new life with us and that children are a blessing to be received with gratitude.
The narration that Ibrahim was kept from prophethood because Muhammad was the last prophet teaches us that God's wisdom in what He withholds is as great as His wisdom in what He grants.
Even the Prophet experienced a moment of doubt at Ibrahim's birth, showing us that uncertainty is a natural human response, not a sign of weak faith.
practical_advice: When a child is born in your family or community, treat it as a moment of divine generosity worth genuine celebration and gratitude.
When you do not understand why something good was taken away or withheld, trust that God's larger plan — like the sealing of prophethood — holds a wisdom beyond what you can currently see.
If you have ever felt doubt or anxiety in a moment that should be joyful, know that even the Prophet experienced this; be compassionate with yourself.

title: The Prophet's Tender Visits to Ibrahim During His Nursing
summary: The Prophet would frequently and eagerly visit his infant son Ibrahim at his wet nurse's home in the outskirts of Madinah, holding and kissing him with great tenderness.
story: Abu ¥aqa stated that Abu Khaythama related to him, quoting Isma'il b. Ibrahim, from Ayyub, from 'Amr b. Sa'id, from Anas, who said, "I never saw any person more kind with children than the Messenger of God (SAAS). Ibrahim was being suckled in the heights of Medina. He would hurry off there, in our company. He would enter the house while Ibrahim was being fed. His wet nurse lived among us. He would take hold of him (Ibrahim) and kiss him, then leave." 'Amr said, "When Ibrahim died, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'IbrahIm is my son; he died on the breast. He has two wet nurses who will complete his suckling in paradise.'"
lessons: The Prophet's eagerness to visit Ibrahim and his physical tenderness — holding and kissing him — is a powerful prophetic model of engaged, affectionate fatherhood.
The image of the Prophet hurrying to see his infant son conveys that spending time with those we love is not a distraction from purpose but an expression of it.
The Prophet's consoling words that Ibrahim's nursing would be completed in paradise show us how faith transforms grief — loss is not the end of love or care.
practical_advice: Make time to be physically present and affectionate with the children in your life; the Prophet's example shows this is spiritually significant, not trivial.
When you feel the pull to spend time with loved ones, honour that impulse — rushing to be with someone you love is a noble and prophetic instinct.

When grieving the loss of a child or infant, hold onto the belief that care and love transcend this life; the Prophet's words about paradise completing what this world could not are a source of real comfort.

title: The Death of Ibrahim ibn Muhammad and the Prophet's Weeping
summary: When Ibrahim, the Prophet's infant son, died at sixteen months, the Prophet wept deeply and said words that have become a foundation of Islamic teaching on grief: 'The eye weeps and the heart is sad, but we will not speak what would anger the Lord.'
story: Ibn 'Asakir narrated from a badith of Mu1}ammad b. Isma'!l b. Samura, from Mu1}arnmad b. aI-l;Iasan aI-Asadi, from Abu Shayba, from Anas, who said, "When Ibrahim died, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Do not wrap him in his shroud until I look at him.' He then carne and leaned over him and wept so hard that his beard and his sides trembled." I note that narratives from Abu Shayba are invalid. He (Ibn 'Asakir) proceeded to narrate from a badith of Muslim b. Khiilid alCZanji, from Ibn Khaytham, from Shahr b. l;Iawshab, from Asm;l>, daughter of ¥ azid b. aI-Sakn, who said, "When Ibrahim died, the Messenger of God (SAAS) wept and Abu Bakr and 'Umar told him, 'You are the one most cognisant of God's right.' He replied, 'The eye weeps and the heart is sad. We will not speak what would anger the Lord. If it were not a matter'"
lessons: The Prophet's unrestrained weeping at Ibrahim's death — trembling with grief — shows that expressing sorrow is not weakness but a deeply human and prophetic response to loss.
His words 'the eye weeps and the heart is sad, but we will not speak what would anger the Lord' offer the perfect balance: feel everything, but anchor your words and actions in faith.
Grief does not mean a lack of trust in God; the Prophet trusted God completely and still wept — these two things are not in contradiction.
practical_advice: When you lose someone you love, give yourself full permission to weep and to feel the weight of that loss — the Prophet's example sanctifies your grief.
In moments of deep pain, be careful with your words; the Prophet's principle of not speaking what would anger God is a guide to protecting your heart during its most vulnerable moments.
If someone around you is grieving deeply, resist the urge to rush them toward acceptance; instead, sit with them in their sorrow as Abu Bakr and Umar sat with the Prophet.

title: Ibrahim's Burial and the Prophet's Words of Comfort About Paradise
summary: After Ibrahim's death, the Prophet instructed that he be buried in the al-Baqi' cemetery and offered comfort by stating that Ibrahim has a wet nurse in paradise who will complete his suckling.
story: Imam A!:lmad stated that 'Abd al-Ra!:lman b. Mahdi related to him, quoting Sufyan, from al~SuddI, from Anas b. Malik, who said, "If IbrahIm, son of the Prophet (SAAS), had lived, he would have been ~iddiqan nabiyyan, 'one fully trusting (in God) and a prophet'." Abu 'Abd Allah b. Manda stated that Mu!:lammad b. Sa'd and Mu!:lammad b. IbrahIm related to him, quoting Mu!:lammad b. 'Uthman al-'AbsI, quoting Minjab, quoting Abu 'Amir al-AsadI, quoting Sufyan, from al-SuddI, from Anas, who said, "IbrahIm, son ofthe Prophet (SAAS), died while a child of 16 months. And' the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Bury him in the al-BaqI' cemetery. He has a nursemaid who will complete suckling him in paradise.'" Jarir and Abu 'Awana narrated, from aI-A'mash, from Muslim b. SabiI; Abu al-OuI;a, from al-Bara', who said, "Ibrahim, the son of the Messenger of God (SAAS), died while a child of 16 months. he said, 'Bury him in al-Baqi'; he has a nursemaid in paradise.'" AI;mad narrated it from a badith ofJabir, from 'Amir, from aI-Bara'. Sufyan al-Thawri narrated it thus from Firas, from al-Sha'bi, from al-Bara' b. 'Azib similarly. AI-ThaWri also narrated it from Abu IsI;aq, from aI-Bara'. Ibn <Asakir traced it through 'Attab b. MuI;ammad b. Shawdhab, from 'Abd Allah b. Abu Awfa, who said, "Ibrahim died and the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'The rest of his suckling will take place in paradise.'" Abu ¥aqa al-Mawsili stated that Zakariyya' b. ¥ a1;J.ya al-Wasip related to him, quoting Hushaym, from Isma'il, who said, "I asked Ibn Abu Awfa" - or "I heard him asked" - about Ibrahim, son of the Prophet (SAAS). He replied, 'He died while still young. If it had been decreed that there be a prophet after the Prophet (SAAS), he would certainly have lived.' Ibn 'Asakir narrated, from a badith ofA1;J.mad b. Mu1}ammad b. Sa'id aI-l;Iafi?, quoting 'Ubayd b. Ibrahim aI-Ju'fi, quoting al-l;Iasan b. Abu 'Abd Allah aI-Fara', quoting Mus'ab b. Salam, from Abu l;Iarnza aI-Thumali, from Abu Ja'far Mu1}ammad b. 'Ali, from Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'If IbrahIm had lived, he would have been a prophet.'"
lessons: The Prophet's instruction to bury Ibrahim in al-Baqi' — a blessed cemetery — and his words about paradise completing Ibrahim's care show that honouring the deceased is an act of love, not merely ritual.
The repeated narration that 'Ibrahim has a nursemaid in paradise' is one of Islam's most tender consolations for parents who lose infants, affirming that God's care for a child does not end at death.
The observation that Ibrahim's death was tied to Muhammad being the last prophet teaches us that sometimes what we lose serves a larger divine wisdom we cannot fully comprehend in this life.
practical_advice: If you have lost a child or know someone who has, share the Prophet's words about paradise with gentleness — the image of continued care in the afterlife can be a genuine source of comfort.
Attend to the proper burial and honouring of those you lose; the care you give to the deceased is an extension of the love you had for them in life.
When a loss feels completely meaningless, consider that some of God's wisdom operates at a scale larger than one life — this perspective does not erase pain but can soften despair.

title: The Death of Ibrahim, Son of the Prophet, and His Grief
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) wept deeply upon the death of his infant son Ibrahim at 18 months old, expressing profound grief while maintaining trust in God and speaking words of dignified sorrow.
story: wept so hard that his beard and his sides trembled." I note that narratives from Abu Shayba are invalid. He (Ibn 'Asakir) proceeded to narrate from a hadith of Muslim b. Khalid al-Zanji, from Ibn Khaytham, from Shahr b. Hawshab, from Asma', daughter of Yazid b. al-Sakn, who said, "When Ibrahim died, the Messenger of God (SAAS) wept and Abu Bakr and 'Umar told him, 'You are the one most cognisant of God's right.' He replied, 'The eye weeps and the heart is sad. We will not speak what would anger the Lord. If it were not a matter of a sincere promise and a universal compact, and that the latter of us will follow the former, we would grieve for you, Ibrahim, more severely than we have ever mourned before. We are deeply saddened for you, Ibrahim.'" Imam Ahmad stated that Aswad b. 'Amir related to him, quoting Isra'il, from Jabir, from al-Sha'bi, from al-Bara', who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) prayed over his son Ibrahim. He died at the age of 16 months. He said, 'There are those in paradise who will complete his suckling, and he is siddiq, innocent, righteous.'" It is also narrated from a hadith of al-Hakam b. 'Uyayna, from al-Sha'bi, from al-Bara'. Abu Ya'la stated that al-Qi'wariri related to him, quoting Isma'il b. Abu Khalid, from Ibn Abu Awfa, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) prayed over his son, and I prayed behind him. He spoke the words Allahu akbar! 'God is most Great!' four times." Yunus b. Bukayr narrated, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, quoting Muhammad b. Talha b. Yazid b. Rukana, who said, "Ibrahim, son of the Messenger of God (SAAS), died while a child of 18 months, and he did not pray over him." Ibn 'Asakir narrated, from a hadith of Ishaq b. Muhammad al-Farwi, from 'Isa b. 'Abd Allah b. Muhammad b. 'Umar b. 'Ali b. Abu Talib, from his father, from his grandfather, who quoted 'Ali as having said that when Ibrahim, the son of the Messenger of God (SAAS), died he sent 'Ali b. Abu Talib to his mother, Mariya, the Copt, who was in an upper chamber. 'Ali carried him out in a basket, placing him before himself on his horse, and brought him to the Messenger of God (SAAS). He washed him and wrapped him in his shroud, then carried him out to the people. He buried him in the alley adjoining the house of Muhammad b. Zayd. 'Ali went into his grave, smoothed (the soil) over him and buried him. He then emerged and sprinkled water over his grave. The Messenger of God (SAAS) placed his hand in his grave and said, "I swear by God, he was a prophet and the son of a prophet." And the Messenger of God (SAAS) wept, as did the Muslims around him. The sound they all made arose above them. 'Ali then quoted the Messenger of God (SAAS), as having said, "The eye weeps and the heart is sad. We will not speak what would anger the Lord. We are deeply saddened for you, Ibrahim." Al-Waqidi stated, "Ibrahim, son of the Messenger of God (SAAS), died on Tuesday the 10th of Rabi' al-Awwal, 10 AH. He was 18 months old. He had been living among Banu Mazin b. al-Najjar, in the home of Umm Barza, daughter of al-Mundhir. He was buried in the al-Baqi' cemetery."
lessons: Grief and tears are a natural human response to loss and do not contradict faith; the Prophet wept openly for his son while still trusting in God's will.
True patience is not the suppression of emotion but the restraint of one's tongue from speaking what displeases God, even in the depths of sorrow.
The death of a beloved child is among the greatest of trials, and the promise of reunion in the next life is a source of genuine comfort for the grieving believer.
practical_advice: When experiencing grief, allow yourself to cry and feel the pain — this is healthy and human — while being mindful not to let despair lead you to words or actions that conflict with your values.
In moments of loss, repeat words of dignified sorrow that affirm your trust in God rather than words of complaint or bitterness, as the Prophet modeled with 'The eye weeps and the heart is sad.'
Find solace in the Islamic belief that children who pass away early are in paradise and that separation from loved ones is temporary, allowing this perspective to soften but not deny your grief.

title: The Solar Eclipse on the Day of Ibrahim's Death and the Prophet's Correction of Misconception
summary: When the sun eclipsed on the day Ibrahim died, people assumed it was because of his death, but the Prophet publicly corrected this false belief and clarified that celestial events are signs of God alone.
story: I note that I have stated above that the sun was eclipsed on the day of his death. People said, "It has eclipsed at the death of Ibrahim!" The Messenger of God (SAAS) made a public address in which he said, "The sun and the moon are two signs of God, Almighty and Glorious is He. Neither of them are eclipsed at either the death or the life of anyone."
lessons: Even in the midst of personal grief, a leader has the responsibility to correct false beliefs and protect the community from superstition.
Natural phenomena are signs pointing to the Creator and should not be connected to the honor or importance of any individual, no matter how beloved.
Truth takes precedence over sentiment; the Prophet corrected the misconception about his own son without allowing personal loss to cloud his commitment to correct belief.
practical_advice: When experiencing personal hardship, resist the temptation to interpret unrelated events as signs or omens — ground your understanding in established principles rather than emotional reasoning.
Seek out accurate knowledge in moments of confusion or emotional vulnerability, as misinformation can compound grief and lead to harmful beliefs.
If you find yourself in a position to correct a misconception, do so gently but clearly, as the Prophet did by making a public address rather than dismissing the concern privately.

title: Usama b. Zayd Appointed Commander at a Young Age and the Prophet's Defense of Him
summary: The Prophet appointed the young Usama b. Zayd as commander of an expeditionary force, and when Companions complained about his youth, the Prophet defended him publicly and affirmed his and his father's worthiness.
story: In the final days of his life, the Messenger of God (SAAS) appointed Usama a commander of an expeditionary force. His age at that time was some 18 or 19. At the time of the death of the Prophet (SAAS), he was commander of a large army that included 'Umar b. al-Khattab and, it is said, Abu Bakr, "the trusting", as well; this, however, is unlikely, since the Messenger of God (SAAS) had appointed Abu Bakr to the Imamate. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) died, the army of Usama was encamped at al-Jurf, as we have narrated above. Abu Bakr requested Usama to release 'Umar b. al-Khattab so that he could remain in Medina with him to give him the benefit of his counsel. Usama did release him. Abu Bakr reaffirmed the commission of Usama's army after many of the Companions had changed their minds about it. He rejected their view, saying, "I will not, by God, take down a flag mounted by the Messenger of God (SAAS)!" And so they proceeded forth as far as the borders of al-Balqa' in Syria, where his father Zayd, Ja'far b. Abu Talib and 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha, may God be pleased with them all, had been killed. He attacked that territory, acquired booty and prisoners and then returned home safe and sound. This is why 'Umar b. al-Khattab, may God be pleased with him, never failed to address him with the words, "Peace be upon you, commander!" When the Messenger of God (SAAS) awarded him the commander's banner, some people complained at his having been placed in charge. The Messenger of God (SAAS) therefore made an address in which he said, "For you to complain about his being in charge would be tantamount to your complaining at his father's having been given command previously. I swear by God, he was fully worthy of the command and this man, after him, has my extremely high regard."
lessons: True leadership is based on character and capability, not age or social status, and those who judge others by superficial criteria miss the deeper qualities that matter most.
When you are appointed to a role and others doubt your worthiness, the best response is to fulfill your responsibility with excellence, as Usama did by completing his mission successfully.
Loyalty to a commitment made by a righteous authority is itself an act of integrity, as Abu Bakr demonstrated by refusing to abandon what the Prophet had established.
practical_advice: If you face skepticism or rejection because of your age, background, or appearance, focus on doing the work with excellence rather than expending your energy defending yourself to critics.
When others undermine your confidence, seek out those who genuinely see your potential and allow their trust to motivate you rather than dwelling on those who doubt you.

If you are in a position of authority like Abu Bakr and face pressure to abandon a sound decision, hold firm to what is right and articulate clearly why the original course of action must be maintained.

title: The Prophet's Love for Usama b. Zayd and His High Status
summary: The Prophet openly expressed his love for Usama b. Zayd, holding him alongside al-Hasan in his lap, and this love was so well-known that 'A'isha encouraged others to love Usama as a reflection of loving the Prophet.
story: It is established in the Sahih collection of al-Bukhari that Usama, may God be pleased with him, said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) would take me and al-Hasan (in his lap) and say, 'O God, I love these two; may You love them both.'" From al-Sha'bi, 'A'isha is quoted as having said, "Let whoever loves God and His Messenger love Usama b. Zayd." Therefore, when 'Umar b. al-Khattab set the salaries in the diwan system, he assigned 5,000 (dirhams) for Usama, while he gave his own son 'Abd Allah 4,000. He was asked about this (by 'Abd Allah) and replied, "He was more highly favoured by the Messenger of God (SAAS) than you were; and his father was more highly favoured than your own!"
lessons: Being loved and valued by the righteous is among the greatest honors a person can receive, and reflecting the qualities that earn such love is a worthy lifelong pursuit.
A just leader honors people according to their true merit rather than nepotism or personal connection, as 'Umar demonstrated by giving Usama a higher stipend than his own son.
Love that is rooted in faith and good character transcends the social divisions of lineage, appearance, and status.
practical_advice: Reflect on the qualities in yourself that are truly worth loving — your sincerity, your character, your service to others — and invest in developing these rather than seeking external validation.
Practice fairness in how you treat and recognize those around you, honoring people based on their actual contributions rather than their relationship to you.
When you feel unloved or overlooked, remember that the love of the righteous is earned through sincere character, and focus on cultivating the inner qualities that draw such love.

title: Doubts About Usama's Parentage Resolved by the Lineage Expert
summary: Ignorant people cast doubt on Usama's parentage because he was dark-skinned while his father Zayd was light-skinned, but a lineage expert confirmed their kinship by observing their feet, which delighted the Prophet greatly.
story: He was as black as night and flat-nosed. He was sweet-tempered and good-looking, tall, eloquent, knowledgeable and masterly. May God be pleased with him. His father had similar qualities, except for being extremely pale-skinned. For this reason certain ignorant people express suspicions about Usama's parentage. On one occasion Mujazziz al-Mudliji passed by them when they were both lying asleep in an outer wrap, their feet being visible — Usama's being black, and his father's white. Mujazziz exclaimed, "Glory be to God! These feet are of the same lineage!" The Messenger of God (SAAS) was most impressed by this comment and went in to see 'A'isha, beaming with delight. He said, "Would you believe that Mujazziz has just looked at Zayd b. Haritha and Usama b. Zayd and commented, 'These feet are of the same lineage!'" Because of this hadith and the decision reached and its acceptance, scholars of the traditions such as al-Shafi'i and Ahmad give credence to such qafa in resolving questions involving mixed lineage, as is established in the appropriate place (in the texts).
lessons: Prejudice based on appearance reveals ignorance rather than truth, and those who judge others by skin color or physical difference diminish themselves, not those they judge.
God's wisdom is reflected in the diversity of human appearance, and superficial differences do not erase the deep bonds of family, faith, and character.
The Prophet's visible delight at the vindication of Usama's lineage shows that it is right to feel joy when truth overcomes slander and a wronged person is vindicated.
practical_advice: If you have faced discrimination or doubt cast upon your identity or belonging because of your appearance, hold firmly to the truth of who you are — slander and ignorance do not define you.
When you witness someone being wrongly judged based on superficial characteristics, speak up in their defense as the expert Mujazziz did, even if only by stating the obvious truth.
Channel the Prophet's response of joy and eagerness to share good news when a loved one is vindicated — celebrating truth and justice is itself an act of faith.

title: Abu Rafi' Refuses to Take from Charity Funds and the Prophet Affirms His Integrity
summary: When a man of Banu Makhzum invited Abu Rafi', a freed-man of the Prophet, to accompany him to collect charity funds, Abu Rafi' refused until he asked the Prophet, who confirmed that charity is not permitted for the household and freed-men of the Prophet.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that both Muhammad b. Ja'far and Bahz stated, "Shu'ba narrated to us, from al-Hakam, from Ibn Abu Rafi', from Abu Rafi', that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent a man of Banu Makhzum to take charge of the sadaqa, 'the charity funds'. The man said to Abu Rafi', 'Come along with me, and you will get some of it.' 'No!' replied Abu Rafi'. 'Not until I go to the Messenger of God and ask him.' And so he did go to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he replied, 'Charity funds are not permitted for us; the freed-man of a family is one of them (in this regard).'"
lessons: Integrity means pausing before accepting something that may not be rightfully yours, even when it seems convenient or harmless, and seeking clarity before proceeding.
The Prophet's household and those connected to them maintained a standard of scrupulousness regarding public funds that is a model for those in positions of trust.
Checking with a trusted authority before taking an action you are uncertain about is a sign of wisdom and sincerity, not weakness.
practical_advice: When you are offered something that feels too easy or you are unsure whether you are entitled to it, pause and verify before accepting rather than rationalizing why it should be fine.
Develop the habit of asking knowledgeable and trustworthy people when you face ethical ambiguity, as Abu Rafi' did by going directly to the Prophet rather than relying on his own judgment alone.

Build a reputation for scrupulousness in your professional and community life so that your integrity is never in question — this is a lasting form of honor that endures beyond any short-term material gain.

title: Abu Rafi' and the Prohibition of Charity for the Prophet's Household
summary: Abu Rafi', a freed-man of the Prophet, was invited to accompany a charity collector to receive a share, but first sought the Prophet's permission. The Prophet clarified that charity funds are not permitted for the Prophet's household or their freed-men.
story: the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent a man of Banu Makhzum to take charge of the ~adaqa, 'the charity funds'. The man said to Abu Rafi', 'Come along with me, and you will get some of it.' 'No!' replied Abu Rafi'. 'Not until I go to the Messenger of God and ask him.' And so he did go to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he replied, 'Charity funds are not permitted for us; the freed-man of a family is one of them (in this regard).'
lessons: Seeking clarification before acting on assumptions demonstrates integrity and avoids mistakes.
Religious rulings apply equally to all members of a community, including those closest to the Prophet.
Pausing before accepting what seems like a benefit, in order to verify its permissibility, is a sign of God-consciousness.
practical_advice: Before accepting any benefit or opportunity, take a moment to verify that it is lawful and appropriate for your situation.
When in doubt about an ethical matter, consult a trusted and knowledgeable source rather than assuming.
Model Abu Rafi's example by prioritizing principle over personal gain, even when the benefit seems harmless.

title: The Prophet Shares His Blanket with Abu Rafi' in the Cold of Khaybar
summary: During the bitter cold of the battle of Khaybar, Abu Rafi' could find no one to share a blanket with him. The Prophet personally covered him with his own blanket, and they slept together until morning.
story: Abu Yac'la narrated it in his musnad hadith collection, also from Abu Rafi', that they suffered from extreme cold at the battle of Khaybar. And the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Whoever has a blanket should share it with those who do not have one.' Abu Rafi' went on, 'I could not find anyone to share their blanket with me. So I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he threw his coverlet over me. We slept until morning. Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) found a snake at his feet and he said, 'Abu Rafi'! Kill it! Kill it!''
lessons: True leadership means meeting the needs of those around you personally, without delegating compassion.
No one in a community should be left without basic comfort when others have it to share.
Even in the midst of hardship and military campaigns, acts of human warmth and care remain essential.
practical_advice: When you see someone left out or struggling while others are comfortable, take the initiative to include them rather than waiting for someone else to act.
If you are feeling isolated or forgotten, reach out directly to those in a position to help — as Abu Rafi' went to the Prophet — rather than suffering in silence.
In times of communal hardship, check on those around you to ensure no one is bearing the cold alone.

title: Anasa's Loyal Service and His Participation at Badr
summary: Anasa b. Ziyad, a freed-man of the Prophet, served as a gatekeeper who controlled access to the Prophet during gatherings. He participated in the battle of Badr, and there is scholarly disagreement over whether he was martyred there.
story: Another slave was Ansa b. Ziyad Abu Mishrah, also known as Abu Misrah. He was of mixed lineage and from al-Sarrat. He was a muhajiri and participated at the battle of Badr, according to 'Urwa, al-Zuhri, Musa b. 'Uqba, Muhammad b. Ishaq, al-Bukhari and others. They stated, 'He was one of those who would allow access to the Messenger of God (SAAS), when he held a meeting.' Khalifa b. Khayyat narrated in his book, ''Ali b. Muhammad stated, from 'Abd al-'Aziz b. Abu Thabit, from Da'ud b. al-Husayn, from 'Ikrama, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'Anasa, freed-man of the Messenger of God (SAAS), was martyred at Badr.'' Al-Waqidi stated, 'In our view, that is not fully established. I have seen some scholars also assert that he witnessed the battle of Uhud and lived on for some time thereafter, dying during the lifetime of Abu Bakr, during his caliphate.'
lessons: Faithful service in modest roles — such as granting access to a leader — carries great spiritual dignity.
Scholarly honesty requires acknowledging uncertainty in historical accounts rather than asserting false certainty.
Those who serve the righteous are remembered with honor across generations.
practical_advice: Embrace whatever role of service you are given with full dedication, recognizing that no act of sincere service is too small.
When you encounter conflicting accounts or information, practice intellectual humility and acknowledge what is uncertain.
