Reflect on those who quietly facilitated goodness behind the scenes, and honor their memory by doing the same in your own community.

title: Ayman b. 'Ubayd Stands Firm at Hunayn and Is Martyred
summary: Ayman b. 'Ubayd, son of Umm Ayman and brother of Usama, was in charge of the Prophet's ablution vessel and was among the few who stood firm when most fled at the battle of Hunayn. He was martyred there alongside the Prophet.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, 'He was in charge of the ablution vessel used by the Prophet (SAAS). He was one of those men who stood firm at the battle of Hunayn. And it is said that it was about him and his companions that the words of the Almighty, "and he who hopes to meet his Lord should do good works, and not associate any other (god) with the worship of his Lord" (surat al-Kahf; XVIII, v.110). Al-Shafi'i stated that Ayman was killed while with the Prophet (SAAS), at the battle of Hunayn.'
lessons: Standing firm in the face of overwhelming danger, when others retreat, is among the highest forms of courage.
Sincere service to a righteous cause — however humble — can bring one to a station honored by God's own words.
Those who remain loyal in the most difficult moments define the true character of faith.
practical_advice: When facing your own moments of panic or retreat, remember those who held their ground and draw on their example to find your footing.
Dedicate yourself to the small daily acts of service and worship, as these are the foundations that hold you firm in larger trials.
Reflect on Qur'anic verses that speak of perseverance in difficulty, and return to them as anchors when fear arises.

title: Thawban Chooses to Remain with the Prophet After Being Freed
summary: Thawban was a slave purchased and then freed by the Prophet, who gave him the choice to return to his people or remain. Thawban chose to stay with the Prophet out of love and loyalty, never leaving his side until the Prophet's death.
story: Also there was Thawban b. Bujdud, also known as Ibn Jahdar Abu 'Abd Allah, as Abu 'Abd al-Karim and as Abu 'Abd al-Rahman. His parentage was among the people of al-Sarrat, a place between Mecca and Yemen; alternatively, he is said to be descended from the Himyar people of Yemen, or from al-Han. He is also said to be of the line of Hakam b. Sa'd al-'Ashira of Madhij, and to have been taken captive in the jahiliyya period. The Messenger of God (SAAS) purchased him, then manumitted him and gave him the choice between returning to his own people or remaining one of the household. He remained loyal to the Messenger of God (SAAS), until his death, attending him constantly, whether at home or travelling. He took part in the conquest of Egypt during the rule of 'Umar and thereafter resided at Hims, where he built a house. He remained there until he died, in 54 AH.
lessons: Freedom of choice is sacred; the most meaningful bonds are those chosen freely, not imposed by obligation.
Loyalty born of love and gratitude endures far beyond any external compulsion.
A life of devoted service to what is right and noble is itself a form of spiritual fulfillment.
practical_advice: Identify the relationships and commitments in your life that you have chosen freely out of love and gratitude, and invest in them deliberately.
When you feel untethered or without belonging, consider where your heart genuinely calls you — and pursue that with the commitment Thawban showed.
Reflect on who has shown you unconditional care, and find ways to honor that relationship through consistent presence and loyalty.

title: Bunayn Stores the Prophet's Leftover Ablution Water Out of Devotion
summary: Bunayn, a freed-man of the Prophet, secretly collected and drank the leftover water from the Prophet's ablutions out of deep devotion. When the Prophet discovered this and was informed, he praised Bunayn's sincerity.
story: Also there was Bunayn, the freed-man of the Prophet (SAAS); he was the grandfather of Ibrahim b. 'Abd Allah b. Bunayn. We have narrated how he served the Prophet (SAAS), and helped him with the ablution. When the Prophet (SAAS) had finished, Bunayn would take the remainder of the ablution water to the Companions. Some of these would drink from it, while others wiped themselves with it. Bunayn would also retain it and keep it in a jar. Eventually they complained about him to the Prophet (SAAS), who asked him, 'What are you doing with it?' He replied, 'I save it up and drink it, Messenger of God.' The latter commented, 'Have you ever seen a slave store away what this one does!' Ultimately the Prophet (SAAS) presented him to his uncle al-'Abbas, who manumitted him; may God be pleased with them both.
lessons: Acts of devotion done quietly and without display are often the most sincere expressions of love.
Being questioned about your private devotions need not be a source of shame when your intention is pure.
The Prophet's response was one of gentle marvel rather than rebuke, teaching us to respond to sincere love with warmth.
practical_advice: Nurture private devotional practices that are meaningful to you personally, even if others do not understand them.
If someone questions a sincere habit of yours, explain your intention calmly and honestly rather than becoming defensive.
Look for small, personal ways to express gratitude and love for what is sacred to you — these acts carry deep spiritual weight.

title: Rabi' the Gatekeeper Announces 'Umar's Visit During the Prophet's Seclusion from His Wives
summary: During the Prophet's period of seclusion from his wives, his freed-man Rabi' served as gatekeeper and was the one who sought permission for 'Umar ibn al-Khattab to enter and see the Prophet.
story: There was also Rabah al-Aswad, who would grant access to visit the Prophet (SAAS). It was he who took permission to 'Umar b. al-Khattab to enter in to see the Messenger of God (SAAS), while he was in that upper room, that day he had turned away from his wives, secluding himself alone there from them.
lessons: Even in moments of personal withdrawal and contemplation, structure and trusted intermediaries allow for appropriate access and care.
Faithful service in a position of trust — such as guarding a leader's privacy — requires both discretion and attentiveness.
The Prophet's periods of solitude remind us that rest, reflection, and emotional withdrawal are legitimate and necessary.
practical_advice: Recognize when you need a period of withdrawal and solitude, and communicate this need to those around you rather than disappearing without explanation.
If someone you care about is going through a period of emotional seclusion, respect their space while remaining available if they call for you.
Cultivate trusted people in your life who can serve as wise intermediaries in sensitive moments.

title: Zayd b. Haritha: Commander at al-Mu'ta and the Prophet's Beloved Freed-Man
summary: Zayd b. Haritha, the Prophet's freed-man and beloved companion, was appointed commander of every military expedition he was sent on. He was martyred as commander-in-chief at the battle of al-Mu'ta in 8 AH, and 'A'isha reported the Prophet said he would have been made his successor had he lived.
story: There was also Zayd b. Haritha al-Kalbi. We have given reference to him, may God be pleased with him, above in the account of his death at the battle of al-Mu'ta; that occurred in Jumada, 8 AH, some months prior to the conquest of Mecca. He was the commander-in-chief. After him in command came Ja'far, then, following them both, came 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha. 'A'isha, may God be pleased with her, is quoted as having said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) appointed Zayd b. Haritha as commander of every military expedition on which he dispatched him. If he had remained alive, he would have appointed him his successor.' It was Ahmad who gave this quotation.
lessons: True leadership is earned through consistent trustworthiness, not through lineage or status.
Grief over losing those closest to us is a natural and honored response — the Prophet himself wept for Zayd.
The love the Prophet showed for his freed-man Zayd challenges all assumptions about rank and social hierarchy.
practical_advice: Honor the people in your life who have consistently shown up for you, regardless of their social status or background.
When you are given responsibility repeatedly, receive it as a sign of trust and meet it with your full effort as Zayd did.
Allow yourself to grieve those you have lost, knowing that deep love and deep grief are inseparable companions.

title: Safina: Named 'The Ship' by the Prophet and His Lifelong Servant
summary: Safina, originally named Mahran, was freed by Umm Salama on the condition that he serve the Prophet for the rest of the Prophet's life. He accepted joyfully, saying that even without the condition he would never have left the Prophet's side.
story: They include Safina Abu 'Abd al-Rahman, known as Abu al-Bakhtari. His given name was Mahran, and is also said to have been 'Abs, Ahmar and Ruman. The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave him his agnomen, for a reason we will relate, and it stuck to him. He was a freed-man of Umm Salama, who set him free on condition that he serve the Messenger of God (SAAS) until he died. He accepted this and would say, 'If she had not stipulated this, I would not have separated from him!' He was of mixed Arab ancestry, his paternal origins being from Persia.
lessons: The most joyful service is that which is freely embraced, not merely endured out of obligation.
A condition of service can become a source of profound blessing when the heart aligns with the duty.
Belonging to a noble cause or person can give one's life a sense of meaning and direction that transcends circumstances.
practical_advice: Reflect on commitments you have made that once felt like constraints — consider whether they might in fact be gifts that have shaped you.
Cultivate a sense of willing devotion to the people and causes most important to you, so that your service feels like privilege rather than burden.
When you feel a deep connection to a person, community, or purpose, honor that connection through consistent presence rather than taking it for granted.

title: Safina Narrates the Hadith of the Thirty-Year Caliphate
summary: Safina, the freed-man of the Prophet, narrated that the Prophet foretold the caliphate of his nation would last thirty years before becoming a kingdom. Safina confirmed this by adding up the caliphates of Abu Bakr, 'Umar, 'Uthman, and 'Ali.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Abu al-Nadr related to him, quoting Bashraj b. Nubata al-'Absi, a man of Kufa, quoting Sa'id b. Jumhan, quoting Safina, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, "The caliphate among my nation will be for 30 years, and thereafter it will become a kingdom."' (Sa'id b. Jumhan continued) 'Safina then said to me, "Take the caliphate of Abu Bakr, that of 'Umar, that of 'Uthman, and then that of 'Ali. We find they total 30 years."'
lessons: The Prophet's prophetic knowledge extended to the political future of his community, strengthening trust in his guidance.
History provides clarity in retrospect — what seems uncertain in the moment is often confirmed by patient observation.
Preserving and transmitting knowledge faithfully, as Safina did, is an act of profound service to future generations.
practical_advice: When you feel uncertain about the direction of your community or society, return to foundational principles and the lessons of history for grounding.
Practice narrating and preserving the wisdom you have received from your elders and teachers, so that it is not lost.
Trust that patterns of meaning in your life may only become clear in retrospect — cultivate patience with the present uncertainty.

title: Safina's Naming: The Human Boat of the Prophet's Companions
summary: The freed-man Safina earned his name from the Prophet when he carried the heavy baggage of the Companions during a journey, bearing the load of many donkeys without complaint. The Prophet called him 'safina' meaning 'boat', a name he carried for the rest of his life.
story: was SafIna b. Mafinna. Imam A!)mad stated that Abu aI-Na4r related to him, quoting Bashraj b. Nubata a/-CAbsj, a man of Kufa, quoting Sa'jd b. ]umhan, quoting SafIna, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'The caliphate among my nation will be for 30 years, and thereafter it will become a kingdom.'" (Sa'id b. ]umhiin continued) "Safina then said to me, 'Take the caliphate of Abu Bakr, that of CUmar, that of 'Uthman, and then that of 'All. We find they total 30 years.' "Later I researched the caliphs, but did not find they totalled 30 years. I asked Sa'id, 'Where did you meet Safina!' He replied, 'In a date~grove, in the era of aI-Bajjaj. I stayed with him for three nights, asking him to tell me things the Messenger of God (SAAS) had said. I asked him, 'What is your name!' He replied, 'I'm not telling you! The Messenger of God (SAAS) named me "Safina", "boat".' I asked him, 'Why did he call you "Safma"!' He replied, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) went on a journey with his Companions. Their baggage grew too heavy for thein and he told me, "Spread out your cloak!" I spread it out and they put their belongings on it and then placed it on me. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "Carry that; you're a safina!" And if I had been burdened that day with the load ofone, two, three, four, five, six or seven donkeys it would not have been too heavy for me, though they would have been overdoing it.''' This /:tadith is given by Abu Da'ud, al-Tirmidhi and aI-Nas;;'l. The phrase they relate is, "the caliphate of the prophethood will be for 30 years. Then there will be a kingdom." Imam A!)mad stated that Bahz related to him, quoting Bammad b. Salama, from Sa'id b. ]umhiin, who quoted SafIna as having said, "We were on a journey, and whenever anyone got tired, he would throw his clothes, shield or sword over on me until I was carrying a whole lot of that. The Prophet (SAAS) told me, 'You're a saflna!'" This was widely accepted to be how he came to be named SafIna. Abu al-Qisim aI-BaghaWl stated that both aI-Rabi' Sulayman b. Da'ud aI-Zahriinl and MuJ.wnmad b.]a'far al-Warikiini told him that Sharik b. 'Abd Allah al-Nakha'l had related to them, from 'Imran aI-Bajali, from a freed-man ofUmm Salama, who said, "We were with the Messenger of God (SAAS), and passed by a gulley - or a river - and I was busy helping people across. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told me, 'From today on, you're nothing but a sajina, a "boat"!'"
lessons: True service to others, even when it demands great personal sacrifice, is a source of dignity and honor rather than shame.
A name or identity given to us through sincere acts of devotion can become our most treasured possession, more valuable than any worldly title.
When we give generously of our strength and capacity for others, we discover reserves within ourselves we never knew existed.
practical_advice: When you feel burdened by the demands placed on you by others, reframe the weight as an opportunity to serve — each load you carry with grace is a mark of your strength.
Reflect on the names or roles others have given you through your acts of kindness; let those identities rooted in service anchor your sense of purpose.
If you are exhausted from giving, remember that Safina carried the loads of many and found it bearable — ask God for the strength to continue, and you may find the burden lighter than you feared.

title: Safina and the Lion: Divine Protection for the Prophet's Freed-Man
summary: After a shipwreck stranded Safina on an island with a lion, he identified himself as the freed-man of the Messenger of God, and the lion peacefully guided him to safety and bade him farewell.
story: Abu 'Abd Allah b. Manda stated that al-Basan b. Makram related to ,him, quoting CUthman b. CUmar, quoting Usama b. Zayd, from Mu!)annnad b. aI-Munkadir, from Sanna, who said, "I went to sea on a ship and it was wrecked. I rode on one of its planks and it cast me up on an island where there was a lion, and that really surprised me. I said, 'Abu aI-I:Iarith, I'm the freed-man of the IMAM ABU AL-FIDA' ISMA'IL IBN KATHIR 449 Messenger of God (SAAS). It then began using its shoulder to gesture to me, and eventually led me to a path. Then it growled, which I took to be its (farewell) greeting to me." Abu a1--Q!lsim al-BaghaWi narrated it from Ibrahim b. Hani', from 'Ubayd Allah b. Musa, from a man, from Mu/:1ammad b. al-Munkadir. He also narrated it from Mu/:1ammad b. 'Abd Allah a1-Makhrami, from I;Iusayn b. Mu/:1ammad, who quoted it from 'Abd a1-'Aziz b. 'Abd Allah b. Abu Salama, from Mu/:1ammad b. a1-Munkadir, who quoted SaHna, as above. Al-Baghawi also stated that Harlin b. 'Abd Allah related to him, quoting 'Ali b. 'A~im, quoting Abu Ray/:1ana, who quoted SafIna, the freed-man ofthe Messenger of God (SAAS), as having said, "When the lion confronted me, I said, 'I'm Safina, the freed-man of the Messenger of God (SAAS).' It then struck its tail on the ground and lay down."
lessons: Our connection to the Prophet and to God can be a source of protection and calm even in the most terrifying and unexpected circumstances.
Faith does not mean the absence of fear; it means turning to God and invoking our relationship with Him even when our hearts are trembling.
The natural world itself may respond to sincere devotion — a reminder that God's mercy encompasses all of creation.
practical_advice: When you face a sudden crisis that leaves you feeling alone and terrified, take a moment to consciously invoke your identity as a believer — remind yourself of whose servant you are, and let that anchor steady you.
Practice speaking your trust in God aloud in moments of fear, as Safina did; verbalizing faith can shift your internal state from panic toward calm.
After surviving a frightening ordeal, take time to reflect on how you were guided through it — recognizing God's hand in your deliverance deepens gratitude and resilience for future trials.

title: Salman al-Farisi: The Persian Who Found Islam Through a Chain of Monks
summary: Salman al-Farisi, originally from Persia, journeyed through a long sequence of spiritual guides and hardships — including being enslaved — before finally reaching Medina, where he accepted Islam and was declared by the Prophet to be of his household.
story: Salman al-Farisi, Abu 'Abd Allah, mawla ai-islam, "the freedman ofIslam", was also one of them. He was originally from Persia and circumstances led him being owned by a Jew of Medina. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) went into exile there, Salman accepted Islam. The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered him to enter into a contract ofmanumission with his Jewish master and helped him to payoff what he owed. He was associated with the Messenger of God (SAAS), who said of him, "Salman is of our household". We have given above a description of his leaving his own country and his association with a sequence of monks, one after the next, until circumstances led him to al-madina al-munawwaram, 'Medina the illuminated'. We described how he became a Muslim, may God be pleased with him, early after the migration of the Prophet (SAAS), to Medina. His death came in 35 AH, at the end of 'Uthman's rule, or early in 36 AH. It is also said that he died during the reign of 'Umar b. a1-Khanab; the first date is more widely accepted. AI-'Abbas b. Yazid al-Ba/:1rani stated, "Scholars had no doubt that he lived for 250 years; they did differ over his having lived to a greater age, up to 350 years." Some later buJJa;., however, claim that he did not live beyond the age of 100. God knows best what is correct.
lessons: The search for truth may be long, winding, and filled with painful detours, yet God guides the sincere seeker to His light in His perfect timing.
No amount of worldly hardship — not slavery, displacement, or loss — can extinguish the flame of genuine spiritual longing in a person's heart.
When we are accepted by God and His Prophet, our origins, our past, and our status in the eyes of others become irrelevant — we belong to something far greater.
practical_advice: If you feel spiritually lost or that your search for meaning has gone on too long without resolution, take heart from Salman's journey — the length of the path does not mean you are heading in the wrong direction.

When your circumstances feel like slavery — trapped by a job, a situation, or a relationship — remember that Salman's physical bondage could not bind his soul, and use your inner freedom to keep moving toward what is right.
Seek out righteous mentors and guides, as Salman did with each monk; even imperfect teachers can pass you closer to the truth, one step at a time.

title: Shuqran's Devotion: Laying the Velvet in the Prophet's Grave
summary: Shuqran, the Prophet's freed-man and loyal companion, participated in washing the Prophet's body and personally descended into his grave to lay the velvet cloth beneath him, swearing no one else would ever use it.
story: Shuqran al-I;Iabashi was also one of them. His given name was Sali/:1 b. 'Adi; the Prophet (SAAS) inherited him from his father. Mu~'ab al-Zubayri and Mu/:1ammad b. Sa'd stated, "He had belonged to 'Abd al-Ra/:1rnan b. 'Awf, who presented him to the Prophet (SAAS)." A/:1rnad b. I;Ianbal narrated, from Is/:1aq b. qsa, from Abu Ma'shar, who stated that Shuqran had told him that he had participated at the battle of Badr. Abu Ma'shar stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had not awarded him a share of the booty. Mu1).ammad b. Sa'd similarly includes him among those who were present at Badr. He was a slave and therefore did not receive a share in the booty, but was placed in charge of the prisoners taken. Each man owning a prisoner made payment to him, and so he received more than the equivalent of a full share. He (Mu1).ammad b. Sa'd) stated, "There were three slaves apart from him at Badr. One was owned by 'Abd al-Ra1).man b. 'Awf, another by I;Iatib b. Abu Balta'a and a third by Sa'd b. Mu'adh. He (the Prophet (SAAS)) did give them a small present." Abu al-Qasim al-Baghawi stated, "There is no mention in al-Zuhri's book or in that of Ibn Is1).aq of him (Shuqran) among those who were present at Badr." AI-Waqidi stated that Abu Bakr b. 'Abd Allah b. Abu Sabra quoted Abu Bakr b. 'Abd Allah b. Abu Jahm as having said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) appointed his freed-man Shuqran in charge ofeverything found in the saddle-bags at the battle at al-MuraySi', including minor items, weapons, cattle and sheep; he assembled the children in one area." Imam A1).mad stated that Aswad b. 'Amir related to him, quoting Muslim b. Khalid, from 'Amr b. Ya1).ya al-Mazini, from his father, who quoted Shuqran as having said, "I saw him" - meaning the Prophet (SAAS) - "making his way to Khaybar mounted on a donkey, performing prayer as he did so, and making gesticulations." These abiidith provide evidence that Shuqran, may God be pleased with him, was present at these engagements. AI-Tirmidhi narrated, from Zayd b. Akhzam, from 'Uthman b. Farqad, from Ja'far b. Mu1).ammad, who quoted Ibn Abu Rafi', as having said, "I heard Shuqran say, 'I swear by God, it was I who spread out the velvet cloth in the grave beneath the Messenger of God (SAAS).'" AI-Tirmidhi went on to state that Ja'far b. Mu1).ammad quoted his father as having said, "It was Abu Tal1).a who made the grave of the Prophet (SAAS), and Shuqran who laid out the velvet cloth." AI-Tirmidhi categorized this badith as being basan gharib. It has been narrated above how Shuqran participated in washing the body of the Messenger of God (SAAS), how he descended into his grave and how he placed beneath him the velvet on which he had prayed, saying, "I swear by God, no one will wear this after you!"
lessons: The most profound acts of love are often the quiet, final ones — tending to those we cherish even after they are gone, with a completeness that asks nothing in return.
Loyalty that spans a lifetime of service and culminates in a final act of devotion is one of the purest expressions of love a person can offer.
The grief of losing someone beloved can be honored not only through tears but through purposeful, tender action taken in their memory.
practical_advice: When you are grieving someone deeply, find one meaningful, concrete act you can do in their honor — a gesture that says, as Shuqran did, 'This is only for you.'
Reflect on the relationships in your life where your service has been consistent and quiet; recognize that such faithfulness has great worth even when it goes unrecorded by others.
In moments of loss, focus on what you still have the power to do rather than what you have lost — purposeful action during grief can be a form of healing.

title: Qumayra's Mother: The Prophet Reunites a Mother and Her Son
summary: The Prophet found a slave woman weeping because she had been separated from her son Qumayra. He declared that a mother and her child must never be separated, then personally arranged an exchange to reunite them and later issued a letter of manumission for the entire family.
story: 'Abd Allah b. Wahb quoted from Ibn AbU Dhi'b, from I;Iusayn b. 'Abd Allah b. Oumayra, from his father, who quoted his grandfather Oumayra as having said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) passed by Oumayra's mother while she was weeping and asked her, "What is making you cry? Are you hungry or unclothed?" She replied, "Messenger of God, I have been separated from my son." The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "A mother and her son can never be separated." He then sent for the man who had Oumayra and exchanged a young camel for Oumayra. Ibn Abu Dhi'b went on to state that he (I;Iusayn b. 'Abd·Allllh b. Oumayra) then read to him a letter he had, as follows, "In the name of God, the most Merciful and Compassionate. This letter is from Mul).ammad, the Messenger of God, to Abu Oumayra and his family. The Messenger of God has manumitted them all. They are a family of Arab descent. If they wish, they may reside with the Messenger of God and if they wish, they may return to their own people without hindrance unless for proper cause. Those Muslims who meet with them should treat them with kindness. Ubayy b. Ka'b wrote (it)."
lessons: The pain of separation from those we love is among the most acute forms of human suffering, and it deserves to be met with immediate compassion and action rather than indifference.
True leadership means stopping for the weeping person by the roadside — noticing grief others may walk past and treating it as an urgent matter.
Restoring human bonds broken by injustice is one of the most Godly acts a person can perform.
practical_advice: When you encounter someone in grief, do not immediately try to solve or minimize their pain — first ask what is hurting them, as the Prophet asked, and listen fully before acting.
If you are currently separated from someone you love — by distance, estrangement, or circumstance — take one concrete step today toward closing that distance, even if it costs you something.

Recognize that the Prophet treated the reunion of this family as urgent enough to personally arrange and document — let that inspire you to treat the relational wounds around you with similar seriousness and care.

title: Two Fasting Women Who Slandered Others
summary: Two women who were fasting but slandering others were called by the Prophet and told to vomit, whereupon they expelled pus, blood and flesh, illustrating that backbiting nullifies the spiritual merit of fasting.
story: There were two women who were fasting and were slandering people. The Messenger of God (SAAS) called for a vessel and told them, 'Vomit!' They did so, bringing up pus, blood, meat and flesh. He then commented, 'These two women abstained from what was permitted and broke their fast (by eating) what was prohibited.'
lessons: Backbiting and slander are spiritually destructive acts that can cancel the rewards of worship such as fasting.
True piety requires guarding one's tongue just as one guards one's body from food and drink.
External acts of worship without inner moral integrity are spiritually hollow and even self-defeating.
practical_advice: When fasting or engaged in worship, make a conscious commitment to guard your speech and avoid speaking ill of others.
If you notice yourself slipping into gossip or backbiting, treat it with the same seriousness as breaking a fast — acknowledge it, seek forgiveness, and redirect yourself.
Use periods of worship as opportunities to audit your words and relationships, not just your eating habits.

title: Kirkira the Baggage-Handler and the Stolen Cloak
summary: A slave named Kirkira who managed the Prophet's baggage on expeditions died and the Prophet declared him destined for hellfire, after which a stolen cloak he had concealed was discovered among his belongings.
story: There was a man named Kirkira in charge of the baggage of the Prophet (SAAS). The man died and the Prophet (SAAS) said, 'He is in hell-fire!' Then they noticed that the man had a striped cloak or an outer garment that he had put on.
lessons: Hidden dishonesty, even in small matters, has grave spiritual consequences that cannot be concealed from God.
The outward appearance of service and proximity to the righteous does not guarantee a sound heart or good standing before God.
Integrity in small, unobserved acts of trust is as important as visible acts of worship.
practical_advice: Regularly examine your conscience for any dishonesty or breach of trust in your daily dealings, no matter how minor it may seem.
If you are in a position of trust — at work, at home, or in any role — treat every act of stewardship as if it is being observed by God.
Seek to make restitution for any past wrongs or betrayals of trust before death makes it impossible to do so.

title: Mid'am the Slave Killed by a Stray Arrow — and the Stolen Shamla
summary: A slave named Mid'am was killed by a stray arrow after the battle of Khaybar, and though people called him a martyr, the Prophet warned that a garment he had stolen from the war booty would earn him hellfire.
story: Mid'am was one of them. He was a black, of mixed Arab descent from Hisma who was a gift from Rifa'a b. Zayd al-Jadhami. He was killed during the life of the Prophet (SAAS), following their return from Khaybar. When they reached Wadi al-Qura, Mid'am was busy unloading the baggage from the camel of the Messenger of God (SAAS), when a stray arrow struck and killed him. People commented, 'Lucky for him! Martyrdom!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'Absolutely not, by Him who holds my soul in His hand, the shamla he took at the battle of Khaybar that had not been assigned from the booty will light the fires of hell for him!' When they heard that a man came up with a shoelace — or two shoelaces — and the Prophet (SAAS) said, 'A shoelace' — or 'two shoelaces' — 'of fire!'
lessons: A dramatic or honourable death does not erase the spiritual debt of unconfessed dishonesty or theft.
Even the smallest act of taking what does not belong to you — as little as a shoelace — carries serious moral and spiritual weight.
People can appear righteous externally while concealing wrongs that only God and their own conscience fully know.
practical_advice: Do not assume that good endings or fortunate circumstances mean all past wrongs have been wiped away — take responsibility and seek forgiveness proactively.
If you have taken something that was not rightfully yours, make restitution or sincerely repent before that opportunity is lost.
Cultivate a habit of scrupulous honesty in matters of shared or communal property, especially in settings of group responsibility.

title: Waqid the Freed-Man's Report on True Remembrance of God
summary: The freed-man Waqid narrated a teaching of the Prophet distinguishing true remembrance of God — rooted in obedience — from the mere performance of prayer and fasting without it.
story: Waqid, a freed-man of the Prophet (SAAS), said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, "Whoever obeys God remembers God, even if his prayer, fasting and recitation of the Qur'an are minimal. Whoever disobeys God does not remember Him, even if his prayer, fasting and recitation of the Qur'an are substantial."'
lessons: True remembrance of God is expressed through obedience and righteous living, not merely through the quantity of ritual acts.
A person whose actions align with God's commands is spiritually connected to God regardless of how little formal worship they perform.
Abundant worship performed alongside persistent disobedience is spiritually contradictory and fails to fulfil the purpose of worship.
practical_advice: Rather than measuring your spiritual health solely by the quantity of prayers or fasts, reflect honestly on whether your daily choices reflect obedience to God.
When you feel spiritually distant from God, examine your conduct first — small consistent acts of obedience can reconnect you more than increasing ritual quantity.
Use moments of worship as anchors that reinforce your commitment to ethical living throughout the rest of your day.

title: Hurmuz the Freed-Man at the Battle of Badr and the Prohibition of Charity for Banu Hashim
summary: A slave named Hurmuz who was present at Badr was manumitted by the Prophet, who told him that a freed-man of a household becomes part of that household and therefore may not receive charity.
story: Twenty slaves were present at Badr, including one slave belonging to the Prophet (SAAS), a man named Hurmuz. The Messenger of God (SAAS) manumitted him and said, 'God has set you free. A people's freed-man is one of themselves. We of the household do not consume al-sadaqa, the charity; you must not do so.'
lessons: Freedom and dignity granted by the righteous elevates a person into full membership of that community with its rights and responsibilities.
True belonging comes with both privilege and responsibility — being part of a noble household means upholding its standards.
The Prophet's household maintained a standard of self-sufficiency and dignity by refusing to depend on charity even when entitled to do so.
practical_advice: When someone extends you trust, belonging, or elevation in status, reflect on the responsibilities that come with it, not only the benefits.
If you are going through a period of dependence or hardship, consider whether there are ways to maintain your dignity and self-reliance that you may have overlooked.
Recognise that true liberation — from poverty, hardship, or bad circumstances — comes with a call to live up to higher standards, not simply to enjoy greater comfort.

title: Abu al-Hamra's Account of the Prophet Visiting Ali and Fatima's Home Each Morning
summary: The freed-man Abu al-Hamra recalled spending seven months in Madinah that felt like a single day, during which the Prophet would come to the door of Ali and Fatima every morning and call out a verse of purification.
story: Abu al-Hamra' said, 'I was stationed in Medina for seven months — it felt like one day — and the Prophet (SAAS) would come to the door of the home of Ali and Fatima every morning and call out, "The prayer! The prayer! People of the household, God wants only to remove the filth from you, and to thoroughly purify you."'
lessons: The Prophet's consistent, gentle reminders to his own household reflect that spiritual nurturing is an act of love and devotion, not merely duty.
A blessed and purposeful environment can make time feel full and meaningful — spiritual community transforms the experience of daily life.
Caring for the spiritual wellbeing of those we love, with constancy and tenderness, is one of the most profound forms of worship.
practical_advice: Establish a morning routine that begins with a reminder of your spiritual purpose — a verse, a prayer, or a moment of intention — to anchor your day in meaning.
If you feel that time is passing emptily, seek out spiritual community or purposeful companionship to restore a sense of fullness and belonging.
Take time to gently encourage those closest to you in their spiritual practice, as the Prophet did for his own family, rather than leaving such reminders only for formal settings.

title: The Prophet Condemning Deception in Food — Abu al-Hamra's Account
summary: Abu al-Hamra narrated that the Prophet placed his hand in a bowl of food being sold and, upon discovering it was adulterated, declared that whoever cheats Muslims is not one of them.
story: The Prophet (SAAS) passed by a man who had food in a bowl (for sale) and he placed his hand in it (to check its quality). He said, 'You cheated! Whoever cheats us is not one of us!'
lessons: Fraud and deception in trade are not minor infractions but a fundamental breach of the bonds of community and faith.
The Prophet's personal inspection of the marketplace reflects that ethical commerce is a matter of prophetic concern, not merely personal choice.
Dishonesty that harms others — especially vulnerable members of society — is among the most serious moral failures a person can commit.
practical_advice: In your professional or commercial dealings, hold yourself to a standard of complete transparency, recognising that cutting corners or deceiving customers is a spiritual failing, not merely a legal or reputational one.
If you have been deceived or defrauded, know that justice is a value the Prophet himself enforced, and do not feel ashamed to seek redress.
Regularly ask yourself whether your work, your products, or your services truly deliver what you promise — and correct any gap between what you advertise and what you provide.

title: Abu Bakra al-Thaqafi — The Slave Lowered Over the Walls of Ta'if
summary: A slave named Nufay', later known as Abu Bakra, escaped the besieged city of Ta'if by being lowered over its walls and came to the Prophet, who manumitted him and named him after the manner of his escape.
story: Nufay', sometimes known as Masruh and as Nafi' b. Masruh. He and a number of slaves were lowered down over the walls of al-Ta'if, and the Prophet (SAAS) manumitted him. He was born in Bakra, and so the Messenger of God (SAAS) named him Abu Bakra. Abu Nu'aym stated, 'He was a righteous man; the Messenger of God (SAAS) established brotherhood between him and Abu Barza al-Aslami.'
lessons: Desperate circumstances that seem like the lowest point can be the threshold to liberation and a new life of dignity.
The Prophet's care in giving the freed slave a meaningful name and establishing brotherhood for him shows that freedom must be accompanied by belonging and identity.
Courage to seek freedom — even at great personal risk — can open doors to a life of purpose and righteousness one could not have imagined before.
practical_advice: When you feel trapped in a situation — whether a relationship, a job, or a mental state — know that taking a courageous step toward freedom, even a frightening one, can lead to transformation.
If you have been given a new start in life, invest in building genuine community and brotherhood with others, as this is what makes freedom truly meaningful.
Do not let the label of your past — your origins, your failures, or what others have made of you — define your identity; allow your choices and character to give you your true name.

title: The Prophet Exposes Food Fraud in the Market
summary: The Prophet Muhammad passed by a man selling food and discovered he had hidden inferior quality beneath the surface, rebuking him with the declaration that cheaters are not of his community.
story: The Prophet (SAAS) passed by a man who had food in a bowl (for sale) and he placed his hand in it (to check its quality). He said, 'You cheated! Whoever cheats us is not one of us!'
lessons: Integrity in all dealings is a core value of faith, not merely an ethical preference.
Small acts of deception carry serious spiritual consequences and distance a person from the community of believers.
Accountability does not require a formal setting — the Prophet corrected wrongdoing immediately and directly, teaching that moral clarity is a form of mercy.
practical_advice: Conduct an honest self-audit of your daily dealings — business, speech, and relationships — and identify any area where you may be giving less than your full truthfulness.
When you feel tempted to cut corners or hide flaws in your work, pause and ask yourself whether this action reflects the person you want to be before God.
If you have cheated or deceived someone in the past, take a concrete step today to make it right, even if it is uncomfortable.

title: Abu Salama the Herdsman Narrates the Path to Paradise
summary: Abu Salama, the herdsman of the Prophet, testified that he heard the Prophet repeatedly declare that whoever meets God with sincere faith in His oneness, the prophethood, the Mission, and the reckoning will enter paradise.
story: Abu Salama, the herdsman of the Prophet (SAAS), said, 'I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, "Whoever meets God, testifies that there is no god but God, that Muhammad is the Messenger of God, and expresses faith in the Mission and in the reckoning will enter paradise."' We asked him, 'Did you hear this from the Messenger of God (SAAS)?' He placed a finger in each ear and replied, 'I heard that from him not once, not twice, not thrice and not four times!'
lessons: The path to salvation is grounded in sincere belief held consistently in the heart, not in complex rituals alone.
Those closest to the Prophet in humble roles — including a herdsman — were entrusted with the most essential teachings of the faith.
Certainty in the promises of God is itself a form of worship that strengthens the soul during times of doubt.
practical_advice: Revisit the foundational declarations of your faith and reflect on whether you hold them with genuine conviction or merely habit.
When you feel spiritually distant or uncertain, return to the basic testimony of faith and let its simplicity ground you.
Share core teachings of your faith with those around you with the same emphasis and energy that Abu Salama showed — repeat what matters most.

title: Abu Safiyya's Daily Devotion with Pebbles and Praise
summary: Abu Safiyya, a freed-man of the Prophet, maintained a disciplined daily practice of repeating 'Subhan Allah' using pebbles from morning until midday and again from the afternoon prayer until evening.
story: Abu Safiyya, freed-man of the Prophet (SAAS), would lay out a leather sheet, and a palm-leaf basket containing pebbles would be brought to him. He would use these to repeat, subhan Allah! 'Praise be to God!' until midday. Then it would be taken away. When he had performed al-uwla, 'the first prayer', he would go on repeating subhan Allah! until evening.
lessons: Consistent, structured remembrance of God throughout the day creates a spiritual rhythm that protects the heart from anxiety and distraction.
Simple acts of devotion, maintained with discipline, carry profound spiritual weight — greatness in faith does not always look dramatic.
The freed-men of the Prophet demonstrated that spiritual elevation has nothing to do with social status and everything to do with commitment to God.
practical_advice: Establish a tangible daily dhikr practice — even using a counter or beads — to anchor yourself in remembrance of God at set times of day.
Identify two fixed points in your day, such as morning and late afternoon, and dedicate even ten minutes at each to focused remembrance or gratitude.
When you feel overwhelmed or spiritually hollow, replace anxious thoughts with a simple repeated phrase of praise until your mind settles.

title: The Letter of the Prophet Protects Abu Dumayra's Family from Thieves
summary: A group from the freed family of Abu Dumayra were robbed by thieves on a journey, but when they produced the Prophet's letter of protection, the thieves read it, returned their belongings, and let them pass safely.
story: This man was a freed-man of the Messenger of God (SAAS). He was of Himyar. A group of them went on a journey, bearing this letter. Thieves blocked their path and took their belongings. They then produced this letter and told the thieves what it contained. The latter read it, returned their belongings to them and allowed them to pass. Husayn b. 'Abd Allah b. Abu Dumayra presented himself to the Commander of the Believers, al-Mahdi, having brought this letter with him. Al-Mahdi took it, examined it carefully, and awarded Husayn 300 dinars.
lessons: The moral authority of the Prophet's word was so powerful that even those outside the faith recognized and respected it.
Divine protection can come through unexpected means — a piece of paper, a written word, or a humble document can become a shield by God's permission.
Preserving and honoring the legacy of the Prophet carries blessings across generations, as seen in Husayn's recognition by the Caliph centuries later.
practical_advice: When you feel vulnerable or unprotected in a difficult situation, reflect on what resources and connections you already have that you may be underestimating.
Trust that acting with integrity and carrying the values of your faith as a visible identity offers a form of protection that worldly means cannot always provide.
Honor and preserve the spiritual heritage passed down to you — in your family, your community, or your tradition — as it may be a source of blessing you have not yet recognized.

title: Abu Ubayd and the Miraculous Shoulder of Meat
summary: Abu Ubayd, a freed-man of the Prophet, cooked meat for him and was repeatedly asked for the shoulder; when he protested that a sheep only has two shoulders, the Prophet revealed that his silence could have sustained the miracle indefinitely.
story: Abu 'Ubayd cooked a pot of meat for the Messenger of God (SAAS). The latter asked him, 'Serve me its shoulder!' Abu 'Ubayd did so, but again he asked, 'Serve me its shoulder!' Abu 'Ubayd did so, but again he asked, 'Serve me its shoulder!' Abu 'Ubayd then said, 'Prophet of God, how many shoulders can a sheep have!' He replied, 'By Him who holds my soul in His hand, if you'd only remained silent, you would have been able to give me a shoulder every time I asked for it!'
lessons: Miraculous provision from God can be interrupted by our own impatience and the limits we place on what we believe is possible.
Trust in the Prophet's requests, even when they seem to contradict our understanding, is an act of faith that opens doors to the extraordinary.
Our rational objections, while natural, can sometimes close off blessings that were flowing freely — patience and trust are themselves forms of worship.
practical_advice: When you are in a situation that seems impossible, resist the urge to immediately declare it so — stay present and trust the process a little longer before drawing conclusions.
Practice restraining your impulse to question or correct in moments that require faith, whether in prayer, in relationships, or in difficult circumstances.
Reflect on moments in your life when your own impatience may have cut short something that was beginning to unfold in your favor.

title: The Prophet Sends Fever to Madinah and Pestilence to Syria
summary: The Prophet revealed that Gabriel came to him with fever and pestilence, and he chose to keep fever in Madinah while dispatching pestilence to Syria, declaring that pestilence is a testimony and mercy for believers but a punishment for unbelievers.
story: Abu 'Asib, a freed-man of the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'The Prophet (SAAS) said, "Gabriel came to me bearing fever and pestilence. I kept the fever in Medina and dispatched the pestilence to Syria. Pestilence is a testimony and a mercy for my own nation and a punishment for the unbelievers."'
lessons: What appears to be suffering or calamity can carry within it mercy and even spiritual elevation for the believer who endures it with patience.
The Prophet made choices on behalf of his community out of love and wisdom, accepting difficulty for his people rather than ease for himself.
Our perspective on hardship transforms when we understand that God assigns different meanings to trials depending on the heart that receives them.
practical_advice: When you are going through illness or hardship, consciously reframe it as an opportunity for spiritual purification and trust in God's plan rather than a sign of abandonment.
Do not measure God's love for you by the presence or absence of ease in your life — a believer's trials carry a different weight than they appear to.
Pray specifically for God to make your trials a source of mercy and testimony rather than merely an experience of pain.

title: A Night Walk with the Prophet: Dates, Water, and Accountability on Judgement Day
summary: Abu Asib narrates that the Prophet took him, Abu Bakr, and Umar on a night walk to an Ansari's garden, where they ate unripe dates and drank water; the Prophet then warned that every blessing would be accounted for on Judgement Day, except three basic necessities.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth one night. He passed by me and called out to me, then passed by and called out to Abu Bakr, who came out to him. He then went on by and called out to 'Umar, who also came out to him. He then walked on and entered a garden owned by an ansari. He asked the owner of the garden, 'Provide us with some unripe dates to eat.' The man brought them and put them down. The Messenger of God (SAAS) ate, as did all the others. He then called for water and, having drunk some of it, said, 'This is a blessing! You will be asked about this on Judgement Day!' 'Umar then took the bunch of dates and struck it on the ground, scattering the unripe dates. He (the ansari owner of the date-grove) then asked, 'Prophet of God, will we really be held responsible for this on Judgement Day?' He replied, 'Yes; for everything except three things: a scrap of cloth with which a man covers his nakedness, a morsel of food with which to blunt his hunger, and some stones (of a structure) he enters' — meaning to escape the heat and the cold.
lessons: Every blessing, no matter how small or ordinary, is a gift from God that will be a subject of gratitude on the Day of Judgement.
True thankfulness means recognizing that even a sip of water and a few dates are not trivial — they are provision from God that deserves acknowledgement.
The Prophet's simplicity in taking joy in small, humble pleasures alongside his Companions teaches that contentment is a spiritual treasure.
practical_advice: Begin a daily gratitude practice focused specifically on the smallest blessings — food, water, shelter, clothing — and articulate your thanks for each one deliberately.
When you feel discontent or ungrateful about your circumstances, pause and identify three basic comforts you have right now that millions of people lack.
Let awareness of divine accountability deepen your appreciation for what you have rather than create fear — it is a reminder that you are surrounded by gifts.

title: The Prophet Models Sexual Self-Restraint for His Companions
summary: After feeling desire upon seeing a woman pass by, the Prophet went to his wife to fulfill that desire lawfully, then openly advised his Companions to do the same, teaching that using what is permitted is an exemplary act.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) was seated among his Companions. Then he went inside and later came out again, having bathed. We asked, 'Messenger of God, was something wrong?' 'Yes,' he replied. 'A certain lady passed by me and I had a desire to be with a woman. So I went to one of my wives and had sexual relations with her. You should all act similarly; to have only what is permitted is a highly exemplary deed for you.'
lessons: The Prophet modeled vulnerability and transparency about human nature, removing shame from natural desires while channeling them toward what is lawful.
Turning to what God has made permissible in moments of temptation is not weakness — it is an act of obedience and self-mastery.
Spiritual perfection does not mean the absence of human desires but the wisdom to fulfill them within the boundaries God has established.
practical_advice: When you experience temptation, do not add the burden of shame or self-judgment on top of it — instead, immediately redirect toward a lawful and healthy alternative.
Build habits and structures in your life that make it easier to fulfill your legitimate needs, so temptation does not find you without an outlet.
If you struggle with guilt over natural human desires, revisit this teaching as a reminder that God created you with these impulses and provided lawful channels for them.

title: The Parable of Four People and Their Intentions with Wealth and Knowledge
summary: The Prophet described four types of people defined by their combinations of wealth and knowledge, teaching that sincere intention to do good is rewarded equally to the action itself, while the intention to do evil is punished equally to the act.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'The like of this nation is as four persons. One is a man to whom God gave wealth and knowledge and who works for Him and expends it as he should. Another is a man to whom God gives knowledge but not wealth; that person says, "If only I had the wealth of this person, I would act as he does." These two men shall be equal in reward. Another is a man to whom God gives wealth but not knowledge; he acts aimlessly and expends it improperly. Then there is the man to whom God gives neither wealth nor knowledge. He says, "If I had money such as does that man, I would do as he does." Those two men shall be equal in punishment.'
lessons: Sincere intention carries the full weight of the action in God's sight — you are rewarded for what you would have done had you the means.
Wealth and knowledge are both trusts from God, and what matters most is not how much of each you have been given but what you do with what you have.
Aspiring to do good with resources you do not yet have is itself a form of worship, while envying the wrongdoing of others and wishing to join it is a spiritual danger.
practical_advice: If you feel limited by lack of resources, money, or opportunity, make a sincere commitment in your heart about exactly what you would do if given more — God sees and rewards that intention.
Assess honestly which of the four categories best describes you right now and identify one concrete change you can make to move toward the first category.
Cultivate gratitude for both what you have and what you do not yet have — your character in the face of limitation defines your spiritual standing as much as your actions.

title: The Prophet's Teaching on Wealth, Envy, and Charity
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) warned against envying others' wealth and taught that charity never diminishes wealth, patience in the face of injustice increases one's honor, and begging opens the door to poverty.
story: the man to whom God gives neither wealth nor knowledge. He says, 'If I had money such as does that man, I would do as he does.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) concluded, "Those two men shall be equal in punishment." Ibn Maja narrated it similarly from Abu Bakr b. Abu Shayba, and 'All b. Mul:Iarnrnad, both of whom quoted from Waki'. Ibn Maja also narrated it from another line, from a {tadith of Man~ur, from Salim b. Abu al-Ja'd, from Ibn Abu Kabsha, from his father. Some sources give his name as 'Abd Allah b. Abu Kabsha.
lessons: Envying others for their wealth and wishing to spend it sinfully carries the same spiritual weight as the sin itself, reminding us to guard our inner thoughts.
Patience in the face of injustice is not weakness — it is a dignified response that God rewards with increased honor and standing.
Relying on begging rather than striving opens a door to lasting poverty, while charity and generosity, counterintuitively, protect and grow one's wealth.
practical_advice: When you feel envy rising toward someone's material success, pause and redirect that energy toward gratitude for what you have been given.
When you face an injustice you cannot immediately correct, practice patient endurance and trust that your dignity is being preserved and elevated through your response.
Before asking others for help in non-urgent situations, reflect on your own resources and effort first; cultivate a habit of giving even small amounts in charity to build a mindset of abundance.

title: Abu Kabsha al-Anmari and the Reward for Loaning a Horse
summary: Abu Kabsha al-Anmari related that the Prophet (SAAS) promised extraordinary spiritual reward for one who loans a horse to a Muslim fighter in God's cause, equivalent to providing seventy horses.
story: Al:Imad stated that Yazid b. 'Abd Rabbihi related to him, quoting Mul:Iarnmad b. Barb, quoting al-Zubaydi, from Rashid b. Sa'd, from Abu 'Amir al-Hawzani, who stated that Abu Kabsha al-Anmari carne to him and said, "Loan me your horse; for I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, 'A person who loans to a Muslim a horse that then gives birth for him will receive a reward equivalent to that for 70 horses he might have provided to bear fighters in God's cause.'"
lessons: Small acts of generosity, like loaning what you have, can carry spiritual weight far beyond what is visible to the human eye.
God's accounting of good deeds is not limited by the scale of the deed but by the sincerity and benefit behind it.
Encouraging others toward generosity, as Abu Kabsha did, is itself an act of virtue and community building.
practical_advice: Look for opportunities to lend or share what you have with those in need, even when the resource seems small or ordinary.
Remind yourself that acts of generosity you feel are insignificant may carry great reward, which can motivate you when giving feels hard.
When you hear an inspiring teaching, share it with others as Abu Kabsha did — spreading wisdom is its own form of charity.

title: Three Sworn Truths the Prophet Taught About Charity, Patience, and Begging
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) swore to three profound truths: charity never diminishes wealth, patience under injustice increases one's honor, and opening the door to begging opens the door to poverty.
story: Al-Tirmidhi narrated it from Mul:Iarnmad b. Isma'II, from Abu Nu'aym, from 'Ubada b. Muslim, from Yunus b. Khabbab, from Sa'id Abu a1-BukhturI al-Ta'i, who quoted Abu Kabsha as having quoted the Prophet (SAAS) as having said, "There are three things to which I will swear. And I will relate to you something; remember it. Charity never diminished a worshipper's wealth. No worshipper was ever maltreated by an injustice and then remained patient in face of it without God increasing his glory thereby. And no worshipper will ever open the door to begging without God opening for him a door to poverty." He categorized this as {tasan Fa{ti{t.
lessons: The spiritual economy operates by different rules than worldly economy — giving does not deplete but rather protects and multiplies one's provision.
Dignity under injustice is not passive defeat; it is an active spiritual stance that God honors with elevation in standing.
Self-sufficiency and reliance on God rather than others protects a person's inner dignity and material stability.
practical_advice: When financial stress makes giving feel impossible, remember that charity is described as a protection of wealth — give even a small amount and observe the shift in your mindset.
When you have been wronged and feel the urge to retaliate or complain excessively, practice restraint and document the strength it takes to remain dignified.
Before asking others for help, exhaust your own creative resources and du'a first; reserve asking for genuine necessity to protect your sense of self-reliance.

title: The Prophet's Practice of Cupping (Hijama)
summary: Companions reported that the Prophet (SAAS) would regularly have blood cupped from the top of his head and from between his shoulders, indicating his attention to physical health.
story: Al:Irnad narrated this from Ghundar, from Shu'ba, from al-A'mash, from Salim b. Abu al-Ja'd. Abu Da'ud and Ibn Maja narrated it from a {tadith of a1-Walld b. Muslim, from Ibn Thawban, from his father, from Abu Kabsha al-Anmari, who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would have blood cupped from the top of his head and from between his shoulders.
lessons: Caring for one's physical health is a practice modeled by the Prophet himself, reminding us that the body is an trust to be maintained.
Traditional and time-tested remedies have value and should not be dismissed in the pursuit of wellness.
Attention to physical wellbeing is connected to one's broader capacity to worship and serve others.
practical_advice: Make regular physical health check-ins part of your routine, following the prophetic model of proactive self-care.
Explore evidence-supported traditional wellness practices with qualified professionals as part of your holistic health approach.
When you are feeling drained or overwhelmed, consider whether you have been neglecting basic physical care — rest, nutrition, and health maintenance.

title: The Description of the Companions' Caps
summary: Abu Kabsha al-Anmari described that the caps worn by the Companions of the Prophet (SAAS) were flat and close-fitting, offering a glimpse into their modest appearance.
story: Al-TirrnidhI narrated that Bumayd b. Mas'ada related to him, quoting Mul:Iarnmad b. Barnran, from Abu Sa'Id - he being 'Abd Allah b. Busr - who said, "I heard Abu Kabsha al-Anmari say, 'The caps worn by the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) were flat and close-fitting.'"
lessons: Simplicity in appearance was a defining characteristic of the early Muslim community, reflecting inner contentment over outward display.
Even small details of the Companions' lives were preserved with care, showing how deeply they were admired and emulated.
Modesty in dress and presentation is a timeless expression of humility and connection to prophetic example.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether your relationship with appearance and clothing is driven by genuine modesty or by social pressure and comparison.
Incorporate simplicity into your daily choices as a small but meaningful way to connect with prophetic values.
When you notice attachment to status symbols, use it as an opportunity to pause and realign with what truly defines your worth.

title: Abu Muwayhiba and the Prophet's Final Night Visit to Al-Baqi' Cemetery
summary: Abu Muwayhiba, a freed servant of the Prophet, accompanied him on a night visit to the Baqi' cemetery where the Prophet prayed for the dead, warned of coming tribulations, and revealed he had chosen to meet his Lord rather than worldly reward — dying just days later.
story: Above is given the {tadith related by Imam Al:Imad with a line of transmission back to Abu Muwayhiba, who reported having gone with the Messenger of God (SAAS) at night to the aI-Baqi' cemetery. He narrated how the latter had stopped there, prayed, and asked forgiveness for them and then said, "You should be more pleased to be where you are than are some people to be where they are. Dissensions have arisen like pieces of dark night, riding the one upon the other, the latter ever more intense than the first. You should be pleased to be where you are." He then came back and said, "Abu Muwayhiba, I have been given to choose between having the keys to those places my nation will conquer after me, going to paradise, or meeting my Lord (soon). I have chosen to meet my Lord." Abu Muwayhiba went on, "Soon - just seven or eight days thereafter - he died."
lessons: The Prophet's choice to meet his Lord over worldly conquest or even paradise's immediate entry reflects the highest form of love — longing for God above all else.
Death, when approached with faith and readiness, is not a tragedy but a homecoming, and those who have lived righteously may be at peace where they are.
Coming tribulations are a reality in every age; the believer is encouraged to hold firm to their faith rather than be swept away by the chaos of successive trials.
practical_advice: When you grieve the dead or fear your own mortality, reflect on the Prophet's peaceful choice — death for the believer is not an end but a meeting with the Beloved.
In times of social and political turmoil, resist the pull of panic and tribalism; return to your personal practice of prayer, remembrance, and forgiveness.
Visit cemeteries occasionally as a spiritual practice to recalibrate your priorities and remember what truly lasts beyond this life.

title: Umm Ayman Weeps at the Prophet's Death Over the Cessation of Revelation
summary: After the Prophet's death, Abu Bakr and Umar visited Umm Ayman who was weeping, and when asked why she wept knowing the Prophet was in a better place, she replied that she wept because revelation from heaven had ceased — and they both wept with her.
story: We have given above the account of the visit made to her by Abu Bakr and 'Vmar, may God be pleased with them both, after the death of the Prophet (SAAS), and how she had wept. They then asked her, "Don't you realize that what is with God is better for the Messenger of God (SAAS)?" She replied, "Yes, indeed; but I am weeping because revelation from heaven has ceased." And they both wept along with her.
lessons: Grief is not a sign of weak faith — even the greatest Companions wept together, and acknowledging loss is a deeply human and spiritually honest response.
One can simultaneously believe in God's mercy and the goodness of the afterlife while still mourning what has been lost in this world.
The end of prophethood was a unique and irreplaceable loss; honoring its weight is itself an expression of love for the faith.
practical_advice: When someone you love tries to reframe your grief with silver linings, know that your tears can coexist with faith — allow yourself to grieve fully and honestly.
Find companions, as Abu Bakr and Umar did, who will sit with you in sorrow rather than rushing you past it; shared grief is a form of healing.
Channel grief over spiritual loss — distance from God, loss of community, or weakening of faith — into renewed commitment and remembrance.

title: Umm Ayman's Miraculous Water During the Hijra
summary: During the Hijra, Umm Ayman was fasting and overcome by intense thirst when a bucket of water descended from heaven, and she drank from it, never experiencing thirst again for the rest of her life.
story: Al-Mufac;lc;lal b. 'Assan stated that Wahb b. Jarlr related to him, quoting his father, who said, "I heard 'Vthman b. al-Qasim say, 'When Umm Ayrnan went on the Hijra, she arrived in the evening at al-Mun~arif, before al-Raw!).a'. She was fasting, and she was overcome by intense thirst. A bucket of water, attached to a white well-rope, came down from heaven for her, and she said, "I drank, and never again did I experience thirst. I was open to thirst through fasting on the Hijra journeys, but I was never actually thirsty thereafter!"'"
lessons: God's provision comes in unexpected and miraculous forms to those who sacrifice in His path, meeting needs that no human arrangement could fulfill.
Acts of faith undertaken at great personal cost — like fasting during a dangerous migration — are witnessed and honored by God in extraordinary ways.
The journey of migration, whether physical or spiritual, is never made alone; divine support accompanies sincere striving.
practical_advice: In moments of desperate need during a difficult transition, resist despair — history shows that provision arrives for those who keep moving forward in faith.
Combine your acts of worship with your seasons of hardship rather than postponing them; Umm Ayman fasted even while fleeing, and was divinely sustained.
When you recall moments where you were unexpectedly helped through a crisis, preserve those memories — they are evidence of God's care that can sustain you in future trials.

title: Umm Ayman's Long Service and the Prophet's Deep Affection for Her
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) called Umm Ayman his mother after his own mother, honored her years of devoted service, and publicly validated her right to ask him for water over others who were surprised by her boldness.
story: Abu Bakr b. Abu Khaythama stated that Sulayrnan b. Abu Sheikh used to tell him, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) would say, 'Umm Ayrnan is my mother who came after my own mother.'" AI-Waqidl quoted his colleagues of Medina as having said, "Umm Ayman looked over at the Messenger of God (SAAS), while he was drinking and said, 'Give me to drink.' 'A'isha demanded, 'Would you say such a thing to the Messenger of God?' She replied, 'I served him far longer!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'You are right.' And he then brought her water for her to drink."
lessons: Years of faithful, quiet service build a bond that commands deep respect — Umm Ayman's decades of devotion earned her the status of a second mother in the Prophet's eyes.
Those who have served and sacrificed in relationships have an earned intimacy that others may not immediately understand; that intimacy should be honored.
The Prophet's public validation of Umm Ayman's request teaches us to recognize and affirm the contributions of those who have served humbly and long.
practical_advice: Express specific, heartfelt gratitude to those who have served you faithfully over long periods — name what they did and let them know it was seen.
When someone's familiarity with you surprises others, resist the impulse to shrink or apologize; trust in the depth of a relationship built over time.
Consider who in your life has been a steady, quiet presence of care — a 'second mother' figure — and find a way to honor that relationship meaningfully.

title: Umm Ayman's Advice from the Prophet on Core Religious Duties
summary: While Umm Ayman served the Prophet (SAAS), a man came and asked for advice, and the Prophet gave him four foundational commands: do not associate partners with God, do not abandon prayer, do not drink intoxicants, and do not disobey your parents.
story: It is the people of Syria who narrate a ftadith from her. ]ubayr b. Nufayr quoted her as saying that she would wash the Messenger of God (SAAS) (before the prayer) and that one day a man came to the latter and asked him, "Give me advice!" He responded, "Do not associate any other with God, even if you should be mutilated or burned by fire. Do not deliberately omit a prayer; anyone who does this deliberately has the protection of God and that of His Messenger withdrawn from him. Do not drink intoxicants, for they are the chief factor of every sin. And do not disobey your parents, even if they should order you to abandon your family and your way of life."
lessons: The Prophet's concise advice covers the full architecture of a righteous life: right relationship with God, spiritual discipline, moral clarity, and family duty.
The deliberate abandonment of prayer is treated with utmost seriousness, signaling that consistent worship is the spine of a believer's life.
Obedience to parents is so fundamental that it remains obligatory even in circumstances of personal cost — reflecting Islam's deep emphasis on family bonds.
practical_advice: When feeling spiritually scattered or overwhelmed, return to these four pillars as a reset: your relationship with God, your prayers, your choices, and your family ties.
If prayer has slipped from your routine, make a concrete plan to restore even one prayer per day before gradually rebuilding the full practice.
If there is tension with a parent, identify one specific act of kindness or obedience you can offer this week regardless of the difficulty — small acts of reconnection matter.

title: Umm Ayman Described as What Remains of the Prophet's Family Household
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) would affectionately address Umm Ayman as 'mother' and describe her as what remained of his original childhood family household, honoring her as a living link to his earliest life.
story: AI-Waqidl stated that Ya!).ya b. Sa'Id b. Dinar informed him, from a sheikh of Banu SaId b. Bakr who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) used to address Umm Ayrnan as 'ya ammah', 'mother'. And he would look over at her and say, 'This woman is what remains of my (childhood) family household.'"
lessons: Those who have accompanied us through the earliest and most vulnerable stages of our lives hold an irreplaceable place, regardless of status or role.
Expressing love and honor to those who have been present through our formation is a prophetic practice worth emulating.
Longevity of faithful companionship creates a form of family that transcends blood — Umm Ayman was family by devotion and shared history.
practical_advice: Identify someone who has known you since your earliest years and reach out to express your gratitude for their long presence in your life.
When you feel untethered or without roots, seek out people and memories that connect you to your origins — they are an anchor for identity and belonging.
Honor the elderly people in your community who carry living memory of your family or community's history; their presence is irreplaceable.

title: Baraka (Umm Ayman) and Her Two Hijras
summary: Baraka, known as Umm Ayman, participated in both the Hijra to Abyssinia and the Hijra to Madinah, serving as the Prophet's nursemaid and one of his most devoted companions throughout his life.
story: She went out on both Hijras, may God be pleased with her. She was the nursemaid to the Messenger of God (SAAS), along with his own mother, Amina, daughter of Wahb. She was one of those received by the Messenger of God (SAAS), from the inheritance of his father. AI-Waqidl stated this. Others say that he inherited her from his mother. Yet others say she belonged to Khadlja's sister, who presented her to the Messenger of God (SAAS), that she accepted Islam early and went on the Hijra, following him later on.
lessons: Commitment to faith can manifest as a willingness to leave security behind not once but multiple times — Umm Ayman's double migration shows the depth of her devotion.
Those who accepted Islam in its earliest, most difficult days demonstrated a courage that shaped the entire future of the community.
God places certain people in a prophet's life from his very infancy — Umm Ayman's role from nursemaid to Companion shows how divinely arranged some relationships are.
practical_advice: When you are called to make a significant sacrifice for your faith or values, remember that some people have done so repeatedly and emerged stronger each time.
Reflect on the people who were placed in your life from the beginning — their presence may be more purposeful than you have recognized.
In seasons of migration or major life transition, draw strength from the example of those who left everything and still found belonging and meaning on the other side.

title: The Prophet's Women Servants: Salma, Khadra, Radwa, and Maymuna
summary: Among the Prophet's household servants were Salma, Khadra, Radwa, and Maymuna bint Sa'd, all of whom the Prophet (SAAS) manumitted, reflecting his consistent practice of freeing those in his service.
story: Mui)ammad b. Sa'd quoted al-Waqidl as having said that Fa'id, the freed-man of 'Ubayd Allah related to him, from CUbayd Allah b. CAli b. Abu Rafi',from his grandmother Salma, who said, "The servants of the Messenger of God (SAAS) were myself, Kha4ra, Ra4wa and Maymuna, daughter of Sa'd. The Messenger of God (SAAS) manumitted them all."
lessons: The Prophet's practice of manumitting all his servants reflects his deeply held value of human dignity and freedom as higher goods.
Those who served in his household did so within a framework of eventual liberation, modeling a community ethic of care and respect.
The preservation of these women's names in Islamic tradition shows that history honors those who serve faithfully, regardless of their social station.
practical_advice: Honor the people who serve in supporting roles in your life — acknowledge them by name, treat them with dignity, and recognize their contributions explicitly.
Consider whether the structures of your household or organization reflect the values of fairness, care, and eventual empowerment for those in supporting roles.
Reflect on the dignity with which you treat those who have less power or status than you in daily interactions — small moments of respect carry great moral weight.

title: The Joke About Al-Dajjal with Sawda
summary: Hafsa and Aisha joked with Sawda that Al-Dajjal had come forth, causing her to hide in fear. The Prophet arrived, clarified the truth gently, and Sawda emerged relieved.
story: Ibn al-Athjr stated in his work al-Ghaba, "CUlayka, daughter of aI-Kumayt narrated her J;adith from her grandmother, from Khulaysa, the freed-woman of I;Iaf~a, who told the story of I;Iaf~a and 'j\'isha with Sawda, daughter of Zam'a and their joking with her that al-DajjaF' had come forth. And so she hid in a house where they would light a fire and laugh together. The Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived and asked, 'What are you two up to?' they told him about what had happened with Sawda, and he went over to her and she asked, 'Messenger of God, has al-Dajjal really come forth?' He replied, 'No; though it is as though he had.' And so she emerged, brushing spiders' eggs from herself."
lessons: Fear of what has not yet come can paralyze us unnecessarily; seeking truth from a trusted source brings relief.
The Prophet's gentle correction rather than anger demonstrates that humor among companions was normal, and that truth must always be restored with kindness.
Even moments of fear can end in laughter and relief when grounded in honest reassurance.
practical_advice: When anxiety about a frightening scenario grips you, seek reliable information before assuming the worst is true.
If a joke or misunderstanding has caused someone distress, gently clarify the truth as soon as possible rather than letting fear linger.
Reflect on past moments where your fear turned out to be unfounded and let that memory calm your current anxieties.

title: Salman al-Farisi's Manumission and the Prophet's Generous Compensation
summary: Salman al-Farisi's mistress manumitted him, and the Prophet Muhammad compensated her by planting 300 palm seedlings on her behalf, an act Khulaysa remembered with distinction.
story: Ibn al-Athir mentioned Khulaysa, the freed-woman of Salman al-Farisi, saying, "Reference is made to her regarding the acceptance ofIslam by Salman al-Farisi, how his mistress manumitted him and the Messenger of God (SAAS) compensated her by planting 300 palm seedlings. She mentioned this as a matter of distinction."
lessons: Freedom from bondage — physical or spiritual — is worth great sacrifice and effort.
The Prophet modeled that those who facilitate goodness for others deserve recognition and fair compensation.
Acts of generosity and justice leave a lasting legacy in the hearts of those who witness them.
practical_advice: When someone helps you achieve a significant life change, find a meaningful way to acknowledge and repay their role in your journey.
Look for opportunities in your own life to plant something lasting — a skill, a habit, or a relationship — that will bear fruit for others over time.
Celebrate the milestones of freedom and personal growth, however they come, as Khulaysa remembered hers with pride.

title: The Interruption of Revelation Due to the Puppy Beneath the Prophet's Bed
summary: Revelation was interrupted because a puppy had died unnoticed beneath the Prophet's bed; once it was removed, the revelation returned with the words of Surah Al-Duha.
story: The !taft:;, Abu Nu'aym narrates her !taduh on a line of transmission through I:laf~ b.
