Sa'id al-Qurashi, from his mother, from her mother Khawla, who was the servant of the Messenger of God (SAAS). He presented her !tadith on the subject of the interruption in the revelation due to a puppy dog having died beneath the bed of the Prophet (SAAS), without anyone having been aware of this. When they took it away, the revelation returned, and the words of Almighty God were revealed, "By the early morning and the night when it becomes dark!" (suyat al-l)u!ta; XCIII, v.l, 2).
lessons: The resumption of divine guidance after a pause teaches that God's mercy is never permanently withdrawn.
Small, unnoticed things in our environment can sometimes block clarity and spiritual connection; tending to our surroundings matters.
Periods of spiritual silence or feeling cut off from God are temporary and do not indicate abandonment.
practical_advice: When you feel a loss of spiritual clarity or connection, examine your immediate environment and routines for things that may be creating barriers.
Trust that periods of spiritual dryness will end; use them as moments of patience and reflection rather than despair.
Read Surah Al-Duha during times when you feel spiritually distant, as it was revealed as a reassurance after a period of interrupted revelation.

title: The Prophet's Practice of Fasting on Ashura and Its Observance for Infants
summary: The Prophet venerated the fast of Ashura greatly and would instruct that even nursing infants not be suckled until nightfall on that day, calling upon the mothers of his household to observe this.
story: The /:taft:;; Abu Bakr al-Bayhaqi stated in his work Dala'il that Ibn 'Abdan informed him, quoting Al)mad b. 'Vbayd al-Saffar, quoting 'AlI b. al-Basan al-Sukari, quoting 'Vbayd Allah b. 'Vmar al-Qtwariri, quoting'Vlayka, daughter of al-Kumayt ai-'Atkiyya, who quoted her mother Amina as having said, "I asked Amat Allah, daughter of Razina, the freed-woman of the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'Amat Allah, did you ever hear your mother relate that she heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) mention the fast of 'Ashura'?'190 She replied, 'Yes; he venerated it highly. He would call that day for his suckling infants and those of his daughter Fatima. He would spit into their mouths and tell their mothers, "Do not suckle them until night."'"
lessons: Acts of worship that require sacrifice — even for the youngest — carry profound spiritual significance in the Islamic tradition.
Communal observance of sacred days strengthens the bonds of family and faith across generations.
Venerating what God has made sacred, even when it is difficult, reflects deep sincerity of faith.
practical_advice: Mark significant days of fasting or worship on your calendar and prepare your household for them with intention, turning them into shared family experiences.
When a spiritual practice feels demanding, remember that the Prophet extended it even to the most vulnerable, which dignifies the act rather than diminishes it.
Use days of fasting to reset your relationship with gratitude by consciously appreciating what you abstain from.

title: Safiyya's Capture, Conversion, Manumission, and Marriage to the Prophet
summary: Safiyya bint Huyayy declared her faith upon being taken captive at Khaybar; the Prophet released her, manumitted her, and then married her, giving her the freed-woman Razina as her dowry.
story: The !taft:;, Abu Yaqa stated that Abu Sa'idal-Jushami related to him, quoting 'Ulayka, daughter of al-Kumayr, quoting her mother Amina, who quoted Amat Allah, daughter of Razina, the freed-woman of the Messenger of God (SAAS), as having stated that the latter took Safiyya captive at the batdes in which God gave victory against Banu Qurana and Banu aI-Nadir. He led her in as a captive. When she saw the women, she called out, "I testify that there is not god but God, and that you are the Messenger of God!" He then released her, having been holding her by the arm, and manumitted her. Later he proposed marriage to her, married her and gave her Razina as her dowry. This is the sequence of events as given in this text which is better than that previously presented from the account of Ibn Abu cA~im. However, the truth is that the Prophet (SAAS), selected Safiyya from the booty taken at Khaybar and that he made her manumission her dowry.
lessons: A sincere declaration of faith transforms one's entire circumstance; it is the starting point of a new life.
Dignity can be restored in moments of vulnerability through the recognition of one's humanity and sincerity.
The events of our lives, even painful ones, can become the foundation of an entirely new and honored chapter.
practical_advice: In moments of profound vulnerability, a clear declaration of your values and beliefs can become the anchor that reorients your life.
When you witness someone sincerely seeking a better path, respond with openness rather than suspicion, as the Prophet responded to Safiyya's testimony.
Reflect on how your most difficult life transitions have ultimately opened doors to new beginnings that you could not have anticipated.

title: Salma's Medical Wisdom: Cupping and Henna for Ailments
summary: Salma, servant of the Prophet, narrated that he consistently recommended cupping for headaches and henna for leg complaints, reflecting his practical care for the health of those around him.
story: Imam A1)mad stated that AbU al-Nagr related to him, quoting Ibrahim b. Sa'd, from Mu1)ammad b. Is1)aq, from 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Ali b. Abu Rafi', from Salma, who said, "Whenever I heard anyone complain to the Messenger of God (SAAS) about a headache, he inevitably responded, 'Have your blood cupped.' When the complaint related to the person's legs, he would say, 'Dye them with henna.'"
lessons: Attending to the physical wellbeing of those in your care is a form of compassion and leadership.
Simple, consistent remedies offered with care can provide both physical and emotional relief.
The Prophet's attentiveness to the everyday health complaints of his household shows that no concern is too small to address with sincerity.
practical_advice: Make a habit of asking those close to you how they are feeling physically, not just emotionally, as the two are deeply connected.
Research and maintain a small set of trusted, evidence-based home remedies so you can respond helpfully to those around you in moments of minor illness.
When someone comes to you with a complaint, resist the urge to dismiss it; even a small, thoughtful response demonstrates care.

title: Fatima's Final Day: Her Dignified Preparation for Death
summary: On the day of her death, Fatima bathed thoroughly, dressed in new clothes, arranged her bed facing the Kaaba, and passed away peacefully while instructing that no one uncover her, witnessed by her servant Salma.
story: Imam A1)mad stated that AbU al-Nagr related to him, quoting Ibrahim b. Sa'd, from Mu1)ammad b. Is1)aq, from 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Ali b. Abu Rafi', from Salma, who said, "Fatima suffered greatly in the illness from which she died, and I would nurse her." One morning Salma went to her as usual during her illness. She said, "'Ali went out on some business of his and Farima said to me, 'ya ammah! \'Maid\'! Pour me some water to wash.' I poured her some and she washed herself more thoroughly than I had ever seen her do before. She then said, 'yii ammah, hand me my new clothes.' And she put them on. She then said, 'ya ammah, put my bed in the centre of the house.' I did so. She lay down on it, facing towards the ka'ba, placed her hand beneath her cheek and said, 'ya ammah, I am dying now. I have cleansed myself and so no one should uncover me.' And she died right there. When 'Ali came, I told him."
lessons: A life well-lived allows one to face death with dignity, composure, and spiritual readiness rather than fear.
Preparation — physical, spiritual, and practical — is an act of love for those who will be left behind.
Fatima's final moments reflect that death approached with faith is not a defeat but a peaceful return.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether you are living in a way that would allow you to face the end of your life with peace; make adjustments where needed.
Tend to your spiritual preparation daily rather than leaving it for a distant future, so that when difficult moments arrive you are already ready.
Honor the dignity of those who are ill or dying by listening carefully to their wishes and carrying them out with care, as Salma did for Fatima.

title: Shirin the Copt Given to Hassan ibn Thabit
summary: Shirin, sister of Mariya the Copt and thus aunt of the Prophet's son Ibrahim, was given by the Prophet to Hassan ibn Thabit, the poet of Islam, and bore him his son Abd al-Rahman.
story: Shirin was also one of them. She was also called SUin. She was the sister of Mariya the Copt and was therefore the aunt of Ibrahim, may peace be upon him. We have explained above how the muqawkis, the Governor of Alexandria, whose name wasJurayj b. Mina had presented her along with a slave named Mabar and a mule named al-Duldui. The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave her to I:Iassan b. Thabit and she bore him his son 'Abd al-Rai)man.
lessons: The Prophet's thoughtful distribution of gifts and people reflects his care for building a harmonious community.
Lives that begin in displacement can take root in new places and bear lasting fruit for generations.
Every person, regardless of their origin, carries the potential to become a cherished part of a community's story.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in an unfamiliar environment or community, focus on the connections you are building rather than what you have left behind.
Recognize that being placed in a new context — even an unexpected one — may be the beginning of your most meaningful contributions.
Celebrate the diversity of backgrounds in your community as a source of richness, as the early Muslim community exemplified.

title: The Prophet Renames a Servant Girl 'Unqada
summary: A servant girl of Aisha whose early name meant 'a grape' was renamed 'Unqada, meaning 'bunch of grapes,' by the Prophet Muhammad, a small act reflecting his attentiveness and warmth toward those in his household.
story: They also included 'Unqada al-I:Iabashiyya, mother of Malii). She was a servant girl belonging to 'A'isha. Her early name was qnba, "a grape", and the Messenger of God (SAAS) renamed her 'Unqada, "bunch of grapes". Aba Nu'aym narrated this. Her name is also given as "Ghafira".
lessons: Small gestures of recognition and personalization — like a thoughtfully chosen name — can profoundly affirm a person's sense of belonging.
The Prophet's attentiveness to even the smallest members of his household reflects the Islamic value of honoring every human being.
Being seen and acknowledged, even in small ways, is a powerful act of kindness.
practical_advice: Make a conscious effort to learn and correctly use the names and preferred identities of people around you, especially those who may feel overlooked.
Find small, personalized ways to acknowledge those who serve or support you, whether through a kind word, a gesture, or simply remembering something meaningful about them.
Reflect on the names or labels you carry and whether they truly reflect who you are; surround yourself with people who see and affirm your best self.

title: The Prophet's Advice to His Wet-Nurse Farwa on Reciting Surah Al-Kafirun at Bedtime
summary: The Prophet told his wet-nurse Farwa that reciting Surah Al-Kafirun before sleep would protect her from unbelief, offering a simple spiritual practice for nightly protection.
story: Also there was Farwa, the wet-nurse of the Prophet (SAAS). She stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said to her, 'When you go to bed, recite, \'Say: "0 you unbelievers"\' (siirat al-Kiifiriin; CIX, v. I). It will keep you free from unbelief.' Aba Ai)rnad made reference to her. This is stated by Ibn al-Athir in his work al-Ghaba.
lessons: Consistent spiritual practices at the beginning and end of each day build a protective fortress of faith around the heart.
The Prophet's personal guidance to individuals in his household demonstrates that spiritual advice is most powerful when it is specific and personal.
Simple acts of worship, consistently performed, have the power to guard against spiritual drift over time.
practical_advice: Establish a nightly spiritual routine — a brief recitation, prayer, or reflection — that grounds you in your values before you sleep.
Make Surah Al-Kafirun part of your bedtime practice as a declaration of your faith and a protection for your heart.
Share simple, actionable spiritual practices with those close to you rather than only discussing faith in abstract terms.

title: Ali, Fatima, and Fiqqa's Vow of Fasting: Feeding the Poor, Orphan, and Captive
summary: When al-Hasan and al-Husayn fell ill and then recovered, Ali, Fatima, and their servant Fiqqa vowed three days of fasting. Each night as they prepared to break their fast, a visitor came to their door — first a beggar, then an orphan, then a captive — and each night they gave away their food, going hungry themselves, until God revealed verses of the Quran in their honor.
story: He then gave, through an indistinct line of transmission, from Mabhab b. Humayd al-Balin, from al-Qasim b. Bahram, from Layth, from Mujahid, material from Ibn 'Abbas, regarding the statement of the Almighty, "And they donate for love of Him food to the poor, to orphans and to captives"(surat al-Insan or al-Dahr; LXXVII, v.S). He went on to relate, in essence, that al-Hasan and al-Husayn fell ill and were visited by the Messenger of God (SAAS), and by the general public. They asked 'Ali, "To what would it be, if you were to swear a resolution?" 'Ali said, "If they recover from what ails them both, I will offer God three days of fasting." Fatima said the same, and so did Fiqqa. God did cure them both, and they fasted. Then 'Ali went and borrowed from Sham'an al-Khaybari three sa' weight of barley. That night they prepared one sa' of it to eat. As they were about to eat it for their evening meal, there was a beggar standing at their door who asked, "Feed the poor! May God feed you at the tables of paradise!" 'Ali gave orders and they fed him, themselves not eating. The third night they prepared the second sa' of barley and when they had it ready before them stood a beggar, who said, "Feed the orphan!" And so they fed him, themselves not eating. The third night he asked, "Feed the captive!" And so they fed him, themselves not eating. And so God sent down regarding them, "Surely there came for man a period of time" to "We desire from you neither reward nor thanks" (surat al-Insan; V, v.1-9).
lessons: True generosity means giving even when you yourself are in need — the family of Ali gave their only food while going hungry, showing that selflessness transcends personal sacrifice.

Fulfilling a vow made to God is a profound act of devotion; Ali, Fatima, and Fiqqa honored their pledge fully despite the difficulty, demonstrating that integrity in worship strengthens one's relationship with the Divine.
God sees and honors sincere sacrifice — the revelation of Quranic verses in recognition of their act affirms that no act of genuine charity done for God's sake goes unnoticed or unrewarded.
practical_advice: When you make a commitment to God during a time of hardship or fear, write it down and hold yourself to it when the hardship passes — the fulfillment of that vow becomes a spiritual anchor.

Practice small acts of giving even when resources are tight; start by donating something you genuinely need rather than only what is surplus, and notice how it shifts your sense of attachment and trust in God's provision.

When you feel overwhelmed by your own struggles, look for one person around you who may be in greater need — redirecting your energy toward helping others can alleviate anxiety and restore a sense of purpose and gratitude.

title: Maymuna, Freed-Woman of the Prophet, Asks About Jerusalem
summary: Maymuna, daughter of Sa'd and freed-woman of the Prophet, asked him about Jerusalem and its spiritual significance. The Prophet described it as the land of resurrection and gathering, and told those unable to travel there that donating oil for its lamps would earn them a reward equivalent to praying there.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that 'Ali b. Bahr related to him, quoting 'Isa — he being Ibn Yusuf — quoting Thawr — he being Ibn Yazid — from Ziyad b. Abu Sawda, from his brother, that Maymuna, the freed-woman of the Prophet (SAAS), asked, "Furnish us with information about Jerusalem." He replied, "(It is) the land of both al-manshar and al-mahshar, 'the dispersal and the in-gathering'. Go there and pray therein; a prayer there is as a thousand prayers (elsewhere)." She asked, "What do you think of someone not being able to withstand the strain of being borne there, or of making his way there!" He replied, "Then let him have oil for lamps taken there. For anyone who presents a gift for it is as one who prays there."
lessons: Islam recognizes human limitation with compassion — those who cannot make a difficult journey are not left without a means of spiritual connection, showing that God's mercy accommodates every circumstance.
Sacred places hold special spiritual weight, and maintaining a connection to them — even symbolically — keeps the believer anchored to the broader community of faith across time and distance.

Questions born of sincere curiosity and desire to worship better are always welcome; Maymuna's question led to guidance that benefited all Muslims who heard it, showing that seeking knowledge is itself an act of worship.
practical_advice: If you feel spiritually distant from sacred places or unable to perform certain acts of worship due to circumstances, identify a small, meaningful contribution you can make — charity, prayer, or supporting a cause connected to that place — to maintain that spiritual bond.

When you feel disconnected from your faith community or unable to participate fully, remember that intention and symbolic gestures that express your sincere desire to connect carry real spiritual weight with God.

Study the significance of sacred sites in your tradition not merely as geography but as reminders of accountability and return to God — let awareness of the Day of Gathering motivate mindful daily choices.

title: The Prophet's Prayer of Healing Taught to a Woman of Quraysh
summary: A woman of Quraysh came to the Prophet through 'A'isha seeking a prayer for contentment and tranquillity. The Prophet taught her to place her right hand over her heart and recite a supplication asking God for His cure and healing, and the narrator attested to its effectiveness.
story: Similarly, al-Munji' b. Mus'ab Abu 'Abd Allah al-'Abdi gives a hadith from Rabi'a, daughter of Marthad, who lived among Banu Quray', from Muhabbih, from Maymuna, daughter of Abu 'Asib, also known as the daughter of Abu 'Anbasa, the freed-woman of the Prophet (SAAS). She stated that a woman of Huraysh came to the Prophet (SAAS), and called out, "'A'isha, help me out with some prayer from the Messenger of God (SAAS), with which you can provide me contentment and tranquillity." He replied to her, "Place your right hand over your heart, rub it and say, 'In the name of God. O God, cure me by your cure, heal me by your healing and by Your grace let me dispense with all others.'" Rabi'a went on, "I did use this prayer and found it excellent."
lessons: The Prophet addressed emotional and physical distress with both spiritual and embodied guidance — placing the hand over the heart while supplicating is a reminder that healing is holistic, touching body, mind, and soul.

Seeking help through people of knowledge and righteousness when you are struggling is not weakness but wisdom; the woman approached through 'A'isha, showing that using the right intermediary to access guidance is praiseworthy.

God's healing encompasses both the cure from illness and the healing of inner distress — the prayer asks for both, affirming that anxiety, grief, and disquiet are legitimate concerns that God responds to.
practical_advice: Memorize this prophetic supplication and use it when you feel anxious, unwell, or emotionally overwhelmed: place your right hand on your chest, breathe slowly, and recite it with full attention to its meaning.

When a friend or family member is suffering and you feel helpless, connect them to sources of wisdom and guidance rather than trying to solve everything yourself — sometimes the most powerful thing you can do is make the right introduction.

Develop a personal collection of short prophetic prayers for specific emotional states — for anxiety, grief, fear, and uncertainty — so that in difficult moments you have an immediate spiritual response ready.

title: Umm 'Ayyash Serves the Prophet's Daughter After Her Marriage to Uthman
summary: The Prophet sent his servant Umm 'Ayyash with his daughter to serve her after her marriage to Uthman ibn Affan. Umm 'Ayyash prepared fresh grape and date drinks for Uthman daily, until he asked her to stop mixing them, which she complied with.
story: Abu al-Qasim al-Baghawi stated that 'Ikrima related to him, quoting 'Abd al-Wahid b. Safwan, quoting Abu Safwan, from his father, from his grandmother Umm 'Ayyash — who had been the servant of the Prophet (SAAS), that the latter sent her along with his daughter to 'Uthman. She said, "I would press out dates for 'Othman in the morning and he would drink it in the evening. In the evening I would press grapes that he would drink in the morning. One day he asked me, 'Do you mix anything into it?' 'Yes,' I replied. 'Don't do that again,' he told me."
lessons: Uthman's careful attention to what he consumed, asking about the preparation of his drink and correcting it when necessary, reflects the Islamic principle of being mindful and conscientious about what enters the body.

Acts of service, however humble, carry dignity when performed with sincerity and care — Umm 'Ayyash's daily labor of preparing food and drink was a form of devoted service that preserved family bonds across a new marriage.

Responding to correction with immediate compliance rather than defensiveness is a mark of good character; when Uthman asked Umm 'Ayyash to change her practice, she simply agreed, modeling gracious acceptance of guidance.
practical_advice: When you are serving others — whether in a professional, familial, or community role — pay attention to their preferences and be genuinely open to feedback, understanding that adjusting your approach is a sign of care, not failure.
Practice mindfulness about daily habits like food and drink; small acts of attentiveness to what you consume can be an extension of your spiritual discipline and self-respect.

If someone corrects you about a habit or practice, pause before reacting defensively — take a moment to consider whether their concern is rooted in care, and respond with the same graciousness that Umm 'Ayyash demonstrated.

title: Anas ibn Malik: Ten Years of Service to the Prophet and the Fulfillment of His Prayer
summary: Anas ibn Malik served the Prophet for the entire ten years of his residence in Medina, during which the Prophet never once criticized or questioned him. His mother had presented him to the Prophet as a child, and the Prophet prayed for him with wealth, children, long life, and paradise — prayers that Anas witnessed fulfilled in his own lifetime.
story: Anas b. Malik b. al-Nadr b. Damdam b. Zayd b. Haram b. Jundab b. 'Asim b. Ghanm b. 'Adi b. al-Najjar al-Ansari al-Najjari, the father of Hamza al-Madani; he was originally from al-Basra. He served the Messenger of God (SAAS), throughout the length of his ten-year residence in Medina. The latter never criticized him for anything. He never asked him why he had done something, nor why he had not done something. His mother was Umm Sulaym, daughter of Milhan b. Khalid b. Zayd b. Haram. It was she who presented him to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who received him kindly. She asked him to say a prayer for him, and he responded, "O God, increase his wealth, grant him children, give him long life and admit him to paradise." Anas stated, "I did see two of these and I await the third. I swear by God, I have much wealth and my children and grandchildren approach 100 in number." In one account he is quoted as having said, "My vineyard bears fruit twice a year. And my progeny totals 106 children." There are differences of opinion over whether or not he was present at the battle of Badr. An Ansari narrated from his father who quoted Thumama as having said that Anas was asked whether he had been present at Badr, and that he replied, "Where else would I have been than at Badr, may you lose your mother!" However, it is widely accepted that he did not attend either the battles of Badr or Uhud, due to his youth. He did not participate in the events of al-Hudaybiyya, Khaybar, the 'umrat al-qada', the conquest of Mecca, Hunayn, al-Ta'if and those subsequent. Abu Hurayra stated, "I never saw anyone perform prayer more like that of the Messenger of God (SAAS) than the son of Umm Salim" — meaning Anas b. Malik. Ibn Sirin stated, "He performed the prayer better than anyone else, whether travelling or at home." He died at Basra, the last of the Companions to survive there, according to 'Ali b. al-Madini. His death was in 90 AH. Others give that date as 91, 92 or 93.
lessons: The Prophet's treatment of Anas — never criticizing him, never asking why he did or did not do something — is a profound model of compassionate leadership that nurtures rather than diminishes those in our care.

Prophetic prayers carry extraordinary power, and Anas's life became a living testimony to that — witnessing the fulfillment of blessings prayed for in childhood is a reminder that God's gifts often unfold across an entire lifetime.

Consistent excellence in worship, as demonstrated by Anas who was praised for his prayer by Abu Hurayra and Ibn Sirin, is built through decades of devotion beginning in youth — the habits formed in service to the Prophet shaped him for life.
practical_advice: If you are in a position of authority over others — whether as a parent, employer, teacher, or leader — practice the Prophetic model of correction: address what truly matters with wisdom, and refrain from constant criticism of minor matters that can erode confidence and trust.

When you feel that your duas are going unanswered, reflect on Anas's story — some of the most profound blessings take years or even decades to fully manifest; maintain your prayers with patience and trust in God's timing.

Invest in the spiritual formation of young people in your life by making sincere prayers for them — a heartfelt dua made for a child can become a source of blessing that shapes their entire life, just as the Prophet's prayer shaped Anas's.

title: Anas b. Malik: The Last Companion of Basra
summary: A brief account of Anas b. Malik's distinguished life as a servant of the Prophet, his excellence in prayer, and his death as the last Companion to survive in Basra at nearly 100 years of age.
story: due to his youth. He did not participate in the events of al-Hudaybiyya, Khaybar, the 'umrat al-qada', the conquest of Mecca, Hunayn, al-Ta'if and those subsequent. Abu Hurayra stated, "I never saw anyone perform prayer more like that of the Messenger of God (SAAS) than the son of Umm Salim" - meaning Anas b. Malik. Ibn Sirin stated, "He performed the prayer better than anyone else, whether travelling or at home." He died at Basra, the last of the Companions to survive there, according to 'Ali b. al-Madini. His death was in 90 AH. Others give that date as 91, 92 or 93 AH. The first date given is the one most widely and fully accepted. Regarding his age at his death, Imam Ahmad stated in his compendium of hadith attributed to him, "Mu'tamir b. Sulayman related to us, from Hamid, that Anas lived to 100 minus 1 year." The least figure given is 96 years. The highest are 107, 106 and 103 years. God knows best.
lessons: Excellence in worship is a lifelong pursuit; Anas b. Malik's dedication to perfecting his prayer demonstrates that devotion to God can define an entire life.
Being the last survivor of a beloved community is a reminder of the passing of time and the importance of preserving and transmitting sacred knowledge while we can.
Age and longevity can be a blessing when spent in service to God and humanity, as Anas lived nearly a century in devoted service to the prophetic legacy.
practical_advice: Reflect on the quality of your prayers, not just their frequency — consider one aspect of your prayer today that you can improve in mindfulness or form.
When you feel like the last one holding on to something good in your community, recognize that your steadfastness is itself a form of worship and legacy.
Use your years as opportunities to deepen your relationship with God, knowing that a long life spent in devotion is one of the greatest gifts.

title: Al-Asla' b. Sharik and the Revelation of Tayammum
summary: Al-Asla' b. Sharik, a servant of the Prophet, experienced a state of ritual impurity while traveling and was unable to purify himself until Gabriel brought the verse permitting tayammum — purification with clean earth — which the Prophet demonstrated personally.
story: They also included al-Asla' b. Sharik b. 'Awf al-A'raji. Muhammad b. Sa'd stated, "His name was Maymun b. Sinbadh. Al-Rabi' b. Badr al-A'raji stated, from his father, from his grandfather, who quoted al-Asla' as having said, 'I used to be a servant of the Prophet and journey with him. One night he told me, "Asla', get up and prepare to travel." I replied, "Messenger of God, I have experienced a major ritual impurity."' He was quiet a while, and Gabriel brought him the ayat al-sa'id (surat al-Nisa'; IV, v.43), and he said, "Asla', get up and cleanse yourself with sand." I wiped myself and prayed. When I reached water, he told me, "Asla', off you go and wash yourself." He showed me how to clean using sand or earth. The Messenger of God (SAAS) plunged his hands into the earth and then shook them and used them to wipe both arms, the right hand wiping the left, the left the right, on both their upper and lower sides.' "Al-Rabi' went on, 'My father showed me, as had his father him, as al-Asla' had him, as the Messenger of God (SAAS) had shown him.' "Al-Rabi' also stated, 'I related this hadith to 'Awf b. Abu Jamila, who said, "I swear by God, that is what I saw al-Hasan do!"'" Ibn Manda and al-Baghawi narrated this in their book Mu'jam al-Sahaba, from a hadith of this al-Rabi' b. Badr. Al-Baghawi stated, "I do not know of anyone else who narrated it." Ibn 'Asakir stated that al-Haytham b. Ruzayq al-Maliki al-Mudliji narrated this hadith, from his father, from al-Asla' b. Sharik.
lessons: Divine guidance arrives precisely when we need it most; the revelation of tayammum came at a moment of genuine need, showing that God's mercy accommodates human circumstances.
The Prophet's patience and gentleness in teaching — demonstrating tayammum by hand — models how knowledge should be transmitted with care and compassion.
Sacred knowledge transmitted across generations retains its power and authenticity; this chain from al-Asla' through four generations to al-Rabi' shows the living tradition of the Sunnah.
practical_advice: When you face an obstacle to worship — whether physical, logistical, or circumstantial — know that Islam provides alternatives; seek out the permitted accommodations rather than abandoning worship altogether.
Learn the physical acts of worship directly from a teacher or knowledgeable person when possible, as embodied demonstration preserves nuance that words alone cannot.
When a difficulty temporarily blocks your usual routine, use that moment to reflect on God's mercy in making the path to Him always accessible, no matter the circumstance.

title: Asma' b. Haritha and the Fast of Ashura
summary: The Prophet sent Asma' b. Haritha to his people ordering them to fast on the day of Ashura, instructing that even those who had already eaten should fast for the remainder of the day.
story: Asma' b. Haritha b. Sa'd b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Abbad b. Sa'd b. 'Amr b. 'Amir b. Tha'laba b. Malik b. Aqsa al-Aslami was also one of them. He was from al-Suffa, according to Muhammad b. Sa'd. He was the brother of Hind b. Haritha; they both served the Prophet (SAAS). Imam Ahmad stated that 'Affan related to him, quoting Wahib, quoting 'Abd al-Rahman b. Harmala, from Yahya b. Hind b. Haritha. Hind was a Companion who had been present at al-Hudaybiyya. It was Hind's brother Asma' b. Haritha whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) had sent to his people with orders to fast the day of al-'ashura. Imam Ahmad went on to state that Yahya b. Hind related to him, from Asma' b. Haritha, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had sent him (Hind) to him and said, "Tell your people to fast today." Asma' asked, "But what if I found they have already eaten?" "Then they should fast the rest of the day," he replied. Ahmad b. Khalid al-Dhahabi narrated it from Muhammad b. Ishaq, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr, from Habib b. Hind b. Asma' al-Aslami, from his father Hind, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent me to Aslam's people. I told them, 'Tell your people to fast today; those you find to have eaten already should fast for the remainder of the day.'" Muhammad b. Sa'd stated that al-Waqidi quoted Muhammad b. Nu'aym b. 'Abd Allah al-Mujammir, who quoted his father as having said, "I heard Abu Hurayra say, 'I used to think of Hind and Asma', the sons of Haritha, as merely two mamluks owned by the Messenger of God (SAAS).'" Al-Waqidi stated, "These two men, along with Anas b. Malik were servants of his and never left his door." Muhammad b. Sa'd stated that Asma' b. Haritha died in 66 AH in al-Basra, at the age of 80.
lessons: Obedience to a righteous command need not be all-or-nothing; the Prophet's instruction to fast the remainder of the day for those who had already eaten shows that partial compliance is still valued.
Sincere servants of God do not abandon their posts; Hind, Asma', and Anas never left the Prophet's door, illustrating the beauty of wholehearted, consistent devotion.
Sacred practices are reinforced through community; sending a messenger to remind a whole people to fast shows that spiritual disciplines are strengthened when observed collectively.
practical_advice: If you miss the beginning of a good practice today — a fast, a prayer time, a good deed — don't abandon it entirely; begin where you are and complete what you can.
Consider who in your life might benefit from a timely reminder about a spiritual practice, and find a gentle way to share that reminder as the Prophet did through his messengers.
Reflect on the people who serve quietly and consistently without recognition; express appreciation to those who support your spiritual or community life without seeking the spotlight.

title: Bukayr b. al-Shaddakh: The Prophet's Prayer and a Life of Honesty
summary: Bukayr b. al-Shaddakh, a servant of the Prophet, honestly disclosed to the Prophet upon reaching puberty that he could no longer freely enter the household; the Prophet prayed for his truthfulness, a prayer whose effect was later witnessed when 'Umar accepted his confession about killing a Jew who had violated a warrior's family honor.
story: Bukayr b. al-Shaddakh was among them. Ibn Manda stated, quoting from a line of transmission through Abu Bakr al-Hudhali, from 'Abd al-Malik b. Yaqa al-Laythi, who said that Bukayr b. Shaddakh al-Laythi was a servant of the Prophet (SAAS). When he attained puberty, the Messenger of God (SAAS) was aware of that. Bukayr said, "I have been going in to your household. But I have now attained puberty, Messenger of God." The latter replied, "O God, (put) trust in his word and grant him success!" (Ibn Manda went on) "During the rule of 'Umar, a Jew was killed. 'Umar stood to make an address and said, 'I adjure you by God, does any man of you have knowledge of that?' Bukayr arose and said, 'I killed him, Commander of the Believers!' 'Umar asked, 'And what solution do you have, now that you have spilled his blood?' "He replied, 'Commander of the Believers, a certain warrior left me in charge of his family and I discovered that Jew with his wife. He was reciting: "Ash'ath was diverted away from me by Islam; I was left alone with his bride on the consummation night. I sleep upon her breast bones, while he spends the night on dusty harnesses. Her inner thighs are as though up and ready for all and sundry!"' "'Umar believed what he said and judged the blood of the Jew to have been shed without need for him to be avenged, in light of the prayer of the Messenger of God (SAAS) for (the truthfulness of) Bukayr, as given above."
lessons: Honesty, even when it is difficult or potentially self-incriminating, is a hallmark of true character; Bukayr's transparency with the Prophet as a youth and later with 'Umar demonstrates that integrity is consistent across circumstances.

The prayers of the righteous carry weight; the Prophet's prayer for Bukayr's truthfulness had tangible consequences decades later, reminding us that sincere supplication plants seeds whose fruits may not be seen immediately.
Accountability before God and community is the foundation of a just society; Bukayr's willingness to come forward publicly when 'Umar asked showed moral courage in the face of serious personal risk.
practical_advice: Practice honesty in small matters today, knowing that a habit of truthfulness in minor things builds the courage for honesty when the stakes are high.
Ask someone you trust to pray for you in a specific area of your character — the prayers of sincere people for one another are a prophetic tradition with real spiritual power.
When you face a situation requiring moral courage, recall that speaking the truth openly, even at personal cost, is the path that leads to vindication and peace of conscience.

title: Bilal b. Rabah: From Enslaved Believer to First Caller of Prayer
summary: Bilal b. Rabah, a formerly enslaved man who endured severe persecution for his faith, was purchased and freed by Abu Bakr, and went on to become the first muezzin in Islam, a participant in major battles, and a towering figure in the early Muslim community.
story: They also include Bilal b. Rabah al-Habashi. Born in Mecca, he had been the freed-man of Umayya b. Khalaf. Abu Bakr purchased him from him for a substantial sum because Umayya was severely persecuting Bilal to persuade him to give up Islam. Bilal, however, may God be pleased with him, insisted on remaining a Muslim. When Abu Bakr purchased Bilal, he set him free as an act of homage to God. He went out on the Hijra with the others and participated in the battles of Badr, Uhud and later engagements. He was known as Bilal, son of Hamama, who was his mother. He was one of the most eloquent of men; he did not, as some believe, mispronounce the letter sin as shin, despite the fact that some relate a groundless hadith from the Messenger of God (SAAS), to that effect. He was one of the four persons who made the call to prayer, as will be explained. Moreover, he was the first to ever make the call, as we have narrated above. He was in charge of the expenses for the children and the sum of the wealth (for distribution) was in his possession. Following the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS), he was one of those who went on the military expeditions against Syria. It is also said, however, that he stayed behind and acted as the mu'azzin for Abu Bakr throughout his caliphate. The first of these two possibilities is the better and is more widely accepted. Al-Waqidi stated that he died in Damascus in 20 AH, at some 60 years of age. Al-Fallas stated that his grave is in Damascus. Others say it is at Dariya. Alternatively, he is said to have died in Aleppo; the truth, however, is that it was his brother Khalid who died in Aleppo. Makhul stated, "Someone who had seen Bilal described him to me as of very dark skin, lean and hump-backed, and with thick hair, the grey of which he did not dye. May God be pleased with him."
lessons: Faith that withstands persecution becomes the foundation of lasting legacy; Bilal's refusal to abandon Islam under torture made him a symbol of spiritual endurance for all generations.

Freedom and dignity are inseparable from faith; Abu Bakr's purchase and liberation of Bilal was not merely an act of kindness but of worship — a recognition that enslaving a believer was an affront to God.

Those who have suffered the most in service of truth are often entrusted with its most honored expressions; Bilal, who endured the greatest trials, was given the honor of being the first voice to call humanity to prayer.
practical_advice: When you face pressure to compromise your values or beliefs, reflect on Bilal's example — his steadfastness under extreme suffering produced a legacy that echoes through history and into eternity.
Consider how you can use whatever resources or influence you have — as Abu Bakr used his wealth — to defend and support those who are being persecuted for righteousness.

When you hear the adhan, remember that it was first uttered by a man who had been tortured for his faith, and let that history transform the call to prayer from routine sound into a profound act of remembrance.

title: Habba and Sawa': The Prophet's Counsel on Never Despairing of Provision
summary: Two brothers, Habba and Sawa', sons of Khalid, visited the Prophet while he was mending something and helped him; he counseled them never to despair of God's provision, reminding them that just as God clothes the newborn child, He will always provide.
story: Habba and Sawa', sons of Khalid, were also among them; may God be pleased with them both. Imam Ahmad stated that Abu Mu'awiya related to him, quoting Waki', quoting al-A'mash, from Sallam b. Shurahbil, who quoted Habba and Sawa' as having said, "We once went in to the Prophet (SAAS), while he was mending something, and we helped him. He then told us, 'Never despair of provision (from God), so long as your heads have movement. Man is born of his mother skinless, with no outer covering, and then God, the Almighty and Glorious, provides it for him.'"
lessons: Despair about sustenance is a form of distrust in God; the Prophet's teaching that we should never lose hope as long as we are alive is a direct antidote to anxiety about material needs.

God's provision is proven from the very moment of birth; the image of a newborn arriving in the world with nothing and yet being clothed and fed is a powerful reminder of divine care that has accompanied us from our first breath.
Even in moments of humble, ordinary work — mending something — the Prophet found opportunities to transmit wisdom, reminding us that profound guidance can emerge from the simplest shared moments.
practical_advice: The next time financial anxiety or fear of scarcity grips you, pause and recall that you entered this world with nothing and have been provided for ever since — let that history of God's provision calm your present fear.
Engage in small acts of helping others with their ordinary tasks; these moments of shared work create the relational intimacy in which meaningful wisdom is naturally exchanged.
Write down three specific ways God has provided for you in the past year that you did not anticipate or arrange yourself, and return to this list when hopelessness about your future arises.

title: Dhu Mikhmar Stands Guard: Sleep, Missed Prayer, and Tayammum on a Journey
summary: Dhu Mikhmar, an Abyssinian servant of the Prophet sent by the Negus, volunteered to stand guard over the sleeping companions during a journey but was overcome by sleep; when the group awoke to find they had missed the dawn prayer, the Prophet calmly arranged for purification and prayer, demonstrating composure and mercy in the face of an inadvertent failing.
story: Dhu Mikhmar was also one of them; he is also named as Dhu Mikhbar. He was the son of the brother of the Negus, the king of Abyssinia; others say he was his sister's son. The first possibility is correct. The king sent him to serve the Messenger of God (SAAS), as a substitute for himself. Imam Ahmad stated that Abu al-Naqr related to him, quoting Jarir, from Yazid b. Sulayt, who quoted Dhu Mikhmar, a man from Abyssinia who served the Prophet (SAAS), as having said, "We were with him on a journey. He made a rapid departure; this he would do because of the paucity of the provisions. Someone told him, 'Messenger of God, the rest have been left behind.' He told us all to stop until they caught up. He then asked them, (or someone asked him) 'Would you like to have a stop for sleep?' He dismounted, and they did the same. People asked, 'Who will stand guard for us tonight?' 'I will, my God make me your ransom!' I replied. He handed me the bridle of his camel, saying, 'Take it then, and don't be negligent.' "So I took the bridle of the camel of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and that of my own camel and led them a little way away. There I released them to graze. I was observing them when sleep overcame me. The next thing I knew was the sun's heat on my face. I woke up and looked to right and left and there were the two camels, not far away from me. I took the bridle of the camel of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and that of my own and led them over to the closest of our people. I woke him up, and asked, 'Have you performed the prayer?' 'No,' he replied. "The rest then woke one another and eventually the Messenger of God (SAAS) awoke. He asked, 'Bilal, is there water in the mayta'a, "the ablution water-vessel"?' He was referring to the iditwa, 'the water skin'. Bilal replied, 'Yes, may God make me your ransom!' He took the ablution water to him, but it did not cleanse him fully of the dirt. He then gave order to"
lessons: Human frailty — even in those who volunteer with the best intentions — is not a cause for despair but an occasion for the community to respond with patience and problem-solving rather than blame.
The Prophet's calm upon waking to find the prayer missed is a model of composed leadership; he did not react with anger but immediately turned to the practical question of how to proceed rightly.

Inadvertent failures, such as missing prayer due to sleep or exhaustion, are addressed in Islamic law with mercy; the missed prayer is made up and life continues — guilt and self-flagellation are not the prophetic response.
practical_advice: When you fail at something you eagerly volunteered to do — and the failure was not from negligence but from human limitation — resist shame spirals; acknowledge what happened, make it right where possible, and move forward.
If you lead a team or family, practice the Prophet's composure when things go wrong through no one's malice; your calm response will set the tone for how others handle future failures.
If you miss an obligatory act of worship due to sleep or genuine forgetfulness, make it up as soon as you realize it, without excessive guilt — this is the Sunnah and the mercy of God in practice.

title: Rabi'a ibn Ka'b's Night Vigil and His Request for Paradise
summary: Rabi'a ibn Ka'b al-Aslami spent his nights serving the Prophet and listening to his glorifications of God; when the Prophet offered to grant him any request, Rabi'a asked to accompany him in Paradise and was told to increase his prostrations in prayer.
story: Al-Awza'i stated that Yahya b. Abu Kathir related to him, from Rabi'a b. Ka'b, who said, "I would spend the night with the Messenger of God (SAAS), and bring him his water for ablution and whatever he needed. He would get up at one time of the night and say, 'Glory be to God, and may He be praised!' Later he would arise again, at another time of the night, and say, 'Glory be to God, Lord of the two worlds!' He asked me, 'Do you have a request?' I replied, 'To accompany you in paradise, Messenger of God.' He replied, 'Help yourself; by often prostrating in prayer.'"
lessons: The greatest aspirations a person can hold are those oriented toward the hereafter rather than the transient pleasures of this world.
Closeness to God is achieved through consistent, humble acts of worship, especially prostration which represents the highest point of nearness to the Divine.
Even the most devoted servants are guided toward self-effort; spiritual closeness cannot be gifted but must be earned through personal devotion.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually empty or distant from purpose, increase your prayers — even two voluntary rakaat can shift your inner state.
Reflect on what you would ask for if given one wish; if your answer is worldly, use it as a prompt to reconnect with your deeper spiritual values.
Incorporate remembrance of God (dhikr) into your nighttime routine as a way to anchor yourself before sleep and upon waking.

title: Rabi'a ibn Ka'b's Full-Day Service and His Request for Intercession
summary: Rabi'a ibn Ka'b served the Prophet all day and sat guard at his door at night; when offered a reward, he reflected deeply and asked the Prophet to intercede for him before God to free him from hellfire, and the Prophet told him to help himself through frequent prostration.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Ya'qub b. Ibrahim related to him, quoting his father, quoting Muhammad b. Ishaq, quoting Muhammad b. 'Aim b. 'Ali', from Nu'aym b. Muhammad, from Rabi'a b. Ka'b, who said, "I used to spend my entire day serving the Messenger of God (SAAS), until he would perform the second 'isha' prayer. Then I would sit at his door when he went into his home. I would say, 'Perhaps something might happen to the Messenger of God (SAAS).' I would hear him constantly repeating, 'Glory be to God; may He be praised!' until I became tired and returned home, or until my eyes overcame me and I fell asleep. "One day, because of his having observed my fulfilment of my duty and service to him, he said, 'Rabi'a b. Ka'b, ask me and I will grant it to you.' I replied, 'I will consider my situation, Messenger of God, then I will tell you.' "I gave the matter much thought, realizing that this life is transitory and subject to interruption, and that there was sufficient bounty in what I had and might come to me. So I told him, 'What I shall ask the Messenger of God will have to do with my life in the hereafter: he does have that (special) status with God.' "So I went to him and he asked, 'Have you done it, Rabi'a?' I replied, 'Yes, Messenger of God. I'd like to ask you to intercede for me to your Lord and to free me from hell-fire.' He asked, 'Who told you to ask for this, Rabi'a?' I replied, 'No, I swear by Him who sent you with the truth, no one ordered me. But when you said, "Ask me and I'll give you," since you have a (special) status with God, I did look into my situation, realized that this world is subject to interruption and is transitory, and that I do have bounty that will come to me, I said, "I'll make a request about my after life."' "The Messenger of God (SAAS), was silent for a long time, and he then told me, 'I will do something; help yourself, by often prostrating in prayer.'"
lessons: Reflecting carefully before making a major request shows wisdom and maturity; hasty decisions often lead to missed opportunities for what truly matters.
The greatest wealth a person can accumulate is not material but spiritual — deeds and devotion that will serve them beyond this life.
Service rendered with sincerity, without expectation of reward, is noticed and honoured — by people and by God.
practical_advice: Before making a major decision or request, take time to reflect on what you truly need versus what you merely want in the moment.
When feeling overwhelmed by the pressures of this world, remind yourself of its transitory nature and redirect your energy toward something that endures.
Add at least two voluntary prostrations to your daily routine as a practical step toward spiritual growth and inner peace.

title: Rabi'a's Marriage Arranged by the Prophet
summary: When the Prophet twice encouraged his servant Rabi'a ibn Ka'b to get married, Rabi'a agreed but had no means; the Prophet sent him to a tribe, arranged a bride for him, collected a dowry, and provided food for the wedding feast through the generosity of the community.
story: The Hafiz Abu Yaqa stated that Abu Khaythama related to him, quoting Yazid b. Harun, quoting Mubarak b. Faqala, quoting Abu 'Imran al-Jawni, from Rabi'a al-Aslami, who had been a servant of the Prophet (SAAS), who said, "One day the Prophet (SAAS) asked me, 'Rabi'a, aren't you going to get married?' I replied, 'Messenger of God, I don't want anything to divert me from serving you. And I don't have anything to give to a woman.' "After that I told myself that the Messenger of God (SAAS) knew more about me than I did myself, and that he was suggesting I get married. I decided that if he repeated the suggestion I would agree. And he did ask me, 'Rabi'a, aren't you going to get married?' I replied, 'Messenger of God, who would marry me; I don't have anything to give the woman?' He replied, 'Go to Banu so-and-so and tell them the Messenger of God orders you to marry me to your girl so-and-so.' "I went to them and said, 'The Messenger of God has sent me to you for you to arrange my marriage with so-and-so.' They asked, 'To so-and-so?' 'Yes,' I replied. They responded, 'Welcome to the Messenger of God and welcome to his messenger!' And they did arrange the marriage. "I returned to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and said, 'Messenger of God, I come to you from the very best of peoples. They gave me their trust and arranged my marriage. But where do I get the dowry to give?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) said to Burayda al-Aslami, 'Collect for Rabi'a a dowry worth the weight of one nawat of gold.' "They collected and gave this to me, I took it to Banu so-and-so and they accepted it. I then returned to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and asked him where I would get the food for a wedding feast. He told Burayda, 'Get for Rabi'a the price of a ram.' They brought it and he then told me, 'Off you go to 'Aisha and ask her to give you what barley she has.' I went to her and she gave it to me. So I took to Banu so-and-so the ram and the barley and they told me, 'We'll look after the barley for you, but ask your friends to slaughter the ram.' And so they cooked the barley and, I swear to God, we thus had bread and meat."
lessons: A wise mentor sees potential in those they guide and encourages them toward fulfilling human needs like companionship, even when the person themselves feels unworthy.
Community generosity, when mobilised with a good intention, can remove barriers that seem insurmountable to the individual.
Trusting the counsel of someone wiser than yourself, even when it challenges your current comfort zone, can open doors you never imagined.
practical_advice: If you feel unworthy of love or companionship due to material circumstances, remember that a supportive community and trust in God can provide what you cannot provide for yourself.
When a trusted mentor or friend repeatedly encourages you toward a positive change, take it seriously — they may see something in you that you cannot yet see in yourself.
Do not let financial anxiety paralyse you from pursuing meaningful life milestones; take the first step and seek community support for the rest.

title: Rabi'a, Abu Bakr, and the Dispute Over a Date-Palm
summary: After a dispute with Abu Bakr over land, in which Abu Bakr said something hurtful and then asked Rabi'a to respond in kind as recompense, Rabi'a refused; the Prophet resolved the matter by instructing Rabi'a to instead say words of forgiveness toward Abu Bakr.
story: "After that the Messenger of God (SAAS) assigned to Abu Bakr some land he had and Abu Bakr and I fell into a dispute over a laden date-palm. I maintained that it was on my land, he that it was on his. We quarrelled and Abu Bakr used a word to me that offended me. He regretted it and came to me, asking, 'You say to me as I did to you.' I replied, 'No, by God, I'll not speak to you as you did to me!' 'Well then,' he said, 'I'm going to the Messenger of God.' "He did go, and I followed him. My people came along as well, saying, 'He's the one who used the word to you, yet he's going off to complain to the Messenger of God (SAAS)!' I turned to them and asked, 'Do you know who this man is? This is al-Siddiq, "the trusting", the dhu shayba of the Muslims! Go back so that he does not turn and see you and think that you are coming to help me against him. Otherwise he might get angry and go and tell the Messenger of God, and then Rabi'a will be finished!' "When he reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), he said, 'I used against Rabi'a a word he found offensive. I told him to say it back to me, but he refused.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked me, 'Rabi'a, what's the problem between you and "the trusting"?' I replied, 'Messenger of God, I swear I'll not speak to him as he did to me.' He told me, 'Don't speak to him as he did to you. Say instead, "May God forgive you, Abu Bakr."'"
lessons: Genuine nobility of character is shown not in retaliation but in the willingness to break a cycle of harm with words of forgiveness.
Recognising the rank and virtue of others, even when you have been wronged by them, reflects spiritual maturity and prevents escalation.
Resolving conflict through a higher authority — with honesty and without mobilising partisans — is the path of integrity and results in lasting peace.
practical_advice: When someone hurts you and then asks you to hurt them back as 'fairness', consider whether responding with forgiveness might heal the relationship far more effectively than retaliation.
In a conflict, resist the urge to gather support or build a 'team' against the other person — this escalates harm and rarely leads to resolution.
Practice responding to hurtful words with a prayer for the other person's wellbeing; this shifts your own inner state from anger to compassion.

title: Sa'd the Freed-Man of Abu Bakr: Liberation at the Prophet's Request
summary: The Prophet was so impressed by Sa'd's qualities as a servant that he asked Abu Bakr to free him, reassuring Abu Bakr that other servants would come to him; Abu Bakr complied and freed Sa'd.
story: Abu Da'ud al-Tayalisi stated that Abu 'Amir related to him, from al-Hasan, from Sa'd, the freed-man of Abu Bakr, who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) told Abu Bakr to set Sa'd free — Sa'd being then a mamluk, a "slave", owned by Abu Bakr. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had been highly impressed by Sa'd's qualities as a servant. Abu Bakr replied, "He is the only servant we have here." The Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, "Free Sa'd; (other) men will come to you. (Other) men will come to you."
lessons: The Prophet recognised human dignity and worth beyond social status, and actively worked to elevate those who were oppressed or bound.
Trust in God's provision means that acts of generosity are not losses — what is given away in service of justice is replaced.
A person's inner qualities and character are their true worth, transcending the circumstances of their social position.
practical_advice: When you are in a position to advocate for someone who cannot advocate for themselves, do not let fear of personal loss hold you back.
Trust that generosity — of resources, time, or opportunity — opens doors rather than closing them.
Reflect on whether any of your current relationships or systems keep others in a diminished position that you have the power to change.

title: Sa'd and the Dates: The Prophet's Instruction on Eating Etiquette
summary: When Sa'd placed dates before the Prophet and companions began eating them two at a time, the Prophet forbade this practice, teaching mindfulness and consideration in communal eating.
story: Abu Da'ud al-Tayalisi also stated that Abu 'Amir related to him from al-Hasan, from Sa'd, who said, "I placed some dates before the Messenger of God (SAAS), and people began eating them two at a time. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then forbad them from doing this."
lessons: Mindfulness extends even to small daily acts like eating — how we consume reflects our awareness of others' needs and our gratitude for what we have.
Social courtesy and moderation are not trivial matters but are part of a comprehensive ethical and spiritual way of life.
A gentle correction from someone of wisdom, without shaming, is a gift that refines character over time.
practical_advice: Practice mindful eating — take what you need without excess, especially in communal settings where others share the same resource.
When you notice someone correcting a small habit of yours, receive it with openness rather than defensiveness; small refinements build great character.
Introduce one small daily mindfulness practice around food or consumption as a way to cultivate gratitude and awareness.

title: 'Abd Allah ibn Rawaha Leads the Prophet's Camel into Mecca with Poetry
summary: During the 'Umrat al-Qada, 'Abd Allah ibn Rawaha led the Prophet's camel into Mecca reciting bold verses declaring that the Muslims had fought for their faith and would fight again, before he was martyred months later at the Battle of Mu'ta.
story: They also include 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha. On the day of the 'umrat al-qada' he entered Mecca leading the camel of the Messenger of God (SAAS), reciting: "Unbelievers, clear out of his path; today we will fight you about its interpretation As we fought you over its revelation..." We have narrated this at length above. 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha was killed some months thereafter at the battle of Mu'ta, as also narrated above.
lessons: Courage in declaring one's faith publicly, even in the heart of hostile territory, is a mark of conviction that transcends fear.
The lives of those who serve a noble cause with total commitment are often short but luminous — their legacy outlives their years.
Every act of bold faithfulness, whether in word or deed, leaves an imprint on history that inspires those who come after.
practical_advice: When facing a situation that requires you to stand publicly for your values, draw on the example of those who did so before you at far greater personal risk.
Reflect on the legacy you wish to leave — not in terms of longevity but in terms of the integrity and courage with which you lived.
Find a form of expression — poetry, writing, art, speech — through which you can articulate your deepest values and share them with others.

title: 'Abd Allah ibn Mas'ud: The Companion Whose Thin Legs Outweigh Mount Uhud
summary: Ibn Mas'ud was a frail companion who served the Prophet closely, was mocked for his thin legs, yet the Prophet declared they would weigh more on the scales of judgment than Mount Uhud, and 'Umar called him a storehouse of knowledge.
story: 'Abd Allah b. Mas'ud b. Ghafil b. Habib b. Shamakh, Abu 'Abd al-Rahman al-Hudhali was also one of them. He was one of the imams of the Companions. He participated in both of the Hijras and took part in Badr and engagements thereafter. He was in charge of the footwear of the Prophet (SAAS), and of cleaning it. He would also load his baggage camel if he wished to ride. He had a major role in interpreting God's words; he had great knowledge, virtue and wisdom. There is a Hadith to the effect that the Messenger of God (SAAS) told his Companions, after they had expressed surprise at the thinness of his legs, "By Him who holds my soul in His hands, they weigh more heavily in the scales than does Uhud!" 'Umar b. al-Khattab stated about Ibn Mas'ud, "He was a storehouse filled with learning." They say he was frail in form, fine in character. It is said that when he walked, he would be face to face with those seated (in stature, that is). He was like the Prophet (SAAS) in his manner, conduct and behaviour. This means that he was like the Messenger of God (SAAS) in his movements and when at rest, as well as in his speech. He imitated his performance of the prayer to the best of his ability. He died during the rule of 'Uthman, in 32 or 33 AH in Medina, at the age of 63.
lessons: Physical appearance and worldly stature are entirely irrelevant measures of a person's worth before God — it is the weight of deeds and devotion that matters.
Consistent emulation of a righteous model — in speech, movement, and worship — is one of the most powerful paths to spiritual development.
Knowledge and virtue, accumulated through a life of service, become a legacy that continues to benefit communities long after a person has gone.
practical_advice: If you have ever felt dismissed or underestimated because of how you look or your apparent limitations, hold fast to the understanding that God measures by the heart and by deeds, not by appearance.
Identify a righteous model in your own life — a scholar, parent, or mentor — and intentionally study and emulate their character and habits.
Invest in building knowledge and inner character; these are the qualities that outlast the body and continue to serve others even after you are gone.

title: 'Uqba ibn 'Amir Leads the Prophet's Camel on a Mountain Trail
summary: While leading the Prophet along a mountain trail, 'Uqba was invited to ride the Prophet's camel; not wishing to disobey, he rode while the Prophet walked, demonstrating the Prophet's humility and care for his companions.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that al-Walid b. Muslim related to him, quoting Ibn Jabir, from al-Qasim Abu 'Abd al-Rahman, from 'Uqba b. 'Amir, who said, "I was leading the Messenger of God (SAAS) along a mountain trail when he asked me, ''Uqba, won't you ride?' I was not to be disobedient. The Messenger of God (SAAS) dismounted and I rode for a while."
lessons: True leadership is expressed in humility — the Prophet dismounted so that his companion could rest, placing the comfort of others above his own dignity.
Obedience born of love and respect, rather than fear, produces a relationship of mutual care and trust between leaders and those who follow them.
Small acts of consideration — offering your seat, your comfort, your convenience — are among the most powerful ways to express genuine care for others.
practical_advice: Look for one opportunity today to offer someone else a comfort or convenience that costs you something — this is the essence of the prophetic character.
Reflect on whether the authority or influence you hold is used to serve others or to elevate yourself, and make one concrete adjustment.
When someone in a position of leadership shows genuine humility toward you, recognise it as a gift and let it inspire you to do the same for those beneath you.

title: Uqba b. Amir and the Two Protective Suras
summary: The Prophet taught Uqba b. Amir the two protective suras (al-Falaq and al-Nas) while traveling on a mountain trail, instructing him to recite them every morning and evening.
story: Imam Aḥmad stated that al-Walid b. Muslim related to him, quoting Ibn Jabir, from al-Qāsim Abu 'Abd al-Raḥmān, from 'Uqba b. 'Āmir, who said, "I was leading the Messenger of God (SAAS) along a mountain trail when he asked me, ''Uqba, won't you ride?' I was not to be disobedient. The Messenger of God (SAAS) dismounted and I rode for a while. Then he rode again and said, ''Uqba, would you like me to teach you how to repeat the two finest sūras people ever recited?' I replied, 'Yes indeed, Messenger of God.' And so he recited to me, 'Say: "I seek refuge with the Lord of the dawn"' (sūrat al-Falaq; CXIII,v.I), and 'Say: "I seek refuge with the Lord of mankind"' (sūrat al-Nās; CXIV, v.l). "The prayer was held and the Messenger of God (SAAS) came forward and recited both sūras. He then passed by me and said, 'Recite them both whenever you go to sleep and whenever you get up.'"
lessons: Spiritual protection is a gift to be treasured and practiced daily.
The Prophet's humility was manifest in his willingness to dismount and let others ride.
Learning sacred knowledge directly from a teacher creates a lasting personal connection to that knowledge.
practical_advice: Recite al-Falaq and al-Nas each morning upon waking and each night before sleeping as a daily spiritual protection habit.
When a mentor or guide offers to share wisdom with you, receive it with full presence and gratitude.
Cultivate humility in leadership by being willing to share burdens and discomforts with those under your care.

title: Qays b. Sa'd: The Noble Police Chief of the Prophet
summary: Qays b. Sa'd b. Ubada served the Prophet in a role comparable to a chief of police to a ruler, and was renowned for his extraordinary height, noble character, and sound judgment.
story: Al-Bukhari quoted Anas as having said that Qāys b. Sa'd b. ʿUbada had the same role with the Prophet (SAAS), as does the Fāḥib al-skurāṯ, "the police chief", to the amīr, "the ruler". This Qāys was an exceptionally tall man. His beard was scarce. It is said that the tallest of men if standing with his feet on the ground would have Qāys's trousers reach up to his nose! Mu'awiya dispatched his trousers to the emperor of Byzantium asking whether they had a man tall enough to wear them. The emperor was amazed at this. It is related that he was a praiseworthy and noble man of sound judgement and intelligence. He was with 'Ali b. Abu Tālib at the battle of Siffin. Mis'ar stated, quoting Ma'bad b. Khalid, that he was always holding up his finger giving praise and prayer to God. May God be pleased with him and give him pleasure. Al-Waqidi, Khalifa b. Khayyat and others said that he died at Medina in the last days of Mu'awiya.
lessons: True nobility is measured by character and devotion to God, not by physical attributes alone.
Constant remembrance of God, even in small gestures, reflects a heart deeply connected to the Divine.
Faithfully serving those who do good is itself a form of worship and contribution to the community.
practical_advice: Find a way to incorporate remembrance of God into small physical habits throughout your day, just as Qays perpetually raised his finger in praise.
Identify a role of faithful service in your community and commit to it with consistency and sincerity.
Let your character and judgment be your lasting legacy rather than seeking recognition for external attributes.

title: Al-Miqdad Drinks the Prophet's Milk Portion
summary: Al-Miqdad succumbed to temptation and drank the Prophet's share of milk while he was away, then found the goats miraculously full of milk again, leading to a touching exchange about divine blessing.
story: Imam Aḥmad stated that 'Affān related to him, quoting Ḥammād b. Salama, from Thābit, from 'Abd al-Raḥmān b. Abu Layla, from al-Miqdād b. al-Aswad, who said, "I and two companions came to Medina. We made our presence known to people, but no one offered us accommodation. So we went to the Prophet (SAAS), and told him. He took us to his home, where he had four goats. He told me, 'Milk them, Miqdād. Divide up their milk in four portions and give one share to each person.' And that is what I would do. "One night the Prophet (SAAS) was invited elsewhere and was delayed. As I lay in my bed, my inner self told me, 'The Prophet has gone to the home of one of the aṣāṯī. You might just as well get up and consume the drink.' The thought persisted until I got up and drank his portion. When it had entered my stomach and settled there, I felt troubled about what had happened. I told myself, 'The Prophet is now going to arrive hungry and thirsty and he'll see nothing in the jug!' So I pulled a cloth up over my face. "When the Prophet (SAAS) arrived, he voiced a greeting that someone awake would hear but would not awaken someone asleep. He uncovered the jug and found nothing. He then raised his head to the sky and said, 'O God, provide drink for those who give me to drink, and feed those who feed me.' "I responded to his call, got up, took a sharp knife and went over to the goats. I felt them all to see which one was the one with the most flesh to slaughter. My hand fell upon the udder of one of them and, to my surprise, it was full! I examined the next and it too was full and so too were the others! I drew milk into a jug and took it to him, asking him to drink it. He said, 'What's going on, Miqdād?' 'Drink up', I told him, 'then I'll tell you.' 'One of your pranks, Miqdād?' he commented. He drank and then said, 'Now you, drink.' I replied, 'No, you drink more, Prophet of God.' He did so until he had his fill and then I took and drank it. After that I told him what had happened and he told me to tell him more. I told him in greater detail and the Prophet (SAAS) then said, 'This was a blessing sent down from heaven! Could you not have told me so that I could have given some to your two companions?' I replied, 'So long as you and I have drunk this blessing, I'm not concerned about whom it has missed!'" Imam Aḥmad also narrated it from Abu al-Naḍr, from Sulaymān b. al-Mughīra, from Thābit, from 'Abd al-Raḥmān b. Abu Layla who quoted al-Miqdād. His account is as above, except that in that version al-Miqdād drew the milk into the vessel they never expected to fill with it, and that he kept on drawing it until it foamed on top. After he brought it back, the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked him (according to that account) "Didn't you all have your drink tonight, Miqdād?" (The account proceeds) "I replied, 'Do drink it, Messenger of God.' He did so and then handed it to me. I asked, 'Drink some more, Messenger of God.' He then again handed it to me, and I took it and drank what remained. "When I knew that the Messenger of God, had quenched his thirst, and that his prayer had touched upon myself, I laughed so hard I rolled on the ground. The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'One of your pranks, Miqdād?' "I replied, 'Messenger of God, this is what I did. I did so-and-so.' He then said, 'This was nothing but a mercy from God. Should you not have warned me to wake up your two companions to share in it?' I replied, 'By Him who sent you with the truth, I don't care whoever else were to receive it so long as you and I did!'"
lessons: Honest confession of wrongdoing, even when difficult, opens the door to divine mercy and forgiveness.
God's blessings can arrive through unexpected channels precisely when we feel we have failed.
The Prophet's response to human weakness was compassion and gentle humor, not harsh condemnation.
practical_advice: When guilt over a mistake weighs on you, resist the urge to hide it; honest acknowledgment is the first step toward relief and healing.
Remember that moments of weakness do not disqualify you from receiving God's mercy — turn back sincerely and watch for the unexpected blessings that follow.
Cultivate a spirit of generosity by thinking of others when blessings arrive, even when your own needs feel most pressing.

title: Muhajir, Freed-Woman of Umm Salama, Serves the Prophet
summary: Muhajir, the freed-woman of Umm Salama, served the Prophet for many years and testified that he never once questioned or criticized her actions in service.
story: Al-Tabrāni stated that Abū al-Zinbā' Rawḥ b. al-Faraj related to him, quoting Yaḥyā b. 'Abd Allāh b. Bukayr, quoting Ibrāhīm b. 'Abd Allāh, who stated that he heard Bukayr say, "I heard Muhājir, the freed-woman of Umm Salma, say, 'I served the Messenger of God (SAAS) for many years. He never asked me why I had done something I had done, nor did he ask me why I had ever not done something.'" In other accounts the wording is that she had served him for ten or for five years.
lessons: True leadership creates an environment where people feel trusted rather than constantly monitored or criticized.
Extending grace and trust to those who serve alongside you is a powerful act of kindness that leaves a lasting impression.
A person of high station who withholds unnecessary criticism demonstrates profound emotional intelligence and mercy.
practical_advice: Practice restraint before criticizing others for how they carry out their responsibilities — ask yourself whether the correction is truly necessary.
When you are in a position of authority, make a conscious effort to express trust in those who serve or work with you.
Reflect on how the Prophet's gracious treatment of servants challenges you to treat those around you with greater patience and generosity.

title: Abu al-Samh Screens the Prophet and the Lesson of Infant Urine
summary: Abu al-Samh, a personal servant of the Prophet, described how he would screen the Prophet during washing, and recalled the Prophet's teaching about the difference in purification needed after a boy versus a girl urinates.
story: Abū al-'Abbās Muḥammad b. Isḥāq al-Thaqafi stated that Mujāhid b. Mūsā related to him, quoting 'Abd al-Raḥmān b. Mahdi, quoting Yaḥyā b. al-Wahd, quoting Muḥill b. Khalīfa, quoting Abū al-Samḥ, who said, "I was a servant of the Messenger of God (SAAS). If he wanted to wash, he would say, 'Pass me my water vessel.' And I would hand it to him and screen him. Once Ḥasan or Ḥusayn had been brought to him and had urinated over his chest. When I came over to wash him, he said, 'Someone upon whom a girl has urinated should wash (the area), while someone upon whom a boy has urinated should merely splash thereon.'" Abū Dā'ud, al-Nasā'ī and Ibn Māja narrated it thus from Mujāhid b. Mūsā.
lessons: The Prophet's calm and measured response to small inconveniences reveals a character free from irritability or ego.
Practical religious guidance can arise from everyday domestic moments, showing that sacred learning is embedded in ordinary life.
Dignity in service — whether giving or receiving — reflects the Prophet's respect for all people around him, regardless of their role.
practical_advice: When faced with minor inconveniences or messes in daily life, respond with calm composure rather than frustration.
Look for moments in your everyday routine where wisdom and mindfulness can be applied, turning ordinary tasks into opportunities for reflection.
Serve others with attentiveness and discretion, as Abu al-Samh did, understanding that faithful service in small things builds character.

title: Abu Bakr's Devoted Companionship in the Migration
summary: Abu Bakr served the Prophet personally and exclusively during the migration, especially in the cave, remaining at his side until they safely reached Medina.
story: They also included that man who was absolutely the finest of the Companions, Abu Bakr, "the trusting", may God be pleased with him. He served him by himself on his journey into exile, especially in the cave, but after his leaving it as well, until they arrived in Medina. This has all been explained above; and to God be all praise and credit.
lessons: Loyalty in times of danger is the truest measure of friendship and devotion.
Willingness to accompany a righteous person through hardship rather than stay behind for personal safety reflects the highest form of courage.
God honors those who stand faithfully beside His messengers and servants in moments of trial.
practical_advice: Think of someone in your life who is going through a difficult migration of any kind — a new city, a new chapter, a loss — and commit to accompanying them faithfully rather than offering only distant support.
When fear is present, let your love and commitment to what is right override the instinct to retreat to safety alone.
Reflect daily on the blessing of a true companion and express gratitude for those who have stood by you in your own difficult passages.

title: Aban b. Sa'id Accepts Islam After Meeting a Monk in Syria
