summary: Aban b. Sa'id, while on a business trip to Syria, met a monk who described the Prophet Muhammad precisely. This encounter led to his acceptance of Islam upon his return to Mecca.
story: Aban b. Sa'id b. al-'As b. Umayya b. 'Abd Shams b. 'Abd Manafb. Qusay al-Umawi. Aban accepted Islam after his two brothers Khalid and 'Amr. He did so following al-Hudaybiyya. This came about because it was he who gave protection to 'Uthman when the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent the latter to meet with the Meccans at al-Hudaybiyya. Others say that his conversion occurred at Khaybar; this is believed because of a hadith mentioning him that is recorded in the sahih collections from Abu Hurayra concerning the division of the booty taken at Khaybar. The reason for his accepting Islam was that, while on business in Syria, he met with a monk to whom he mentioned the activities of the Messenger of God (SAAS). The monk asked him to name him and he told him his name was Muhammad. The monk then offered to describe him, and did so exactly. He asked Aban, 'When you return home to your people, give him my greetings.' And so he accepted Islam after his return.
lessons: Truth has a way of reaching the sincere heart through unexpected means and encounters.
A single sincere encounter or conversation can be the turning point that redirects one's entire life.
God places guides along the paths of those who are genuinely searching, even if they do not yet know what they seek.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually lost or uncertain, remain open to unexpected sources of guidance — a conversation, a book, or a stranger may carry the answer you need.
Reflect on pivotal moments in your own journey where an encounter or piece of information changed your perspective, and draw strength from how God guided you then.
If you are in a period of searching or confusion about faith, actively expose yourself to sincere people and meaningful conversations rather than waiting passively.

title: Ubayy b. Ka'b Is Chosen by God to Receive the Qur'an's Recitation from the Prophet
summary: The Prophet Muhammad told Ubayy b. Ka'b that God had specifically commanded him to recite the Qur'an to Ubayy by name, causing Ubayy to weep with gratitude and awe.
story: In both of these collections a hadith is given from Anas stating that Ubayy quoted the Messenger of God (SAAS), as having said, 'God ordered me to recite the Qur'an to you.' Ubayy asked, 'Did he actually name me, Messenger of God?' 'Yes,' he replied. And Ubayy's eyes welled with tears. What is meant here by 'to recite the Qur'an to you' is a recitation to inform and to make heard, not a recitation from which learning comes.
lessons: God honors those who dedicate themselves sincerely to His word, sometimes in ways that are deeply personal and unexpected.
Tears of gratitude and awe in the presence of divine favor are a sign of a spiritually alive heart.
Being chosen or recognized for one's sincere effort is a reminder that God sees every act of devotion, no matter how quiet.
practical_advice: When you feel overlooked or unappreciated in your spiritual efforts, remember that God acknowledges every sincere deed, even if no human witness is present.
Practice moments of quiet gratitude — reflect on times when you felt God's recognition in your life, however small, and let those memories renew your motivation.
Draw near to the Qur'an with a personal, intimate relationship, not merely as an academic exercise, for it is a direct communication from God to the sincere believer.

title: Ubayy b. Ka'b's Doubt Dissolved After the Prophet Strikes His Chest
summary: When Ubayy b. Ka'b heard two different valid recitations of the same Qur'anic verse, he was seized by doubt and fear. The Prophet struck his chest and Ubayy was immediately filled with awe and spiritual clarity.
story: That related to Ubayy b. Ka'b having berated another man for having recited the sura in a manner different from Ubayy's own recitation. Ubayy complained about him to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who said, 'Recite then, Ubayy.' When Ubayy had done so, he said, 'That is how it was revealed.' He then said to the other man, 'Now you recite.' He did so and the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'That is how it was revealed.' Ubayy narrated, 'At this doubt seized me, that I might still be in the jahiliyya! The Messenger of God (SAAS) struck me on the chest and I broke out in sweat, feeling that I was looking differently at God.' After that the Messenger of God (SAAS) recited to him this chapter to establish and explain to him that this Qur'an was truth and veracity, and that it was revealed in many recitations in mercy and kindness to the worshippers.
lessons: Doubt, when brought honestly to a teacher or mentor, can become the very doorway to deeper certainty and understanding.
God's mercy is expressed in diversity and accommodation — the Qur'an's multiple valid recitations were a mercy for the community, not a source of division.
Even the most knowledgeable and devoted believers may experience moments of doubt; what matters is how they respond and where they turn.
practical_advice: When spiritual doubt arises, do not suppress it or feel ashamed — bring it into the open through prayer, study, or honest conversation with a trusted scholar or mentor.
Recognize that diversity in valid religious practice need not be a source of anxiety; learn to distinguish between differences of mercy and genuine deviation.
If you experience a moment of spiritual confusion, return to foundational texts and trusted teachers rather than letting doubt spiral in isolation.

title: Arqam b. Abu Arqam Opens His Home as the First Gathering Place for Muslims
summary: Arqam b. Abu Arqam, one of the earliest converts to Islam, offered his home near al-Safa as a secret refuge where the Prophet and the early Muslims could gather, making it a pivotal sanctuary in the earliest days of Islam.
story: Arqam b. Abu Arqam was also one of them. His name was 'Abd Manaf b. Asad b. Jundab b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar b. Makhzum al-Makhzumi. He accepted Islam early. It was he in whose home at al-Safa the Messenger of God (SAAS) remained hidden. That house later became known as al-khayzuran, 'the tiller of the rudder'. He participated in the Hijra, at the battle of Badr and thereafter. The Messenger of God (SAAS) established a tie of brotherhood between him and 'Abd Allah b. Anis. It was he who wrote out the tenancy document for land at Fakh for 'Asim b. al-Harith al-Muharibi, and other documents, as ordered by the Messenger of God (SAAS).
lessons: Sometimes the greatest contribution one can make to a noble cause is simply to open one's door — safety and sanctuary are forms of worship.
Those who quietly support a righteous mission in its most vulnerable early stages are among its most indispensable pillars.
Practical service — offering a home, writing a document, accompanying in difficulty — is as spiritually significant as any act of worship.
practical_advice: Consider what resources you already possess — a home, a skill, a position — that could be placed in service of something greater than yourself.
When a worthy cause is in its fragile early stages, be among those who offer concrete support rather than waiting for it to become established and easy to join.
Small, consistent acts of service, like Arqam's open door, often leave legacies far outlasting the person who performed them — let this motivate you to act now rather than later.

title: Thabit b. Qays Praised by the Prophet and Martyred at al-Yamama
summary: The Prophet Muhammad publicly praised Thabit b. Qays among a select group of companions, guaranteeing his place in paradise. Thabit later died as a martyr at the battle of al-Yamama.
story: Al-Tirmidhi narrated in his hadith collection, with a line of transmission that meets the criteria of Muslim, from Abu Hurayra, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'What an excellent man is Abu Bakr! What an excellent man is 'Umar! What an excellent man is Abu 'Ubayda b. al-Jarrah! What an excellent man is Usayd b. Huqayr! What an excellent man is Thabit b. Qays b. Shammas! What an excellent man is Mu'adh b. 'Amr b. al-Jamuh.' Thabit b. Qays, may God be pleased with him, was martyred at the battle of al-Yamama, in 12 AH, during the rule of Abu Bakr.
lessons: Public recognition from a person of integrity and wisdom is among the greatest honors one can receive, yet it is only a reflection of what God already knows of a person's worth.
A life of sincere service, even if it ends in sacrifice, is a life fulfilled — Thabit's martyrdom was the seal of a life already praised.
The true measure of excellence is not fame or worldly success but the quality of one's character and commitment as recognized by those closest to God.
practical_advice: When you feel your efforts go unnoticed, take comfort in knowing that sincerity of heart is recognized by God before it is recognized by any person.
Reflect on the people in your life whom you genuinely admire for their character, and let their example set the standard for how you want to live.
Do not fear an end that comes in the course of sincere service — focus instead on the quality and intention of the life you are living right now.

title: Hanzala al-Katib Remains Aloof from Civil Strife and Is Mourned by His Wife
summary: Hanzala al-Katib, a scribe of the Prophet, chose to remain neutral during the civil conflicts after the Prophet's death. Upon his death, his wife mourned him deeply and composed verses in his memory.
story: Al-Waqidi stated, 'He wrote a document for the Prophet (SAAS).' Others said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent him to the people of al-Ta'if to make peace. He participated in Khalid's campaigns in Iraq and elsewhere. He lived on into the rule of 'Ali, but he did not participate with him in the battle of the camel and other engagements. He moved away from Kufa because of the curses 'Uthman spoke on it, and died after the period of 'Ali's rule.' Ibn al-Athir narrated in al-Ghaba that after his death, his wife expressed her grief over him, leading her neighbouring women to criticize her. She spoke the following verses on the subject: 'Da'd was surprised at a woman mourning for a venerable grey-beard! If you would ask me today what it is that pained me, I would tell you what is no lie; My eye is deathly afflicted by sorrow for Hanzala, the scribe.' Ahmad b. 'Abd Allah b. al-Raqqi stated, 'He remained aloof from civil strife until he died, after 'Ali.'
lessons: Choosing peace and withdrawal from destructive conflict, even when pressured to take sides, is a form of wisdom and moral integrity.
Grief is a natural and honorable expression of love — those who criticize others for mourning sincerely often lack the depth of feeling they disparage.
A life of quiet, principled service leaves a lasting impression on those closest to us, as evidenced by the depth of his wife's grief.
practical_advice: When surrounded by divisive conflict or pressure to take sides in disputes that may harm you spiritually or emotionally, consider the wisdom of principled withdrawal and neutrality.
Allow yourself to grieve losses fully and honestly — grief is not weakness but the evidence of love, and suppressing it does not serve your healing.
Invest deeply in close relationships, for it is often those nearest to us who carry our legacy most faithfully after we are gone.

title: Khalid b. Sa'id's Dream and Conversion to Islam
summary: Khalid b. Sa'id accepted Islam early after a dream in which he saw himself being pushed toward hell by his father while the Prophet saved him. His conversion led to severe punishment from his father, who beat him, expelled him from the home, and forbade his brothers from speaking to him.
story: Khalid b. Sa'id b. al-'A~ b. Umayya b. 'Abd Shams b. 'Abd Manaf Abu Sa'Id al-Umawi was among them. He accepted Islam early. It is said that he was the third, fourth or at most fifth after Abu Bakr. It is said that his accepting Islam was due to a dream he had. In it he was standing on the edge of hell, the extent of which only God knew. "It was as though his father was pushing him towards it, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was holding him by the hand to prevent him from falling. He narrated this vision to Abu Bakr, who told him, 'Nothing but good was wished for you; this is the Messenger of God; follow him and you will escape what you feared.'" And so he went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and accepted Islam. "When news of this reached his father, he was very angry and hit him so hard with a stick he held that he broke it over his head. He threw him out of the house, denied him food and forbad his brothers from speaking to him. And so Khalid remained with the Messenger of God (SAAS), night and day. Thereafter his brother 'Amr also accepted Islam."
lessons: A sincere dream or inner awakening can be the turning point that leads a person to truth, showing that God guides hearts in unexpected ways.
Family rejection and worldly punishment for following one's conscience is a trial that tests the depth of one's conviction and sincerity.
Staying close to righteous companionship — as Khalid stayed with the Prophet night and day — is a source of strength when all other support is removed.
practical_advice: When you feel abandoned or rejected by family for your beliefs or values, seek the company of those who remind you of God and reinforce your spiritual grounding.
If a vivid dream or moment of clarity pulls you toward a better path, take it seriously and seek counsel from someone wise and trustworthy before acting.
When someone cuts off your material support, focus on building your spiritual and communal resources — community can sustain what family has withdrawn.

title: Khalid b. Sa'id's Exile to Abyssinia, Role in the Prophet's Marriage, and Death at Ajnadayn
summary: Khalid b. Sa'id and his brother 'Amr emigrated to Abyssinia to escape persecution, later arranged the Prophet's marriage to Umm Habiba, returned to Medina via Abyssinia with Ja'far, received shares at Khaybar, and Khalid was eventually martyred at the battle of Ajnadayn during the caliphate of Abu Bakr.
story: "When the Muslims went to exile in Abyssinia, these two men accompanied them. It was he who eventually arranged the marriage between Umm Habiba and the Messenger of God (SAAS), as we have told above. Both men thereafter went on the Hijra from Abyssinia (to Medina) in the company of Ja'far. They reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), at Khaybar after his conquest of it. After consultation with the other Muslims, he gave them both shares of the spoils. Their brother Aban b. Sa'id also arrived and participated at Khaybar, as narrated above, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) assigned them various duties. "During the caliphate of Abu Bakr both men went to battle in Syria, and Khalid was killed at Ajnadayn; however, some say it was at Marj al-Saffar. God knows best."
lessons: Enduring exile and displacement for the sake of one's faith is a sacrifice that God does not leave unrewarded, as Khalid and his companions were eventually reunited with the Prophet and given honored roles.
Service to the community — such as arranging a noble marriage — is a form of worship that earns lasting honor.
Martyrdom in the path of righteousness is the ultimate seal on a life of sacrifice and dedication.
practical_advice: When you are forced to leave a place or situation that harms you, trust that the journey of migration — even if long — leads to better circumstances.
Look for practical ways to serve your community during times of hardship; small acts of facilitation and peacemaking carry enormous weight.
Honor the memory of those who sacrificed before you by continuing the work they began and living by the values they upheld.

title: Khalid b. al-Walid Accepts Islam and Becomes Commander of the Muslim Armies
summary: Khalid b. al-Walid, the celebrated military commander, accepted Islam after al-Hudaybiyya alongside 'Amr b. al-'As and 'Uthman b. Talha. He was swiftly given command of expeditions by the Prophet and later became commander-in-chief under Abu Bakr, though 'Umar later dismissed him in favor of Abu 'Ubayda.
story: They also included Khalid b. al-Walid b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar b. Makhzum Abu Sulayman al-Makhzumi. He was the commander of the victorious armies, the man in charge of those illustrious soldiers, and glorious and famous battles. He was of firm judgement, great bravery and fine conduct. He was father to Sulayman b. Khalid b. al-Walid. It is said that he was never a participant in an army that was defeated, either before or after Islam. Al-Zubayr b. Bakkar stated, "He had the status of a chief among Quraysh and was in command of the cavalry." He, along with 'Amr b. al-'As and 'Uthman b. Talha b. Abu Talha, accepted Islam after al-Hudaybiyya and before Khaybar. The Messenger of God (SAAS) soon gave him charge of expeditions and then, during the rule of Abu Bakr, he became commander-in-chief of all the Muslim forces. When 'Umar b. al-Khattab succeeded to power, he dismissed him and appointed Abu 'Ubayda as amin al-umma, "the guardian of the community", with orders that he not disregard the views of (Khalid b. al-Walid) Abu Sulayman. Khalid died during the reign of 'Umar, it is said in 21 AH or 22 AH, the former date being correct, in a village a mile distant from Hims.
lessons: It is never too late to turn toward righteousness; Khalid spent years opposing Islam before becoming one of its greatest champions, showing that transformation is always possible.
True ability and sincere commitment, when aligned with a righteous cause, are recognized and utilized for the greater good.
Positions of leadership are a trust, not a right — even the greatest commander can be reassigned, and accepting that with humility is a mark of genuine faith.
practical_advice: If you have spent years on the wrong path, do not let your past define your future — commit sincerely to change and your skills may serve a far greater purpose than you imagined.
Offer your strengths and competencies in service of your community without clinging to titles or recognition.
When you are passed over or replaced in a role, focus on continuing to contribute wisely rather than withdrawing in bitterness.

title: Al-Zubayr b. al-'Awwam: The Disciple of the Prophet
summary: Al-Zubayr b. al-'Awwam accepted Islam at the age of 16, was the first to draw his sword in God's cause, participated in both exiles and all the battles, and was honored by the Prophet as his personal disciple. He displayed extraordinary bravery at the battle of al-Yarmuk, twice piercing the Byzantine ranks.
story: They also include al-Zubayr b. al-'Awwam b. Khuwaylid b. Asad b. 'Abd al-'Uzza b. Qusayy, Abu 'Abd Allah al-Asadi. He was one of "the ten" and also one of the ashab al-shura, "the Companions consulted" about whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) expressed his satisfaction at the time of his death. He was also regarded as a hawari, "disciple", of the Messenger of God (SAAS), along with his cousin, the son of his aunt Safiyya, daughter of Abd al-Muttalib, and was the husband of Asma', daughter of Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him. Al-Zubayr, may God be pleased with him, accepted Islam early, while he was 16 years old; some say his age was 18. He took part in both exiles and participated in all the battles. He was the first person to draw his sword in God's cause. He participated, with the greatest distinction of all, at the battle of al-Yarmuk. That day he twice pierced the ranks of the Byzantines from front to rear, emerging safely from the other side. However, he received two wounds on his back. May God be pleased with him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) called for both his parents at the battle of al-khandaq, "the trench" and said, "Every prophet has a disciple, and my disciple is al-Zubayr." Many virtues and fine deeds are attributed to him.
lessons: Youth is no barrier to sincere commitment; al-Zubayr accepted Islam at 16 and became the Prophet's own disciple, showing that the strength of one's devotion matters more than age.
Courage in the face of overwhelming opposition — such as twice piercing enemy lines alone — comes from a conviction that transcends fear of death.
Being recognized and honored by a righteous leader for one's loyalty and sacrifice is among the highest worldly rewards a person can receive.
practical_advice: Do not underestimate yourself because of your youth or inexperience; commit fully to your values and let your actions speak over time.
In moments of fear or danger, reconnect with your deepest convictions — they are the source of a courage that circumstances alone cannot provide.
Seek to earn the trust of those you respect through consistent, selfless action rather than seeking recognition directly.

title: The Death of Al-Zubayr b. al-'Awwam at Wadi al-Siba'
summary: Al-Zubayr b. al-'Awwam, one of the ten Companions promised paradise, was treacherously killed by 'Amr b. Jurmuz while sleeping at Wadi al-Siba' after he had turned away from the battle of the Camel. He died at 67 years of age and left behind one of the largest estates of any Companion.
story: He died at the battle of the camel. His death came about after he had turned around and was returning from the battle. 'Amr b. Jurmuz, Faqala b. Babis and a third man called al-Na'r, all of Banu Tamim, caught up with him at a place called Wadi al-Siba'. 'Amr b. Jurmuz came upon him while he slept and killed him. That was on a Thursday, ten days prior to the end of Jumada al-Uwla in 36 AH. At that time he was 67 years old. He died leaving a substantial inheritance. After the deduction of 2,200,000 dinars, he left a testamentary bequest of one-third. When his debts had been satisfied, and the third had been extracted, the remainder was divided among his heirs. Each of his wives - of whom there were four - received 1,200,000 dinars. The total amount of his estate to which we are referring was some 59,800,000 dinars. All this came to him by legitimate means during his life, from income he received from the al-fay' and the maghanim and through various kinds of permissible business activities. All that accrued after his payment of the zakat, "the charity payments", at the appropriate times, and numerous contacts with the recipients at the times of their need. May God be pleased with him and give him pleasure, making the gardens of paradise his residence; and indeed He has! For the Lord of both those who came first and those who came after, the Messenger of the Lord of the worlds, has testified that there will be paradise for him. And to God be all praise and credit!
lessons: Even the greatest of people can meet sudden, unexpected ends — reminding us that this world is temporary and that our ultimate standing is with God, not in the manner of our death.
A lifetime of legitimate earnings, honest dealings, and consistent charity is a legacy that outlasts a person's life and testifies to their character.
The promise of paradise given by the Prophet to al-Zubayr is a reminder that God's judgment is the one that truly matters, not the chaos or injustice of worldly events.
practical_advice: Build your wealth and reputation through honest, lawful means so that your legacy reflects your values rather than your circumstances.
Give regularly to those in need without waiting for a grand occasion — consistent small acts of generosity, like al-Zubayr's ongoing zakat, accumulate into a life of profound impact.
When you witness injustice or the tragic end of a righteous person, resist despair — trust that divine justice encompasses what human justice cannot reach.

title: Hanzala al-Katib's Hadith on the Five Prayers and Entry into Paradise
summary: Hanzala al-Katib, the scribe of the Prophet, narrated a hadith in which the Prophet promised paradise to those who faithfully observe the five daily prayers with their prostrations, kneeling, and proper ablutions, recognizing these as a duty owed to God.
story: Iman stated that 'Abd al-Samad and 'Affan both stated that Hamam related to them, that Qatada quoted Hanzala al-Katib as having said, "I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, 'Those who observe the five prayers, performing their prostrations, and the kneelings and their ablution at their proper times, and recognize that these are a duty owed to God, will enter paradise.''' Alternately, the words were "their due will be paradise".
lessons: The five daily prayers are not merely rituals but a conscious acknowledgment of one's relationship with God — performed with awareness, they are a path to the highest reward.
Consistency in worship, even in small daily acts, is the foundation of a life oriented toward God and the source of lasting spiritual peace.
Recognizing an act as a duty owed to God, rather than a burden, transforms the quality of its performance and deepens its meaning.
practical_advice: Approach each of your five daily prayers with the intention of fulfilling a duty of gratitude to God, not merely completing an obligation on a checklist.
If you struggle with consistency in prayer, focus on establishing the proper physical elements — ablution, kneeling, prostration — and let the spiritual depth grow from that physical discipline.
Remind yourself before each prayer that this moment of connection is a gift and a duty simultaneously — that dual awareness can renew your motivation each day.

title: Hanzala al-Katib's Hadith on the Companionship of Angels
summary: Hanzala al-Katib narrated that the Prophet told his Companions that if they remained in their state of spiritual presence with him at all times, the angels would greet them in every circumstance — but acknowledged that people only maintain such presence part of the time.
story: Ahmad, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi and Ibn Maja narrated from a hadith of Sa'id al-Jariri, from Abu 'Uthman al-Nahdi, who quoted Hanzala as having (quoted the Prophet (SAAS)), as having said, "If all of you remain as you are with me, the angels will greet you while you are meeting, while you are travelling and while you are in your beds; but you (only spend) part of your time with me."
lessons: Spiritual presence and mindfulness of God are not states reserved for moments of formal worship but ideals to strive for in every circumstance of life.
The acknowledgment that people maintain spiritual presence only part of the time is not a condemnation but an honest, compassionate recognition of human nature.
Striving to bring the awareness of God into everyday moments — meetings, travel, rest — gradually transforms the quality of one's entire life.
practical_advice: Practice bringing a moment of conscious remembrance of God into ordinary activities: before a meeting, at the start of a journey, or before sleep.
Do not be discouraged when your spiritual focus wavers throughout the day — the goal is to increase the moments of presence gradually, not to achieve perfection immediately.
Surround yourself with reminders — whether people, words, or environments — that help you return to spiritual awareness when daily life pulls you away from it.

title: Al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam: His Death at Wadi al-Siba' and His Legacy
summary: Al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam was killed at Wadi al-Siba' by Amr ibn Jurmuz, and when his head and sword were brought to Ali, Ali lamented his loss and condemned his killer to hellfire. The story recounts the aftermath, including the fate of his killer and tributes paid to al-Zubayr.
story: It has been recorded above that 'Arnr b. ]urmuz al-TamJmi killed him in Wadi al-Siba' while he was asleep. Alternately, it is said that he emerged in a daze from his sleep and rode away, but was confronted by Ibn Jurmuz. When al-Zubayr overcame him, Ibn Jurmuz was rescued by his two companions, FaqaIa and al-Na'r, and they all killed him. 'Amr b. Jurmuz took his head and his sword. When he brought both of these to 'All, the latter said on seeing al-Zubayr's sword, "Many is the time this sword deflected danger from the face of the Messenger of God ($AAS)." Among other comments he made, 'All said, "Announce to the killer of $afiyya's son that he's going to hell-fire!" It is said that when 'Amr b. Jurmuz heard that, he killed himself. The fact is, however, that he lived on after 'AlJ into the rule ofIbn al-Zubayr (al-Zubayr's son 'Abd Allah). The latter appointed his brother Mu~'ab to rule in Iraq on his own behalf, and 'Amr b. Jurmuz disappeared, fearing that once in authority Mu~'ab would kill him to avenge his father. Mu~'ab ordered, "Inform him that he is safe; does he really think I would kill him for 'Abd Allah's father (al-Zubayr)? Certainly not, I swear by God; they are not equal!" This decision stems from Mu~'ab's qualities of shrewdness, intelligence and leadership. AI-Zubayr narrated numerous alzadith relating to the Messenger of God ($AAS), too many to mention here.
lessons: The legacy of a righteous person outlives them — al-Zubayr's sword was remembered by Ali not for its material value but for the countless times it protected the Prophet, reminding us that our deeds define our memory.
True leadership sometimes means choosing mercy and restraint over revenge, as demonstrated by Mu'sab's decision to spare the man who killed his father.

Those who betray trust and shed the blood of the righteous carry a heavy spiritual burden — Ali's condemnation of al-Zubayr's killer reflects the gravity with which Islam regards the sanctity of a believer's life.
practical_advice: When you grieve the loss of someone dear, honor their memory by recalling the good they contributed to the world rather than focusing only on the pain of their absence.
If you are in a position of authority or power, practice the restraint of Mu'sab — choosing reconciliation over retaliation can break cycles of harm and demonstrate true strength of character.
Reflect on what legacy your daily actions are building; ask yourself whether the way you are living today will be remembered as a shield for others or a burden upon them.

title: Hassan ibn Thabit's Eulogy of Al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam
summary: The companion-poet Hassan ibn Thabit composed verses praising al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, commemorating his bravery, his close ties to the Prophet, and his lifelong dedication to Islam.
story: l:;Iassan b. Thabit spoke the following verses giving him praise and credit for that: "He grew up in the era and guidance of the Prophet, as his disciple, and speech with merit rings true. He grew up in accord with his way and his path, allying with the agent of truth, and the truth is fully just. He was that famous knight, that hero who sprang forward at every famous battle. He was a man whose mother was Safiyya, and he descended from a proud man named Asad, 'lion'. He had close family ties with the Messenger of God, and from fighting for Islam comes glory deeply secure. Many were the dangers al-Zubayr deflected with his sword from al-MU$tafii, 'the chosen one', and God rewards most generously. When war emerged, he would slice at it, his blade flashing forward, charging proudly towards death. He had no peer among them, nor was ever his like before, and his fame will never, for all time, fade."
lessons: Honoring and commemorating the righteous through words of remembrance is a noble tradition — poetry and praise keep the light of good examples alive for future generations.
A life lived in close companionship with truth and righteousness, as al-Zubayr lived in proximity to the Prophet, naturally produces a character that others feel compelled to celebrate.
Courage and dedication to a noble cause create a lasting legacy that transcends a person's physical life.
practical_advice: Take time to verbally or in writing honor the people in your life who have positively shaped you — expressions of gratitude and tribute strengthen community bonds and inspire others.
Reflect on whose footsteps you are growing up in or being shaped by, and ask whether that influence is guiding you toward truth and justice.
When someone you admire passes away, channel grief into meaningful tribute — write, speak, or act in ways that carry their good values forward.

title: The Wife of Al-Zubayr Mourns His Death
summary: After al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam was killed, his wife Atika bint Zayd composed mournful and defiant verses condemning his killer and lamenting the loss of a great warrior who never fled from battle.
story: When al-Zubayr b. al-'Awwam was killed at Wadi al-Siba', as related above, his wife 'Mika, daughter of Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl, recited the following verses: "Ibn ]urmuz deceived the anny's champion knight, that day the forces clashed; he was not one to flee! cAmr, if you had warned him, you would not have found him effeminate, his hand or heart trembling. Many were the battles he engaged, no resistance turning him aside, you utter coward! May your mother mourn you! You were never victorious over his like in any that went before or ever will be. By God, your Lord, if you kill a Muslim, the punishment of the Resolute One will alight upon you!"
lessons: Grief expressed authentically, even when tinged with anger, is a natural and human response to the loss of a loved one — Islam does not demand we suppress genuine sorrow.
The pain of losing someone through betrayal or deception carries a particular weight; allowing oneself to name that injustice is part of processing loss.
Atika's verses remind us that the memory of those who died bravely and faithfully deserves to be defended and honored.
practical_advice: If you are grieving a loss that also involved injustice, allow yourself to name both emotions — the sorrow and the anger — rather than suppressing one to appear more composed.
Find a creative or verbal outlet for deep grief, such as writing, poetry, or trusted conversation, as Atika did, rather than internalizing pain alone.
When someone you love is taken unfairly, focus not on the perpetrator but on the legacy of the one you lost — let their story, not the manner of their death, define how you remember them.

title: Zayd ibn Thabit: The Young Companion Who Mastered the Jewish Script
summary: Zayd ibn Thabit, at just eleven years old, came to the Prophet in Medina and impressed him with his knowledge of Quranic recitations. The Prophet then personally tasked him with learning the writing system of the Jews in fifteen days so he could read and respond to their correspondence on the Prophet's behalf.
story: Zayd b. Thabit b. al~Oa.l:tl:>ak b. Zayd b. Lawdhan b. 'Amr b. 'Ubayd b. 'Awfb. Ghanm b. Malik b. al-Najjar al-An~iili al-Najjari, Abu Sa'id, was among them. He was also known as Abu Kharija, and as Abu 'Abd al-Ral:>man al-Madani. He came to the Messenger of God ($AAS), in Medina when he was 11 years old. Therefore, due to his youth, he did not participate in the batde ofBadr, nor in that at Ul:>ud. The first battle in which he took part was that of al-khandaq, "the trench". He participated in all thereafter. He was a lzafif', a learned man of high intelligence. It is established about him in the $alzilz collection of al-Bukhari that the Messenger of God ($AAS) ordered him to learn the writing system of the Jews in order to be able to read to the Prophet ($AAS) whatever they might write to him. He learned this in 15 days. Imam A!).mad stated that Sulayman b. Da'ud related to him, quoting 'Abd aI-Ra!).man, from Abu aI-Zinad, from Kharija b. Zayd, who quoted his father Zayd as having said, after having come to see the Messenger of God ($AAS) at Medina, "I was taken to him, and he was impressed with me. People said, 'Messenger of God, this youth is from Banu ai-Najjar; he knows from ten to twenty or so ofthe surat that God revealed to you.' This delighted the Messenger of God ($AAS), and he told him, 'Zayd, learn for me the writing of the Jews; I swear by God, I don't trust a Jew to read to me.'" Zayd went on, "So I set about learning their writing and had mastered it before 15 days had passed. I used to read to him whatever letters they wrote to him, and answered them on his behalf." A!).mad then narrated this from Shuray!). b. aI-Nu'man, from Ibn Abu aI-Zinad, from his father, from Kharija, from his father who related it similarly. AI-Bukhar! gave it in his work al-A/tkam from Kharija b. Zayd b. Thabit in an abbreviated form. AbU Da'ud narrated it from AJ:unad b. Yunus. AI-Tirmidh! gave it from 'Ail b. l;Iajr. They both related it from 'Abd aI-Ra!).man b. Abu aI-Zinad, from his father, from Kharija, from his father. AI-Tirmidh! categorized it as /tasan $a/tf/t. This was ttuly extraordinary intelligence.
lessons: When we are given a task by someone we trust and respect, applying ourselves fully and completing it with excellence is itself an act of worship and loyalty.
Youth is no barrier to greatness — Zayd's extraordinary contributions began when he was a child, reminding us never to underestimate young people's capacity for learning and responsibility.

Learning new skills, even those outside our comfort zone or tradition, can become a means of serving a greater good — Zayd learned a foreign script specifically to protect the Muslim community's interests.
practical_advice: When you face a challenging new task, commit to it fully and set a realistic deadline for yourself as Zayd did — mastery often comes faster than we expect when we are motivated by purpose.
Identify a skill or area of knowledge that would make you more useful to your community or those you care for, and begin learning it, even if it feels unfamiliar at first.

If you feel overlooked or underestimated because of your age, background, or circumstances, let Zayd's story remind you that consistent excellence and sincere effort are what ultimately earn recognition.

title: Zayd ibn Thabit Collects the Quran at Abu Bakr's Command
summary: After the battle of Yamama, Abu Bakr entrusted Zayd ibn Thabit with the enormous task of gathering and compiling the full text of the Quran, citing his trustworthiness and his previous role as the Prophet's scribe.
story: Zayd participated in the expedition to al-Yamilma and he was struck by an arrow that did not seriously harm him. It was he whom Abu Bakr thereafter ordered to seek out and assemble the Qur'an. He told him, "You are an intelligent young man about whom we have no suspicions; and you used to write down the revelation for the Messenger of God (SAAS). Therefore, seek out and assemble the Qur'an." And Zayd did as Abu Bakr had ordered him. There was much good in that; and to God be all praise and credit.
lessons: Being known for trustworthiness and integrity means that when the most important tasks arise, you are the one called upon — character is the foundation of true leadership.
Even in the aftermath of hardship and battle, the community's spiritual and intellectual preservation must continue — protecting sacred knowledge is itself a form of jihad.
When a daunting responsibility is placed upon you by those in authority and guided by sincere intention, trusting in God and proceeding with diligence will bring about great good.
practical_advice: Invest in building a reputation for reliability and integrity in your daily life — these qualities will open doors of trust and responsibility that talent alone cannot.
If you are assigned an overwhelming task, break it into its components and begin with what you know, trusting that the task can be completed step by step, as Zayd did in assembling the Quran.

When you feel the weight of an important responsibility, remind yourself that you were chosen because others see in you the qualities needed — accept the trust placed in you with humility and resolve.

title: Zayd ibn Thabit Witnesses Quranic Revelation Being Recorded
summary: Zayd ibn Thabit recounts the precise moment he was present when revelation descended upon the Prophet, including the addition of the words 'except those who have suffered injury' to a verse about believers, which he recorded in real time.
story: Zayd wrote down the revelations in the presence, in several locations, of the Messenger of God ($AAS). One of the clearest evidences of this is in the statement from him established in the $a/tf/t collections, in which he said, "When the words of Almighty God came down, 'Those believers who stay back and those who strive hard in God's cause are not equal'" (surat al-Nisa'; IV, v.95), the Messenger of God ($AAS) called out to me and said, 'Write down, "Those believers who stay back and those who strive hard in God's cause are not equal."' "Then Ibn Umm MaktUm arrived and began complaining about his injuries. While his leg was pressing hard against my own - hard enough to almost bruise it - revelation again came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), with the words ghayru iilt al-tlarar, 'except those who have suffered injury'. He ordered me to add these words, and I did so. And Iwell know the place where it was added - at a crack in that tablet - of bone, that is."
lessons: The Quran was preserved with meticulous care and in real time — the memory of those who witnessed revelation being recorded is a profound testament to the authenticity and careful transmission of Islamic scripture.

Divine mercy is precise and responsive — the addition of the phrase 'except those who have suffered injury' shows that God's guidance accounts for the realities and vulnerabilities of every individual.
Being present and attentive in moments of significance — as Zayd was — allows one to bear witness to and preserve truth in ways that benefit generations to come.
practical_advice: Cultivate the habit of careful, accurate recording in your own life — whether journaling, documenting decisions, or keeping records — because precision in preservation protects communities and individuals alike.

When you feel that a general rule or expectation does not account for your personal hardship or limitation, remember that God's guidance explicitly makes space for the injured and the vulnerable — bring your real circumstances honestly before God in prayer.
Be fully present in important moments rather than distracted — the details Zayd remembered, down to the crack in the tablet, show that attentiveness in meaningful moments produces lasting benefit.

title: The Prophet's Praise of His Companions' Distinct Qualities
summary: The Prophet Muhammad described each of his leading companions by their most outstanding moral or scholarly quality, praising Abu Bakr for kindness, Umar for strictness in religion, Uthman for humility, Ali for decisiveness, Muadh for jurisprudence, Zayd ibn Thabit for knowledge of religious duties, and Abu Ubayda for trustworthiness.
story: A!).mad and ai-Nasa'! narrated from a /tadith of Abu QalabaJrom Anas, who quoted the Messenger of God ($AAS) as having said, "The person most kind towards my nation is Abu Bakr. The most severe in it for God's religion is 'Vrnar. The one in it most sincere in humility is 'Vthman. The most decisive of them is 'Ail b. Abu Talib. The man of them most knowledgeable of what is permissible and what forbidden is Mu'adh b. Jabal. The one most knowledgeable about the religious duties ofIslam is Zayd b. Thabit. And every nation has an amfn,1m 'a fully trustworthy man'. The amfn of this nation is Abu 'Uhayda b. al-Jarra!)." There are some /tuffaf? who consider this /tadrth mursal, "incomplete", except in so far as it refers to Abu 'Uhayda. It is given in this line of transmission in the $a/tf/t of aI-Bukhan.
lessons: Every individual has a unique excellence — the Prophet did not rank his companions against one another but celebrated each one's distinct contribution, reminding us that a healthy community needs diverse strengths.
Recognizing and naming the strengths of those around you is itself an act of prophetic character — it builds confidence, affirms purpose, and binds communities together.
Knowing your own strengths and accepting that others excel in areas where you do not is a mark of wisdom and humility, not weakness.
practical_advice: Identify your own distinctive strength — whether it is kindness, precision, decisiveness, or trust — and invest in developing it rather than trying to be excellent at everything simultaneously.
Practice recognizing and verbally acknowledging the specific strengths of people in your life; this prophetic habit builds morale and deepens relationships.

When you feel inadequate because others seem more capable in certain areas, remember the Prophet's example — different people are honored for different qualities, and your unique contribution matters.

title: Zayd ibn Thabit: Scholar, Governor, and Compiler of the Uthman Mushaf
summary: Zayd ibn Thabit served as governor of Medina on behalf of Umar and Uthman, was beloved by Ali, and played a central role in producing the authorized Uthmanic copies of the Quran that were sent across the Muslim world.
story: 'Umar twice, while making the pilgrimage, appointed Zayd to govern Medina on his behalf, as he did when he made an expedition to Syria. 'Uthman also appointed him to govern Medina. 'Ali liked him very well. Zayd extolled 'Ail and gave recognition to his worthiness, although he did not participate with him in any of his battles. He lived on after 'Ail, dying in 45 AH; that date is also given as 51 or 55 AH. He was one of those who wrote the ma$iiJ:zifal_a>imma, "the master copies", that 'Uthman b. 'Atran dispatched to all parts. It was these that gave to the reading the official stamp of consensus and approval, as we have established in our book Tafttr (Exegesis oj) al-Qur'an. And to God be all praise and credit.
lessons: A life of sincere service across multiple caliphates — from the Prophet's time through Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, and Ali — shows that genuine usefulness transcends political allegiance and endures through changing circumstances.
Contributing to the preservation of what is sacred, as Zayd did in compiling the Quran's authorized text, is among the highest forms of service a person can render to their community.
Dying well, with a legacy of scholarship, leadership, and integrity, is the fruit of a life lived in consistent devotion to God and service to others.
practical_advice: Aim to be someone whose contributions are valued across different contexts and by people of varied perspectives — versatility rooted in integrity makes you a stabilizing force in any community.

Ask yourself: what work am I doing today that will benefit people long after I am gone? Intentional contributions to lasting goods — education, preservation of knowledge, community building — are among the most meaningful investments of a lifetime.

When political or social divisions arise around you, as they did in Zayd's time between Ali's supporters and others, choose the path of principled neutrality and continued service rather than being swept into factional conflict.

title: The Hadith of al-Sijil (The Scribe): Scholarly Debate on Its Authenticity
summary: Islamic scholars including Ibn Kathir, Sheikh al-Mizzi, and Ibn Taymiyya debate the authenticity of hadiths claiming the Prophet had a scribe named al-Sijil, concluding the narrations are fabricated or very weak.
story: 'Ali, from Nul:> b. Q;lys, a source considered trustworthy and among those quoted by Muslim. Ibn Macro, however, categorizes him as a weak source in one account quoting him. Regarding his teacher Sheikh Yazid b. Ka'b al-'Awdhi al-Ba~ri, only Nul:> b. Q;lys quotes from him. Nevertheless, Ibn J:::!ibban includes him among those to be considered trustworthy sources. I brought this (ladith to the attention of my teacher the (laji" Sheikh Abu al-J:::!ajjaj al-Mizzi; he deprecated it strongly. I informed him that our very learned Sheikh Abu ai-'Abbas b. Taymiyya used to say, "This (ladith is fabricated, even if included in the sunan ofAbu Da'ud!" Our teacher Sheikh al-Mizzi commented, "I say the same!" I note that the (laji" Ibn 'Adi narrated it in his work al-Kamil from a (ladith of Mul:>ammad b. Sulayman, known as "Burna", from Yal:>ya b. 'Amr, from Malik al-Bakri, from his father, from Abu al-Jawza', from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) had a scribe named al-Sijil. He is referred to in the words of the Almighty, 'That day when we will roll up the heavens as al-Sijil rolls up writings.' Just as al-Sijil rolls up writings, thus will the heavens be rolled up." AI-Bayhaqi narrated it similarly from Abu Na~r b. Q;ltada, from Abu 'Ali al-Rafa', from 'Ali b. 'Abd al-'Aziz, from Muslim b. Ibrahim, from Yal:>ya b. 'Amr b. Malik. This Yal:tyais considered a very weak source, one upon whom one should not depend. But God knows best. Stranger still than that is what was also narrated by the (laji" Abu Bakr al-Kha1:ib and Ibn Manda from a (ladith of Al:tmad b. Sa'id ai-Baghdadi, known as "J:::!amdan", from Ibn Bahz, from 'Vbayd Allah, from Na!i', from Ibn 'Vmar, who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) had a scribe called Sijil and God revealed, 'That day when we will roll up the heavens as al-Sijil rolls up writings.'" Ibn Manda commented, "This is gharib. J:::!amdan is alone in giving it." AI-Barqani stated, "Abu al-Fatl:> al-Azdi stated, 'Ibn Numayr was alone in giving it, if authentic.'" This is also objectionable when narrated from Ibn 'Vrnar, just as it was when from Ibn 'Abbas. For a quite different statement is also attributed to both. AI-Walibi and al-'Awfj quote Ibn 'Abbas as having stated with reference to this verse, "(it means) like the rolling of a ~a(lift (sheet of paper etc) over writings". Mujahid stated the same. Ibn Jarir stated, "It is well known in the language that al-sijil means a ~a(lifa." He went on, "No one among the Companions is known by the name al-Sijil." And it is objectionable for al-Sijil to have been the name of one of the angels, as was narrated from Abu Kurayb, from Ibn Yaman, who stated that Abu ai-Wafa' al-Ashja'i related to him, from his father, from Ibn 'Vmar, who said, regarding the verse, "That day when we will roll up the heavens as al-Sijil rolls up writings", "AI-Sijil was an angel. Whenever he ascended with a request for forgiveness, God would say, 'Write it in light.'" He also stated that Bandar related to him, from Mu'ammil, from Sufyan, who said, "I heard al-Suddi say ..." And he went on to narrate it similarly. Abu Jacfar al-Baqir stated the same, narrating from Abii Kurayb, from Mubarak, from Macriif b. Kharrabiidh, from someone who heard Abii Jacfar say, "AI-Sijil is an angel." Ibn Janr's denial that al-Sijil was the name of either a Companion or an angel is very convincing, while the I;zadith to the contrary is very Objectionable. Those, like Ibn Manda, Abii Nu'aym al-Isbahani and Ibn al-Athir, in al-Ghaba, only do so to give credibility to this I;zadith, or to comment upon its authenticity. But God knows best.
lessons: Scholarly integrity requires courage to reject weak narrations even when they appear in well-known compilations — truth is not determined by popularity but by sound evidence.
The Islamic tradition built rigorous systems of verification precisely because preserving authentic knowledge is a form of worship and service to the community.
Humility before knowledge means acknowledging uncertainty with phrases like 'but God knows best' rather than forcing conclusions beyond what evidence supports.
practical_advice: When you encounter conflicting information, slow down and seek out trustworthy, knowledgeable sources rather than accepting the first account you hear.
Practice intellectual honesty by being willing to say 'I don't know' or 'I'm not certain' — this is a sign of strength, not weakness.
Build your own habit of verification: before sharing something important, ask yourself whether it is well-sourced and whether multiple reliable people confirm it.

title: 'Amir b. Fuhayra: The Freed Slave Who Died a Martyr and Was Raised to the Sky
summary: 'Amir b. Fuhayra, a freed slave of Abu Bakr who endured torture for his faith in Mecca, participated in the Hijra and major battles, and was martyred at Bi'r Ma'una — after which witnesses reported his body was miraculously raised into the sky.
story: They also included Sa'd b. Abii Sar˥ʸ, according to a statement made by Khalifa b. Khayyat. It has been imagined that this man was his son cAbd Allah b. Sa'd b. Abii Sar˥ʸ, as will be explained shortly, if God so wills it. cAmir b. Fuhayra, a freedman of Abii Bakr, was also one of them. Imam Aḥrnad stated, "cAbd al-Raṣṣaq related to me, from Macmar, who quoted al-Zuhri as having said, "'Abd ai-Malik b. Malik al-Mudliji informed me, he being the cousin of Suraqa b. Malik, that his father informed him that he heard Suraqa say ...'" And he proceeded to narrate the Hijra of the Prophet (SAAS). In this narrative, he said, "So I told him (the Prophet (SAAS», 'Your people have put a price on your head!' And I related to them news relating to their departure and what people wanted to do with them. Then 1 offered them provisions, but they would not accept anything from me and asked me to leave them alone. I asked him if he would write down a safe conduct for me, and he told 'Amir b. Fuhayra, who did so on a piece ofleather. Then he left." I note that the I;zadith is given complete above in the material relating to the Hijra. It is also recorded that it was Abii Bakr who wrote this document for Suraqa. But God knows best. Amir b. Fuhayra - known as Abii 'Amr — was of mixed blood of Banii Azd. He was black in complexion. He had earlier been a freed~man of Tufayl b. al-I;Iarith, the brother of 'A'isha through her mother Umm Ruman. He accepted Islam early, before the Messenger of God (SAAS) took refuge in the home of al-Arqam b. Abii al-Arqam at al-Safa. 'Amir was tortured along with all the others persecuted at Mecca to make them give up their faith. He refused, and Abii Bakr purchased him, then set him free. He would herd goats for him outside Mecca. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) left on the Hijra, he accompanied Abii Bakr, who bore him on his mount. They also had al-Dili, the guide, with them. This is narrated in full above. Upon their arrival in Medina, 'Amir b. Fuhayra stayed with Sa'd b. Khaythama. The Messenger of God (SAAS) established a tie of brotherhood between 'Amir and Aws b. Mu'adh. He participated in Badr and at Uḥud. He was killed at the battle of Bi'r Ma'una, as was related above. That was in 4 AB. At that time he was 40 years of age. But God knows best. 'Urwa, Ibn Isṣḥaq, al-Waqidi and other scholars state that 'Amir was killed at Bi'r Ma'una by a man named Jabbar b. Sulma, ofBanu Kilab. When 'Amir was pierced by the spear, he said, "I have succeeded, by the Lord ofthe ka'ba!" 'Amir was then raised up until he disappeared from sight, so that 'Amir b. al-Tufayl said, "He was raised up until I could see the sky and not him!" "Amr b. Umayya was asked about him and he replied, "He was one ofthe best of us, and one of the earliest members of the household of our Prophet (ṣAAS).' Jabbar stated, "I asked al-J;>aṣṣak b. Sufyan what he meant by what he said. He replied, 'He was referring to paradise.' "Al-J;>aṣṣak invited me to accept Islam, and I did so when I saw what happened at the death of 'Amir b. Fuhayra. Al-J;>aṣṣak wrote to the Messenger of God (ṣAAS), informing him of my acceptance of Islam and of what had happened to 'Amir. He commented, 'The angels concealed him and he has taken up residence with the spiritual hierarchy.'" In both ṣa{zi{ṣ collections Anas is quoted as having said, "He recited to us a Qur'an verse about them: 'Inform our people that we have met our Lord. And He is pleased with us and has pleased us.'" Muṣanunad b. Isṣaq stated that Hisham b. 'Urwa related to him, from his father, that 'Amir b. al-Tufayl used to ask, "Which of your men was it whom, when killed, was raised up between earth and sky so that you could see sky only, and not him?" "That was 'Amir b. Fuhayra," they would reply. Al-Waqidi stated that Muṣammad b. 'Abd Allah related to him, from al-Zuhri, from 'Urwa, who quoted 'A'isha as having said, "'Amir b. Fuhayra was raised up into the sky; his body was not found." They say that the angels concealed it.
lessons: True freedom is not only freedom from physical bondage but freedom of the soul — 'Amir was freed from slavery and then freely chose a life of complete devotion to God, showing that inner liberation is the greatest gift.

The highest honor belongs not to those with wealth or status but to those with sincerity of faith — 'Amir, a freed slave of humble origins, was honored by God in a miraculous way that even his enemies witnessed.
When you face suffering for doing what is right, remember that God sees your endurance and that your story, like 'Amir's, may inspire others toward goodness long after you are gone.
practical_advice: When you feel overlooked or underestimated because of your background or circumstances, remind yourself that God's measure of value is your sincerity and steadfastness, not your social standing.
In moments of intense hardship, try to hold onto the conviction — as 'Amir did at the moment of death — that your struggle is meaningful and that God is pleased with your effort.
Let the example of those who sacrificed greatly for their values inspire you to hold firm to your own principles, even under social pressure to abandon them.

title: 'Abdullah b. al-Arqam: The Trusted Scribe Who Refused a Salary for God's Work
summary: 'Abdullah b. al-Arqam served as one of the Prophet's most trusted scribes, writing diplomatic letters to kings; he later served under Abu Bakr and Umar but refused a large salary from Uthman, declaring that his service was purely for God's reward.
story: 'Abd Allah b. Arqam b. Abu Arqam al-MakhZilmi was also among them. He accepted Islam the year of the conquest of Mecca and he wrote on behalf of the Prophet (ṣAAS). Imam Malik stated, "He (the Prophet (ṣAAS» would send out what he did and would thank him and express his appreciation." Salama stated, quoting Muṣammad b. Isṣaq b. Yasar, from Muṣammad b. Ja'far b. al-Zubayr, from 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr, that the Messenger of God (ṣAAS) asked 'Abd Allah b. al-Arqam b. 'Abd YaghUth to write letters for him in response to the (letters) of the kings. His trust in 'Abd Allah b. al-Arqam so developed that he would tell him to write to certain kings; he would write and the Messenger of God (SAAS) would put his seal on what he read (back) because of his trust in him. He acted as scribe for Abu Bakr, who gave him charge of the treasury. 'Umar b. al-Khattab confmned him in both positions. When 'Uthman succeeded, he dismissed him from them. I note that this was after 'Abd Allah b. Arqam had tendered his resignation. It is said that 'Uthman offered him 300,000 dirhams as his salary, but he refused to accept this. He replied, "What I did was for God; my reward is up to God, Almighty and Glorious is He." Ibn Isṃaq stated, "Zayd b. Thabit wrote for the Messenger of God (SAAS). If neither Ibn al-Arqam nor Zayd b. Thabit were there, then someone else present would write. "'Umar, 'Ali, Zayd, al-Mughira b. ShuCba, Mu'awiya, Khalid b. Sa'id b. al-'Aṣ and others named by the Arabs wrote for him." AI-A'mash stated, "I asked Shaqiq b. Salama, 'Who was the scribe for the Prophet (SAAS)?' He replied, "Abd Allah b. al-Arqam. 'Umar brought out at al-Qitdisiyya a document of Abu Bakr and at its base were the words, 'Written by 'Abd Allah b. al-Arqam.'" Al~Bayhaqi stated that Abu 'Abd Allah al-l;Iafi" related to him, quoting Muḥamrnad b. Siiliḥ b. Hani', quoting al-Fac!1 b. Muḥamrnad al-Bayhaqi, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Saliḥ, quoting 'Abd ai-'Aziz b. Abu Salama al-Majushun, from 'Abd al-Waḥid b. Abu 'Awn, from al~sim b. Muḥammad, who quoted 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar, as having said, "The Prophet (SAAS) brought a: letter from some man and he asked 'Abd Allah b. al-Arqam, 'Respond for me.' He wrote the reply, then read it to him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'You have done it correctly and well. May God give him success!' "When 'Umar acceded, he would seek 'Abd Allah's counsel. And 'Umar b. al-Khanab is quoted as having said, 'I never saw anyone more fearful of God than him.' He meant anyone of the workers." He was, may God be pleased with him, injured before his death.
lessons: The most enduring motivation for excellent work is not financial reward or recognition but sincere devotion to God — when that is your anchor, no loss of position or salary can diminish your sense of purpose.
Trust is built through consistent integrity over time, and those who are most trusted are often those who care least about the rewards that trust might bring them.
Stepping back from a position of power or privilege, as 'Abdullah did when he resigned, can be an act of profound spiritual courage and self-awareness rather than defeat.
practical_advice: When you feel unappreciated at work or in service to others, reconnect with your original intention — if you began out of a genuine desire to contribute, let that intention sustain you regardless of external recognition.
Consider whether the reward or compensation you are seeking from people is something you could redirect toward seeking from God instead; this shift in mindset can bring profound peace.
Practice asking yourself regularly: 'Am I doing this for God, or for what people think of me?' — and let your answer guide you toward greater sincerity in your daily actions.

title: 'Abdullah b. Zayd al-Ansari: The Man Who Received the Vision of the Adhan
summary: 'Abdullah b. Zayd al-Ansari, a companion who accepted Islam early and participated in Badr, is honored as the one who saw in a dream the words of the Islamic call to prayer (adhan) and reported it to the Prophet, who confirmed the vision as true and instructed Bilal to use it.
story: 'Abd Allah b. Zayd b. 'Abd Rabbihi al-Anṣarl al-Khazraji, the originator of the call to prayer, was one of them. He accepted Islam early and was present at the 'aqabat al-sab'in, the "'Aqaba meeting of the 70". He participated at the battle of Badr and later engagements. One of his greatest credits is his having had a vision of the call to prayer in his sleep and his having repeated this to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who responded, "These are visions of truth. Stand with Biliil and deliver them to him so that he can use them to announce the call to prayer; he has a stronger voice than you."
lessons: God can place extraordinary gifts and responsibilities in the hearts of those who seem ordinary — a companion's dream became one of the most recognized and beloved calls in human history, heard five times a day across the world.

Sharing your inner experiences and inspirations with wise, trustworthy people — as 'Abdullah did with the Prophet — allows those gifts to be refined and amplified in ways far beyond what you could achieve alone.
Your contribution to something good, even if others ultimately carry it forward more visibly, is not diminished — 'Abdullah received the vision, Bilal gave it his voice, and both are honored.
practical_advice: Don't dismiss dreams, intuitions, or moments of inspiration as meaningless — reflect on them, and when appropriate, share them with someone wise who can help you discern whether they point toward something meaningful.
If you feel called to contribute to something but feel unqualified compared to others, remember that the contribution itself matters; offer what you have and trust that it will find its right form.
When someone else is chosen to carry forward something you initiated, practice gratitude for having been part of it rather than focusing on who receives the visible credit.

title: Abdullah ibn Sa'd ibn Abi Sarh Dies in Prayer as He Had Asked
summary: Abdullah ibn Sa'd ibn Abi Sarh had prayed to God to take his life during prayer, and his supplication was answered — he died between the two salutations of the dawn prayer.
story: When 'Uthman was killed, he took up residence in 'Asqalan (Ashkelon) - or alternatively, at Ramla - and prayed to God to take him during prayer. One day he performed the dawn prayer, in the first part of which he recited thefli.til;ta and the (surat) al-Adiyat (C), and in the second part the fatil;a and a surat. When he had finished the al-tashahhud he performed the salutation of the first taslima. He was then about to perform the second tasltma, but died between the two salutations; may God be pleased with him. His death occurred in 36 AH or in 37 AH.
lessons: The supplications of the sincere believer are heard by God, even those as personal as the manner of one's death.
Aspiring to end one's life in a state of worship reflects a heart that has truly reconnected with its purpose after trials.
A beautiful ending is available to those who persist in righteousness — the journey matters, but so does the destination.
practical_advice: Cultivate the habit of making sincere du'a about your deepest hopes, including how you wish to live and meet your end — God listens.
Let this story inspire you to be in a state of worship and mindfulness regularly, so that whenever your moment comes, you are found in a good state.
When you witness the death of a righteous person, let it motivate you to recommit to your own spiritual goals rather than simply mourning.

title: The Fabricated Document of the Jews of Khaybar
summary: A group of Jews from Khaybar falsely claimed to hold a document from the Prophet exempting them from the jizya tax, forged with the names of Ali ibn Abi Talib and prominent Companions as witnesses, which scholars exposed as a complete fabrication.
story: Regarding the claim made by a group of Jews of Khaybar that they hold a document from the Prophet (SAAS), concerning their exemption from payment of the jizya tax, and its having been signed as having been written by 'AlI b. Abu Talib and witnessed by a group of Companions, including Sa'd b. Mu'adh and Mu'awiya b. Abu Sufyan - this is all a lie and a fabrication, a pure invention. A number of scholars have made clear the falseness of their claim; however, certain early jurists were deceived and recommended that they be exempt from payment of the jizya. This decision is very weak. I have gathered all material relating to this, dedicating to it a separate section in which I established its falseness and the fact that it was a fabrication. They completely invented it, as they are capable of doing! I have examined this fully and gathered all the variety of statements made about it by the imams. And all praise and credit go to God.
lessons: Scholarship and critical verification are essential religious duties — accepting claims without evidence can lead communities into error.
The integrity of the historical record must be protected; fabrications, even when convenient, ultimately unravel under careful scrutiny.
Deception for material gain is a serious moral failing, and the Islamic scholarly tradition has always worked diligently to expose and correct such falsehoods.
practical_advice: When someone presents a claim that seems to serve their immediate interest, take time to verify it carefully before acting on it, especially in important matters.
Do not let social pressure or the desire to be accommodating lead you to accept falsehoods — respectful but firm truth-seeking protects everyone.
Develop the habit of consulting reliable sources and knowledgeable people when you encounter dubious claims rather than accepting them at face value.

title: Ali ibn Abi Talib Writes the Treaty of Hudaybiyya
summary: Ali ibn Abi Talib served as the scribe for the Treaty of Hudaybiyya between the Prophet and Quraysh, documenting the terms of peace including safety for Muslims, no drawn swords, no chains, and a ten-year cessation of warfare.
story: It is narrated above how he wrote the peace agreement between the Messenger of God (SAAS) and Q!.rraysh at I;Iudaybiyya, specifying that the Muslims would be safe, that there would be no swords drawn, nor chains used, and that there would be no warfare for ten years. He also wrote other documents in the presence of the Prophet (SAAS).
lessons: Documentation and formal agreements are part of the prophetic tradition — putting terms of peace in writing protects all parties and honors their commitments.
Acts of service like scribing, however administrative they may seem, play a vital role in preserving justice and truth for generations to come.
Peace, even when it comes with difficult conditions, is worth pursuing and formalizing — the Treaty of Hudaybiyya ultimately opened the door to the greatest expansion of Islam.
practical_advice: When resolving a conflict, put the terms of agreement in writing clearly so both parties have a shared reference point and misunderstandings are minimized.
Be willing to take on unglamorous but essential roles in service of a greater good, as Ali did in recording the treaty.
Trust that difficult peace agreements made in good faith can lead to outcomes better than you imagined — patience with the process is part of the wisdom.

title: Al-Ala ibn al-Hadrami Sent as Governor to Bahrain and His Miraculous Deeds
summary: The Prophet sent al-Ala ibn al-Hadrami to al-Mundhir ibn Sawa, the king of Bahrain, and appointed him its governor; he was later known for extraordinary miraculous deeds including leading his army across the sea without the water reaching their stirrups.
story: The Prophet (SAAS) sent al-'Ala' b. al-I:Iaqrami to al-Mundhir b. Sawa, the king of al-Bahrayn. He appointed al-'AI;;' as its ruler after he conquered it, and Abu Bakr confirmed him in that position, as did 'Vmar b. al-Khanab. He remained in that role there until 'Vmar removed him and appointed him Governor of Basra. He died while en route, in 2I AH. AI-Bayhaqi and others attribute to him many wonderfnl deeds. One such is his having led his army out upon the sea without the water having reached up to the stirrups of their horses! Other accounts say the undersides of their horses' hooves did not even get wet, after he had ordered them all to call out, Ya I;:Iilim! Ya '~im! "0 Wise One! "0 Mighty One!". Another incident related to his being with his army when they were desperate for water. He prayed to God who sent down rain to satisfy their need. Also, when he was about to be bnried, no trace at all of him could be seen! He had asked God for that.
lessons: Sincere reliance on God (tawakkul) in moments of impossibility can result in outcomes that transcend ordinary human capability.
Leadership that is grounded in prayer and trust in God creates a different kind of courage — one that does not depend on material means alone.
The lives of the Companions demonstrate that closeness to God is not merely spiritual but has practical manifestations in the real world.
practical_advice: When you face a situation that seems humanly impossible, turn to God with the names and attributes that are most relevant to your need, as al-Ala did by calling upon Al-Halim and Al-Asim.
In moments of collective crisis — whether your team, family, or community — lead by calling others to prayer and supplication before despair sets in.
Let the accounts of miraculous divine assistance strengthen your certainty that God is actively involved in the affairs of those who sincerely rely on Him.

title: The Companion Shurayh ibn al-Hadrami: One Who Never Abandoned the Quran
summary: The Prophet praised the Companion Shurayh ibn al-Hadrami by saying he was a man who does not use the Quran as a pillow, meaning he remained conscious of it and engaged with it at all times of the day and night.
story: They also included Shuray!). b. al-I:Iaqrami, one of the very best of the Companions. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said of him, "That is a man who does not use the Qur'an as a pillow!" By this he meant that he did not go to sleep and abandon it, but was conscious of it at all times of the day and night.
lessons: The highest praise from the Prophet for a Companion was not wealth or status but consistent, living engagement with the Quran — it is a relationship, not a ritual.
Using the Quran as a 'pillow' — something set aside when convenient — is a subtle but serious form of spiritual neglect that the Prophet warned against.
Consciousness of God's words throughout the day and night is what transforms a person's character at the deepest level.
practical_advice: Evaluate your current relationship with the Quran honestly — is it something you engage with daily and throughout your day, or something you return to only occasionally?
Set a practical goal to increase your Quran engagement: even five minutes of reflection on a verse during different parts of your day can transform your spiritual state.
When you feel spiritually lost or disconnected, returning to regular Quran recitation and reflection is one of the most reliable paths back to inner clarity and peace.

title: Al-Ala ibn al-Hadrami Writes to the Prophet About Zakat on an Orchard Owned by Brothers
summary: Al-Ala ibn al-Hadrami wrote to the Prophet from Bahrain asking what the tax consequences would be if one brother among co-owners of an orchard accepted Islam, demonstrating the Prophet's role in clarifying legal and religious matters for distant communities.
story: The third badith is narrated by Al)mad and by Ibn Maja, through Mnl;iammad b. Zayd, from I;:Iibban aI-Nraj, to the effect that aI-'Ala' b. aI-I;:Iaqrami wrote to the Messenger of God (SAAS), from al-Bal;irayn concerning a ba'i! - an orchard, that is - that was owned by brothers. He asked what (would be the tax consequences) if one of the brothers accepted Islam. He ordered him to collect
lessons: Even in practical matters of finance and law, the early Muslims turned to the Prophet for guidance, showing that Islam integrates the spiritual and the practical.
Governors and leaders in the prophetic tradition saw their role as serving the people's needs comprehensively, including ensuring just and clear religious rulings.
Asking qualified authorities when facing genuine confusion in important matters is itself an act of responsible faith.
practical_advice: When you encounter confusing situations at the intersection of your faith and daily life — finances, relationships, ethics — seek out knowledgeable guidance rather than guessing or avoiding the question.
Do not let complexity paralyze you; write out your question clearly, as al-Ala did, and seek an answer from a reliable source.
Recognize that caring about doing things correctly in everyday matters, like taxation and property rights, is a form of God-consciousness that the Companions modeled.

title: Al-'Ala' b. al-Hadrami's Letter on Tax Consequences of Conversion
summary: Al-'Ala' b. al-Hadrami wrote to the Prophet from Bahrain asking about the tax consequences when one of a pair of brother landowners accepts Islam, and the Prophet ordered the tithe to be collected from the Muslim and the poll tax from the non-Muslim.
story: The third hadith is narrated by Ahmad and by Ibn Maja, through Muhammad b. Zayd, from Hibban al-Nraj, to the effect that al-'Ala' b. al-Hadrami wrote to the Messenger of God (SAAS), from al-Bahrayn concerning a ha'it - an orchard, that is - that was owned by brothers. He asked what (would be the tax consequences) if one of the brothers accepted Islam. He ordered him to collect al-'ushr, "the tithe", from the Muslim and then the kharaj, "the poll tax"; from the one who had not accepted Islam, that is.
lessons: Divine guidance extends even to complex practical and financial matters, showing that Islam addresses all dimensions of life.
The Prophet's rulings balanced fairness to both Muslim and non-Muslim, demonstrating justice as a core Islamic value.
Seeking scholarly guidance on difficult questions is a mark of wisdom and sincerity, not weakness.
practical_advice: When facing complex ethical or legal dilemmas, seek guidance from trusted scholars rather than acting on assumption alone.
Treat those who differ from you in belief with fairness and equity, as the Prophet's ruling protected both the Muslim and non-Muslim brother equally.
Document your questions and the answers you receive from reliable sources so you can act with confidence and clarity.

title: Land Grants Witnessed by Al-'Ala' b. 'Uqba
summary: The Prophet Muhammad granted lands to 'Abbas b. Mirdas al-Sulami and 'Awsaja b. Harmala al-Juhani, with the deeds written and witnessed by al-'Ala' b. 'Uqba, affirming full rights to the recipients.
story: He went on to give a line of transmission to 'Atiq b. Ya'qub, who quoted 'Abd al-Malik b. Abu Bakr b. Muhammad b. 'Amr b. Hazm, from his father, from his grandfather, from 'Amr b. Hazm, who detailed land grants made by the Messenger of God (SAAS) to certain persons. He gave the wording of the deed as follows, "In the name of God, the most Merciful and Beneficent: this is a grant made by the Prophet, Muhammad to 'Abbas b. Mirdas al-Sulami. He has given him Madmur. No one has a right to interfere with him there; he has full rights. Written and witnessed by al-'Ala' b. 'Uqba." He also went on to quote, "In the name of God, the most Merciful and Beneficent. This records what Muhammad, the Messenger of God (SAAS), has awarded to 'Awsaja b. Harmala al-Juhani: from Dhu al-Marwa and what lies between Balkatha to al-Zubya, to al-Jaqat, to Mt. al-Qibliyya. No one has a right to interfere with him there. He has full rights. Written by al-'Ala' b. 'Uqba."
lessons: The Prophet's practice of formally documenting grants and rights shows that Islam values the protection of individuals' material and legal security.
Beginning every important act with 'In the name of God, the most Merciful and Beneficent' sanctifies our endeavors and reminds us of our ultimate dependence on God.
Ensuring that rights are clearly defined and witnessed protects communities from future conflict and injustice.
practical_advice: Formalize important agreements in writing to protect the rights of all parties involved and prevent misunderstanding.
When you receive a blessing or provision, acknowledge it as a gift from God and use it responsibly.
Serve your community by being someone who can be trusted to document and bear witness to important matters accurately.

title: Muhammad b. Maslama: The Trusted Companion and Scribe
summary: Muhammad b. Maslama al-Ansari was one of the most excellent Companions, a scribe of the Prophet, his deputy during the expedition to Tabuk, and a man who avoided civil unrest entirely, dying in Medina in 43 AH.
story: Also among them was Muhammad b. Maslama b. Huraysh b. Khalid b. 'Adi b. Majda'a b. Haritha b. al-Harith b. al-Khazraj al-Ansari al-Harithi al-Khazraji Abu 'Abd Allah, known as Abu 'Abd al-Rahman, and as Abu Sa'd al-Madani, the ally of Banu 'Abd al-Ashhal. He accepted Islam in the presence of Mus'ab b. 'Umayr, or alternatively Sa'd b. Mu'adh and Usayd b. Huqayr. Upon his arrival in Medina, the Messenger of God (SAAS) established a tie of brotherhood between him and Abu 'Ubayda b. al-Jarrah. He participated at Badr and at battles thereafter. The Messenger of God (SAAS) appointed him his vice-regent over Medina in the year of the expedition to Tabuk. Ibn 'Abd al-Barr stated in his work al-Isti'ab that he had an extremely dark complexion, was tall, bald and corpulent. He was one of the most excellent of the Companions. He avoided involvement in civil unrest and carried a wooden sword. He died in Medina in 43 AH, as is widely known. Marwan b. al-Hakam prayed over him. He narrated many ahadith from the Prophet (SAAS). Muhammad b. Sa'd narrated, from 'Ali b. Muhammad al-Mada'ini, with lines of transmission back from him, that it was Muhammad b. Maslama who, at the order of the Messenger of God (SAAS), wrote the document for the delegation from Banu Murra.
lessons: True excellence is not only found in fame or power but in faithfulness, trustworthiness, and avoiding harm to others — as Muhammad b. Maslama demonstrated by carrying a wooden sword during civil unrest.
