Staying out of unnecessary conflict and division is itself a form of wisdom and spiritual strength.
Brotherhood forged in faith, as between Muhammad b. Maslama and Abu 'Ubayda, creates bonds that sustain us through life's hardships.
practical_advice: In times of social or political conflict, ask yourself whether your involvement brings peace or adds to division, and choose accordingly.
Cultivate trustworthiness in your professional and personal life so that others can rely on you in moments of great responsibility.
Seek out relationships of genuine brotherhood and sisterhood in faith; these bonds will support you when you face difficulties alone.

title: Abu Sufyan Requests Three Favors from the Prophet
summary: Abu Sufyan asked the Prophet for three favors — military command, the appointment of his son Mu'awiya as a scribe, and the Prophet's marriage to Umm Habiba — all of which the Prophet granted.
story: Muslim narrated, in his Sahih collection, from a hadith of 'Ikrama b. 'Ammar, from Abu Zumayl Simak b. al-Walid, from Ibn 'Abbas, that Abu Sufyan asked, "Messenger of God, are there three requests you would fulfil for me?" "Yes," he replied. He then said, "Give me command, so that I can battle the unbelievers, just as I used to battle the Muslims." "Granted," he replied. He then asked, "Appoint Mu'awiya as your scribe." "Granted," he replied. And the hadith continues thereafter. I have devoted a separate section to this hadith since it incorporates reference to his request to the Messenger of God (SAAS) that the latter marry Umm Habiba. Moreover, it does include reference to Abu Sufyan's appointment as a commander and to Mu'awiya's part as scribe on behalf of the Prophet (SAAS). On this issue scholars are unanimous.
lessons: The Prophet's willingness to grant Abu Sufyan's requests after years of enmity demonstrates that Islam values sincere transformation and the power of forgiveness.
Holding positions of responsibility — whether as a scribe, commander, or leader — is a trust that must be fulfilled with integrity.
Even former opponents can find redemption and meaningful contribution when they sincerely embrace truth and are given the opportunity to serve.
practical_advice: Do not allow past mistakes or enmity to define your future; seek reconciliation and renewed purpose with sincerity.
When someone demonstrates a genuine change of heart, give them the opportunity to contribute positively rather than holding their past against them.
Approach moments of transition in your life — new roles, new relationships, new responsibilities — with humility and a desire to serve others.

title: The Weak Hadith Regarding Gabriel's Advice on Appointing Mu'awiya
summary: A hadith claiming that the Prophet consulted Gabriel about appointing Mu'awiya as his scribe, and that Gabriel endorsed him as trustworthy, is analyzed and found to be unreliable due to weak narrators in its chain of transmission.
story: Then there is the hadith relating to Mu'awiya given by the hafiz Ibn 'Asakir in his work of history that is relevant here. He stated that Abu Ghalib b. al-Bana related to him, quoting Abu Muhammad al-Jawhari, quoting Abu 'Ali Muhammad b. Ahmad b. Yahya b. 'Abd Allah al-'Atashi, quoting Ahmad b. Muhammad al-Buhtri, quoting al-Sari b. 'Asim, quoting al-Hasan b. Ziyad, from al-Qasim b. Bahram, from Abu al-Zubayr, from Jabir to the effect that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sought the advice of Gabriel over appointing Mu'awiya as his scribe and that the latter said, "Appoint him as a scribe; he is trustworthy." This hadith is gharib and, indeed, objectionable. This man al-Sari b. 'Asim is Abu 'Asim al-Hamadhani, who used to act as tutor to al-Mu'taz Bi Allahi; Ibn Khirash stated that he was untruthful in hadith narration. Ibn Hibban and Ibn 'Adi stated that he would steal ahadith. Ibn Hibban added, "He would ascribe to (the Prophet (SAAS)) statements that belonged to the Companions. It is not permissible to use such materials in arguments." Al-Darqutni stated that his ahadith were weak. His teacher was Sheikh al-Hasan b. Ziyad, who stated, "If the latter was al-Lu'lu'i several of the imams have ignored him. Many of them asserted that he was mendacious. If applicable to some other person, nothing whatsoever is known about him." The source in the above hadith named as al-Qasim b. Bahram might refer to either of two persons. One possibility would be a man known as al-Qasim b. Bahram al-Asadi al-Wasiti al-A'raj, whose origins were in Isfahan. Al-Nasa'i quoted him from Sa'id b. Jubayr, from Ibn 'Abbas, at length for his statement relating to the hadith al-qunut. Ibn Ma'in, Abu Hatim, Abu Da'ud and Ibn Hibban categorize him as a trustworthy source. The second man was al-Qasim b. Bahram Abu Hamdan, the qadi of Hit. Ibn Mu'in stated that he was a notorious liar. In sum, therefore, this hadith from this line of transmission is not firmly established and is one by which one should not be misled. It is surprising that the hafiz Ibn 'Asakir, a scholar of such illustrious reputation and a man of greater knowledge regarding the fabrication of ahadith than his contemporaries - or his predecessors for many years before - should give this in his work of history along with many other such ahadith, without any (negative) commentary, direct or subtle. Such behaviour is questionable. But God knows best.
lessons: The Islamic scholarly tradition has always prioritized rigorous verification of religious knowledge, reminding us that not everything attributed to a great source is authentic.
Intellectual honesty requires acknowledging the weaknesses in evidence even when the conclusion might seem desirable, as the author does here.
Scholarship carries a grave responsibility; transmitting unreliable information without scrutiny, even unintentionally, can mislead generations.
practical_advice: Before accepting and sharing religious or historical information, make an effort to verify its source and authenticity.
Cultivate intellectual humility — be willing to say 'this is uncertain' or 'I don't know' rather than presenting weak evidence as established fact.
When you discover an error or weakness in something you previously believed, correct it honestly rather than defending it out of pride.

title: Al-Mughira b. Shu'ba Writes a Land Deed for the Prophet
summary: Al-Mughira b. Shu'ba al-Thaqafi served as one of the scribes of the Prophet, writing out the land tenure deed for Busayn b. Nadla al-Asadi at the Prophet's orders.
story: They also included al-Mughira b. Shu'ba al-Thaqafi. I have given his biography above in the section devoted to those of his Companions apart from his freed-man who served the Messenger of God (SAAS). I noted that it was he who would hold a sword above the latter's head. Ibn 'Asakir narrated from 'Atiq b. Ya'qub, along several lines of transmissions, as above, that it was al-Mughira b. Shu'ba who wrote out the land tenure deed for Busayn b. Nadla al-Asadi awarded him at the orders of the Messenger of God (SAAS). These, then, were the scribes who would write at his command and in his presence.
lessons: Serving in a supporting role — whether as a scribe, an assistant, or a witness — is an act of great honor when done in service of truth and justice.
Every skill and talent, including literacy and writing, can be placed in service of God and one's community.
The Prophet surrounded himself with people of varied abilities, showing that a healthy community values diverse contributions.
practical_advice: Identify the skills you have and consider how they might be used in service of your community or a cause greater than yourself.
Do not underestimate the value of administrative, organizational, or behind-the-scenes work; such contributions often hold communities together.
Be reliable and precise in any task entrusted to you, knowing that your work may have lasting consequences for others.

title: Abu 'Ubayda b. al-Jarrah: The Trustworthy One of This Community
summary: The Prophet declared Abu 'Ubayda b. al-Jarrah the 'amin' — the fully trustworthy person — of the Muslim community, and sent him as a trusted representative to a delegation from Najran.
story: Ibn 'Asakir included among those who were trusted (scribes) Abu 'Ubayda 'Amir b. 'Abd Allah b. al-Jarrah al-Qurashi al-Fihri, one of "the ten", may God be pleased with him, and 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Awf al-Zuhri. Regarding Abu 'Ubayda, al-Bukhari narrated from a hadith of Abu Qilaba, from Anas, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "Every nation had an amin, 'a fully trustworthy person'. And the amin of this community is Abu 'Ubayda b. al-Jarrah." In one text the wording is, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) told a delegation of (Banu) 'Abd al-Qays of Najran, 'I will send with you an amin, a true amin.' And he sent Abu 'Ubayda with them."
lessons: Trustworthiness is one of the highest virtues a person can develop; it is so rare that an entire community may have only one person who fully embodies it.
Being chosen for a responsibility because of your character and not just your capability is the highest form of professional and spiritual recognition.
The Prophet's praise of Abu 'Ubayda shows that God-consciousness and integrity in all dealings are what truly distinguish a person.
practical_advice: Strive to be someone whose word and commitments can always be relied upon, in small matters as well as large ones.
Reflect regularly on whether your actions in private match the person you present yourself to be in public — this alignment is the foundation of trustworthiness.
When you are entrusted with a responsibility, carry it as if the Prophet himself assigned it to you, giving it your full integrity and care.

title: Mu'ayqib b. Abu Fatima: Keeper of the Prophet's Ring and Treasury
summary: Mu'ayqib b. Abu Fatima al-Dawsi, who accepted Islam early and emigrated twice, was appointed keeper of the Prophet's seal ring and later placed in charge of the treasury, despite contracting leprosy later in his life.
story: Ibn 'Asakir went on, "Among them also was Mu'ayqib b. Abu Fatima al-Dawsi, the freed-man of Banu 'Abd Shams. He was in charge of his (seal) ring; it is also said that he was his servant. Others have said that he accepted Islam early and emigrated with the others to Abyssinia, and thereafter to Medina. He participated in the battle of Badr and engagements thereafter, and he was in charge of his ring. Both of the sheikhs placed him in charge of the treasury." They also say that he contracted leprosy, that 'Umar b. al-Khattab ordered that he be treated with colocynth and that the disease was arrested. His death came during the caliphate of 'Uthman, in the year 40 AH, it is said. But God knows best.
lessons: Physical affliction does not diminish a person's worth or their capacity to serve; Mu'ayqib held the highest positions of trust even while suffering from leprosy.
Faithfulness from the very beginning of one's commitment — emigrating twice, participating at Badr — builds a legacy that endures beyond one's lifetime.
Community care for the sick, as demonstrated by 'Umar seeking treatment for Mu'ayqib, is a prophetic value that strengthens the bonds of the Muslim community.
practical_advice: If you or someone you know is struggling with illness or limitation, remember that your value is not defined by your physical condition but by your character and commitment.
Be present for the long journey of faith — consistency over years and decades matters more than momentary acts of devotion.
When someone in your community is sick or suffering, take practical steps to help them access care, as 'Umar did for Mu'ayqib.

title: The Prophet's Hadith on Smoothing Earth in Prayer
summary: The Prophet advised a man who repeatedly smoothed the earth in his place of prostration that if he must do it, he should do it only once, as narrated by Mu'ayqib.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Yahya b. Abu Bakr related to him, quoting Shayban, from Yahya b. Abu Kathir, from Abu Salama, who quoted Mu'ayqib to the effect that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said about a man smoothing out the earth where he would prostrate in prayer, "If you have to do it, let it be once (only)." Both compilers of the Sahih collections gave it from a hadith of Shayban al-Nahwi. Muslim added, "And also (from) Hisham al-Dastuwa'i." Al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i and Ibn Maja also gave it from al-Awza'i. All three of them also gave it from Yahya b. Abu Kathir. Al-Tirmidhi categorized it as hasan sahih.
lessons: The Prophet's guidance on prayer shows that unnecessary fidgeting or distraction, even when well-intentioned, detracts from the quality of worship.
Simplicity and focus in worship are more valuable than elaborate preparation; one deliberate action is better than repeated anxious movement.
The Prophet corrected behavior gently and practically, showing that guidance given with compassion is most effective.
practical_advice: Before you begin your prayers, take a moment to prepare your space and mindset so you are not distracted once you have started.
Practice reducing unnecessary movements and habits during worship to build deeper focus and presence in your connection with God.
When you notice a habit that disrupts your peace of mind, address it minimally and move on rather than letting it grow into an obsession.

title: The Prophet's Ring: From Gold to Silver
summary: The Prophet initially wore a gold ring but later discarded it, swearing never to wear it again, and replaced it with a silver ring kept and worn by Mu'ayqib, as narrated in hadith literature.
story: Abu Da'ud and al-Nasa'i narrated from a hadith of Abu 'Attab Sahl b. Hammad al-Dallal, from Abu Makin Nuh b. Rabi'a, from Iyyas b. al-Harith b. al-Mu'ayqib, who quoted his grandfather - who was in charge of the (seal) ring of the Prophet (SAAS) - as having said, "The ring of the Prophet (SAAS) was made of iron with silver overlaid upon it. Sometimes it was in my hand." I note that the ring of the Prophet (SAAS) was actually made of silver, with its protrusion integral to it, as will be shown in the quotations from the Sahih collections. He had earlier had a gold ring that he had worn for a period but later discarded, saying, "I swear by God, I'll not wear it!" He then began use of this ring.
lessons: The Prophet's abandonment of his gold ring demonstrates the Islamic principle of voluntary simplicity and the avoidance of ostentation in personal adornment.
Making a firm resolution and sticking to it — as the Prophet did when he swore off the gold ring — is a mark of spiritual strength and integrity.
Even small choices in our personal lives, such as what we wear, can reflect our values and our relationship with worldly attachment.
practical_advice: Examine your own relationship with material possessions and ask whether any of them have become a source of pride, distraction, or excessive attachment.
When you make a resolution to change a habit or behavior, state it clearly and commit to it fully, as the Prophet modeled.
Choose simplicity in your personal presentation not as a deprivation but as a conscious act of inner freedom from the need for outward status symbols.

title: The Prophet's Ring: Material, Inscription, and History
summary: An account of the Prophet Muhammad's ring — its material (silver), inscription ('Muhammad, Messenger of God'), and its passage through Abu Bakr, Umar, and Uthman before being lost in the Aris well.
story: who was in charge of the (seal) ring of the Prophet (SAAS) - as having said, "The ring of the Prophet (SAAS) was made of iron with silver overlaid upon it. Sometimes it was in my hand." I note that the ring of the Prophet (SAAS) was actually made of silver, with its protusion integral to it, as will be shown in the quotations from the ~al;il; collections. He had earlier had a gold ring that he had worn for a period but later discarded, saying,"I swear by God, I'll not wear it!" He then began use of this ring made of silver with its raised design integral, inscribed with the words, "MuiJammad, Messenger of God", With "MuiJammad" on one line, "rasul, 'messenger'", on another, and "Allah, 'God''', on the third. After remaining in his possession, it was passed on after him to Abu Bakr, then to 'Umar and then to 'Uthman, with whom it remained for six years. Then it dropped from him into the well at AtiS. He made every effort to retrieve it, but failed.
lessons: Objects of symbolic or spiritual significance connect communities across generations, and their loss can be a reminder of the impermanence of the material world.
The Prophet's willingness to abandon gold in favor of silver demonstrates that status and leadership are expressed through humility and simplicity, not outward wealth.
Even the most precious and meaningful possessions can be lost; trust in God means accepting such losses without despair.
practical_advice: When you lose something of great sentimental or practical value, reflect on whether your attachment to it was healthy, and practice letting go with gratitude for the time you had it.
Follow the example of simplicity in your personal adornments and possessions — choose function and meaning over ostentation.
When something important slips from your grasp despite every effort to recover it, accept it as part of God's plan and redirect your energy toward what remains.

title: The Prophet's Origin of the Seal Ring for Official Correspondence
summary: The Prophet Muhammad had a ring made and inscribed with 'Muhammad, Messenger of God' after being informed that non-Arab rulers would not read letters without an official seal.
story: Abu Da'ud stated that 'Abd al-R.a1).im b. Mutrif al-Ru'iisi narrated to him, quoting qsa, from Sa'id, from Q;ttada, who quoted Anas b. Malik as having said, "The Messenger of God's (SAAS) wished to write to certain non-Arabs and he was told that they will not read a letter unless it has been signed with a seal. He therefore took a ring and had inscribed on it, 'Mu1).ammad, Messenger of God.'"
lessons: Adapting one's method of communication to the norms and expectations of others is a sign of wisdom and effective leadership, not compromise of principle.
Practical problem-solving, even in matters of religious outreach, is a prophetic virtue — the Prophet sought to remove barriers to conveying his message.
Preparation and professionalism in communication reflect respect for those being addressed, even when the message is of a spiritual nature.
practical_advice: When reaching out to someone from a different background or culture, take time to understand and respect their customs so your message is received effectively.
Don't let procedural or bureaucratic obstacles discourage you from your mission — find legitimate and creative solutions to move forward.
Invest in the tools and formats of communication that your audience trusts, whether that is a formal tone, a particular platform, or a specific medium.

title: The Prophet Discards His Gold Ring After Seeing Others Imitate Him
summary: The Prophet wore a gold ring briefly, but when people began imitating him and wearing gold rings themselves, he discarded it permanently and switched to silver.
story: Abu Da'ud continued that Nagr b. aI-Faraj related to him, quoting Abu Usama, from 'Obayd Allah, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Omar, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) obtained a gold ring and wore it with its stone facing towards the centre of his palm. And on it he had inscribed, 'Mul:tammad, Messenger of God'. People then began wearing gold rings. When he saw them doing this, he threw his away, saying, 'I'll never wear it!' He then began wearing a silver ring inscribed with the words, 'Mul:tammad, Messenger of God'. After his death, Abu Bakr wore it. Then, after him, 'Omar wore it, and then 'Uthman {did so} until it dropped inside the Arts well."
lessons: True leadership involves constant self-awareness about the influence one's choices have on those who follow; the Prophet modeled responsible use of influence.
Voluntarily giving up something permissible for the sake of guiding a community toward greater modesty is a mark of sincere concern for others' spiritual wellbeing.
Simplicity and restraint in personal lifestyle choices can have a powerful ripple effect on an entire community's values and culture.
practical_advice: Be mindful of how your choices — in lifestyle, spending, or appearance — influence those around you, especially those who look up to you.
When you notice that something you are doing is leading others toward excess or imitation that may harm them, consider setting a different example even if what you are doing is not wrong in itself.
Choosing simplicity over luxury when both are available is a form of gratitude and a guard against pride.

title: Uthman's Ring Falls into the Aris Well and Cannot Be Recovered
summary: The Prophet's silver ring, which had passed to Abu Bakr, Umar, and then Uthman, fell into the Aris well during Uthman's caliphate. Despite exhaustive efforts to drain and search the well, it was never found.
story: Wahb b. Baqiyya narrated to him, from Khalid, from Sa'id, from Qttada, from Anas, to the same effect as in the baditk of qsa b. YUnus. he added, "It was on his hand until he died; then it was worn by Abu Bakr until he died, and by 'Vmar until he died. Then 'Vthman wore it. While he was at a well it fell into that well. He ordered it to be emptied, but it could not be found." ... Mul,tammad b. Yal,tya b. Faris related to him, quoting Abu 'Asim, from al-Mughira b. Ziyad, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Omar, who said as follows concerning this statement from the Prophet (SAAS), "They searched but could not locate it. And so 'Utlnnan took (another) ring and inscribed on it 'Mul,tammad, Messenger of God'. And he used it as a seal, or it was so used."
lessons: Some losses in life are final and irreversible, and wisdom lies in accepting this reality without obsessing over what cannot be undone.
Even the most earnest and thorough efforts do not always yield the results we hope for — this is part of the divine will and a test of faith.
When one door closes permanently, the right response is to find a meaningful way forward, as Uthman did by creating a new ring with the same inscription.
practical_advice: When you have done everything in your power to recover or fix a loss and still cannot, practice acceptance — channel your energy into rebuilding rather than grieving indefinitely.
Draw a distinction between situations that require persistent effort and those that require graceful acceptance; not every loss can or should be reversed.
Find ways to honor what was lost by carrying forward its meaning or purpose in a new form, as Uthman honored the legacy of the Prophet's seal.

title: The Prophet's Ring Inscription: 'Muhammad, Messenger of God' in Three Lines
summary: Scholars detail the precise inscription of the Prophet's silver ring — 'Muhammad, Messenger of God' written across three lines — and debate whether the letters were written in reverse for sealing purposes.
story: He wore the silver ring frequently. It was on his hand until his death. Its "stone" was integral to it; that is, it had no stone separate from it. Those who narrate that in it there was engraved the image of a person are totally mistaken. In fact it was entirely of silver and its "stone" integral to it. And it was engraved with the three words "Mul,tammad, Messenger of God", in three lines with "Mubammad", "Messenger", and "God", on separate lines. It is likely, though God knows best, that the letters were written in reverse so that it would print out correctly, as was typical (of such seals). It has been said, however, that the inscription was not reversed but was printed that way. The correctness of this view is disputed. I do not know a single line of transmission for that, neither one that is authentic, nor one that is weak.
lessons: Attention to detail and accuracy in preserving religious and historical knowledge reflects deep reverence for the truth and for those who embody it.
Scholarly integrity requires acknowledging the limits of one's knowledge — saying 'I do not know' when evidence is absent is a sign of trustworthiness, not weakness.
Even in small material details, the Prophet's life offers lessons in intentionality — every aspect of his practice carried meaning and purpose.
practical_advice: When researching or sharing information about matters of importance, be honest about what is verified and what is uncertain rather than filling gaps with assumption.
Pay attention to the small details of your commitments and responsibilities — integrity in minor things reflects and builds integrity in major ones.
When others make claims without clear evidence, seek verification before accepting or spreading them, regardless of how widely believed they may be.

title: The Sandals of the Prophet (SAAS)
summary: Descriptions of the Prophet's sandals, including their double thongs and plaited laces, as narrated by multiple companions including Ibn Abbas and Abu Hurayra, along with the account of a sandal preserved and venerated after his death.
story: in al-Sham821 that Aba Kurayb narrated to him, quoting Wakic,from Sufyan, from Khdid al-HadhaJ, from CAbd Allah b, al-Harith, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "The sandals of the Messenger of God (SAAS) had double thongs, their laces plaited." He also stated that Ishaq b. Mansar related to him, quoting 'Abd al-Razzaq, from Macmar, from Ibn Abn Dhi'b, from Salih, the freed-man of al-TawJama, from Aba Hurayra, who said, "The sandals of the Messenger of God (SAAS) had two thongs." Al-Tirmidhi stated that Muhammad b. Marzaq Aba CAbd All& related to h i , quoting CAbd al-Rahman b. Qays Abii Mulawiya, quoting Hishm, from Mu?mnmad, from Aba Hurayra, who said, "The sandals of the Messenger of God (SAAS) had two thongs, as did those of Abti Bakr and Wmar. The first to tie his sandal using one thong was Wthman." Al-Jawhari stated, "The word 'thong7, qibal, of a sandal, spelled with an 'i', is the lace that is positioned between the middle toe and the one next to it." I note that in the period shortly after 600 AH a merchant named Ibn Aba al-Hadrad became widely known as having a single sandal that he claimed to have belonged to the Prophet (SAAS). Al-Malik al-Ashraf Masa b. a l - M m M M ABU AL-FIDA' I S m q L IBN KATHIR al-CAdil Abu Eakr b. AyyiibZ4offered to buy it from him for a large sum of money, but he refused to sell it. But shortly thereafter, he happened to die and the sandal came into the possession of that al-Mid& al-Ashraf. He venerated it having built the dar al-hadith aI-ashra@yya next to the qaFa, the mightily and h, citadel of Cairo, placed it in one of its treasuries. He appointed a curator to look after it who was paid 40 dirhams a month. It is still there in thatbuilding to this day.
lessons: Preserving and honoring the relics of the Prophet reflects the deep love the companions and later Muslims held for him, reminding us that love is expressed through action and remembrance.
The simplicity of the Prophet's sandals — plain leather with double thongs — teaches that true greatness is not measured by material extravagance but by character and closeness to God.
The story of Uthman changing the sandal design to a single thong illustrates how even small personal choices of the Prophet were observed, remembered, and emulated by those who loved him.
practical_advice: Reflect on small, everyday objects in your life as reminders of gratitude — just as companions treasured the Prophet's sandals, use ordinary moments to reconnect with what you value most.
When you feel disconnected from your faith, revisit the simple practices and descriptions of the Prophet's life; simplicity itself can be a powerful anchor for the soul.
Consider how you honor and preserve the teachings and traditions that matter to you — active stewardship of what you love, whether knowledge or practice, keeps it alive for future generations.

title: The Drinking Cup of the Prophet (SAAS) Preserved by Anas
summary: Descriptions of the Prophet's drinking cup, preserved by his companion Anas ibn Malik, which had cracked and been repaired with silver wire, and which Anas poured from innumerable times for the Messenger of God.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Y*ya b. Adam related to him, quoting Shar~k,from CASim, who said, "At the home of Anas, I saw the cup of the Prophet (SAAS). It had a silver band." The hafig al-Bayhaqi stated that Abn CAbd All& Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah informed him, quoting Ahmad b. Muhammad al-Nasawi, quoting Hammsd b. Shakir, quoting Muhammad b. IsmaCil,al-Bukhari, that is, quoting al-Hasan b. Mudrik, quoting Yahya b. Hammad, quoting Abii CAwana, who quoted 'lisim al-Ahwal as having said, "I saw the cup of the Prophet (SAAS), at the home of Anas b. Malik. It had cracked and he had wired it together with silver." The narrator went on, "It was a fine, wide cup of tamarisk wood." Anas stated, "I have poured into that cup for the Messenger of God (SAAS) innumerable times." Ibn Sirin stated, "It had on it a ring of iron. Anas wanted to replace that with a ring of gold or silver, but Abn Talha told him, 'Don't change anything made by the Messenger of God (SAAS).' So he left it as it was." Imam Ahmad stated that Rawh b. CUbsda related to him, quoting Hajjaj b. Hassan, who said, "We were at the home of Anas and he called for a vessel to be brought. It had three bands and a ring, all of iron. It was less than a rubcbut more than a half a rub' in capacity. Anas gave orders and water was poured into it. We then took it and drank, pouring some water over our heads and faces; then we spoke prayers for the Prophet (SAAS)." Alpnad is alone in giving this.
lessons: Anas's refusal to discard the cracked cup, and Abu Talha's counsel not to change what the Prophet had made, teaches that authentic love preserves what is real even when imperfect — our flaws and wounds do not diminish our worth.
The simple act of pouring water 'innumerable times' for the Prophet speaks to how sacred repetitive, humble service can become when done with love and devotion.
Sharing the cup communally and praying upon the Prophet afterward shows how objects of remembrance can become vehicles for community, blessing, and spiritual renewal.
practical_advice: When you feel broken or cracked like the repaired cup, remember that repair does not diminish value — silver mended the cup and made it more precious; your healing journey can do the same for you.

Find a simple, recurring act of service or devotion in your daily life and perform it with full presence, as Anas poured water innumerable times — consistency in small acts of love builds a deeply meaningful life.

Gather with others around shared remembrance of God and the Prophet; communal acts of worship and gratitude, even small ones, can lift the heart and strengthen bonds in ways that solitary practice cannot.

title: The Antimony Case of the Prophet (SAAS)
summary: An account of the Prophet's antimony case and his practice of applying antimony to each eye three times before sleep, along with a note about relics preserved in Egypt.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Yazid related to him, quoting CAbd Allah b. Maqiir, from 'Ikrama, from Ibn <Abb%,who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) had an antimony case. Before going to sleep he would put antimony into each eye three times." Al-Tirmidhi and Ibn Maja narrated from a chain of Yazid b. Haron in which CAlA b. al-Madini is quoted as having said, "I heard Yahya b. Sacid say, 'I asked CAbW b. Maqffr, "Did you hear this hadith from Ikrama?" He replied, "Ibn Abi Yahya told it to me, from Dayadb. al-Husayn."'" I note that I have heard that somewhere in Egypt there is a shrine containing many of the personal effects of the Prophet (SAAS), that certain recent government ministers have assembled. These include an antimony case, along with, it is said, a comb and other items. But God knows best.
lessons: The Prophet's consistent nighttime practice of applying antimony reflects that caring for one's health and body is itself an act of worship and prophetic sunnah.

The preservation of the Prophet's personal effects by later generations demonstrates how love for the Prophet transcended time — the desire to remain close to him through tangible memory is a deeply human and spiritually meaningful impulse.

Saying 'God knows best' about uncertain matters, as the author does, is a model of intellectual humility — not everything needs to be definitively resolved; trusting God with the unknown is itself a form of peace.
practical_advice: Establish a consistent, calming nighttime routine inspired by prophetic practice — intentional habits before sleep, such as dhikr or reflection, can ease anxiety and bring your day to a peaceful close.
When you encounter uncertainty or unverifiable information, practice saying 'God knows best' internally — releasing the need to control or conclusively resolve every ambiguity is deeply liberating.
Honor your body's need for care and rest as the Prophet did; self-neglect is not piety, and tending to yourself allows you to serve others and God more fully.

title: The Cloak (Al-Burda) of the Prophet and the Garments He Wore
summary: An account of the Prophet's cloak given to the people of Aelia, its later acquisition by the Abbasid caliphs who wore it with dignity on festival days, and descriptions of the garments the Prophet wore at his death.
story: The al-Bayhaqi stated as follows, "Regarding the cloaks in the possession of the caliphs, we learn from the account of Muhammad b. Ishaq relating to the battle of Tab&, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) presented to the people of Aelia a burda, 'a cloak', along with the safe conduct document he wrote for them. Aba al-(Abb% <Abd All& b. Muhammad, the first of the 'Abbasid caliphs, known as al-Saffah, 'the shedder of blood', later purchased it for 300 dinr~rs." The (Abbasid rulers inherited this burda from one another. The Caliph would wear it over his shoulders on festival days, holding in one hand the staff said to have been used by the Prophet (SAAS). He would appear in it with such a sense of tranquillity and dignity that would completely overwhelm and bedazzle all. They would dress in black on Fridays and festivals; this they did to emulate the practice of him who was Lord of all, whether bedouin, farmers or town-dwellers. This they did because of the accounts given by al-Bukhari and Muslim, the two prime scholars of the historical record, from a hadith of Malik, from al-Zuhri, from Anas, to the effect that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca, he was wearing a helmet. In one such account, he is said to have been wearing a black turban. And in another that he let its end hang down between his shoulders. Al-Bukhiiri stated that Musaddad related to him, quoting Ismacil, quoting Ayyab, from Muhammad, from Aba Burda, who said, "cA'isha brought out to us a kzsB, 'an upper garment', and an ixiir, 'a waist-wrapper', made of coarse cloth and said, 'The spirit of the Prophet (SAAS) was taken while he was wearing these two garments.'" Al-Bukhiiri gave a hadith of al-Zuhri from Wbayd Allah b. CAbd Allah, from %'isha and Ibn 'Abbas which quoted them both as having said, "When revelation came down to the Messenger of God (SAAS), he immediately threw a black-bordered cloak of his over his face. If distressed, he would uncover his face and say, while thus, 'God curse the Jews and the Christians! They treat the graves of their prophets as shrines; what they do is to be avoided.'" I note that the later disposition of these three items of clothing is not known. It is stated above that a piece of red velvet upon which he had prayed was placed beneath him in his grave. It would consume a great deal of time for us to detail the items of clothing he wore throughout his life. The proper place for this will be the chapter devoted to clothing in my major study the Kitab al-Al;kam, if God so wills it. And in Him is all trust and reliance.
lessons: The Prophet wore coarse, simple garments at his death despite being the leader of a vast community — a profound reminder that closeness to God is never measured by the fineness of one's clothing or possessions.
The Abbasid caliphs wearing the Prophet's cloak with 'tranquillity and dignity' illustrates how remembrance of the Prophet is meant to inspire composure and nobility, not arrogance.

The Prophet's practice of covering his face during revelation and his warning against venerating graves shows his constant concern for pure monotheism — even in moments of divine intimacy, he guarded against what could lead others astray.
practical_advice: On days when you feel the need for dignity and grounding, dress intentionally and simply — the Prophet's example reminds us that what we wear can reflect and reinforce our inner state.

When you feel overwhelmed or distressed, as the Prophet would uncover his face and speak, find your own way to 'uncover' — step outside, speak aloud your trust in God, or make a small act of worship to break the heaviness.

Emulate those you admire not through imitation alone but through absorbing their values; as the caliphs wore the cloak to embody the Prophet's dignity, ask yourself what qualities of the Prophet you can genuinely internalize today.

title: The Horses, Mounts, Weapons, and Personal Effects of the Prophet (SAAS)
summary: A detailed account of the named horses, camels, mules, weapons, and personal effects belonging to the Prophet, along with the narration that he died leaving no wealth except a mule and a charitable endowment.
story: Ibn Isl).aq stated, from Yazid b. l;Iabib, from Marthad b. 'Abd Allah aI-Muzani, from 'Abd Allah b. Razin, who quoted 'Ali as having said, "The Prophet (SAAS) owned a horse named 'al-Murtajiz', a donkey named ''Ofayr' and a mule named 'Ouldul'; his sword was 'Ohu al-Fiqar', and his armour was 'Ohu aI-Fuqul'." Al-Bayhaqi narrated this from a l;adith of aI-l;Iakam, from Yal).ya b. al-Azzar, from 'Ali in similar words. Al-Bayhaqi stated, "We have narrated in our work Kitab al-Sunan the names of his horses that were with their fortunate recipients. Those horses were named 'Lazaz', 'al-Lal).if also known as 'al-Lakhif and 'aI-Zarib'. The horse he rode that belonged to Abu TaII).a was called aI-Mandub. His camels were named 'aI-Q;1~wa)', 'aI-'Aqba" and 'aI-Jad'a", while his mule was named 'al-Shahba' and 'al-Bayqa'." Al-Bayhaqi also stated, "There is nothing in the accounts to indicate that he died leaving them as inheritance, except for what information we have given about his mule 'al-Bayqa", his weapons, and a piece of land he left to charity." We have made reference to his clothing, his mule and his ring in this chapter. Abu Oa'ud aI-Tayalisi stated that Zam'a b. Salil). narrated to him, from Abu l;Iazim, from Sahl b. Sa'd, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) died leaving ajubba, 'a long, wide-sleeved outer garment', made of woven wool." The line of transmission for this is excellent. The I;afi'!' Abu Yaqa stated, in his musnad collection, that Mujahid narrated to him, from Musa, quoting 'Ali b. Thabit, quoting Ghalib aI-Jazar!, from Anas, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) died, a woollen garment was being woven for him." This testifies to what is narrated hereabove. Abu Sa'id b. aI-A'rabi stated that Sa'dan b. N~ir related to him, quoting SufYan b. 'Oyayna, from aI-Walid b. Kathir, from l;Iusayn, from Fatima, daughter of al-l;Insayn, that at the time when the Messenger of God (SAAS) died, two leather vessels were being made for him. This I;adtth is mursal, "incomplete in its line". Abu al-Qasim al-Tabrani stated that aI-l;Iasan b. Isl).aq aI-Tasturi related to him, quoting Abu Umayya 'Amr b. Hisham al-l;Iarrani, quoting 'Othman b. 'Abd al-Ral;1man b. 'All b. 'Orwa, from 'Abd al-Malik b. AbU Sulayman, from 'At" and 'Amr b. Dinar, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "The Messenger of God ($AAS) had a sword the hilt and pommel of which were made of silver; it was known as 'Dhu al-Fiqar'. He had a bow called 'al-Sidad' and a quiver named 'al-Jam". He had a coat of armour inlaid with brass called 'Dhat al-FuQul', and a spear called 'al-$ughra". He had a shield called 'al-Dhaqan'. He also had a white leather shield called 'al-Mujiz', a black horse named 'al-Sakb', a saddle called 'al-Mujiz', a grey mule called 'Duldul', a camel called 'al~~wa", a donkey called '¥a'fiir', a carpet called 'al-Karr', a woollen garment called 'al-Namir', a leather bag called 'al-$adir', a mirror called 'al-Mira'a', scissors called 'al-Jal;1l;1', and a fir-wood staff called 'al-Mamshuq'." I note that it has been related above from several of the Companions that the Messenger of God ($AAS) did not leave a single dinar, nor even a dirham, and not a slave, male or female. All he left was a mule and a piece of land that he had given to charity. This requires him to have fully emancipated the slaves to whom we have referred, to have given to charity all the weapons we have ennumerated, along with his animals, and all his personal effects, whether or not mentioned by myself above. Regarding his mule, its name was "al-Shahba" and also "al-BaYQa'"; though God knows best. It was that animal that the Muqawqis, the Governor of Alexandria, whose name was Jurayj b. Mina', had given him along with other valuables. It was this mule that the Messenger of God ($AAS) rode at the battle ofI:Iunayn into the very midst ofthe enemy, bravely extolling God's name and placing all his reliance in God the Almighty and Glorious. It is said that the mule lived on a long time after him,
lessons: The Prophet possessed named weapons, horses, camels, and fine items during his life, yet died leaving not a single dinar — a testament that worldly means are tools to be used in service of God, not ends to be hoarded.

His emancipation of all his slaves and gifting of all his possessions to charity before death demonstrates that true wealth lies in what you give away, not what you accumulate — generosity is the final and greatest act of a great life.

The mule Duldul, gifted by a foreign governor and ridden bravely into the heart of battle at Hunayn, illustrates that blessings come from unexpected sources and that courage consists not in the absence of danger but in advancing through it with God's name on your lips.
practical_advice: Take inventory of what you own and ask honestly: what do I hold onto out of need, and what do I hold onto out of attachment or fear? The Prophet's example of releasing everything invites us to examine our own relationship with possessions.

When you are in the midst of a crisis or conflict — your own 'battle of Hunayn' — recall the Prophet riding into enemy lines on a mule, calling God's name. Audible dhikr in moments of fear can reorient your heart from panic to reliance.

Identify one thing you can give away or release this week — an object, a grudge, a habit — as an act of deliberate generosity inspired by the Prophet's example of departing this world with open hands.

title: The Prophet's Mule Al-Shahba and Its History
summary: An account of the Prophet's mule al-Shahba, gifted by the Governor of Alexandria, which he rode bravely into battle at Hunayn, and which lived long after him through the caliphates of Ali and Abd Allah b. Ja'far.
story: animals, and all his personal effects, whether or not mentioned by myself above. Regarding his mule, its name was "al-Shahba" and also "al-BaYQa'''; though God knows best. It was that animal that the Muqawqis, the Governor of Alexandria, whose name was Jurayj b. Mina', had given him along with other valuables. It was this mule that the Messenger of God ($AAS) rode at the battle ofI:Iunayn into the very midst ofthe enemy, bravely extolling God's name and placing all his reliance in God the Almighty and Glorious. It is said that the mule lived on a long time after him, belonging to 'All during his caliphate and surviving on after his death and passing into the ownership of'Abd Allahb. Ja'far who would mash up its barley after it became too weak to eat it otherwise.
lessons: True courage means advancing into difficulty while placing complete reliance on God, as the Prophet demonstrated at Hunayn.
Care for living creatures is an expression of gratitude and mercy, as shown by Abd Allah b. Ja'far who mashed barley for the aged mule.
The belongings and companions of the righteous carry a kind of blessed memory that inspires those who come after them.
practical_advice: When facing overwhelming situations, consciously invoke God's name and remind yourself that your reliance is on Him, not on your own strength.
Show kindness and extra care to those who are aging or weakening around you, whether people or animals entrusted to you.
Reflect on the legacies left by people you admire and let those stories strengthen your own resolve in difficult moments.

title: The Prophet's Donkey Ya'fur and the Incident with Abd Allah b. Ubayy
summary: The Prophet rode his donkey to visit the sick Sa'd b. Ubada, pausing to invite an assembly including Abd Allah b. Ubayy to accept God, only to be mocked and insulted, while a loyal Companion defended him and the Prophet peacefully diffused the resulting tension.
story: His donkey "¥a'fur" is also known by its diminutive name "Dfayr". This was a mount he would occasionally ride. Al;1mad narrated, from a ftadith of Mul;1ammad b. Isl;1aq, from ¥azid b. Abu I:Iabib, from ¥ azid b. 'Abd Allah al-'Awfi, from 'Abd Allah b. Razim, who quoted 'Ali as having said, "The Messenger of God ($AAS) used to ride a donkey named ''0fayr'." Abu ¥ a'ia narrated it from a ftadith of 'Awn b. 'Abd Allah, from Ibn Mas'ud. It is narrated in a number of aftadith that he rode the donkey. In both Faftift collections, it is narrated that the Prophet ($AAS), mounted on a donkey, passed by an assembly at which 'Abd Allah b. Dbayy b. Salal was present with a mixture of Muslims, idol-worshippers and Jews. He dismounted and invited all to accept God, the Almighty and Glorious. This occurred prior to the battle of Badr. The Prophet ($AAS) was intending to visit (the sickbed) of Sa'd b. 'Obada. 'Abd Allah (b. Ubayy) said to him, "¥ou did not do well by what you said! If it is all true, you still shouldn't bother us with it in our meetings." That was before Islam appeared. It is said that this 'Abd Allah puckered up his nose at the odour coming from the dung from his donkey, and said, "Don't disgust us with the filth from your donkey!" 'Abd Allah b. RawalJa responded, "I swear by God, the odour from the donkey of the Messenger of God is sweeter than the stench from you!" He went on, "Messenger of God, do come and tell us of it in our meetings; we would enjoy that." The two tribes then rose to confront one another and were about to do battle when the Messenger of God (SAAS) pacified them. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then went on to visit Sa'd b. 'Ubada and complained to him about 'Abd Allah b. Obayy. Sa'd told him, "Go easy on him, Messenger of God; by Him who honoured you with the truth, God has truly sent you with the truth. We were about to make a diadem with which to make him our king. When God brought the truth, he choked on his own spittle!"
lessons: Mockery and rejection from those in positions of influence do not diminish the truth of one's message or mission.
A loyal friend who defends your honor in a moment of public humiliation is one of God's greatest gifts.
Peacemaking, even when you are the one wronged, is a sign of true strength and prophetic character.
practical_advice: When you are publicly mocked or belittled, resist the urge to retaliate; focus instead on completing the good deed you set out to do.
Confide in a trusted, wise person after experiencing rejection or humiliation, as the Prophet did with Sa'd, to gain perspective and emotional relief.
Recognize that opposition from others sometimes stems from their own unfulfilled ambitions and wounded pride, not from a flaw in you.

title: The Donkey Ya'fur at Khaybar and the Narration of the Talking Donkey
summary: A remarkable and scholarly-debated narration recounts that at Khaybar a black donkey approached the Prophet, spoke to him, and identified itself as Ya'fur, claiming it had been waiting to serve him and had suffered beatings from a Jewish owner whenever it thought of him.
story: We have narrated above how the Messenger of God (SAAS) rode his donkey in some of the battles at Khaybar, and how he mounted Mu'adh behind himself on it. This section would be over-long if we were to now give all the phraseology and lines of transmission for this. The qa4i 'Ayyad b. Milsa al-Sabli stated in his book al-ShifiP, as had Imam al-I:!aramayn225 in his great book on the origins of the faith, and as others have also maintained, that the Messenger had a donkey named Ziyad b. Shihab. These authorities also related that he would send off this donkey to fetch one or other of his Companions; when he did so, it would stop at their door and bray, so that the man would know that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was requesting his presence. The above scholars also state that this donkey was the seventieth in a line of donkeys, each one of which had been ridden by a prophet. They maintain that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) died, this donkey wandered off, threw itself into a well, and died. No lines of transmission whatsoever are given for these accounts. Their veracity has been denied by various of the /:tufa:;" including 'Abd al-Ra1)man b. Abu I:!atim, and his father, may God have mercy upon them both. On several occasions, I heard my teacher, Sheikh Abu al-I:!ajjaj al-MizzI, may God have mercy upon him, vehemently deny its authenticity. The /:taft:;, Abu Nu'ayrn stated in his work Dala'il al-Nubuwwa, that Abu Bakr A1)mad b. Mul;tammad b. Musa ai-'Anbarl related to him, quoting Al;tmad b. Mul;tammad b. Yusuf, quoting IbrahIm b. Suwayd al-JadhU'I, quoting 'Abd Allah b. 'Udhayn al-Ta'I, from Thawr b. Yazld, from Khalid b. Macdan, from Mu'adh b. Jabal, who said, "A black donkey came to the Prophet (SAAS), while he was at Khaybar and stood right in front of him. He asked the donkey, 'And who are you?' It replied, 'I am 'Amr, son of so-and-so. We were seven brothers, each one of whom was ridden by a prophet. I am the youngest. I was to be for you. A Jew owned me, and whenever I thought of you, I would stumble and he would beat me.' The Messenger of God (SAAS), replied, 'You are Ya'fur.'" This /:tadith is extremely ghanb.
lessons: Islamic scholarship models intellectual honesty by preserving narrations while also transparently noting doubts about their authenticity.
Even the most extraordinary stories from tradition deserve careful scrutiny rather than blind acceptance or outright dismissal.
Longing for a righteous leader or guide, and suffering because of that longing, resonates as a human and spiritual experience regardless of the story's literal status.
practical_advice: When you encounter inspiring stories whose authenticity is uncertain, appreciate their moral meaning while remaining grounded in verified knowledge.
Practice intellectual humility: be willing to say 'I do not know' or 'this is disputed' rather than asserting certainty where none exists.
Use stories, even allegorical ones, as prompts for reflection on loyalty, longing, and the cost of staying true to what you love.
